The 802.1X authentication exchange proceeds through a tightly defined state machine. We model the cumulative number of **EAPOL frame exchanges** required to reach an **Authorized** state.

### 3.1.1 Step-By-Step Mathematical Walkthrough

Let $k$ represent the index of the EAPOL message exchange. Each round trip between the supplicant and the RADIUS server (via the authenticator) consumes **4 message slots** (EAP-Request, EAP-Response, RADIUS-Access-Challenge, RADIUS-Access-Request).

$$
\text{Total messages} = 2 \cdot N_{\text{rt}} + 2
$$

Where $N_{\text{rt}}$ is the number of EAP round trips and the trailing $+2$ accounts for the initial **EAPOL-Start** and the final **EAP-Success** frame.

**Step 1 — Initialization (k = 0):**
The supplicant sends **EAPOL-Start** to the authenticator's PAE (Port Access Entity). This is message count $M_0 = 1$.

$$
M_0 = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Identity Request (k = 1):**
The authenticator replies with **EAP-Request/Identity**. The supplicant responds with **EAP-Response/Identity** containing the username. Cumulative messages:

$$
M_1 = M_0 + 2 = 1 + 2 = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Encapsulation into RADIUS:**
The authenticator forwards the Response/Identity inside a **RADIUS-Access-Request** packet. The RADIUS server replies with **RADIUS-Access-Challenge** containing the first EAP method (e.g., EAP-TLS hello). Cumulative messages:

$$
M_2 = M_1 + 2 = 3 + 2 = 5
$$

**Step 4 — Recursive Challenge-Response Loop:**
For each cryptographic handshake iteration $i$ (e.g., TLS certificate exchange may require $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$), two EAP frames traverse the LAN and two RADIUS frames traverse the backbone. After $n$ such iterations:

$$
M_{\text{loop}}(n) = M_2 + 4n = 5 + 4n
$$

**Step 5 — Final Authorization:**
The RADIUS server returns a **RADIUS-Access-Accept** carrying the authorized VLAN, ACL, and session timeout. The authenticator translates this into an **EAP-Success** and transitions the port to the **Authorized** state.

$$
M_{\text{final}} = M_{\text{loop}}(n) + 1 = 6 + 4n
$$

### 3.1.2 Generalized Closed-Form Expression

Combining all steps, the total message count in a full 802.1X session is:

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 6 + 4n
$$

**For EAP-TLS with a single certificate round trip (n = 1):**

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 6 + 4(1) = 10 \text{ messages}
$$

**For PEAP-MSCHAPv2 with two round trips (n = 2):**

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 6 + 4(2) = 14 \text{ messages}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why **PEAP** has a slightly higher authentication latency (~250 ms) than **EAP-TTLS** (~180 ms) in enterprise benchmarks.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Posture Compliance Score Calculation

A corporate endpoint is subjected to **5 posture checks** with the following weights and outcomes. Determine the access decision.

| Check Index $i$ | Description | Weight $w_i$ | Outcome $c_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OS Patch Level | 0.30 | Pass (1) |
| 2 | Antivirus Signature | 0.25 | Pass (1) |
| 3 | Disk Encryption | 0.20 | Fail (0) |
| 4 | Firewall Enabled | 0.15 | Pass (1) |
| 5 | USB Port Policy | 0.10 | Fail (0) |

**Step 1 — Compute the numerator term-by-term:**

$$
w_1 c_1 = 0.30 \cdot 1 = 0.30
$$

$$
w_2 c_2 = 0.25 \cdot 1 = 0.25
$$

$$
w_3 c_3 = 0.20 \cdot 0 = 0.00
$$

$$
w_4 c_4 = 0.15 \cdot 1 = 0.15
$$

$$
w_5 c_5 = 0.10 \cdot 0 = 0.00
$$

**Step 2 — Sum all weighted terms:**

$$
PCS = 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.00 + 0.15 + 0.00 = 0.70
$$

**Step 3 — Verify weight normalization:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00 \;\;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the threshold decision logic** with $T_{\text{compliant}} = 0.80$ and $T_{\text{remediation}} = 0.40$:

$$
T_{\text{remediation}} = 0.40 \leq PCS = 0.70 < T_{\text{compliant}} = 0.80
$$

**Decision: The endpoint is placed in the QUARANTINE VLAN** for remediation. Disk encryption must be enabled, and USB port policy must be fixed before re-attempting access.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of EAP Methods

| EAP Method | Credential Type | Mutual Auth | Tunnel | Certificate Req. | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| EAP-MD5 | Password | No | No | None | Medium |
| EAP-TLS | Certificate | Yes | Yes (TLS) | Both client & server | **High** |
| EAP-TTLS | Certificate + Password | Yes | Yes (TLS) | Server only | Medium |
| PEAP | Certificate + Password | Yes | Yes (TLS) | Server only | **High** |
| EAP-FAST | PAC tokens | Yes | Yes (TLS) | None (PAC) | Low |
| EAP-SIM | SIM card | Yes | No | None | Low (Telecom) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NAC Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the end-to-end NAC architecture, mapping the interactions between the Supplicant, Authenticator, and Authentication/Posture Servers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Endpoint Device - Supplicant] -->|EAPOL Start Ethertype 0x888E| B[Layer 2 Switch - Authenticator PEP]
    B -->|EAP Request Identity| A
    A -->|EAP Response Identity| B
    B -->|RADIUS Access Request UDP 1812| C[RADIUS Server - PDP]
    C -->|LDAP Query TCP 389| D[Active Directory - Identity Store]
    D -->|User Attributes| C
    C -->|RADIUS Access Challenge| B
    B -->|EAP Request Method| A
    A -->|EAP Response Credentials| B
    B -->|RADIUS Access Request| C
    C -->|Posture Query HTTPS| E[Posture Validation Server]
    E -->|Compliance Report| C
    C -->|RADIUS Access Accept with VLAN 10| B
    B -->|EAP Success Port Authorized| A
    A -->|Compliant Traffic| F[Protected Corporate Network VLAN 10]
    C -->|RADIUS Access Accept with VLAN 99 Quarantine| B
    B -->|EAP Success Quarantine| A
    A -->|Remediation Portal HTTP| G[Remediation Server VLAN 99]
    C -->|COA Disconnect UDP 3799| B
    B -->|Port Re-evaluated| A
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style C fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
    style D fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003366
    style E fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066
    style F fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
    style G fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000
```

## 4.2 NAC Decision State Machine

This diagram captures the **state transitions** of a network port under 802.1X NAC enforcement.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unauthorized
    Unauthorized --> Authenticating : EAPOL Start Received
    Authenticating --> Authorized : RADIUS Access Accept Compliant
    Authenticating --> Quarantine : RADIUS Access Accept Non Compliant
    Authenticating --> Unauthorized : RADIUS Access Reject
    Authorized --> Reauthenticating : Session Timeout
    Reauthenticating --> Authorized : Posture Recheck Pass
    Reauthenticating --> Quarantine : Posture Recheck Fail
    Quarantine --> Authenticating : Endpoint Initiates Remediation
    Authorized --> Unauthorized : COA Disconnect from Server
    Quarantine --> Unauthorized : Admin Lockout
    Unauthorized --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Layered Enforcement Model (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Layer 1 Identity - Who] --> L2[Layer 2 Posture - How Safe]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Policy - What Access]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Enforcement - VLAN ACL Firewall]
    L1 -->|RADIUS LDAP| M1[AAA Engine]
    L2 -->|Posture Agent SNMP| M2[Posture Broker]
    L3 -->|Rule Set| M3[Policy Server]
    L4 -->|Switch AP VPN| M4[Network Device PEP]
    M1 --> P[Central NAC Controller]
    M2 --> P
    M3 --> P
    M4 --> P
    style L1 fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003366
    style L2 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
    style L3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style L4 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000
    style P fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066,stroke-width:3px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Network Access Control (NAC). List its three primary objectives.**

**Model Answer:**
Network Access Control (NAC) is a security framework that enforces policy-based access control on endpoint devices seeking to connect to a network. It restricts access based on identity authentication, security posture validation, and authorization rules before granting entry to protected network resources.

The three primary objectives of NAC are:
1. **Authentication** — Verify the identity of the user and device attempting connection.
2. **Posture Assessment** — Validate that the endpoint complies with the security baseline (OS patches, antivirus, encryption).
3. **Authorization** — Grant appropriate network privileges (VLAN, ACL, bandwidth) based on identity and posture.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only "authentication" and miss posture assessment. KTU examiners explicitly allocate **1 mark for each objective**. Listing only two will cost you 1 mark.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Pre-Admission NAC and Post-Admission NAC.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Pre-Admission NAC | Post-Admission NAC |
|---|---|---|
| **When check occurs** | Before IP address assignment | After IP assignment, before full access |
| **Network access during check** | None (blocked) | Limited (default VLAN) |
| **Security strength** | Higher (zero trust from start) | Lower (brief window of vulnerability) |
| **User experience** | Slower login | Faster, smoother |
| **Typical use case** | High-security zones, defense | Guest Wi-Fi, BYOD |
| **Example protocol** | 802.1X with full posture | Captive portal with delayed scan |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the 802.1X authentication framework in detail. Describe the roles of the supplicant, authenticator, and authentication server with a neat diagram.**

**Model Solution:**

The **IEEE 802.1X standard** is a port-based Network Access Control mechanism that restricts unauthorized devices from gaining access to a LAN. It operates at Layer 2 using the **EAPOL (Extensible Authentication Protocol over LAN)** protocol encapsulated in Ethernet frames identified by **EtherType 0x888E**.

**Roles in 802.1X:**

1. **Supplicant** — The client device (laptop, phone) requesting network access. It runs 802.1X client software such as Windows Native Supplicant, Cisco AnyConnect, or wpa_supplicant on Linux. It initiates the handshake via EAPOL-Start.

2. **Authenticator** — The Layer-2 access device (managed switch, wireless AP, VPN gateway). It acts as a **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)**. It maintains two logical ports per physical port: an **uncontrolled port** (always open for EAPOL/RADIUS traffic) and a **controlled port** (opens only after authorization).

3. **Authentication Server** — Typically a RADIUS server (FreeRADIUS, Cisco ISE, Microsoft NPS). It validates credentials, performs posture lookup, and returns an **Access-Accept, Access-Reject, or Change-of-Authorization (CoA)** message.

**Reference Diagram (covered in SECTION_4.1 above).**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare the different EAP methods (EAP-TLS, EAP-TTLS, PEAP) used in NAC. Which is the most secure and why?**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | EAP-TLS | EAP-TTLS | PEAP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Authentication type** | Mutual certificate-based | Server cert + client password | Server cert + client password |
| **Mutual authentication** | Yes (both sides) | Yes | Yes |
| **Client certificate** | Required | Not required | Not required |
| **Tunnel encryption** | Built-in TLS | Inner TLS tunnel | Inner TLS tunnel |
| **Complexity** | High (PKI deployment) | Medium | Medium |
| **Security rating** | **Highest** | High | High |
| **Deployment ease** | Difficult | Easier | Easier |

**Most Secure: EAP-TLS**

EAP-TLS is the most secure EAP method because it requires **mutual X.509 certificate-based authentication** for both the supplicant and the server. This eliminates vulnerabilities to dictionary attacks, man-in-the-middle, and rogue AP attacks. The TLS handshake occurs in clear text over EAPOL, but the certificate exchange ensures strong cryptographic binding of identity. The trade-off is the operational overhead of managing a full **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** with client certificate enrollment, revocation, and renewal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes:** Students frequently confuse **PEAP** with **EAP-TLS**. Remember: PEAP uses a **server-side certificate only** and authenticates the user via MSCHAPv2 inside the TLS tunnel, while EAP-TLS requires certificates on **both** ends. Examiners award 2 marks for this distinction.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the five phases of NAC operation. Illustrate the complete flow with a state diagram.**

**Model Solution:**

The NAC lifecycle consists of five sequential phases:

**Phase 1 — Discovery:**
The endpoint sends an **EAPOL-Start** frame to the Layer-2 authenticator. The switch detects the supplicant and transitions the port to the **Authenticating** state. The uncontrolled port is open for 802.1X traffic.

**Phase 2 — Authentication:**
The authenticator sends an **EAP-Request/Identity**. The supplicant replies with its username. This is forwarded to the RADIUS server in a **RADIUS-Access-Request** packet. Credentials (certificate, password, OTP) are exchanged using the chosen EAP method (EAP-TLS, PEAP, etc.).

**Phase 3 — Posture Assessment:**
Once identity is verified, the **Posture Validation Server (PVS)** queries the endpoint for security baseline compliance — OS version, antivirus signature date, patch level, disk encryption status. This is done via a posture agent or via agentless scanning (SNMP, SSH).

**Phase 4 — Authorization:**
The PDP (RADIUS server) maps the identity + posture tuple against policy rules. Based on compliance, it issues:
- **Access-Accept** with production VLAN (e.g., VLAN 10) for compliant devices.
- **Access-Accept with Quarantine VLAN** (e.g., VLAN 99) for non-compliant devices.
- **Access-Reject** for blacklisted or unknown devices.

**Phase 5 — Remediation and Continuous Monitoring:**
Non-compliant devices are redirected to a remediation portal where they can install missing patches or enable encryption. Compliant devices are re-authenticated periodically (typically every 24–72 hours) to ensure continuous compliance. **Change of Authorization (CoA)** messages (UDP port 3799) allow the RADIUS server to dynamically revoke or modify access without waiting for session expiry.

(Refer to state diagram in SECTION_4.2 above.)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**An enterprise NAC system performs 6 posture checks with the following weights: w1=0.25, w2=0.20, w3=0.15, w4=0.15, w5=0.15, w6=0.10. A user endpoint passes checks 1, 2, 4, 5 and fails checks 3 and 6. Compute the Posture Compliance Score and determine the access decision if T_compliant = 0.80 and T_remediation = 0.40.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify weight normalization:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i = 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00 \;\;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — List outcomes as binary compliance indicators:**

| $i$ | $w_i$ | $c_i$ | $w_i c_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.25 | 1 | 0.25 |
| 2 | 0.20 | 1 | 0.20 |
| 3 | 0.15 | 0 | 0.00 |
| 4 | 0.15 | 1 | 0.15 |
| 5 | 0.15 | 1 | 0.15 |
| 6 | 0.10 | 0 | 0.00 |

**Step 3 — Compute PCS:**

$$
PCS = 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.00 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.00 = 0.75
$$

**Step 4 — Apply decision logic:**

$$
T_{\text{remediation}} = 0.40 \leq PCS = 0.75 < T_{\text{compliant}} = 0.80
$$

**Decision: The endpoint is assigned to the QUARANTINE VLAN.**

The user must remediate check 3 (weight 0.15) and check 6 (weight 0.10) to raise the score to:

$$
PCS_{\text{new}} = 0.75 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00
$$

After remediation, the score will be 1.00, exceeding $T_{\text{compliant}} = 0.80$, and the device will be promoted to the production VLAN.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** KTU examiners often test whether students **verify weight normalization** before computing the score. Forgetting to state $\sum w_i = 1$ may cost you **1 mark**. Also, students sometimes incorrectly add the weights of *failing* checks instead of multiplying by zero — read the question carefully: "fails" means $c_i = 0$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **NAC** = Authentication + Posture Assessment + Authorization. Always list all three in definitions.
- **802.1X** is the **port-based Layer-2** NAC standard using **EAPOL frames with EtherType 0x888E**.
- The **three roles** are: **Supplicant, Authenticator (PEP), Authentication Server (PDP)** — memorize in this order for protocol flow questions.
- **RADIUS** uses **UDP 1812** (auth) and **UDP 1813** (accounting). **TACACS+** uses **TCP 49**.
- **EAP-TLS** is the most secure (mutual certificates) but hardest to deploy. **PEAP** is the most common in enterprises (server cert + MSCHAPv2).
- **Pre-Admission** = check before IP assignment (stricter). **Post-Admission** = check after IP (smoother UX).
- **Inline NAC** = in traffic path (single point of failure). **Out-of-Band NAC** = off-path, uses VLAN/CoA (scalable).
- **Posture Compliance Score** formula: $PCS = \sum w_i c_i$, with weights summing to 1.0.
- Decision thresholds: **Compliant $\geq 0.80$, Quarantine 0.40–0.79, Reject $< 0.40$** (default values).
- **Quarantine VLAN** is an 802.1Q tagged broadcast domain that isolates non-compliant devices.
- **CoA (Change of Authorization)** uses **UDP 3799** and allows dynamic re-authentication.
- The **5 phases** are: **Discovery, Authentication, Posture Assessment, Authorization, Remediation/Continuous Monitoring**.
- **Agentless NAC** uses **SNMP, SSH, DHCP fingerprinting** to inspect unmanaged devices (IoT, printers).
- NAC integrates with **LDAP/Active Directory** for identity and **antivirus/SIEM APIs** for posture data.
- Total 802.1X message count formula: $M_{\text{total}} = 6 + 4n$ where $n$ is the number of TLS round trips.
- **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** and **SASE** frameworks are built on top of NAC principles — a likely future KTU question.
- Common **valuation traps**: confusing PEAP with EAP-TLS, forgetting weight normalization, mis-stating RADIUS as TCP instead of UDP.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-1
> - **Topic:** Web Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Web Filtering** is a security and policy enforcement technology that monitors, categorizes, and controls an organization's or user's access to web-based content. Formally, it is defined as the process of intercepting, inspecting, and either allowing, blocking, or logging HTTP/HTTPS requests made by a client to a remote web server, based on a pre-defined security policy, URL reputation database, content classification engine, or contextual policy rules.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCCT502 – Network and System Security, Module-1)**, web filtering is positioned as a critical perimeter defense mechanism that complements firewalls and intrusion detection systems (IDS) by adding an **application-layer (Layer-7)** filtering dimension to network security.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module-1):** Web filtering is treated as a *proactive content-control* mechanism. Students must understand its types, architecture, working principle, and its difference from traditional packet filtering.

Mathematically, a web filter acts as a **policy decision function** $F$ that maps a tuple $(U, R, C, P)$ to a decision $D \in \{\text{ALLOW}, \text{BLOCK}, \text{MONITOR}, \text{QUARANTINE}\}$, where:

$$D = F(U, R, C, P)$$

- $U$ = User identity / source IP
- $R$ = Requested Resource (URL, domain, IP)
- $C$ = Content classification result
- $P$ = Active policy rule-set

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a security guard standing at the entrance of a large office complex. Every visitor (a web request) hands over a visiting card (the URL). The guard checks it against three things: (1) a guest list (whitelist), (2) a banned list (blacklist), and (3) a category rulebook (e.g., "no food delivery after 9 PM" = "no social media during work hours"). Depending on the rule triggered, the guard allows entry, denies it, watches them closely, or holds them at a side desk for verification. A **Web Filter is precisely this security guard for your organization's internet traffic**.

The filter sits logically (and sometimes physically) between the user's browser and the World Wide Web, inspecting every "visiting card" (the HTTP request line) and the "luggage" (the page content) before deciding whether the visitor is permitted to enter.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Box

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **URL Filtering** | Blocking/allowing based on the URL string or domain name |
| **Content Filtering** | Blocking based on keywords, patterns, or page content |
| **DNS Filtering** | Blocking at the Domain Name System resolution stage |
| **Proxy-Based Filter** | Filter implemented inside a forward/reverse web proxy |
| **Blacklist** | Explicit deny-list of known malicious/unwanted destinations |
| **Whitelist** | Explicit allow-list overriding all deny rules |
| **Reputation Database** | Continuously updated feed of known-bad URLs (e.g., Google Safe Browsing) |
| **Block Page** | HTTP response served to client when access is denied |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Metric:** A commercial-grade URL reputation database (e.g., BrightCloud, Webroot, Symantec) catalogues over **600 million** classified URLs across more than **80 content categories** as of 2024, with updates pushed every 5–15 minutes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown – How Web Filtering Works

A web filtering system executes the following structured logic pipeline for every outbound HTTP/HTTPS request:

- **Step 1 – Request Interception:** The filter is positioned in-line (via proxy, ICAP, WPAD/PAC, or browser plugin) and captures every outbound request. For HTTPS, this requires TLS interception using a trusted root CA installed on endpoints.
- **Step 2 – URL Normalization:** The raw URL is decoded, lowercased, stripped of tracking parameters, and converted into a canonical form. This is critical because attackers use obfuscation (e.g., `HTTP://%65xample.com`, IP-encoded URLs, IDN homograph attacks).
- **Step 3 – Lookup against Reputation Database:** The normalized URL/domain is hashed and looked up in a local or cloud-hosted reputation feed. Hits return a category (e.g., `phishing`, `malware`, `gambling`, `social-media`) and a confidence score $S \in [0, 1]$.
- **Step 4 – Content Classification (if needed):** If the URL is unknown, the page body is fetched (or sandboxed-rendered) and passed through a classifier — keyword-based, regular-expression-based, or ML-based (e.g., Naive Bayes, SVM, BERT).
- **Step 5 – Policy Evaluation:** The result is evaluated against the enterprise policy: $P(u, r, c) \rightarrow D$. Time-of-day, user group, device posture, and bandwidth quotas are all factored in.
- **Step 6 – Action Enforcement:** The filter returns one of: **ALLOW** (pass-through), **BLOCK** (serve block page, HTTP 403), **MONITOR** (log but allow), or **QUARANTINE** (redirect to a warning page requiring user acknowledgement).
- **Step 7 – Logging & Reporting:** Every decision is logged with timestamp, user, URL, category, action, and bytes — feeding SIEM dashboards for compliance audits (e.g., ISO 27001, CIPA, RBI cybersecurity framework).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the architecture:** Plain firewalls operate at Layers 3–4 and see only IP/port tuples — they cannot tell that `bank-login-secure.com` is a phishing site hosted on a legitimate cloud IP. Web filtering operates at Layer 7, where **semantic context** becomes visible.

## 2.2 Classification of Web Filtering Techniques

| # | Technique | Layer of Operation | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DNS-based Filtering** | Layer 7 (Application) via resolver | Easy to deploy, no proxy needed | Cannot inspect path/URL subdirectories; bypassable via DoH/DoT |
| 2 | **URL/Domain Blacklist Filtering** | Layer 7 | Fast O(1) hash lookup | Zero-day malicious sites not yet listed |
| 3 | **Keyword/Regex Content Filtering** | Layer 7 (Deep inspection) | Catches novel threats by pattern | High false-positive rate |
| 4 | **Category-Based Filtering** | Layer 7 (Reputation DB) | Granular policy control | Requires regularly-updated DB subscription |
| 5 | **Proxy-Based Filtering** | Layer 7 (Man-in-the-middle for HTTP) | Full payload visibility | Requires TLS interception for HTTPS |
| 6 | **Machine-Learning Classifier** | Layer 7 (Statistical) | Detects zero-day phishing | Needs training corpus, GPU for inference |
| 7 | **Reputation Scoring** | Cross-layer | Aggregates global threat intel | Vendor lock-in, latency for cloud lookup |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX RULE:** All vertical bars in formulas below are rendered using `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. Do not use the raw pipe character `|` inside any cell.

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Policy Decision Function** | $D = F(U, R, C, P)$ | Maps a 4-tuple of User, Resource, Content, Policy to a Decision |
| 2 | **Decision Domain** | $D \in \{\text{ALLOW}, \text{BLOCK}, \text{MONITOR}, \text{QUARANTINE}\}$ | The four canonical enforcement actions |
| 3 | **Naive Bayes Content Classification** | $P(c \vert d) = \dfrac{P(c) \cdot P(d \vert c)}{P(d)}$ | Posterior probability of class $c$ given document $d$; classify if above threshold $\tau$ |
| 4 | **Bayesian Decision Threshold** | $D = \arg\max_{c} P(c \vert d) \; \text{ s.t. } P(c \vert d) \geq \tau$ | Accept classification only when confidence crosses $\tau$ (typically $\tau = 0.5$ or 0.85) |
| 5 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $\text{tf-idf}(t, d) = \text{tf}(t, d) \cdot \log\!\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$ | $N$ = total documents; $\text{df}(t)$ = docs containing term $t$ |
| 6 | **Classifier Precision** | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all pages blocked, how many were truly malicious |
| 7 | **Classifier Recall** | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of all truly malicious pages, how many were caught |
| 8 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean — the headline metric for a content classifier |
| 9 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Legitimate sites wrongly blocked — the *annoyance* metric |
| 10 | **URL Hash Lookup (Blacklist)** | $T_{\text{lookup}} = O(1) \text{ via hash table}$ | Average-case constant-time membership test |
| 11 | **TLS Interception Latency** | $L_{\text{TLS}} = 2 \cdot RTT + T_{\text{handshake}}$ | Roughly doubles handshake cost because the filter terminates the session |
| 12 | **Blacklist Storage Lower Bound** | $M_{\text{storage}} = N \cdot \vert H \vert$ | $N$ entries of hash size $\vert H \vert$ bits (e.g., SHA-256 = 256 bits) |
| 13 | **Bypass-Resistance Score** | $B = 1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{bypass}}}{N_{\text{attempts}}}$ | Ratio of attacks successfully blocked; $B \in [0, 1]$ |
| 14 | **Entropy of URL (Obfuscation Detector)** | $H(\text{url}) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | High entropy → likely randomized/DGA-style malicious URL |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprises:** Block phishing, C2 (Command-and-Control) callbacks, and non-work distractions. Average productivity uplift measured at **8–15%** post-deployment (Gartner, 2023).
- **Educational Institutions:** Enforce CIPA (Children's Internet Protection Act) compliance in the U.S. and similar Indian CII (Critical Information Infrastructure) norms.
- **ISPs:** Provide family-safe DNS resolvers (e.g., OpenDNS FamilyShield, CleanBrowsing).
- **Healthcare (HIPAA):** Prevent leakage of PHI via webmail and unauthorized cloud uploads.
- **Defense / Government:** Block nation-state APT infrastructure and enforce compartmentalized access.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayesian Content Classifier for Web Pages

A web filter often needs to classify a fetched page $d$ as either **benign** ($c_b$) or **malicious** ($c_m$). We derive the optimal decision rule using Bayes' theorem.

**Step 1 — Start with the Bayes posterior for malicious class:**

$$P(c_m \vert d) = \dfrac{P(c_m) \cdot P(d \vert c_m)}{P(d)}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the evidence $P(d)$ using the law of total probability:**

$$P(d) = P(d \vert c_m) \cdot P(c_m) + P(d \vert c_b) \cdot P(c_b)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the posterior expression:**

$$P(c_m \vert d) = \dfrac{P(c_m) \cdot P(d \vert c_m)}{P(d \vert c_m) \cdot P(c_m) + P(d \vert c_b) \cdot P(c_b)}$$

**Step 4 — Define the likelihood ratio $\Lambda(d)$ to simplify the decision rule:**

$$\Lambda(d) = \dfrac{P(d \vert c_m)}{P(d \vert c_b)}$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite the posterior in terms of the prior odds and likelihood ratio:**

$$P(c_m \vert d) = \dfrac{\Lambda(d) \cdot P(c_m)}{\Lambda(d) \cdot P(c_m) + (1 - P(c_m))}$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule with threshold $\tau$:** Block the page iff the posterior exceeds $\tau$:

$$\dfrac{\Lambda(d) \cdot P(c_m)}{\Lambda(d) \cdot P(c_m) + (1 - P(c_m))} \geq \tau$$

**Step 7 — Algebraically isolate $\Lambda(d)$ to get the explicit threshold condition:**

$$\Lambda(d) \cdot P(c_m) \geq \tau \cdot \Lambda(d) \cdot P(c_m) + \tau \cdot (1 - P(c_m))$$

**Step 8 — Group the $\Lambda(d)$ terms on one side:**

$$\Lambda(d) \cdot P(c_m) \cdot (1 - \tau) \geq \tau \cdot (1 - P(c_m))$$

**Step 9 — Final explicit likelihood-ratio threshold rule:**

$$\Lambda(d) \geq \dfrac{\tau \cdot (1 - P(c_m))}{P(c_m) \cdot (1 - \tau)}$$

**Interpretation:** The right-hand side is a constant that depends only on the prior $P(c_m)$ and chosen threshold $\tau$. If the observed likelihood ratio of the page's feature vector exceeds this constant, the page is classified as malicious and **BLOCK** is enforced.

## 3.2 Derivation: F1-Score as the Balanced Classifier Metric

Raw accuracy is misleading for web filtering because >99% of pages are benign — a trivial "always-allow" classifier would score 99% accuracy while missing every threat. The F1-score corrects this.

**Step 1 — Start from the harmonic mean of Precision $P$ and Recall $R$:**

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{1}{P} + \dfrac{1}{R}}$$

**Step 2 — Combine the two fractions under a common denominator:**

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{R + P}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the definitions $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ and $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$:**

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}}{\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} + \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$ to clear denominators:**

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP \cdot (TP + FP) + TP \cdot (TP + FN)}$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $TP$ from the denominator and simplify:**

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

**Final compact form used in every KTU answer:**

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

A web filter processes 10,000 web requests in a day. Of these, 50 are actual phishing pages, and the filter correctly blocks 45 of them while wrongly blocking 100 legitimate pages.

| Parameter | Value | Computation |
|---|---|---|
| Total requests $N$ | 10,000 | given |
| True phishing pages | 50 | given |
| Correctly blocked (TP) | 45 | given |
| Missed phishing (FN) | 5 | $50 - 45$ |
| Wrongly blocked benign (FP) | 100 | given |
| Correctly allowed (TN) | 9,855 | $10,000 - 50 - 100 - 45 + 5$ (adjusted) |
| **Precision $P$** | $\dfrac{45}{45+100} = \dfrac{45}{145} \approx 0.310$ | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ |
| **Recall $R$** | $\dfrac{45}{45+5} = \dfrac{45}{50} = 0.900$ | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ |
| **F1-Score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot 0.310 \cdot 0.900}{0.310 + 0.900} \approx 0.462$ | Harmonic mean |

**Engineering insight:** Recall of **0.900** is excellent (90% of phishing is caught), but the F1 of **0.462** signals the precision is too low — too many legitimate sites are collateral damage. A real-world tuning exercise would raise the threshold $\tau$ to trade recall for precision.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Web Filtering Architecture (Layer-7 Inline Proxy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ClientBrowser] --> B[NetworkEgressRouter]
    B --> C{IsTrafficHTTPHTTPS}
    C -- Yes --> D[InlineWebFilterProxy]
    C -- No --> E[DirectInternet]
    D --> F[URLNormalizationModule]
    F --> G[ReputationDBLookup]
    G --> H{IsURLCategorized}
    H -- Yes --> I[PolicyDecisionEngine]
    H -- No --> J[ContentClassifierML]
    J --> I
    I --> K{Decision}
    K -- ALLOW --> L[ForwardToOriginServer]
    K -- BLOCK --> M[ServeBlockPageHTTP403]
    K -- MONITOR --> N[LogAndForward]
    K -- QUARANTINE --> O[RedirectToWarning]
    L --> P[OriginWebServer]
    M --> A
    N --> P
    O --> A
    D --> Q[SIEMLoggingAnalytics]
    I --> Q
    Q --> R[AdminDashboardReports]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `ClientBrowser` issues an HTTP/HTTPS request.
- The `InlineWebFilterProxy` (a Layer-7 device — could be a cloud SWG, on-prem appliance, or ZTNA broker) intercepts it.
- `URLNormalizationModule` strips obfuscation tricks.
- `ReputationDBLookup` performs O(1) hash-based membership test against a vendor feed.
- Uncategorised URLs go to `ContentClassifierML` (e.g., BERT-based phishing detector).
- `PolicyDecisionEngine` weighs user group, time-of-day, device posture, and category.
- One of four actions is returned; every event is shipped to `SIEMLoggingAnalytics` for compliance audit.

## 4.2 DNS-Based Web Filtering (Lightweight Variant)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ClientApp] --> B[StubResolver]
    B --> C{CheckLocalHostsFile}
    C -- Found --> D[ReturnCachedIP]
    C -- NotFound --> E[RecursiveResolver]
    E --> F{DNSFilterActive}
    F -- No --> G[UpstreamAuthoritativeNS]
    F -- Yes --> H[PolicyLookupTable]
    H --> I{IsDomainAllowed}
    I -- Yes --> G
    I -- No --> J[ReturnNXDOMAINOrSinkholeIP]
    G --> K[ReturnRealIP]
    J --> L[BrowserShowsErrorOrBlockPage]
    K --> M[BrowserConnectsToRealSite]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- DNS filtering operates earlier in the request lifecycle than proxy filtering — before any TCP connection is even opened.
- The trade-off: cheap, fast, and works for all apps (not just browsers), but cannot see the URL path or HTTP method, so it blocks entire domains rather than specific pages.
- Modern evasion tactics (DoH — DNS over HTTPS, DoT — DNS over TLS) bypass this layer unless enforced at the firewall.

## 4.3 Web Filter Decision State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RequestReceived
    RequestReceived --> NormalizingURL
    NormalizingURL --> LookingUpReputation
    LookingUpReputation --> ClassifyingContent : UnknownURL
    LookingUpReputation --> EvaluatingPolicy : KnownCategory
    ClassifyingContent --> EvaluatingPolicy
    EvaluatingPolicy --> AllowState : PolicyPermits
    EvaluatingPolicy --> BlockState : PolicyDenies
    EvaluatingPolicy --> MonitorState : PolicyWatches
    EvaluatingPolicy --> QuarantineState : PolicyHolds
    AllowState --> ForwardedToOrigin
    BlockState --> BlockPageServed
    MonitorState --> ForwardedToOrigin
    MonitorState --> AuditLogged
    QuarantineState --> WarningPageServed
    ForwardedToOrigin --> [*]
    BlockPageServed --> [*]
    AuditLogged --> [*]
    WarningPageServed --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The filter is a finite-state machine. Every request traverses the same canonical pipeline; the exit state is determined entirely by the policy engine's evaluation of the four-tuple $(U, R, C, P)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Web Filtering. List any two advantages of deploying it in a corporate network.**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

> [!NOTE]
> *Web Filtering* is a Layer-7 security technology that monitors, categorizes, and controls user access to web resources based on URL reputation, content classification, and organizational policy. It enforces an allow/block/monitor decision on every outbound HTTP/HTTPS request.
>
> **Advantages:** (1) **Proactive threat defence** — blocks phishing, malware and C2 callbacks before payload delivery. (2) **Productivity & bandwidth control** — restricts non-work categories (e.g., social media, streaming) during business hours, conserving WAN capacity. (3) **Regulatory compliance** — supports CIPA, HIPAA, RBI mandates. (4) **Visibility** — generates per-user browsing logs feeding SIEM analytics.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series-B]
**Differentiate between DNS-based filtering and proxy-based filtering in 5 lines.**

**Model Answer (≈ 70 words):**

| Aspect | DNS-Based Filtering | Proxy-Based Filtering |
|---|---|---|
| Layer | Application (resolver) | Application (inline) |
| Granularity | Entire domain only | Full URL + payload |
| HTTPS visibility | None (encrypted) | Yes, with TLS interception |
| Bypass resistance | Low (DoH/DoT evade) | High (needs traffic redirection) |
| Deployment | Trivial (change resolver) | Requires proxy config / PAC / WPAD |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Question Slot]
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **architecture and working of a web filtering system** with a neat block diagram. Clearly describe the role of each module.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **any four types of web filtering techniques** with their merits and demerits.

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Architecture and Working [7 Marks]

A web filtering system is logically a **tuple-decision function** $F(U, R, C, P) \rightarrow D$ that processes every outbound HTTP/HTTPS request. Its standard architecture comprises the following six modules:

1. **Request Interceptor** — Sits inline between the client and the internet. In enterprise networks, it is implemented as a forward proxy, an ICAP server, a ZTNA broker, or a Cloud Secure Web Gateway (SWG). All outbound traffic is force-routed using WPAD/PAC files, explicit proxy settings, or routing tricks (PBR — Policy-Based Routing).

2. **URL Normalization & Decoder** — The raw URL is decoded (URL-decode, punycode decode, percent-decode), lowercased, stripped of tracking parameters (`utm_*`, `fbclid`), and converted to a canonical form. This defeats simple obfuscation attacks like `g⁣oogle.com` (zero-width characters) or `xn--ggle-55da.com` (IDN homograph).

3. **Reputation Database Lookup** — The normalized URL/domain is hashed (SHA-256) and matched against a local copy of a vendor reputation feed (e.g., BrightCloud, Webroot, Symantec). The lookup is $O(1)$ amortized using a hash table or Bloom filter, returning a category and a confidence score $S \in [0, 1]$.

4. **Content Classifier (Optional / On-Demand)** — If the URL is unknown, the filter sandbox-fetches the page and runs it through a classifier. **Naive Bayes** classifiers use $P(c \vert d) = \dfrac{P(c) \cdot P(d \vert c)}{P(d)}$; **SVMs** separate feature vectors in high-dimensional space; modern systems use **Transformer-based** (BERT/RoBERTa) models for phishing detection with F1-scores above **0.96** on standard datasets (e.g., PhishTank).

5. **Policy Decision Engine** — The category, user group, time-of-day, device posture (e.g., EDR-healthy?), and bandwidth quota are evaluated. The engine emits one of four actions:
   - $D = \text{ALLOW}$: transparent pass-through
   - $D = \text{BLOCK}$: serve HTTP 403 with branded block page
   - $D = \text{MONITOR}$: log and pass through (used for borderline cases during policy tuning)
   - $D = \text{QUARANTINE}$: redirect to interstitial warning page

6. **Logging / SIEM Connector** — Every decision is shipped to a SIEM (Splunk, QRadar, Elastic) as a CEF or LEEF formatted event with fields: timestamp, user, src_ip, url, category, action, bytes_sent, user_agent. This powers audit reports and is mandatory for compliance (ISO 27001 A.12.6, RBI Cyber Security Framework).

**Diagrammatic Summary (already covered in SECTION 4.1):** Client → Proxy → URL Normalizer → Reputation DB → Classifier → Policy Engine → ALLOW/BLOCK/MONITOR/QUARANTINE → Origin Server / Block Page, with logging hook to SIEM.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Four Types of Web Filtering [7 Marks]

**1. URL / Domain Blacklist Filtering** *(~1.5 marks)*
- **Mechanism:** Maintain a static list of blocked URLs/domains. Membership tested via $O(1)$ hash lookup.
- **Merits:** Extremely fast, easy to implement (single `iptables` rule or DNS sinkhole), almost zero false positives for listed entries.
- **Demerits:** Zero-day threats not in the list pass through; list maintenance is burdensome; no contextual content awareness.

**2. Keyword / Regular-Expression Content Filtering** *(~1.5 marks)*
- **Mechanism:** Inspect page body or URL path for forbidden patterns using regex (e.g., `(?i)(free\s+money|click\s+here)`).
- **Merits:** Catches novel, never-before-seen pages; tunable to organizational policy; language-agnostic with UTF-aware regex.
- **Demerits:** High false-positive rate; easily evaded with Unicode homoglyphs, image-only content, or obfuscated words (`fr.ee m0ney`).

**3. Category-Based Reputation Filtering** *(~2 marks)*
- **Mechanism:** Subscribe to a vendor reputation feed (e.g., BrightCloud) that categorizes ~600M URLs across ~80 classes (Phishing, Malware, Gambling, Adult, Social-Network, News, Sports, etc.). Block entire categories per user-group.
- **Merits:** Granular policy control (e.g., "block Social-Media for engineering, allow for marketing"); updated every 5–15 minutes; backed by global threat intelligence.
- **Demerits:** Vendor subscription cost; slight cloud-lookup latency; legitimate sites occasionally mis-classified (e.g., medical content wrongly tagged as "Adult").

**4. Machine-Learning / Behavioural Filtering** *(~2 marks)*
- **Mechanism:** Train a supervised classifier (Naive Bayes, Random Forest, BERT) on labelled URL and page features — lexical (URL length, special-char ratio, entropy $H(\text{url})$), host-based (ASN reputation, age, TLS cert issuer), and content-based (TF-IDF vectors, DOM tree, favicon hash).
- **Merits:** Detects zero-day phishing and malware distribution; state-of-the-art models achieve **F1 > 0.95**; adapts to evolving threats with retraining.
- **Demerits:** Requires a curated, balanced training corpus; opaque "black-box" decisions (GDPR right-to-explanation concerns); GPU resources for inference; possible adversarial evasion with crafted inputs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *web filtering* with *firewall filtering* and lose 2–3 marks. Remember: **firewall = Layer 3/4 (IP/Port/Protocol)**, **web filter = Layer 7 (URL/Content/Application protocol semantics)**. Always mention the OSI layer explicitly in your answer to score the layer-attribution mark.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative): [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Question Slot]
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain **how Bayes' theorem is used in content-based web filtering** for phishing page classification. Derive the decision threshold condition.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A web filter has the following daily confusion-matrix totals: $TP = 480$, $FP = 120$, $FN = 20$, $TN = 9380$. Compute the **precision, recall, F1-score, accuracy, and false-positive rate**, and comment on whether the classifier is production-ready.

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Bayes Theorem in Content Filtering [7 Marks]

**1. Problem Setup** *(1 mark)*
When a URL is uncategorised, the filter fetches the page and must decide: is it benign ($c_b$) or malicious/phishing ($c_m$)? Let $d$ be the feature vector of the document (e.g., TF-IDF vector, n-gram counts, lexical URL features).

**2. Bayes Posterior** *(1 mark)*
The probability the page is malicious given its features is, by Bayes' theorem:

$$P(c_m \vert d) = \dfrac{P(c_m) \cdot P(d \vert c_m)}{P(d)}$$

where $P(c_m)$ is the prior probability of any random page being malicious (~0.0001 in a typical web traffic mix).

**3. Total-Probability Expansion** *(1 mark)*
Expanding the evidence term:

$$P(d) = P(d \vert c_m) P(c_m) + P(d \vert c_b) P(c_b)$$

**4. Likelihood Ratio and Threshold Rule** *(2 marks)*
Define the likelihood ratio $\Lambda(d) = \dfrac{P(d \vert c_m)}{P(d \vert c_b)}$. The classifier blocks the page iff $P(c_m \vert d) \geq \tau$, which after algebra (see SECTION 3.1, Steps 7–9) reduces to:

$$\Lambda(d) \geq \dfrac{\tau (1 - P(c_m))}{P(c_m)(1 - \tau)}$$

**5. Concrete Numerical Example** *(2 marks)*
Suppose $P(c_m) = 0.001$ and $\tau = 0.95$. The threshold is:

$$\dfrac{0.95 \cdot 0.999}{0.001 \cdot 0.05} = \dfrac{0.94905}{0.00005} = 18{,}981$$

The filter blocks any page whose likelihood ratio of "looks like phishing" to "looks like benign" exceeds **18,981** — a deliberately strict threshold to keep false positives low. In practice, this is why **anti-phishing precision is prioritised** over recall: a single wrongly-blocked banking site destroys user trust far more than a missed phishing attempt.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Confusion-Matrix Computation [7 Marks]

**Given:** $TP = 480$, $FP = 120$, $FN = 20$, $TN = 9380$, $N = 10{,}000$.

**Step 1 — Precision** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{480}{480 + 120} = \dfrac{480}{600} = 0.800$$

**Step 2 — Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{480}{480 + 20} = \dfrac{480}{500} = 0.960$$

**Step 3 — F1-Score** *(1.5 marks)*

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.800 \cdot 0.960}{0.800 + 0.960} = \dfrac{1.536}{1.760} \approx 0.873$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy and FPR** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{N} = \dfrac{480 + 9380}{10{,}000} = \dfrac{9860}{10{,}000} = 0.986$$

$$\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{120}{120 + 9380} = \dfrac{120}{9500} \approx 0.0126$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Verdict** *(1 mark)*
With **Precision 0.80, Recall 0.96, F1 0.873, FPR 1.26%**, the classifier is **production-acceptable but not excellent**. Recall of 96% means only 20 phishing pages per 10,000 requests escape — strong performance. However, the 1.26% false-positive rate means 120 legitimate pages per day are blocked, which is noticeable in a 10,000-user enterprise (~12,000 false-blocks per day). Recommended next step: **raise the decision threshold $\tau$** to push precision toward 0.90, accepting a small drop in recall.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Numerical part):** Do not confuse $TN$ with $FN$ when reading the confusion matrix. Convention: $TP$ = positive class correctly predicted, $TN$ = negative class correctly predicted, $FP$ = negative wrongly predicted as positive (Type-I error), $FN$ = positive wrongly predicted as negative (Type-II error). Mis-swapping $FP$ and $FN$ will cost you 2–3 marks across the entire 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Web Filtering (Module-1)**

- **Definition:** Layer-7 security technology controlling access to web resources via policy-based decision function $D = F(U, R, C, P)$.
- **Four canonical actions:** ALLOW, BLOCK, MONITOR, QUARANTINE.
- **OSI Layer:** Application (Layer 7) — distinguish from firewall (L3/L4).
- **Pipeline:** Interception → Normalization → Reputation Lookup → (Optional) Classification → Policy Evaluation → Enforcement → Logging.
- **Types (must know 4+):** URL/Domain Blacklist, Keyword/Regex, Category/Reputation, ML-based, DNS-based, Proxy-based.
- **Key formulas to memorize verbatim:** $P(c \vert d) = \dfrac{P(c) P(d \vert c)}{P(d)}$, $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$, $\text{tf-idf}(t,d) = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log\!\left(\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$.
- **Bayesian threshold rule:** $\Lambda(d) \geq \dfrac{\tau (1 - P(c_m))}{P(c_m)(1 - \tau)}$ — derive it cold in the exam.
- **Metrics to compute:** Precision, Recall, F1, FPR, Accuracy — and the trade-off between them.
- **HTTPS challenge:** Requires TLS interception with a trusted root CA on endpoints; doubles handshake latency.
- **Bypass techniques to mention:** DoH, DoT, VPN tunnels, Tor, URL obfuscation (IDN homographs, percent-encoding, zero-width chars).
- **Real-world examples to cite:** Zscaler Internet Access (ZIA), Netskope SWG, Palo Alto URL Filtering, Cisco Umbrella (DNS), Fortinet FortiGuard.
- **Compliance angles:** CIPA, ISO 27001 A.12.6, RBI Cyber Security Framework, HIPAA, GDPR (right-to-explanation for ML-based filters).
- **Examiner-favourite keywords:** "Layer-7 visibility," "URL canonicalization," "reputation database," "policy decision point," "false positive vs false negative trade-off."
- **Common exam traps:** (1) Confusing web filter with firewall. (2) Mixing up FP/FN in confusion-matrix calculations. (3) Forgetting to specify the action set. (4) Not drawing a block diagram (lose 1–2 marks). (5) Skipping units/metrics in numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Security Policies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY > - **Module:** Module-1 > - **Topic:** Network Security Policies # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Network Security Policy (NSP)** is a formalized, documented set of rules, practices, and procedures that govern the access, use, management, and protection of an organization's network infrastructure and the data traversing it. In the KTU 2024 outcome-based framework, a network security policy is classified under the **management-plane controls** of information security — it defines *what* must be protected, *who* may access it, *under what conditions*, and *what actions* are permissible or prohibited. The internationally accepted reference definition comes from **RFC 2196 — The Site Security Handbook (1997)**, which states that a security policy is *"a formal statement of rules by which people who are given access to an organization's technology and information assets must abide."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Examiner's Expectation):** > A Network Security Policy is a **living document** that translates an organization's security objectives into enforceable technical, administrative, and physical controls across the OSI layers, particularly the **Network (Layer 3)**, **Transport (Layer 4)**, and **Application (Layer 7)** layers. ## 1.2 Key Vocabulary Anchors (Glossary) | Term | Precise Definition | |---|---| | **Policy** | A high-level statement of intent and principle (the *what* and *why*). | | **Standard** | A mandatory requirement that supports a policy (uniform implementation). | | **Guideline** | A non-mandatory recommendation; assists in policy compliance. | | **Procedure** | Step-by-step instructions to operationalize a policy (the *how*). | | **Baseline** | Minimum acceptable security configuration for any system. | > [!NOTE] > The hierarchy of security documentation is: **Policy → Standard → Baseline → Procedure → Guideline**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *differentiate* these — a 3-mark favourite. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Apartment Building Security" Model Imagine a high-rise apartment complex (your network). Before tenants move in, the building association publishes a **rulebook**: - *Visitors must register at the lobby* → maps to **Access Control Policy** - *No smoking in common areas* → maps to **Acceptable Use Policy (AUP)** - *CCTV records all hallways 24×7* → maps to **Network Monitoring & Logging Policy** - *Moving trucks may enter only between 9 AM–5 PM* → maps to **Perimeter & VPN Policy** - *Tenants must report lost keys within 1 hour* → maps to **Incident Response Policy** - *Failing to comply = eviction* → maps to **Enforcement, Auditing & Sanctions** A **security policy is exactly this rulebook** — but for routers, switches, firewalls, servers, and the data packets flowing through them. Without a policy, every security decision is *ad-hoc*, *inconsistent*, and *legally indefensible*. ## 1.4 Scope of Network Security Policies Network security policies govern, but are not limited to: 1. **User access** — Authentication, Authorization, Accounting (AAA). 2. **Traffic filtering** — Firewall rules, ACLs, segmentation. 3. **Remote connectivity** — VPN usage, BYOD, third-party access. 4. **Wireless networks** — SSID exposure, WPA3 enforcement. 5. **Data in transit** — TLS/SSH/IPsec mandates. 6. **Logging & audit** — SIEM forwarding, retention. 7. **Incident response** — Breach notification, escalation tree. 8. **Change management** — Patch cycles, configuration changes. 9. **Acceptable use** — Personal use, prohibited content. 10. **Physical network access** — Server room entry, console ports. > [!TIP] > A robust NSP is **technology-agnostic** at the policy layer and **technology-specific** at the standard/procedure layer. The policy says *"encrypt all confidential data in transit"*; the standard says *"use TLS 1.2 or higher with AES-256-GCM."* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of a Network Security Policy (RFC 2196 Triad) RFC 2196 recommends structuring every site security policy into three coordinated layers. KTU Module-1 explicitly tests this classification. ### 2.1.1 Pillar 1 — **Computer Security Policies** Govern the *endpoints* (hosts, servers, endpoints) connected to the network. Sub-policies include: - **Operating System Hardening Policy** — Disable unused services, enforce least-privilege accounts. - **Malware Protection Policy** — Mandate EDR/AV, define signature update cadence (e.g., every **4 hours**). - **Patch Management Policy** — Critical patches within **72 hours** of CVE publication (NIST SP 800-40r4 reference). - **Account & Password Policy** — Minimum **12 characters**, NIST 800-63B, no forced periodic rotation unless compromise is suspected. - **Backup Policy** — 3-2-1 rule: **3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite**. ### 2.1.2 Pillar 2 — **Network Access Policies** Govern *how* entities enter and traverse the network. - **Acceptable Use Policy (AUP)** — Defines permitted activities on company networks. - **Remote Access / VPN Policy** — Mandates IPsec or SSL-VPN, MFA, split-tunnel rules. - **Wireless Access Policy** — WPA3-Enterprise, 802.1X, rogue AP detection. - **Guest Network Policy** — Internet-only, isolated VLAN, captive portal. - **Network Segmentation Policy** — Zones such as IT/OT/PCI-DSS, enforced by firewall ACLs and VRFs. ### 2.1.3 Pillar 3 — **Internet Access Policies** Govern *outbound* traffic from the network to the public Internet. - **Egress Filtering Policy** — Block RFC 1918 leakage, block known C2 IPs. - **Web Filtering Policy** — URL category-based blocking, DNS sinkholing. - **Email Security Policy** — SPF, DKIM, DMARC enforcement. - **Data Loss Prevention (DLP) Policy** — Prevent exfiltration of PII/PHI/PCI data. - **Cloud & SaaS Acceptable Use Policy** — Shadow IT governance. ## 2.2 Characteristics of an Effective Network Security Policy > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners award marks for these *descriptive attributes* — memorize the list. 1. **Realistic & Implementable** — Written for the actual environment, not a textbook ideal. 2. **Concise & Clear** — Avoids legalese; understandable by a non-technical employee. 3. **Enforceable** — Every rule is technically and procedurally enforceable. 4. **Supported by Management** — Signed by the CIO/CISO with budget backing. 5. **Communicated** — Disseminated, read-acknowledged by all employees. 6. **Living Document** — Reviewed at least **annually** or after major incidents. 7. **Consistent** — Does not contradict other organizational policies. 8. **Auditable** — Compliance can be objectively measured. ## 2.3 Policy Design Principles (Foundational Theory) | Principle | Explanation | KTU 2024 Relevance | |---|---|---| | **Least Privilege** | Grant minimum permissions required for the role. | Often asked as a 3-mark question. | | **Defense in Depth** | Multiple overlapping controls; failure of one ≠ failure of all. | Directly tested in Module-1. | | **Separation of Duties (SoD)** | No single individual controls an entire critical process. | Module-2 overlap; Module-1 introduction. | | **Need-to-Know** | Information access is restricted to those with a legitimate need. | AUP backbone. | | **Fail-Safe Defaults** | Default-deny posture for firewalls/ACLs. | Direct mapping to ACL rule ordering. | | **Open Design vs. Security Through Obscurity** | Rely on strong crypto, not on attacker ignorance. | Frequently debated in KTU answers. | | **Complete Mediation** | Every access request must be checked against authorization. | AAA, RBAC. | | **Psychological Acceptability** | Users must be able to perform tasks without circumventing controls. | Reduces shadow IT. | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Key Frameworks & Standards) > [!NOTE] > This table is a *revision-ready* cheat sheet for the KTU ESE. | Framework / Standard | Issuing Body | Mandatory For | Key Concept in NSP | |---|---|---|---| | **RFC 2196** | IETF (1997) | Site Security Policy structure | Three-pillar model | | **ISO/IEC 27001:2022** | ISO/IEC | ISMS certification | Annex A controls (93 controls in 4 themes) | | **ISO/IEC 27002:2022** | ISO/IEC | Implementation guidance | Control objectives & implementation | | **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** | NIST (USA) | U.S. federal systems | 1000+ controls across 20 families | | **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1** | NIST | Risk assessment | Threat × Vulnerability × Impact | | **NIST CSF 2.0** | NIST (2024) | All sectors | Identify–Protect–Detect–Respond–Recover–Govern | | **COBIT 2019** | ISACA | IT governance | Aligns policy with business goals | | **PCI-DSS v4.0** | PCI SSC | Card-data environments | Network segmentation mandate | | **HIPAA Security Rule** | HHS (USA) | Healthcare (PHI) | §164.308 administrative safeguards | | **GDPR Art. 32** | EU | Personal data | "Appropriate technical and organizational measures" | | **CIS Controls v8** | CIS | Prioritized actions | Implementation Groups IG1/IG2/IG3 | | **IT Act 2000 + 2021 amendment** | Govt. of India | Indian networks | Section 43A, 72A — penalties | ## 2.5 The NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0 — Policy Mapping The **NIST CSF 2.0** (released February 26, 2024) is the latest reference. A network security policy directly maps to the **GOVERN (GV)** function, while the technical controls map to **PROTECT, DETECT, RESPOND, RECOVER**. - **GV.PO (Govern — Policies):** Organizational policy is established, communicated, and enforced. - **PR.AC (Protect — Access Control):** Identities, credentials, and access permissions are managed. - **PR.IR (Protect — Infra Resilience):** Networks and environments are protected. - **DE.CM (Detect — Continuous Monitoring):** Networks are monitored for anomalies. - **RS.MI (Respond — Mitigation):** Incidents are contained and mitigated. ## 2.6 Policy Lifecycle (ISO 27001 PDCA Alignment) The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to know the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle for policy management. 1. **Plan** — Risk assessment, scope definition, policy drafting. 2. **Do** — Communication, training, implementation, deployment of controls. 3. **Check** — Monitoring, audits, compliance measurement, KPI tracking. 4. **Act** — Update policy, corrective actions, lessons learned, version increment. A typical policy lifecycle follows: **Draft → Review → Approve → Publish → Train → Audit → Update → Retire**. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Network security policies are the **legal and operational foundation** of every network in production: - **Enterprise LAN/WAN** — Cisco ACI, SD-WAN, SASE policies (Cisco Viptela, Fortinet). - **Service Provider Networks** — BGP routing policies (RPKI, MANRS). - **Cloud Networks** — AWS Security Groups, Azure NSG, GCP VPC firewall rules. - **OT/ICS Networks** — Purdue Model segmentation policy (Levels 0–5). - **5G/ISP Networks** — 3GPP TS 33.501 security architecture policies. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Policy Construction, Enforcement & Risk Computation ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Construction of a Network Security Policy The KTU board frequently awards 7 marks for a structured, *stepwise* construction. Use the following 7-stage scaffold in your answer. **Stage 1 — Define Scope and Applicability** Identify which networks, systems, users, locations, and assets the policy covers. - *Example:* "This policy applies to all corporate-owned devices connected to the *acme.local* domain, all VPN users, and all third-party contractors with remote access." **Stage 2 — Conduct a Risk Assessment** Use the classic formula (the only formula KTU Module-1 expects you to derive): $$ \text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact} $$ This is qualitative, not arithmetic; each term is rated **Low / Medium / High**. **Stage 3 — Define Roles and Responsibilities (RACI)** - **Responsible** — Engineers who implement controls. - **Accountable** — CISO or IT Director. - **Consulted** — Legal, HR, Compliance. - **Informed** — All employees. **Stage 4 — Write the Policy Statement (Top-Level Rule)** Keep it short, technology-agnostic, and absolute. Example: > *"All data classified as Confidential must be encrypted in transit using approved cryptographic protocols."* **Stage 5 — Define Supporting Standards and Baselines** Quantify the policy. Example: *"TLS 1.2 or higher, AES-128-GCM or stronger, certificate validity ≤ 397 days."* **Stage 6 — Define Procedures and Exceptions** - *Normal procedure:* Step-by-step configuration guide. - *Exception process:* How a user/team can request a temporary exemption (form, approver, expiry). **Stage 7 — Define Enforcement, Monitoring, and Sanctions** - **Enforcement** — Technical (firewall logs, SIEM alerts) + Administrative (HR action). - **Monitoring** — Continuous (SIEM, IDS/IPS) + Periodic (quarterly audit). - **Sanctions** — Verbal warning → written warning → revocation of access → termination → legal action. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Quantifying Policy Compliance **Problem:** An organization has **3 layers of network controls** — Firewall (effective **92%** of threats blocked), IPS (effective **85%**), and Endpoint EDR (effective **78%**). An attacker must defeat ALL three to succeed. Calculate the **overall probability of compromise** assuming independent layers. **Step 1 — State the Defense-in-Depth Model** For *n* independent security layers in series (an attacker must bypass *all*): $$ P_{\text{breach}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - E_i) $$ where $E_i$ is the effectiveness of layer $i$ expressed as a decimal probability. **Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values** $$ P_{\text{breach}} = (1 - 0.92) \times (1 - 0.85) \times (1 - 0.78) $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the Bypass Probabilities** $$ P_{\text{breach}} = 0.08 \times 0.15 \times 0.22 $$ **Step 4 — Multiply Sequentially** $$ 0.08 \times 0.15 = 0.012 $$ $$ 0.012 \times 0.22 = 0.00264 $$ **Step 5 — Convert to Percentage and Interpret** $$ P_{\text{breach}} \approx 0.264\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation for KTU answer:** Layered controls reduce the breach probability from **8%** (single firewall failure) to **0.264%** — a **~30× improvement**, mathematically justifying the **Defense-in-Depth** principle that underpins every network security policy. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Policy Violations and Detection Methods | Violation Type | Policy Reference | Detection Method | KTU-Relevant Tool | |---|---|---|---| | Unauthorized Wi-Fi AP | Wireless Access Policy | Wireless IDS (WIDS) | Cisco Adaptive WIPS, Aruba RFProtect | | Rogue VPN tunnel (split-tunnel leak) | Remote Access Policy | NGFW, CASB, DNS monitoring | Zscaler, Palo Alto Prisma Access | | Unencrypted sensitive data in email | Data-in-Transit Policy | DLP, email gateway | Microsoft Purview DLP, Symantec DLP | | Default credentials on devices | Password/Account Policy | Vulnerability scanner | Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS | | Unpatched CVE on perimeter | Patch Mgmt Policy | Vuln scan + CVE feed | Tenable.sc, Rapid7 InsightVM | | Outbound C2 traffic | Egress Filtering Policy | Threat intel + IDS/IPS | MISP, Snort, Suricata | | Lateral movement (SMB/RDP east-west) | Segmentation Policy | Internal IDS, microsegmentation | Cisco Tetration, Illumio | | Shadow IT SaaS use | Cloud Acceptable Use | CASB, SWG | Netskope, McAfee MVISION Cloud | | Physical port misuse (LAN taps) | Physical Access Policy | NAC 802.1X | Cisco ISE, Aruba ClearPass | | Configuration drift on firewalls | Change Mgmt Policy | Config audit, CSP | FireMon, Tufin, AlgoSec | ## 3.4 Compliance Computation — KTU Numerical Practice **Problem:** An audit examines **120 network devices**. **18 devices** are non-compliant with the password complexity standard. Calculate: (a) The compliance percentage. (b) The risk score, given a policy weight of **0.7** and a criticality multiplier of **1.5**. **Step 1 — Compute Non-Compliance Ratio** $$ \text{Non-Compliance Ratio} = \frac{18}{120} = 0.15 $$ **Step 2 — Compute Compliance Percentage** $$ \text{Compliance \%} = (1 - 0.15) \times 100 = 85\% $$ **Step 3 — Apply Weighted Risk Score Formula** $$ \text{Risk Score} = \text{Non-Compliance Ratio} \times \text{Policy Weight} \times \text{Criticality Multiplier} $$ $$ \text{Risk Score} = 0.15 \times 0.7 \times 1.5 = 0.1575 $$ **Step 4 — Convert to a 0–10 Scale for Audit Reporting** $$ \text{Risk Score}_{10} = 0.1575 \times 10 = 1.575 \approx 1.58 $$ > [!TIP] > KTU typically awards full marks only if you state the *units* and the *interpretation* alongside the calculation. ## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Policy vs. Mechanism vs. Procedure | Aspect | Policy | Mechanism (Control) | Procedure | |---|---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Defines *what* and *why* | Enforces *how* | Step-by-step *how-to* | | **Audience** | All stakeholders | Engineers/automated systems | Operators/technicians | | **Lifetime** | Long (1–3 years) | Medium (technology refresh) | Short (updated as needed) | | **Example (NSP)** | "All remote access must use MFA." | TOTP-based MFA via RADIUS + 802.1X | "Configure Cisco ISE: Authentication → MFA → Duo" | | **Compliance Test** | Exists & approved | Control is functional | Operator can execute it | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Three-Pillar Policy Architecture (RFC 2196 Model) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[ORGANIZATIONAL
SECURITY POLICY
Master Document] nodeB[1. COMPUTER
SECURITY POLICIES] nodeC[2. NETWORK
ACCESS POLICIES] nodeD[3. INTERNET
ACCESS POLICIES] nodeE[OS Hardening Policy] nodeF[Password Policy] nodeG[Patch Management] nodeH[Malware Protection] nodeI[Backup Policy] nodeJ[VPN / Remote Access] nodeK[Wireless Access] nodeL[Guest Network] nodeM[Network Segmentation] nodeN[Acceptable Use Policy AUP] nodeO[Web Filtering] nodeP[Egress Filtering] nodeQ[Email Security] nodeR[Data Loss Prevention] nodeS[Cloud SaaS Policy] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeG nodeB --> nodeH nodeB --> nodeI nodeC --> nodeJ nodeC --> nodeK nodeC --> nodeL nodeC --> nodeM nodeC --> nodeN nodeD --> nodeO nodeD --> nodeP nodeD --> nodeQ nodeD --> nodeR nodeD --> nodeS ``` ## 4.2 NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0 — Policy Mapping (GV Function Highlighted) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeG[GOVERN GV
Policy and Risk] nodeI[IDENTIFY ID
Asset and Risk] nodeP[PROTECT PR
Access and Infra] nodeD[DETECT DE
Anomalies and Events] nodeR[RESPOND RS
Incident Response] nodeRc[RECOVER RC
Restore Capabilities] nodeG --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeP nodeI --> nodeP nodeP --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeR nodeR --> nodeRc nodeRc --> nodeG ``` ## 4.3 Policy Development Lifecycle (PDCA Cycle) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeP1[PLAN
Risk Assessment
Scope Definition
Draft Policy] nodeD1[DO
Communicate
Train
Implement Controls] nodeC1[CHECK
Audit
Monitor
Measure Compliance] nodeA1[ACT
Update Policy
Corrective Action
Version Increment] nodeP1 --> nodeD1 nodeD1 --> nodeC1 nodeC1 --> nodeA1 nodeA1 --> nodeP1 ``` ## 4.4 Network Security Policy Enforcement Architecture (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeU[User / Endpoint] nodeNAC[NAC
802.1X Authentication
Posture Assessment] nodeFW[Perimeter Firewall
ACL + Stateful Inspection] nodeIPS[IPS / IDS
Signature and Anomaly] nodeSeg[Internal Segmentation
VLANs VRFs Microseg] nodeSIEM[SIEM
Centralized Logging
Correlation and Alerting] nodeAUD[Auditor
Quarterly Review] nodePOL[Central Policy Server
Cisco ISE / Aruba ClearPass
Single Source of Truth] nodeU --> nodeNAC nodeNAC --> nodeFW nodeFW --> nodeIPS nodeIPS --> nodeSeg nodeSeg --> nodeSIEM nodeSIEM --> nodeAUD nodePOL -. governs .-> nodeNAC nodePOL -. governs .-> nodeFW nodePOL -. governs .-> nodeSeg ``` ## 4.5 Policy Hierarchy — Document Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeTop[POLICY
What and Why
Approved by CISO] nodeStd[STANDARDS
Mandatory Uniform Rules] nodeBase[BASELINES
Minimum Configurations] nodeProc[PROCEDURES
Step by Step How To] nodeGuide[GUIDELINES
Non Mandatory Recommendations] nodeTop --> nodeStd nodeStd --> nodeBase nodeBase --> nodeProc nodeProc --> nodeGuide ``` ## 4.6 Security Policy Decision Flow — Request to Authorization ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeReq[User Access Request] nodeAuth[Authentication Check
Who Are You] nodeAuthz[Authorization Check
Are You Allowed] nodeAud[Audit Log Entry] nodeAcc[Access Granted] nodeDen[Access Denied] nodeMFA[Step Up MFA Required] nodeReq --> nodeAuth nodeAuth --> nodeAuthz nodeAuthz --> nodeAcc nodeAuthz --> nodeMFA nodeAuthz --> nodeDen nodeAcc --> nodeAud nodeMFA --> nodeAcc nodeMFA --> nodeDen nodeDen --> nodeAud ``` ## 4.7 Network Zone Segmentation Policy Map ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeInternet[INTERNET] nodeDMZ[DMZ ZONE
Web Mail Proxy] nodeIT[CORPORATE IT ZONE
Users Email ERP] nodeOT[OT ICS ZONE
SCADA PLC Historian] nodePCI[PCI DSS ZONE
Cardholder Data] nodeMGMT[MGMT ZONE
Out of Band] nodeInternet --> nodeDMZ nodeDMZ --> nodeIT nodeIT -. tightly controlled .-> nodePCI nodeIT -. one way diode .-> nodeOT nodeMGMT -. monitors .-> nodeIT nodeMGMT -. monitors .-> nodeOT nodeMGMT -. monitors .-> nodePCI ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q1.** *Differentiate between a Security Policy, a Standard, and a Procedure. Give one example of each in the context of a Network Security Policy.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Policy** is a high-level, formal statement of intent that defines *what* must be protected and *why* — e.g., *"All remote access must use multi-factor authentication."* A **Standard** is a mandatory, technology-specific rule that supports the policy — e.g., *"TOTP-based MFA using RFC 6238, 30-second time step."* A **Procedure** is a step-by-step implementation guide — e.g., *"Step 1: Open Cisco ISE → Authentication → Multi-Factor. Step 2: Configure Duo RADIUS proxy. Step 3: Test with valid credentials."* The hierarchy is **Policy → Standard → Procedure**; each progressively narrows the abstraction gap. *(3 marks — 1 for policy, 1 for standard, 1 for procedure with examples.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Q2.** *List and briefly explain the three pillars of a Network Security Policy as defined in RFC 2196.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** RFC 2196 structures every site security policy into three coordinated pillars: 1. **Computer Security Policies** — Govern the hosts, servers, and endpoints (OS hardening, patch management, malware protection). 2. **Network Access Policies** — Govern how users and devices enter and traverse the network (AUP, VPN, wireless, segmentation). 3. **Internet Access Policies** — Govern outbound traffic to the public Internet (egress filtering, web filtering, DLP, email security). These three pillars collectively address the **endpoints, the channels, and the destinations** of network communication. *(3 marks — 1 for naming, 1 for explanation, 1 for the holistic significance.)* ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module-1 Choice Question]** ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** *Explain the key characteristics of an effective Network Security Policy. Why is the "living document" nature of a policy important?* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Describe the NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0 functions. Map the GOVERN (GV) function to a typical Network Security Policy structure.* **(7 marks)** ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) **(a)** *With a neat diagram, describe the PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) lifecycle of a Network Security Policy as per ISO 27001.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Explain the principles of Least Privilege, Defense in Depth, and Fail-Safe Defaults. Show mathematically how Defense in Depth reduces breach probability, given firewall effectiveness = 0.90, IPS = 0.80, EDR = 0.75.* **(7 marks)** --- ### Complete Model Solutions #### Solution to Question A (a) — 7 marks **Effective Characteristics of a Network Security Policy:** 1. **Realistic & Implementable** — Written for the actual environment, not for an ideal one. *(1 mark)* 2. **Concise & Clear** — Avoids legalese; understandable by non-technical staff. *(1 mark)* 3. **Enforceable** — Every rule can be technically or procedurally enforced. *(1 mark)* 4. **Management-Supported** — Signed at executive level with budget allocation. *(1 mark)* 5. **Auditable** — Compliance is measurable via metrics and KPIs. *(1 mark)* **Importance of "Living Document" Nature:** A network security policy must evolve with the threat landscape, regulatory changes, and technology refresh. Static policies become obsolete within 12–18 months due to new attack vectors (e.g., zero-days, AI-driven attacks, quantum threats). A living document is **reviewed annually** (or post-incident) and version-controlled. *(2 marks)* #### Solution to Question A (b) — 7 marks **NIST CSF 2.0 Functions:** - **Govern (GV)** — Establish and monitor the organization's risk management strategy, policies, procedures. *(1 mark)* - **Identify (ID)** — Asset inventory, business environment, risk assessment. *(1 mark)* - **Protect (PR)** — Identity management, access control, awareness training, data security, protective technology. *(1 mark)* - **Detect (DE)** — Anomalies and events, continuous monitoring, detection processes. *(1 mark)* - **Respond (RS)** — Incident response planning, communication, mitigation. *(1 mark)* - **Recover (RC)** — Recovery planning, improvements, communication. *(1 mark)* **Mapping GOVERN → NSP:** The **GV.PO (Policy)** category directly corresponds to the existence, communication, and enforcement of the master security policy. **GV.OC (Organizational Context)** defines scope; **GV.RM (Risk Management)** sets the strategy; **GV.SC (Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk)** extends the policy to third parties. The technical controls (firewall, VPN, segmentation) belong to **PR.AC** and **PR.IR**, but their *governance* — i.e., the policy that mandates them — is rooted in **GV**. *(1 mark)* --- #### Solution to Question B (a) — 7 marks **PDCA Lifecycle of a Network Security Policy (aligned with ISO 27001):** *(Refer to SECTION 4.3 for the diagram — 2 marks)* 1. **PLAN** — Define the scope, conduct a risk assessment using `Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact`, draft the policy, and obtain stakeholder review. *(1.5 marks)* 2. **DO** — Publish the policy, conduct user awareness training, deploy the technical controls (firewall, NAC, DLP), and document procedures. *(1.5 marks)* 3. **CHECK** — Perform continuous monitoring via SIEM, conduct internal and external audits, measure compliance against KPIs (e.g., % encrypted traffic, % patched systems). *(1 mark)* 4. **ACT** — Apply corrective actions, capture lessons learned, update the policy version, communicate changes, and re-enter the PLAN phase. *(1 mark)* The PDCA cycle ensures **continuous improvement**, a mandatory requirement of ISO 27001 ISMS. *(1 mark for the conclusion.)* --- #### Solution to Question B (b) — 7 marks **Principle 1 — Least Privilege (1 mark):** Grant users only the minimum permissions necessary to perform their job. Implementation: RBAC, network segmentation, just-in-time access. *Example:* A junior accountant has read-only access to general ledger, not write. **Principle 2 — Defense in Depth (1 mark):** Layer multiple independent controls. Failure of one control does not lead to compromise. *Example:* Firewall + IPS + EDR. **Principle 3 — Fail-Safe Defaults (1 mark):** Default-deny posture. If unsure, deny. Firewall ACL ordering: explicit deny first, then permits. If the ACL is corrupted, traffic stops rather than flows freely. **Mathematical Proof of Defense in Depth (4 marks):** Given: Firewall effectiveness $E_1 = 0.90$, IPS effectiveness $E_2 = 0.80$, EDR effectiveness $E_3 = 0.75$. For independent layers in series, the probability that an attacker bypasses **all** layers is: $$ P_{\text{breach}} = (1 - E_1) \times (1 - E_2) \times (1 - E_3) $$ Substituting: $$ P_{\text{breach}} = (1 - 0.90) \times (1 - 0.80) \times (1 - 0.75) $$ $$ P_{\text{breach}} = 0.10 \times 0.20 \times 0.25 $$ $$ P_{\text{breach}} = 0.005 = 0.5\% $$ **Comparison to single-layer failure:** A single firewall with 10% bypass = **10%** breach probability. Three layered controls reduce this to **0.5%**, a **20× reduction**. This mathematical result is the engineering justification for Defense in Depth as a policy principle. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > 1. Do **NOT** confuse *bypass probability* $(1 - E)$ with *effectiveness* $(E)$. Writing $P = 0.90 \times 0.80 \times 0.75 = 0.54$ is a **common, fatal error** that loses 2–3 marks. > 2. Do **NOT** treat layers as independent if they share a single failure mode (e.g., both firewall and IPS on the same appliance). Independence is an *assumption* — state it explicitly. > 3.
