For Part A answers, **write 3 distinct points** — examiners strictly award **1 mark per valid point**, not for one long paragraph. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Mile Revision — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam.** - ✅ A **Network Security Policy** is a formalized, living, enforceable document covering what, why, who, and how. - ✅ **RFC 2196** defines the three-pillar model: **Computer / Network Access / Internet Access** policies. - ✅ The **documentation hierarchy** is: **Policy → Standard → Baseline → Procedure → Guideline**. - ✅ **NIST CSF 2.0** has **6 functions**: **G**overn, **I**dentify, **P**rotect, **D**etect, **R**espond, **R**ecover (mnemonic: **"Govern I Protect Detect Respond Recover"**). - ✅ **ISO 27001:2022** uses **PDCA** for ISMS — the policy lifecycle aligns to this cycle. - ✅ Key **design principles** (must-memorize): Least Privilege, Defense in Depth, SoD, Fail-Safe Defaults, Complete Mediation, Need-to-Know. - ✅ The **Defense in Depth formula**: $P_{\text{breach}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - E_i)$ — assume *independent* layers. - ✅ **AUP (Acceptable Use Policy)** is the most fundamental user-facing network policy. - ✅ **Egress filtering** blocks outbound attacks and C2 traffic — the often-forgotten counterpart of ingress firewalling. - ✅ **PCI-DSS v4.0**, **HIPAA §164.308**, **GDPR Art. 32**, and **India IT Act §43A/72A** are the major regulatory drivers. - ✅ The **policy is technology-agnostic**; the **standard is technology-specific**. - ✅ **Annual review** is the minimum cadence; **post-incident review** is mandatory. - ✅ **CIS Controls v8 IG1** is a great starting checklist for any new NSP. - ✅ Always include an **exception handling process** in every policy — a policy with no exception process is not implementable in the real world. - ✅ **NAC (802.1X)** + **SIEM** + **Central Policy Server** form the modern enforcement triad for NSPs. - ✅ **Compliance % = (1 − Non-Compliance Ratio) × 100** — the base metric for any audit answer. - ✅ Always end a KTU answer on the **real-world impact** statement — examiners reward contextualization.

topics.title: Network Segments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Segments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Network Segmentation** is the deliberate architectural process of dividing a larger computer network into smaller, isolated sub-networks (called **segments**) — each functioning as a distinct logical or physical broadcast domain. In the context of **Network and System Security**, segmentation is classified as a foundational **defense-in-depth** control, where policy enforcement points (firewalls, ACLs, routers, SDN controllers) are inserted between segments to control traffic flow, limit lateral movement of attackers, and enforce the **principle of least privilege** at the network layer.

A **Network Segment** itself is defined as a contiguous range of IP addresses (a subnet) or a Layer-2 broadcast domain (a VLAN / physical switch fabric) that shares a common security policy boundary.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Building"

Imagine a large corporate office building with hundreds of employees. Instead of letting everyone roam freely into every room (server room, HR records, R&D lab, reception), the building is divided into **floors and locked doors**:
- The **Server Room** (DMZ segment) — only IT staff have keycards.
- The **Finance Department** (restricted VLAN) — only finance employees.
- The **Reception Lobby** (guest Wi-Fi segment) — visitors, fully isolated from internal systems.
- The **Building Walls + Security Desk** (firewall / router ACLs) — inspect everyone moving between floors.

If a visitor (malware / attacker) sneaks into the lobby, they cannot simply walk into the server room — the **security desk checks them** (firewall policy) and the **doors are locked** (segment isolation). This is exactly what network segmentation does inside a digital infrastructure.

### 1.3 Types of Network Segments (Standard KTU Taxonomy)

| Segment Type | Full Form | Scope / Reach | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PAN** | Personal Area Network | ~10 m, body-centric | Bluetooth, wearable devices |
| **LAN** | Local Area Network | Building / campus | Office Ethernet / Wi-Fi |
| **VLAN** | Virtual LAN | Logical, within switches | Departmental isolation without rewiring |
| **MAN** | Metropolitan Area Network | City-wide | ISP metro rings, municipal networks |
| **WAN** | Wide Area Network | Global | Internet, MPLS links between branches |
| **CAN** | Campus Area Network | Multiple buildings, same campus | University / corporate campus backbone |
| **DMZ** | Demilitarized Zone | Buffer between LAN and WAN | Public-facing web/DNS/mail servers |
| **Intranet** | Internal-only network | Organization-wide | Private web apps, internal portals |
| **Extranet** | Extended trusted network | Partners / vendors | B2B portals, supplier integration |

### 1.4 Security-Centric Segmentation Zones

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In any secure enterprise architecture, traffic is classified into **trust zones**. KTU examiners frequently test this classification — memorize it.

- **Trusted Zone (Internal LAN):** Employees, authenticated workstations, internal servers.
- **Semi-Trusted Zone (DMZ):** Public-facing servers — web, mail, DNS, VPN gateways.
- **Untrusted Zone (WAN / Internet):** Unknown external entities, public networks.
- **Management Plane:** Out-of-band network used *only* for administrative access to switches, routers, and firewalls (never carries user data).
- **Guest / IoT Zone:** Isolated low-trust segments for visitors' devices and IoT hardware.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Segment a Network? — The "Five Pillars" Reasoning

1. **Performance / Broadcast Containment** — Every broadcast (ARP, DHCP discover) is confined to its own segment; the rest of the network is spared. Large flat networks suffer **broadcast storms** that cripple throughput.
2. **Security / Blast Radius Reduction** — If one host in *Segment A* is compromised, the attacker cannot freely probe *Segment B*. Lateral movement requires crossing a policy enforcement point.
3. **Compliance & Regulatory Isolation** — PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and GDPR mandate that cardholder data, PHI, and PII reside on **isolated, auditable segments**.
4. **Fault Isolation** — A loop, misconfiguration, or hardware failure in one segment does not propagate to others.
5. **Policy Granularity** — Different departments (HR, R&D, Finance) can have distinct QoS, firewall rules, and access controls tailored to their needs.

### 2.2 Methods of Implementation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module-1 expects students to differentiate between **Physical Segmentation** and **Logical Segmentation**.

**A. Physical Segmentation**
- Done by deploying **separate routers, switches, and cabling** for each sub-network.
- Highest security assurance (no shared hardware to bridge segments).
- Costly, inflexible.

**B. Logical Segmentation**
Achieved via software/protocol features:
- **Subnetting (Layer 3):** Dividing an IP address space using subnet masks.
- **VLANs (Layer 2):** IEEE 802.1Q tagging on switches; broadcasts stay inside the VLAN.
- **VRF (Virtual Routing and Forwarding):** Multiple routing tables on a single router.
- **Firewalls / ACLs:** Statefull inspection + rules between segments.
- **Micro-segmentation:** Host-level (hypervisor / workload) policy, often via SDN overlays (VXLAN, NSX, Cisco ACI).
- **Software-Defined Networking (SDN):** Centralized controller (e.g., OpenDaylight, ONOS) programs flow rules dynamically.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Subnetting, Masking & Segmentation Math

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Rule:** Never use the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside markdown table cells. Use `\vert` for "such that" or "divides".

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of subnets from a borrowed-bits classful network | $N_s = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = number of **borrowed** host bits |
| 2 | Usable hosts per subnet | $H_u = 2^{h} - 2$ | $h$ = remaining host bits; $-2$ subtracts network & broadcast addresses |
| 3 | Total host addresses (raw) per subnet | $H_t = 2^{h}$ | Includes network and broadcast |
| 4 | CIDR block aggregation rule | $N_{agg} = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = common prefix length difference between longest and shortest mask |
| 5 | Wildcard mask (inverse of subnet mask) | $W = 255.255.255.255 - M$ | Used in Cisco ACLs |
| 6 | Block size (increment between subnets) | $B = 256 - \text{last\_octet\_of\_mask}$ | Quick mental-math trick for IPv4 |
| 7 | Number of subnets required (VLSM) | $\lceil \log_2(\text{hosts\_needed} + 2) \rceil$ | Round up to next power of 2 |
| 8 | Usable host range | $[\text{Network} + 1,\ \text{Broadcast} - 1]$ | Excludes boundary addresses |
| 9 | Total addresses in a /n prefix | $A = 2^{32 - n}$ | IPv4 Internet total |
| 10 | Maximum VLSM hosts assignable to a /n block | $H_u = 2^{32 - n} - 2$ | Boundary deduction |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Centers:** East-West traffic between servers is micro-segmented; the entire data center may contain **tens of thousands of VXLAN segments**.
- **Banking:** ATM switches, Core Banking servers, and customer Wi-Fi are physically and logically separated — auditors check this annually.
- **Healthcare:** MRI machines, infusion pumps, and EMR systems run on isolated VLANs to prevent ransomware from jumping from a doctor's laptop to a life-critical device.
- **Cloud (AWS / Azure):** Each **VPC, Subnet, Security Group, and NACL** is a form of network segmentation implemented as a service.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section executes an **exhaustive worked derivation** of an IPv4 subnetting problem typical of KTU Module-1 numerical questions. No step is skipped.

### 3.1 Problem Statement

> An organization is assigned the IPv4 block **192.168.40.0 /24**. The network administrator must divide it into **four (4) equal subnets** to serve the Engineering, Finance, HR, and Guest departments. For each subnet, determine:
> (i) the new subnet mask,
> (ii) the number of usable host addresses,
> (iii) the network address, first usable host, last usable host, and broadcast address.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the original prefix and host bits.**

The given prefix is **/24**, meaning the first 24 bits are the network ID and the remaining 8 bits are host bits.

$$
\text{Host bits available} = 32 - 24 = 8 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine how many bits to borrow to create at least 4 subnets.**

We require $N_s \geq 4$ subnets. From Formula #1, $N_s = 2^{n}$, so:

$$
2^{n} \geq 4 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = 2 \text{ bits}
$$

Borrowing 2 bits from the host portion yields exactly $2^{2} = 4$ subnets.

**Step 3 — Compute the new prefix length and subnet mask.**

$$
\text{New prefix} = 24 + n = 24 + 2 = \text{/26}
$$

The new subnet mask, written in dotted decimal, places the additional 2 bits in the **first 2 positions** of the 4th octet:

$$
\text{Mask} = 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000_2
$$

Converting the 4th octet $11000000_2$ to decimal:

$$
128 + 64 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 192
$$

Therefore:

$$
M = 255.255.255.192
$$

**Step 4 — Compute usable hosts per subnet (Formula #2).**

After borrowing 2 bits, the remaining host bits are:

$$
h = 8 - n = 8 - 2 = 6 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
H_u = 2^{h} - 2 = 2^{6} - 2 = 64 - 2 = 62 \text{ usable hosts per subnet}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine the block size using the quick mental-math trick (Formula #6).**

$$
B = 256 - 192 = 64
$$

So the subnets increment by **64** in the 4th octet.

**Step 6 — Enumerate the four subnets, including all boundary addresses.**

| Subnet # | Network Address | First Usable Host | Last Usable Host | Broadcast Address |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Engineering) | 192.168.40.0 | 192.168.40.1 | 192.168.40.62 | 192.168.40.63 |
| 2 (Finance) | 192.168.40.64 | 192.168.40.65 | 192.168.40.126 | 192.168.40.127 |
| 3 (HR) | 192.168.40.128 | 192.168.40.129 | 192.168.40.190 | 192.168.40.191 |
| 4 (Guest) | 192.168.40.192 | 192.168.40.193 | 192.168.40.254 | 192.168.40.255 |

> Derivation check for Subnet 2's broadcast address:
>
> $$
> \text{Network} + B - 1 = 64 + 64 - 1 = 127
> $$
>
> Derivation check for Subnet 4's last usable host:
>
> $$
> \text{Broadcast} - 1 = 255 - 1 = 254
> $$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Segmentation Method to Use?

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often frame a 7-mark question as: *"Compare physical vs logical segmentation"* or *"When would you choose micro-segmentation over VLANs?"*

| Criterion | Physical Segmentation | VLAN / Logical (L2) | Micro-segmentation (SDN) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Security strength | **Highest** | Medium | Very High (per-workload) |
| Cost | High (duplicate hardware) | Low | Medium (controller license) |
| Scalability | Poor | Good (4094 VLANs) | Excellent (millions of segments) |
| Reconfiguration effort | Physical rewiring | Software-only (trunk change) | Software-only (policy push) |
| Fault isolation | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Suitable for | Defense, nuclear, SCADA | Enterprise campus | Cloud data centers |
| Latency overhead | None | Negligible | Small (encapsulation, e.g., VXLAN ~50 B) |
| Example tech | Separate switches, air-gap | IEEE 802.1Q | VMware NSX, Cisco ACI, Calico |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Enterprise Network Segmentation Architecture (Block-Level Mermaid)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates a **typical three-tier enterprise security architecture** with clearly demarcated segments and the firewall policy-enforcement points between them. All node identifiers are alphanumeric and free of markdown formatting inside their quoted labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeU["UNTRUSTED ZONE INTERNET AND EXTRANET"] --> nodeFW1["PERIMETER FIREWALL POLICY ENFORCEMENT"]
    nodeFW1 --> nodeDMZ["DMZ SEGMENT PUBLIC WEB DNS MAIL VPN GATEWAY"]
    nodeDMZ --> nodeFW2["INTERNAL FIREWALL POLICY ENFORCEMENT"]
    nodeFW2 --> nodeVLAN1["VLAN 10 ENGINEERING TRUSTED"]
    nodeFW2 --> nodeVLAN2["VLAN 20 FINANCE RESTRICTED"]
    nodeFW2 --> nodeVLAN3["VLAN 30 HR TRUSTED"]
    nodeFW2 --> nodeVLAN4["VLAN 40 GUEST ISOLATED LOW TRUST"]
    nodeFW2 --> nodeVLAN5["VLAN 50 IOT SEGMENT"]
    nodeMGMT["OUT OF BAND MANAGEMENT NETWORK"] -.-> nodeFW1
    nodeMGMT -.-> nodeFW2
    nodeVLAN1 --- nodeBACK["SERVER FARM SEGMENT DB APP INTERNAL"]
    nodeVLAN2 --- nodeBACK
    nodeVLAN3 --- nodeBACK
    nodeFW2 --- nodeBACK
    classDef trusted fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef dmz fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef untrusted fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef mgmt fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    class nodeU untrusted
    class nodeFW1,nodeFW2 untrusted
    class nodeDMZ dmz
    class nodeVLAN1,nodeVLAN2,nodeVLAN3,nodeBACK trusted
    class nodeVLAN4 untrusted
    class nodeVLAN5 dmz
    class nodeMGMT mgmt
```

### 4.2 Segmentation Method Taxonomy Diagram

```mermaid

    nodeROOT["NETWORK SEGMENTATION METHODS"] --> nodeA["PHYSICAL"]
    nodeROOT --> nodeB["LOGICAL"]
    nodeA --> nodeA1["Separate switches"]
    nodeA --> nodeA2["Separate routers"]
    nodeA --> nodeA3["Air gapped networks"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Subnetting L3"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["VLAN 802.1Q L2"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["VRF L3"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["Firewall ACL"]

    nodeB5 --> nodeB5A["VXLAN overlay"]

```

### 4.3 Zero-Trust Micro-Segmentation Data Flow

```mermaid

    nodeUSER["USER DEVICE IDENTITY VERIFIED"] --> nodePOL["SDN POLICY ENGINE CHECKS USER DEVICE LOCATION TIME"]
    nodePOL -->|"PERMIT"| nodeWORKLOAD["TARGET WORKLOAD EAST WEST MICRO SEGMENT"]
    nodePOL -->|"DENY"| nodeBLOCK["BLOCKED AND LOGGED"]
    nodeWORKLOAD --> nodeTELE["TELEMETRY FED BACK TO POLICY ENGINE"]
    nodeTELE --> nodePOL
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define network segmentation. List any four benefits of segmenting a network.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Network segmentation is the practice of dividing a computer network into smaller, isolated sub-networks (segments), each treated as a separate security and broadcast domain, by using routers, switches, firewalls, or software-defined controls.

> **Benefits (½ mark each, any four):**
> 1. **Reduces broadcast domain size** — improves performance.
> 2. **Limits lateral movement** of attackers after a breach.
> 3. **Enforces least-privilege access** between departments.
> 4. **Simplifies compliance** with PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR.
> 5. **Improves fault isolation** — a failure in one segment does not affect others.
> 6. **Allows granular QoS and policy enforcement.**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*Differentiate between a DMZ segment and an internal LAN segment in terms of trust level and typical workload.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | DMZ Segment | Internal LAN Segment |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust Level** | **Semi-trusted** — exposed to the Internet via the perimeter firewall | **Fully trusted** — not directly reachable from the Internet |
| **Typical Workload** | Public-facing servers: web servers, DNS, mail relays, VPN gateways | Employee workstations, file servers, internal databases, intranet apps |
| **Access Control** | Strict ingress from Internet + filtered egress to LAN | Restricted primarily to authenticated internal users |
| **Failure Impact** | Compromise does not (ideally) allow lateral movement into internal LAN | Highest protection priority |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Subnetting + Segmentation Design*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**
>
> An organization is assigned the IPv4 address block **172.16.0.0 /16**. The network administrator must design a segmented network with the following requirements:
>
> **(a)** Create **at least 8 subnets** of **equal size**. For each subnet, calculate:
> - the new subnet mask,
> - the number of usable hosts,
> - the network and broadcast addresses of the **first two subnets**.
> - *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain with a diagram how these 8 subnets can be deployed in a **3-zone security architecture** (DMZ, Internal, Guest) using routers and a stateful firewall. Justify why flat (non-segmented) networks are insecure. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Subnetting Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Original prefix is **/16**, hence host bits available:

$$
h_{\text{original}} = 32 - 16 = 16 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Number of subnets needed $\geq 8$:

$$
2^{n} \geq 8 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = 3 \text{ bits borrowed}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** New prefix:

$$
16 + 3 = \text{/19}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Subnet mask — $11111111.11111111.11100000.00000000_2$, i.e.:

$$
M = 255.255.224.0
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Usable hosts per subnet:

$$
h = 16 - 3 = 13 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad H_u = 2^{13} - 2 = 8192 - 2 = 8190 \text{ hosts}
$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Block size in the 3rd octet:

$$
B = 256 - 224 = 32
$$

Enumerating the first two subnets:

| Subnet | Network Address | First Host | Last Host | Broadcast |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 172.16.0.0 | 172.16.0.1 | 172.16.31.254 | 172.16.31.255 |
| 2 | 172.16.32.0 | 172.16.32.1 | 172.16.63.254 | 172.16.63.255 |

---

**Part (b) — 3-Zone Architecture with Diagram (7 Marks)**

**Architecture description (4 marks):**

- **Zone 1 — Guest Segment (lowest trust):** Assign Subnet 1 (`172.16.0.0/19`). Connected to a dedicated firewall interface (`Gi0/1`). Only HTTP/HTTPS outbound allowed; no inbound from any other zone.
- **Zone 2 — DMZ (semi-trusted):** Assign Subnet 2 (`172.16.32.0/19`). Hosts public web, DNS, mail. Connected to firewall interface `Gi0/2`. Allows inbound HTTP/HTTPS/DNS from the Internet and limited outbound to Zone 3 (only to update from internal app server).
- **Zone 3 — Internal LAN (highest trust):** Remaining 6 subnets (`172.16.64.0/19` … `172.16.224.0/19`). Hosts employee workstations, file servers, DB. Connected to firewall interface `Gi0/3`.

**ASCII Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   [INTERNET]
       |
   [Perimeter Router]
       |
   [Firewall]
   |     |     |
 Zone1  Zone2  Zone3
 Guest  DMZ    Internal LAN
 /19    /19    /19 x 6
```

**Justification — Why flat networks are insecure (1 mark):**

> In a flat network, any compromised host can directly ARP-scan and attack every other host because there is **no policy enforcement boundary**. Segmentation inserts firewalls/ACLs between zones so that an attacker in the Guest segment literally cannot route a packet to a database in the Internal LAN without explicit policy approval — achieving the **principle of least privilege** at the network layer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice — Conceptual & Comparative*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**
>
> **(a)** Compare and contrast **Physical Segmentation**, **VLAN-based Segmentation**, and **Micro-segmentation** under the headings: security strength, cost, scalability, and ideal use-case. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** and describe how network segmentation supports it. List any **three** principles of ZTA. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 Marks — 1.5 marks per row + 1 mark for conclusion):**

| Attribute | Physical Segmentation | VLAN (Logical L2) | Micro-segmentation (SDN) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Security Strength** | **Highest** — separate hardware; near-air-gap possible | **Medium** — shared switch fabric; VLAN-hopping attacks possible | **Very High** — per-workload policy, encrypted overlays (VXLAN) |
| **Cost** | High — duplicate switches, routers, cabling | Low — software-only on existing switches | Medium — SDN controller + license |
| **Scalability** | Poor — physical rewiring required | Good — up to 4094 VLANs per switch | Excellent — millions of segments |
| **Ideal Use Case** | Military, SCADA, nuclear, air-gapped R&D labs | Enterprise campus, departmental isolation | Hyperscale data centers, multi-cloud, east-west traffic control |
| **Conclusion (1 mark):** Choice depends on threat model, budget, and scale. | | | |

**Part (b) — Zero Trust Architecture (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
> Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) is a security model — formalized in **NIST SP 800-207** — that assumes **no implicit trust** is granted to any user, device, or network segment based solely on physical or logical location. Every access request must be **authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated** before being granted, regardless of whether it originates from inside or outside the traditional network perimeter.

**Role of Network Segmentation in ZTA (3 marks):**
1. **Limits blast radius:** If credentials are stolen, segmentation ensures the attacker can only reach resources inside the compromised micro-segment, not the entire network.
2. **Enforces per-segment policy:** Each segment becomes a ZTA *enforcement point* where identity, device posture, and context are re-evaluated before granting access.
3. **Enables least-privilege routing:** Software-defined perimeters route users only to the specific applications they need (often via brokered connections), making the underlying network invisible.

**Three Principles of ZTA (2 marks — any three):**
1. **Verify explicitly** — authenticate and authorize every request based on identity, device, location, and workload.
2. **Least-privilege access** — grant just-in-time, just-enough permissions.
3. **Assume breach** — design as if the attacker is already inside; segment, log, and monitor accordingly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - In subnetting answers, students often **forget to subtract 2** for network and broadcast addresses — losing 1 mark.
> - Many write `255.255.255.192` but compute the block size as `128` instead of `64`. Always verify: $B = 256 - \text{last\_octet\_mask}$.
> - In comparison questions, students list similarities but **fail to justify which method is preferred in which scenario** — the "ideal use case" column is mandatory.
> - Drawing a diagram without labeling **trust zones or policy enforcement points** scores only partial marks. Always annotate firewalls/ACLs.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for KTU Module-1 — Network Segments:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Segmentation = dividing a network into isolated sub-networks for security, performance, and policy enforcement.
- ✅ **Three standard trust zones:** Untrusted (Internet), Semi-trusted (DMZ), Trusted (Internal LAN). Know the workloads in each.
- ✅ **Five pillars of segmentation:** Performance, Security, Compliance, Fault Isolation, Policy Granularity.
- ✅ **Methods hierarchy (cost vs security):** Physical → VLAN/Subnet → VRF → Firewall ACL → Micro-segmentation (SDN).
- ✅ **Key subnetting formulas:** $N_s = 2^{n}$, $H_u = 2^{h} - 2$, $B = 256 - M_{\text{octet}}$, prefix $+n =$ new CIDR.
- ✅ **Boundary addresses:** First = Network + 1, Last = Broadcast − 1.
- ✅ **Block size trick:** $B = 256 - \text{(last meaningful octet of mask)}$ — works for all classless IPv4.
- ✅ **DMZ placement:** Always between two firewalls (or dual-homed firewall); never inside the trusted LAN.
- ✅ **Zero Trust + Segmentation:** Segmentation is the *enforcement backbone* of ZTA — it is what makes "verify every request" operationally feasible.
- ✅ **Management network:** Always out-of-band; never share a VLAN with user traffic.
- ✅ **Micro-segmentation tech:** VXLAN (RFC 7348), NSX, Cisco ACI, Open vSwitch, Calico.
- ✅ **VLAN limit:** 4094 usable VLANs per switch (VLAN IDs 1–4094, with 1 and 1002–1005 reserved).
- ✅ **Lateral movement:** The primary threat that segmentation defeats; remember this phrase — examiners love it.
- ✅ **Compliance drivers:** PCI-DSS Req 1.2, HIPAA §164.312(e), ISO 27001 A.13.1 — quick citation may earn bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perimeter Defense
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Perimeter Defense

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perimeter Defense** is a security architecture strategy that establishes a well-defined boundary (the "security perimeter") between a trusted internal network and an untrusted external network (typically the Internet). It deploys a layered set of hardware and software controls — such as **firewalls, Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS), Demilitarized Zones (DMZ), bastion hosts, and proxy gateways** — at the network edge to filter, inspect, log, and control all traffic attempting to cross this boundary in either direction.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Perimeter Defense is treated as the **first line of defense** in the broader framework of *Defense-in-Depth*. The goal is to **reduce the attack surface** so that malicious traffic is dropped or quarantined before it ever reaches sensitive internal assets (servers, databases, workstations).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Castle & The Moat"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine your internal corporate network as a **medieval castle** housing the kingdom's gold (your data). The Perimeter Defense is the **outer wall + moat + drawbridge** that surrounds it.
>
> - The **moat** = Firewall (filters out obvious threats)
> - The **drawbridge gate** = Packet Filter / Access Control List
> - The **watchtowers with archers** = IDS/IPS (actively scanning for attackers)
> - The **outer courtyard (between the two walls)** = DMZ (where visitors like public web servers are kept, away from the inner sanctum)
> - The **King's personal bodyguard** = Bastion Host (the hardened, heavily monitored server that talks to the outside world on behalf of the king)
>
> Just as a castle has multiple concentric defenses rather than a single wall, perimeter defense is built from **multiple coordinated layers**, not just one device.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Terminology

- **Attack Surface**: The sum of all points where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter or extract data. Measured in number of open ports, exposed services, and reachable IPs.
- **Throughput (Gbps)**: The volume of traffic a perimeter device can inspect per second without becoming a bottleneck. Enterprise-grade firewalls typically handle **10–100 Gbps**.
- **Latency (ms)**: The delay added by perimeter inspection. Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) typically adds **< 5 ms**.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)**: Probability that a legitimate packet is mistakenly flagged as malicious.
- **False Negative Rate (FNR)**: Probability that a malicious packet slips through undetected.

### 1.4 Position in the KTU 2024 Module 1 Syllabus

Module 1 of PCCCT502 typically covers **Network Security Foundations**, and Perimeter Defense is the gateway concept. It links directly to:
- **Cryptography & Encryption** (used inside VPN tunnels at the perimeter)
- **Access Control** (firewall rule bases are essentially ACLs)
- **Malicious Software & Threats** (the reason the perimeter exists)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Perimeter Defense

Perimeter Defense is not a single product — it is a **system of systems**. The KTU syllabus expects students to recognize and explain each component.

#### 2.1.1 Firewall (The Gatekeeper)

A firewall enforces an **administratively defined security policy** by examining packet headers (and optionally payloads) and deciding to **ALLOW**, **DENY**, or **DROP** each packet.

**Three Generations of Firewalls:**

| Generation | Inspection Depth | State Awareness | Typical Speed | KTU Term |
|------------|------------------|------------------|---------------|----------|
| 1st Gen — Packet Filter | Layer 3/4 headers only | Stateless | Very fast (~line rate) | *Stateless Firewall* |
| 2nd Gen — Stateful Inspection | Layer 3/4 + connection state table | Stateful | Fast | *Stateful Firewall* |
| 3rd Gen — Application Layer Gateway | Up to Layer 7 (payload) | Stateful + protocol-aware | Slower | *Application Firewall / WAF* |

#### 2.1.2 Demilitarized Zone (DMZ)

A **physically or logically separate subnetwork** that sits *between* the untrusted Internet and the trusted internal LAN. Public-facing servers (web, mail, DNS) are placed here so that **even if compromised, they cannot directly reach the internal corporate LAN**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Screened Subnet Architecture (the gold standard):**
> `Internet → External Firewall → DMZ (public servers) → Internal Firewall → Internal LAN`
> This is the configuration most KTU diagrams test on.

#### 2.1.3 Intrusion Detection System (IDS) & Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)

| Feature | IDS (Passive) | IPS (Active) |
|---------|---------------|--------------|
| Action on threat | Logs & alerts only | Logs, alerts, AND blocks |
| Placement | Out-of-band (TAP / SPAN port) | In-line with traffic flow |
| Risk | None — cannot break traffic | Can become a single point of failure |
| KTU Term | *Intrusion Detection* | *Intrusion Prevention* |

**Detection Methods:**
- **Signature-based**: Compares traffic against a database of known attack patterns (e.g., Snort rules). High accuracy on known threats, blind to zero-days.
- **Anomaly-based**: Builds a baseline of "normal" behavior and flags deviations. Catches novel attacks but higher FPR.
- **Stateful Protocol Analysis**: Tracks protocol state machines (e.g., RFC-correct FTP handshakes) to catch violations.

#### 2.1.4 Bastion Host

A **specially hardened computer** that is fully exposed to the outside network, designed to be the **only host the firewall will allow to be directly accessed from the Internet** (e.g., a public SSH jump host or proxy). It runs the *minimum* necessary services, is patched religiously, and is heavily logged.

#### 2.1.5 Honeypot / Honeynet

A **decoy system** deployed inside the DMZ with no production value, intended to attract, study, and waste the time of attackers. Purely a detection and intelligence-gathering tool.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **verifiable** and aligned with standard references (Stallings *Cryptography and Network Security*, NIST SP 800-41 *Guidelines on Firewalls and Firewall Policy*).

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Firewall Rule Lookup Complexity | $T_{lookup} = O(\log_2 N)$ | seconds | For sorted rule sets (binary search); $N$ = number of rules |
| 2 | Anomaly Detection Threshold (z-score) | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | dimensionless | $\|z\| > 3$ ⇒ flag as anomaly (3-sigma rule) |
| 3 | IDS False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | ratio (0–1) | $FP$ = false positives, $TN$ = true negatives |
| 4 | IDS False Negative Rate | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | ratio (0–1) | $FN$ = missed attacks, $TP$ = correctly detected attacks |
| 5 | IDS Detection Rate (Recall/Sensitivity) | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = 1 - FNR$ | ratio (0–1) | KTU frequently tests this |
| 6 | IDS Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | ratio (0–1) | "Of all alerts raised, how many were real attacks?" |
| 7 | Packet Filtering Match Probability | $P_{match} = \dfrac{k}{N}$ | ratio | $k$ = matching rules, $N$ = total rules |
| 8 | DMZ Hop Count (logical) | $H = 2$ (firewalls) + 1 (DMZ) | hops | For a single-DMZ screened-subnet design |
| 9 | Effective Throughput after DPI | $T_{eff} = T_{raw} \times (1 - D)$ | bps | $D$ = DPI processing overhead, typically $0.001$–$0.05$ |
| 10 | Rule Set Conflict Indicator | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i \neq 0 \Rightarrow \text{conflict}$ | — | $A_i$ = action flag (+1 allow, −1 deny); any non-zero sum = ambiguity |

### 2.3 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why" Behind the Architecture)

- **Why a DMZ?** Because placing a public web server directly on the internal LAN means a single web-app vulnerability compromises your entire corporate network. The DMZ **isolates blast radius**.
- **Why both an IDS and IPS?** Because pure blocking (IPS) without monitoring (IDS) loses the **forensic evidence** you need post-incident.
- **Why a bastion host?** Because the principle of *least exposure* dictates that if a service *must* be reachable from the Internet, it should be **the most hardened and most logged** machine on the network.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: IDS Detection Performance from a Confusion Matrix

The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests the derivation of performance metrics from a raw confusion matrix. We walk through every transition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (2 marks per step is the KTU valuation pattern):**
> A signature-based IDS processes **10,000 packets** in one hour. Of these:
> - **Malicious packets** actually present: $M = 1{,}000$
> - **Benign packets**: $B = 9{,}000$
>
> The IDS raises alerts on **1,100 packets**, of which **850 are truly malicious** and **250 are benign (false alarms)**.

**Step 1 — Identify the Confusion Matrix entries**

From the data above:
- $TP$ (True Positives, attacks correctly flagged) $= 850$
- $FP$ (False Positives, benign wrongly flagged) $= 250$
- $FN$ (False Negatives, attacks missed) $= M - TP = 1{,}000 - 850 = 150$
- $TN$ (True Negatives, benign correctly ignored) $= B - FP = 9{,}000 - 250 = 8{,}750$

**Step 2 — Verify the row and column sums**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Malicious row total: } & TP + FN = 850 + 150 = 1{,}000 = M \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Benign row total: } & FP + TN = 250 + 8{,}750 = 9{,}000 = B \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Alerted total: } & TP + FP = 850 + 250 = 1{,}100 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Not alerted: } & FN + TN = 150 + 8{,}750 = 8{,}900 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Detection Rate (Recall / Sensitivity)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR &= \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \dfrac{850}{850 + 150} \\
&= \dfrac{850}{1{,}000} \\
&= 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{DR = 0.85 \text{ or } 85\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** The IDS catches **85%** of malicious traffic.

**Step 4 — Compute False Negative Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FNR &= \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP} \\
&= 1 - DR \\
&= 1 - 0.85 \\
&= 0.15
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{FNR = 0.15 \text{ or } 15\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute False Positive Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FPR &= \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} \\
&= \dfrac{250}{250 + 8{,}750} \\
&= \dfrac{250}{9{,}000} \\
&\approx 0.02778
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{FPR \approx 0.0278 \text{ or } 2.78\%}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Precision**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \dfrac{850}{850 + 250} \\
&= \dfrac{850}{1{,}100} \\
&\approx 0.7727
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 0.7727 \text{ or } 77.27\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** Of every **100 alerts** the IDS raises, about **77 are real attacks** and **23 are false alarms**.

**Step 7 — Compute Accuracy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Acc} &= \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \\
&= \dfrac{850 + 8{,}750}{10{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{9{,}600}{10{,}000} \\
&= 0.96
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Acc} = 0.96 \text{ or } 96\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why accuracy can mislead:** If 99% of traffic is benign, an IDS that *never alerts* would have 99% accuracy but 0% detection rate. Always pair Accuracy with **Detection Rate (Recall)** when evaluating an IDS. KTU examiners **love** this gotcha.

### 3.2 Derivation: Firewall Rule Conflict Detection

**Problem:** A junior admin writes the following rule base (top rule has highest priority). Detect any shadowing or redundancy.

| Priority | Source IP | Dest IP | Dest Port | Protocol | Action |
|----------|-----------|---------|-----------|----------|--------|
| 1 | 10.0.0.0/8 | 192.168.1.0/24 | 80 | TCP | ALLOW |
| 2 | 10.1.1.0/24 | 192.168.1.0/24 | 80 | TCP | DENY |
| 3 | 10.0.0.0/8 | 192.168.1.0/24 | * | * | DENY |

**Step 1 — Check Rule 1 vs Rule 3 (Shadowing Analysis)**

Rule 1 matches: `Src ∈ 10.0.0.0/8, Dst ∈ 192.168.1.0/24, Port 80, TCP → ALLOW`
Rule 3 matches: `Src ∈ 10.0.0.0/8, Dst ∈ 192.168.1.0/24, Port *, * → DENY`

Rule 3's match set is a **superset** of Rule 1's match set (since `Port = *` and `*` covers all protocols, this includes the TCP/80 traffic). Since Rule 1 has *higher* priority, **Rule 3 is effectively dead code for the TCP/80 subset** — this is a **shadowing anomaly**.

**Step 2 — Check Rule 2 vs Rule 1 (Redundancy / Conflict)**

Rule 2 has *lower* priority than Rule 1 but denies a *subset* of what Rule 1 allows. Therefore Rule 2 will **never fire** — it is **redundant**.

**Step 3 — Formal indicator using the rule-set action sum**

For traffic matching both Rule 1 and Rule 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum A_i &= A_{ALLOW} + A_{DENY} = (+1) + (-1) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

A non-zero action sum indicates effective conflict, but here the *priority* (Rule 1 above Rule 2) resolves the conflict → **ALLOW wins**. The 0 sum combined with priority weighting indicates **resolved redundancy**.

**Step 4 — Recommendation**

Remove Rule 2 (redundant) and rewrite Rule 3 to *exclude* port 80/TCP to eliminate shadowing.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Perimeter Devices

| Device | OSI Layer | State Aware | Inline? | Can Drop? | Primary Use |
|--------|-----------|-------------|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Packet Filter Firewall | 3–4 | No | Yes | Yes | Cheap, fast edge filtering |
| Stateful Firewall | 3–4 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Most enterprise perimeters |
| Application Firewall (WAF) | 7 | Yes (protocol) | Yes | Yes | Protect HTTP/HTTPS apps |
| IDS (NIDS) | 3–7 | Optional | **No** (passive) | **No** (alerts only) | Detection + forensics |
| IPS (NIPS) | 3–7 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Active inline blocking |
| Bastion Host | 7 (apps) | N/A | Yes (entry point) | N/A | Controlled external access |
| Honeypot | 7 | N/A | Yes (decoy) | N/A | Attacker deception + intel |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Screened-Subnet (Dual Firewall / DMZ) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    internetNode[Internet]:::untrusted
    extFW[External Firewall]:::perimeter
    dmz[DMZ Public Servers Web DNS Mail]:::dmz
    intFW[Internal Firewall]:::perimeter
    internalLAN[Internal Corporate LAN]:::trusted
    idsNode[IDS Sensor TAP]:::detection
    ipsNode[IPS Inline]:::prevention
    bastionNode[Bastion Host]:::bastion
    honeypotNode[Honeypot Decoy]:::decoy
    adminNode[Security Admin SIEM]:::admin

    internetNode -->|HTTP HTTPS SMTP| extFW
    extFW -->|Filtered Traffic| dmz
    extFW -.->|Mirror Copy| idsNode
    dmz -->|Application Traffic| ipsNode
    ipsNode -->|Sanitized Traffic| intFW
    intFW -->|Trusted Traffic| internalLAN
    bastionNode -->|SSH from Internet| extFW
    honeypotNode -->|Trap Attacker| extFW
    idsNode -->|Alerts| adminNode
    ipsNode -->|Logs Blocks| adminNode
    extFW -->|Logs| adminNode
    intFW -->|Logs| adminNode

    classDef untrusted fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef trusted fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dmz fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef perimeter fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003399,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef detection fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prevention fill:#ffccff,stroke:#990066,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bastion fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decoy fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef admin fill:#d6f5f5,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows = real traffic flow.
- Dotted arrow (`-.->`) = mirrored/tap traffic (passive monitoring by IDS).
- Color coding: **red** = untrusted, **green** = trusted, **yellow** = DMZ, **blue** = firewall/perimeter device, **purple** = detection/prevention, **orange** = bastion, **grey** = honeypot, **teal** = admin/SIEM.

### 4.2 Decision Flow Inside a Stateful Firewall

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pktStart([Inbound Packet Arrives]):::start
    check1{Valid IP Header Checksum?}:::decision
    drop1[Drop Packet Silently]:::drop
    check2{Source IP on Blacklist?}:::decision
    drop2[Drop and Log]:::drop
    check3{Stateful Connection Match in Table?}:::decision
    check4{Rule Base Match?}:::decision
    drop3[Drop per Default Deny Policy]:::drop
    allow[Forward Packet Update State Table]:::allow
    log[Log Event Forward to SIEM]:::log

    pktStart --> check1
    check1 -->|No| drop1
    check1 -->|Yes| check2
    check2 -->|Yes| drop2
    check2 -->|No| check3
    check3 -->|Yes| allow
    check3 -->|No| check4
    check4 -->|Match ALLOW| allow
    check4 -->|Match DENY| drop2
    check4 -->|No Match| drop3
    allow --> log

    classDef start fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#ffe6b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef drop fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef allow fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef log fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Pyramid (Perimeter as the Base Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L5[Layer 5 Data Encryption Application Level]:::top
    L4[Layer 4 Host Based Security EDR AV]:::layer4
    L3[Layer 3 Internal Network Segmentation VLANs NAC]:::layer3
    L2[Layer 2 Perimeter Defense IDS IPS Firewalls DMZ]:::layer2
    L1[Layer 1 Physical Security Locks Cameras Biometrics]:::layer1

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5

    classDef top fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer4 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer3 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer2 fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003399,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef layer1 fill:#d6f5f5,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> Perimeter Defense is **Layer 2** of the Defense-in-Depth model — essential, but **never sufficient on its own**. A determined attacker who breaches the perimeter must still face internal segmentation, host hardening, encryption, and physical security.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a stateless packet filter firewall and a stateful inspection firewall. Which provides stronger security and why? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Stateless Packet Filter | Stateful Inspection Firewall |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Inspection level | Each packet inspected **independently** in isolation | Each packet inspected in **context of the connection** it belongs to |
| State table | **None** — no memory of prior packets | **Maintains a state table** (5-tuple: Src IP, Src Port, Dst IP, Dst Port, Protocol) |
| Speed | Very fast — simple header checks | Slightly slower due to table lookup/update |
| Vulnerability | Cannot detect **spoofed packets inside an established flow**; vulnerable to **SYN flood variants** | Detects packets that **do not belong to any valid connection** and rejects them |
| Example | Traditional `iptables` rule filtering on port | Cisco ASA, pfSense, Linux `conntrack` |

**Verdict:** **Stateful firewall provides stronger security** because it understands the *context* of a conversation and can reject packets that violate TCP state machines (e.g., a TCP packet with both SYN and FIN flags set).

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

**Q2. What is a Demilitarized Zone (DMZ) in perimeter defense? Why are public-facing servers placed in the DMZ instead of the internal LAN? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **DMZ (Demilitarized Zone)** is a perimeter network segment that sits **between the untrusted external network and the trusted internal LAN**, physically or logically isolated by **two firewalls** (an external firewall facing the Internet and an internal firewall facing the LAN).

**Why public servers go in the DMZ:**
1. **Blast-radius isolation** — If a public web server is compromised by an SQL injection or buffer overflow, the attacker is *contained* in the DMZ and cannot directly pivot to internal file servers, HR databases, or Active Directory domain controllers.
2. **Differentiated policy** — The internal firewall can apply *stricter* rules between the DMZ and the LAN (e.g., "DMZ may initiate *outbound* to LAN on port 443 only"), preventing an attacker from establishing arbitrary internal sessions.
3. **Regulatory compliance** — Standards like **PCI-DSS** mandate that any system handling card data must be isolated from publicly accessible systems; the DMZ provides that physical/logical separation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End-Semester Exam]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Screened Subnet Architecture (Dual Firewall / DMZ) for perimeter defense** with a neat labeled diagram. List the advantages of this architecture over a single-firewall design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IDS processes 8,000 packets. Of these, 600 are malicious. The IDS flags 700 packets as suspicious, of which 520 are truly malicious. Compute the **Detection Rate, False Positive Rate, False Negative Rate, Precision, and Accuracy** of the IDS. Comment on whether this IDS is suitable for a high-security perimeter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Screened Subnet Architecture (7 marks)**

The **Screened Subnet** (also called the **DMZ architecture** or **dual-firewall architecture**) is the most robust classical perimeter defense topology.

**Components (1 mark):**
1. **External Firewall** — Faces the Internet, filters inbound/outbound traffic to the DMZ.
2. **DMZ** — Hosts public services (web, mail, DNS).
3. **Internal Firewall** — Sits between the DMZ and the internal LAN, applying a *stricter* policy.
4. **Bastion Host** — A hardened system within the DMZ that mediates all external access.
5. **IDS Sensor** — Monitors DMZ traffic via a TAP (passive).

**Architecture description (3 marks):**
- All Internet traffic first hits the **external firewall**, which performs stateful filtering and anti-spoofing checks.
- Public-facing servers in the DMZ serve inbound HTTP/HTTPS/SMTP traffic.
- The **internal firewall** permits *only* explicitly allowed sessions from the DMZ to the LAN (e.g., web server → database server on port 3306, and nothing else).
- The **IDS** observes mirrored traffic from the DMZ switch and forwards alerts to the SIEM.

**Advantages over a single-firewall design (3 marks):**
1. **Redundancy** — A single misconfigured rule in either firewall does not expose the LAN; an attacker must defeat *two* independent policies.
2. **Defense-in-Depth** — Compromise of the DMZ does not equal compromise of the LAN.
3. **Segregated Policy Domains** — Different teams can manage the two firewalls with different rule sets and audit trails.
4. **Internal firewall as "second gate"** — Prevents *outbound* exfiltration from a compromised DMZ server on unauthorized channels.
5. **Regulatory compliance** — Satisfies PCI-DSS, ISO 27001 control A.13.1.3 (segregation of networks).

> **Diagram** — Refer to **Section 4.1** (Mermaid schematic above) for the labeled architecture.

---

**(b) IDS Performance Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Total packets $N = 8{,}000$
- Actual malicious $M = 600$
- Actual benign $B = N - M = 7{,}400$
- Total alerts raised $= 700$
- True Positives $TP = 520$
- False Positives $FP = 700 - 520 = 180$
- False Negatives $FN = M - TP = 600 - 520 = 80$
- True Negatives $TN = B - FP = 7{,}400 - 180 = 7{,}220$

**Step 1 — Detection Rate (1 mark)**
$$
DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{520}{600} = 0.8667 \Rightarrow 86.67\%
$$

**Step 2 — False Negative Rate (1 mark)**
$$
FNR = 1 - DR = 1 - 0.8667 = 0.1333 \Rightarrow 13.33\%
$$

**Step 3 — False Positive Rate (1 mark)**
$$
FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{180}{7{,}400} \approx 0.02432 \Rightarrow 2.43\%
$$

**Step 4 — Precision (1 mark)**
$$
P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{520}{700} \approx 0.7429 \Rightarrow 74.29\%
$$

**Step 5 — Accuracy (1 mark)**
$$
\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{N} = \dfrac{520 + 7{,}220}{8{,}000} = \dfrac{7{,}740}{8{,}000} = 0.9675 \Rightarrow 96.75\%
$$

**Step 6 — Suitability Comment (2 marks)**
- The IDS misses **13.33%** of attacks (FNR) — for a **high-security perimeter**, the FNR should be below **5%**.
- The FPR of **2.43%** is acceptable, but a better-tuned signature base could reduce it further.
- **Verdict:** The IDS is **moderately suitable** — usable as a *layered* sensor in a Defense-in-Depth deployment, but **not sufficient as a standalone perimeter control** for a high-security environment. Pairing it with an IPS and a Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW) is recommended.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a perimeter defense system** including firewall, IDS, IPS, DMZ, bastion host, and honeypot. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Defense-in-Depth**? Why is perimeter defense alone insufficient to secure a modern enterprise network? List **four** internal threats that bypass the perimeter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Architecture of a Perimeter Defense System (7 marks)**

Refer to **Section 4.1** Mermaid diagram for the schematic (3 marks for diagram + 4 marks for component roles).

**Roles of each component:**

1. **External Firewall (1 mark):** First line of defense. Performs stateless and stateful packet filtering, anti-spoofing, and blocks known-bad IP ranges. Implements the **default-deny** policy: anything not explicitly allowed is dropped.

2. **DMZ (1 mark):** Network segment for public-facing servers (web, mail, DNS). Isolates these services from the internal LAN, so that a server compromise does not directly threaten internal assets.

3. **Internal Firewall (1 mark):** Second layer of defense. Applies a *stricter* policy between DMZ and LAN, restricts DMZ-to-LAN communications to specific ports/protocols, and prevents lateral movement.

4. **IDS (1 mark):** Passively monitors mirrored DMZ traffic, detects signatures of known attacks (e.g., SQL injection patterns, malware C2 callbacks), and forwards alerts to the SIEM. Does **not** drop traffic.

5. **IPS (1 mark):** Sits inline between the DMZ and the internal firewall. Detects and **actively blocks** malicious traffic in real time. Can reset TCP connections or quarantine offending IPs.

6. **Bastion Host (1 mark):** Hardened, single-purpose server in the DMZ that mediates all administrative access from the Internet (e.g., SSH jump host). Runs minimal services, undergoes continuous patching, and is heavily logged.

7. **Honeypot (1 mark):** Decoy server with no production value, designed to attract attackers. Provides early warning of probes and intelligence on attacker tools/techniques.

---

**(b) Defense-in-Depth & Limitations of Perimeter-Only Defense (7 marks)**

**Defense-in-Depth (2 marks):**
Defense-in-Depth is a security strategy that uses **multiple, overlapping layers of security controls** so that the failure of any one layer does not result in a complete compromise. It assumes that **no single control is perfect** and that attackers may breach any individual layer.

**Why Perimeter Defense Alone is Insufficient (2 marks):**
1. **Insider threats** — Malicious or careless employees are already *inside* the perimeter.
2. **Zero-day exploits** — A new vulnerability in a perimeter device (e.g., a firewall CVE) can be used to bypass the perimeter entirely.
3. **Supply-chain attacks** — Malware introduced via a trusted vendor or USB device never crosses the perimeter in a traditional attack.
4. **Encrypted traffic** — Perimeter devices that cannot decrypt TLS may let malware pass inside legitimate-looking HTTPS sessions.
5. **Cloud & mobile** — Modern workforces connect to SaaS apps and corporate resources from outside the perimeter, eroding the "perimeter" concept itself.

**Four Internal Threats that Bypass the Perimeter (3 marks):**
1. **Malicious Insider** — A disgruntled employee with legitimate network access exfiltrates data via sanctioned channels (email, cloud drives).
2. **Compromised Endpoint (Lateral Movement)** — A laptop infected via phishing email before VPN connection brings malware *inside* the perimeter.
3. **Rogue Device** — An unauthorized Wi-Fi access point plugged into a LAN port by a well-meaning employee.
4. **Privilege Misuse** — A privileged admin abuses legitimate credentials to access sensitive data.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not confuse IDS and IPS** — IDS *detects* and *alerts*; IPS *blocks* in real time. Swapping these in a 7-mark question is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **In confusion-matrix problems, always show the intermediate calculations** for TP, FP, TN, FN first. Examiners award 1 mark per metric — but only if you show the arithmetic, not just the final number.
> 3. **Never write "DMZ is a firewall"** — the DMZ is a *network segment*, not a device. The *firewalls* are what create and protect it.
> 4. **In FPR/FNR questions, the denominator differs from accuracy's denominator** — FPR denominator is `FP + TN` (all benign), not total packets. Many students incorrectly use the total — instant 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam Quick Recall — Perimeter Defense**

- ✅ Perimeter defense = the **outer layer** of a Defense-in-Depth strategy; establishes a clear boundary between trusted and untrusted networks.
- ✅ The **five core components** to remember: **Firewall, DMZ, IDS, IPS, Bastion Host** (+ optional honeypot).
- ✅ **Three firewall generations:** Stateless → Stateful → Application-Layer (WAF).
- ✅ **IDS is passive (alerts only)**; **IPS is active (blocks inline)**. Never invert.
- ✅ **Screened Subnet** = `Internet → Ext-FW → DMZ → Int-FW → LAN` is the gold-standard perimeter topology.
- ✅ **DMZ = network segment**, NOT a device. Two firewalls create the DMZ.
- ✅ **Bastion Host** = single hardened entry point for external access; least-privilege, maximum logging.
- ✅ **Honeypot** = decoy for attacker detection and intel; has zero production value.
- ✅ Key metrics: **DR = TP/(TP+FN)**, **FPR = FP/(FP+TN)**, **FNR = FN/(FN+TP)**, **Precision = TP/(TP+FP)**, **Accuracy = (TP+TN)/Total**.
- ✅ **Accuracy alone misleads** on imbalanced datasets — always pair with **Detection Rate (Recall)**.
- ✅ Perimeter defense is **not sufficient** against insider threats, zero-days, supply-chain attacks, or encrypted-malware channels.
- ✅ Modern trend: **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** is gradually replacing classical perimeter thinking — the 2024 syllabus expects awareness of this shift.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Penetration Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY > - **Module:** Module Module-1 > - **Topic:** Penetration Testing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Penetration Testing (Pen Testing / Ethical Hacking)** is a structured, authorized, and methodical simulation of a cyber-attack against a computer system, network, or web application, performed to evaluate the **security posture** of the target by safely exploiting identified vulnerabilities. The primary deliverable is a comprehensive report detailing discovered vulnerabilities, their risk severity (CVSS score), exploitation paths, and actionable remediation strategies. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, penetration testing is a **proactive security assessment methodology** that goes beyond passive vulnerability scanning by actively attempting to breach security controls to measure the true **real-world exploitability** of weaknesses in a system. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a homeowner worried about burglars breaking into your house. You wouldn't just sit and wait. Instead, you would hire a **friendly locksmith** to: 1. **Walk around your property** (Reconnaissance) — checking all entry points. 2. **Try the locks, windows, and doors** (Scanning) — testing which ones are weak. 3. **Actually try to break in using common burglary tools** (Exploitation) — simulating a real attack. 4. **Report back with a list of vulnerabilities** (Reporting) — "Your back window latch is broken, your garage has a weak PIN pad." The locksmith doesn't steal anything — he is **authorized**, he **documents everything**, and he gives you a blueprint to fix weaknesses. That locksmith is the **penetration tester**, and your house is the **target system**. > [!NOTE] > **Three Keyword Distinctions for KTU Board Exams:** > - **Authorized** → Must have explicit written permission (Rules of Engagement). > - **Simulated** → Real tools, real techniques, but controlled environment. > - **Documented** → Every step is logged for audit and remediation purposes. --- ## 1.3 Why Penetration Testing is Required | Reason | Engineering Justification | |---|---| | **Regulatory Compliance** | PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, ISO 27001 mandate regular pentests. | | **Risk Quantification** | Converts abstract vulnerabilities into measurable business risk. | | **Validation of Defenses** | Verifies that firewalls, IDS/IPS, and WAFs function as configured. | | **Incident Response Readiness** | Tests SOC team's detection and response capabilities. | | **Zero-Day Simulation** | Mimics advanced persistent threats (APTs). | > [!TIP] > **Key Metric to Remember:** Industry reports show the **average cost of a data breach in 2024 is USD 4.88 million** (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report). A single penetration test typically costs between **USD 5,000 – USD 50,000**, making it a high-ROI security investment. --- ## 1.4 Penetration Testing vs. Vulnerability Assessment (Critical KTU Distinction) | Parameter | Vulnerability Assessment | Penetration Testing | |---|---|---| | **Goal** | Identify & list vulnerabilities | Exploit vulnerabilities to measure real impact | | **Depth** | Broad, surface-level scan | Deep, targeted exploitation | | **Automation** | Mostly automated (Nessus, Qualys) | Hybrid (manual + tools) | | **Output** | List of CVEs with severity scores | Proof-of-concept exploits + business risk | | **Frequency** | Continuous / monthly | Quarterly or annually | | **Risk to System** | Very low (passive) | Moderate (active exploitation) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Standardized Penetration Testing Methodologies KTU examiners often ask students to enumerate the standard frameworks. Memorize these acronyms: > [!NOTE] > **PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard)** — 7 Phases, the most commonly tested framework. > **OSSTMM (Open Source Security Testing Methodology Manual)** — Focuses on measurable security metrics. > **OWASP Testing Guide** — Web application specific. > **NIST SP 800-115** — Technical guide for security testing. --- ## 2.2 The 7 Phases of Penetration Testing (PTES Framework) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **highest-weightage topic** in Module-1 for KTU exams. Memorize the order, the purpose of each phase, and the tools used. ### **Phase 1: Pre-Engagement / Scoping** - **Goal:** Define Rules of Engagement (RoE), legal contracts (NDA), IP scope, testing window, emergency contacts. - **Output:** Signed authorization letter + Scoping document. - **Why it matters:** Without written permission, the test is **illegal** under the **IT Act 2000 (India) §66** and equivalent global laws. ### **Phase 2: Reconnaissance (Information Gathering)** - **Goal:** Collect maximum information about the target with minimal direct interaction. - **Two Sub-Types:** | Sub-Type | Method | Example | |---|---|---| | **Passive Reconnaissance** | OSINT — no direct contact with target | Google Dorking, Shodan, WHOIS, social media scraping | | **Active Reconnaissance** | Direct probing of target | DNS enumeration, ping sweeps, traceroute | ### **Phase 3: Scanning & Enumeration** - **Goal:** Identify live hosts, open ports, running services, and OS fingerprints. - **Tools:** **Nmap**, **Masscan**, **Nikto**, **Nessus**. - **Key Nmap Scan Types:** | Scan Type | Switch | What It Does | |---|---|---| | TCP SYN Scan (Half-open) | `-sS` | Stealth scan, doesn't complete TCP handshake | | TCP Connect Scan | `-sT` | Completes full 3-way handshake | | UDP Scan | `-sU` | Scans for open UDP ports | | Service Version Detection | `-sV` | Identifies software version on open ports | | OS Detection | `-O` | Fingerprints the operating system | | Aggressive Scan | `-A` | Combines `-sV`, `-O`, traceroute, script scanning | ### **Phase 4: Vulnerability Analysis** - **Goal:** Map discovered services to known CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures). - **Tools:** **Nessus**, **OpenVAS**, **Qualys**, **Nexpose**. - **Database Reference:** **CVE** (MITRE) and **NVD** (National Vulnerability Database). ### **Phase 5: Exploitation (Gaining Access)** - **Goal:** Actively exploit identified vulnerabilities to penetrate the system. - **Tools:** **Metasploit Framework (MSF)**, **SQLmap**, **Burp Suite**, **Hydra**, **John the Ripper**. - **Common Attack Vectors:** SQL Injection, XSS, Buffer Overflow, Credential Brute-force, Privilege Escalation. ### **Phase 6: Post-Exploitation** - **Goal:** Determine the value of the compromised system — pivot to other systems, exfiltrate mock data, establish persistence. - **Activities:** Lateral movement, privilege escalation to root/admin, dumping password hashes (Mimikatz), setting up backdoors. ### **Phase 7: Reporting** - **Goal:** Deliver executive summary + technical report. - **Report Structure:** Executive summary (for management) + Technical findings (for IT team) + Risk ratings (CVSS scoring) + Remediation roadmap. --- ## 2.3 Types of Penetration Testing (KTU Favorite Question) > [!NOTE] > KTU frequently asks: *"Classify penetration testing based on the level of knowledge given to the tester."* The classification is based on **information disclosure to the tester**. | Type | Knowledge Given | Real-World Analogy | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Black-Box Testing** | Zero prior knowledge | A burglar trying to break in from scratch | Simulates external attacker | | **White-Box Testing** | Full source code, network diagrams, credentials | A locksmith given the house blueprints | Maximum coverage, internal audit | | **Grey-Box Testing** | Partial knowledge (e.g., user-level credentials) | A former employee with limited insider info | Simulates insider threat, most realistic & cost-effective | ### **Other Classification Axes:** | Axis | Types | |---|---| | **Location** | External Pentest (over Internet) / Internal Pentest (inside LAN) | | **Target** | Network Pentest / Web Application Pentest / Mobile App Pentest / Wireless Pentest / Social Engineering Pentest / Physical Pentest | | **Approach** | Manual / Automated / Hybrid | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use | |---|---|---| | **CVSS Base Score Range** | $0.0 \leq CVSS \leq 10.0$ | Severity rating | | **CVSS Severity Bands** | $0.0–3.9$ Low, $4.0–6.9$ Medium, $7.0–8.9$ High, $9.0–10.0$ Critical | Risk classification | | **Risk Score Formula** | $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$ | Quantitative risk | | **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Cost-benefit of pentest | | **Single Loss Expectancy** | $SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$ | Cost per incident | | **Return on Security Investment** | $ROSI = \frac{ALE - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$ | Pentest ROI | | **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \frac{\sum Detection\ Times}{Number\ of\ Incidents}$ | SOC efficiency | | **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \frac{\sum Response\ Times}{Number\ of\ Incidents}$ | Incident response | | **Exploitability Metric (CVSS)** | $E = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$ | Exploit probability | | **Impact Metric (CVSS)** | $I = 6.42 \times (1 - (1-C)(1-I)(1-A))$ | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability impact | > [!TIP] > For KTU board exams, focus on **CVSS bands** and the **ALE formula**. These are the most frequently tested quantitative concepts. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Penetration testing is not academic — it is a **mandatory compliance activity** in: - **Banking & Finance (RBI Guidelines for Indian Banks)** — Quarterly pentests. - **e-Governance systems** — Cert-In empanelled pentest vendors only. - **Healthcare (HIPAA)** — Annual mandatory testing. - **E-commerce platforms** — PCI-DSS requirement before handling card data. In Kerala specifically, the **Kerala State IT Mission** and **CERT-Kerala** mandate penetration testing for all state government digital infrastructure under the **Kerala Digital Security Act**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: CVSS Score Calculation > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests CVSS calculation in the analytical section. The following is a **board-exam-style problem** with full solution. ### **Problem Statement:** A web application vulnerability has the following CVSS v3.1 vector: `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N` Calculate the **Base Score** and determine the severity band. --- ### **Step 1: Decode the Vector Components** | Metric | Value | Meaning | |---|---|---| | AV (Attack Vector) | N (Network) | Exploitable remotely over the internet | | AC (Attack Complexity) | L (Low) | No special conditions required | | PR (Privileges Required) | N (None) | No authentication needed | | UI (User Interaction) | N (None) | Victim interaction not required | | S (Scope) | U (Unchanged) | Impact limited to vulnerable component | | C (Confidentiality) | H (High) | Total information disclosure | | I (Integrity) | H (High) | Total integrity loss possible | | A (Availability) | N (None) | No availability impact | --- ### **Step 2: Retrieve the Metric Values from CVSS v3.1 Specification** The official CVSS v3.1 specification assigns numerical weights: $$\begin{aligned} AV_{N} &= 0.85 \\ AC_{L} &= 0.77 \\ PR_{N} &= 0.85 \quad (\text{since Scope} = U) \\ UI_{N} &= 0.85 \\ C_{H} &= 0.56 \\ I_{H} &= 0.56 \\ A_{N} &= 0.00 \end{aligned}$$ --- ### **Step 3: Calculate the Exploitability Sub-Score (ISS)** The formula from the CVSS v3.1 specification: $$ISS = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$$ Substituting the values: $$\begin{aligned} ISS &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\ &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.7225 \\ &= 8.22 \times 0.47259 \\ &= 3.887 \end{aligned}$$ Rounded to one decimal: **ISS = 3.9** --- ### **Step 4: Calculate the Impact Sub-Score (Impact)** The impact formula: $$Impact = 6.42 \times \left(1 - (1-C)(1-I)(1-A)\right)$$ For Scope Unchanged, we use the base Impact formula. Substituting: $$\begin{aligned} Impact &= 6.42 \times (1 - (1-0.56)(1-0.56)(1-0)) \\ &= 6.42 \times (1 - (0.44)(0.44)(1)) \\ &= 6.42 \times (1 - 0.1936) \\ &= 6.42 \times 0.8064 \\ &= 5.177 \end{aligned}$$ Rounded: **Impact = 5.2** --- ### **Step 5: Calculate the Base Score** When Scope is Unchanged and Impact > 0: $$Base = RoundUp\left(\min\left((Impact + ISS), 10\right)\right)$$ Substituting: $$\begin{aligned} Base &= RoundUp(\min(5.2 + 3.9, 10)) \\ &= RoundUp(\min(9.1, 10)) \\ &= 9.1 \end{aligned}$$ --- ### **Step 6: Determine Severity Band** | Range | Severity | |---|---| | $0.0 - 3.9$ | Low | | $4.0 - 6.9$ | Medium | | $7.0 - 8.9$ | High | | $9.0 - 10.0$ | **Critical** | **Result:** Base Score = **9.1** → Severity = **CRITICAL** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** A remotely exploitable, unauthenticated vulnerability causing full confidentiality and integrity loss with no availability impact scores **9.1 (Critical)** on the CVSS v3.1 scale. This warrants immediate patching within 24–48 hours. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) Calculation ### **Problem Statement:** A company has an e-commerce server worth **₹10,00,000**. The probability of a SQL injection attack in a year is **0.4 (40%)**. The Exposure Factor (fraction of asset lost per incident) is **0.6**. A penetration test costs **₹2,00,000 per year**. Calculate the **ALE**, the **cost-benefit of the pentest**, and the **ROSI**. --- ### **Step 1: Calculate Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** $$SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$$ $$\begin{aligned} SLE &= 10,00,000 \times 0.6 \\ &= 6,00,000 \end{aligned}$$ So **SLE = ₹6,00,000** --- ### **Step 2: Calculate Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** $$ALE = SLE \times Annual\ Rate\ of\ Occurrence\ (ARO)$$ $$\begin{aligned} ALE &= 6,00,000 \times 0.4 \\ &= 2,40,000 \end{aligned}$$ So **ALE = ₹2,40,000** --- ### **Step 3: Calculate ROSI (Return on Security Investment)** Assume the pentest mitigates 80% of the risk. $$Mitigation\ Benefit = ALE \times Mitigation\ Percentage$$ $$\begin{aligned} Mitigation\ Benefit &= 2,40,000 \times 0.80 \\ &= 1,92,000 \end{aligned}$$ $$ROSI = \frac{Mitigation\ Benefit - Cost\ of\ Pentest}{Cost\ of\ Pentest} \times 100$$ $$\begin{aligned} ROSI &= \frac{1,92,000 - 2,00,000}{2,00,000} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{-8,000}{2,00,000} \times 100 \\ &= -4\% \end{aligned}$$ --- ### **Step 4: Interpret the Result** > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** A negative ROSI of **-4%** means the pentest is currently **not cost-justified** at this risk level. The company should either: > 1. Reduce the pentest cost (e.g., use a smaller scope). > 2. Improve the mitigation effectiveness above 80%. > 3. Accept the residual risk. If mitigation effectiveness is raised to **90%**: $$\begin{aligned} ROSI &= \frac{(2,40,000 \times 0.90) - 2,00,000}{2,00,000} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{16,000}{2,00,000} \times 100 \\ &= 8\% \end{aligned}$$ Now the pentest is **8% cost-justified**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Tools & Their Phase Mapping | Tool | Phase Used | Category | Open Source? | |---|---|---|---| | **Nmap** | Scanning & Enumeration | Network mapper | ✅ Yes | | **Nessus** | Vulnerability Analysis | Vulnerability scanner | ❌ Commercial (free for personal) | | **Metasploit** | Exploitation | Exploitation framework | ✅ Community Edition | | **Burp Suite** | Web App Exploitation | Web proxy / scanner | ✅ Community Edition | | **Wireshark** | Reconnaissance / Sniffing | Packet analyzer | ✅ Yes | | **John the Ripper** | Post-Exploitation | Password cracker | ✅ Yes | | **Hydra** | Exploitation | Network logon cracker | ✅ Yes | | **SQLmap** | Exploitation | SQL injection tool | ✅ Yes | | **Aircrack-ng** | Wireless Pentest | WiFi security auditing | ✅ Yes | | **Mimikatz** | Post-Exploitation | Credential extraction | ✅ Yes | | **Maltego** | Reconnaissance | OSINT / data mining | ✅ Community Edition | | **OWASP ZAP** | Web App Scanning | Web vulnerability scanner | ✅ Yes | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **Define penetration testing. List any THREE differences between penetration testing and vulnerability assessment.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Penetration testing is a simulated, authorized cyber-attack against a system, network, or application performed to identify exploitable vulnerabilities and evaluate the real-world security posture of the target. **Three Differences:** | S.No. | Penetration Testing | Vulnerability Assessment | |---|---|---| | 1 | Actively exploits vulnerabilities to prove impact | Only identifies and lists vulnerabilities | | 2 | Mostly manual with tool assistance | Mostly automated (Nessus, Qualys) | | 3 | Higher risk to system (active probing) | Lower risk (passive scanning) | --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Explain the THREE types of penetration testing based on the level of information disclosure.** **Model Answer:** | Type | Knowledge Given | Real-World Scenario | |---|---|---| | **Black-Box** | No prior information; tester acts as an external attacker with no internal knowledge | Simulates an external hacker trying to breach public-facing systems | | **White-Box** | Full source code, network diagrams, IP addresses, and credentials provided to tester | Simulates an insider threat or thorough internal audit; offers maximum code coverage | | **Grey-Box** | Partial knowledge (e.g., low-level user credentials) provided to tester | Most realistic scenario; simulates a disgruntled ex-employee or partner with limited insider access | --- ## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)** --- ### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **seven phases of penetration testing** as per the **PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard)** framework. For each phase, mention the **objective, key activities, and tools used**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A healthcare web application has been identified with the following CVSS v3.1 vector: `CVSS:3.1/AV:L/AC:L/PR:L/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H` Calculate the **Base Score** and identify the **severity band**. Justify your answer. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to (a):** The seven phases of PTES are: **Phase 1 — Pre-Engagement (Scoping):** - **Objective:** Define legal scope, Rules of Engagement (RoE), testing window, in-scope IP ranges, and emergency contacts. - **Key Activities:** NDA signing, authorization letter from client, defining testing methodology, identifying out-of-scope assets. - **Tools:** Legal contract templates, scoping questionnaires. **Phase 2 — Intelligence Gathering (Reconnaissance):** - **Objective:** Collect information about the target with minimal direct contact. - **Key Activities:** Passive (Google Dorking, Shodan, WHOIS lookup) and active (DNS enumeration, social engineering, dumpster diving). - **Tools:** Maltego, Recon-ng, theHarvester, Shodan. **Phase 3 — Threat Modeling:** - **Objective:** Identify highest-value assets and map potential attack vectors. - **Key Activities:** Asset classification, attacker profiling, attack surface mapping. - **Tools:** Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool. **Phase 4 — Vulnerability Analysis:** - **Objective:** Identify and validate weaknesses. - **Key Activities:** Automated scanning, manual testing, CVE mapping. - **Tools:** Nessus, OpenVAS, Qualys, Burp Suite. **Phase 5 — Exploitation:** - **Objective:** Actively exploit identified vulnerabilities. - **Key Activities:** Gaining initial access, privilege escalation, lateral movement. - **Tools:** Metasploit Framework, SQLmap, Hydra, BeEF. **Phase 6 — Post-Exploitation:** - **Objective:** Determine the value of the compromised system. - **Key Activities:** Data exfiltration simulation, persistence, clean-up. - **Tools:** Mimikatz, Cobalt Strike, Empire. **Phase 7 — Reporting:** - **Objective:** Deliver actionable findings to the client. - **Key Activities:** Executive summary writing, technical report, remediation roadmap. - **Tools:** Dradis, Faraday, MagicTree. --- #### **Model Solution to (b):** **Decoding the Vector:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:L/AC:L/PR:L/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H` | Metric | Value | Numerical Weight | |---|---|---| | AV | L (Local) | 0.55 | | AC | L (Low) | 0.77 | | PR | L (Low), Scope = U | 0.62 | | UI | R (Required) | 0.62 | | C | H (High) | 0.56 | | I | H (High) | 0.56 | | A | H (High) | 0.56 | **Step 1: Exploitability Sub-Score (ISS):** $$\begin{aligned} ISS &= 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI \\ &= 8.22 \times 0.55 \times 0.77 \times 0.62 \times 0.62 \\ &= 8.22 \times 0.1625 \\ &= 1.336 \\ &\approx 1.3 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Impact Sub-Score:** $$\begin{aligned} Impact &= 6.42 \times (1 - (1-0.56)(1-0.56)(1-0.56)) \\ &= 6.42 \times (1 - (0.44)^3) \\ &= 6.42 \times (1 - 0.085184) \\ &= 6.42 \times 0.914816 \\ &= 5.873 \\ &\approx 5.9 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Base Score (Scope = Unchanged):** $$\begin{aligned} Base &= RoundUp(\min(Impact + ISS, 10)) \\ &= RoundUp(\min(5.9 + 1.3, 10)) \\ &= RoundUp(7.2) \\ &= 7.2 \end{aligned}$$ **Result:** Base Score = **7.2 → Severity = HIGH** **Justification:** A local, low-complexity vulnerability requiring basic privileges and user interaction, causing full CIA impact, scores **7.2 (HIGH)**.
