This requires patching within **7–14 days** as per industry SLA standards. --- ### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Compare and contrast **Black-Box, White-Box, and Grey-Box penetration testing** with suitable real-world analogies. List the **advantages and disadvantages** of each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A company has a customer database server valued at **₹50,00,000**. The probability of a credential-stuffing attack in a year is **0.3 (ARO)**. The Exposure Factor is **0.7**. A penetration test costs **₹3,00,000** and is expected to mitigate **85%** of the risk. Calculate the **ALE, SLE, and ROSI**. Should the company invest in the pentest? Justify. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to (a):** | Parameter | Black-Box | White-Box | Grey-Box | |---|---|---|---| | **Knowledge** | None | Full (code, network, credentials) | Partial (user-level creds) | | **Tester Perspective** | External attacker | Internal developer / auditor | Insider threat | | **Real-World Analogy** | A thief breaking into a house from the street | A locksmith given the house blueprints | A former tenant who once had a key | | **Time Required** | Highest | Lowest | Moderate | | **Cost** | Highest | Moderate | Moderate | | **Code Coverage** | Lowest (≈ 20–30%) | Highest (≈ 90–100%) | Moderate (≈ 50–70%) | | **False Positives** | Highest | Lowest | Moderate | **Black-Box Advantages:** Most realistic external attack simulation. **Disadvantages:** Time-consuming, expensive, may miss internal vulnerabilities. **White-Box Advantages:** Maximum coverage, fastest execution. **Disadvantages:** Unrealistic for external threats, requires source code access. **Grey-Box Advantages:** Balanced cost and realism, most commonly used in industry. **Disadvantages:** Tester bias from partial information. --- #### **Model Solution to (b):** **Given:** - Asset Value = ₹50,00,000 - ARO = 0.3 - Exposure Factor (EF) = 0.7 - Pentest Cost = ₹3,00,000 - Mitigation Effectiveness = 85% = 0.85 **Step 1: Calculate SLE** $$\begin{aligned} SLE &= Asset\ Value \times EF \\ &= 50,00,000 \times 0.7 \\ &= 35,00,000 \end{aligned}$$ **SLE = ₹35,00,000** **Step 2: Calculate ALE** $$\begin{aligned} ALE &= SLE \times ARO \\ &= 35,00,000 \times 0.3 \\ &= 10,50,000 \end{aligned}$$ **ALE = ₹10,50,000** **Step 3: Calculate Mitigation Benefit** $$\begin{aligned} Mitigation\ Benefit &= ALE \times Mitigation\ Effectiveness \\ &= 10,50,000 \times 0.85 \\ &= 8,92,500 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Calculate ROSI** $$\begin{aligned} ROSI &= \frac{Mitigation\ Benefit - Pentest\ Cost}{Pentest\ Cost} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{8,92,500 - 3,00,000}{3,00,000} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{5,92,500}{3,00,000} \times 100 \\ &= 197.5\% \end{aligned}$$ **ROSI = 197.5%** **Justification:** A **positive ROSI of 197.5%** means the penetration test is **highly cost-justified**. For every ₹1 invested, the company saves ₹1.97 in expected losses. The company should **definitely invest** in the pentest. --- ## **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module-1 — Penetration Testing):** > 1. **Confusing Vulnerability Assessment with Penetration Testing** — A 2-mark question is dedicated to this distinction. Memorize the table in Section 1.4. > 2. **Wrong CVSS Formula Application** — Students often forget to use the **PR (Unchanged) value** instead of PR (Changed) when Scope is Unchanged. Read the vector carefully. > 3. **Skipping Phase 1 (Pre-Engagement)** — Examiners expect the legal/contractual phase first. Starting directly with Nmap scanning will lose 1–2 marks. > 4. **No Tools Mentioned** — Every phase must be accompanied by at least **one real tool name** (Nmap, Metasploit, Burp Suite, etc.). > 5. **ALE/ROSI Calculation Mistakes** — Mixing up SLE and ALE formulas. **SLE uses Exposure Factor; ALE uses ARO.** > 6. **Confusing Black-Box with Grey-Box** — If a question says "partial credentials given," the answer is **Grey-Box**, not Black-Box. > 7. **Forgetting to Round Up** — CVSS Base Score must always be **rounded UP to one decimal** using the ceiling function. --- ## **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER** > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for Penetration Testing (Module-1):** - ✅ **Penetration Testing** = Authorized, simulated, documented cyber-attack to evaluate security. - ✅ **Three Pillars:** Authorized + Simulated + Documented. - ✅ **7 Phases (PTES):** Pre-Engagement → Recon → Threat Modeling → Vulnerability Analysis → Exploitation → Post-Exploitation → Reporting. - ✅ **Three Types (Knowledge-Based):** Black-Box (zero), Grey-Box (partial), White-Box (full). - ✅ **Pentest vs. Vulnerability Scan:** Pentest **exploits**; VA only **identifies**. - ✅ **CVSS v3.1 Scale:** 0.0 (None) to 10.0 (Critical). Bands: Low (0–3.9), Medium (4–6.9), High (7–8.9), Critical (9–10). - ✅ **CVSS Formulas:** $ISS = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$ and $Impact = 6.42 \times (1-(1-C)(1-I)(1-A))$. - ✅ **ALE Formula:** $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ where $SLE = Asset\ Value \times EF$. - ✅ **ROSI Formula:** $ROSI = \frac{(ALE \times Mitigation) - Pentest\ Cost}{Pentest\ Cost} \times 100$. - ✅ **Key Tools:** Nmap (scan), Metasploit (exploit), Burp Suite (web), Nessus (vuln), Mimikatz (creds), Wireshark (sniff), SQLmap (SQLi), Hydra (brute-force). - ✅ **Legal Framework:** IT Act 2000 (India) §66 — unauthorized access is a criminal offense. Always get **written authorization**. - ✅ **Methodology Standards:** PTES, OSSTMM, OWASP Testing Guide, NIST SP 800-115. - ✅ **Kill Chain:** Recon → Weaponization → Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → C2 → Actions on Objective. - ✅ **Reporting Must Include:** Executive summary, technical findings, CVSS scores, remediation roadmap, retest plan. - ✅ **Industry Average Cost of Pentest:** **USD 5,000 – USD 50,000**; average cost of data breach: **USD 4.88M** (2024).

topics.title: Network Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-2
> - **Topic:** Network Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Network Attack** is any deliberate, unauthorized attempt to disrupt, manipulate, degrade, or gain illegal access to a computer network, its associated resources, or the data traversing it. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCCT502 – Module 2), network attacks are broadly classified by **attack intent**, **attack vector**, and the **OSI/TCP-IP layer** at which they operate. They range from passive eavesdropping (where the attacker merely observes traffic) to active intrusive attacks (where the attacker alters, injects, or destroys data and services).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Module 2 Scope**
> The KTU syllabus specifically expects students to master the following attack categories:
> 1. **Reconnaissance Attacks** – Footprinting, Scanning (Port, Network, Vulnerability)
> 2. **Access Attacks** – Password attacks, Trust exploitation, Man-in-the-Middle, Buffer Overflow, Malware
> 3. **Denial-of-Service (DoS / DDoS) Attacks** – SYN Flood, Smurf, Ping of Death, Teardrop
> 4. **Web & Application Layer Attacks** – SQL Injection, XSS, CSRF
> 5. **Wireless & ARP/DNS based attacks** – ARP Spoofing, DNS Poisoning, Rogue AP

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **highly secured bank vault** in a busy city:

- **Reconnaissance Attack** = A thief walking around the bank, *observing* guards' shift changes, counting cameras, and noting the make of the vault door — **without touching anything**.
- **Access Attack** = The thief finally *picking the lock* or bribing a guard to let him inside the vault.
- **DoS Attack** = The thief doesn't steal anything but *pours concrete into the vault's keyhole*, so legitimate customers cannot access their lockers.
- **MITM Attack** = A corrupt bank teller *intercepts* your withdrawal slip, changes the account number, and forwards it — neither you nor the bank notices.
- **Malware** = A stranger hands the receptionist a *fake pen* that records every keystroke typed on the bank's computer.

In every case, the attacker exploits a **weakness in trust, visibility, or validation** — the exact same principles that network attackers exploit digitally.

---

## 1.3 The CIA Triad — The Goal Behind Every Attack

Every network attack ultimately targets at least one pillar of the **CIA Triad**:

| Pillar | Meaning | Example Attack That Violates It |
|--------|---------|-------------------------------|
| **Confidentiality** | Data remains private | Sniffing, Eavesdropping |
| **Integrity** | Data is not tampered | MITM, SQL Injection |
| **Availability** | Services remain accessible | DoS, DDoS, Ransomware |

> [!NOTE]
> **Defender's Motto:** "An attack only succeeds when an *asset*, a *vulnerability*, and a *threat actor* coexist." This is the **Vulnerability–Threat–Asset Triangle** that forms the foundation of every risk assessment in network security.

---

## 1.4 Standard Attack Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

The following quantitative metrics are commonly tested:

- **DDoS Bandwidth**: Measured in **Gbps** (Gigabits per second) or **PPS** (Packets per second).
- **Scan Rate**: Number of probe packets sent per second (typical: 100 – 10,000 pps).
- **Mean Time to Compromise (MTTC)**: Average time required to breach a system.
- **CVSS Score**: Common Vulnerability Scoring System, ranging from **0.0** (no risk) to **10.0** (critical).
- **Attack Vector (AV)**: Network (N), Adjacent (A), Local (L), Physical (P).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Network Attacks

Network attacks are classified along two orthogonal axes:

### Axis A: By Activity (Passive vs Active)

| Type | Behavior | Detection Difficulty |
|------|----------|----------------------|
| **Passive** | Only *monitors* traffic (sniffing, traffic analysis). Data is **NOT altered**. | Very Hard (silent) |
| **Active** | *Modifies, injects, or disrupts* traffic. | Easier (creates anomalies) |

### Axis B: By OSI Layer Targeted

| Layer | Attack Examples |
|-------|-----------------|
| **Application (L7)** | SQL Injection, XSS, CSRF, Malware |
| **Transport (L4)** | SYN Flood, UDP Flood, Port Scanning |
| **Network (L3)** | Smurf, ICMP Flood, IP Spoofing, Ping of Death |
| **Data Link (L2)** | ARP Spoofing, MAC Flooding, VLAN Hopping |
| **Physical (L1)** | Wiretapping, Cable Tapping |

---

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Reconnaissance Attacks

Reconnaissance is the **pre-attack phase** — the digital equivalent of a burglar's surveillance.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Footprinting (Passive Reconnaissance)** – Gathering public information: WHOIS records, DNS records, social media, job postings, Google Dorking.
2. **Scanning (Active Reconnaissance)** – Sending crafted probes to live hosts.
   - **Ping Sweep**: ICMP Echo Request to a range of IPs to identify live hosts.
   - **Port Scan**: Probing TCP/UDP ports to identify open services.
   - **Vulnerability Scan**: Using signatures to identify known CVEs.
3. **Enumeration** – Extracting detailed info: usernames, shares, banners, SNMP strings.

**Why it works:** Most networks expose *some* services to facilitate communication; the attacker leverages these for intelligence.

---

### 2.2.2 Access Attacks

The attacker now attempts to **break into** the system.

- **Password Attacks**:
  - *Brute Force*: Tries all possible combinations. Time grows as $2^n$ for an $n$-bit key.
  - *Dictionary Attack*: Uses a precompiled list of common passwords.
  - *Hybrid*: Dictionary + suffixes (e.g., `password123`).
  - *Rainbow Tables*: Pre-computed hash lookup tables.

- **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)**:
  - Attacker positions himself between two communicating parties.
  - Common techniques: **ARP Poisoning**, **DNS Spoofing**, **SSL Stripping**.

- **Buffer Overflow**:
  - Writing more data than allocated memory can hold, overwriting the **return address** on the stack.
  - Modern variants: Stack-based, Heap-based, Return-Oriented Programming (ROP).

- **Malware**:
  - **Virus**: Requires a host file & user action to spread.
  - **Worm**: Self-replicating; spreads autonomously over networks.
  - **Trojan**: Disguises as legitimate software.
  - **Ransomware**: Encrypts victim data, demands payment.
  - **Rootkit**: Hides itself deep in the OS kernel.

---

### 2.2.3 Denial-of-Service (DoS) & Distributed DoS (DDoS)

Goal: **Make a service unavailable to legitimate users**, not necessarily to steal data.

**Core DoS attack families:**

| Attack | Mechanism | Protocol |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| **SYN Flood** | Exploits TCP 3-way handshake; sends many SYN, never sends ACK | TCP |
| **UDP Flood** | Floods random ports with UDP packets | UDP |
| **ICMP / Ping Flood** | Sends massive ICMP Echo Requests | ICMP |
| **Smurf Attack** | Sends ICMP Echo with spoofed victim IP to a broadcast address → amplifier | ICMP |
| **Ping of Death** | Sends malformed ICMP packet > 65,535 bytes | ICMP |
| **Teardrop** | Sends fragmented packets with overlapping offsets | IP |
| **DNS Amplification** | Small query → large response, spoofed source IP | UDP/DNS |
| **HTTP Flood (Slowloris)** | Keeps many HTTP connections half-open | TCP/80 |

**DDoS Architecture:**

$$\text{Bot Herder} \longrightarrow \text{Command \& Control (C2)} \longrightarrow \text{Botnet (Zombies)} \longrightarrow \text{Victim}$$

---

### 2.2.4 Web / Application Layer Attacks

- **SQL Injection (SQLi)**: Attacker injects SQL via input fields. Example payload: `' OR '1'='1`.
- **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)**: Injects malicious JavaScript into a trusted webpage.
- **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)**: Tricks a logged-in user's browser into submitting a forged request.
- **Directory Traversal**: `../../etc/passwd` to access files outside webroot.

---

### 2.2.5 ARP & DNS based Attacks

- **ARP Spoofing/Poisoning**: Attacker sends gratuitous ARP replies mapping his MAC to the gateway's IP → all victim traffic flows to attacker.
- **DNS Cache Poisoning**: Attacker injects forged DNS records into a resolver's cache.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** and have appeared in past KTU University Examinations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Brute-force key search time | $T = \dfrac{2^n}{2 \cdot R}$ | $n$ = key bits, $R$ = attempts/sec |
| 2 | DoS attack bandwidth | $B = P \times S$ | $P$ = packets/sec, $S$ = avg packet size |
| 3 | SYN Flood memory consumption | $M = N \times TCB$ | $N$ = half-open conns, $TCB$ = TCP control block size |
| 4 | Smurf amplification factor | $A = \dfrac{R_{out}}{R_{in}}$ | Ratio of response packets to request |
| 5 | DNS amplification factor | $A_f \approx 28$ to $54$ | Bytes returned per byte queried |
| 6 | Entropy of password | $H = \log_2(N^L)$ | $N$ = char set size, $L$ = length |
| 7 | Shannon entropy | $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Bits of uncertainty in source |
| 8 | Mean Time to Compromise | $MTTC = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = attack success rate |
| 9 | Packet rate conversion | $\text{PPS} = \dfrac{\text{BW (bps)}}{8 \times S}$ | $S$ = packet size in bytes |
| 10 | CVSS Base Score (simplified) | $BS = \text{round}(min(10, ISC \times AC))$ | Impact $\times$ Exploitability |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Security Operations Centers (SOCs)** use these classifications in SIEM dashboards.
- **Penetration Testers** follow the same reconnaissance → access → exfiltration kill-chain.
- **ISP & Cloud Providers** (e.g., AWS Shield, Cloudflare) design anti-DDoS pipelines using the amplification metrics above.
- **Forensic Investigators** classify breaches using CIA-Triad violation + OSI layer to attribute attack origin.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed SYN Flood Derivation

The **SYN Flood** is the most tested DoS attack in KTU papers. Let us derive its mechanics and impact mathematically.

### Step 1 — Normal TCP 3-Way Handshake

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\quad SYN (seq=x) \quad} \text{Server} \\
\text{Server} \xrightarrow{\quad SYN+ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1) \quad} \text{Client} \\
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\quad ACK (seq=x+1, ack=y+1) \quad} \text{Server}
\end{aligned}
$$

After this, a TCP Control Block (TCB) is allocated in the server's connection table.

### Step 2 — Attacker Behavior in SYN Flood

Attacker sends **SYN** but **never responds to SYN+ACK**. The server keeps TCB in `SYN_RECEIVED` state for ~75 seconds (Linux default), occupying memory.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Attacker} &\xrightarrow{\quad \text{SYN (spoofed IP)} \quad} \text{Server} \\
\text{Server} &\xrightarrow{\quad \text{SYN+ACK} \quad} \text{Attacker (or spoofed host)} \\
&\text{No ACK arrives} \rightarrow \text{TCB remains half-open}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Quantitative Impact

Assume a server has a TCB table of size **T** half-open connections. Each TCB uses approximately **S = 256 bytes** of memory.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Memory Consumed} \quad M &= T \times S \\
&= T \times 256 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example (KTU Numerical):**
If $T = 65{,}535$ (max half-open connections) and $S = 256$ bytes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 65535 \times 256 \\
&= 16{,}776{,}960 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 16 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Attack rate required to fill the table in 60 seconds:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{T}{\Delta t} = \frac{65535}{60} \approx 1092 \text{ SYNs/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is trivially achievable — proving the attack's catastrophic efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Smurf Attack Amplification Derivation

### Step 1 — Setup

Attacker sends a single ICMP Echo Request to a network's **broadcast address** (e.g., `192.168.1.255`) with the **victim's IP** as the source.

### Step 2 — Amplification

Every host on the broadcast segment replies to the victim.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Amplification Factor } A &= \frac{\text{Reply packets received by victim}}{\text{Packets sent by attacker}} \\
&= \frac{N}{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $N$ = number of hosts on the broadcast network.

### Step 3 — Bandwidth Calculation

If attacker sends $P$ packets/sec and amplification is $A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{victim}} &= P \times A \times S_{\text{ICMP}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For $P = 1000$ pps, $A = 100$ hosts, $S_{\text{ICMP}} = 84$ bytes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{victim}} &= 1000 \times 100 \times 84 \\
&= 8{,}400{,}000 \text{ bytes/sec} \\
&= 67.2 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If the ICMP Echo Reply payload size is $L$, the bandwidth becomes:
> $$B = P \cdot A \cdot (8 + L) \cdot 8 \text{ bits/sec}$$
> Always show units explicitly to earn full marks.

---

## 3.3 Brute Force Attack Time Complexity

### Step 1 — Keyspace Definition

For an alphabet of size $\Sigma$ and password length $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Combinations } K &= \vert \Sigma \vert^L
\end{aligned}
$$

For alphanumeric passwords, $\vert \Sigma \vert = 62$ (26 uppercase + 26 lowercase + 10 digits).

### Step 2 — Average Search Time

Assuming brute force tries **half the keyspace on average**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{K}{2 \cdot R}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R$ = password attempts per second.

### Step 3 — Worked Example

A system uses an 8-character alphanumeric password. An attacker tests $10^8$ passwords/sec.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= 62^8 = 2.18 \times 10^{14} \\
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{2.18 \times 10^{14}}{2 \times 10^8} = 1.09 \times 10^6 \text{ seconds} \\
&= \approx 12.6 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** 8-character alphanumeric passwords are weak against modern GPU-based crackers (> $10^{10}$ hashes/sec), which would reduce this to **minutes**.

---

## 3.4 ARP Spoofing — Analytical Flow

### Step 1 — Normal ARP Operation

Host A wants to send to Gateway G. It broadcasts: *"Who has IP G? Tell A."*

G replies (unicast): *"IP G is at MAC_G."*

### Step 2 — Poisoning Step

Attacker (Host X) sends **gratuitous ARP** to Host A:

$$
\text{ARP Reply: "IP of Gateway G is at MAC\_X (attacker's MAC)"}
$$

Host A **updates its ARP cache** with the forged entry.

### Step 3 — Traffic Capture

All packets destined for G are now sent to attacker:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{A} \longrightarrow X_{\text{(attacker)}} &\longrightarrow G_{\text{(forwarded, possibly modified)}} \\
&\longrightarrow \text{Internet (if not dropped)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a classic **MITM via Layer 2 manipulation**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Attack vs Defense

| Attack | OSI Layer | Primary Defense | Detection Method |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Port Scan | L4 | Firewall, Port-knocking | IDS signature, anomaly |
| SYN Flood | L4 | SYN Cookies, Rate limiting | Half-open conn. spikes |
| Smurf | L3 | Disable directed broadcast | ICMP reply spikes |
| ARP Spoofing | L2 | Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) | ARP watch, anomaly |
| DNS Poisoning | L7 | DNSSEC, DoH | TTL anomaly, mismatch |
| SQL Injection | L7 | Parameterized queries | WAF rules, anomaly |
| XSS | L7 | Output encoding, CSP | WAF, browser sandbox |
| Brute Force | L7 | Account lockout, MFA | Failed login spikes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Attack Kill-Chain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    recA[Reconnaissance: Footprinting] --> recB[Reconnaissance: Scanning]
    recB --> recC[Reconnaissance: Enumeration]
    recC --> accA[Access: Password Attack]
    recC --> accB[Access: MITM Attack]
    recC --> accC[Access: Malware Delivery]
    accA --> exp[Exploitation and Privilege Escalation]
    accB --> exp
    accC --> exp
    exp --> dosA[DoS: Single Source]
    exp --> dosB[DDoS: Botnet Mediated]
    exp --> exf[Exfiltration or Destruction]
    dosA --> impact[Impact on CIA Triad]
    dosB --> impact
    exf --> impact
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The attack progresses from left to right. Each box represents a distinct kill-chain stage as defined by the **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain**, which is mandatory for KTU Module 2.

---

## 4.2 SYN Flood — TCP State Transition

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker as Attacker / Bot
    participant Server as Target Server
    participant Client as Legitimate Client
    Attacker->>Server: SYN (SYN_SENT)
    Server->>Attacker: SYN+ACK (SYN_RCVD)
    Note over Attacker: No ACK sent
    Server->>Server: TCB retained for timeout
    Attacker->>Server: SYN (new spoofed src)
    Server->>Server: Queue fills
    Client->>Server: SYN
    Server-->>Client: Connection refused (queue full)
```

---

## 4.3 DDoS Botnet Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BH[Bot Herder / Attacker] -->|Command| C2[Command and Control Server]
    C2 -->|Instruction| Z1[Zombie Bot 1]
    C2 -->|Instruction| Z2[Zombie Bot 2]
    C2 -->|Instruction| Z3[Zombie Bot 3]
    C2 -->|Instruction| ZN[Zombie Bot N]
    Z1 -->|Flood| V[Victim Server]
    Z2 -->|Flood| V
    Z3 -->|Flood| V
    ZN -->|Flood| V
```

---

## 4.4 ARP Spoofing — MITM Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Host A: Victim] -->|ARP Request| SW[Layer 2 Switch]
    SW -->|Forwarded to Gateway| G[Real Gateway]
    X[Attacker Host] -->|Gratuitous ARP: I am Gateway| A
    A -->|All Gateway-bound traffic| X
    X -->|Modified or Forwarded traffic| G
    G -->|Internet Traffic| INT[Internet]
```

---

## 4.5 SQL Injection — Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User Input Field] --> V[Web Application Server]
    V -->|Unsanitized Query| DB[(Database Server)]
    DB -->|Auth Bypass / Data Dump| V
    V -->|Sensitive Data Returned| A[Attacker]
    note1[Attacker injects: ' OR 1=1 --] -.-> U
```

---

## 4.6 Network Attack vs Defense — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Attacker_Side
        A1[Reconnaissance Module]
        A2[Exploit Engine]
        A3[Payload / Bot]
    end
    subgraph Network_Layer
        N1[Firewall]
        N2[IDS / IPS]
        N3[Honeypot]
    end
    subgraph Victim_Side
        V1[Web Server]
        V2[Database]
        V3[Endpoint]
    end
    A1 -->|Scans| N1
    N1 -->|Filtered Traffic| N2
    N2 -->|Blocked or Logged| N3
    N3 -->|Alert| SOC[Security Operations Center]
    A2 -->|Exploit Attempt| V1
    V1 -->|Query| V2
    A3 -->|Malware Delivery| V3
```

> **Reading Note:** The **Honeypot** is a deception technology that traps attackers and alerts SOC teams. In KTU Module 2, honeypots are part of the "Attack Countermeasures" sub-topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between Passive and Active network attacks. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Definition | Monitors/captures traffic without altering it | Modifies, injects, or disrupts data |
| Visibility to victim | Generally invisible | Often leaves traces (logs, anomalies) |
| Detection difficulty | Very hard | Relatively easier |
| Impact | Confidentiality breach | Integrity & availability breach |
| **Example** | **Packet sniffing** (e.g., Wireshark on a hub) | **SYN Flood** (DoS attack) |

Passive attacks require preventive controls (encryption, VPNs); Active attacks require detective + corrective controls (IDS/IPS, firewalls).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is a Smurf Attack? Why is it called an amplification attack?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Smurf Attack** is a **DoS attack** where the attacker sends an **ICMP Echo Request** to a network's **broadcast address**, with the **victim's IP address** spoofed as the source. Every host on the broadcast network replies to the victim, generating a flood of ICMP Echo Replies.

It is called an **amplification attack** because:
- The attacker sends **1 packet**.
- The victim receives **N replies** (where $N$ = number of hosts on the broadcast network).
- The **amplification factor** $A = N / 1$ can be 100x or higher.
- **Defense**: Disable **directed broadcast** forwarding on routers.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern – Two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks]**

---

### **QUESTION A — [14 Marks] [KTU Model Paper Pattern]**

#### (a) Explain the different types of reconnaissance attacks with suitable diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Reconnaissance attacks are the **first phase of the cyber kill chain**. The attacker gathers intelligence about the target without necessarily triggering alarms. They are of two types:

**1. Passive Reconnaissance:**
- Gathering publicly available information.
- Tools/Methods: WHOIS lookup, DNS enumeration, Google Dorking (`site:ktu.edu.in filetype:pdf`), social media mining, job posting analysis.
- **No direct interaction with the target system** — extremely stealthy.

**2. Active Reconnaissance:**
- Involves **direct probing** of the target system.
- Techniques:
  - **Ping Sweep**: Uses `nmap -sP 192.168.1.0/24` to identify live hosts.
  - **Port Scanning**:
    - `TCP SYN Scan (nmap -sS)` — half-open scan.
    - `TCP Connect Scan (nmap -sT)` — full handshake.
    - `UDP Scan (nmap -sU)`.
    - `FIN / NULL / XMAS Scans` — stealth scans.
  - **OS Fingerprinting** (`nmap -O`) — determines the OS via TCP/IP stack behavior.
  - **Vulnerability Scanning** — using tools like Nessus, OpenVAS, Qualys.

**Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1 Kill-Chain):**
The reconnaissance stage sits at the **leftmost end** of the kill chain, providing the intelligence required to launch access attacks.

**Countermeasures:**
- Disable ICMP Echo replies on public-facing hosts.
- Use **IDS/IPS** with signature rules for port scans.
- Implement **honeypots** to detect and log scanners.

---

#### (b) With neat diagrams, explain the working of a SYN Flood attack. Calculate the memory consumed if the TCP backlog is 65,535 and each TCB occupies 256 bytes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of SYN Flood:**

The attacker exploits the TCP 3-way handshake by sending many **SYN** packets with **spoofed source IPs** and **never sending the final ACK**.

```
Client (Attacker)        Server
    |--- SYN (spoofed IP) ---->|
    |<-- SYN + ACK ------------|
    |   (No ACK from attacker)
    |--- SYN (another spoof) -->|
    |<-- SYN + ACK ------------|
    ...
```

The server keeps each half-open connection in a **TCP Backlog Queue**, allocating a **Transmission Control Block (TCB)**. When the queue fills, **legitimate clients are denied service**.

**Memory Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= T \times S \\
M &= 65535 \times 256 \text{ bytes} \\
M &= 16{,}776{,}960 \text{ bytes} \\
M &\approx 16 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Even a small amount of memory consumption, when sustained across millions of half-open connections (in DDoS), causes server crash or kernel panic.

**Defenses:**
- **SYN Cookies** — server does not allocate TCB until ACK is received.
- **Rate limiting** on SYN packets.
- **Firewall rules** to drop spoofed source IPs.
- **Increase backlog** (only a stop-gap, not a solution).

---

### **QUESTION B — [14 Marks] [KTU Alternative Choice]**

#### (a) Explain the working of a Man-in-the-Middle attack using ARP Spoofing. How can it be detected and prevented? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:**
A **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attack positions the attacker logically between two communicating hosts, allowing interception and modification of traffic.

**Working via ARP Spoofing:**

1. Attacker connects to the same LAN as Victim (V) and Gateway (G).
2. Attacker sends **gratuitous ARP replies** to V claiming: *"The IP of Gateway G is at MAC_Attacker."*
3. V updates its ARP cache, mapping G's IP → attacker's MAC.
4. All of V's outbound traffic goes to the attacker.
5. Attacker forwards traffic to the real G (possibly modified), and vice versa — making the attack invisible.

```
Victim  --->  Attacker  --->  Real Gateway  --->  Internet
         (intercepts)         (forwards)
```

**Detection Methods:**
- Use **ARP Watch** tools (e.g., `arpwatch` on Linux) to detect gratuitous ARP anomalies.
- Compare MAC-IP mappings with a static reference table.
- Use **Wireshark** to spot duplicate IPs with different MACs.

**Prevention Methods:**
- **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** on managed switches.
- Use **Static ARP Entries** for critical hosts.
- Implement **802.1X port-based authentication**.
- Encrypt traffic using **HTTPS, SSH, IPsec** so even if intercepted, payload remains confidential.

---

#### (b) Differentiate between a Virus, Worm, and Trojan. What are rootkits and ransomware? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Virus | Worm | Trojan |
|----------|-------|------|--------|
| **Requires host file?** | Yes | No | Disguised as one |
| **User action needed?** | Yes (to execute host) | No (self-propagating) | Yes (to install) |
| **Spreads over network?** | Limited | Yes, autonomously | Limited |
| **Replication** | Modifies other files | Copies itself | Does not replicate |
| **Example** | "ILOVEYOU" | "Code Red", "WannaCry (worm module)" | "Zeus", "Emotet" |

**Rootkits:**
- Malicious programs that gain **privileged (root/admin) access** and **hide themselves** from the OS.
- Can operate at **user-level**, **kernel-level**, or **bootkit (firmware) level**.
- Extremely difficult to detect — often require **re-installation of OS** or hardware-level forensic tools.

**Ransomware:**
- A type of malware that **encrypts the victim's files** using strong cryptography (e.g., RSA-2048).
- Demands a **ransom** (usually in cryptocurrency) for the decryption key.
- Famous examples: **WannaCry (2017)**, **NotPetya**, **LockBit**.
- Defense: **Regular backups**, **patching**, **least-privilege**, **email filtering**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Students Lose Marks in Module 2 Attacks:**
> 1. **Confusing Virus vs Worm vs Trojan** — examiners specifically test the *replication & host requirement* distinction.
> 2. **Missing units in numericals** — for SYN flood memory, always write `16 MB`, not just `16,776,960`.
> 3. **Writing "DoS = DDoS"** — DoS has **single source**, DDoS has **multiple distributed sources (botnet)**. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts. KTU norms mandate a diagram for full marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning spoofed IP** when describing SYN Flood — this is the *core mechanism*, not optional.
> 6. **ARP Spoofing vs ARP Poisoning** — these are *synonymous*; do not describe them as separate attacks.
> 7. **SQL Injection answer** — must explicitly mention **parameterized queries / prepared statements** as the defense.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Passive vs Active** attacks: Passive = eavesdrop; Active = modify/disrupt.
- **Reconnaissance** is the first kill-chain phase, divided into **passive (footprinting)** and **active (scanning)**.
- **Port scanning** types: SYN, Connect, FIN, NULL, XMAS, UDP.
- **SYN Flood** exploits TCP handshake; defense = **SYN Cookies**.
- **Smurf attack** uses ICMP broadcast with spoofed source; defense = **disable directed broadcasts**.
- **DDoS** uses **botnets** under a **C2 server**; key metric = **packets/sec & Gbps**.
- **MITM via ARP** = gratuitous ARP with spoofed IP→MAC mapping; defense = **Dynamic ARP Inspection**.
- **SQL Injection** defense = **parameterized queries** (prepared statements); never string concatenation.
- **XSS** types: Stored, Reflected, DOM-based.
- **Malware** classification: Virus (needs host), Worm (self-replicates), Trojan (disguised), Rootkit (hides), Ransomware (encrypts).
- **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — is the *goal* of every attack.
- **OSI layer** of attack matters — Layer 2 (ARP), Layer 3 (Smurf), Layer 4 (SYN Flood), Layer 7 (SQLi).
- **Amplification Factor** is a key quantitative metric: $A = \dfrac{\text{Reply packets}}{\text{Request packets}}$.
- **Defense in Depth** principle: Use multiple layers (firewall + IDS + encryption + MFA) — never rely on a single control.
- **MTTC** (Mean Time to Compromise) decreases with attacker skill — quantify risk whenever possible.
- **Zero Trust Architecture** is the modern defense philosophy: *"never trust, always verify."*

> **End of Module 2 — Network Attacks | PCCCT502 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Services and Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-2
> - **Topic:** Services and Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Security Services and Mechanisms?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (ITU-T X.800 / ISO 7498-2):**
> A **Security Service** is a service that enhances the security of the data processing systems and the information transfers of an organization. A **Security Mechanism** is the means by which one or more security services are provided. Mechanisms are the building blocks; services are the goals.

In simpler words, **services** describe *what* we want to achieve (the security goal), while **mechanisms** describe *how* we achieve it (the tools and techniques).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Bank Vault System

Imagine a high-security bank vault protecting valuables (your data):

| Bank Element | Security Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **The goal of protecting money from thieves** | Security Service (e.g., Confidentiality) |
| **The actual lock, camera, and guard** | Security Mechanism (e.g., Encipherment, Access Control) |
| **Verifying the customer's identity** | Authentication Service |
| **Keeping records of who entered** | Non-repudiation / Accountability |

A bank offers several **services** (protect, verify, record) using several **mechanisms** (locks, biometrics, CCTV). Similarly, a secure network offers security services using security mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The X.800 standard defines **5 main categories of services** and **8 specific mechanisms**. Every KTU question on this topic is based on this ITU-T recommendation. Memorize both lists and their relationships.

## 1.3 The ITUT X.800 Framework Context

The **X.800 standard** was published by **ITU-T (International Telecommunication Union – Telecommunication Standardization Sector)** in 1991 as the **Security Architecture for OSI**. It provides a systematic way to define security requirements and the mechanisms to satisfy them. This framework is **universally adopted** in modern network security textbooks (including Stallings and Forouzani) and forms the backbone of KTU Module-2 questions.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This topic is a **high-frequency, high-scoring area** in KTU examinations because:

- It contains **direct, definition-based Part A questions** (3 marks each).
- It allows examiners to ask **mapping/matching questions** (which mechanism provides which service).
- Students often **confuse services with mechanisms** — a guaranteed mark-loser pitfall that we will address in detail.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The FIVE Security Services (X.800)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember all 5:** "**A**uthentic **C**ats **D**on't **N**ap **A**fter **I**t's **D**ark" → **A**uthentication, **C**onfidentiality, **D**ata **I**ntegrity, **N**on-repudiation, **A**ccess **C**ontrol (Availability is separate).

### 2.1.1 Authentication
- **Goal:** Verify the identity of a communicating entity (peer-entity) or the source of data (data-origin).
- **Two sub-forms:**
  - **Peer-Entity Authentication:** Confirms both parties to a connection are who they claim to be.
  - **Data-Origin Authentication:** Confirms the source of a specific data unit (used in connectionless communication).
- **Real-world example:** Logging into your Gmail account with a password and OTP.

### 2.1.2 Access Control
- **Goal:** Prevent unauthorized use of a resource.
- **Includes:** Login permissions, file access rights, firewall rules.
- **Real-world example:** Your college attendance system only allows faculty to mark attendance, not students.

### 2.1.3 Data Confidentiality
- **Goal:** Protect data from unauthorized disclosure.
- **Sub-forms:**
  - **Connection Confidentiality:** Protects all user data on a connection.
  - **Connectionless Confidentiality:** Protects a single message block.
  - **Selective-Field Confidentiality:** Protects specific fields within a message.
  - **Traffic-Flow Confidentiality:** Hides the fact that traffic is flowing (who, when, how much).
- **Real-world example:** HTTPS encrypts your credit card number during online shopping.

### 2.1.4 Data Integrity
- **Goal:** Ensure that data is received exactly as sent (no modification, insertion, deletion, or replay).
- **Sub-forms:**
  - **Connection Integrity with Recovery:** Detects and recovers from modification.
  - **Connection Integrity without Recovery:** Detects but does not recover.
  - **Selective-Field Connection Integrity:** Integrity only for selected fields.
  - **Connectionless Integrity:** Integrity for a single block in connectionless service.
- **Real-world example:** Checksums on downloaded software files.

### 2.1.5 Non-Repudiation
- **Goal:** Prevent the sender (or receiver) from denying that a message was sent (or received).
- **Two sub-forms:**
  - **Non-Repudiation of Origin:** Proves the message was sent by the claimed sender.
  - **Non-Repudiation of Destination:** Proves the message was received by the claimed receiver.
- **Real-world example:** A digitally signed email cannot be denied by the sender.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Availability is often debated as a 6th service.** Stallings and Forouzani include it as a separate service (X.800 lists availability under the broader concept, but Forouzani's textbook on which KTU is based treats it as a distinct service). KTU questions frequently ask: *"List the security services"* and expect the **5 services** with **availability** sometimes listed separately.

## 2.2 The EIGHT Security Mechanisms (X.800)

### 2.2.1 Encipherment (Encryption)
- The mathematical transformation of data into an unreadable form using cryptographic algorithms (symmetric or asymmetric).
- Provides **confidentiality** primarily.

### 2.2.2 Digital Signature
- Cryptographic transformation that allows verification of:
  - The signer's identity.
  - The integrity of the signed data.
- Provides **data integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation**.

### 2.2.3 Access Control
- A variety of mechanisms that enforce access rights to resources.
- Examples: ACLs, passwords, biometrics, capability lists.

### 2.2.4 Data Integrity
- A set of techniques used to verify the integrity of data units or streams.
- Examples: Checksums, CRC, hash functions (MD5, SHA).

### 2.2.5 Authentication Exchange
- The exchange of information between entities to prove their identities.
- Examples: Passwords, challenge-response protocols, tokens.

### 2.2.6 Traffic Padding
- Insertion of bits into gaps in a data stream to frustrate traffic analysis attempts.
- Provides **traffic-flow confidentiality**.

### 2.2.7 Routing Control
- Selection of physically or logically secure routes for certain data.
- Allows data to bypass compromised nodes.

### 2.2.8 Notarization
- Use of a trusted third party (TTP) to certify certain properties of the data exchange.
- Examples: Digital certificates issued by a Certificate Authority (CA).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table

| **#** | **Service** | **Primary Mechanism(s)** | **Secondary Mechanism(s)** |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Authentication | Digital Signature, Encipherment | Authentication Exchange, Notarization |
| 2 | Access Control | Access Control Mechanism | (Encipherment can support it) |
| 3 | Data Confidentiality | Encipherment | Routing Control, Traffic Padding, Notarization |
| 4 | Data Integrity | Digital Signature, Data Integrity | Encipherment, Notarization |
| 5 | Non-Repudiation | Digital Signature, Notarization | (Data Integrity supports it) |
| 6 | Availability | (No specific mechanism in X.800) | Routing Control, Access Control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Table:** No vertical pipes were used inside the cells — only for column separation. Absolute value notation has been written as `\vert x \vert` if needed elsewhere.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

In modern engineering, these services and mechanisms are not abstract — they are the **foundation of every secure system** you build:

- **HTTPS (web security):** Uses encipherment (TLS), authentication exchange (certificates), data integrity (MACs), and non-repudiation (digital signatures).
- **Banking Apps:** Combine access control (PIN/biometric) with authentication (OTP), data integrity (transaction signing), and non-repudiation (digital receipts).
- **IoT Systems:** Use lightweight encryption (encipherment) and routing control (secure gateways) to protect sensor data.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Mapping Matrix

## 3.1 The Service-to-Mechanism Relationship (Detailed Mapping)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following mapping is the single most-tested KTU concept in this module.** Examiners love asking: *"Which mechanism provides which service?"* Master this matrix thoroughly.

### 3.1.1 Authentication Service

**Step 1: Identify the requirement.**
- We need to confirm the identity of a sender or the source of data.

**Step 2: Match with mechanisms.**
- **Encipherment:** Encrypted messages prove identity because only the legitimate user holds the decryption key.
- **Digital Signature:** A signature mathematically bound to the user's private key proves identity (origin authentication).
- **Authentication Exchange:** The act of exchanging credentials (password, OTP, challenge-response) directly authenticates the user.

**Step 3: Examples.**
- Username + Password (authentication exchange).
- Digital Certificate verification (digital signature + notarization via CA).

### 3.1.2 Access Control Service

**Step 1: Identify the requirement.**
- Decide who can use a resource and how.

**Step 2: Match with mechanisms.**
- **Access Control Mechanism:** The dedicated mechanism for this service.
- **Encipherment (secondary):** Encrypted data can only be read by authorized users.

**Step 3: Examples.**
- File permission bits in Linux (`chmod 600`).
- Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) in enterprise networks.

### 3.1.3 Data Confidentiality Service

**Step 1: Identify the requirement.**
- Prevent disclosure of data to unauthorized parties.

**Step 2: Match with mechanisms.**
- **Encipherment:** The PRIMARY and most fundamental mechanism.
- **Routing Control:** Route data through trusted paths only.
- **Traffic Padding:** Hide the patterns of communication.
- **Notarization:** A trusted third party can vouch for confidentiality in some protocols.

**Step 3: Sub-form analysis.**
- For connection confidentiality, **encipherment** is applied to the entire data stream.
- For traffic-flow confidentiality, **traffic padding** and **routing control** are essential.

### 3.1.4 Data Integrity Service

**Step 1: Identify the requirement.**
- Detect any modification, insertion, deletion, or replay of data.

**Step 2: Match with mechanisms.**
- **Data Integrity Mechanism:** Hash functions (SHA-256), MACs, CRC.
- **Digital Signature:** A signed hash provides integrity + authentication + non-repudiation.
- **Encipherment (secondary):** Modified ciphertext fails to decrypt correctly.

**Step 3: Mathematical underpinning.**
- Given message $M$, a hash function $H$ produces $h = H(M)$.
- Sender transmits $(M, h)$.
- Receiver computes $H(M)$ and compares with received $h$.
- If $H(M) \neq h$, integrity is violated.

### 3.1.5 Non-Repudiation Service

**Step 1: Identify the requirement.**
- Sender cannot deny sending; receiver cannot deny receiving.

**Step 2: Match with mechanisms.**
- **Digital Signature:** The signature is mathematically unique to the sender and the message.
- **Notarization:** A trusted third party records the transaction.

**Step 3: Mathematical underpinning.**

$$
\text{Signature} = S(H(M)) \quad \text{(Signed using sender's private key)}
$$

$$
\text{Verification: } V(M, \text{Signature}) = \text{True} \iff \text{Message came from claimed sender}
$$

## 3.2 The Complete X.800 Mapping Table (Detailed)

| **Service \ Mechanism** | **Encipherment** | **Digital Signature** | **Access Control** | **Data Integrity** | **Auth Exchange** | **Traffic Padding** | **Routing Control** | **Notarization** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Authentication** | Y | Y | — | — | Y | — | — | Y |
| **Access Control** | — | — | **Y** | — | — | — | — | — |
| **Confidentiality** | **Y** | — | — | — | — | Y | Y | Y |
| **Integrity** | Y | Y | — | **Y** | — | — | — | Y |
| **Non-Repudiation** | — | **Y** | — | Y | — | — | — | Y |

Legend: **Y** = Mechanism can provide this service. **—** = Not typically used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** **Digital Signature** is the most versatile mechanism — it can provide 3 services (Authentication, Integrity, Non-Repudiation). **Encipherment** can also support multiple services. This is a common KTU question.

## 3.3 Attacks: The Reason Services and Mechanisms Exist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The X.800 framework also categorizes **security attacks** that motivate these services. KTU Module-2 (and Module-1) often asks about attacks. Here is the consolidated view:

### 3.3.1 Passive Attacks
- **Goal:** Monitor/eavesdrop on data without modifying it.
- **Types:**
  - **Release of Message Contents:** Reading the actual data (violates confidentiality).
  - **Traffic Analysis:** Inferring information from communication patterns (violates traffic-flow confidentiality).
- **Defense:** Encipherment + Traffic Padding + Routing Control.

### 3.3.2 Active Attacks
- **Goal:** Modify data or disrupt the system.
- **Types:**
  - **Masquerade:** One entity pretends to be another (violates authentication).
  - **Replay:** Re-transmitting a captured valid message (violates integrity).
  - **Modification of Messages:** Altering part of a legitimate message (violates integrity).
  - **Denial of Service (DoS):** Preventing normal use of resources (violates availability).

## 3.4 Services-Mechanisms-Attacks Triangle (Analytical Synthesis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Framework:** Every attack is countered by a service, and every service is implemented by a mechanism. This triangle is the essence of X.800.

**Example 1: Replay Attack**
- Attack: Active, Integrity violation.
- Service needed: Data Integrity.
- Mechanism used: Sequence numbers + Data Integrity check (MAC).

**Example 2: Traffic Analysis**
- Attack: Passive, Traffic-Flow Confidentiality violation.
- Service needed: Confidentiality (Traffic-Flow).
- Mechanism used: Traffic Padding + Routing Control.

**Example 3: Masquerade**
- Attack: Active, Authentication violation.
- Service needed: Peer-Entity Authentication.
- Mechanism used: Digital Signature + Authentication Exchange.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Security Architecture of X.800 (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[X.800 Security Architecture] --> B[Security Services]
    A --> C[Security Mechanisms]
    A --> D[Security Attacks]
    B --> B1[Authentication]
    B --> B2[Access Control]
    B --> B3[Data Confidentiality]
    B --> B4[Data Integrity]
    B --> B5[Non Repudiation]
    C --> C1[Encipherment]
    C --> C2[Digital Signature]
    C --> C3[Access Control Mechanism]
    C --> C4[Data Integrity Mechanism]
    C --> C5[Authentication Exchange]
    C --> C6[Traffic Padding]
    C --> C7[Routing Control]
    C --> C8[Notarization]
    D --> D1[Passive Attacks]
    D --> D2[Active Attacks]
    D1 --> D1a[Release of Message Contents]
    D1 --> D1b[Traffic Analysis]
    D2 --> D2a[Masquerade]
    D2 --> D2b[Replay]
    D2 --> D2c[Modification of Messages]
    D2 --> D2d[Denial of Service]
```

## 4.2 Services-to-Mechanisms Mapping Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SVC1[Authentication] --> MECH1[Digital Signature]
    SVC1 --> MECH2[Encipherment]
    SVC1 --> MECH3[Authentication Exchange]
    SVC2[Access Control] --> MECH4[Access Control Mechanism]
    SVC3[Data Confidentiality] --> MECH5[Encipherment]
    SVC3 --> MECH6[Traffic Padding]
    SVC3 --> MECH7[Routing Control]
    SVC4[Data Integrity] --> MECH8[Data Integrity Mechanism]
    SVC4 --> MECH9[Digital Signature]
    SVC5[Non Repudiation] --> MECH10[Digital Signature]
    SVC5 --> MECH11[Notarization]
```

## 4.3 Attack-Defense Relationship (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ATK1[Release of Message Contents] --> SVC_A[Confidentiality Service]
    SVC_A --> MECH_A[Encipherment]
    ATK2[Traffic Analysis] --> SVC_B[Traffic Flow Confidentiality]
    SVC_B --> MECH_B[Traffic Padding]
    ATK3[Masquerade] --> SVC_C[Authentication Service]
    SVC_C --> MECH_C[Digital Signature]
    ATK4[Replay and Modification] --> SVC_D[Data Integrity Service]
    SVC_D --> MECH_D[Data Integrity Mechanism]
    ATK5[Denial of Service] --> SVC_E[Availability Service]
    SVC_E --> MECH_E[Routing Control]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Q1]** [3 Marks]
> **Q: Define the term "Security Service" as per the X.800 standard. List any two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
A **security service** is a service provided by a protocol layer of a communicating open system that ensures adequate security of the systems or of data transfers, as defined by the **ITU-T X.800 recommendation**.

The primary purpose of a security service is to counter security attacks and to enhance the security of information transfer. It describes **what** needs to be achieved.

**Two examples:**
1. **Data Confidentiality:** Protects data from unauthorized disclosure.
2. **Authentication:** Verifies the identity of a communicating entity or the source of data.

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Q2]** [3 Marks]
> **Q: Differentiate between a security service and a security mechanism.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Security Service** | **Security Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Aims to enhance the security of data transfer and processing. | The means by which one or more security services are provided. |
| **Focus** | Describes **what** to achieve (the goal). | Describes **how** to achieve it (the tool). |
| **Examples** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication. | Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control. |
| **Quantity (X.800)** | 5 main services listed. | 8 specific mechanisms listed. |
| **Analogy** | "Lock the door" (intent). | "The lock and key" (instrument). |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write *"Confidentiality is a mechanism"* or *"Encryption is a service."* This is the most common 3-mark killer. **Confidentiality = Service, Encipherment/Encryption = Mechanism.** Always use the exact X.800 terminology.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) – Module-2 Choice Option**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Q, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]** [14 Marks]
> **(a)** Explain the **five security services** defined by the **ITU-T X.800 standard**. For each service, clearly state its **goal** and give a **real-world example**. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **eight security mechanisms** defined by X.800. For each mechanism, state which **service(s)** it primarily supports, and illustrate with an example. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) – The Five Security Services**

**1. Authentication**
- **Goal:** Verify the identity of a communicating entity (peer-entity) or the source of data (data-origin).
- **Sub-types:** Peer-entity authentication and Data-origin authentication.
- **Example:** Logging into a banking app using a username and OTP. The system verifies that you are the legitimate account holder.

**2. Access Control**
- **Goal:** Prevent unauthorized use of a resource. Restrict access to systems, data, or applications to authorized users only.
- **Example:** In a college ERP, only the HOD can approve student leave applications. Students cannot, even if they are logged in.

**3. Data Confidentiality**
- **Goal:** Protect data from unauthorized disclosure during transmission or storage.
- **Sub-types:** Connection confidentiality, connectionless confidentiality, selective-field confidentiality, and traffic-flow confidentiality.
- **Example:** When you make an online purchase, HTTPS encrypts your credit card number so that a network sniffer cannot read it.

**4. Data Integrity**
- **Goal:** Ensure that the data received is exactly what was sent — no modification, insertion, deletion, or replay.
- **Sub-types:** Connection integrity (with/without recovery) and connectionless integrity.
- **Example:** When downloading software, the SHA-256 hash provided on the website lets you verify the file was not corrupted or tampered with in transit.

**5. Non-Repudiation**
- **Goal:** Prevent the sender from denying that a message was sent (origin) and the receiver from denying that a message was received (destination).
- **Sub-types:** Non-repudiation of origin and non-repudiation of destination.
- **Example:** A digitally signed contract email — the sender cannot later deny sending it, and the receiver cannot deny receiving it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Availability** is sometimes considered a sixth service. KTU accepts 5 (strict X.800) or 5 + availability depending on the textbook reference (Forouzani includes availability explicitly).

#### **Part (b) – The Eight Security Mechanisms**

| **#** | **Mechanism** | **Primary Service** | **Example** |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Encipherment (Encryption)** | Confidentiality | AES encryption of a file before sending. |
| 2 | **Digital Signature** | Non-Repudiation, Integrity, Authentication | RSA-signed software updates. |
| 3 | **Access Control Mechanism** | Access Control | Linux `chmod` permissions. |
| 4 | **Data Integrity Mechanism** | Data Integrity | SHA-256 hash verification. |
| 5 | **Authentication Exchange** | Authentication | Password + OTP login. |
| 6 | **Traffic Padding** | Traffic-Flow Confidentiality | Sending random dummy packets to obscure real traffic patterns. |
| 7 | **Routing Control** | Confidentiality, Availability | Using a VPN tunnel for sensitive data. |
| 8 | **Notarization** | Non-Repudiation, Authentication | Digital certificate issued by a Certificate Authority (CA). |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When listing mechanisms, students often miss **Traffic Padding**, **Routing Control**, and **Notarization** because these are less commonly seen in real apps. Examiners specifically check for all 8. Memorize the full list.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Module-2 Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Q, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]** [14 Marks]
> **(a)** What are **passive** and **active security attacks**? Explain with suitable examples. How does the X.800 framework recommend countering each category? **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Construct a **detailed mapping matrix** showing the relationship between the **five security services** and the **eight security mechanisms** of X.800. Justify why **Digital Signature** is considered the most versatile mechanism. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) – Passive vs. Active Attacks**

**Passive Attacks:**
- **Nature:** Monitor or eavesdrop on data transmission without modifying the data. The attacker observes and learns.
- **Detection:** Very difficult to detect because they do not alter the data.
- **Countering:** Prevented, not detected. The X.800 framework uses **encipherment** to hide the message content, **traffic padding** to obscure traffic patterns, and **routing control** to use secure paths.
- **Types:**
  1. **Release of Message Contents:** A snooper reads your email contents.
  2. **Traffic Analysis:** A snooper infers your communication frequency, partners, or location without reading the actual content.

**Active Attacks:**
- **Nature:** Modify the data stream or create a false stream. Involve **modification, fabrication, or interruption**.
- **Detection:** Easier to detect (though not always prevent).
- **Countering:** X.800 uses **digital signatures** (for authentication and non-repudiation), **data integrity mechanisms** (hashes, MACs), **authentication exchange** (to verify identity), and **notarization** (trusted third parties). DoS attacks are countered by **routing control** and **access control**.
- **Types:**
  1. **Masquerade:** An attacker pretends to be a legitimate user by stealing credentials.
  2. **Replay:** An attacker captures and re-sends a valid message to gain unauthorized access.
  3. **Modification of Messages:** An attacker alters part of a legitimate message (e.g., changing the bank account number in a transaction).
  4. **Denial of Service (DoS):** An attacker overwhelms a server with traffic, making it unavailable to legitimate users.

**Comparison Summary:**

| **Property** | **Passive Attacks** | **Active Attacks** |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Observation only | Modification/Fabrication |
| **Effect on Data** | Unchanged | Changed/Forged |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Hard | Easier |
| **Defense Strategy** | Prevention (encryption) | Detection + Recovery |
| **Example** | Eavesdropping on Wi-Fi | Man-in-the-Middle attack |

#### **Part (b) – Mapping Matrix & Justification**

**Complete Service-Mechanism Mapping Matrix:**

| **Service \ Mechanism** | **Encipherment** | **Digital Signature** | **Access Control** | **Data Integrity** | **Auth Exchange** | **Traffic Padding** | **Routing Control** | **Notarization** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Authentication** | Y | **Y** | — | — | **Y** | — | — | Y |
| **Access Control** | — | — | **Y** | — | — | — | — | — |
| **Confidentiality** | **Y** | — | — | — | — | Y | Y | Y |
| **Integrity** | Y | **Y** | — | **Y** | — | — | — | Y |
| **Non-Repudiation** | — | **Y** | — | Y | — | — | — | **Y** |

**Why Digital Signature is the Most Versatile Mechanism:**

A **Digital Signature** is the most versatile mechanism in the X.800 framework because a single signature can simultaneously provide **three of the five security services**:

1. **Authentication (Data-Origin):** The signature is mathematically bound to the sender's private key, so verification proves the identity of the sender.

2. **Data Integrity:** The signature is computed over a hash of the message. If the message is altered, the hash changes, and the signature verification fails.

3. **Non-Repudiation:** Because the private key is known only to the sender and the signature cannot be forged, the sender cannot later deny having signed the message.

By combining a **hash function** (to ensure integrity) with an **asymmetric encryption** operation (to ensure authentication and non-repudiation), a digital signature efficiently achieves three security goals in a single mechanism. No other mechanism in the X.800 list provides this level of multi-service coverage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked "Why is digital signature versatile?", students often answer vaguely like *"because it is secure."* Examiners expect the **specific three services** listed above with a clear explanation. Also, some students confuse **non-repudiation with integrity** — they are distinct: integrity detects changes; non-repudiation prevents denial.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **5 Security Services (X.800):** Authentication, Access Control, Data Confidentiality, Data Integrity, Non-Repudiation. (Availability is sometimes considered a 6th.)
- **8 Security Mechanisms (X.800):** Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control, Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization.
- **Digital Signature is the most versatile mechanism** — provides authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation.
- **Encipherment is the primary mechanism for confidentiality.**
- **Authentication Exchange is a mechanism, not a service** — a common confusion.
- **Passive attacks** are countered by **prevention** (encryption, padding, routing).
- **Active attacks** are countered by **detection and recovery** (signatures, hashes, notarization).
- **X.800 standard** is from **ITU-T (1991)** for OSI security architecture.
- **Authentication has two sub-types:** Peer-Entity and Data-Origin.
- **Confidentiality has four sub-types:** Connection, Connectionless, Selective-Field, Traffic-Flow.
- **Non-Repudiation has two sub-types:** Origin and Destination.
- Always use **X.800 standard terminology** in KTU answers — avoid casual terms like "hiding" or "locking" for confidentiality and access control respectively.
- **The mapping matrix is the most-tested KTU concept** — practice drawing it from memory.
- **Traffic Padding, Routing Control, and Notarization** are the three mechanisms most often forgotten by students — examiners specifically test these.
- **Availability** has no specific dedicated mechanism in X.800 but is supported indirectly by routing control, access control, and redundancy mechanisms.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Security Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Security Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Network Security Model:** A *general model* of network security provides a standardized framework that defines (a) the **security goals** that must be achieved over an open network, (b) the **threats / attacks** that endanger those goals, and (c) the **services and mechanisms** that counter those attacks. It is the conceptual blueprint used to design, analyze, and reason about secure communication between two end systems across an insecure channel.

According to the classical treatment found in network security references, the model is described as:

> *"All communications between two parties need to be secured. The security of the information transferred between two parties over a network must ensure that an unauthorized third party (attacker / opponent) cannot read, alter, or fabricate the information while it is in transit."*

The model therefore identifies **four canonical actors**:

1. **Sender** — origin of the data / message.
2. **Receiver** — legitimate destination of the data.
3. **Secure Channel** — the (logical) path over which protected information flows.
4. **Trusted Third Party (TTP)** — an external, mutually-trusted authority (e.g., a Key Distribution Center or Certificate Authority) that supplies the *secrets* (keys, certificates) needed to secure the channel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024 Examinations:**
> The Network Security Model is the *mother* of all crypto-protocol questions. Every protocol you will study (DES, AES, RSA, Diffie–Hellman, Digital Signatures, Kerberos, IPSec) is a specific *instantiation* of this general model. Memorising the four components and the classification of attacks/services/mechanisms earns you easy marks.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — "The Royal Letter" Analogy

Imagine a king (Sender) who wants to send a secret battle plan to his general (Receiver) across enemy territory (the insecure network channel). The king's advisor — a high-priest who lives in a distant temple — is trusted by both sides and can hand out wax-sealed letter boxes and the matching keys (the Trusted Third Party).

- The king **writes the letter** (plaintext).
- He **puts it in a lock-box** with a key only the general possesses (encryption using a secret key).
- A **messenger runs through enemy territory** (transmission over the network).
- The messenger is stopped by bandits (the **Opponent/Attacker**) who want to read, copy, change, or destroy the letter.
- The general **unlocks the box** with his key (decryption) and reads the plan.
- The **high-priest's seal** on the box assures both sides that the box is genuine (digital signatures / certificates).

The boxes, locks, seals, and the high-priest's role together form the **Network Security Model** — the *abstract design pattern* that we instantiate with actual cryptographic primitives.

### 1.3 Why the Model is Needed

A network is an **open, shared medium** (e.g., the Internet, Wi-Fi, Ethernet) where any adversary with physical or logical access can:

- **Eavesdrop** (read the bits)
- **Inject** (insert forged packets)
- **Replay** (re-send old messages)
- **Modify** (tamper with bits in flight)
- **Block / delay** (denial of service)

Because the channel itself cannot be made *physically* secure across thousands of kilometers, security must be **provided by the endpoints using cryptography** — and that is exactly what the network security model formalises.

### 1.4 Core Security Goals (The "CIA-Triad Plus")

| # | Goal | Meaning |
|---|------|---------|
| 1 | **Confidentiality** | Only authorised parties can read the message. |
| 2 | **Integrity** | The message has *not been altered* in transit. |
| 3 | **Authentication** | The sender is *who they claim* to be. |
| 4 | **Non-Repudiation** | Sender *cannot later deny* having sent the message. |
| 5 | **Access Control** | Only authorised users get *access* to resources. |
| 6 | **Availability** | Service remains *usable* when needed (anti-DoS). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above six services are the *direct* exam-relevant output of the network security model. You will be tested on the difference between a *service* (what is achieved) and a *mechanism* (how it is achieved).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Model — Step-by-Step Logic

The general model (Stallings-style) executes the following logical pipeline:

1. **Identification of a security goal** (e.g., confidentiality of email body).
2. **Selection of a security service** that satisfies that goal (e.g., confidentiality service).
3. **Selection of a security mechanism** that implements the service (e.g., symmetric encryption using AES).
4. **Identification of the appropriate location** in the protocol stack where the mechanism must be inserted (application layer, transport layer, or network layer).
5. **Engagement of a Trusted Third Party** if a secret (key) needs to be distributed in advance.
6. **Deployment of the mechanism on both endpoints** so that:
   - The sender applies the transformation (encryption) before transmission.
   - The receiver applies the inverse transformation (decryption) after reception.
7. **Continuous monitoring** for new attacks that might defeat the chosen mechanism.

### 2.2 The Two Sub-Models

KTU 2024 expects you to distinguish two complementary sub-models:

#### 2.2.1 Model A — General Information Transfer (Data-in-Transit)
Covers the situation where *two parties* (e.g., Alice and Bob) want to communicate securely across an *insecure channel*, possibly with the help of a *Trusted Third Party* to distribute keys in advance.

#### 2.2.2 Model B — Network Access (Data-at-Rest / Access Control)
Covers the situation where a *principal* (user/process) wants to access *objects* (files, services, hosts) across a *network access channel*. An **access policy** enforced by a **reference monitor / firewall** blocks unauthorised access. A Trusted Third Party may authenticate the principal.

### 2.3 The Triangle: Attacks → Services → Mechanisms

This is the *single most important diagram* in the entire module:

> **Attacks are the problem. Services are the goals. Mechanisms are the solutions.**

| Threat (Attack) | Security Service that counters it | Typical Mechanism used |
|------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------|
| Interception | Confidentiality | Encipherment (Symmetric / Asymmetric Encryption) |
| Interruption | Availability | Redundancy, fail-over, traffic-policing |
| Modification | Integrity | Hashing (SHA-256), MAC, digital signatures |
| Fabrication | Authentication | Digital signatures, message authentication codes |
| Impersonation | Authentication + Non-Repudiation | Certificates from TTP, digital signatures |
| Replay | Freshness | Timestamps, nonces, sequence numbers |
| Denial-of-Service | Availability | Rate-limiting, firewalls, DDoS scrubbing |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although the Network Security Model is a *conceptual* topic, the following relationships are the **canonical equations** a KTU 2024 examiner loves to write on the board.

$$
\text{Security} \;=\; f(\text{Attacks}, \text{Services}, \text{Mechanisms})
$$

The model demands:

$$
\text{Goal achieved} \iff \exists \; \text{Service} \; s \;\text{such that}\; s \text{ counters the attack using a valid mechanism.}
$$

Formally, the protected message transformation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= E_K(M) \quad \text{(sender applies encryption)} \\
M &= D_K(C) \quad \text{(receiver applies decryption)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The information flow over the channel is:

$$
\text{Sender} \;\xrightarrow{\;E_K(M)\;}\; \text{Channel} \;\xrightarrow{\;E_K(M)\;}\; \text{Receiver}
$$

For the integrity-protected variant:

$$
\text{Tag} = H(M \mid\mid K) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Receiver verifies: } H(M \mid\mid K) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Tag}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Domain |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $M$ | Plaintext message | bits |
| $C$ | Ciphertext | bits |
| $K$ | Secret key | bits (e.g., 128-bit AES key) |
| $E_K(\cdot)$ | Encryption function with key $K$ | deterministic or randomised |
| $D_K(\cdot)$ | Decryption function with key $K$ | inverse of $E_K$ |
| $H(\cdot)$ | Cryptographic hash function | output: $n$-bit digest |
| $\mid\mid$ | Concatenation operator | string / bit-string |
| TTP | Trusted Third Party | e.g., KDC, CA |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 answer-sheets, **always** show the **direction of the arrow** and **label the transformation** explicitly. The examiner awards a mark each for the *sender-side transformation*, the *receiver-side inverse transformation*, and the *attack vector*. Missing any one of these costs you a 14-mark question to a 7.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Where the Model is Used |
|-------|--------------------------|
| **Banking (NEFT / SWIFT)** | Confidentiality + Integrity for money-transfer packets. |
| **5G Telecom (3GPP)** | AKA (Authentication and Key Agreement) protocol — exact instantiation of TTP-based model. |
| **IoT / Smart Grid** | Mutual authentication between sensor and cloud gateway. |
| **Cloud (AWS / Azure)** | TLS handshake, IAM, KMS — all built on the trusted-third-party sub-model. |
| **E-Governance (DigiLocker)** | Aadhaar-signed e-documents use the integrity + non-repudiation services. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Secure Email Exchange via the Network Security Model

**Scenario.** Alice wants to send a confidential, tamper-proof, signed e-mail "M" to Bob. Eve is an attacker on the network. Trent is the Trusted Third Party (e.g., a Certificate Authority).

**Step 1 — Define the Goal.** Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation.

**Step 2 — Map Goal to Services.** Confidentiality $\to$ encryption service. Integrity $\to$ message-integrity service. Authentication & Non-Repudiation $\to$ digital-signature service.

**Step 3 — Select Mechanisms.**
- Encryption: Symmetric AES with session key $K_s$ (fast for bulk data).
- Key protection: RSA public-key encryption of $K_s$ using Bob's public key $PU_B$.
- Integrity: SHA-256 hash of message.
- Signature: RSA private-key signing of the hash using Alice's private key $PR_A$.

**Step 4 — Alice's Transformations (Sender Side).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4a: Hash} &\quad h = H(M) \\
\text{Step 4b: Sign} &\quad s = E_{PR_A}(h) \quad \text{(Alice's private key)} \\
\text{Step 4c: Concatenate} &\quad (M \mid\mid s) \\
\text{Step 4d: Symmetric encrypt} &\quad C = E_{K_s}(M \mid\mid s) \\
\text{Step 4e: Protect the session key} &\quad C_K = E_{PU_B}(K_s) \quad \text{(Bob's public key)} \\
\text{Step 4f: Transmit} &\quad (C, C_K) \;\text{over the channel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Bob's Inverse Transformations (Receiver Side).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 5a: Recover session key} &\quad K_s = D_{PR_B}(C_K) \\
\text{Step 5b: Decrypt} &\quad (M \mid\mid s) = D_{K_s}(C) \\
\text{Step 5c: Verify signature} &\quad h' = D_{PU_A}(s) \quad \text{(Alice's public key)} \\
\text{Step 5d: Re-hash} &\quad h = H(M) \\
\text{Step 5e: Compare} &\quad h \stackrel{?}{=} h' \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Integrity + Authenticity verified.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Eve's Possible Attacks (and Why They Fail).**

| Eve's Attack | What Eve gets | Why it fails |
|--------------|---------------|--------------|
| Interception of $C$ | Ciphertext only | $K_s$ unknown; cannot decrypt |
| Modification of $C$ | Tampered ciphertext | Hash comparison at Step 5e fails |
| Fabrication of new $(C', C_K')$ | She needs $PR_B$ | She does not have Bob's private key |
| Replay of old $(C, C_K)$ | Old session key | Session keys are short-lived + timestamped |
| Impersonation as Alice | She needs $PR_A$ | Cannot sign without Alice's private key |

> [!NOTE]
> The above 6-step walk-through is the *complete* model in action. If your KTU 2024 answer is a flow like this with **arrow directions clearly shown**, the examiner will give full 14 marks.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Every Security Service to its Mechanism and Attack

| Service | Goal | Underlying Mechanism | Attack Defeated | Mathematical Basis |
|---------|------|----------------------|-----------------|--------------------|
| Confidentiality | Hide content from third parties | Encipherment (Symmetric or Asymmetric) | Interception, traffic analysis | $C = E_K(M)$; AES, 3DES, RSA |
| Integrity | Detect bit-flips | Hash + MAC, Digital Signature | Modification | $h = H(M)$; SHA family |
| Authentication | Verify identity of sender | Passwords, certificates, digital signatures | Impersonation, fabrication | Challenge-response, PKI |
| Non-Repudiation | Prevent denial of sending | Asymmetric digital signature | Repudiation | $s = E_{PR_A}(H(M))$ |
| Access Control | Decide who can do what | ACLs, RBAC, capability lists | Unauthorised access | Policy look-up: $\text{perm}(u,r) \in ACL$ |
| Availability | Keep service alive | Redundancy, replication, rate-limits | Interruption, DoS, DDoS | $P_{\text{up}} \geq 1 - P_{\text{fail}}^{\,n}$ |
| Notarisation | Time-stamp & certify a message | TTP signs a hash of $(M \mid\mid t)$ | Disputes, fabrication | $\text{stamp} = E_{PR_{\text{TTP}}}(H(M \mid\mid t))$ |
| Key Management | Safely distribute/refresh keys | KDC, Kerberos, Diffie–Hellman | All key-recovery attacks | $K_{AB} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ |

### 3.3 Quantitative Worked Example — Cost of Insecure Channel

Suppose AES-128 is the chosen mechanism. The key size is $k = 128$ bits, so the brute-force attack complexity is:

$$
\text{Work factor} = 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38} \text{ key trials}
$$

If an attacker uses a GPU cluster at $10^{15}$ keys/second:

$$
T_{\text{break}} = \frac{2^{128}}{10^{15}} \approx 1.08 \times 10^{16} \text{ years}
$$

> This quantifies *why* the network security model picks AES-128 as a sufficient mechanism to deliver the confidentiality service.

### 3.4 The Information-Theoretic Interpretation

A network security model succeeds when the attacker's mutual information about the plaintext given the ciphertext is zero:

$$
I(M; C) = H(M) - H(M \mid C) = 0
$$

In a perfectly-secret (one-time-pad) system this is exactly zero. In a computationally-secure (AES) system:

$$
I(M; C) \;\approx\; 0 \quad \text{(negligibly small for any polynomial-time adversary)}
$$

This is the *formal definition of confidentiality* in the model.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — General Network Security Model (Sender–Receiver–TTP)

```mermaid
graph LR
    senderA[Sender Alice] -->|Step 1 Encrypt and Sign M| channelB[Insecure Network Channel]
    channelB -->|Step 2 Transmit C and Ck| receiverC[Receiver Bob]
    ttpD[Trusted Third Party Trent] -.->|Step 0 Distribute Public Keys or Session Keys| senderA
    ttpD -.->|Step 0 Distribute Public Keys or Session Keys| receiverC
    attackerE[Opponent Eve] -.->|Interception Modification Fabrication Replay| channelB
```
