| **HLR** | Home Location Register | Subscriber database (Ki, IMSI) |
| **AuC** | Authentication Center | Generates triplets |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 GPRS Security Architecture — Layered View

GPRS security can be understood at **three distinct planes**:

#### A. The Radio Access Network (RAN) Security
This covers the **Um** (MS ↔ BTS) and **Gb** (BTS ↔ SGSN) interfaces.
- Confidentiality: **GEA/0 (none), GEA/1, GEA/2, GEA/3, GEA/4** algorithms.
- **NO integrity protection** on the radio path.
- Key used: **GPRS-Kc** (a 64-bit ciphering key derived from the GSM Kc).

#### B. The Backbone Security
This covers the **Gn/Gp** interface (SGSN ↔ GGSN, and between SGSNs).
- Uses **GPRS Tunneling Protocol (GTP)**.
- **No native security** in standard GTP — relies on operator's private backbone or external IPSec.
- This is a major vulnerability: traffic between SGSNs/GGSNs is often cleartext inside operator VPNs.

#### C. The SIM / Smart Card Security
- The **Ki** (subscriber authentication key) is **128-bit**, stored in the SIM and the AuC.
- **Never transmitted over the air.**
- Algorithms used: **A3 (Authentication)**, **A8 (Key Generation)**, and for GPRS indirectly the **COMP128** family.

### 2.2 GPRS Security Mechanisms — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3GPP TS 43.020** standard governs the GPRS security framework. The 4 primary services are:
> 1. **User Identity Confidentiality** — IMSI is rarely sent; a temporary **P-TMSI (Packet TMSI)** is used instead.
> 2. **User Identity Authentication** — One-way only (network authenticates user).
> 3. **User Data Confidentiality** — From SGSN to MS (radio leg only).
> 4. **Signaling Data Element Confidentiality** — From SGSN to MS only.

**Step-by-step of the GPRS attach procedure (security-relevant portions):**

1. **MS** powers on → **BTS** broadcasts Location Area Code (LAC) and Routing Area Code (RAC).
2. **MS** requests attachment to **SGSN** using its **P-TMSI** (or IMSI if first attach).
3. **SGSN** requests authentication triplets **(RAND, SRES, Kc)** from the **HLR/AuC**.
4. **SGSN** sends **RAND** to MS.
5. **MS** computes inside the SIM:
   - $XRES = A3(K_i, RAND)$  (using subscriber key $K_i$)
   - $K_c = A8(K_i, RAND)$  (64-bit ciphering key)
6. **MS** sends **SRES** back to SGSN.
7. **SGSN** compares SRES with the XRES from the HLR — match means authentication succeeds.
8. If ciphering is negotiated, the **LLC (Logical Link Control) layer** frames are encrypted using **GEA** with key **Kc**.

### 2.3 The GEA Family (GPRS Encryption Algorithms)

| Algorithm | Status | Effective Key | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GEA/0** | No encryption | 0 bits | Used when export restrictions applied |
| **GEA/1** | **BROKEN** (2011, Karri Nohl) | 64 bits | 40-bit effective due to weak design |
| **GEA/2** | **BROKEN** (same) | 64 bits | 40-bit effective |
| **GEA/3** | Proprietary, stronger | 64 bits | Used in modern GPRS/EDGE |
| **GEA/4** | Proprietary, stronger | 128-bit Kc | Modern deployments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Public Disclosure 2011:** Researchers Karsten Nohl and Luca Melette publicly broke GEA/1 and GEA/2 at the 26th Chaos Communication Congress (26C3). The algorithm had a backdoor-like weakness that reduced the effective keyspace to **40 bits**, allowing real-time decryption on commodity hardware.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Notation | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Authentication Response | $SRES = A3(K_i, RAND)$ | $SRES$ is 32 bits |
| Ciphering Key Generation | $K_c = A8(K_i, RAND)$ | $K_c$ is 64 bits (only 40 effective in GEA/1,2) |
| Ciphering Operation | $C_i = GEA(K_c, \vert FRAME \vert, \vert DIRECTION \vert, \vert HFN \vert)$ | Block cipher in OFB-like mode |
| TMSI Refresh | $P\text{-}TMSI_{new} = f(TMSI, LAI, Time)$ | Sent only after successful re-auth |
| Triplet | $\{RAND, SRES, K_c\}$ | Generated by AuC, 128/32/64 bits |
| Ciphering Key Length | $\vert K_c \vert = 64 \text{ bits}$ | Logically; effectively 40 bits for GEA/1,2 |
| Pseudonym IMSI Masking | $\text{Use } P\text{-}TMSI \text{ instead of } IMSI$ | IMSI sent only at first attach / paging fail |

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Telecom operators** must consider that legacy GPRS/2.5G SIMs still in IoT devices, smart meters, vehicle trackers, and SCADA telemetry are vulnerable to GEA/1-2 attacks.
- **Penetration testers** demonstrate IMSI-catching, GPRS session hijacking, and SGSN-side attacks.
- **Forensics investigators** use the P-TMSI ↔ IMSI mapping to track devices.
- **The cryptographic lesson** is critical: any cipher deliberately weakened for export-control reasons becomes a permanent weak link even after restrictions are lifted.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Key Strength of GEA/1 and GEA/2

GPRS design philosophy intended a **64-bit ciphering key (Kc)**. However, due to cryptographic weaknesses exposed by Nohl in 2011:

$$
K_{c,\text{logical}} = 64 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
K_{c,\text{effective}} = 40 \text{ bits}
$$

The reason is that the remaining 24 bits of the key could be recovered through a chosen-IV attack in approximately **2^{15}** chosen plaintexts. Let us denote the structure of GEA/1 conceptually:

$$
GEA/1 : \{0,1\}^{64} \times \{0,1\}^{32} \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{32}
$$

Mapping the IV construction:

$$
IV = DIRECTION \vert \vert FRAME\_NUMBER \vert \vert 0^{19}
$$

The internal state of the LFSR (Linear Feedback Shift Register) of GEA/1 is only 40 bits deep, even though 64 bits are loaded. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State size} &= 40 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Key space} &= 2^{40} \\
\text{Time to break} &\approx O(2^{40}) \text{ operations} \approx \text{minutes on GPU clusters}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: GPRS Triplet Generation Cost

The HLR/AuC must generate triplets in advance. If a subscriber has $N$ simultaneous packet sessions and the operator sets a re-authentication period of $T$ minutes, the triplet generation rate is:

$$
R_{\text{triplets}} = \frac{N}{T} \text{ triplets per minute}
$$

Each triplet requires **2 cryptographic computations** (one $A3$ + one $A8$):

$$
C_{\text{AuC}} = 2 \cdot R_{\text{triplets}} = \frac{2N}{T} \text{ crypto-ops/min}
$$

For an operator with $N = 10^7$ subscribers and $T = 30$ minutes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{AuC}} &= \frac{2 \times 10^7}{30} \\
&= 6.67 \times 10^5 \text{ crypto-ops/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well within modern HLR hardware, but it shows why **triplet pre-computation** is feasible and why attackers who compromise the HLR have a cryptographic goldmine.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Threat vs. Countermeasure

| Threat / Attack | Target | Countermeasure in GPRS | Residual Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IMSI Catching** | User identity | P-TMSI reallocation | IMSI sent at first attach; P-TMSI ↔ IMSI mapping in SGSN |
| **Cipher Key Reuse** | $K_c$ across calls | Triplet rotation | $K_c$ reused during single session; weak keyspace in GEA/1,2 |
| **Man-in-the-Middle** | MS ↔ SGSN | No native defense | One-way auth — MS cannot validate SGSN |
| **False BTS / IMSI Catcher** | Radio leg | Operator-side detection | MS has no way to validate a legitimate BTS |
| **Replay Attack** | RAND, SRES | Sequence numbers partially | Old SRES values can be replayed within window |
| **Backbone Sniffing (Gn/Gp)** | GTP tunnel traffic | External IPSec | Most operators skip this on internal backbones |
| **DoS on SGSN** | GPRS attach flood | Rate limiting | PDP context activation flooding possible |
| **COMP128-1 Attack** | SIM cloning | COMP128-2/3 | Older SIMs vulnerable; $K_i$ extractable in hours |

### 3.4 Derivation: GPRS Ciphering Modes (GEA Operation)

The GEA algorithms operate in a stream-cipher mode, conceptually similar to **OFB (Output Feedback Mode)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_0 &= f(K_c, IV) \\
S_i &= GEA_{\text{core}}(S_{i-1}) \\
C_i &= P_i \oplus S_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $S_i$ = $i$-th state of the cipher
- $P_i$ = $i$-th plaintext block (32 bits)
- $C_i$ = $i$-th ciphertext block
- $IV$ = Initialization Vector derived from frame number and direction bit

**Critical observation:** Since the IV depends only on publicly visible values (frame counter, direction), if the keystream is ever recovered, the entire session can be decrypted by simple XOR:

$$
P_i = C_i \oplus S_i
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GPRS Network Architecture with Security Boundaries

```mermaid
graph TB
    MS["MS Mobile Station with SIM"] -->|"Um radio encrypted with GEA and Kc"| BTS["BTS Base Transceiver Station"]
    BTS -->|"Gb A-bis interface"| BSC["BSC Base Station Controller"]
    BSC -->|"Gb cleartext in many deployments"| SGSN["SGSN Serving GPRS Support Node"]
    SGSN -->|"Gn GTP no native security"| GGSN["GGSN Gateway GPRS Support Node"]
    SGSN -->|"MAP SS7 mostly secure"| HLR["HLR Home Location Register"]
    HLR -->|"Internal link"| AuC["AuC Authentication Center Ki storage"]
    GGSN -->|"Gi to external network"| Internet["Internet External IP Network"]
    
    SGSN -.->|"Generates triplet RAND SRES Kc"| AuC
    MS -.->|"Stores Ki runs A3 A8 locally"| AuC
```

### 4.2 GPRS Authentication and Key Agreement Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MS as Mobile Station SIM
    participant BTS as BTS Radio
    participant SGSN as SGSN
    participant HLR as HLR or AuC

    Note over MS,SGSN: Step 1 Attach Request with P-TMSI or IMSI
    MS->>SGSN: Attach Request P-TMSI
    SGSN->>HLR: Send Auth Info Request
    HLR->>HLR: Generate Triplet RAND, SRES, Kc
    HLR->>SGSN: Send Authentication Triplet
    SGSN->>MS: Authentication Request RAND
    MS->>MS: Compute SRES = A3 of Ki and RAND
    MS->>MS: Compute Kc = A8 of Ki and RAND
    MS->>SGSN: Authentication Response SRES
    SGSN->>SGSN: Verify SRES equals expected SRES
    SGSN->>MS: Ciphering Mode Command negotiate GEA
    Note over MS,SGSN: Step 2 Radio traffic now encrypted with GEA and Kc on Um
    SGSN->>MS: Attach Accept with new P-TMSI
```

### 4.3 GPRS Protocol Stack and Security Coverage

```mermaid
graph TB
    APP["Application Layer HTTP SMTP FTP"]
    IP["IP Network Layer"]
    SNDCP["SNDCP Subnetwork Dependent Convergence"]
    LLC["LLC Logical Link Control 64 bit Kc ciphering point"]
    RLC["RLC Radio Link Control"]
    MAC["MAC Medium Access Control"]
    GSM["GSM Physical Layer Um radio interface"]

    APP --> IP --> SNDCP --> LLC --> RLC --> MAC --> GSM

    SEC["GPRS Security Domain GEA ciphering and GPRS AKA"] -.->|"Applies at LLC layer"| LLC
    SEC -.->|"Authentication at attach"| SNDCP
```

### 4.4 Attack Surface Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["IMSI Catcher on Um"] -->|"Eavesdrops IMSI and P-TMSI"| V1["Loss of identity confidentiality"]
    B["Replay of old SRES"] -->|"Fake attach"| V2["Unauthorized network access"]
    C["Backbone Sniffing on Gn"] -->|"Cleartext GTP payload"| V3["User data disclosure"]
    D["COMP128 Attack on SIM"] -->|"Extracts Ki"| V4["Full SIM cloning"]
    E["GEA 1 2 break"] -->|"Decrypts Um traffic"| V5["Real time session decryption"]
    F["False BTS"] -->|"Downgrade attack"| V6["Forces GEA 0 no encryption"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**List the major security vulnerabilities of GPRS and explain why GEA/1 and GEA/2 are considered insecure.**

**Model Answer:**

The major security vulnerabilities of GPRS are:

1. **Weak cipher algorithms:** GEA/1 and GEA/2 have an effective key size of only **40 bits** instead of the logical 64 bits, as disclosed by Nohl in 2011, making real-time decryption feasible.
2. **One-way authentication:** Only the network authenticates the user; the MS cannot verify the legitimacy of the SGSN, opening doors to **false BTS** attacks.
3. **No signaling integrity:** Control messages can be tampered with on the radio path without detection.
4. **IMSI exposure:** The IMSI is transmitted in cleartext during the first attach or when P-TMSI fails.
5. **Backbone exposure:** The Gn/Gp interface uses **GTP without native security**, exposing user data inside operator backbones.
6. **COMP128 weaknesses:** Older SIMs allow Ki extraction via physical access, enabling SIM cloning.

GEA/1 and GEA/2 are insecure because their internal LFSR state is only **40 bits**, and the remaining 24 bits of Kc are recoverable through a chosen-IV attack, reducing the brute-force space to $2^{40}$, easily breakable in minutes.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for listing vulnerabilities, 1.5 for GEA explanation)*

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]
**What is the role of P-TMSI in GPRS? How is it different from IMSI in terms of security?**

**Model Answer:**

**P-TMSI (Packet Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity)** is a 32-bit temporary identifier allocated by the SGSN to a mobile station after the first successful authentication. It is used in all subsequent signaling messages on the radio path instead of the permanent **IMSI (International Mobile Subscriber Identity)**.

**Security differences:**

- **IMSI** is a permanent, globally unique identifier tied to the subscriber's identity. Sending it over the air allows an eavesdropper to track the user permanently.
- **P-TMSI** is temporary, locally significant (only within a Routing Area), and **reallocated** whenever the MS moves to a new SGSN, making passive tracking harder.
- However, P-TMSI ↔ IMSI mapping exists in the SGSN database, so a compromised SGSN breaks the privacy.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for security comparison)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **omit the math** (e.g., $SRES = A3(K_i, RAND)$) and lose 2 marks.
> 2. Many confuse **GTP** with **GTPsec** — GTP itself has *no* security.
> 3. Forgetting to mention that **GEA/1 and GEA/2 are broken** is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. Diagrams without labels are marked 0 — always label arrows with protocol names (Um, Gb, Gn).

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected]

**(a)** Explain the GPRS security architecture with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of SGSN, HLR/AuC, and the triplet mechanism. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the GPRS authentication and key agreement procedure. Explain the significance of A3, A8, and GEA algorithms and their cryptographic weaknesses. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution:

**(a) GPRS Security Architecture — 7 marks**

GPRS security architecture spans three security planes:

1. **MS ↔ SGSN Plane (Um and Gb):**
   - Confidentiality via GEA cipher algorithms.
   - Authentication via challenge-response (RAND/SRES).
   - Identity confidentiality via P-TMSI.

2. **SGSN ↔ HLR/AuC Plane (MAP/SS7):**
   - Secured by SS7/MAP security and operator's internal network.
   - Triplets generated by AuC using subscriber's master key $K_i$.

3. **SGSN ↔ GGSN and SGSN ↔ SGSN Plane (Gn/Gp):**
   - Uses **GTP tunneling** to carry IP packets between nodes.
   - **No native security** — relies on external IPSec or operator's private MPLS backbone.

**Block diagram (drawn by student):**

```
+----------+     Um      +-----+    Gb    +------+    Gn    +------+
| MS + SIM | <--------> | BTS | <-----> | SGSN | <-----> | GGSN |
+----------+   GEA/Kc   +-----+          +------+         +------+
                                    |        |
                                    | MAP    | Triplet
                                    v        v
                                +------+ +------+
                                | HLR  | | AuC  |
                                +------+ +------+
```

**Role of SGSN:** Performs authentication, ciphering negotiation, P-TMSI allocation, session management, and packet routing.

**Role of HLR/AuC:** Stores subscriber's permanent identity (IMSI), services profile, and the secret key $K_i$. AuC generates authentication triplets: $\{RAND, SRES, K_c\}$.

**Triplet Mechanism (2 marks):**
- AuC generates $n$ triplets in advance.
- For each triplet: $RAND = $ random 128 bits, $SRES = A3(K_i, RAND)$ (32 bits), $K_c = A8(K_i, RAND)$ (64 bits).
- HLR delivers them to SGSN in batches.
- SGSN uses one triplet per authentication event.

---

**(b) Authentication and Key Agreement — 7 marks**

**Step-by-step procedure:**

1. **MS → SGSN:** Attach Request (P-TMSI or IMSI).
2. **SGSN → HLR/AuC:** Send Authentication Info request.
3. **AuC:** Generates a fresh triplet.
4. **SGSN → MS:** Authentication Request (RAND).
5. **MS inside SIM:**
   - Computes $SRES = A3(K_i, RAND)$
   - Computes $K_c = A8(K_i, RAND)$
6. **MS → SGSN:** Authentication Response (SRES).
7. **SGSN:** Verifies $SRES \stackrel{?}{=} XRES$ from HLR. If match, authentication succeeds.
8. **SGSN → MS:** Ciphering Mode Command (selects GEA/1, 2, 3, or 4).
9. **All subsequent LLC frames** are encrypted using GEA with $K_c$.

**Significance of A3, A8, GEA:**

- **A3:** Authentication algorithm. Output: 32-bit SRES.
- **A8:** Key generation algorithm. Output: 64-bit $K_c$.
- **GEA:** GPRS Encryption Algorithm. Used for ciphering at the LLC layer.

**Cryptographic Weaknesses:**

- **A3/A8 in COMP128-1:** Side-channel attacks (power analysis, **Differential Fault Analysis**) allow extraction of $K_i$ in hours.
- **GEA/1, GEA/2:** Effective key size only **40 bits** due to LFSR state; broken publicly in 2011.
- **One-way authentication:** MS cannot validate SGSN, enabling **rogue BTS** attacks.
- **No integrity:** Signaling messages can be forged on the radio path.
- **Cipher key reuse:** $K_c$ is reused for the entire GPRS session; no per-packet freshness.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Compare and contrast the security of GPRS with that of GSM. Highlight at least four improvements and four inherited weaknesses. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the GPRS backbone security issues. Why is the Gn/Gp interface considered a weak link? Explain GTP, GTPsec, and how IPSec is used to secure the backbone. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution:

**(a) GPRS vs. GSM Security Comparison — 7 marks**

| Aspect | GSM | GPRS | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cipher algorithms | A5/1, A5/2, A5/3 | GEA/1, 2, 3, 4 | GPRS inherits broken A5/1-2 family in spirit |
| Ciphering scope | Speech channel (TRAU) | LLC PDUs only | GPRS limits ciphering to data plane |
| Authentication | One-way (RAND/SRES) | One-way (RAND/SRES) | **No improvement** |
| Identity | TMSI | P-TMSI | Similar mechanism, similar risk |
| Integrity | None | None | **No improvement** |
| Key freshness | Per-call | Per-session | Slight improvement |
| Backbone | Circuit-switched E1/T1 | Packet-switched IP/GTP | New attack surface introduced |
| Tunneling | None | GTP (insecure) | **New vulnerability** |

**Four inherited weaknesses:**
1. One-way authentication → rogue BTS attack.
2. Weak cipher algorithms (A5/1-2 → GEA/1-2 family broken).
3. No integrity protection.
4. Permanent identity (IMSI) exposure possible.

**Four new improvements (modest):**
1. P-TMSI used for all data sessions.
2. Per-session $K_c$ derived via A8.
3. Packet-switched security at LLC layer.
4. GEA/3, 4 with stronger design (where deployed).

---

**(b) GPRS Backbone Security — 7 marks**

The GPRS backbone comprises the **Gn** (intra-PLMN) and **Gp** (inter-PLMN) interfaces connecting SGSNs to GGSNs and SGSNs to other SGSNs.

**Why Gn/Gp is a weak link:**

1. **GTP (GPRS Tunneling Protocol)** is a tunneling protocol operating over UDP/IP. It carries user IP packets inside GTP-U tunnels. Standard **GTP has no cryptographic confidentiality or integrity** — it is a plain tunneling layer.
2. Operators historically relied on the **physical security of their private MPLS/ATM backbones**, assuming attackers cannot tap them. This assumption has failed with the rise of insider threats, compromised routers, and cross-border Gp links.
3. **Cross-operator Gp links** (roaming) are particularly risky because two competing operators must trust each other's security.
4. **GTP-in-GTP tunneling attacks** and header manipulation are well-documented.

**GTPsec (3GPP TS 33.210 / 33.310):**
- Adds integrity and replay protection to GTP-U and GTP-C.
- Uses NDS/IP (Network Domain Security / IP) framework.
- Mandatory for some 4G/5G interconnects.

**IPSec for GPRS backbone:**
- Most operators deploy **IPSec ESP in tunnel mode** between SGSNs and GGSNs to provide confidentiality and integrity.
- ESP uses IKEv2 for key agreement and AES or 3DES for encryption.
- However, **IPSec deployment is not universal**, especially in older 2.5G/3G operators.

**Best practice (per 3GPP):**
- NDS/IP: Use IPSec ESP with AES-128 or stronger.
- Use IKEv2 with PKI certificates for inter-operator roaming.
- Enforce anti-spoofing, rate limiting on GTP-C traffic.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — GPRS Security**

- ✅ GPRS is **packet-switched data on top of GSM**; speeds up to 114 kbps using 8 TDMA slots.
- ✅ Core security algorithms: **A3** (auth), **A8** (key gen), **GEA/1-4** (ciphering).
- ✅ Ciphering key: $K_c$ = 64-bit, but **only 40-bit effective** in GEA/1, GEA/2.
- ✅ Authentication is **one-way only** — SGSN authenticates MS, not vice versa.
- ✅ **No integrity protection** on signaling or user data.
- ✅ Triplet = $\{RAND, SRES, K_c\}$ generated by AuC.
- ✅ **P-TMSI** masks IMSI; P-TMSI is 32 bits, locally significant, refreshed on SGSN change.
- ✅ **GPRS Tunneling Protocol (GTP)** carries user data on Gn/Gp — **no native security**.
- ✅ **IPSec / GTPsec** can secure the backbone, but deployment is inconsistent.
- ✅ GEA/1 and GEA/2 publicly broken in **2011 (26C3)** by Nohl and Melette.
- ✅ GPRS retains **COMP128** SIM vulnerabilities — older SIMs allow $K_i$ extraction.
- ✅ False BTS / IMSI catcher is a practical attack due to one-way auth.
- ✅ **3GPP TS 43.020** is the authoritative spec for GPRS security.
- ✅ Modern mitigation: prefer **GEA/3 or GEA/4**, deploy **IPSec on Gn/Gp**, use **USIM** in 3G+ (UMTS AKA fixes most of these issues).

---

**END OF NOTES — PCCCT502 / Module 2 / GPRS Security**

topics.title: GSM Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-2
> - **Topic:** GSM Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of GSM Security

**GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) Security** refers to the set of cryptographic protocols, algorithms, and architectural mechanisms standardized by the **3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP)** to protect the radio interface (the **Um** or **air interface**) of the **2G cellular network** against threats such as unauthorized access, eavesdropping, identity theft, and signaling manipulation.

The security framework is built around the **SIM (Subscriber Identity Module)** as the tamper-resistant trust anchor and relies on **shared-secret cryptography** between the user's smart card and the **Authentication Center (AuC)** of the home network.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> "GSM Security encompasses subscriber identity confidentiality, subscriber identity authentication, signaling-data confidentiality, and connection confidentiality, realized through the SIM, the A3/A8 algorithms in the AuC, and the A5 family of stream ciphers on the air interface."

## 1.2 The Five Pillar Security Goals of GSM

| # | Security Service | Protects Against | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Subscriber Identity Confidentiality** | IMSI exposure on air | TMSI + LA update |
| 2 | **Subscriber Identity Authentication** | Impersonation / Cloning | A3 challenge-response |
| 3 | **Signaling Data Confidentiality** | Ciphering control snooping | A5 stream cipher (selectable) |
| 4 | **User Data Confidentiality** | Eavesdropping on voice/data | A5 stream cipher |
| 5 | **Subscriber Identity Confidentiality on Network Link** | Wiretaps inside operators | Optional Kc encryption on Abis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> GSM security is **unilateral (one-way)** — only the **network authenticates the user**, never the reverse. This single design decision is the root of almost every major GSM vulnerability.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Club Membership Card

Imagine a **private club** (the GSM network) that uses a **membership card with a hidden secret PIN written in invisible ink** (the **SIM with the 128-bit Ki**). When you arrive at the door (the **base station BTS**):

1. The **doorman** generates a random 16-digit number and shouts it at you — that is the **RAND challenge**.
2. You take the RAND, combine it with your invisible PIN inside the card, and the card spits out a **handshake password (SRES)**.
3. The doorman, who secretly has a copy of your PIN at the back office (the **AuC**), checks whether the password matches the one he computed.
4. If it matches, you get in; both you and the doorman silently derive a **shared session key (Kc)** to encrypt all further conversation.

The problem? The doorman **never proves to you that *he* is the real doorman** — a clever imposter with a fake desk (an **IMSI catcher / false base station**) can fool you. That is GSM security in a single picture.

## 1.4 Key Cryptographic Primitives in GSM

- **A3 Algorithm** — Authentication function; produces a **32-bit Signed RESponse (SRES)**.
- **A8 Algorithm** — Key-generation function; produces a **64-bit Ciphering Key (Kc)**.
- **A5 Algorithm Family** — On-air stream cipher (A5/0, A5/1, A5/2, A5/3, A5/4).
- **COMP128** — The de-facto combined A3/A8 implementation widely deployed in SIMs.
- **Ki (128 bits)** — The long-term subscriber authentication key, never leaves the SIM.
- **IMSI (≤ 15 digits)** — International Mobile Subscriber Identity (permanent, plaintext-equivalent).
- **TMSI (32 bits)** — Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity (rotating, air-interface alias).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components Involved in GSM Security

The GSM security machinery spans three logical layers:

1. **Mobile Station (MS)** = Mobile Equipment (ME) + SIM card.
2. **Radio Access Network (RAN)** = Base Transceiver Station (BTS) + Base Station Controller (BSC).
3. **Core Network (CN)** = Mobile Switching Center (MSC/VLR) + **Authentication Center (AuC)** + Home Location Register (HLR).

The **AuC** stores the master copy of every subscriber's **Ki** and computes the *authentication triplets* in advance.

## 2.2 The Authentication Triplet (RAND, SRES, Kc)

When a subscriber roams into a new VLR, the HLR/AuC ships a batch of triplets:

$$
\text{Triplet} = \{ RAND \;(128 \text{ bit}),\ SRES \;(32 \text{ bit}),\ Kc \;(64 \text{ bit}) \}
$$

For $N$ anticipated sessions, the AuC computes $N$ triplets and pushes them as an *authentication vector batch* to the VLR.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Authentication Logic

1. **VLR** picks an unused triplet and forwards the **RAND** (challenge) to the MS via the BTS over the air.
2. The **SIM** inputs $RAND$ and its internally stored $Ki$ into the **A3 algorithm** to derive $SRES_{UE}$, and into the **A8 algorithm** to derive $Kc_{UE}$.
3. The MS transmits only $SRES_{UE}$ over the air — **Ki never leaves the SIM**.
4. The VLR compares $SRES_{UE}$ with the $SRES_{AuC}$ from the stored triplet.
5. On match, the VLR commands the BTS to enable ciphering using the $Kc$ in the **A5** stream cipher; the SIM and BTS both hold the same $Kc$.

## 2.4 The Three Functional Algorithms in Detail

### A3 — Authentication Algorithm

$$
SRES = A3(K_i, RAND) \quad \rightarrow \quad 32 \text{ bits}
$$

- Operates on a **128-bit RAND** and the **128-bit Ki** (concatenated 256-bit input).
- Output is truncated to **32 bits**, which is cryptographically thin and a major weakness.

### A8 — Ciphering Key Generation Algorithm

$$
K_c = A8(K_i, RAND) \quad \rightarrow \quad 64 \text{ bits}
$$

- Same inputs as A3; output is a **64-bit session key**, of which only **54 bits are effective** (10 bits are zero-padded to fit the GSM frame structure — another weakness).

### A5 — Air-Interface Stream Cipher (Family)

$$
\text{Keystream}_i = A5(K_c, \text{Frame Number}_i)
$$

- Generates a **114-bit keystream block per TDMA frame** (since each burst carries two 57-bit traffic blocks).
- XORed with plaintext to produce ciphertext.

The five A5 variants are summarized below.

| Variant | Effective Length | Status | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A5/0** | No ciphering | Allowed | Disables encryption; used in regions with export restrictions |
| **A5/1** | 54-bit | Mandatory for GSM | Standard strong cipher in most networks |
| **A5/2** | 40-bit | Mandatory export variant | Deliberately weak; cracked in real-time (Barkan–Biham–Keller, 2003) |
| **A5/3** | 128-bit (KASUMI) | Optional 3GPP | Modern block-cipher-based stream |
| **A5/4** | 128-bit (KASUMI + GSMmil) | Optional | Strengthened over A5/3 |

### COMP128 — The Practical A3 + A8 Realization

Because A3 and A8 are deliberately *unspecified* by 3GPP to allow operator customization, almost all SIMs implement them jointly as **COMP128**:

$$
\{ SRES, K_c \} = COMP128(K_i, RAND)
$$

There are three known versions: **COMP128-1, COMP128-2, COMP128-3**, with COMP128-1 famously broken by **IBM researchers (Wagner, Schneier, Briceno) in 1998**, allowing SIM cloning in ~8 hours of chosen-challenge queries.

## 2.5 Subscriber Identity Confidentiality via TMSI

- On the very first registration, the IMSI is sent in cleartext once.
- Subsequently, the VLR assigns a **32-bit TMSI**, valid only inside the current **Location Area (LA)**.
- TMSI is re-encrypted with the A5 cipher when transmitted, so passive over-the-air eavesdroppers cannot link a TMSI to an IMSI.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Definition | Size |
|---|---|---|
| $K_i$ | Subscriber authentication key (secret, in SIM) | 128 bits |
| $RAND$ | Random challenge from AuC | 128 bits |
| $SRES$ | Signed response, output of A3 | 32 bits |
| $K_c$ | Session ciphering key, output of A8 | 64 bits (54 effective) |
| $IMSI$ | International Mobile Subscriber Identity | ≤ 15 decimal digits |
| $TMSI$ | Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity | 32 bits |
| $LAI$ | Location Area Identifier | 40 bits |
| $CKSN$ | Ciphering Key Sequence Number | 3 bits |
| $MSIN$ | Mobile Subscriber Identification Number | ≤ 10 digits |
| $MCC$ + $MNC$ | Mobile Country + Network Code | 3 + 2/3 digits |
| $MSRN$ | Mobile Station Roaming Number | variable |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Forensic investigators** use **IMSI catchers** (Stingrays) that exploit GSM's lack of mutual authentication to localize phones.
- **Lawful interception** mandates operator-side delivery of plaintext voice at the **MSC**, since the **Abis** link is the only place where plaintext is naturally available.
- **SIM cloning attacks** rely on COMP128-1 chosen-challenge extraction of Ki.
- **3G/UMTS** inherits the Ki but introduces **mutual authentication (AKA)** and the **128-bit Kc replacement (CK, IK)** to fix GSM's defects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Ciphering Key Length

GSM framing allocates **four bursts of 114 bits** per traffic channel in a **4.615 ms TDMA frame**. The ciphering key $K_c$ is 64 bits as produced by A8, but at the radio layer only 54 of those bits are placed into the ciphering register.

$$
K_c^{A8} = \{ b_{63} b_{62} \dots b_{10} b_9 b_8 b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 \}_{64 \text{ bits}}
$$

$$
K_c^{\text{used}} = \{ b_{63} b_{62} \dots b_{10} \}_{54 \text{ bits}} \quad \text{(bits } b_9 \dots b_0 \text{ are set to 0)}
$$

The rationale: GSM's voice codec (FR/HR/EFR) operates on 260-bit blocks per 20 ms, and 54 bits is the maximum *guaranteed uniqueness* per call under all codec modes.

> **Analytical conclusion:** Even before the cryptographic weakness of A5/1 is considered, the secret space of GSM encryption is bounded above by $2^{54}$, which is well within brute-force reach of modern ASICs.

## 3.2 Bit-Length Analysis of the Authentication Pipeline

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input to A3 / A8:} \quad & (K_i \,\|\, RAND) = 128 + 128 = 256 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Output of A3 (SRES):} \quad & 32 \text{ bits} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{collision birthday bound } \approx 2^{16} \\
\text{Output of A8 (Kc):} \quad & 64 \text{ bits raw, 54 bits effective} \\
\text{Over-the-air exposure:} \quad & SRES \text{ (32 bits)} + \text{ciphertext} \\
\text{Secret retained in SIM:} \quad & K_i \text{ (128 bits)} \quad \text{(never transmitted)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The 32-bit SRES means that the probability of a random challenge accidentally producing a match is

$$
P_{\text{false-accept}} = \frac{1}{2^{32}} \approx 2.33 \times 10^{-10}.
$$

While this seems tiny, an IMSI catcher can launch an **online dictionary attack** by replaying the same RAND thousands of times to a coerced SIM, which is a practical threat.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical: A5/1 Keystream Block Length

For one GSM TDMA frame carrying voice in the **TCH/F (Full Rate)** channel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frame duration} &= 4.615 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Plaintext bits per frame} &= 2 \times 57 = 114 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Keystream bits per frame} &= A5(K_c, FN_i) = 114 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Plaintext XOR keystream} &\rightarrow 114 \text{ bits of ciphertext} \\
\text{Throughput (single direction)} &= \frac{114 \text{ bits}}{4.615 \text{ ms}} \approx 24.7 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the **gross rate of GSM FR codec (≈ 13 kbps codec + signaling overhead)** after channel coding.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Where Each Attack Hits GSM

| Layer | Asset | Algorithm | Attack Vector | Resulting Damage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SIM–AuC | $K_i$ (128-bit) | COMP128-1 | Side-channel chosen-challenge | SIM cloning |
| Air (RAND) | 128-bit RAND | A3 | Replay | Impersonation |
| Air (SRES) | 32-bit SRES | A3 | Online brute force | False accept |
| Air (cipher) | 54-bit Kc | A5/2 | Real-time cryptanalysis (2003) | Full plaintext recovery |
| Air (cipher) | 54-bit Kc | A5/1 | Rainbow tables (Barkan 2006) | Plaintext recovery in minutes |
| Network side | Abis link | None by default | Wiretap | Plaintext recovery |
| Subscriber | IMSI | None on first attach | Passive sniffer | Identity disclosure |

## 3.5 Comparative Strength: GSM vs UMTS AKA

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GSM:} \quad & \text{One-way auth, } K_c = 54 \text{ effective bits, A5/1 stream cipher, COMP128-1} \\
\text{UMTS (3G):} \quad & \text{Mutual auth (AUTN), } CK = IK = 128 \text{ bits, KASUMI block cipher, MILENAGE}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **5-element authentication vector (AV)** in 3G is:

$$
AV = \{ RAND \;(128),\, XRES \;(32\text{-}128),\, CK \;(128),\, IK \;(128),\, AUTN \;(128) \}
$$

Notice the size of the *response* field expands from 32 bits (GSM SRES) to up to 128 bits (UMTS XRES), eliminating the brute-force concern.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GSM Authentication & Key Agreement (AKA) Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MS1["Mobile Station - SIM Card with Ki"] -->|Insert SIM| BTS1["BTS - Air Interface"]
    BTS1 -->|Attach Request with IMSI| VLR1["VLR - Visitor Location Register"]
    VLR1 -->|Send IMSI| HLR1["HLR - Home Location Register"]
    HLR1 -->|Lookup subscriber| AuC1["AuC - Authentication Center with Ki copy"]
    AuC1 -->|Generate N triplets| VLR2["VLR receives Triplet batch"]
    VLR2 -->|Select unused triplet| BTS2["BTS sends RAND to MS"]
    BTS2 -->|Challenge RAND 128 bit| MS2["SIM runs A3 and A8 with Ki and RAND"]
    MS2 -->|SRES 32 bit over air| BTS3["BTS forwards SRES to VLR"]
    BTS3 -->|Compare SRES| VLR3["VLR compares with stored SRES"]
    VLR3 -->|Match| BTS4["VLR enables ciphering mode"]
    BTS4 -->|Cipher mode command| MS3["MS stores Kc in SIM"]
    MS3 -->|Acknowledge| BTS5["BTS activates A5 cipher with Kc"]
    BTS5 -->|Encrypted voice and signaling| AUDIO["Secure voice session"]

    classDef secure fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef exposed fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef neutral fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000

    class MS1,MS2,MS3,AuC1 secure
    class BTS1,BTS2,BTS3,BTS4,BTS5 exposed
    class VLR1,VLR2,VLR3,HLR1,AUDIO neutral
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Green blocks hold the secret `Ki` (never leave the SIM or AuC). Red blocks represent the **air interface**, the most exposed link. Note the **unidirectional arrow** — the network authenticates the MS, but the MS never authenticates the network.

## 4.2 Functional Mapping: GSM Security Services vs Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUB["Subscriber Domain"]
        SIMCARD["SIM Card"]
        KIMOD["Ki 128 bit secret"]
    end

    subgraph NET["Network Domain"]
        AUC["AuC Ki database"]
        VLRSIDE["VLR triplet store"]
    end

    subgraph AIR["Air Interface Um"]
        CHAL["RAND challenge"]
        SRESV["SRES response"]
        KSESSION["Kc 54-bit effective"]
        CIPHER["A5 keystream"]
    end

    KIMOD --> SIMCARD
    AUC --> VLRSIDE
    VLRSIDE -->|RAND| CHAL
    CHAL -->|input| SIMCARD
    SIMCARD -->|A3 result| SRESV
    SIMCARD -->|A8 result| KSESSION
    SRESV -->|verify| VLRSIDE
    KSESSION --> CIPHER
    VLRSIDE --> KSESSION
    CIPHER -->|secure voice| SIMCARD

    classDef secret fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef algo fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    classDef air fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000

    class KIMOD,AUC,KSESSION secret
    class SIMCARD,VLRSIDE,CIPHER algo
    class CHAL,SRESV air
```

## 4.3 Attack-Surface Map: Where Each GSM Vulnerability Lives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["GSM Threat Surface"]

    ROOT --> A1["Passive Eavesdropping on Um"]
    ROOT --> A2["Active IMSI Catcher on Um"]
    ROOT --> A3["SIM Card Physical Access"]
    ROOT --> A4["Core Network Wiretap on Abis"]

    A1 --> A1a["A5 1 or A5 2 cipher weakness"]
    A1 --> A1b["Rainbow tables on Kc"]

    A2 --> A2a["No mutual authentication"]
    A2 --> A2b["Forced cipher A5 0 downgrade"]

    A3 --> A3a["COMP128 1 chosen challenge"]
    A3 --> A3b["Power side channel"]

    A4 --> A4a["Plaintext available at MSC"]
    A4 --> A4b["No encryption on Abis by default"]

    classDef root fill:#343a40,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#fbe9e7,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000

    class ROOT root
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A1a,A1b,A2a,A2b,A3a,A3b,A4a,A4b leaf
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **Explain the role of the SIM in GSM Security. Why is the Ki never transmitted over the air?** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **SIM (Subscriber Identity Module)** is a tamper-resistant smart card issued by the home operator and personalized with two long-term secrets: the **128-bit subscriber authentication key Ki** and the **IMSI**.

Its role in GSM security is threefold:

1. **Storage of Ki in a hardware-protected enclave** — Ki is written during personalization in a secure facility and is *not readable* by either the mobile equipment or the radio network. The SIM exposes only the *outputs* of A3 (SRES) and A8 (Kc).
2. **Execution of A3 and A8** — The SIM is the only entity in the MS that holds Ki; therefore it is the natural location for these algorithms.
3. **Binding subscriber to subscription** — Removing the SIM from one phone and inserting it into another transfers the subscription, supporting operator portability.

**Why Ki is never transmitted:**

- Transmitting Ki would allow an attacker with a single radio capture to clone the subscriber indefinitely.
- Instead, GSM uses a **challenge-response protocol** — the network sends a random **RAND**, and the SIM returns the derived **SRES**, never revealing Ki.
- This is the **Kerberos-style "never send the password" principle** adapted to cellular networks.
- Even the network operator stores Ki only in the **AuC**, a physically hardened, access-controlled datacenter node.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Students often state that "Ki is encrypted before transmission." Ki is **never transmitted at all**, encrypted or otherwise. Losing marks for this single word ("encrypted" instead of "never sent") is one of the most frequent KTU valuation errors.

### Question 2. **What is TMSI? Why is it used in GSM? How is it protected?** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**TMSI (Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity)** is a **32-bit locally significant identifier** assigned by the **VLR** to a mobile subscriber, used in place of the **IMSI** for all subsequent signaling on the air interface inside a **Location Area (LA)**.

**Why it is used:**

- The **IMSI is a permanent, globally unique, plaintext-equivalent identity** of the subscriber. Repeatedly transmitting it in cleartext over the air allows an eavesdropper to:
  1. Track the physical movement of a specific subscriber across cell towers.
  2. Link different calls made by the same person.
  3. Build a behavioral profile or perform targeted attacks.
- **TMSI breaks this linkability** because it is changed on every **Location Area Update**, every **VLR re-allocation**, and periodically (typically every 24 hours).

**How it is protected:**

- The new TMSI is transmitted to the MS *inside an encrypted message* — i.e., after A5 ciphering has been activated.
- It is meaningful only inside the LA where it was issued; the same TMSI value can be reused in a different LA without conflict.
- The mapping `TMSI ↔ IMSI` is held solely in the VLR's database.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. **(a)** Explain in detail the **GSM Authentication and Key Agreement (AKA)** procedure with a clear flow diagram. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Pre-registration (AuC pre-computation):**
For each subscriber, the **AuC** generates $N$ authentication triplets in advance:

$$
T_i = \{ RAND_i,\, SRES_i = A3(K_i, RAND_i),\, Kc_i = A8(K_i, RAND_i) \}
$$

These $N$ triplets are sent as a *batch* to the **VLR** of the current LA over the **HLR–VLR** secure backbone.

**Step 2 — Challenge generation:**
When the MS attaches, the VLR selects an unused triplet, transmits only the **128-bit RAND** over the air interface (Um) to the BTS, which forwards it to the MS.

**Step 3 — Response computation in the SIM:**
The SIM, holding Ki, computes:

$$
SRES_{MS} = A3(K_i, RAND) \quad ; \quad Kc = A8(K_i, RAND)
$$

The MS transmits only **SRES_{MS} (32 bits)** back to the VLR. **Ki never leaves the SIM.** Kc is also held in the SIM for later ciphering.

**Step 4 — Verification:**
The VLR compares $SRES_{MS}$ with $SRES_{AuC}$ from the stored triplet.

**Step 5 — Ciphering command:**
On match, the VLR sends a `Cipher Mode Command` containing the chosen A5 algorithm and the **Ciphering Key Sequence Number (CKSN)**. The MS acknowledges, and both the BTS and the MS start generating keystreams with $A5(Kc, FN)$.

**Flow diagram (simplified):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["AuC computes Triplet"] --> A2["VLR stores Triplet"]
    A2 --> A3["VLR sends RAND to MS"]
    A3 --> A4["SIM computes SRES and Kc"]
    A4 --> A5["MS returns SRES"]
    A5 --> A6["VLR compares SRES"]
    A6 -->|match| A7["Activate A5 ciphering with Kc"]
```

**Key evaluation points (for 7 marks):**

- Definition of triplet (1 mark)
- Challenge generation and transmission (1 mark)
- SIM-side A3 and A8 computation (1.5 marks)
- VLR-side verification (1 mark)
- Ciphering activation with Kc (1 mark)
- Diagram (1 mark)
- Mention of Ki never leaving SIM (0.5 marks)

#### (b) **What is COMP128? Discuss its variants and the famous COMP128-1 attack. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**

**Definition:**
**COMP128** is the de-facto industry implementation of the combined **A3 (authentication) and A8 (key generation)** algorithms, widely deployed in **GSM SIM cards**. It was designed and kept semi-proprietary by the GSM Association; only the input/output interface was standardized.

**Functional interface:**

$$
\{ SRES, K_c \} = COMP128(K_i, RAND)
$$

Internally COMP128 consists of **8 rounds** of substitution–permutation over a 128-bit state, using a 5-byte butterfly structure derived from a 512-bit compression of $Ki \oplus RAND$.

**Variants:**

| Variant | Year | Distinguishing Feature | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| COMP128-1 | 1992 | Original 8-round version | **Cryptographically broken** |
| COMP128-2 | 1998 | Internal re-keying; resists 1998 attack | Considered robust |
| COMP128-3 | 2000s | Strengthened structure | Used in modern SIMs |

**The COMP128-1 Attack (Wagner–Schneier–Briceno, 1998):**

1. Attacker possesses physical access to the victim's SIM.
2. Issues ~$2^{19}$ chosen **RAND** challenges; observes the corresponding **SRES** outputs.
3. COMP128-1's internal compression leaks information about Ki in the first two rounds.
4. After approximately **8 hours** of queries (roughly **150,000–500,000 chosen challenges**), the attacker recovers the full **128-bit Ki**.
5. With Ki, the attacker can clone the SIM into a programmable smart card.

**Impact:**

- 100+ million legacy SIMs in circulation were vulnerable.
- Forced a global migration to COMP128-2/3.
- Influenced the design of **3G MILENAGE** algorithm (which is public and openly audited).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often describe COMP128 as *"the GSM encryption algorithm."* It is **not** the encryption algorithm — encryption is **A5**. COMP128 is the **A3+A8** implementation that produces the *authentication response* and the *session key*. Mark deduction: typically **1–2 marks** for this confusion.

---

### Question B. **(a)** Discuss the **major security vulnerabilities of the GSM architecture**. Explain **IMSI catching** as a representative attack. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

#### Model Solution

The GSM security architecture, designed in the late 1980s, suffers from seven well-documented classes of weaknesses:

**1. One-way (Unilateral) Authentication**
- Only the **MS is authenticated** to the network; the **network is never authenticated** to the MS.
- This permits a **false base station (IMSI catcher)** attack.

**2. Weak Ciphering Algorithm (A5/2)**
- A5/2 was a **40-bit deliberately weakened** export cipher.
- In **2003, Barkan, Biham, and Keller** demonstrated real-time plaintext recovery using a PC in seconds.
- Result: any GSM phone supporting A5/2 can be downgraded and decrypted on demand.

**3. Reduced Effective Key Length of A5/1**
- Although A5/1 was the *strong* algorithm, its effective key is **only 54 bits** (10 bits of Kc are unused).
- **Barkan–Biham–Keller (2006)** showed A5/1 can be broken in minutes using pre-computed **rainbow tables** of size ~2 TB.

**4. COMP128-1 Cloning Attack**
- The 1998 chosen-challenge attack extracts Ki from physical SIMs in ~8 hours, enabling **SIM cloning**.

**5. Unprotected IMSI on Initial Attach**
- The IMSI is sent in cleartext on the *very first* attach (before any TMSI has been assigned).
- Even a passive sniffer learns the subscriber's identity.

**6. Abis Link Has No Native Encryption**
- The **Abis interface** (BTS–BSC) carries plaintext voice by default; lawful intercept or insider attacks recover voice trivially.

**7. Cryptographic Algorithm Secrecy (Kerckhoffs Violation)**
- The A5 family was kept secret for years, leading to **security by obscurity**. Once A5/1 and A5/2 were reverse-engineered (Briceno in 1999), all their flaws were simultaneously revealed.

**IMSI Catcher Attack — Step by Step:**

1. Attacker places a **rogue BTS** ("IMSI catcher", commercially e.g., *Stingray*, *Hailstorm*) in the target area.
2. The rogue BTS **broadcasts a stronger signal** than the real operator's BTS, forcing nearby phones to camp on it.
3. Since GSM has **no network authentication**, phones accept the rogue BTS and transmit their **IMSI in cleartext** during Location Update.
4. The IMSI catcher captures the IMSI, and may then relay the call to the real network while acting as a **man-in-the-middle**.
5. The attacker can force the phone into **A5/0 (no encryption)** under the guise of *"network capability negotiation."*
6. Result: **identity disclosure, location tracking, and possibly full plaintext recovery** without the subscriber's knowledge.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse "IMSI catching" with "SIM cloning." *IMSI catching* exploits the **air-interface authentication gap** and does **not** require Ki. *SIM cloning* requires **physical access** to extract Ki via COMP128-1. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this conflation.

#### (b) **Compare the A5/1 and A5/2 algorithms. Why was A5/3 introduced? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**

| Parameter | **A5/1** | **A5/2** | **A5/3** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Effective Key | **54 bits** of Kc | **40 bits** of Kc | **128 bits** (CK) |
| Algorithm Type | LFSR-based stream (3 LFSRs, 64-bit total) | LFSR-based stream (4 LFSRs, 81-bit total) | Block-cipher-based, **KASUMI** in OFB mode |
| Origin | Strong variant | **Deliberately weakened** export cipher | Designed for 3GPP / UMTS |
| Clocking | Irregular (stop-and-go) | Irregular (more frequent) | N/A (block cipher rounds) |
| Real-time break | Yes (rainbow tables, 2006) | Yes (real-time PC, 2003) | No known practical break |
| Status in 2024 | Mostly **deprecated** | **Discontinued** by 3GPP | **Standard for 3G/4G fallback** |

**Why A5/3 was introduced:**

- A5/1 and A5/2 were designed in a pre-1990s regulatory environment that prioritized **export controls** over security.
- Both were reverse-engineered and broken; the GSM community needed a publicly specified, modern primitive.
- A5/3 is based on the **MISTY1** block cipher, with the **KASUMI** modification standardized by 3GPP for **UMTS (3G) confidentiality and integrity**.
- It supports the full **128-bit CK (Ciphering Key)** of UMTS, removing the 54-bit truncation flaw.
- The algorithm is **publicly auditable** (satisfying Kerckhoffs' principle), unlike A5/1 and A5/2.

**Migration status (as of 2024):**
- 3GPP Release 18 marks **A5/1 and A5/2 as MUST-NOT** in user equipment.
- Modern SIMs support **A5/3 and A5/4** as the default.
- 5G NR has its own **NEA1/NEA2/NEA3** cipher suites derived from similar primitives.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU)**

- [ ] GSM security operates on the **Um (air) interface**; the **SIM** is the trust anchor.
- [ ] **Ki (128-bit)** lives only in the **SIM** and the **AuC** — *never on the wire*.
- [ ] Authentication is **one-way**: VLR authenticates MS; MS does **not** authenticate VLR.
- [ ] **A3 → SRES (32 bits)**; **A8 → Kc (64 bits raw, 54 effective)**; **A5 → air ciphering**.
- [ ] **COMP128** is the *combined A3+A8* implementation; **COMP128-1 is broken**.
- [ ] The **authentication triplet** has exactly three elements: $\{RAND, SRES, Kc\}$.
- [ ] **TMSI (32 bits)** is a rotating, LA-scoped alias for IMSI, transmitted only after A5 ciphering is on.
- [ ] **A5/1** = 54-bit strong variant; **A5/2** = 40-bit weak export; **A5/3** = KASUMI block cipher.
- [ ] **IMSI catcher** exploits *no mutual auth*; **SIM cloning** exploits *COMP128-1*.
- [ ] GSM transmits **IMSI in cleartext** on the first attach (before TMSI is allocated).
- [ ] **Abis** (BTS–BSC) is **not encrypted by default** in GSM.
- [ ] 3G fixes GSM by adding **mutual auth (AUTN)**, expanding SRES to **XRES (up to 128 bits)**, and using **KASUMI**.
- [ ] Always remember to write **"A3 is authentication, A8 is key generation, A5 is encryption"** — this single sentence scores 2 marks in Part A.
- [ ] KTU examiners love the phrase: *"What goes on the air and what does not."* Memorize: **RAND on air, SRES on air, Kc on air (in encrypted form via A5), Ki never on air, IMSI on first attach only.**
- [ ] Memorize the size table: $Ki = 128$, $RAND = 128$, $SRES = 32$, $Kc = 64/54$, $TMSI = 32$ bits.

> **One-line takeaway for the KTU exam hall:**
> *GSM Security is a Ki-based unilateral challenge-response scheme using A3 for authentication, A8 for key derivation, and A5 for on-air ciphering — elegant in 1991, broken in the 2000s, and superseded by 3G/4G/5G AKA.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IP Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-2
> - **Topic:** IP Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**IP Security (IPSec)** is a suite of protocols standardized by the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** under **RFC 4301**, **RFC 4302**, **RFC 4303**, and **RFC 5996** (consolidated later in **RFC 7296**), designed to provide **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, replay protection, and key management** for data transmitted at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model.

IPSec operates below the transport layer (TCP/UDP) and is therefore **transparent to applications** — any application that uses TCP/IP can leverage IPSec without modification. It protects entire IP packets, making it the foundational technology for **Virtual Private Networks (VPNs)**, site-to-site secure tunnels, and end-to-end host security.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"IPSec is a framework of open standards developed by the IETF that provides security services for IP network traffic at the Internet Layer. It uses cryptographic security services to protect communications between two peers, supporting authentication, integrity, confidentiality, and anti-replay through protocols AH, ESP, and key management via IKE."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Armored Diplomatic Pouch**
>
> Imagine you are a diplomat sending an important sealed letter from the Indian Embassy in Washington D.C. back to the Ministry of External Affairs in New Delhi.
>
> - The **letter content** is your **payload (data)**.
> - You place the letter inside a **tamper-proof, wax-sealed envelope** (this is **Authentication + Integrity**, courtesy of the **Authentication Header / AH**).
> - You then put this sealed envelope inside a **locked, opaque steel diplomatic pouch** that no customs officer can open (this is **Confidentiality + Encryption**, courtesy of the **Encapsulating Security Payload / ESP**).
> - The pouch is then sent through a **secure courier service** that has negotiated a "pass" with the receiving embassy — the courier carries a **handshake key** (this is the **Security Association (SA)** negotiated via the **Internet Key Exchange (IKE)**).
> - The courier's identity is verified at every checkpoint using an **ID card with a holographic seal** (this is **mutual authentication**).
>
> Now, **IPSec works exactly this way** for every IP packet traversing an untrusted network like the public internet. It does not care whether the data is an email, a web page, or a banking transaction — it wraps the entire packet in cryptographic armor.

---

### 1.3 IP Security Architecture — The Three Pillars

| Pillar | Protocol | RFC | Primary Service |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Authentication Header** | AH | RFC 4302 | **Integrity + Authentication (NO encryption)** |
| **Encapsulating Security Payload** | ESP | RFC 4303 | **Confidentiality + Integrity + Authentication** |
| **Internet Key Exchange** | IKEv2 | RFC 7296 | **Mutual Authentication + SA Establishment + Key Management** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why IPSec at the Network Layer?**
> - **Application Transparency:** Secure ANY protocol (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet) without modifying the application.
> - **End-to-End Security:** Operates between any two IP-addressable nodes — host-to-host, host-to-gateway, or gateway-to-gateway.
> - **Transparent to Users:** Provides "always-on" security at the infrastructure level.
> - **Mandatory in IPv6:** IPSec was originally mandatory in IPv6 specifications (now strongly recommended).

---

### 1.4 Security Services Offered by IPSec

> [!NOTE]
> IPSec provides the following five security services, as defined in RFC 4301:
>
> 1. **Data Origin Authentication** — Verifies the claimed sender of the IP packet.
> 2. **Data Integrity** — Ensures the packet has not been modified in transit (via MAC — Message Authentication Code).
> 3. **Confidentiality (Encryption)** — Protects payload from eavesdropping (typically using AES, 3DES).
> 4. **Replay Protection (Anti-Replay)** — Uses sequence numbers and sliding windows to prevent attackers from re-injecting captured packets.
> 5. **Key Management** — Secure negotiation, exchange, and refresh of cryptographic keys via IKE.

---

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Configuration Parameters (KTU High-Yield)

- **SPI (Security Parameters Index):** A **32-bit** identifier used to uniquely identify a Security Association. Combined with destination IP and protocol (AH/ESP), it forms the **triple** that identifies an SA.
- **Sequence Number:** A **32-bit** monotonically increasing counter used for anti-replay protection.
- **ICV (Integrity Check Value):** A **96-bit (or 128-bit for AES-GCM)** MAC computed using HMAC-SHA-1, HMAC-SHA-256, or AES-GMAC.
- **SA Lifetime:** Typically **28,800 seconds (8 hours)** for IKEv2, configurable.
- **Anti-Replay Window:** Default **64 packets** in IKEv2.
- **MTU Considerations:** IPSec with ESP tunneling adds overhead of **50–57 bytes** (IPv4) or **30 bytes** (IPv6), causing potential fragmentation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The IPSec Operational Pipeline — Logic Steps

IPSec communication follows a strict ordered sequence:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5-Stage IPSec Workflow (Exam-Favorite Diagram):**
>
> 1. **Stage 1 — Interesting Traffic Trigger:** A packet matches a predefined **Security Policy Database (SPD)** entry, marking it as needing protection (PROTECT, BYPASS, or DISCARD).
> 2. **Stage 2 — IKE Phase 1 (Main/Aggressive Mode):** Peers authenticate each other and establish a secure channel called the **ISAKMP SA (IKE SA)**. This is done using **pre-shared keys, digital certificates (RSA), or ECDSA**.
> 3. **Stage 3 — IKE Phase 2 (Quick Mode):** Negotiates the **IPSec SA** parameters — encryption algorithm, hash algorithm, mode (tunnel/transport), and lifetime.
> 4. **Stage 4 — Data Transfer (AH/ESP Processing):** The actual data is protected using the negotiated SA. Each outgoing packet gets an AH or ESP header; each incoming packet is verified.
> 5. **Stage 5 — SA Termination / Re-keying:** When lifetime expires or traffic volume threshold is reached, a new SA is negotiated. Old SAs are deleted after **re-key overlap window** for seamless transition.

---

### 2.2 Deep Dive: Authentication Header (AH)

The **AH protocol (Protocol Number 51)** provides:
- **Data integrity** (using HMAC)
- **Data origin authentication**
- **Anti-replay service**
- **NOT confidentiality** (no encryption)

> [!NOTE]
> **AH Coverage in the Packet:**
> AH protects the **entire IP packet EXCEPT for mutable fields** (TTL, Header Checksum, DSCP, ECN) that change in transit. In **Transport Mode**, AH covers the payload + original IP header (excluding mutable fields). In **Tunnel Mode**, AH covers the **entire original IP packet + new IP header**.

#### AH Header Format (RFC 4302):

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Next Header** | 8 bits | Identifies the next protocol (e.g., 6=TCP, 17=UDP) |
| **Payload Length** | 8 bits | Length of AH in 32-bit words minus 2 |
| **Reserved** | 16 bits | Must be zero |
| **SPI** | 32 bits | Security Parameters Index |
| **Sequence Number** | 32 bits | Anti-replay counter |
| **ICV (Integrity Check Value)** | Variable | MAC — 96 bits for HMAC-MD5-96 / HMAC-SHA-1-96; 128 bits for HMAC-SHA-256 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU):** Students often claim AH provides encryption. **It does NOT.** AH provides only authentication and integrity. If confidentiality is required, ESP must be used.

---

### 2.3 Deep Dive: Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)

The **ESP protocol (Protocol Number 50)** provides:
- **Confidentiality** (encryption of payload)
- **Data integrity** (optional but recommended)
- **Data origin authentication** (optional but recommended)
- **Anti-replay service**
- **Limited traffic flow confidentiality** (in tunnel mode)

#### ESP Packet Format (RFC 4303):

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **SPI** | 32 bits | Identifies the SA |
| **Sequence Number** | 32 bits | Anti-replay counter |
| **Payload Data** | Variable | Encrypted transport segment or entire IP packet |
| **Padding (0–255 bytes)** | Variable | Aligns data to cipher block boundary |
| **Pad Length** | 8 bits | Number of pad bytes preceding this field |
| **Next Header** | 8 bits | Type of payload (e.g., 4=IPv4, 41=IPv6) |
| **ICV (Authentication Data)** | Variable | MAC over ESP header + ciphertext + ESP trailer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU Board Favorite):**
> - **AH authenticates the entire IP header (except mutable fields).**
> - **ESP encrypts + authenticates ONLY the payload (transport mode) or the entire inner packet + ESP trailer (tunnel mode).**
> - **ESP does NOT authenticate the outer IP header.**

---

### 2.4 Transport Mode vs Tunnel Mode (CRITICAL — Frequently Asked)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mode Comparison (Verbatim Board Expectation):**
>
> - **Transport Mode:**
>   - Protects **payload of the IP packet only**.
>   - **Original IP header is preserved.**
>   - Used for **host-to-host** communication (end-to-end security between two end systems).
>   - Lower overhead (20 bytes for AH, ~20 bytes + IV for ESP).
>   - AH covers: IP Header (mutable fields excluded) + AH + TCP/UDP + Data.
>   - ESP covers: IP Header + ESP Header + (Encrypted: TCP/UDP + Data) + ESP Trailer + ESP ICV.
>
> - **Tunnel Mode:**
>   - **Entire original IP packet is encapsulated** in a new IP packet.
>   - Used for **gateway-to-gateway** (site-to-site VPN) or **host-to-gateway** (remote access VPN).
>   - Higher overhead (~20 bytes for new IP header + ESP overhead).
>   - AH covers: New IP Header + AH + (Original IP Header + TCP/UDP + Data).
>   - ESP covers: New IP Header + ESP Header + (Encrypted: Original IP Header + TCP/UDP + Data) + ESP Trailer + ESP ICV.

#### Visual Mode Summary:

```
TRANSPORT MODE (AH): [OrigIP|TCP|Data]  →  [OrigIP|AH|TCP|Data|ICV]
TRANSPORT MODE (ESP): [OrigIP|TCP|Data]  →  [OrigIP|ESP|Enc(TCP|Data)|Trailer|ICV]
TUNNEL MODE (AH):     [OrigIP|TCP|Data]  →  [NewIP|AH|[OrigIP|TCP|Data]|ICV]
TUNNEL MODE (ESP):    [OrigIP|TCP|Data]  →  [NewIP|ESP|Enc[OrigIP|TCP|Data]|Trailer|ICV]
```

---

### 2.5 Security Association (SA) — The Heart of IPSec

A **Security Association (SA)** is a **one-way (simplex)** logical connection between two IPSec peers that defines:
- The **cryptographic algorithms** to use (e.g., AES-256-GCM, HMAC-SHA-256)
- The **session keys** (encryption key + authentication key)
- The **mode** (transport or tunnel)
- The **lifetime** (in seconds and/or kilobytes)
- The **SPI (Security Parameters Index)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties of SAs (Verbatim KTU Board Expectation):**
> 1. **Simplex:** A separate SA is required for each direction. Two-way communication requires **at least TWO SAs** (one inbound, one outbound).
> 2. **Identified by Triple:** `<SPI, Destination IP Address, Protocol (AH/ESP)>`
> 3. **Stateful:** Each peer maintains an **SAD (Security Association Database)**.
> 4. **Bound by SPI:** The SPI in the AH/ESP header tells the receiver which SA to use.
> 5. **Lifetime-bound:** SAs expire and are renegotiated automatically.

#### SAD Entry Fields (KTU):

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| **SPI** | 32-bit SA identifier |
| **Sequence Number Counter** | For anti-replay |
| **Sequence Number Overflow Flag** | Indicates counter overflow |
| **Anti-Replay Window** | Sliding window of received sequence numbers |
| **AH/ESP Algorithm Information** | Algorithms + keys + IV |
| **Lifetime** | Time/byte limit before re-keying |
| **Mode** | Tunnel or Transport |
| **Path MTU** | Maximum transmission unit for the path |

#### SPD (Security Policy Database):

The **SPD** determines what action to take on each packet:
- **DISCARD:** Do not allow the packet through.
- **BYPASS:** Allow the packet through without IPSec protection.
- **PROTECT:** Apply IPSec processing (specifies which SA, which protocol, which mode).

---

### 2.6 Internet Key Exchange (IKE) — Phases and Modes

#### IKE Phase 1: ISAKMP SA Establishment

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase 1 establishes a secure, authenticated channel** (the IKE SA) for subsequent Phase 2 negotiations.

**Phase 1 has two modes:**

- **Main Mode (6 messages):**
  - Messages 1–2: SA negotiation (encryption, hash, authentication, DH group).
  - Messages 3–4: Diffie-Hellman key exchange (produces shared secret).
  - Messages 5–6: Mutual authentication (encrypted with derived keys) — using pre-shared key, certificates, or ECDSA.
  - **Provides identity protection.**

- **Aggressive Mode (3 messages):**
  - Combines SA, DH, and authentication in fewer rounds.
  - **NO identity protection** (identities sent in cleartext before encryption is established).
  - Faster, but less secure. Used when initiator IP is dynamic.

#### IKE Phase 2: IPSec SA Negotiation (Quick Mode)

- **Quick Mode (3 messages):** Negotiates the actual IPSec SA parameters (ESP/AH, algorithms, lifetime).
- Protected by the IKE SA established in Phase 1.
- Supports **PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy)** via optional new DH exchange.

#### IKEv2 Simplification (Modern, RFC 7296):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IKEv2 (RFC 7296) is the modern replacement** and is the de facto standard in KTU 2024 syllabus:
> - Reduces Phase 1 + Phase 2 to just **4 messages** total (uses **IKE_SA_INIT** and **IKE_AUTH** exchanges).
> - Built-in **NAT traversal**.
> - Built-in **reliability** (retransmission).
> - Uses **EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol)** for additional authentication methods.
> - Mandatory support for **MOBIKE** (mobility and multihoming).
> - Supports **AES-GCM** for combined encryption + integrity.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **IPSec High-Yield Table for KTU Board Exams:**

| Concept | Value / Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| AH Protocol Number | **51** | IANA assigned |
| ESP Protocol Number | **50** | IANA assigned |
| IKE UDP Port | **500** (legacy) / **4500** (NAT-T) | UDP |
| SPI Size | 32 bits | SA identifier |
| AH Sequence Number | 32 bits | Anti-replay |
| ESP Sequence Number | 32 bits | Anti-replay |
| Default SA Lifetime (IKEv2) | 28,800 seconds | 8 hours |
| Default IKE SA Lifetime (IKEv1) | 86,400 seconds | 24 hours |
| Anti-Replay Window (IKEv2) | 64 packets | Configurable |
| ICV for HMAC-MD5-96 | 96 bits | 12 bytes |
| ICV for HMAC-SHA-1-96 | 96 bits | 12 bytes |
| ICV for HMAC-SHA-256 | 128 bits | 16 bytes |
| ICV for AES-GCM-128 | 128 bits | 16 bytes (AEAD) |
| ICV for AES-GMAC | 128 bits | 16 bytes |
| AH Transport Mode Overhead | 24 bytes (fixed) | +ICV |
| ESP Transport Mode Overhead | ~26 bytes + padding | +ICV |
| ESP Tunnel Mode Overhead | ~46 bytes + padding | +ICV |
| Diffie-Hellman Group 1 | 768-bit MODP | Legacy |
| Diffie-Hellman Group 2 | 1024-bit MODP | Common |
| Diffie-Hellman Group 14 | 2048-bit MODP | Recommended |
| Diffie-Hellman Group 19 | 256-bit ECP | NIST P-256 |
| Diffie-Hellman Group 20 | 384-bit ECP | NIST P-384 |
| AES Key Size | 128 / 192 / 256 bits | AES-128, AES-192, AES-256 |
| 3DES Key Size | 168 bits (112 effective) | Deprecated |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MTU Overhead Calculation Formula:**
>
> $$ \text{Effective MTU} = \text{Physical MTU} - (\text{New IP Header} + \text{ESP Header} + \text{IV} + \text{ESP Trailer} + \text{ICV}) $$
>
> For **IPv4 ESP Tunnel Mode** with AES-CBC + HMAC-SHA-256:
>
> $$ \text{Overhead} = 20_{\text{NewIP}} + 8_{\text{ESP}} + 16_{\text{IV}} + 2_{\text{Trailer}} + 16_{\text{ICV}} = 62 \text{ bytes} $$
>
> So if physical MTU = 1500 bytes, IPSec MTU = **1438 bytes**.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Utility of IPSec

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications (KTU Application-Oriented Question Favorites):**
>
> 1. **Site-to-Site VPNs:** Connecting branch offices across the public internet (e.g., Cisco ASA, pfSense, AWS Site-to-Site VPN).
> 2. **Remote Access VPNs:** Employees connecting from home/hotel to corporate network (using IKEv2 + EAP-TLS).
> 3. **IPv6 Security:** Native IPSec is mandatory/recommended in IPv6 stacks (Windows, Linux, macOS).
> 4. **Routing Protocol Security:** Protecting BGP, OSPF, RIP packets (e.g., OSPF Authentication using IPSec AH/ESP).
> 5. **Application-Layer Security Gateway:** End-to-end encryption for VoIP (SIP over IPSec), database replication.
> 6. **Cloud Interconnectivity:** AWS, Azure, GCP all use IPSec tunnels for hybrid cloud connectivity.
> 7. **Critical Infrastructure:** SCADA systems, power grid communication protection.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Anti-Replay Sliding Window Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Given a 32-bit sequence number space in ESP, derive the maximum number of packets that can be sent before counter rollover, and explain the anti-replay window verification logic.

#### Step 1: Compute Maximum Sequence Number Value

The sequence number is a 32-bit unsigned integer, so:

$$ N_{\max} = 2^{32} - 1 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}295 $$

#### Step 2: Compute Number of Packets Before Rollover

$$ N_{\text{rollover}} = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ packets} $$

At 1 Gbps with 1500-byte packets, packet rate is:

$$ R = \frac{10^9 \text{ bits/s}}{1500 \times 8 \text{ bits}} \approx 83{,}333 \text{ packets/s} $$

#### Step 3: Time to Sequence Number Rollover

$$ T_{\text{rollover}} = \frac{N_{\text{rollover}}}{R} = \frac{4{,}294{,}967{,}296}{83{,}333} \approx 51{,}539 \text{ seconds} \approx 14.32 \text{ hours} $$

**Interpretation:** Even at 1 Gbps, rollover takes over 14 hours. Since SA lifetime is typically 8 hours, the receiver never sees a rollover in normal operation — but the implementation MUST handle it correctly.

#### Step 4: Anti-Replay Window Verification Logic

Let $W$ = window size (e.g., 64 for IKEv2), $R$ = right edge of window (highest sequence number received so far).

**On receiving sequence number $n$:**

$$
\text{If } n > R:
\quad \Rightarrow \text{Accept packet, advance } R := n
$$

$$
\text{If } n < R - W + 1:
\quad \Rightarrow \text{Reject packet (too old, left of window)}
$$

$$
\text{If } R - W + 1 \leq n \leq R:
\quad \Rightarrow \text{Check if } n \text{ already received in window bitmap; if duplicate, reject; if new, accept}
$$

#### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $W = 64$, $R = 1000$, and the window bitmap is initially empty (all 0s).

| Incoming Seq $n$ | Decision Rule | Action | New $R$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n = 999$ | $R - W + 1 = 937 \leq 999 \leq 1000$ | Check bitmap[999−937] = bitmap[62] = 0 → Accept; mark bitmap[62] = 1 | 1000 |
| $n = 999$ again | Same range | bitmap[62] = 1 → **REJECT (replay)** | 1000 |
| $n = 1100$ | $n > R$ → Accept | Advance | 1100 |
| $n = 800$ | $n < R - W + 1 = 1037$ → Too old | **REJECT** | 1100 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This logic is the basis of anti-replay protection. KTU often asks for the "window sliding" concept with a small numerical example.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: IPSec Overhead and Effective Bandwidth

#### Problem Setup:

A user transmits 1 GB (8 × 10⁹ bits) of data through an IPv4 ESP Tunnel Mode connection using **AES-128-CBC + HMAC-SHA-1-96** over a 1500-byte MTU link.

#### Step 1: Identify Overhead Bytes

- New IPv4 Header: **20 bytes**
- ESP Header (SPI + SeqNum): **8 bytes**
- AES-128-CBC IV: **16 bytes**
- ESP Trailer (Pad + PadLen + NextHdr): **2+1+1 = 4 bytes** (minimum)
- ICV (HMAC-SHA-1-96): **12 bytes**

**Total Overhead per packet = 20 + 8 + 16 + 4 + 12 = 60 bytes**

#### Step 2: Maximum Payload per Packet

$$ \text{MaxPayload} = \text{MTU} - \text{Overhead} = 1500 - 60 = 1440 \text{ bytes} $$

#### Step 3: Number of Packets Required

$$ N_{\text{packets}} = \left\lceil \frac{8 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}}{1440 \times 8 \text{ bits/packet}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{8 \times 10^9}{11{,}520} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 694{,}444.44 \right\rceil = 694{,}445 \text{ packets} $$

#### Step 4: Total Overhead in Bytes

$$ \text{TotalOverhead} = N_{\text{packets}} \times 60 = 694{,}445 \times 60 = 41{,}666{,}700 \text{ bytes} \approx 39.74 \text{ MB} $$

#### Step 5: Effective Throughput Efficiency

$$ \eta = \frac{\text{MaxPayload}}{\text{MTU}} = \frac{1440}{1500} = 0.96 = 96\% $$

So **4% bandwidth loss** is incurred due to IPSec overhead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Tunnel Mode with IPv4 + ESP + AES-CBC + HMAC-SHA-1, expect 4% overhead. This is a KTU-favorite numerical problem.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation: IKEv2 Key Exchange Cryptographic Steps

#### Phase: IKE_SA_INIT Exchange (4 messages, 2 round trips)

**Message 1 (Initiator → Responder):**
- $HDR_{i}$ contains: **SPIi, SPIt (cookies), Exchange Type = 34 (IKE_SA_INIT)**
- $SA_{i1}$: Proposal (encryption algorithm, PRF, DH group, etc.)
- $KE_{i}$: Initiator's Diffie-Hellman public value $g^{a} \mod p$
- $N_{i}$: Initiator's nonce (random value, used in key derivation)

**Message 2 (Responder → Initiator):**
- $HDR_{r}$: Response header
- $SA_{r1}$: Chosen proposal
- $KE_{r}$: Responder's DH public value $g^{b} \mod p$
- $N_{r}$: Responder's nonce

**Both peers now compute the shared DH secret:**

$$ S_{\text{shared}} = g^{ab} \mod p $$

This is the **Diffie-Hellman shared secret**, computable only by the two parties.

**Both peers derive the SKEYSEED (master key):**

$$ \text{SKEYSEED} = \text{PRF}(N_i \mid N_r, g^{ab}) $$

where PRF is the negotiated Pseudo-Random Function (e.g., HMAC-SHA-256).

**From SKEYSEED, derive seven keys:**

$$
\{SK\_d \mid SK\_ai \mid SK\_ar \mid SK\_ei \mid SK\_er \mid SK\_pi \mid SK\_pr\} = \text{PRF+}(\text{SKEYSEED}, \text{Ni} \mid \text{Nr} \mid \text{SPIi} \mid \text{SPIr})
$$

Where:
- $SK\_d$ — Key derivation key for child SAs
- $SK\_ai$ / $SK\_ar$ — Integrity keys (Initiator → Responder, Responder → Initiator)
- $SK\_ei$ / $SK\_er$ — Encryption keys
- $SK\_pi$ / $SK\_pr$ — Authentication keys (used to verify IKE_AUTH exchange)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "+" in PRF+ means iterative PRF chaining:**
> $$ T_1 = \text{PRF}(K, S \mid 0x01) $$
> $$ T_2 = \text{PRF}(K, T_1 \mid S \mid 0x02) $$
> $$ T_3 = \text{PRF}(K, T_2 \mid S \mid 0x03) $$
> And the output is the concatenation $T_1 \mid T_2 \mid T_3$ (truncated to required length). This is the standard IKEv2 key derivation chain.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: AH vs ESP Comparison

| Feature | AH (RFC 4302) | ESP (RFC 4303) |
|---|---|---|
| Protocol Number | **51** | **50** |
| Integrity (Whole Packet) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (only ESP-protected parts) |
| Integrity (ESP Header + Payload) | N/A | ✅ Yes |
| Authentication of IP Header | ✅ Yes (except mutable fields) | ❌ No |
| Confidentiality / Encryption | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (AES, 3DES, ChaCha20) |
| Anti-Replay | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Traffic Flow Confidentiality | ❌ No | ✅ Partial (Tunnel Mode hides addresses) |
| NAT Traversal Compatibility | ❌ Incompatible (hashes ports) | ✅ Compatible (with NAT-T encapsulation) |
| Overhead | Lower (no encryption) | Higher |
| Use Case When NAT is Present | ❌ Avoid | ✅ Use with NAT-T (UDP 4500) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Why AH fails with NAT:** AH's ICV computation covers the **original IP header**, including the source/destination IP addresses. When a NAT device translates addresses, the ICV becomes invalid, breaking AH. ESP (without authentication of outer IP header) tolerates NAT, and NAT-T encapsulates ESP in UDP/4500 to traverse NAT.

---

### 3.5 Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Mode + Protocol

| Scenario | Recommended Protocol + Mode |
|---|---|
| Host-to-host on private LAN, no NAT | **AH Transport** or **ESP Transport** |
| Host-to-host across public internet, no NAT | **ESP Transport** (confidentiality needed) |
| Site-to-site VPN (gateway-to-gateway) | **ESP Tunnel Mode** |
| Remote access VPN (host-to-gateway) | **ESP Tunnel Mode + IKEv2 + EAP** |
| IPv6 with end-to-end, no NAT | **AH or ESP Transport** |
| Behind NAT (home users) | **ESP Tunnel Mode + NAT-T (UDP 4500)** |
| Routing protocol (BGP/OSPF) authentication | **AH (Transport) or ESP (NULL encryption)** |
