# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are **verbatim-style KTU 2024 ESE pattern**. Tags indicate simulated past-year references for student familiarity.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]`**
*What is the WAP Security Architecture? Briefly list its main components.*

**Model Answer (3 points):**

1. The WAP Security Architecture is a layered collection of protocols and services — anchored at the **WTLS layer** of the WAP protocol stack — that provides **authentication, integrity, confidentiality, and replay protection** to upper WAP layers over wireless bearers. **(1 mark)**
2. Its main components are: **WTLS (core cryptographic protocol), WIM (identity module for key storage), WPKI (wireless PKI), and the WMLSCrypto API (application-layer crypto interface).** **(1 mark)**
3. It is designed to be **bearer-independent, device-independent, and optimised for low-bandwidth, low-memory handsets.** **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]`**
*Differentiate between TLS and WTLS in any three aspects.*

**Model Answer (3 points):**

1. **Transport basis** — TLS runs over **TCP**; WTLS runs over **WDP (datagram protocol)** which can be unreliable. **(1 mark)**
2. **Crypto profile** — TLS is heavyweight (RSA-2048, AES-256); WTLS supports **lightweight ciphers (RC5, DES, 3DES, IDEA)** and **ECC** to suit handset constraints. **(1 mark)**
3. **Handshake** — WTLS has an **optimised handshake** that may use **anonymous or ECDH key exchange**, while TLS typically requires RSA-based key transport. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice pattern**, two independent alternatives are provided. **Attempt either Question A OR Question B in full.**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model) | Module 2]`**
*Explain the **WAP Security Architecture** in detail, with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **WTLS, WIM, and WPKI**.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** The WAP Security Architecture is the layered set of protocols and services that delivers confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation in wireless applications built on the WAP stack. It sits between the WTP/WSP and WDP layers.

2. **WTLS (2 marks):** The **Wireless Transport Layer Security** is the cryptographic heart. It provides:
   - Server and optional client authentication using **X.509-derived certificates** (WAP Cert Profile).
   - **Data integrity** using keyed hash MACs (MD5/SHA-1).
   - **Confidentiality** using symmetric ciphers (RC5, DES, 3DES, IDEA).
   - Replay and re-ordering protection using **sequence numbers**.
   - Internally it has 4 sub-protocols: Handshake, Record, Alert, ChangeCipherSpec.

3. **WIM (1.5 marks):** The **WAP Identity Module** is a tamper-resistant chip (similar to a SIM) that stores the user's private key and certificates. It performs RSA/ECC signing inside the chip so the private key never leaves — analogous to a hardware token.

4. **WPKI (1.5 marks):** The **Wireless Public Key Infrastructure** is a wireless-optimised PKI defined by the WAP Forum. It uses **short-lived certificates, URL-based certificates, and ECC** to reduce bandwidth, memory, and processing overhead on handsets.

5. **Block diagram (extra benefit):** Refer to **Diagram 4.1** and **4.7** in SECTION 4.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model) | Module 2]`**
*Describe the **WTLS Record Protocol** with reference to fragmentation, MAC computation, encryption, and record header format.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Fragmentation (1 mark):** The upper-layer PDU is split into records of max **2^14 bytes**. Each record is processed independently.

2. **Compression (1 mark):** Optional. WTLS supports **NULL compression** and a lightweight dictionary-based scheme. The negotiated method is fixed for the session.

3. **MAC Computation (2 marks):** The MAC is computed as
   $$ \text{MAC}_i = H(K_{\text{mac}} \,\vert\, \text{seq}_i \,\vert\, \text{type} \,\vert\, \text{ver} \,\vert\, \text{len} \,\vert\, F) $$
   Length can be 0, 5, or 20 bytes depending on the cipher suite (NULL, MD5, SHA-1).

4. **Encryption (2 marks):** Symmetric cipher in **CBC mode** (DES, 3DES, IDEA) or **stream mode** (RC5) is applied to (fragment $\vert$ MAC). For block ciphers, WTLS uses its own padding (last byte = length).

5. **Record Header (1 mark):**
   - `ContentType` (1 byte): change_cipher_spec, alert, handshake, application_data
   - `ProtocolVersion` (1 byte)
   - `SequenceNumber` (2 bytes)
   - `Length` (2 bytes)
   - `Fragment` (variable)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model) | Module 2]`**
*With a neat diagram, explain the **WTLS Handshake Protocol**. List the messages exchanged and show how the **pre-master secret, master secret, and key block** are derived.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Diagram (2 marks):** Show the 10-step handshake (ClientHello → ServerHello → Certificate → ServerKeyExchange → ClientKeyExchange → ChangeCipherSpec ↔ → Finished ↔). Refer to **Diagram 4.3**.

2. **Messages Exchanged (2 marks):** As above; mention that **ClientKeyExchange** carries the client's DH/ECDH public value, and **Certificate** may be omitted in **Class 1** (anonymous).

3. **Pre-Master Secret (1 mark):**
   $$ \text{pre\_master} = g^{x_c x_s} \bmod p $$

4. **Master Secret (1 mark):**
   $$ \text{master} = \text{PRF}(\text{pre\_master},\, \text{server\_nonce} \,\vert\, \text{client\_nonce},\, \text{"master\_secret"}) $$

5. **Key Block (1 mark):**
   $$ \text{key\_block} = \text{PRF}(\text{master},\, \text{"key\_block"},\, \text{server\_nonce} \vert \text{client\_nonce}) $$
   Sliced into: $K_{\text{client\_MAC\_write}}, K_{\text{server\_MAC\_write}}, K_{\text{client\_write\_key}}, K_{\text{server\_write\_key}}, K_{\text{client\_IV}}, K_{\text{server\_IV}}$.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model) | Module 2]`**
*Explain the **WAP Gap** vulnerability. How does **WAP 2.0** resolve it? What are the three **security classes** defined in WTLS?*

**Model Solution:**

1. **WAP Gap Definition (2 marks):** In WAP 1.x, the WAP gateway must **terminate WTLS** on the wireless side and **re-establish TLS** on the Internet side. Between the two terminations, plaintext exists in gateway memory, creating a window of vulnerability to insider attacks, malware, or subpoenas.

2. **Risk Quantification (1.5 marks):**
   $$ P_{\text{leak}} = 1 - (1-p_{\text{gw}})^N $$
   For $p_{\text{gw}} = 10^{-5}$ and $N = 10{,}000$ sessions/hour,
   $$ P_{\text{leak}} \approx 1 - e^{-0.1} \approx 9.52\% \text{ per hour.} $$

3. **WAP 2.0 Solution (1.5 marks):** WAP 2.0 **eliminates the gateway translation** and runs **TCP + HTTP + TLS end-to-end**, completely removing the WAP Gap. The gateway now acts only as a **transparent HTTP proxy**.

4. **Security Classes (2 marks):**
   - **Class 1:** Anonymous server (no cert), encrypted + integrity-checked.
   - **Class 2:** Server authenticated with certificate, encrypted + integrity-checked.
   - **Class 3:** Mutual authentication, WIM-based client cert, encrypted + integrity-checked.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Top 7 Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing WTLS with WAP** — WTLS is *one layer*; WAP is the *whole stack*.
> 2. **Writing "WTLS uses TCP"** — WTLS runs over **WDP**, not TCP. TLS uses TCP.
> 3. **Forgetting the WAP Gap** — Examiner will deduct 2 marks if you describe WTLS without mentioning the gateway translation issue.
> 4. **Mixing up Class 2 and Class 3** — Class 3 has **mutual authentication**, not anonymity.
> 5. **Writing `|x|` inside a markdown table** — Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in math inside tables.
> 6. **Omitting the Finished message** in the handshake — It is mandatory and validates the master secret.
> 7. **Confusing WIM with SIM** — WIM is **WAP-specific**; SIM is for GSM network access. They can be co-located but are not the same.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Night Revision)**

- **WAP** = Wireless Application Protocol; **WTLS** = its security layer.
- WTLS sits **above WDP** and **below WTP/WSP**.
- WTLS has **4 sub-protocols**: Handshake, Record, Alert, ChangeCipherSpec.
- WTLS has **4 connection states**: Pending, Current, Read, Write.
- WTLS has **3 security classes**: anonymous, server-auth, mutual-auth.
- **MAC equation:** $\text{MAC} = H(K_{\text{mac}} \,\vert\, \text{seq} \,\vert\, \text{type} \,\vert\, \text{ver} \,\vert\, \text{len} \,\vert\, F)$.
- **Pre-master** = $g^{x_c x_s} \bmod p$ (DH) or shared ECDH secret.
- **Master secret** = PRF(pre_master, server_nonce $\vert$ client_nonce, "master_secret").
- **Key block** = PRF(master, "key_block", nonces); sliced into 6 (or more) keys.
- **WIM** stores private keys and performs signing off-host CPU.
- **WPKI** uses ECC, short-lived certs, URL-based certs.
- **WAP Gap** = plaintext at the gateway between WTLS and TLS terminations.
- **WAP 2.0** removes the gap by using **TLS end-to-end**.
- **Cipher suites** in WTLS: RC5, DES, 3DES, IDEA (with SHA-1 / MD5 MACs).
- **Record header** = Type(1) + Version(1) + SeqNum(2) + Length(2) + Fragment.
- **Padding**: WTLS uses its own scheme; last byte = padding length.
- **Risk equation:** $P_{\text{leak}} = 1 - (1 - p_{\text{gw}})^N$.
- **Finished message** = PRF(master, label, H(all_handshake_msgs)) — mandatory integrity check.
- **ChangeCipherSpec** promotes Pending → Current state.
- **WMLSCrypto** is the WMLScript API exposing sign/encrypt/digest to applications.

> **End of Topic Notes — WAP Security Architecture**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WAP Gateway
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY > - **Module:** Module Module-2 > - **Topic:** WAP Gateway # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **WAP Gateway (Wireless Application Protocol Gateway)** is an intermediary network element that acts as a **protocol translator and content encoder/decoder** between the **wireless domain** (mobile clients using WAP protocol stack) and the **Internet domain** (web servers using HTTP/TCP/IP). It enables mobile devices with limited processing power, memory, and bandwidth to access Internet content efficiently. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology for *Network and System Security*, the WAP Gateway is positioned as a critical **trusted intermediary** that: 1. Performs **WML (Wireless Markup Language) to HTML** content conversion. 2. Translates **WAP protocol stack (WSP/WTP/WDP)** into **TCP/IP/HTTP** protocols. 3. Provides an additional **layer of security** by hiding the internal IP addressing of mobile subscribers from the public Internet. 4. Implements the **Push Proxy Function (PPF)** for server-initiated content delivery. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The WAP Gateway is a fundamental component of the **WAP Push Architecture** and the **Wireless Telephony Application (WTA)** framework. It serves as the security and protocol bridge in the **3-tier WAP Reference Model** (Content Server ↔ Gateway ↔ Mobile Client). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **WAP Gateway** as a **UN-style simultaneous interpreter** working at a diplomatic summit: | Real-World Object | WAP Gateway Equivalent | |-------------------|------------------------| | Diplomat (Speaker) using complex vocabulary | Web Server (HTTP/HTML/XML) | | Interpreter (Translates) | WAP Gateway (Protocol & Content Translator) | | Foreign Delegate (Limited understanding) | Mobile Phone (WML/WBXML, limited resources) | | Translation Booth (Soundproof) | Gateway (Hides internal network) | | Earpiece (Wireless delivery) | Wireless Bearer (GSM/GPRS/3G) | Just as an interpreter: - **Translates** complex legal jargon into simple words, - **Filters** sensitive information to protect privacy, - **Compresses** the message to fit attention span, - **Adapts** the pace to the listener's understanding, … the WAP Gateway **encodes, compresses, translates, and secures** Internet content before delivering it to resource-constrained mobile devices. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology The following are the **standard WAP Gateway metrics** used in KTU 2024 Scheme evaluations: - **Bandwidth utilization reduction:** Typically achieves **~80% bandwidth savings** by converting HTML to compact **WML/WBXML**. - **Default WAP Gateway port:** **TCP Port 9200** (WAP Push Access Protocol - PAP) and **UDP Port 9200** (WAP Connectionless Session Service - WCSS). - **Protocol stack layers (WAP 1.x):** WAE → WSP → WTP → WTLS → WDP → Bearer - **Encoding ratio:** WML typically achieves **1:6 to 1:8 compression** over equivalent HTML. - **Security protocol:** **WTLS (Wireless Transport Layer Security)** — operates at layer 4-equivalent with overhead of **~2-10 bytes per record**. > [!NOTE] > **Cross-Reference:** In KTU Module-2, the WAP Gateway discussion directly precedes **Wireless Security (WEP, WPA, Bluetooth)** and complements the **WTLS** discussion in cryptographic security sections. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 WAP Reference Architecture (3-Tier Model) The WAP architecture is formally defined as a **three-layer logical architecture** in the WAP Forum specifications: ### Tier 1: The Mobile Client (User Agent) - Mobile device running a **WAP Microbrowser** (e.g., Openwave, Nokia browser). - Implements the **full WAP protocol stack** up to WAE. - Stores **cookies (WML cookies)**, user preferences, and cached WML decks. ### Tier 2: The WAP Gateway ⭐ (Core Focus) - Performs **bi-directional protocol translation**. - Implements **content encoders** (WML → WBXML, HTML → WML). - Provides **WTLS termination** (terminates secure sessions, optionally re-encrypts to TLS/SSL for the server). - Acts as a **proxy** for both pull (client-initiated) and push (server-initiated) content. ### Tier 3: The Origin Server - Standard **HTTP/1.1 web server** hosting WAP content. - Delivers **WML, WMLScript, images (WBMP)**, and multimedia content. - May include **Push Initiator (PI)** servers for server-pushed content. --- ## 2.2 Detailed Functions of the WAP Gateway > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The following 7 functions are frequently asked in 14-mark questions. Memorize with the mnemonic **"E-C-C-P-T-F-A"** — *Encode, Compress, Convert, Proxy, Translate, Filter, Authenticate*. 1. **Content Encoding (HTML → WML → WBXML)** - Converts verbose HTML/XHTML to lightweight WML. - Compiles WML into **WBXML (WAP Binary XML)** for transmission efficiency. 2. **Protocol Translation** - Translates **WSP (Wireless Session Protocol)** requests to **HTTP/1.1** requests. - Translates **WTP (Wireless Transaction Protocol)** acknowledgments to **TCP** acknowledgments. - Translates **WDP (Wireless Datagram Protocol)** datagrams to **UDP/IP** datagrams. 3. **Compression and Optimization** - Removes whitespace, comments, and non-essential markup. - Achieves typical **compression ratios of 4:1 to 10:1**. 4. **Caching and Proxying** - Maintains **WAP proxy caches** for popular content. - Reduces latency and bearer network traffic. 5. **Security Services (WTLS ↔ TLS Bridge)** - **Terminates WTLS** sessions from the mobile client. - **Establishes TLS/SSL** sessions to the origin server (if required). - May optionally perform **re-encryption** for end-to-end security (WAP 2.0). 6. **Push Proxy Gateway (PPG) Function** - Acts as the **Push Proxy Function (PPF)** for WAP Push services. - Receives **PAP (Push Access Protocol)** messages from Push Initiators. - Forwards via **OTA (Over-The-Air)** protocol to mobile clients. 7. **User-Agent Profile Handling (UAProf)** - Receives device capability information (CC/PP). - Routes content to the **WAP Gateway's content adaptation engine** to tailor the response. --- ## 2.3 WAP Protocol Stack Mapping The WAP Gateway operates primarily at the **translation point** between the WAP protocol stack and the Internet stack: | Layer | WAP Stack (Mobile → Gateway) | Internet Stack (Gateway → Server) | KTU Notation | |-------|------------------------------|------------------------------------|--------------| | **Application** | WAE (WML, WMLScript, WTA) | HTTP/1.1, HTML, XML | Layer 7 | | **Session** | WSP (Wireless Session Protocol) | HTTP Sessions, Cookies | Layer 5 | | **Transaction** | WTP (Wireless Transaction Protocol) | TCP | Layer 4 | | **Security** | WTLS (Wireless TLS) | TLS/SSL | Layer 4-5 | | **Datagram/Transport** | WDP (Wireless Datagram Protocol) | UDP / IP | Layer 3-4 | | **Bearer** | GSM CSD, GPRS, SMS, USSD, CDMA | TCP/IP, Ethernet | Layer 1-2 | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Representation | Units | KTU Use Case | |-------------------|------------------------------|-------|--------------| | **WBXML Compression Ratio** | $C_{ratio} = \frac{\vert S_{HTML} \vert}{\vert S_{WBXML} \vert}$ | Dimensionless | Calculate bandwidth savings | | **Effective Throughput** | $T_{eff} = \frac{P_{size} \times N_{req}}{T_{latency}}$ | bits/second | Performance evaluation | | **WDP Datagram Header** | $H_{WDP} = 2 + 4 + L_{port} + L_{addr}$ | Bytes | Protocol overhead | | **WTLS Record Size** | $R_{WTLS} = P_{payload} + H_{MAC} + H_{cipher}$ | Bytes | Security overhead | | **WAP Push OTA Size** | $S_{OTA} = S_{PAP} + S_{PUSH} + S_{SI}$ | Bytes | Push delivery | | **Bearer Network Bandwidth** | $BW_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i \times U_i$ | kbps | Capacity planning | | **Gateway Processing Latency** | $L_{total} = L_{encode} + L_{translate} + L_{proxy}$ | milliseconds | QoS analysis | | **WAP Push Delivery Time** | $T_{push} = T_{PI} + T_{PPG} + T_{OTA} + T_{bearer}$ | seconds | Push service design | > [!NOTE] > **Important:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of literal `|` in tables. The vertical bar character breaks Markdown table parsers. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The WAP Gateway concept, although **largely superseded by WAP 2.0** and modern 4G/5G IMS (IP Multimedia Subsystem) architectures, remains critically relevant in KTU examinations and in the following engineering domains: - **IoT and M2M Communication:** Lightweight protocol translation principles apply to **CoAP-to-HTTP** and **MQTT-to-HTTP** gateways in IoT. - **Edge Computing:** Modern **Multi-access Edge Computing (MEC)** gateways employ identical encode-compress-translate paradigms. - **Legacy Industrial Telemetry:** SCADA systems still use SMS/USSD bearers requiring WAP-style gateway translation. - **Network Security Education:** WAP Gateway serves as the **canonical example** of a "trusted intermediary" in security architecture courses. - **Content Adaptation Engines:** Modern CDN edge servers implement the same WAP-style content transcoding (e.g., image format conversion, text reformatting). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Process Flows ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: WAP Pull Transaction (Client-Initiated Request) The following is the **exhaustive step-by-step process** of a mobile client requesting a webpage through a WAP Gateway: ### Step 1: User Initiates Request on Mobile Browser The user navigates to a URL (e.g., `wap.example.com/index.wml`) on the mobile WAP browser. The WAP microbrowser constructs a **WSP (Wireless Session Protocol) request** that contains: - **WSP Method:** `GET` - **Request URI:** `http://www.example.com/index.wml` - **Headers:** `User-Agent`, `Accept: text/vnd.wap.wml`, `Profile`, `Cookie` (if session exists) ### Step 2: WSP Request Encapsulation into WTP The WSP PDU is passed to the **WTP (Wireless Transaction Protocol)** layer, which provides: - **Reliable transaction service** (Class 2 with acknowledgments). - **Segmentation and reassembly** of large WSP PDUs into smaller WTP segments. - **Retransmission timers** to handle lossy wireless bearers. ### Step 3: WTLS Encryption (Optional but Recommended) If the URL uses `wtls://` or `https://wap.`, the payload is encrypted using **WTLS**: $$ M_{encrypted} = E_{K_{session}}(M_{plaintext} \Vert \text{PAD} \Vert \text{MAC}) $$ where: - $E_{K_{session}}$ is the symmetric cipher (RC5, DES, 3DES, or IDEA in WTLS 1.x). - $K_{session}$ is the negotiated session key from the **WTLS handshake**. - $\text{MAC}$ provides integrity protection (using **MD5** or **SHA-1**). ### Step 4: WDP Encapsulation over Bearer The WTP segment is encapsulated in a **WDP (Wireless Datagram Protocol)** datagram, which adds: $$ H_{WDP} = \text{Source Port} \Vert \text{Destination Port} \Vert \text{Source Address} \Vert \text{Destination Address} $$ This is then transmitted over the **wireless bearer** (GSM, GPRS, SMS, or USSD). ### Step 5: WAP Gateway Reception and WDP Decapsulation The WAP Gateway receives the WDP datagram, removes the WDP header, and passes the payload to its WTP layer for **reassembly and acknowledgment**. The WTP layer then delivers a complete WSP PDU to the WSP layer. ### Step 6: WSP-to-HTTP Translation ⭐ (Core Gateway Function) The WAP Gateway performs the **critical translation step**: $$ \text{WSP Method: GET} \xrightarrow{\text{Translate}} \text{HTTP/1.1 Method: GET} $$ $$ \text{WSP Headers} \xrightarrow{\text{Mapping}} \text{HTTP Headers} $$ Specific translations include: - `Accept: text/vnd.wap.wml` → `Accept: text/vnd.wap.wml` (preserved for WAP-aware servers) - `Profile` header → `X-Wap-Profile` (passed as an HTTP header) - WSP session ID → HTTP cookie (if session continuity is required) ### Step 7: Forwarding to Origin Server via TCP/IP The HTTP request is sent over a **standard TCP connection (port 80 or 443)** to the origin server. The Gateway uses its own **public IP address** as the source — the mobile client's IP is hidden. ### Step 8: Origin Server Response and Reverse Translation The server returns an HTTP response. If the content is **HTML**, the Gateway runs the **HTML-to-WML transcoder**. If the content is already **WML**, the Gateway performs **WML-to-WBXML binary encoding**: $$ S_{WBXML} = \text{encode}(S_{WML}) = \text{compress}(S_{WML}) - \text{whitespace} - \text{comments} $$ ### Step 9: WBXML Compression and Return The binary WBXML is encapsulated back through WDP, transmitted over the bearer, and **decoded by the mobile browser** into a renderable WML deck. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: WAP Push Architecture WAP Push allows a **server (Push Initiator)** to deliver content to a mobile client without a prior client request. The WAP Gateway serves as the **Push Proxy Gateway (PPG)** in this model. ### Step 1: Content Authoring on Push Initiator (PI) The Push Initiator (a server-side application) creates a **Service Indication (SI)** or **Service Load (SL)** push message. Example SI content: ``` Breaking News: Exam Results Published ``` ### Step 2: PAP Submission to Push Proxy Gateway The PI sends a **PAP (Push Access Protocol)** request to the PPG (WAP Gateway) over **TCP port 9200** using HTTP POST: ``` POST / HTTP/1.1 Host: ppg.example.com Content-Type: application/xml X-Wap-Push-URL: http://wap.example.com/news.wml X-Wap-Push-Signal: High X-Wap-Push-Expires: 3600 X-Wap-Push-Accept: text/vnd.wap.si Content-Length: 234 12345@pi.example.com yes ...SI document... ``` ### Step 3: PPG Validation and Routing The WAP Gateway (PPG) performs: - **Authentication** of the Push Initiator (using PAP authentication or IP whitelist). - **Spam filtering** and content policy enforcement. - **User agent profile lookup** to verify the target device supports push. - **Address resolution** (MSISDN or IP address of the mobile client). ### Step 4: OTA Push to Mobile Client The PPG sends the push message to the mobile client using the **OTA (Over-The-Air) protocol** over the wireless bearer (typically SMS or GPRS). The mobile client receives an **SMS WAP Push** (binary SMS with WAP SI/SL content). ### Step 5: Client Confirms Receipt The mobile client sends a **delivery confirmation** back through the WAP stack to the PPG, which forwards a **PAP delivery report** to the PI. ### Step 6: User Action and HTTP Pull (if Accepted) If the user accepts the push notification, the mobile browser issues a **WAP pull request** for the indicated URL — this goes through the standard WAP Gateway pull flow described in Section 3.1. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: WAP 1.x vs WAP 2.0 Gateway | Feature | WAP 1.x Gateway | WAP 2.0 Gateway | KTU Security Implication | |---------|-----------------|------------------|--------------------------| | **Transport** | WDP/WTP/WSP | TCP/IP directly (HTTP/1.1) | WAP 2.0 removes translation; gateway may be bypassed | | **Security** | WTLS (between client and gateway) | TLS end-to-end (client to server) | Eliminates "WAP Gap" vulnerability | | **Content Format** | WML / WMLScript | XHTML Mobile Profile (XHTML-MP) | Aligns with web standards | | **Push Protocol** | PAP + OTA over SMS | SIP-based (3GPP IMS) | Integration with IMS | | **Gateway Necessity** | Mandatory | Optional (transparent proxy possible) | Shift to all-IP networks | | **Bearer** | GSM CSD, SMS, GPRS | GPRS, EDGE, 3G, 4G | Higher bandwidth bearers | > [!WARNING] > **The WAP Gap Problem (Famous Security Vulnerability):** In WAP 1.x, the gateway terminates WTLS and establishes a separate TLS connection to the server. This creates a brief moment where the data exists **in plaintext** at the gateway — a "gap" in encryption. WAP 2.0 mitigates this with end-to-end TLS. KTU examiners frequently test this concept. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Effective WAP Bandwidth Savings The total bandwidth savings achieved by the WAP Gateway through content encoding and compression can be derived as: Let: - $S_{HTML}$ = size of equivalent HTML content (bytes) - $S_{WML}$ = size of equivalent WML content (bytes), where $S_{WML} = 0.4 \times S_{HTML}$ (typical 60% reduction due to WML's compactness) - $S_{WBXML}$ = size after WBXML binary encoding, where $S_{WBXML} = 0.5 \times S_{WML}$ (typical 50% further reduction) - $H_{HTTP}$ = HTTP header overhead (typically **~200 bytes** for full request) - $H_{WSP}$ = WSP header overhead (typically **~15-30 bytes** for connectionless WSP) The total compression ratio is: $$ R_{total} = \frac{S_{HTML} + H_{HTTP}}{S_{WBXML} + H_{WSP}} $$ Substituting typical values: $$ R_{total} = \frac{10000 + 200}{2000 + 20} = \frac{10200}{2020} \approx 5.05 $$ Therefore, the WAP Gateway achieves an effective **bandwidth reduction of approximately 80%**, which justifies its inclusion in the 3-tier architecture for resource-constrained wireless devices. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 WAP Reference Architecture (3-Tier Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Mobile Client
WAP Microbrowser
WML WMLScript] -->|WSP WTP WTLS WDP| B{WAP Gateway
Protocol Translator} B -->|HTTP TLS TCP IP| C[Origin Server
Web Application
WML HTML Database] C -->|HTTP Response| B B -->|WSP Response
WBXML Encoded| A B -.->|PPG Function| D[Push Initiator
PI Server] D -.->|PAP TCP 9200| B B -.->|OTA Push
SMS or GPRS| A A --> E[Wireless Bearer
GSM GPRS 3G SMS] E --> B style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style C fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900 style D fill:#f5e1ff,stroke:#6600cc style E fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 ``` --- ## 4.2 WAP Protocol Stack Translation Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph sub1[Mobile Client Side] WAE1[WAE - WML WMLScript WTA] WSP1[WSP - Wireless Session] WTP1[WTP - Wireless Transaction] WTLS1[WTLS - Wireless Security] WDP1[WDP - Wireless Datagram] B1[Bearer - GSM GPRS SMS] WAE1 --> WSP1 WSP1 --> WTP1 WTP1 --> WTLS1 WTLS1 --> WDP1 WDP1 --> B1 end subgraph sub2[WAP Gateway Functions] GW1[Content Encoder
WML to WBXML] GW2[Protocol Translator
WSP to HTTP] GW3[Security Bridge
WTLS to TLS] GW4[PPG Push Proxy] GW5[UAProf Handler] GW1 --> GW2 --> GW3 GW3 --> GW4 GW4 --> GW5 end subgraph sub3[Origin Server Side] HTTP1[HTTP 1.1 - SSL TLS] TCP1[TCP - IP Layer] ETH1[Ethernet Bearer] HTTP1 --> TCP1 --> ETH1 end B1 --> GW1 GW5 --> HTTP1 ETH1 --> GW1 style GW1 fill:#ffcccc style GW2 fill:#ffcccc style GW3 fill:#ffcccc style GW4 fill:#ffcccc style GW5 fill:#ffcccc ``` --- ## 4.3 WAP Push Architecture (Detailed Flow) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant PI as Push Initiator participant PPG as WAP Gateway PPG participant Network as Wireless Network participant Client as Mobile Client participant Server as Origin Server PI->>PI: Create SI or SL Content PI->>PPG: PAP POST TCP Port 9200 PPG->>PPG: Authenticate PI PPG->>PPG: Content Policy Check PPG->>PPG: Resolve MSISDN PPG->>Network: OTA Push over SMS or GPRS Network->>Client: Deliver WAP Push SMS Client->>Client: User Accepts Push Client->>PPG: WSP GET Request PPG->>Server: HTTP GET Request Server->>PPG: HTTP Response WML Content PPG->>PPG: Encode WML to WBXML PPG->>Client: WSP Response WBXML Client->>PPG: Push Receipt Confirmation PPG->>PI: PAP Delivery Report ``` --- ## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram of WAP Gateway Internals ```mermaid flowchart TB INPUT[WAP Bearer Input
WDP Datagram] --> DECAP1[WDP Decapsulation] DECAP1 --> WTLS_D[WTLS Decryption] WTLS_D --> WTP_D[WTP Reassembly] WTP_D --> WSP_D[WSP PDU Parser] WSP_D --> TRANS{Protocol Translator} TRANS --> HTTP_R[HTTP Request Builder] HTTP_R --> TLS_E[TLS Encryption to Server] TLS_E --> OUT1[TCP IP to Origin Server] IN2[HTTP Response from Server] --> TLS_D2[TLS Decryption] TLS_D2 --> TRANS2{Content Adaptation Engine} TRANS2 --> ENC[HTML to WML Transcoder] TRANS2 --> BIN[WBXML Encoder] BIN --> WTLS_E[WTLS Encryption] WTLS_E --> WTP_E[WTP Segmentation] WTP_E --> WSP_E[WSP PDU Builder] WSP_E --> ENCAP[WDP Encapsulation] ENCAP --> OUT2[WAP Bearer Output] style TRANS fill:#ffeb99 style TRANS2 fill:#ffeb99 style ENC fill:#ccffff style BIN fill:#ccffff ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define WAP Gateway. List any four of its key functions.** **Model Answer:** > **WAP Gateway** is a network element that acts as a **protocol translator and content encoder/decoder** between wireless mobile clients (using the WAP protocol stack) and Internet web servers (using HTTP/TCP/IP). It enables resource-constrained mobile devices to efficiently access Internet content. **Four Key Functions:** 1. **Protocol Translation:** Converts WSP/WTP/WDP requests to HTTP/TCP/IP and vice versa. 2. **Content Encoding:** Converts HTML to WML, then encodes WML into compact **WBXML (WAP Binary XML)** format. 3. **Security Bridge:** Terminates **WTLS** sessions from mobile clients and establishes **TLS/SSL** sessions with origin servers. 4. **Push Proxy Gateway (PPG):** Receives server-initiated push content via **PAP (Push Access Protocol)** and delivers it to mobile clients via **OTA** over wireless bearers. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Explain the significance of the Push Proxy Gateway (PPG) function in the WAP architecture.** **Model Answer:** The **Push Proxy Gateway (PPG)** is a critical WAP Gateway function that enables **server-initiated (push) content delivery** to mobile clients without a prior client request. Its significance includes: 1. **Enables Server-Initiated Communication:** Unlike traditional HTTP which is request-driven, PPG allows servers (Push Initiators) to proactively notify users — useful for news alerts, stock prices, email notifications, and emergency alerts. 2. **Acts as Trusted Intermediary:** The PPG authenticates Push Initiators using **PAP authentication**, validates content against push policies, and prevents spam. 3. **Protocol Bridging:** The PPG receives push content via **PAP over TCP port 9200** and forwards it to mobile clients via **OTA (Over-The-Air) protocol** over SMS or GPRS bearers. 4. **Delivery Reporting:** The PPG tracks delivery confirmation and forwards **delivery reports** back to the Push Initiator using PAP. 5. **Security Layer:** It ensures that only **authorized Push Initiators** can send content to the mobile subscriber, protecting against unsolicited push spam. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **a)** Explain the **WAP reference architecture** with a neat diagram. Describe the **3-tier model** and the role of the WAP Gateway. **(7 marks)** **b)** Discuss in detail the **WAP protocol stack** and explain how the WAP Gateway performs **protocol translation** between the wireless and Internet domains. Illustrate with examples. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **WAP Reference Architecture — 3-Tier Model** The WAP Forum defined a **3-tier logical architecture** to enable mobile devices to access Internet content: **Tier 1 — Mobile Client (User Agent):** - The mobile device runs a **WAP Microbrowser** (e.g., Openwave, Nokia browser). - It implements the full **WAP protocol stack** (WAE, WSP, WTP, WTLS, WDP). - Displays **WML (Wireless Markup Language) decks** — analogous to HTML pages. - Supports **WMLScript** for client-side scripting (analogous to JavaScript). - Stores **WML cookies** and **UAProf** (User Agent Profile) capability information. **Tier 2 — WAP Gateway ⭐ (Core Focus):** - Sits between the mobile network and the Internet. - Performs **protocol translation** (WAP stack ↔ TCP/IP/HTTP). - Performs **content encoding** (HTML → WML → WBXML). - Provides **security services** (WTLS termination and TLS initiation). - Acts as a **Push Proxy Gateway (PPG)** for server-pushed content. - Maintains **content caches** for performance optimization. **Tier 3 — Origin Server:** - A standard **HTTP/1.1 web server** (e.g., Apache, IIS) hosting WAP content. - Serves **WML, WMLScript, WBMP images**, and multimedia. - May include a **Push Initiator (PI)** for server-pushed content delivery. - Communicates with the WAP Gateway using standard Internet protocols. **Block Diagram of the 3-Tier Architecture:** ``` ┌─────────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ MOBILE CLIENT │ WAP │ WAP GATEWAY │ HTTP │ ORIGIN SERVER │ │ WAP Microbrowser │ <───> │ Protocol │ <───> │ Web Application │ │ WML/WMLScript │ WSP/WTP │ Translator │ TCP/IP │ WML/HTML/DB │ │ WTLS/WDP │ WTLS │ Content Encoder│ TLS/SSL │ Push Initiator │ │ UAProf │ Bearer │ PPG Function │ │ │ └─────────────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ GPRS/GSM/SMS Public Internet ``` **Significance:** This 3-tier model decouples the wireless domain from the Internet domain, allowing **independent evolution** of mobile networks and web technologies while maintaining interoperability. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **WAP Protocol Stack and Translation Process** The WAP protocol stack is structured in **5 layers**, each with a specific purpose. The WAP Gateway acts as the **translator** between this stack and the standard Internet stack. **WAP Protocol Stack (from top to bottom):** 1. **WAE (Wireless Application Environment):** - Application layer containing **WML, WMLScript, WTA (Wireless Telephony Application)**. - Analogous to the HTTP/HTML layer in the Internet stack. 2. **WSP (Wireless Session Protocol):** - Provides session management between the client and the WAP Gateway. - Supports **connection-oriented** and **connectionless** services. - Handles **header compression** for efficient wireless transmission. 3. **WTP (Wireless Transaction Protocol):** - Provides reliable request-response transactions over the unreliable wireless bearer. - Supports **Class 0** (unreliable), **Class 1** (reliable), and **Class 2** (reliable with last acknowledgment) services. 4. **WTLS (Wireless Transport Layer Security):** - Security layer providing **encryption, authentication, and integrity**. - Optional layer — used when secure communication is required. 5. **WDP (Wireless Datagram Protocol):** - Transport layer providing datagram service. - Bearer-independent — can run over GSM, GPRS, SMS, USSD, CDMA, etc. **Protocol Translation Process at the WAP Gateway:** When a mobile client requests a webpage, the following translations occur at the WAP Gateway: | WAP Layer | Translation Action at Gateway | Internet Equivalent | |-----------|------------------------------|---------------------| | **WAE (WML)** | Content transcoding (HTML → WML → WBXML) | HTTP/HTML | | **WSP** | WSP method (GET, POST) → HTTP method | HTTP Request | | **WTP** | Transactional reliability → TCP reliability | TCP | | **WTLS** | WTLS termination → TLS initiation (if used) | TLS/SSL | | **WDP** | WDP datagram → UDP/IP packet | UDP/IP | **Example of WSP-to-HTTP Translation:** **Client WSP Request:** ``` WSP/1.1 GET User-Agent: Nokia7250/1.0 Accept: text/vnd.wap.wml Host: www.example.com ``` **Translated HTTP Request at Gateway:** ``` GET /index.wml HTTP/1.1 User-Agent: Nokia7250/1.0 Accept: text/vnd.wap.wml Host: www.example.com X-Forwarded-For: 10.45.67.89 (mobile client IP) Via: WAP-Gateway/2.0 ``` **Reverse Translation (Response):** The HTTP response is converted to a **WSP response** with WML content, and the WML is encoded into **WBXML (binary format)** for efficient transmission to the mobile client. **Significance:** The protocol translation performed by the WAP Gateway enables **transparent interoperability** between legacy mobile networks and modern Internet infrastructure, allowing WAP applications to be served by standard web servers without modification. --- ### Question B (Option 2): [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **a)** Describe the **WAP Push architecture** in detail. Explain the role of **Push Initiator (PI)**, **Push Proxy Gateway (PPG)**, and the **OTA protocol** with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)** **b)** Explain **WTLS (Wireless Transport Layer Security)** and discuss the **WAP Gap security problem**. How does **WAP 2.0** address this issue? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **WAP Push Architecture — Detailed Explanation** WAP Push enables **server-initiated content delivery** to mobile clients. Unlike traditional HTTP which is client-driven (pull), WAP Push allows servers to proactively deliver content such as news alerts, stock prices, sports scores, and emergency notifications. **Key Components of WAP Push Architecture:** **1. Push Initiator (PI):** - A server-side application that **authors and sends push content**. - Typically an **application server** (e.g., news portal, stock trading platform). - Communicates with the PPG using **PAP (Push Access Protocol)** over **TCP port 9200**. - Formats content as **Service Indication (SI)** for user prompts, or **Service Load (SL)** for silent content delivery. **2. Push Proxy Gateway (PPG):** - Implemented as a function of the **WAP Gateway**. - Acts as the **trusted intermediary** between PIs and mobile clients. - Performs: - **Authentication** of Push Initiators. - **Content policy enforcement** and spam filtering. - **Address resolution** (mapping MSISDN to mobile device). - **Delivery tracking and reporting**. - Stores pending push messages for offline mobile clients. **3. Over-The-Air (OTA) Protocol:** - Standardized protocol (WAP-183-PushOTA) for delivering push content to mobile clients. - Runs over the **wireless bearer** (typically SMS or GPRS). - Uses **binary SMS encoding** to deliver WAP SI/SL content. - The mobile client **decodes the SMS** and presents the SI notification to the user. **4. Mobile Client (Push Recipient):** - The mobile device's **WAP microbrowser** handles incoming push messages. - Presents **SI notifications** to the user with options to **Accept** or **Discard**. - For **SL content**, silently receives and processes the data. **Detailed WAP Push Sequence Diagram:** ``` ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ Push │ │ WAP Gateway │ │ Wireless │ │ Mobile │ │ Initiator PI │ │ (PPG) │ │ Network │ │ Client │ └──────┬───────┘ └──────┬───────┘ └──────┬───────┘ └──────┬───────┘ │ │ │ │ │ 1. PAP POST (SI) │ │ │ │ ──────────────────> │ │ │ TCP/9200 │ │ │ │ │ 2. Authenticate PI │ │ │ 3. Content Policy Check │ │ │ 4. Resolve MSISDN │ │ │ 5. OTA Push │ │ │ ──────────────────> │ │ │ SMS or GPRS │ │ │ │ │ 6. Deliver SMS │ │ │ │ ────────────────> │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ 7. Display SI │ │ │ │ 8. User Accepts │ │ │ │ │ │ │ 9. WSP GET │ │ │ <───────────────────────────────────── │ │ (Pull URL) │ │ │ │ 10. HTTP GET to Origin Server │ │ │ 11. HTTP Response (WML) │ │ │ 12. WSP Response (WBXML) │ │ │ ─────────────────────────────────> │ │ │ │ │ │ 13. PAP Delivery │ │ │ │ Report │ │ │ │ <────────────────── │ │ ``` **Key Benefits of WAP Push:** - Enables **real-time alerts** for time-sensitive content. - **Reduces user effort** — no need to manually browse. - Supports **personalized content delivery** based on user profile. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **WTLS (Wireless Transport Layer Security)** **WTLS** is the security layer of the WAP protocol stack. It is derived from **TLS (Transport Layer Security)** but adapted for the constraints of wireless networks — limited bandwidth, higher latency, lower processing power, and unreliable bearers. **WTLS Functions:** 1. **Privacy (Encryption):** Encrypts the data payload to prevent eavesdropping. Supports ciphers like **RC5, DES, 3DES, and IDEA** in WAP 1.x. 2. **Integrity (Message Authentication):** Provides message integrity using **MAC (Message Authentication Code)** algorithms — typically **MD5** or **SHA-1**. 3. **Authentication:** Supports **server authentication**, **client authentication**, and **mutual authentication** using **X.509 digital certificates** and **WTLS-specific certificate formats** (WTLS certificates use ECC for smaller sizes). 4. **Replay Protection:** Uses **sequence numbers** and **nonces** to prevent replay attacks. **WTLS Protocol Stack:** ``` ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ WTLS Record Protocol │ ├────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Change Cipher Spec │ Alert Protocol │ ├────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Handshake Protocol │ └────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` **WTLS Handshake Process:** 1. **Client Hello** — client sends supported cipher suites and random nonce. 2. **Server Hello** — server selects cipher suite and sends its certificate. 3. **Key Exchange** — client and server exchange key material. 4. **Cipher Spec Change** — both sides switch to the negotiated cipher. 5. **Finished** — handshake verification messages exchanged. --- **The WAP Gap Security Problem** The **WAP Gap** (also called the **"WAP Gap Vulnerability"** or **"Decryption Gap"**) is a **well-known security weakness** inherent in WAP 1.x architecture: **The Problem:** In WAP 1.x, the **WAP Gateway terminates the WTLS session** from the mobile client and **establishes a separate TLS session** to the origin server. This means: 1. **Client → Gateway:** Data is encrypted using **WTLS**. 2. **At Gateway:** The Gateway **decrypts the WTLS payload** to inspect/transcode content. 3. **Gateway → Server:** The Gateway **re-encrypts the data using TLS**. 4. **At the Gateway (briefly):** The data exists in **plaintext** for a very short time. **The "Gap":** $$ \underbrace{M \cdot E_{WTLS}}_{\text{Client to Gateway}} \longrightarrow \underbrace{M_{\text{plaintext}}}_{\text{AT GATEWAY (briefly)}} \longrightarrow \underbrace{M \cdot E_{TLS}}_{\text{Gateway to Server}} $$ This **plaintext moment** at the gateway is called the **WAP Gap**. If an attacker compromises the WAP Gateway, they can intercept the plaintext data — including **sensitive information like passwords, credit card numbers, and personal data**. **Why This Matters:** - The user believes their data is **end-to-end encrypted**, but it is only **hop-by-hop encrypted**. - The WAP Gateway becomes a **single point of trust** and a **high-value attack target**. - Legal and compliance implications: Some jurisdictions require **end-to-end encryption** for sensitive transactions (e.g., banking, healthcare). --- **How WAP 2.0 Addresses the WAP Gap** **WAP 2.0** (released in 2002) fundamentally redesigned the architecture to **eliminate the WAP Gap**: 1. **End-to-End TLS:** In WAP 2.0, the mobile client speaks **TCP/IP directly** and uses **TLS directly** with the origin server. The WAP Gateway is no longer in the security path. 2. **Optional Transparent Proxy:** The WAP Gateway can act as a **transparent HTTP proxy** without terminating TLS. It simply forwards encrypted bytes without inspecting them. 3. **WAP 2.0 Profile of TLS:** A WAP-specific profile of TLS that allows mobile clients to use **X.509 certificates** (not WTLS certificates) and standard cipher suites (AES, etc.). 4. **XHTML Mobile Profile (XHTML-MP):** WAP 2.0 uses **XHTML-MP** instead of WML, aligning with web standards and enabling end-to-end content delivery. **Comparison Diagram:** ``` WAP 1.x: [Client] --WTLS--> [Gateway] --TLS--> [Server] ^^^ PLAINTEXT GAP ^^^ WAP 2.0: [Client] --------TLS (end-to-end)----------> [Server] [Gateway as transparent proxy] (no decryption, no gap) ``` **Conclusion:** While WAP 1.x introduced a security gap at the gateway, WAP 2.0's end-to-end TLS model provides **true end-to-end security** comparable to standard web browsing, making the WAP Gap a historical issue in modern WAP deployments. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > > **Common Mistake 1 — Confusing the layers:** Students often confuse WAP stack layers with the OSI model. **Remember:** WAP has its own 5-layer stack (WAE, WSP, WTP, WTLS, WDP) that maps to the **Internet stack**, not directly to the OSI 7-layer model. > > **Common Mistake 2 — WAP Gap explanation:** Many students incorrectly say the "gap" is a timing delay. The gap is a **plaintext exposure at the gateway** where WTLS is terminated and TLS is initiated — not a time delay. > > **Common Mistake 3 — PPG vs PI:** Students confuse the **Push Initiator (PI)** (server-side) with the **Push Proxy Gateway (PPG)** (gateway-side). The PI **originates** push content; the PPG **delivers** it. Both are distinct components. > > **Common Mistake 4 — Port number confusion:** The PAP port is **TCP 9200**, not 80 or 8080. Mark deduction for incorrect port numbers. > > **Common Mistake 5 — Compression ratio calculation:** When asked to calculate bandwidth savings, students often forget to include **header overhead** (HTTP vs WSP) in the calculation. Always include both payload and header sizes. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — WAP Gateway** ### 🔑 Core Concepts (Must Know): - ☐ WAP Gateway = **Protocol translator + Content encoder + Security bridge** between mobile and Internet. - ☐ **3-Tier Architecture:** Mobile Client ↔ WAP Gateway ↔ Origin Server. - ☐ **WAP Stack Layers (top to bottom):** WAE → WSP → WTP → WTLS → WDP → Bearer. - ☐ **WBXML** = WAP Binary XML — compact binary format for WML. - ☐ **WML** = Wireless Markup Language — markup language for mobile devices. - ☐ **WTLS** = Wireless Transport Layer Security — security layer (WAP 1.x). ### 🔑 WAP Push Essentials: - ☐ **PI (Push Initiator)** = Server that creates push content. - ☐ **PPG (Push Proxy Gateway)** = Gateway function that delivers push content. - ☐ **PAP (Push Access Protocol)** = Protocol between PI and PPG (TCP **port 9200**). - ☐ **OTA (Over-The-Air)** = Protocol between PPG and mobile client (over SMS or GPRS). - ☐ **SI (Service Indication)** = Push message that prompts user action. - ☐ **SL (Service Load)** = Push message that silently delivers content. ### 🔑 Security Essentials: - ☐ **WAP Gap** = Plaintext exposure at gateway in WAP 1.x. - ☐ **WAP 2.0** = End-to-end TLS, eliminates WAP Gap. - ☐ **UAProf** = User Agent Profile — device capability information. - ☐ **WTA** = Wireless Telephony Application — telephony integration. ### 🔑 Key Formulas to Memorize: - ☐ **Compression Ratio:** $C_{ratio} = \frac{\vert S_{original} \vert}{\vert S_{compressed} \vert}$ - ☐ **WAP 1.x Bandwidth Savings:** ~**80%** reduction (HTML → WML → WBXML). - ☐ **PAP Port:** TCP **9200**. - ☐ **WTLS Ciphers:** RC5, DES, 3DES, IDEA (WAP 1.x). - ☐ **WAP Stack Layers Count:** **5 layers** (WAE, WSP, WTP, WTLS, WDP). ### 🔑 Mnemonics to Remember: - ☐ **WAP Stack (top-down):** **"W**orld **S**ecurity **T**rusts **W**ireless **D**ata" = **W**AE, **W**SP, **W**TP, **W**TLS, **W**DP. - ☐ **Gateway Functions (7):** **"E-C-C-P-T-F-A"** = Encode, Compress, Convert, Proxy, Translate, Filter, Authenticate. - ☐ **Push Components (3):** **"P-I-P-P-G-O"** = PI, PPG, OTA. ### 🔑 Real-World Connection: - ☐ WAP Gateway principles are the **precursor to modern edge computing** and **CDN gateways**. - ☐ The WAP Gap problem taught the industry the importance of **end-to-end encryption** (HTTPS, TLS 1.3). - ☐ WAP Push was the **ancestor of modern push notifications** (Apple APNs, Google FCM). ### 🔑 Likely 14-Mark Question Patterns: - ☐ "Explain WAP architecture with diagram" → 3-tier model + WAP Gateway role. - ☐ "Describe WAP Push architecture" → PI + PPG + OTA + sequence diagram. - ☐ "Explain WAP Gap and how WAP 2.0 solves it" → WTLS termination problem + end-to-end TLS. - ☐ "WAP protocol stack vs Internet stack" → Layer-by-layer translation table. - ☐ "WAP Gateway functions" → Use the **"E-C-C-P-T-F-A"** mnemonic. ---

topics.title: Attacks Against Windows System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-3
> - **Topic:** Attacks Against Windows System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Attacks Against Windows System** refers to a class of cyber-attacks specifically engineered to exploit architectural vulnerabilities, configuration weaknesses, authentication mechanisms, and software flaws in Microsoft Windows operating systems (Windows NT, 2000, XP, Vista, 7, 8, 10, 11, and Server editions). These attacks target the Windows kernel, registry, authentication subsystems (LM/NTLM/Kerberos), access control lists (ACLs), and user-mode services (LSASS, SAM, SMB).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCCT502 / Module-3):**
> *Attacks against Windows systems are categorized based on the attack vector — remote, local, offline, and physical — and exploit the Authentication, Authorization, and Auditing (AAA) model of Windows NT-based systems, as well as known protocol-level vulnerabilities in SMB, RDP, NetBIOS, and Kerberos.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House With Many Doors" Model

Imagine a large office building (the Windows OS) with hundreds of doors, windows, and ventilation shafts (services, ports, and APIs). Each door has a lock (NTFS permissions, ACLs), and every visitor (user/process) carries an ID badge (Security Token + SID).

- **Most doors are guarded**, but some (default open shares, anonymous SMB access) are propped open for "convenience."
- **Locksmiths (attackers)** don't usually pick the front lock — they look for the forgotten back door, a window left open, or a fake badge (credential forgery).
- **The building manager (LSASS process)** keeps a master list of badges, but if he can be tricked or copied, the whole building falls.
- **The blueprint of the building (Windows Registry)** reveals where valuables are stored, but the blueprint itself is often accessible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Windows AAA Model** — Every attack, in some form, violates one of these:
> 1. **Authentication** — "Are you really who you claim?" (LM/NTLM/Kerberos)
> 2. **Authorization** — "Are you allowed to do this?" (ACLs, privileges)
> 3. **Auditing** — "Can we see what you did?" (Event logs, Sysmon)

## 1.3 Why Windows Is the Most-Targeted OS

| **Metric** | **Value** | **Engineering Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| Global Desktop OS Market Share | **≈ 72–75%** | Largest attack surface in the world |
| Default Open Ports | **135, 139, 445, 3389, 5985** | RDP, SMB, WMI, WinRM — primary remote attack vectors |
| Default Services Exposed | **SMB, RDP, NetBIOS, RPC** | High-value protocol-level attack surface |
| Pre-installed Legacy Components | **LM Hash, NTLMv1, SMBv1** | Cryptographically weak, deprecated but still active |

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Windows Attacks

```
Windows System Attacks
├── Remote Attacks (Network-Based)
│   ├── Password Guessing / Brute Force
│   ├── SMB Exploits (EternalBlue, SMBGhost)
│   ├── RDP Attacks (BlueKeep, DejaBlue)
│   ├── Kerberoasting & AS-REP Roasting
│   ├── Pass-the-Hash (PtH) & Pass-the-Ticket (PtT)
│   └── NTLM Relay Attacks
├── Local Attacks (On-Host)
│   ├── Privilege Escalation (UAC Bypass, Token Impersonation)
│   ├── DLL Hijacking / Side-Loading
│   ├── Registry Manipulation
│   ├── Scheduled Task / Service Abuse
│   └── Application Shimming
├── Offline Attacks
│   ├── SAM Database Extraction
│   ├── NTDS.dit Exfiltration
│   ├── LSASS Memory Dump
│   └── Hash Cracking (Rainbow Tables)
└── Physical Attacks
    ├── Bootkit / Evil Maid
    ├── Cold Boot Attack
    └── USB HID Injection (Rubber Ducky)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners frequently ask students to *classify a given attack* as **Remote / Local / Offline / Physical** — memorize the four-bucket taxonomy above.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Windows Authentication Stack (Conceptual Foundation)

Windows authentication flows through a layered stack. Every attack we study exploits a specific layer.

| **Layer** | **Protocol/Mechanism** | **Hash Algorithm** | **Weakness Exploited** |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 — Legacy | LM Hash | DES-based (no salt) | Case-insensitive, 14-char split, fast crack |
| L2 — Modern | NTLM / NTLMv1 / NTLMv2 | MD4 (NT) / HMAC-MD5 | Pass-the-Hash, NTLM Relay |
| L3 — Enterprise | Kerberos v5 | AES-128/256 | Kerberoasting, Golden/Silver Ticket |
| L4 — Cloud | Microsoft Account / Azure AD | OAuth 2.0 + JWT | Token theft, MFA bypass |
| L5 — Smart Card | PKINIT | RSA 2048+ | Physical theft, middleware flaws |

## 2.2 LM Hash — The Original Sin

The **LAN Manager (LM) Hash** was the default password storage algorithm in Windows NT, 95, 98, and 2000. It is the most cryptographically broken hash in the history of Windows.

### Operational Steps of LM Hashing

1. Convert the user's password to **UPPERCASE**.
2. Pad the password with **null bytes** to exactly **14 characters**.
3. Split the 14-byte string into **two 7-byte halves**.
4. Use each half as a **DES key** to encrypt a fixed constant `KGS!@#$%`.
5. Concatenate the two 32-bit DES ciphertexts → final **16-byte LM hash**.

### Why It Is Broken (Step-by-Step Logic)

- **No salt** → identical passwords produce identical hashes → enables rainbow tables.
- **Case-insensitive** → password complexity rules are halved in entropy.
- **7-character halves** → modern GPU can brute-force each half in **seconds**.

## 2.3 NT Hash (NTLM) — The Modern Successor

The **NT Hash** uses **MD4** (RFC 1320) on the **UTF-16LE** encoding of the password, with no salt and no key stretching.

$$\text{NT-Hash} = \text{MD4}\big(\text{UTF-16LE}(\text{password})\big)$$

| **Property** | **LM Hash** | **NT Hash** |
|---|---|---|
| Algorithm | DES (ECB) | MD4 |
| Case Sensitivity | Insensitive | Sensitive |
| Salt | None | None |
| Max Effective Length | **7 characters** (split) | Unlimited (technically) |
| Rainbow-Table Resistance | **None** | Weak |
| Modern GPU Crack Time (8-char) | < 1 hour | 1–10 hours |

## 2.4 NTLM Authentication — The Challenge-Response Protocol

NTLM uses a **three-leg challenge-response** flow that is vulnerable to relay and replay attacks.

$$\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{Negotiate}} \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{Challenge }(C_8)} \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{Response }(R)} \text{Server}$$

Where the response is computed as:

$$R = \text{DES-LM}(\text{NT-Hash}, C_8) \quad \text{or} \quad R = \text{HMAC-MD5}(\text{NT-Hash}, C_8, C_8, C_8)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **NT-Hash itself is never sent over the network** — only the challenge-response. However, if an attacker captures the challenge-response pair AND the NT-Hash (via SAM dump or memory), they can authenticate without knowing the plaintext password. This is the foundation of **Pass-the-Hash (PtH)** attacks.

## 2.5 Kerberos Authentication — The Enterprise Standard

Kerberos (RFC 4120) replaces NTLM in Active Directory environments. It uses **tickets** instead of transmitting credentials.

### Standard Kerberos Flow (The 6 Steps)

1. **AS-REQ** — Client → Authentication Server (KDC) : `[Principal, Time, KDC_Realm]`
2. **AS-REP** — KDC → Client : `[TGT encrypted with krbtgt key, Session Key encrypted with user NT-Hash]`
3. **TGS-REQ** — Client → KDC : `[TGT, Service Principal Name (SPN), Authenticator]`
4. **TGS-REP** — KDC → Client : `[Service Ticket encrypted with service account key, Session Key encrypted with TGS session key]`
5. **AP-REQ** — Client → Service : `[Service Ticket, Authenticator]`
6. **AP-REP** — Service → Client : `[Mutual Auth Confirmation]` (optional)

### Key Kerberos Attack Vectors

| **Attack** | **Target** | **Goal** | **Defense** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Kerberoasting** | Service account TGS-REP | Crack service account password offline | Strong 25+ char service passwords |
| **AS-REP Roasting** | Accounts with "Do not require Kerberos preauth" | Crack account password offline | Enable pre-authentication for all accounts |
| **Golden Ticket** | krbtgt account hash | Forge arbitrary TGT (10-year validity) | Reset krbtgt password twice |
| **Silver Ticket** | Service account hash | Forge arbitrary Service Ticket | Restricted KRB5 settings |
| **Pass-the-Ticket** | Captured TGS | Reuse service ticket to access service | Use Kerberos armoring (FAST) |
| **Kerberos Brute-Force** | Username enumeration via AS-REQ | Valid account discovery | Disable pre-auth is a misconfiguration |

## 2.6 SMB (Server Message Block) — The Crown Jewel of Windows Attacks

**SMB** operates on **TCP port 445** and is the primary file-sharing protocol in Windows networks. It has been the source of the most devastating attacks in history.

| **CVE / Attack** | **Year** | **Impact** | **KTU Relevance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| MS08-067 (Conficker) | 2008 | RCE via SMB stack | Historical, high-yield |
| MS17-010 (EternalBlue) | 2017 | RCE, exploited by WannaCry | Frequently asked |
| CVE-2020-0796 (SMBGhost) | 2020 | RCE via SMBv3 compression | Recent, important |
| CVE-2017-0144 (EternalBlue) | 2017 | Same as MS17-010 | Bundled terminology |
| SMBv1 Downgrade | 2017+ | Forces legacy, insecure protocol | Mitigation practice |

### EternalBlue — The Engineering Breakdown

EternalBlue exploits a **buffer overflow** in the **SMBv1 Transaction2** handler. The vulnerable parsing function fails to validate the length of `TotalDataCount`, allowing an attacker to send a malformed SMB packet that overflows the kernel-mode buffer and achieves **Remote Code Execution (RCE) as NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **WannaCry ransomware** (May 2017) infected 230,000+ computers in 150 countries in a single day by combining EternalBlue with a self-propagating worm. This is a **mandatory KTU case study**.

## 2.7 Pass-the-Hash (PtH) — The Silent Killer

Pass-the-Hash does **not require** cracking the hash. The attacker:
1. Extracts the NT-Hash of a privileged user from memory (LSASS) or SAM.
2. Forges an NTLM authentication response using the hash directly.
3. Authenticates to any service that accepts NTLM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Property:** Because NTLM uses `R = HMAC(NT-Hash, C)`, if the attacker possesses the NT-Hash, they can compute `R` for any new challenge `C` without ever knowing the plaintext. The NT-Hash IS the credential.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| **Concept** | **Equation / Definition** | **Variables & Units** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| LM Hash | $H_{LM} = D_{K_1}(C) \mid\mid D_{K_2}(C)$ | $K_i$ = 7-byte DES key, $C = $ "KGS!\@#\$%" | Legacy password storage (deprecated) |
| NT Hash | $H_{NT} = \text{MD4}(\text{UTF-16LE}(P))$ | $P$ = plaintext password | Modern Windows hash |
| NTLMv2 Response | $R = \text{HMAC-MD5}(H_{NT}, S, C, C_8)$ | $S$ = server name, $C$ = client challenge, $C_8$ = 8-byte server challenge | NTLM authentication |
| Kerberos Session Key | $K_{S} = E_{K_{user}}(R)$ | $K_{user}$ = user key, $R$ = random | Symmetric session establishment |
| Password Entropy | $E = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ | $L$ = length, $N$ = charset size (bits) | Strength estimation |
| Brute-Force Time | $T = \dfrac{2^{E}}{R}$ | $R$ = hash rate (hashes/sec) | Time-to-crack estimation |
| Rainbow Table Lookup | $T_{RT} = \dfrac{N}{M} \cdot t_h$ | $N$ = table size, $M$ = chains, $t_h$ = hash time | Offline cracking speedup |
| Birthday Attack (hash collision) | $T \approx 2^{n/2}$ | $n$ = hash output bits | MD4 collision complexity ($2^{64}$) |
| UAC Bypass Integrity Level | $IL < 4$ → bypass feasible | $IL \in \{Low, Medium, High, System\}$ | Privilege escalation logic |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time to Crack an NTLM Hash Using Brute Force

We derive the **expected time-to-crack** a Windows NT-Hash for a password of length $L$ using lowercase alphanumeric characters on a GPU rig.

### Step 1 — Define the Search Space

For lowercase letters + digits (a–z, 0–9), the charset size is:

$$N = 26 + 10 = 36 \text{ characters}$$

### Step 2 — Total Possible Passwords

For a password of length $L$:

$$P_{\text{total}} = N^L = 36^L$$

### Step 3 — Entropy in Bits

$$E = L \cdot \log_2(N) = L \cdot \log_2(36) = L \cdot 5.1699 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4 — Average Number of Guesses

On average, the attacker tries **half** the search space:

$$G_{\text{avg}} = \frac{36^L}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 36^L$$

### Step 5 — Hash Rate of Modern GPU

A modern GPU (e.g., NVIDIA RTX 4090 with hashcat) achieves:

$$R \approx 1.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ hashes/sec} \quad \text{(NTLM, MD4)}$$

### Step 6 — Expected Time-to-Crack

$$T = \frac{G_{\text{avg}}}{R} = \frac{36^L}{2R}$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Evaluation Table

| **Password Length $L$** | **Search Space $36^L$** | **Entropy (bits)** | **Avg. Guesses** | **Time @ $10^{11}$ H/s** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6 | $2.18 \times 10^{9}$ | 31.0 | $1.09 \times 10^{9}$ | **10.9 ms** ⚡ |
| 8 | $2.82 \times 10^{12}$ | 41.4 | $1.41 \times 10^{12}$ | **14.1 s** |
| 10 | $3.66 \times 10^{15}$ | 51.7 | $1.83 \times 10^{15}$ | **5.08 hours** |
| 12 | $4.74 \times 10^{18}$ | 62.0 | $2.37 \times 10^{18}$ | **27.5 days** |
| 14 | $6.14 \times 10^{21}$ | 72.4 | $3.07 \times 10^{21}$ | **9.7 years** |
| 16 | $7.96 \times 10^{24}$ | 82.7 | $3.98 \times 10^{24}$ | **1262 years** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** An 8-character alphanumeric Windows password is **crackable in seconds** by a determined attacker. This is why Microsoft enforces complexity AND recommends 14+ character passwords (matching LM's original length limit).

## 3.2 Derivation: LM Hash Total Effective Keyspace Reduction

The LM hash splits a 14-byte password into **two independent 7-byte halves**. We derive why this reduces security exponentially.

### Step 1 — Total Effective Keyspace (Without LM Split)

For a full 14-character lowercase-alphanumeric password:

$$K_{\text{full}} = 36^{14} = 6.14 \times 10^{21}$$

### Step 2 — Keyspace With LM Split (Independent Halves)

$$\begin{aligned}
K_{LM} &= 36^7 \cdot 36^7 = (36^7)^2 \\
&= (78.36 \times 10^{10})^2 \\
&= 6.14 \times 10^{21}
\end{aligned}$$

> At first glance, identical. But **LM does not check inter-half dependencies**.

### Step 3 — GPU Parallelization Exploits the Split

Because the two halves are **cryptographically independent**, an attacker can crack them in **parallel** on a single GPU:

$$T_{LM} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{36^7}{R}$$

For $R = 10^{11}$ H/s:

$$T_{LM} = \frac{36^7}{2 \times 10^{11}} = \frac{7.84 \times 10^{11}}{2 \times 10^{11}} = 3.92 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The entire 7-character half of any LM-hashed Windows password is crackable in under 4 seconds on a consumer GPU.** This is why Microsoft has completely disabled LM hashing since Windows Vista (and in modern OS by default).

## 3.3 Derivation: Kerberoasting Offline Crack Time

Kerberoasting extracts a TGS-REP ticket whose encrypted part uses the **service account's NT-Hash as the key**. The attacker brute-forces the service account password offline.

### Step 1 — Ticket Structure

A TGS-REP contains an `EncPart` field:

$$\text{EncPart} = E_{K_{\text{service}}}(\text{session key}, \text{authorization data})$$

Where $K_{\text{service}} = \text{NT-Hash of service account}$.

### Step 2 — Attack Logic

The attacker:
1. Requests a TGS for an SPN (e.g., `MSSQLSvc/sqlserver.corp.local:1433`).
2. Receives `EncPart` encrypted with $K_{\text{service}}$.
3. Tries candidate passwords $P_i$ by recomputing $\text{NT-Hash}(P_i)$ and attempting to decrypt.

### Step 3 — Per-GPU Crack Rate for AES-encrypted Kerberos Tickets

Modern GPUs achieve approximately:

$$R_{\text{KR}} \approx 5 \times 10^{5} \text{ candidate passwords/sec}$$

(5×10⁵ H/s — much slower than raw NTLM, but still effective because tickets are ticket-replayable and accounts often have weak passwords.)

### Step 4 — Time for a 9-character Password

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{KR}} &= \frac{36^9}{2 \cdot R_{\text{KR}}} \\
&= \frac{1.02 \times 10^{14}}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{5}} \\
&= \frac{1.02 \times 10^{14}}{10^6} \\
&= 1.02 \times 10^{8} \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 3.23 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Mitigation by Strong Service Passwords

For a 25-character randomly generated service password (using printable ASCII, charset = 95):

$$E = 25 \cdot \log_2(95) = 25 \cdot 6.57 = 164.3 \text{ bits}$$

$$T_{\text{crack}} = \frac{95^{25}}{2 \cdot 5 \times 10^{5}} \gg \text{age of universe}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Kerberoasting defense is not protocol-level — it is password length.** This is why Microsoft recommends 25+ character randomly generated service account passwords (often as Group Managed Service Accounts / gMSAs).

## 3.4 Derivation: Pass-the-Hash Mathematical Equivalence

We prove that possessing the NT-Hash is **mathematically equivalent** to possessing the plaintext password in NTLM authentication.

### Step 1 — Original NTLM Authentication

A legitimate user with password $P$ authenticates by computing:

$$R_{\text{legit}} = \text{HMAC-MD5}\big(\text{MD4}(\text{UTF-16LE}(P)), \;\text{server\_challenge}\big)$$

### Step 2 — Attacker Who Has NT-Hash

Attacker has $H = \text{MD4}(\text{UTF-16LE}(P))$. They compute:

$$R_{\text{attacker}} = \text{HMAC-MD5}\big(H, \;\text{server\_challenge}\big)$$

### Step 3 — Equivalence

$$R_{\text{attacker}} = \text{HMAC-MD5}\big(\text{MD4}(\text{UTF-16LE}(P)), \;\text{server\_challenge}\big) = R_{\text{legit}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The server **cannot distinguish** between the two responses because they are byte-for-byte identical. This is the **fundamental reason** Pass-the-Hash works. The NT-Hash is a one-way function of the password, but NTLM treats it as the credential itself.

### Step 4 — Implication for Defense

- **Disable NTLM** entirely → force Kerberos (the only real fix).
- **Use Credential Guard** → virtualize LSASS so memory dumps cannot extract hashes.
- **Tiered admin model** → reduce hash reuse across systems.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Attack-vs-Defense Cross-Reference

| **Attack** | **Attack Vector** | **Target Layer** | **Prerequisite** | **Primary Defense** | **Detection Method** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LM Hash Crack | Offline | Hash Storage | NT-Hash or LM-Hash extracted | Disable LM in Group Policy | Audit `NoLMHash` registry key |
| Pass-the-Hash | Local/Remote | NTLM | NT-Hash from LSASS | Credential Guard, disable NTLM | Event 4624 with NTLM LogonType 3 |
| Kerberoasting | Remote | Kerberos TGS | Valid user account | 25+ char service passwords, gMSA | Event 4769 with weak encryption |
| EternalBlue | Remote | SMBv1 | Port 445 open | Disable SMBv1, patch MS17-010 | IDS signature, Event 4624 |
| BlueKeep | Remote | RDP | Port 3389 open | Patch CVE-2019-0708, MFA | RDP brute-force alerts |
| UAC Bypass | Local | User Account Control | Local user code execution | Set UAC to "Always Notify" | AppLocker, Process Monitoring |
| DLL Hijacking | Local | Application Loader | Write access to app dir | DLL search-path hardening | Sysmon Event 7 (Image Load) |
| Token Impersonation | Local | Access Token | SYSTEM-level access | Credential Guard, least privilege | Event 4672, 4673, 4674 |
