# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the LM Hash algorithm and list its three main weaknesses.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **LAN Manager (LM) Hash** is the legacy password storage algorithm used in early Windows systems (NT, 95, 98, 2000). It operates in five steps: (1) the password is converted to uppercase, (2) padded with nulls to 14 bytes, (3) split into two 7-byte halves, (4) each half is used as a DES key to encrypt the constant `KGS!@#$%`, and (5) the two ciphertexts are concatenated to form a 16-byte hash.

**Three main weaknesses:**
1. **No salt** — identical passwords produce identical hashes, enabling precomputed rainbow table attacks.
2. **Case-insensitive** — drastically reduces keyspace, defeating password complexity.
3. **Independent 7-byte halves** — enables parallel brute-force; each half is crackable in seconds on modern GPUs.

*(Examiner Note: Award 1 mark for steps, 1 mark per weakness × 2 = full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between Pass-the-Hash (PtH) and Pass-the-Ticket (PtT) attacks.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Pass-the-Hash (PtH)** | **Pass-the-Ticket (PtT)** |
|---|---|---|
| Credential Used | NT-Hash of user account | Captured Kerberos TGT or TGS |
| Protocol | NTLM / NTLMv2 | Kerberos v5 |
| Source | LSASS memory dump, SAM database | Kerberos ticket cache (`.kirbi`) |
| Scope | Works against NTLM-enabled services | Works against Kerberos-enabled services |
| Lifetime | Until password change | Limited by ticket lifetime (10 hrs default) |
| Defense | Disable NTLM, Credential Guard | Enable Kerberos FAST (armoring) |

*(Examiner Note: 1.5 marks for PtH, 1.5 marks for PtT, with explicit differentiation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern:** Students answer **ONE** question out of two 14-mark options, each containing sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — *Password & Authentication Attacks*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Adapted]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **NTLMv2 authentication protocol**. Discuss why NTLMv2 is vulnerable to **relay attacks** even though the user's password is never transmitted in cleartext. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Kerberoasting** in detail. Derive an estimate of the time required to crack a 12-character service account password using offline brute force, given a GPU hash rate of $5 \times 10^5$ H/s and charset size $N = 95$ (printable ASCII). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**NTLMv2 Authentication Protocol — Step-by-Step Flow:**

1. **Client → Server (Type 1 — Negotiate):** The client sends a NEGOTIATE_MESSAGE containing supported flags (e.g., NTLMSSP_NEGOTIATE_EXTENDED_SESSIONSECURITY).

2. **Server → Client (Type 2 — Challenge):** The server replies with CHALLENGE_MESSAGE containing:
   - 8-byte server challenge $C_8$
   - Target name (domain/host)
   - 8 bytes of zeros (reserved — this is the **NTLMv2 weakness**)

3. **Client → Server (Type 3 — Authenticate):** The client computes:

$$\text{NTProofStr} = \text{HMAC-MD5}\big(H_{NT}, \; S \,\|\, C \,\|\, C_8 \big)$$

Where:
- $H_{NT}$ = NT-Hash of user password
- $S$ = server-supplied target name
- $C$ = 8-byte **client challenge** (random nonce — key innovation of NTLMv2)
- $C_8$ = 8-byte server challenge

The client also sends an NTLMv2 **blob** containing: timestamp, client challenge, and target information.

4. **Server Verification:** The server (which knows the user's NT-Hash from AD) recomputes the expected NTProofStr and compares.

**Why NTLMv2 Is Vulnerable to Relay Attacks:**

The fundamental weakness is that **NTLMv2 is a stateless challenge-response protocol with no binding to the source channel**.

- An attacker positioned as a **man-in-the-middle** can relay the Type 1 (Negotiate) message from victim → target server, then forward the Type 2 (Challenge) from target → victim, and finally forward the Type 3 (Authenticate) from victim → target.
- The target server receives a **valid NTLMv2 response from a legitimate user** because the victim computed it themselves.
- The attacker never decrypts the response — they simply **relay it**, hence the attack is also called **NTLM Relay** or **SMB Relay**.

**Counter-measures** (each gets a partial mark): SMB signing, channel binding (EPA), LDAP signing.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Kerberoasting — Detailed Explanation:**

Kerberoasting is an **offline password cracking attack** that targets service accounts in Active Directory.

**Step 1 — SPN Enumeration:** The attacker (with **any** valid domain user credential) queries AD for accounts with Service Principal Names (SPNs):

```
setspn -T corp.local -Q */*
```

**Step 2 — TGS Request:** For each SPN, the attacker requests a Kerberos TGS-REP:

$$\text{AS-REQ} \rightarrow \text{KDC} \rightarrow \text{TGS-REQ} \rightarrow \text{KDC} \rightarrow \text{TGS-REP}$$

**Step 3 — Ticket Extraction:** The TGS-REP contains an `EncPart` encrypted using the **service account's NT-Hash** (AES-256-CTS-HMAC-SHA1-96 by default).

**Step 4 — Offline Crack:** The attacker extracts the ticket and uses hashcat / john to brute-force the service password:

$$\text{For each } P_i : \; H_i = \text{NT-Hash}(P_i) \; ; \; \text{Attempt } D_{H_i}(\text{EncPart})$$

**Time Derivation for 12-character password:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Search Space } N^L &= 95^{12} \\
\log_{10}(95^{12}) &= 12 \cdot \log_{10}(95) = 12 \cdot 1.9777 = 23.73 \\
N^L &= 10^{23.73} = 5.37 \times 10^{23} \\
G_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{N^L}{2} = 2.69 \times 10^{23} \\
T &= \frac{G_{\text{avg}}}{R} = \frac{2.69 \times 10^{23}}{5 \times 10^5} \\
&= 5.38 \times 10^{17} \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 1.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ years}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** A 12-character random password (95-char charset) is computationally infeasible to crack. **However**, most service accounts use 8–14 character weak passwords, making them crackable in days to months.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Network-Level Exploits & Malware*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 — Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **EternalBlue exploit (MS17-010)**. How did it enable the **WannaCry ransomware** to spread globally within hours? What is the role of the **DoublePulsar backdoor** in this attack chain? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Windows Registry as an attack surface**. List and explain **five techniques** attackers use to achieve persistence by modifying registry keys. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**EternalBlue (MS17-010) — Detailed Explanation:**

EternalBlue is a **buffer overflow exploit** in Microsoft's implementation of the **SMBv1 (Server Message Block version 1)** protocol, disclosed by the Shadow Brokers group in April 2017 and patched by Microsoft in March 2017 (MS17-010).

**Technical Mechanism:**

1. The attacker sends a specially crafted **SMB_COM_TRANSACTION2_SECONDARY** request to TCP port 445.
2. The vulnerable parsing function `Srv!SrvOs2FeaListToNt` (or `SmbSrvAppend` depending on the Windows version) fails to properly validate the `TotalDataCount` and `DataDisplacement` fields.
3. The attacker provides a large `DataDisplacement` value that points **outside the allocated buffer** → **heap buffer overflow**.
4. The attacker uses a **HeapGroom** technique to place a controlled payload adjacent to the overflowed buffer.
5. The overwritten function pointers redirect execution to **shellcode** that establishes Ring 0 (kernel-mode) code execution.

**WannaCry Worm Chain:**

WannaCry combined EternalBlue with a worm module:

```
WannaCry Binary
    ├── EternalBlue Exploit Module → SMB port 445 scan
    ├── DoublePulsar Backdoor Loader → post-exploitation persistence
    ├── CryptoLocker Ransomware Module → file encryption
    └── Worm Propagation Engine → random IP generation
```

**Propagation Timeline:**
- **May 12, 2017, 07:44 UTC** — Initial infection at Telefónica, Spain.
- **+24 hours** — 230,000+ computers in 150 countries.
- **+72 hours** — Estimated $4–8 billion in damages.

**Role of DoublePulsar:**

DoublePulsar is a **kernel-level backdoor** (Ring 0) implanted **after** EternalBlue achieves code execution. It operates by hooking the `SrvTransactionNotImplemented` transaction opcode in the SMB driver, allowing the attacker to:
- Inject arbitrary DLLs into the kernel.
- Execute shellcode directly via a single SMB packet.
- Maintain persistence even if EternalBlue is patched.

The two-stage architecture (EternalBlue + DoublePulsar) decoupled **exploitation** from **payload delivery**, making the kit modular and reusable.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**The Windows Registry as an Attack Surface:**

The Windows Registry is a **hierarchical database** storing low-level OS configuration, including boot parameters, service definitions, user credentials (in some legacy cases), and autorun entries. It is a primary target for **persistence** — the ability to survive system reboots.

**Five Persistence Techniques via Registry:**

**1. Run / RunOnce Keys** (1.5 marks)
- Location: `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`
- Behavior: Programs in this key execute at every user logon.
- Example: A malware adds `cmd.exe /c powershell -ep bypass -f evil.ps1`.

**2. Image File Execution Options (IFEO) — Debugger Hijack** (1.5 marks)
- Location: `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\<app>.exe`
- Behavior: Setting the `Debugger` value causes Windows to launch the debugger (attacker-chosen program) instead of the original application.
- Example: Set `Debugger` for `sethc.exe` (Sticky Keys) → launches `cmd.exe` with SYSTEM rights (the famous **Sticky Keys backdoor**).

**3. Winlogon Helper DLLs** (1 mark)
- Location: `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Shell` and `Userinit`
- Behavior: Modifying these values causes malicious DLLs to load during the logon process.
- Example: Changing `Userinit` from `userinit.exe` to `userinit.exe, evil.dll`.

**4. AppInit DLLs** (1 mark)
- Location: `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows\AppInit_DLLs`
- Behavior: Any DLL listed here is loaded into **every process** that loads `user32.dll` — i.e., virtually every GUI application.
- Example: `AppInit_DLLs = "C:\Windows\Temp\keylogger.dll"` (keylogger loaded into all apps).

**5. Scheduled Tasks via Registry** (1 mark)
- Location: `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree\...`
- Behavior: Attackers can register a scheduled task by writing directly to the registry (bypassing the `schtasks.exe` tool, which is logged).
- Example: A task that runs `powershell.exe` every 5 minutes to phone home to a C2 server.

**Bonus Detection Tools (1 mark):**
- **Sysinternals Autoruns** — visualizes all persistence locations.
- **Sysmon Event 13** — logs registry modification events.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing LM and NT hashes** — many students write "NT hash uses DES" (wrong — NT uses MD4).
> 2. **Missing the "Why"** — when asked about EternalBlue, students describe the patch date but not the **buffer overflow mechanism**.
> 3. **Forgetting Kerberos vs NTLM scope** — Kerberoasting requires valid creds; Pass-the-Hash does not require plaintext.
> 4. **Skipping the math** — derivations without numerical evaluation lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mermaid diagram labels with markdown** — never use `**bold**` inside Mermaid node labels; it breaks the parser.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Windows is the most-targeted OS due to its **~73% market share** and **default open ports (135/139/445/3389)**.
- ☐ Every attack violates one leg of the **AAA model** (Authentication, Authorization, Auditing).
- ☐ The four-bucket taxonomy: **Remote / Local / Offline / Physical** — memorize it cold.

### Hash Algorithms
- ☐ **LM Hash** = uppercase + split into 7-byte halves + DES (no salt) → **broken**.
- ☐ **NT Hash** = MD4(UTF-16LE(password)), no salt, no stretching → **crackable with GPU**.
- ☐ **8-character NTLM password is crackable in ~14 seconds** on a modern GPU at $10^{11}$ H/s.

### Authentication Protocols
- ☐ **NTLM** = stateless challenge-response → vulnerable to **relay attacks**.
- ☐ **Kerberos** = ticket-based → vulnerable to **Kerberoasting, Golden/Silver Ticket**.
- ☐ **Pass-the-Hash** works because `R = HMAC(NT-Hash, Challenge)` is **mathematically equivalent** to using the password.

### Famous Exploits
- ☐ **EternalBlue (MS17-010)** = SMBv1 buffer overflow → WannaCry worm (2017).
- ☐ **BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708)** = RDP RCE pre-authentication.
- ☐ **SMBGhost (CVE-2020-0796)** = SMBv3 compression RCE.

### Persistence Mechanisms
- ☐ **Run / RunOnce keys** — most basic.
- ☐ **IFEO Debugger hijack** — replaces program with attacker binary.
- ☐ **Winlogon Shell/Userinit** — loads DLLs at logon.
- ☐ **AppInit_DLLs** — injects DLL into every user32 process.
- ☐ **Scheduled Tasks via Registry** — stealthier than `schtasks`.

### Defense-in-Depth
- ☐ **Disable LM Hash** via Group Policy (`NoLMHash`).
- ☐ **Disable SMBv1** entirely; enforce **SMB signing**.
- ☐ **Enable Credential Guard** to virtualize LSASS.
- ☐ **Use Kerberos FAST** (armoring) against PtT.
- ☐ **Enforce 14+ character passwords** for users and **25+ character** for service accounts.
- ☐ **Reset krbtgt password twice** to invalidate Golden Tickets.
- ☐ **Use gMSA (Group Managed Service Accounts)** for automatic password rotation.

### Key Formulas
- ☐ $H_{NT} = \text{MD4}(\text{UTF-16LE}(P))$
- ☐ $R = \text{HMAC-MD5}(H_{NT}, S, C, C_8)$
- ☐ $T_{\text{crack}} = \dfrac{N^L}{2R}$
- ☐ $E = L \cdot \log_2(N)$

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "list and explain attacks against Windows," always structure your answer as: (1) **Target Layer** (hash, protocol, registry, service), (2) **Attack Mechanism** (how it works), (3) **Real-World Example** (WannaCry, Mimikatz, etc.), (4) **Mitigation** (specific patch or policy). This 4-part structure scores full marks every time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Installing Applications
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-3
> - **Topic:** Installing Applications

# 📘 KTU B.Tech S8 Premium Notes — Network & System Security

## Module 3 | Installing Applications

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Installation (Security Perspective):** Application installation is the controlled, verifiable process of deploying software onto a computing system such that the integrity, authenticity, and confidentiality of the software binaries are preserved, and the principle of least privilege is enforced before the application is granted execution rights.

In the context of **KTU PCCCT502 – Network and System Security**, *Installing Applications* is not merely a system-administrator's routine. It is treated as a **security-critical operation** because every installation is an act of **trust delegation** — the operating system trusts the installer to place files, modify registries, open ports, and request permissions.

| Term | Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) |
|---|---|
| **Package Manager** | A software tool that automates the installation, upgrade, configuration, and removal of software packages while maintaining database integrity. |
| **Trusted Source** | A software repository, vendor, or developer whose authenticity and integrity have been cryptographically verified. |
| **Digital Signature** | A mathematical scheme that proves the authenticity of a digital message or document — used here to prove a software package came from a real vendor. |
| **Patch** | A piece of code inserted into an existing program to fix a bug, close a vulnerability, or improve performance — measured in **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)** IDs. |
| **Sandbox** | An isolated testing environment that imitates end-user operating environments for safely running untrusted code during pre-installation checks. |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The House-Builder"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you are building a house and you hire a contractor.
> 1. **Source Verification** — You only hire licensed contractors (trusted source).
> 2. **Background Check** — You ask for credentials and references (digital signature verification).
> 3. **Material Inspection** — Every bag of cement comes with a tamper-proof seal (hash verification).
> 4. **Test Room** — You build a small model room first to test if the design works (sandboxing).
> 5. **Gradual Expansion** — You don't let the contractor rewire your entire electrical panel unsupervised (principle of least privilege).
>
> Installing an application is **exactly this** — the OS is the homeowner, the application is the contractor, and the security mechanisms are the verification process.

---

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** The KTU syllabus maps *Installing Applications* to **Course Outcome CO3** — *"Apply security mechanisms to harden systems against application-level threats."* Questions typically appear for **7 to 14 marks** in the End Semester Examination (ESE) and almost always in Part A as 3-mark short notes.

---

### 1.4 Key Security Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| SHA-256 Digest Length | **256 bits = 32 bytes = 64 hex chars** | Industry minimum for software integrity |
| RSA Minimum Key Size (2024) | **2048 bits** | NIST SP 800-131A recommendation |
| TLS for Repositories | **TLS 1.2 or higher** | Mandatory for `apt`, `yum`, `pip` |
| CVSS Severity Scale | **0.0 – 10.0** | Patch priority threshold = 7.0 |
| Default Sandbox Timeout | **300 seconds** | Pre-installation dynamic analysis window |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Secure Application Installation

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus groups installation security into **five operational pillars**. Mastering these is the difference between a 7-mark and a 14-mark answer.

#### Pillar 1 — Source Authentication (WHO is providing the software?)

- Always install from **official repositories** (e.g., Ubuntu's `archive.ubuntu.com`, Red Hat's `rhns.redhat.com`).
- For proprietary software, use **vendor-signed `.msi`, `.pkg`, `.dmg`, or `.deb`** files.
- Avoid **third-party download mirrors** unless they are cryptographically chained to the vendor's PKI.

#### Pillar 2 — Integrity Verification (Was the software TAMPERED with in transit?)

- The vendor publishes a **hash digest** (SHA-256, SHA-3) alongside the binary.
- The user re-computes the hash and **compares bit-by-bit**.
- Any mismatch ⇒ the package has been **MITM-attacked** or **trojanized** — abort installation.

#### Pillar 3 — Authenticity Verification (Is the binary really from the vendor?)

- A **digital signature** is attached to the package.
- The package manager (e.g., `apt`, `dnf`, `pacman`) maintains a **local trust store** of vendor public keys.
- Verification equation: 

$$ \text{Verify}_{PK_{\text{vendor}}}\bigl(\text{Sign}_{SK_{\text{vendor}}}\bigl(H(M)\bigr)\bigr) \stackrel{?}{=} H(M) $$

- If equality holds ⇒ **authentic**. If not ⇒ **forged**.

#### Pillar 4 — Sandboxed Pre-Installation Testing (What does the binary actually DO?)

- Modern EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response) systems run the installer in a **VM or container** before allowing it on the host.
- Static analysis: scan for **PE anomalies, packed sections, suspicious imports**.
- Dynamic analysis: observe **syscalls, file writes, network connections, registry edits**.

#### Pillar 5 — Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)

- The installer should request **only the permissions it absolutely needs**.
- Use **User Account Control (UAC)** on Windows, `sudo` with specific command lists on Linux.
- Avoid "Run as Administrator" unless the vendor explicitly documents why.

---

### 2.2 Patch Management — The Continuous Installation Cycle

Patches are a **special category of installation** that the KTU syllabus treats as part of the same Module 3 topic.

| Patch Type | Description | Reboot Required | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Critical Security Patch** | Closes actively exploited CVE | Usually yes | MS Patch Tuesday releases |
| **Important Patch** | Fixes high-severity bug, no known exploit | Sometimes | Monthly cumulative updates |
| **Moderate Patch** | Hardening or non-exploitable bug fix | No | TLS library deprecation fix |
| **Low / Informational** | Cosmetic or documentation | No | Readme updates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** is a frequent 3-mark question. Memorize that scores **0.1 – 3.9 = Low**, **4.0 – 6.9 = Medium**, **7.0 – 8.9 = High**, **9.0 – 10.0 = Critical**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below are the **mathematical foundations** of secure installation that you must memorize for KTU ESE. Every equation is **examiner-verifiable**.

| Concept | Equation / Formula | Notation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| SHA-256 Digest | $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{256}$ | $H(M)$ is the 256-bit hash of message $M$ | Verifying package integrity |
| Digital Signature | $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{SK}(H(M))$ | $SK$ = private key, $PK$ = public key | Authenticity check |
| Signature Verification | $\text{Verify}_{PK}(\sigma, H(M)) = \text{True}$ | Accepts if $\sigma$ was made with matching $SK$ | Vendor authentication |
| RSA Key Generation | $n = p \cdot q$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | $p,q$ = large primes ($\geq 1024$ bits each) | Underpins software signing |
| RSA Signature | $S \equiv H(M)^{d} \pmod{n}$ | $d$ = private exponent | Producing a signature |
| RSA Verification | $H(M) \equiv S^{e} \pmod{n}$ | $e$ = public exponent (commonly $65537$) | Verifying a signature |
| HMAC | $\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\bigl((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M)\bigr)$ | $K$ = secret key, $ipad, opad$ = padding constants | Used by `apt` for Release file |
| CVSS Base Score | $\text{BaseScore} = f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A)$ | $f$ = official CVSS v3.1 equation | Patch prioritization |
| Hash Mismatch Probability | $P_{\text{coll}} \approx \frac{n^2}{2^{257}}$ for $n$ attempts | Birthday bound on SHA-256 | Justifies 256-bit length |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Installation Security Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Banking & FinTech** | Signed mobile app binaries to prevent trojanized banking apps |
| **Healthcare (HIPAA)** | Verified patches on MRI machines and PACS servers |
| **Aerospace & Defense** | Multi-signature code-signing for avionics firmware updates |
| **Cloud & DevOps** | Signed container images verified by Kubernetes admission controllers (e.g., `cosign`) |
| **IoT & OT** | Secure boot + signed firmware over-the-air (FOTA) updates |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Why SHA-256 Produces a 256-bit Digest

SHA-256 operates on **512-bit message blocks** through **64 rounds** of compression. The final output is truncated to 256 bits.

**Step 1 — Padding**  
The message $M$ is padded to a multiple of 512 bits:

$$ M_{\text{pad}} = M \parallel 1 \parallel 0^{k} \parallel \vert M \vert_{64} $$

where $k$ is the smallest non-negative integer such that the total length becomes a multiple of 512, and $\vert M \vert_{64}$ is the 64-bit big-endian representation of the original message length.

> **Logic:** A '1' bit marks the end of the original message. Zero bits fill the gap, and the 64-bit length field prevents length-extension ambiguity.

**Step 2 — Initialization**  
Eight 32-bit working variables $H_0^{(0)}$ to $H_7^{(0)}$ are loaded with the first 32 bits of the fractional parts of the square roots of the first 8 primes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_0^{(0)} &= \texttt{6a09e667} \\
H_1^{(0)} &= \texttt{bb67ae85} \\
H_2^{(0)} &= \texttt{3c6ef372} \\
H_3^{(0)} &= \texttt{a54ff53a} \\
H_4^{(0)} &= \texttt{510e527f} \\
H_5^{(0)} &= \texttt{9b05688c} \\
H_6^{(0)} &= \texttt{1f83d9ab} \\
H_7^{(0)} &= \texttt{5be0cd19}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** These specific constants come from $\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}, \sqrt{5}, \sqrt{7}, \sqrt{11}, \sqrt{13}, \sqrt{17}, \sqrt{19}$. They are **not random** — they are public, deterministic IVs that allow anyone to reproduce the hash.

**Step 3 — Message Schedule Expansion**  
Each 512-bit block is expanded into 64 32-bit words $W_0, W_1, \dots, W_{63}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_t &= M_t^{(i)} \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le 15 \\
W_t &= \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16} \quad \text{for } 16 \le t \le 63
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\sigma_0$ and $\sigma_1$ are the standard SHA-256 sigma functions involving right-rotates and right-shifts.

> **Logic:** Expanding 16 words into 64 words creates **avalanche** — a 1-bit change in $M$ cascades through all 64 rounds.

**Step 4 — Compression Function**  
The 64-round compression function uses six logical functions: $\text{Ch}, \text{Maj}, \Sigma_0, \Sigma_1, \sigma_0, \sigma_1$. After 64 rounds, the working variables are added back to the hash state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_0^{(i+1)} &= H_0^{(i)} + a \\
H_1^{(i+1)} &= H_1^{(i)} + b \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
H_7^{(i+1)} &= H_7^{(i)} + h
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Addition (mod $2^{32}$) is non-invertible without the message schedule. This is the **one-way property** of SHA-256.

**Step 5 — Final Output**  
After processing all 512-bit blocks, the final hash is:

$$ H(M) = H_0^{(N)} \parallel H_1^{(N)} \parallel H_2^{(N)} \parallel H_3^{(N)} \parallel H_4^{(N)} \parallel H_5^{(N)} \parallel H_6^{(N)} \parallel H_7^{(N)} $$

This concatenation of 8 × 32 bits = **256 bits**, justifying the name "SHA-256."

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Digital Signature Verification of a Package

Given a package binary $P$, vendor private key $SK_v$, and a user holding the vendor's public key $PK_v$:

**Step 1 — Vendor Side (Signing):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \text{SHA-256}(P) \\
\sigma &= h^{d_v} \pmod{n_v} \quad \text{where } n_v = p_v \cdot q_v,\;\; d_v \equiv e_v^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n_v)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The vendor ships $(P, \sigma)$ to the user. Note that $h$ is **not encrypted** — it is **signed**, which is faster since $h$ is only 256 bits regardless of package size.

**Step 2 — User Side (Verification):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h' &= \text{SHA-256}(P_{\text{received}}) \\
v &= \sigma^{e_v} \pmod{n_v} \\
\text{Accept} &\iff v = h'
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** If the package $P$ was altered by even a single bit, $h' \neq h$ and verification fails. If $\sigma$ was forged without $d_v$, the attacker cannot produce a valid $\sigma^{e_v} \equiv h' \pmod{n_v}$ — this requires breaking RSA, which is computationally infeasible for $n_v \geq 2048$ bits.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Installation Mechanisms

| Platform | Package Manager | Default Signature Scheme | Hash Used | Update Channel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Debian/Ubuntu** | `apt` / `dpkg` | GPG over `Release` file | SHA-256 | `apt update && apt upgrade` |
| **Red Hat/Fedora** | `dnf` / `yum` / `rpm` | GPG per-package | SHA-256 | `dnf update` |
| **Arch Linux** | `pacman` | GPG per-package | md5, SHA-256 | `pacman -Syu` |
| **Windows** | `winget` / `MSIX` | Authenticode (RSA) | SHA-1/SHA-256 | Windows Update |
| **macOS** | `brew` / `Installer.app` | Apple Developer ID | SHA-256 | App Store / `softwareupdate` |
| **Python** | `pip` | PGP (PEP 458) | SHA-256 | `pip install --upgrade` |
| **Node.js** | `npm` | ECDSA | SHA-512 | `npm audit fix` |
| **Containers** | `docker` / `podman` | Cosign (Sigstore) | SHA-256 digest | `docker pull` |
| **Mobile (Android)** | Google Play | APK Signature v2/v3 | SHA-256 | Play Store auto-update |
| **Mobile (iOS)** | App Store | Apple FairPlay | SHA-256 | App Store auto-update |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked *"Compare package managers from a security perspective"*, this table is your **gold standard answer scaffold**. Always finish with the principle: *"Security is enforced by chain-of-trust, not by the package manager itself."*

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Verifying a Downloaded `.deb` Package

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic 7-mark KTU Part B sub-question**: *"Explain with an example how a user verifies the integrity and authenticity of a downloaded package."*

**Step 1 — Download the package and its signature from the vendor site:**

```
$ wget https://vendor.example.com/app.deb
$ wget https://vendor.example.com/app.deb.asc
```

**Step 2 — Verify the GPG signature:**

```
$ gpg --verify app.deb.asc app.deb
gpg: Good signature from "Vendor <vendor@vendor.example.com>"
```

> **Logic:** The GPG tool extracts $H(\text{app.deb})$, decrypts the signature using the vendor's public key (which is in your local keyring), and compares the two hashes.

**Step 3 — Independently verify the SHA-256 hash:**

```
$ sha256sum app.deb
a3f5b9c2...d4e7f1a8  app.deb
```

Cross-check this string with the hash published on the vendor's HTTPS-secured website.

**Step 4 — Install only after both checks pass:**

```
$ sudo apt install ./app.deb
```

> **Why both checks?**  
> - The GPG signature proves **authenticity** (it came from the vendor) AND **integrity** (it wasn't tampered with).  
> - The SHA-256 sum is a **defense-in-depth** check in case the GPG key itself was compromised — a manual comparison against a third channel (e.g., a TLS-secured website, a printed README, or a PGP-signed mailing-list announcement) is a **trust anchor**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Secure Installation Pipeline (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete decision-and-action flow** of installing an application securely. This is the highest-weightage visual you should be able to reproduce in the KTU exam hall.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Initiates Install] --> B{Source Identified?}
    B -- Official Repo --> C[TLS Handshake with Repo]
    B -- Vendor Website --> D[TLS 1.2 Plus Download]
    B -- Third Party --> E[BLOCK and Warn User]
    C --> F[Fetch Signed Index Files]
    D --> F
    F --> G{Verify GPG Signature}
    G -- Pass --> H[Download Package Binary]
    G -- Fail --> I[Abort and Log Incident]
    H --> J[Compute SHA-256 Hash Locally]
    J --> K{Hash Matches Vendor Hash}
    K -- Match --> L[Sandbox Static Analysis]
    K -- Mismatch --> M[Quarantine and Alert]
    L --> N{Suspicious Behavior Found}
    N -- None --> O[Request Elevation via UAC or sudo]
    N -- Detected --> P[Block and Submit to EDR]
    O --> Q[Install with Least Privilege]
    Q --> R[Enable Auto Update Channel]
    R --> S[Log to SIEM and Update Asset DB]
    S --> T[Installation Complete]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every diamond is a **decision gate**. Every rectangle is a **state**. The flow always terminates at `Installation Complete`, but the **security posture** at that terminal state depends on how many gates were properly enforced. A *shortcut* (e.g., skipping `Verify GPG Signature`) is the **#1 way malware enters a corporate network**.

---

### 4.2 Patch Management Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vulnerability Disclosed CVE Assigned] --> B[Vendor Develops Patch]
    B --> C[Internal QA and Staging Test]
    C --> D[Digital Signature Applied]
    D --> E[Release to Update Server]
    E --> F[Client Polls for Updates]
    F --> G{CVSS Score above 7.0}
    G -- Yes --> H[Force Critical Push]
    G -- No --> I[Schedule Standard Window]
    H --> J[User Reviews and Approves]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Apply Patch in Transactional Mode]
    K --> L{Install Succeeded}
    L -- Yes --> M[Reboot if Required]
    L -- No --> N[Rollback to Snapshot]
    M --> O[Verify Service Health]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Close Ticket and Report]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The **"Transactional Mode"** step is unique to modern Windows (`.msu`, `.msp` with Component-Based Servicing) and Linux (`dnf` with `package-cleanup --rollback`). KTU examiners love asking *"What happens if a patch fails mid-install?"* — the answer is **automated rollback to a known-good snapshot**, ensuring **atomicity** of the install operation.

---

### 4.3 Trust Chain for a Signed Package (Hierarchical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[Root CA Trust Anchor] --> ica[Intermediate CA Vendor]
    ica --> code[Code Signing Certificate]
    code --> bin[Signed Software Binary]
    bin --> hash1[SHA-256 Digest Embedded]
    bin --> ts[RFC 3161 Timestamp Token]
    hash1 --> verify[Verification Engine]
    ts --> verify
    verify --> result{Valid}
    result -- Yes --> exec[Allow Execution]
    result -- No --> block[Block and Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Timestamp?** The **RFC 3161 timestamp** proves the signature was valid **at the time of signing**, even if the code-signing certificate later expires. This is critical for long-lived binaries (e.g., firmware in medical devices). KTU has asked this as a 3-mark question in previous years.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
> *What is meant by a digital signature? How is it used to verify the authenticity of a software package?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **digital signature** is a cryptographic mechanism that proves the authenticity and integrity of a digital message. It uses **asymmetric key cryptography** — the signer uses a **private key ($SK$)** to sign, and the verifier uses the **public key ($PK$)** to verify.

For a software package $P$:

1. The vendor computes $H(P) = \text{SHA-256}(P)$ and signs it to produce $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{SK_v}(H(P))$.
2. The package manager on the user system, holding $PK_v$ in its trust store, recomputes $H'(P_{\text{received}})$ and checks $\text{Verify}_{PK_v}(\sigma, H'(P))$.
3. If the verification returns `True`, the package is **authentic** (came from the vendor) and **intact** (not tampered with).

*Split: 1 mark definition, 1 mark process, 1 mark verdict.* ✅

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, S8 Supple]**  
> *Briefly explain the role of a package manager in enforcing system security during software installation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **package manager** (e.g., `apt`, `dnf`, `pacman`) enforces security during installation by:

1. **Maintaining a cryptographic trust store** of vendor public keys for signature verification.
2. **Verifying GPG/RSA signatures** on every package and its metadata (`Release`, `InRelease` files).
3. **Resolving dependencies securely** to prevent **dependency-confusion attacks** and **supply-chain injection**.
4. **Enforcing transactional installs** with automatic rollback on failure.
5. **Tracking installed files** in a local database to detect tampering (`debsums`, `rpm -V`).

*Split: 1 mark introduction, 2 marks for any four roles listed.* ✅

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-3 Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, S8 Regular]**  
> **(a)** Explain the **five-pillar framework** for secure application installation with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*  
> **(b)** Describe the **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** and explain how it is used to prioritize patch deployment. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

The **five-pillar framework** for secure application installation comprises:

1. **Source Authentication:** Software must be sourced from **official, cryptographically verified repositories** (e.g., Ubuntu's `archive.ubuntu.com` over HTTPS). Third-party mirrors are accepted only if they are chained to the vendor's PKI. *Example:* Installing Docker only from `download.docker.com` with GPG key `9DC858229FC7DD38854AE2D88D81803C0EBFCD88` imported.

2. **Integrity Verification:** After download, the user re-computes the **SHA-256 hash** of the package and compares it with the vendor-published value. *Example:* `sha256sum docker.deb` must match the hash on the official site.

3. **Authenticity Verification:** The package's **digital signature** is verified against the vendor's public key stored in the local trust ring. *Example:* `gpg --verify docker.deb.asc docker.deb` must return *"Good signature"*.

4. **Sandboxed Pre-Installation Testing:** The installer is run in a **virtual machine, container, or sandbox** to observe its behavior — system calls, file writes, network connections — before granting it host access. *Example:* Windows Defender SmartScreen runs `.msi` installers in an isolated environment first.

5. **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP):** The installer requests **only the minimum permissions necessary**. UAC prompts on Windows and `sudo` invocations on Linux enforce this. *Example:* A text editor installer should not need `CAP_NET_RAW` capability.

*Split: 1.4 marks per pillar × 5 = 7 marks.* ✅

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** is an open framework for communicating the **characteristics and severity of software vulnerabilities**. CVSS v3.1 computes a **Base Score** between **0.0 and 10.0** based on eight metrics:

| Metric Group | Metrics | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Exploitability** | AV, AC, PR, UI | How easy is it to exploit? |
| **Impact** | C, I, A | What damage does it cause? |
| **Scope** | S | Does it cross security boundaries? |

**Severity Bands:**

- **None:** 0.0
- **Low:** 0.1 – 3.9
- **Medium:** 4.0 – 6.9
- **High:** 7.0 – 8.9
- **Critical:** 9.0 – 10.0

**Use in Patch Prioritization:**

1. **Critical (≥ 9.0):** Patch within **24 hours** — out-of-cycle emergency change.
2. **High (7.0 – 8.9):** Patch within the **next maintenance window** (typically ≤ 7 days).
3. **Medium (4.0 – 6.9):** Patch within **30 days** as part of standard monthly cycle.
4. **Low (≤ 3.9):** Bundle with the next **quarterly cumulative update**.

The official CVSS equation is:

$$ \text{BaseScore} = \text{roundUp}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\; 10)) $$

where Impact and Exploitability are themselves sub-equations of the eight metrics.

*Split: 2 marks for CVSS overview, 2 marks for severity bands, 2 marks for patch-priority mapping, 1 mark for the equation.* ✅

---

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, S8 Regular — OR Option]**  
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **end-to-end secure installation pipeline** for a software package. *(7 marks)*  
> **(b)** Discuss the **patch management lifecycle** and the role of **transactional installs with rollback** in maintaining system stability. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

*(See Section 4.1 diagram above. Walk through the flow step-by-step.)*

The **end-to-end secure installation pipeline** consists of seven stages:

1. **Source Identification:** Determine whether the source is an official repository, a vendor site, or a third party. Third-party sources are blocked by default.
2. **Transport Security:** All downloads occur over **TLS 1.2 or higher** to prevent eavesdropping and MITM attacks.
3. **Index Verification:** Repository metadata (`Release`, `InRelease`) is verified using the repository's **GPG key**.
4. **Package Download:** The signed binary is fetched.
5. **Hash Verification:** A local **SHA-256 computation** is compared against the vendor-published hash.
6. **Sandbox Analysis:** The installer is executed in an **isolated VM** to detect malicious behavior statically (e.g., packed PE sections) and dynamically (e.g., outbound C2 connections).
7. **Elevated Install with PoLP:** The installer is granted only the **specific capabilities it needs** via UAC or scoped `sudo` rules.

*Split: 1 mark per stage × 7 = 7 marks.* ✅

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

The **patch management lifecycle** has six phases (see Section 4.2 diagram):

1. **Vulnerability Discovery** — A CVE is assigned by MITRE.
2. **Patch Development** — Vendor writes and internally tests the fix.
3. **Digital Signing & Release** — The patch is signed and pushed to update servers.
4. **Client Polling & Deployment** — Endpoints check for updates; critical CVSS ≥ 7.0 patches are force-pushed.
5. **Transactional Install with Rollback** — The patch is applied in **transactional mode**, which captures a **system snapshot** before installation. If any step of the install fails (e.g., corrupted binary, dependency conflict, post-install script error), the system **automatically rolls back** to the snapshot.
6. **Verification & Reporting** — Service health is monitored, and the change is logged in the SIEM and ITSM.

**Why Transactional Installs Matter:**

- **Atomicity:** Either the patch is fully applied, or the system remains in its pre-patch state. There is no "half-patched" inconsistency.
- **Downtime Minimization:** Rollback is faster than manual uninstallation, crucial for **SLA-bound production systems**.
- **Compliance:** Many regulatory frameworks (PCI-DSS 6.3, ISO 27001 A.12.6.1) require evidence of **controlled, reversible change management**.

*Split: 3 marks for lifecycle phases, 2 marks for transactional install mechanics, 2 marks for compliance/atomicity value.* ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Reasons:**
> 1. **Confusing *integrity* with *authenticity*.** Integrity = "Was the package modified?" Authenticity = "Did the vendor really send it?" Both are checked, but by **different mechanisms** (hash vs. signature). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for mixing them.
> 2. **Writing "MD5 is enough for integrity."** MD5 is **broken** for collision resistance. Always use **SHA-256 minimum**, and SHA-3 for new designs.
> 3. **Forgetting TLS in the answer.** Almost every installation pipeline diagram loses a mark if the download arrow isn't labeled **"HTTPS / TLS 1.2+"**.
> 4. **Saying "patch" instead of "update" or vice versa.** KTU 2024 scheme uses **patch** for security fixes and **update** for feature additions. Examiners notice.
> 5. **Skipping the rollback step** in patch management. This is a **guaranteed 1-mark loss** in any 7-mark patch-management question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Installing Applications**

- **Definition:** Installation is a **trust delegation event** — treat it as such.
- **Five Pillars:** Source, Integrity, Authenticity, Sandbox, PoLP.
- **Hash Function:** Always **SHA-256 or stronger**; **never MD5/SHA-1** for new designs.
- **Digital Signature Equation:** $S = H(M)^d \pmod{n}$ — verify with $H(M) = S^e \pmod{n}$.
- **Package Manager Trust Store:** Local keyring of vendor GPG/RSA public keys.
- **CVSS Bands:** Low (0.1–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), Critical (9.0–10.0).
- **Patch Priority SLA:** Critical = 24h, High = 7d, Medium = 30d, Low = quarterly.
- **Transactional Installs:** Snapshot before, rollback on failure, atomicity guaranteed.
- **Transport Security:** **TLS 1.2+** mandatory for all repository communications.
- **Timestamp Token (RFC 3161):** Proves signature validity at signing time, survives cert expiry.
- **Defense in Depth:** Even after a valid GPG check, **independently verify the SHA-256 hash** through a second channel.
- **Code Signing Hierarchy:** Root CA → Intermediate CA → Code Signing Cert → Signed Binary.
- **Real-World Tools:** `apt`, `dnf`, `pacman`, `winget`, `brew`, `cosign`, `gpg`, `sha256sum`, `rpm -V`, `debsums`.
- **Exam Mantra:** *"Verify before you trust, sandbox before you elevate, rollback before you commit."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authentication and Access Control (Windows)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-3
> - **Topic:** Authentication and Access Control (Windows)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Authentication in Windows

**Authentication** is the process of verifying the identity of a user, process, or system before granting access to protected resources. In the Windows operating system, authentication is the **first line of defense** and the gateway to all subsequent security operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> Windows Authentication is a two-stage process comprising (1) **Interactive Logon** — where a user supplies credentials to prove identity, and (2) **Network Authentication** — where these credentials are validated against a security authority (Local SAM database or Active Directory Domain Controller) to issue a **Logon Session** governed by an **Access Token**.

### 1.2 Access Control in Windows

**Access Control** is the mechanism that determines *what an authenticated user is permitted to do*. After authentication, Windows enforces authorization through a model known as **Discretionary Access Control (DAC)** — every **Securable Object** (files, folders, registry keys, services, processes) is protected by a **Security Descriptor** that lists who can read, write, execute, or take ownership.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Highlight:**
> The Windows security architecture is built upon four foundational pillars:
> 1. **Security Principals** (users, groups, services)
> 2. **Security Identifiers (SIDs)** (unique numeric identifiers)
> 3. **Access Tokens** (credentials cached at logon)
> 4. **Security Descriptors** (DACL + SACL + Owner)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Windows security as a **high-security corporate office building**:

| Real-World Element | Windows Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Visiting card / Photo ID at the gate | **Logon Credentials (Password / Smart Card / PIN)** | Proves *who you are* (Authentication) |
| Reception desk issuing a visitor badge | **LSASS issuing an Access Token** | Caches your identity for the day |
| List of rooms each badge can open | **Access Token's Privileges & SIDs** | Determines *what you can reach* |
| Door with an electronic lock on each office | **Security Descriptor on a Securable Object** | Controls entry to that specific resource |
| CCTV cameras recording entry attempts | **SACL (System ACL / Audit)** | Tracks *who tried what* (Auditing) |

**Plain English Summary:** When you log into Windows, you prove your identity (Authentication). Windows then gives your process an **Access Token** (a badge). Every time your process tries to open a file or change a setting, the file's **Security Descriptor** (the door lock) checks your badge and says "yes" or "no" (Access Control).

### 1.4 Key Metrics & Standard Values in Windows

- **SID Length:** Variable length (typically **84 bits / 28 bytes** for user SIDs, max 68 bytes including domain prefix)
- **RID (Relative Identifier):** The portion that identifies the principal within a domain (e.g., Administrator = **500**, Guest = **501**)
- **Local SAM Database Path:** `%SystemRoot%\system32\config\SAM`
- **NTLM Hash Length:** **128 bits (16 bytes)** — MD4 hash of UTF-16LE password
- **Default Kerberos Ticket Lifetime:** **10 hours (TGT)** with **7-day renewal**

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examinations, you are expected to remember the **Built-in RID values** (500 = Administrator, 501 = Guest, 512 = Domain Admins) and the difference between **Authentication (who)** and **Authorization (what)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Windows Security Architecture Stack

Windows security operates in a **layered, modular architecture**. The flow can be broken into discrete components:

**Layer 1 — User Interaction (Logon UI)**
- `Winlogon.exe` presents the secure attention sequence (**SAS = Ctrl + Alt + Del**)
- Captures credentials via the **Credential Provider API** (modern) or **GINA** (legacy, pre-Vista)

**Layer 2 — Authentication Subsystem (`LSASS.exe`)**
- **Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS)** is the heart of Windows authentication
- Validates credentials against either the **Local SAM** (workgroup) or the **Active Directory Domain Controller** (domain)
- Generates the **Access Token** upon successful authentication

**Layer 3 — Authentication Protocols**
- **NTLM (NT LAN Manager)** — Challenge-Response, used in workgroups
- **Kerberos v5** — Ticket-based, default for Active Directory domains
- **Negotiate (SPNEGO)** — Selects between NTLM and Kerberos

**Layer 4 — Authorization Subsystem**
- The **Security Reference Monitor (SRM)** in kernel mode enforces access decisions
- Compares the **Access Token** against the **Security Descriptor** of the requested object

**Layer 5 — Securable Objects & Resource Managers**
- NTFS, Registry, Print Spooler, Services, Processes, Named Pipes — each has its own resource manager that consults the SRM

### 2.2 The Authentication Process — Step-by-Step Logic

#### A. Interactive Logon (Local Account)

1. User presses **Ctrl+Alt+Del** → generates the **Secure Attention Sequence (SAS)**
2. `Winlogon` invokes the **Credential Provider** to collect username + password
3. LSASS hashes the password using **NTLM Hash = MD4(UTF-16LE(password))**
4. LSASS queries the **SAM database** (in the registry hive `%SystemRoot%\system32\config\SAM`)
5. If the stored hash matches → credentials are accepted
6. LSASS creates a **Logon Session** and crafts an **Access Token**
7. The token is attached to the user's initial process (`explorer.exe`)

#### B. Interactive Logon (Domain Account — Kerberos)

1. User enters credentials → `Winlogon` → LSASS
2. LSASS contacts the **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** on a Domain Controller
3. **AS-REQ** (Authentication Service Request) sent with pre-authentication data
4. KDC verifies the principal's secret key → issues a **TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket)** in **AS-REP**
5. The TGT is encrypted with the **krbtgt account's secret key** (only the KDC can decrypt it)
6. When the user needs a service ticket → LSASS sends **TGS-REQ** with the TGT
7. KDC responds with **TGS-REP** containing a **Service Ticket** for the target resource
8. Client presents the Service Ticket to the application server in **AP-REQ**
9. The application server grants access without re-contacting the KDC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Kerberos over NTLM?**
> Kerberos supports **mutual authentication** (the server also proves its identity), **delegation**, and **transitive trust**. NTLM is a fallback for legacy systems, workgroups, or when DNS/KDC is unreachable.

### 2.3 Security Identifiers (SIDs) — The Identity Token

A **SID** is a variable-length numeric value that uniquely identifies a security principal. Structure:

```
S-1-5-21-XXXXXXXXXX-XXXXXXXXXX-YYYYYYYYYY-RID
│  │ │  │            │            │           │
│  │ │  │            │            │           └─ Relative ID (principal within domain)
│  │ │  │            │            └─ Sub-authority 2 (domain identifier)
│  │ │  │            └─ Sub-authority 1 (domain identifier)  
│  │ │  └─ Sub-authority 0 (domain identifier, 96 bits)
│  │ └─ Revision (always 1)
│  └─ Identifier Authority (5 = NT Authority)
└─ "S" prefix
```

**Built-in SIDs (Frequently Asked in KTU):**

| SID | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `S-1-1-0` | Everyone (World) |
| `S-1-3-0` | Creator Owner |
| `S-1-5-11` | Authenticated Users |
| `S-1-5-32-544` | Built-in Administrators |
| `S-1-5-32-545` | Built-in Users |
| `S-1-5-32-546` | Built-in Guests |
| `S-1-5-domain-500` | Domain Administrator |
| `S-1-5-domain-501` | Domain Guest |
| `S-1-5-domain-512` | Domain Admins group |

### 2.4 Access Tokens — The Authorization Passport

An **Access Token** is a kernel-mode object attached to every process and thread. It contains:

- **User SID** — the principal's identity
- **Group SIDs** — all groups the user is a member of (including nested group expansion)
- **Privileges** — special rights (e.g., `SeBackupPrivilege`, `SeDebugPrivilege`, `SeShutdownPrivilege`)
- **Default DACL** — applied to objects created by the user
- **Impersonation Level** — whether the token can be used to impersonate
- **Source (Primary vs. Impersonation)** — primary tokens identify processes; impersonation tokens represent a client

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Token vs. Impersonation Token:**
> - A **Primary Token** is assigned once at process creation and represents the process's default security context.
> - An **Impersonation Token** is temporarily attached to a thread (e.g., when a service must act on behalf of a client RPC caller).

### 2.5 Security Descriptors — The Door Locks

Every securable object carries a **Security Descriptor** with these components:

| Component | Purpose | Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Owner SID** | Who owns the object (can change DACL) | SID |
| **Primary Group** | Used only for POSIX compatibility | SID |
| **DACL** | Discretionary ACL — who can access | List of ACEs |
| **SACL** | System ACL — what to audit | List of ACEs |
| **Control Flags** | Bits controlling inheritance, protection, etc. | Bitmask |

**Access Control Entry (ACE) — the atomic permission rule:**

| ACE Field | Description |
|---|---|
| **Type** | `0 = Allow`, `1 = Deny`, `2 = Audit`, `5 = Alarm` |
| **Flags** | Inheritance flags (Object Inherit, Container Inherit, etc.) |
| **Mask** | 32-bit access mask specifying the rights |
| **SID** | The principal to whom this ACE applies |

**Standard Access Rights Mask (commonly tested):**

| Bit (Hex) | Right | Applies To |
|---|---|---|
| `0x0001` | `READ_CONTROL` | Read security descriptor |
| `0x0002` | `WRITE_DAC` | Modify DACL |
| `0x0004` | `WRITE_OWNER` | Take ownership |
| `0x0010` | `DELETE` | Delete the object |
| `0x0020` | `SYNCHRONIZE` | Wait on the object |
| `0x10000` | `FILE_READ_DATA` | Read file contents |
| `0x20000` | `FILE_WRITE_DATA` | Write file contents |
| `0x40000` | `FILE_APPEND_DATA` | Append to file |
| `0x100000` | `FILE_READ_EA` | Read extended attributes |
| `0x80000000` | `GENERIC_ALL` | All access |
| `0xC0000000` | `GENERIC_READ` | Read access |
| `0xA0000000` | `GENERIC_WRITE` | Write access |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authenticate-then-Authorize Equation (Conceptual):**
> $$\text{Access Granted} \iff \exists \text{ ACE}_{\text{Allow}} \in \text{DACL} : \text{SID}_{\text{Token}} \subseteq \text{SID}_{\text{ACE}} \land \text{Desired Access} \subseteq \text{Access Mask}$$
> **AND** no preceding $\text{ACE}_{\text{Deny}}$ matches the token SID for any requested access bit.

**DACL Evaluation Order (Mandatory ACE ordering):**

| Step | Rule |
|---|---|
| 1 | All **Explicit Deny** ACEs are evaluated first (top of DACL) |
| 2 | All **Explicit Allow** ACEs are evaluated second |
| 3 | If no ACE matches and the DACL contains an ACE, access is **DENIED** (deny by default) |
| 4 | If the DACL is **NULL (empty)**, the object is **accessible to everyone** |
| 5 | If the DACL is **missing** entirely, only the **owner** can access (full access) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** A **NULL DACL ≠ Empty DACL**. A NULL DACL (not present) grants **full access to Everyone**. An **Empty DACL** (present but no ACEs) grants **NO access to anyone**. Examiners love to test this distinction.

**NTLM Authentication Protocol (Challenge-Response Steps):**

| Step | Notation | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C \to S$ | Client sends username (plaintext) |
| 2 | $S \to C$ | Server sends **8-byte challenge** $N_c$ |
| 3 | $C$ | Client computes $R_1 = \text{DES}(\text{NTLMHash}, N_c)$ |
| 4 | $C \to S$ | Client sends $R_1$ |
| 5 | $S$ | Server retrieves stored hash, recomputes expected response |
| 6 | $S$ | If responses match → authentication succeeds |

### 2.7 User Account Control (UAC) — The Privilege Isolation Mechanism

Introduced in **Windows Vista (2007)**, UAC is a mandatory access control policy for applications.

**Operation:**
- Standard users run with a **filtered token** (medium integrity level)
- The **Administrator** user has **two tokens**: a *filtered standard token* (used for normal apps) and a *full elevated token* (used only after consent)
- Any process requiring elevation triggers the **UAC consent dialog** (consent.exe)
- Integrity levels: **Low (IE Protected Mode) < Medium < High < System**

> [!NOTE]
> **UAC does NOT prevent malware**, it only prevents *unauthorized elevation*. A user clicking "Yes" on a UAC prompt defeats the protection.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Enterprise IT Administration** | Centralized authentication via Active Directory eliminates per-machine password management |
| **Penetration Testing** | Understanding Kerberos tickets enables detection of **Pass-the-Ticket** and **Golden Ticket** attacks |
| **Incident Response** | SACL auditing tracks file access for forensic reconstruction |
| **Secure Software Development** | Least-privilege DACLs and access token design are foundational to secure coding |
| **Compliance** | Auditing (SACLs) is required by standards like PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOX |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Access Check Algorithm

Windows uses a deterministic algorithm to evaluate access requests. Below is the formal step-by-step derivation as required by the KTU analytical framework.

**Given:**
- A process holds an **Access Token** $T$ with the set of SIDs:

$$ T = \{\text{SID}_{\text{user}}, \text{SID}_{g_1}, \text{SID}_{g_2}, \ldots, \text{SID}_{g_n}, \text{SID}_{\text{integrity}}, \text{SID}_{\text{capabilities}}\} $$

- A **Securable Object** $O$ has a **DACL** containing a sequence of ACEs:

$$ \text{DACL} = [\text{ACE}_1, \text{ACE}_2, \ldots, \text{ACE}_k] $$

- The process requests a set of **Desired Access Rights** $D \subseteq \mathbb{A}$ where $\mathbb{A}$ is the universe of all access rights.

**Step 1 — ACE Ordering Normalization**

The DACL must be canonically ordered. Windows uses a three-level sort:

$$ \text{Sort}(\text{DACL}) = \text{Sort}_{\text{type}}(\text{Sort}_{\text{inheritance}}(\text{Sort}_{\text{deny-first}}(\text{DACL}))) $$

This means **Explicit Deny ACEs** are placed first, **Explicit Allow ACEs** come second, and **Inherited ACEs** are placed last.

**Step 2 — Iterative ACE Evaluation**

Initialize the **Granted Set** $G = \emptyset$ and **Denied Set** $X = \emptyset$.

For each ACE $i$ from $1$ to $k$:

$$ \text{If } (\text{SID}_{\text{ACE}_i} \in T) \land (\text{Type}_{\text{ACE}_i} = \text{Deny}) $$

$$ \text{Then } X = X \cup \text{Mask}_{\text{ACE}_i} $$

$$ \text{If } (\text{SID}_{\text{ACE}_i} \in T) \land (\text{Type}_{\text{ACE}_i} = \text{Allow}) $$

$$ \text{Then } G = G \cup \text{Mask}_{\text{ACE}_i} $$

**Step 3 — Privilege and Integrity Check**

Let $P$ be the set of privileges held in the token. Let $I_T$ be the integrity level of the token and $I_O$ be the integrity level of the object.

$$ \text{Effective Integrity} = I_T \geq I_O $$

If $I_T < I_O$ → access is **DENIED** (mandatory integrity check) regardless of DACL outcome.

**Step 4 — Final Decision**

$$ \text{Access Granted} \iff (D \cap X = \emptyset) \land (D \subseteq G) \land (I_T \geq I_O) $$

**Step 5 — Null vs Empty DACL Edge Cases**

$$ \text{If } \text{DACL} = \text{NULL} \rightarrow G = \mathbb{A} \quad \text{(full access to Everyone)} $$

$$ \text{If } \text{DACL} = \emptyset \rightarrow G = \emptyset \quad \text{(no access for anyone)} $$

### 3.2 Worked Example — Full Access Check

**Scenario:** A user **Alice** (SID = `S-1-5-21-100-500`) attempts to **read and write** a file `report.docx`. Her token also contains group SIDs: `S-1-5-32-544` (Administrators) and `S-1-5-32-545` (Users).

The file's DACL contains:

| # | Type | SID | Mask (Hex) | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Deny | S-1-1-0 (Everyone) | 0x10000 | Deny READ |
| 2 | Allow | S-1-5-32-544 (Admins) | 0xFFFFFFFF | Full access |
| 3 | Allow | S-1-5-32-545 (Users) | 0x20000 | WRITE only |
| 4 | Allow | S-1-5-21-100-500 (Alice) | 0x10000 | READ only |

**Step 1: Identify Token SIDs**

$$ T = \{\text{S-1-5-21-100-500}, \text{S-1-5-32-544}, \text{S-1-5-32-545}\} $$

**Step 2: Desired Access**

$$ D = \{0x10000 \, (\text{READ}), \, 0x20000 \, (\text{WRITE})\} $$

**Step 3: Evaluate ACE 1 — Deny Everyone READ**

Since `S-1-1-0` ∉ $T$ (Alice is an authenticated user, not the literal "Everyone" unprivileged SID — note: in practice `S-1-1-0` IS the Everyone SID and **is** always added to every token including anonymous), we must clarify:

In Windows, **all tokens implicitly include the `S-1-1-0` (Everyone) SID**. So:

$$ \text{S-1-1-0} \in T \implies X = \{0x10000\} \quad \text{(READ is denied)} $$

**Step 4: Evaluate ACE 2 — Allow Admins Full Access**

$$ \text{S-1-5-32-544} \in T \implies G = \{0xFFFFFFFF\} \text{ — but this happens AFTER the deny evaluation} $$

**Step 5: Evaluate ACE 3 — Allow Users WRITE**

$$ \text{S-1-5-32-545} \in T \implies G = G \cup \{0x20000\} = \{0x20000\} $$

**Step 6: Evaluate ACE 4 — Allow Alice READ**

$$ \text{S-1-5-21-100-500} \in T \implies G = G \cup \{0x10000\} = \{0x20000\} $$

**Step 7: Final Decision**

$$ D \cap X = \{0x10000\} \cap \{0x10000\} = \{0x10000\} \neq \emptyset $$

$$\text{Therefore, ACCESS IS DENIED — even though ACE 2 grants Admins full access, the Explicit Deny to Everyone in ACE 1 takes precedence.}$$

**Resolution:** Remove ACE 1 or change the denied right. The Deny ACE prevents Alice from reading, even though her Admin membership would normally permit it.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Authentication Protocols

| Feature | NTLM | Kerberos v5 |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust Model** | Trusted third-party (Domain Controller) | Same, but DC = Key Distribution Center |
| **Authentication Type** | Challenge-Response (3-leg) | Ticket-based (3-party: Client, KDC, Service) |
| **Mutual Auth** | **No** (server identity not verified) | **Yes** (server ticket is encrypted) |
| **Delegation** | Limited (constrained) | Full (constrained, unconstrained, resource-based) |
| **Single Sign-On** | Limited | **Native** |
| **Cryptographic Strength** | MD4 + RC4 (legacy) | AES-256 (modern), 3DES, RC4 |
| **Default Port** | 445 (SMB) / 139 (NetBIOS) | 88 (KDC) |
| **Required DNS Setup** | None | **Strict** (SPN registration required) |
| **Performance** | 1 round-trip to DC | 2 round-trips (AS-REQ/AS-REP + TGS-REQ/TGS-REP) |
| **Offline Attack Surface** | **Vulnerable** (pass-the-hash) | **Resistant** (tickets expire, encrypted) |
| **Ticket Lifetime** | N/A | TGT: 10h, Service Ticket: 10h |
| **Default in Windows AD** | Fallback only | **Yes (since Windows 2000)** |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Object Types and Their DACL Behavior

| Object Type | Resource Manager | Default Owner | Default DACL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NTFS File/Folder** | NTFS.sys | Creator | Inherits from parent |
| **Registry Key** | Registry Manager | Creator | Inherits from parent |
| **Process** | Process Manager | Creator (becomes SYSTEM for services) | Restricted |
| **Thread** | Thread Manager | Process owner | Restricted |
| **Service** | SCM (services.exe) | LocalSystem | Locked down (only Admins) |
| **Named Pipe** | NPFS.sys | Creator | Custom |
| **Printer** | Print Spooler | Spooler service | Custom per ACL |
| **Shared Folder** | Server service | Creator | Custom (default: Everyone Read) |
| **Active Directory Object** | NTDS | Domain Admins | Per-schema defaults |

### 3.5 UAC Elevation Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Process Launch**

A user double-clicks `installer.exe`. The shell calls `CreateProcess()`.

**Step 2 — Manifest Inspection**

The application's **application manifest** (XML embedded in the EXE) declares:

```xml
<requestedExecutionLevel level="requireAdministrator" />
```

If `level = asInvoker` → runs with current token, no prompt.
If `level = highestAvailable` → prompts only for Admins.
If `level = requireAdministrator` → always prompts.

**Step 3 — Consent Prompt Generation**

If the process requires elevation, the AppInfo service (`appinfo.dll` / `consent.exe`) shows a prompt on the **Secure Desktop** (isolated window station).

**Step 4 — Token Switch**

If the user clicks **Yes**, the process is re-launched using the user's **full (elevated) token**. If **No**, the process is not started.

**Step 5 — Integrity Level Assignment**

| Token | Integrity Level | UAC Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Standard user | Medium | Cannot elevate |
| Admin (filtered) | Medium | Can prompt for elevation |
| Admin (full) | High | Already elevated |
| SYSTEM | System | Above UAC (not subject to prompts) |

### 4.4 Kerberos Authentication Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Client
    participant LS as LSASS
    participant KDC as Key Distribution Center
    participant S as Target Service Server

    U->>LS: Submit credentials
    LS->>KDC: AS-REQ (Pre-auth with hashed timestamp)
    KDC-->>LS: AS-REP (TGT encrypted with krbtgt key)
    LS->>KDC: TGS-REQ (TGT + SPN of target service)
    KDC-->>LS: TGS-REP (Service Ticket encrypted with service account key)
    LS->>S: AP-REQ (Service Ticket + Authenticator)
    S-->>LS: AP-REP (Mutual authentication, optional)
    S-->>U: Access Granted
```

### 4.5 UAC Elevation Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    launch["Application Launch Requested"]
    manifest{"Manifest Level?"}
    asInvoker["Run with current token (Medium Integrity)"]
    highest["Prompt if Admin, else run as standard"]
    require["Always prompt for elevation"]
    consent["UAC Consent Dialog on Secure Desktop"]
    userYes{"User clicks Yes?"}
    elevated["Launch with Full Admin Token (High Integrity)"]
    denied["Process Not Started"]

    launch --> manifest
    manifest -->|"asInvoker"| asInvoker
    manifest -->|"highestAvailable"| highest
    manifest -->|"requireAdministrator"| require
    require --> consent
    highest --> consent
    consent --> userYes
    userYes -->|"Yes"| elevated
    userYes -->|"No"| denied
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sysL["SYSTEM (Highest) — Services, OS components"]
    highL["HIGH — Elevated Admin processes"]
    medL["MEDIUM — Standard user processes (Default)"]
    lowL["LOW — Internet Explorer Protected Mode, Sandboxed apps"]
    untrusted["UNTRUSTED — Anonymous access"]

    sysL -->|"Inherits down"| highL
    highL -->|"Inherits down"| medL
    medL -->|"Inherits down"| lowL
    lowL -->|"Inherits down"| untrusted

    sysL -."No write down allowed".-> lowL
    highL -."No write down allowed".-> lowL
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Authentication and Authorization. How does Windows implement both?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Authentication | Authorization |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Verifies *who* the user is | Determines *what* the user can do |
| **Stage** | First (pre-logon) | Second (post-logon) |
| **Mechanism in Windows** | LSASS validates credentials via Kerberos/NTLM against SAM/AD | Security Reference Monitor (SRM) checks Access Token against Security Descriptor DACL |
| **Output** | Logon Session + Access Token | Access Allowed / Denied decision |
| **Component** | `LSASS.exe`, `Kerberos.dll`, `Msv1_0.dll` | `Secursrv.dll`, kernel-mode SRM |

Windows first **authenticates** the user by validating their credentials and producing an **Access Token**. The token is then used in every subsequent **authorization** check when accessing a securable object (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for Windows components, 1 mark for sequence flow).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is a Security Identifier (SID)? Explain the structure with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Security Identifier (SID)** is a unique variable-length numeric value assigned to every security principal (user, group, computer, service) by Windows (1 mark).

**Structure of `S-1-5-21-1234567890-1234567890-1234567890-500`:**

| Component | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `S` | Prefix | Indicates SID |
| `1` | Revision | Always 1 |
| `5` | Identifier Authority | NT Authority |
| `21` | Sub-authority | Domain identifier (NDB) |
| `1234567890` × 3 | Sub-authorities | Unique domain ID |
| `500` | RID | Relative ID (500 = Administrator) |

The SID is **never reused** — even if an account is deleted and recreated, it gets a new SID (1 mark for structure, 1 mark for example and non-reuse property).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the Windows Authentication process in detail for a domain-joined computer using the Kerberos protocol. List the key components involved.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the structure of a Security Descriptor in Windows. Differentiate between DACL and SACL with examples.

**Model Solutions:**

**(a) Windows Domain Authentication via Kerberos (7 marks):**

**Components Involved (2 marks):**
1. **Client Machine** — User's workstation running `Winlogon`
2. **LSASS.exe** — Local Security Authority Subsystem Service
3. **Credential Provider** — Modern API for capturing credentials
4. **KDC (Key Distribution Center)** — Service on the Domain Controller (3 sub-services: AS, TGS, Authentication Service)
5. **Target Service Server** — The resource the user wants to access (e.g., file server, mail server)
6. **Active Directory Database** — Stores principal credentials (hashed)

**Step-by-Step Process (5 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Initial Authentication (AS-REQ / AS-REP)**
1. User enters username and password on the secure desktop
2. Winlogon forwards the credentials to LSASS
3. LSASS computes the NT hash of the password and uses it as the long-term secret key
4. LSASS constructs an **AS-REQ** message containing:
   - Client principal name
   - Timestamp encrypted with the user's secret key (pre-authentication)
   - Requested TGT lifetime
5. LSASS sends the AS-REQ to the KDC
6. The KDC decrypts the pre-auth data using the stored hash for that user
7. If valid, the KDC creates a **TGT** encrypted with the **krbtgt account's secret key** (no one but the KDC can decrypt it)
8. The TGT contains: client SID, group memberships, ticket lifetime, and a **session key** encrypted with the user's secret key
9. The KDC responds with **AS-REP** containing the TGT + session key

**Stage 2 — Service Ticket Acquisition (TGS-REQ / TGS-REP)**
10. User wants to access a service (e.g., `cifs/fileserver.domain.com`)
11. LSASS sends **TGS-REQ** to the KDC containing:
    - The TGT obtained earlier
    - The **SPN (Service Principal Name)** of the target service
    - An authenticator encrypted with the session key
12. The KDC decrypts the TGT, validates the authenticator, and creates a **Service Ticket**
13. The service ticket is encrypted with the **service account's secret key**
14. The KDC responds with **TGS-REP** containing the service ticket + a new client-service session key

**Stage 3 — Service Access (AP-REQ / AP-REP)**
15. LSASS sends **AP-REQ** to the target service containing:
    - The service ticket
    - A new authenticator encrypted with the client-service session key
16. The service decrypts the ticket using its secret key, obtains the client-service session key
17. The service validates the authenticator
18. The service establishes a secure session — **mutual authentication** is achieved if the service sends back **AP-REP**
19. User is granted access; the entire process is **transparent (SSO)** for subsequent services within the ticket lifetime

---

**(b) Security Descriptor, DACL vs SACL (7 marks):**

**Security Descriptor Structure (3 marks):**

A **Security Descriptor** is a data structure attached to every securable object in Windows. It contains the following components:

| Component | Purpose | Size (typical) |
|---|---|---|
| **Owner SID** | Identifies the owner (can modify DACL) | Variable |
| **Primary Group** | Used for POSIX-style group ownership | Variable |
| **DACL** | Discretionary ACL — discretionary access control | Variable list of ACEs |
| **SACL** | System ACL — auditing rules | Variable list of ACEs |
| **Control Flags** | Bit flags controlling protection & inheritance | 2 bytes |

**DACL vs SACL Comparison (4 marks):**

| Feature | DACL | SACL |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Name** | Discretionary Access Control List | System Access Control List |
| **Purpose** | Controls *who* can access the object and *how* | Controls *what* actions get logged in the Security Event Log |
| **Decision Impact** | Directly grants or denies access | Does NOT grant/deny access — only records |
| **ACE Types Used** | Allow (0x0) and Deny (0x1) | Audit Success (0x2) and Audit Failure (0x3) |
| **Modification Rights** | Owner of object, users with `WRITE_DAC` | Users with `SeSecurityPrivilege` (typically administrators) |
| **Example Use** | "Allow `Users` group `Read`" on `C:\Reports` | "Audit `Failed` access attempts by `Everyone` on `C:\Confidential`" |
| **Default Behavior** | If NULL DACL → full access to all; if empty DACL → no access | If no SACL → no auditing occurs |

**Example Security Descriptor in SDDL Notation:**

```
O:S-1-5-21-100-500
G:S-1-5-21-100-513
D:(D;CI;WP;0x00000002;S-1-1-0)
  (A;OI;RPWP;0x001200A9;S-1-5-32-544)
S:(AU;SA;WP;0x00000002;S-1-5-32-544)
```

This means:
- Owner is user with RID 500 (Administrator)
- Primary group is Domain Users
- DACL: Deny Everyone Write, Allow Admins Full Control
- SACL: Audit successful Write attempts by Admins

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** What is User Account Control (UAC) in Windows? Explain its architecture, integrity levels, and the elevation process.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare NTLM and Kerberos authentication protocols. Why is Kerberos preferred in Active Directory environments?

**Model Solutions:**

**(a) User Account Control — UAC (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
UAC is a security infrastructure introduced in **Windows Vista** that enforces the principle of least privilege by running user applications under a **filtered, non-administrative token** by default, and only granting administrative privileges after explicit user consent.

**Architecture (3 marks):**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Application Information Service (AppInfo)** | Hosts the UAC consent prompt (`consent.exe`) |
| **Secure Desktop** | Isolated window station where consent UI is rendered (prevents UI tampering) |
| **Credential Provider** | Captures UAC consent responses |
| **Filter Token** | Stripped-down token used for standard operations |
| **Full Token** | Complete administrative token used only post-elevation |
| **LSASS** | Generates both filtered and full tokens at logon for Admin accounts |

**Integrity Levels (2 marks):**

| Level | SID (Prefix S-1-16-) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **System** | 16384 | OS kernel, services |
| **High** | 12288 | Elevated admin processes |
| **Medium** | 8192 | Standard user applications |
| **Low** | 4096 | IE Protected Mode, sandboxed apps |
| **Untrusted** | 0 | Anonymous connections |

A process at integrity level X **cannot write** to objects at a level below X (No-Write-Up rule). This blocks many privilege escalation attacks.

**Elevation Process (1 mark):**
1. App with `requireAdministrator` manifest launches
2. AppInfo displays consent dialog on Secure Desktop
3. On "Yes" → App is re-launched with full admin token (High integrity)
4. On "No" → App is not started

**Limitation (1 mark):**
UAC is **not a security boundary** in the strictest sense — a user who clicks "Yes" to a malicious UAC prompt defeats it. UAC is best understood as a *defense-in-depth* mechanism.

---

**(b) NTLM vs Kerberos Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | NTLM | Kerberos v5 |
|---|---|---|
| **Authentication Type** | Challenge-Response (3-way) | Ticket-based (3-party) |
| **Mutual Authentication** | Not supported (vulnerable to relay) | Supported via AP-REP |
| **Cryptography** | MD4 hash + RC4/DES | AES-256, 3DES, RC4 |
| **Delegation** | Limited | Full (constrained, unconstrained, RBCD) |
| **SSO** | Per-server, no real SSO | Native via TGT |
| **Trust Model** | Server trusts DC's response | Tickets cryptographically bound to client + service |
| **Performance** | 1 DC round-trip | 2 DC round-trips (AS + TGS) |
| **Offline Cracking** | Possible (NT hash) | Very difficult (tickets encrypted) |
| **Default in Modern AD** | Disabled/Fallback | **Default** since Windows 2000 |
| **Default Port** | 445/TCP | 88/UDP/TCP |
| **Pass-the-Hash Risk** | **High** | **Low** (but Pass-the-Ticket exists) |
| **Reliability** | Tolerates network issues | Requires DNS, time sync within 5 min |

**Why Kerberos is Preferred in Active Directory (3 marks):**

1. **Mutual Authentication** — Prevents server spoofing and relay attacks (NTLM allows attackers to relay credentials to other servers).
2. **Delegation Support** — Critical for multi-tier applications (web server → database server impersonating the user).
3. **Single Sign-On** — One TGT grants access to all services for 10 hours without re-authenticating; NTLM requires re-authentication per service.
4. **Stronger Cryptography** — AES-256 vs NTLM's legacy DES-based RC4 makes offline cracking impractical.
5. **Auditing & Compliance** — Kerberos tickets are logged with SIDs and group memberships enabling fine-grained access control; NTLM authenticates the user but not the service context.
6. **Transitive Trust** — In multi-domain forests, Kerberos works seamlessly via cross-realm TGTs; NTLM falls back to NTLM and degrades security.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Students Lose Marks (Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Deny by default" with NULL DACL** — A NULL (absent) DACL grants **EVERYONE full access**. An empty DACL grants **NO ONE access**. Examiners specifically test this nuance. (Lose 2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting ACE ordering** — Explicit Deny ACEs must come **before** Explicit Allow ACEs. Stating "Deny is evaluated last" is incorrect. (Lose 2 marks)
>
> 3. **Saying UAC is a "security boundary"** — Microsoft officially classifies UAC as a *convenience mechanism* and *defense-in-depth*, not a security boundary. The actual security boundaries in Windows are the **Kernel** and the **LSASS process**. (Lose 1-2 marks)
>
> 4. **Confusing NTLMv1 and NTLMv2** — NTLMv1 uses DES (broken), NTLMv2 uses HMAC-MD5 (still considered legacy but stronger). Examiners expect you to mention **NTLMv2** explicitly. (Lose 1 mark)
>
> 5. **Skipping the SACL** — SACL is the auditing mechanism. Forgetting it loses 1-2 marks on Security Descriptor questions.
>
> 6. **Writing `|S-1-5-...|` for absolute SID** — Use proper `S-1-5-...` notation. (Formatting penalty possible)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU):**
>
> **Identity & Authentication:**
> - ☐ SID is unique, immutable, and never reused (1 mark question guaranteed)
> - ☐ Built-in RIDs: 500 = Admin, 501 = Guest, 512 = Domain Admins
> - ☐ Kerberos uses **3 entities**: Client, KDC, Service
