> - ☐ Kerberos uses **2 message exchanges**: AS-REQ/AS-REP and TGS-REQ/TGS-REP
> - ☐ TGT is encrypted with **krbtgt account's key** (never leaves the KDC plaintext)
> - ☐ NTLM = Challenge-Response (8-byte challenge) using MD4(UTF-16LE(password))
> - ☐ NTLM does **NOT** support mutual authentication
>
> **Authorization & Access Control:**
> - ☐ Access Token contains: User SID, Group SIDs, Privileges, Default DACL, Integrity Level
> - ☐ Primary Token = process identity; Impersonation Token = temporary client context
> - ☐ Security Descriptor = Owner + Group + **DACL** + **SACL** + Control Flags
> - ☐ DACL controls access; SACL controls auditing
> - ☐ ACE types: **Deny (0x1)**, **Allow (0x0)**, **Audit (0x2)**
> - ☐ **Deny ACEs are evaluated FIRST** in DACL processing
> - ☐ NULL DACL = full access; Empty DACL = no access
>
> **UAC & Integrity:**
> - ☐ UAC introduced in **Windows Vista (2007)**
> - ☐ Integrity levels: Untrusted (0) < Low (4096) < Medium (8192) < High (12288) < System (16384)
> - ☐ No-Write-Up rule: process at level X cannot write to objects at level < X
> - ☐ UAC consent prompt runs on **Secure Desktop** to prevent UI tampering
> - ☐ UAC is a defense-in-depth mechanism, **not a security boundary**
>
> **Architecture:**
> - ☐ **LSASS.exe** = authentication engine
> - ☐ **SRM (Security Reference Monitor)** = kernel-mode authorization enforcer
> - ☐ SAM database = `%SystemRoot%\system32\config\SAM`
> - ☐ Default Kerberos ports: **88/TCP and 88/UDP**
> - ☐ NTLM default port: **445/TCP** (SMB) or **139/TCP** (legacy NetBIOS)
> - ☐ KDC runs on every Domain Controller by default

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Trick — "SAD PAWS":**
> - **S**ID identifies
> - **A**ccess Token authorizes
> - **D**ACL decides access
> - **P**rivilege elevates
> - **A**udit (SACL) records
> - **W**indows uses **S**ecure Desktop for UAC
> - **S**ecurity Descriptor is the umbrella

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Upgrades and Patches (Windows)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-3
> - **Topic:** Upgrades and Patches (Windows)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Microsoft Windows operating systems, **Upgrades and Patches** refer to the systematic deployment of software updates that fix vulnerabilities, enhance functionality, improve stability, and address compatibility issues in the OS, drivers, and bundled applications. According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology aligned with NIST SP 800-40 (Guide to Enterprise Patch Management), a **patch** is a piece of code inserted into an executable program to fix a bug, defect, vulnerability, or performance issue, while an **upgrade** is a major version transition that introduces new features, architectural changes, and significant functional improvements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – System Security):**
> *Patches and upgrades* form a critical sub-unit under **Operating System Security**. The Windows update ecosystem is the most widely deployed patch management framework in enterprise environments, making it a high-frequency question area in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of your **Windows operating system as a high-rise apartment building**:

- **Patches** are like **small repair jobs** — fixing a leaking pipe, replacing a broken window lock, or sealing a crack in the wall. These are routine maintenance tasks that keep the building safe and functional without changing its structure.
- **Upgrades** are like **renovating the entire building** — adding a new floor, installing a modern elevator, or rewiring the entire electrical system. The building still functions the same way, but with major new capabilities and improved infrastructure.
- **Windows Update Service** is the **building maintenance contractor** — it periodically inspects the building, identifies problems, downloads fixes from Microsoft's servers, and installs them.
- **WSUS (Windows Server Update Services)** is the **centralized property management office** — instead of each apartment calling contractors individually, the management office receives all updates and pushes them out in a controlled manner.

If you neglect patches, it's like leaving your front door unlocked — hackers (attackers) walk right in using known exploits that Microsoft has already fixed.

### 1.3 Classification of Windows Updates

Windows categorizes updates into several distinct types, each with a specific purpose and deployment mechanism:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Classification Categories (KTU-Exam Ready):**

| Update Type | Purpose | Frequency | User Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Critical/Security Patch** | Fixes exploitable vulnerabilities | Monthly (Patch Tuesday) | Auto-installed |
| **Definition Update** | Updates malware signature database (Defender) | Multiple times daily | Auto-downloaded |
| **Cumulative Update (LCU)** | Roll-up of all previous patches for a Windows version | Monthly | Auto-downloaded |
| **Service Pack (SP)** | Major collection of patches and improvements | Rare (legacy, e.g., SP1, SP2) | Manual/Scheduled |
| **Feature Update** | Major version upgrade (e.g., Win 10 → Win 11, 22H2 → 23H2) | Annually/Semi-annually | Manual/Scheduled |
| **Driver Update** | Hardware compatibility fixes | As released by OEMs | Often auto-installed |
| **Hotfix** | Targeted, single-issue fix released out-of-band (OOB) | As needed | Manual deployment |

### 1.4 The Monthly Update Rhythm

Microsoft follows a predictable **Patch Tuesday** cycle — updates are released on the **second Tuesday of every month**, with the following timeline for the rest of the cycle:

- **Tuesday (Patch Tuesday):** Security and cumulative updates released.
- **Wednesday–Thursday:** Initial testing, telemetry analysis.
- **Following Tuesday (Optional "C" week):** Out-of-band (OOB) updates for critical issues.
- **Final week of month:** Preview "C" rollup updates for the next month.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** *Service Packs* are often confused with *Cumulative Updates*. In modern Windows (Windows 10 and 11), Service Packs no longer exist — they have been replaced by **Cumulative Updates (LCUs)**. The last true Service Pack was **Windows 7 SP1** (2011).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Patch Management Lifecycle (NIST Framework)

The **patch management lifecycle** is a structured, five-phase process mandated by NIST SP 800-40r4 and frequently tested in KTU 14-mark questions. Each phase has explicit deliverables:

**Phase 1 — Asset Inventory & Baseline:**
- Identify all hardware, OS versions, and third-party software.
- Maintain a Configuration Management Database (CMDB).
- **Output:** Complete inventory with OS build numbers, patch levels, and criticality tags.

**Phase 2 — Vulnerability Identification:**
- Subscribe to **Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC)** bulletins.
- Use **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)** databases.
- Deploy vulnerability scanners (e.g., Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management, Nessus).
- **Output:** Prioritized list of CVEs affecting the estate.

**Phase 3 — Patch Acquisition & Testing:**
- Download patches from **Microsoft Update Catalog**.
- Test in a **staging/lab environment** that mirrors production.
- Verify application compatibility, regression potential, and rollback feasibility.
- **Output:** Test report with pass/fail criteria and deployment recommendation.

**Phase 4 — Deployment (Rollout):**
- Stage the deployment: **Pilot → Limited Production → Full Rollout**.
- Use change management windows to minimize business disruption.
- Monitor deployment success via telemetry and reporting dashboards.
- **Output:** Deployment report with success/failure metrics.

**Phase 5 — Verification & Audit:**
- Re-scan systems post-deployment to confirm patch installation.
- Document exceptions and compensating controls for unpatched assets.
- Generate compliance reports (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001, CIS Benchmarks).
- **Output:** Audit trail and compliance evidence.

### 2.2 Windows Update Delivery Mechanisms

Windows uses multiple delivery channels depending on the deployment context:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield: Windows Update Service Architecture**

| Mechanism | Target Environment | Key Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Windows Update (WU)** | Consumer / SOHO | Direct internet fetch from Microsoft servers |
| **Microsoft Update (MU)** | Consumer / SOHO | WU + Office + other MS products |
| **WSUS (Windows Server Update Services)** | Enterprise | Internal server caches and approves updates |
| **SCCM / MECM** | Large Enterprise | Full lifecycle management with task sequences |
| **Intune / Windows Autopatch** | Cloud-Managed / Modern Workplace | SaaS-based patch as a service |
| **Windows Update for Business (WUfB)** | Hybrid Enterprise | Group Policy-based deferral controls |

### 2.3 Patch Severity Classification (CVSS)

Microsoft assigns a **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)** score to every patched CVE. This score is the **primary triage metric** for patch prioritization:

| CVSS Score Range | Severity | KTU / Industry Action |
|---|---|---|
| **9.0 – 10.0** | **Critical** | Patch within 24–48 hours |
| **7.0 – 8.9** | **High** | Patch within 1–2 weeks |
| **4.0 – 6.9** | **Medium** | Patch within 30 days (next cycle) |
| **0.1 – 3.9** | **Low** | Patch as resources allow |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Patch Management

Patches are released for three fundamental reasons, each with different security implications:

1. **Vulnerability Remediation:** A security flaw is discovered (often reported by researchers via **Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (CVD)**) that could allow Remote Code Execution (RCE), privilege escalation, or information disclosure. The patch closes this attack vector.

2. **Stability & Reliability:** Non-security bugs — Blue Screens of Death (BSOD), memory leaks, driver conflicts — are fixed to ensure system stability.

3. **Feature Enhancement:** New capabilities (e.g., new Windows Security features like **Credential Guard**, **HVCI**, **Smart App Control**) are introduced to improve the security posture proactively.

### 2.5 The Rollback (Uninstall) Mechanism

Modern Windows maintains a **Component-Based Servicing (CBS)** store, which tracks every installed update. This enables:
- **Uninstall** of most updates within **10 days** of installation (post-10 days, uninstall requires specific commands).
- **System Restore Points** are automatically created before major updates.
- **Reset This PC** and **In-Place Upgrade Repair** are last-resort recovery options.

> [!WARNING]
> **Security vs. Stability Trade-off:** Rolling back a security patch **reintroduces the vulnerability**. This must be documented as an exception with a compensating control (e.g., network segmentation, enhanced monitoring).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Decision Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Workflow: From Vulnerability Disclosure to Patch Deployment

This section breaks down the complete operational flow that a Windows system administrator follows. Since this is a process-oriented topic, the "derivation" here is a **logical decision sequence** rather than a mathematical proof.

**Step 1 — Vulnerability Disclosure:**
A security researcher (or threat actor) discovers a flaw in a Windows component. They report it to the **Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC)** at `msrc.microsoft.com`. Microsoft assigns a **CVE-ID** and begins developing a fix.

**Step 2 — Patch Development & Testing:**
Microsoft's product team writes the fix code, internal QA validates it, and security engineers confirm the vulnerability is closed. The patch is then packaged as a **.msu** (Microsoft Standalone Update) file or aggregated into a **Cumulative Update (LCU)**.

**Step 3 — Staged Release via Windows Update:**
The patch is published to Windows Update servers but is deployed in a **phased rollout**:
- **Phase 1:** Telemetry-gated release to a small percentage of devices.
- **Phase 2:** Broader rollout based on Phase 1 health signals.
- **Phase 3:** Universal availability via Windows Update, WSUS, and Catalog.

**Step 4 — Enterprise Validation (Customer Side):**
Enterprise administrators receive the update in their WSUS console. They:
- Review the **KB (Knowledge Base) article** for known issues.
- Test in a lab environment.
- Approve the update for deployment in WSUS.

**Step 5 — Deployment Strategy Selection:**
The administrator selects a deployment ring structure:

$$
\text{Ring}_1 \rightarrow \text{Ring}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ring}_3 \rightarrow \text{Ring}_4
$$

where **Ring 1** = IT/Canary (a few volunteers), **Ring 2** = Early Adopters (pilot users), **Ring 3** = Broad Business, **Ring 4** = Mass Deployment.

**Step 6 — Deployment Execution:**
The patch is pushed via **SCCM/MECM task sequences**, **WSUS GPO**, **Intune**, or **Windows Update for Business** policies. Deployment typically occurs during the **3rd or 4th week of the month** (after Patch Tuesday validation).

**Step 7 — Post-Deployment Verification:**
A rescan confirms the patch is installed and reports compliance. Any failures are triaged and remediated.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Update Sources Compared

| Decision Criterion | Windows Update | WSUS | SCCM/MECM | Intune |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internet Required (Client) | Yes | No | No | No (cloud-mediated) |
| Granular Approval Control | None | Yes (per-update) | Yes (per-update + collection) | Yes (per-group) |
| Bandwidth Optimization | Low | High (local cache) | Very High (DPs) | High (peer-to-peer) |
| Third-Party Patch Support | No | Limited | Yes (with Intune/SUP) | Yes (with Intune) |
| Reporting Depth | Basic | Moderate | Comprehensive | Comprehensive |
| Best Fit | Home / SOHO | Medium Enterprise | Large / Regulated Enterprise | Cloud-First / Hybrid |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Feature Update vs. Cumulative Update

| Aspect | Cumulative Update (LCU) | Feature Update |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Security + non-security fixes | New Windows version (e.g., 22H2 → 23H2) |
| **Install Time** | 10–30 minutes | 30–90+ minutes |
| **Reboot Required** | Usually once | Usually once |
| **Rollback Window** | 10 days | **10 days** (longer via `DISM /Online /Set-OSUninstallWindow`) |
| **Frequency** | Monthly | Annually / Semi-annually |
| **KTU Exam Tag** | "Patch" | "Upgrade" |

### 3.4 Decision Logic: When to Patch, When to Defer

A simplified decision tree that maps to the KTU examination question type "Discuss the patch management strategy for a Windows enterprise":

1. **Is the CVE CVSS ≥ 9.0 AND is the system internet-facing?**
 → Deploy within **24 hours** via emergency change process.
2. **Is the CVE CVSS 7.0–8.9?**
 → Include in the **next maintenance window** (typically within 2 weeks).
3. **Is the patch causing compatibility issues in the lab?**
 → Defer, but apply **compensating controls** (firewall rules, AppLocker, exploit guards).
4. **Is the system end-of-life (e.g., Windows 7 post-2020)?**
 → **Migrate/upgrade immediately** — patches are no longer released.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The patch management discipline directly impacts an organization's compliance posture with regulations like **PCI-DSS Requirement 6.2** (timely patching), **HIPAA §164.308(a)(1)(ii)(B)**, and **ISO 27001 Annex A.12.6.1 (Technical Vulnerability Management)**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Windows Update End-to-End Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Microsoft Patch Tuesday Release] --> B[Windows Update Cloud Infrastructure]
    B --> C[Microsoft Update Catalog]
    B --> D[WSUS Internal Server]
    B --> E[Intune Cloud Service]
    D --> F[SCCM MECM Primary Site]
    E --> G[Cloud Managed Devices]
    D --> H[Domain Joined Workstations]
    F --> H
    F --> I[Servers and Member Servers]
    H --> J[Phase 1 Canary Ring]
    J --> K[Phase 2 Pilot Ring]
    K --> L[Phase 3 Business Ring]
    L --> M[Phase 4 Mass Deployment]
    M --> N[Compliance Verification Scan]
    N --> O{All Systems Compliant?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Generate Audit Report]
    O -->|No| Q[Remediation and Rescan]
    Q --> N
    P --> R[Close Change Ticket]
```

### 4.2 Patch Management Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AssetInventory
    AssetInventory --> VulnerabilityScan: Establish Baseline
    VulnerabilityScan --> RiskAssessment: Correlate with CVE Feeds
    RiskAssessment --> PatchAcquisition: Prioritize by CVSS
    PatchAcquisition --> LabTesting: Download from Catalog
    LabTesting --> ChangeApproval: Validate Compatibility
    ChangeApproval --> Deployment: CAB Approves
    Deployment --> Verification: Push via WSUS or SCCM
    Verification --> AuditReporting: Rescan and Confirm
    AuditReporting --> [*]: Close Cycle
    LabTesting --> Rollback: Test Failure Detected
    Rollback --> VulnerabilityScan: Re-enter Cycle
```

### 4.3 Windows Update Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientSide[Windows Client Components]
        WUAgent[Windows Update Agent WUA]
        USO[Update Session Orchestrator USO]
        CBS[Component Based Servicing CBS]
        WUAgent --> USO
        USO --> CBS
    end
    subgraph NetworkLayer[Network Delivery]
        HTTP[HTTPS over port 443]
        WUAgent --> HTTP
    end
    subgraph ServerSide[Microsoft Backend]
        MUCatalog[Microsoft Update Catalog]
        CDNs[Global CDN Edge Nodes]
        MUCatalog --> CDNs
    end
    HTTP --> CDNs
    CBS --> Result[Reboot and Finalize Update]
```

### 4.4 Phased Ring Deployment Strategy (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Microsoft Release] --> R1[Ring 1 Canary IT Team]
    R1 --> R2[Ring 2 Early Adopters 5 percent]
    R2 --> R3[Ring 3 Business Units 30 percent]
    R3 --> R4[Ring 4 Mass Deployment 100 percent]
    R1 -->|Issues Detected| HOLD[Pause and Investigate]
    R2 -->|Issues Detected| HOLD
    R3 -->|Issues Detected| HOLD
    HOLD --> R0
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** *Differentiate between a Hotfix and a Cumulative Update in Windows. List any two distinguishing characteristics.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Hotfix** is a single-issue, targeted patch released out-of-band (OOB) by Microsoft to address a specific, urgent bug or security vulnerability that cannot wait for the next scheduled release cycle. A **Cumulative Update (LCU)**, on the other hand, is a roll-up package released on Patch Tuesday that aggregates all previously released patches, fixes, and improvements for a given Windows version into a single installable bundle.

**Distinguishing Characteristics:**

1. **Scope:** Hotfix addresses a single issue; LCU contains hundreds of fixes in one package.
2. **Frequency:** Hotfixes are unscheduled and rare; LCUs follow a predictable monthly cadence.
3. **Reboot:** Hotfixes may or may not require a reboot; LCUs almost always require one.
4. **Numbering:** Hotfixes are tied to a specific KB article only; LCUs supersede all prior LCUs for that branch.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *What is WSUS? Why is it preferred over direct Windows Update in enterprise environments?*

**Model Answer:**

**WSUS (Windows Server Update Services)** is a Microsoft server role that enables administrators to manage and distribute updates to Windows-based computers in an enterprise network from a single, internal console.

**Reasons for Enterprise Preference over direct Windows Update:**

1. **Bandwidth Conservation:** Client machines download updates from the local WSUS server, which in turn fetches them once from Microsoft — preventing duplicate internet downloads.
2. **Centralized Approval:** Administrators can review, approve, or decline updates before they are deployed, giving granular control.
3. **Compliance Reporting:** WSUS provides detailed reports on which machines are missing which patches, supporting audit requirements.
4. **Reduced Attack Surface:** Internal WSUS servers can be hardened, air-gapped, and configured to scan updates with internal AV before deployment.
5. **Scheduled Deployment:** Updates can be synchronized and approved on a schedule, aligning with change management windows.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the complete **patch management lifecycle** as defined by NIST SP 800-40. Describe each phase with a suitable example from a Windows enterprise context. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **phased ring deployment strategy** for Windows updates. Why is it considered a best practice in large enterprises? Illustrate with a deployment timeline for a critical CVE. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

The **NIST SP 800-40 patch management lifecycle** consists of five structured phases:

**Phase 1 — Asset Discovery and Baseline Creation:**
The enterprise enumerates every Windows device (workstations, servers, VMs, IoT endpoints), records the OS build number, installed software inventory, and current patch level. Tools used: **Microsoft Endpoint Configuration Manager (MECM/SCCM)**, **Microsoft Intune**, **Active Directory discovery**. The baseline is stored in a CMDB and refreshed continuously.

**Phase 2 — Vulnerability Identification:**
The security team subscribes to **MSRC bulletins**, **CVE databases (NVD)**, and runs **Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management** scans. The output is a prioritized list of CVEs. *Example:* On Patch Tuesday, MSRC releases **CVE-2024-38063** (a critical RCE in Windows TCP/IP) with CVSS 9.8. All Windows 10/11 systems become candidates.

**Phase 3 — Patch Acquisition and Testing:**
Administrators download the LCU from the **Microsoft Update Catalog**, deploy it to a lab environment (a "pilot OU" or sandbox collection) representing the production estate, and test for application compatibility, driver conflicts, and regression. *Example:* Testing reveals the TCP/IP patch breaks a legacy VPN client — decision made to defer for that collection and apply a compensating firewall rule.

**Phase 4 — Deployment and Rollout:**
The patch is approved in **WSUS** or **SCCM** and rolled out using a **phased ring strategy** to minimize business risk. Deployment occurs during approved change windows with monitoring enabled.

**Phase 5 — Verification and Compliance Reporting:**
A post-deployment rescan confirms patch installation. Exceptions (unpatched systems) are documented with compensating controls in a **Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M)**. Reports are generated for compliance audits (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001).

---

#### Model Solution (b):

The **Phased Ring Deployment Strategy** is a graduated rollout model where updates are deployed to increasingly larger groups of devices, allowing early issue detection before mass exposure. It is considered a best practice because it **limits the blast radius** of a bad patch and **leverages real-world telemetry** before broad release.

**Typical Ring Structure (Windows Enterprise):**

- **Ring 1 – IT Canary (1–2% of estate):** Volunteer IT staff, hand-selected workstations. Patches deployed within 24–48 hours of release.
- **Ring 2 – Early Adopters (5–10%):** Power users and pilot departments. Patches deployed 1 week after Ring 1 validation.
- **Ring 3 – Broad Business (30–50%):** Standard business units. Deployed 2–3 weeks after Ring 2 validation.
- **Ring 4 – Mass Deployment (100%):** Remaining estate, deployed after all rings are validated as stable.

**Illustrative Timeline for a Critical CVE (e.g., CVSS 9.5 RCE released on Patch Tuesday, 9th of the month):**

- **Day 1 (9th):** Patch released by Microsoft.
- **Day 2–3 (10th–11th):** Ring 1 deployment + telemetry monitoring.
- **Day 8 (16th):** Ring 2 deployment (if Ring 1 is green).
- **Day 15 (23rd):** Ring 3 deployment.
- **Day 22 (30th):** Ring 4 mass deployment.

**Why it is a best practice:**

1. **Risk Containment:** If the patch causes issues (BSOD, app breakage), only a small subset is affected.
2. **Real-World Validation:** Lab tests cannot replicate all production scenarios; ring users provide realistic feedback.
3. **Rollback Feasibility:** A 1% exposure is far easier to remediate than 100%.
4. **Change Management Alignment:** Supports ITIL change advisory board (CAB) processes and audit trails.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice – KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of WSUS**. Describe how synchronization, approval, and client detection work. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Feature Updates, Cumulative Updates, and Service Packs** in Windows. Explain the rollback mechanism available for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**WSUS Architecture Block Diagram:**

```
┌─────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────┐
│  Microsoft      │ Sync    │   WSUS Server    │
│  Update         │◄────────│   (Internal)     │
│  Servers        │ HTTPS   │                  │
└─────────────────┘         └────────┬─────────┘
                                     │ Approves & Distributes
                                     ▼
                          ┌──────────────────────┐
                          │  Client Auto-Update  │
                          │  (GPO/Registry Set)  │
                          └────────┬─────────────┘
                                   │
              ┌────────────────────┼────────────────────┐
              ▼                    ▼                    ▼
        ┌──────────┐         ┌──────────┐         ┌──────────┐
        │ Workstation│         │  Laptop   │         │  Server   │
        │  Client 1 │         │  Client 2│         │  Client 3│
        └──────────┘         └──────────┘         └──────────┘
```

**Working Phases:**

1. **Synchronization:** The WSUS server connects to Microsoft Update over **port 443 (HTTPS)** using the **WUServer** and **WUStatusServer** GPO/registry values. It downloads the **update metadata** (titles, KB numbers, applicability rules) and optionally the **update binaries** based on a configured sync schedule (e.g., daily at 03:00).

2. **Approval:** Administrators review the synchronized updates in the **WSUS console**, read associated KB articles, and either **Approve for Install**, **Decline**, or **Approve for Removal**. Approvals can be scoped to specific **Computer Groups** (e.g., "Finance PCs", "HR Servers").

3. **Client Detection:** The Windows Update Agent (WUA) on each client connects to the WSUS server (every ~17–22 hours by default, configurable via `DetectionFrequencyEnabled`). It sends its hardware inventory and current patch state. The WSUS server compares the state against approved updates and instructs the client on which updates to download and install.

4. **Reporting:** WSUS generates reports on update status, compliance, and synchronization health, viewable in the WSUS console or via **WSUS Reporting Rollup** for executives.

---

#### Model Solution (b):

| Aspect | Service Pack (SP) | Cumulative Update (LCU) | Feature Update |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Era** | Windows XP–Windows 7 (legacy) | Windows 10/11 (modern) | Windows 10/11 (modern) |
| **Content** | Major collection of all prior fixes | Roll-up of monthly security + non-security fixes | New OS version with new features |
| **Release Cadence** | Rare, multi-year gaps | Monthly (Patch Tuesday) | Annually / Semi-annually |
| **Installation Time** | Hours | 10–30 minutes | 30–90+ minutes |
| **Reboot** | Multiple reboots | One reboot typically | One reboot typically |
| **Rollback Window** | Uninstall via Programs & Features | 10 days post-install | 10 days (extendable to 60 days) |
| **Rollback Method** | Add/Remove Programs → Service Pack uninstall | Settings → Windows Update → Update History → Uninstall | Recovery Environment → Rollback; or `DISM /Online /Set-OSUninstallWindow` to extend |
| **Example** | Windows 7 SP1 (2011) | KB5034763 (Feb 2024 LCU) | Windows 11 23H2 |

**Detailed Rollback Mechanism Explanation:**

1. **Service Pack Rollback:** Performed via **Control Panel → Programs and Features → View Installed Updates → Uninstall**. The system reverts to the pre-SP state. If the SP was installed over 120 days ago, uninstallation is blocked to prevent system instability.

2. **Cumulative Update Rollback:** Windows automatically creates a **System Restore Point** and stores the previous component state in the **Component-Based Servicing (CBS)** store. The user can uninstall via **Settings → Windows Update → Update History → Uninstall**. The rollback window is **10 days**; after that, manual intervention is required and is not recommended.

3. **Feature Update Rollback:** Windows creates a **Windows.old** folder and an uninstall entry in the Recovery environment. The user can rollback via **Settings → System → Recovery → Advanced Options → Restart Now → Troubleshoot → Uninstall latest feature update**. The 10-day window can be extended up to **60 days** using the command `DISM /Online /Set-OSUninstallWindow /Value:60`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "Service Pack" with "Cumulative Update"** in your answer — they are from different eras of Windows. Writing about SPs in a Windows 11 context will cost you marks.
> 2. **Always mention specific KB article numbers or CVE IDs** in your answers where possible. It demonstrates depth of knowledge and earns differentiation marks.
> 3. **For Part B 14-mark questions**, the sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**. Skipping either sub-part guarantees a maximum of 7/14. Always address both.
> 4. **Do not omit diagrams** in 7-mark sub-parts that ask for an "explain with diagram" question. Even a simple labeled block diagram is mandatory.
> 5. **Memorize the CVSS severity ranges and the Patch Tuesday schedule** — these are favorite 2-mark sub-questions in Part A.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap: Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE – Upgrades and Patches (Windows):**

- **Patch Tuesday** = Second Tuesday of every month; predictable Microsoft update release cadence.
- **Cumulative Update (LCU)** is the modern replacement for Service Packs in Windows 10/11.
- **Hotfix** = single-issue, out-of-band (OOB) fix; **LCU** = monthly roll-up; **Feature Update** = annual OS version jump.
- **WSUS** = enterprise on-premise update management; **SCCM/MECM** = full lifecycle management for large enterprises; **Intune** = cloud-native; **WUfB** = Group Policy for update deferrals.
- **NIST SP 800-40r4** lifecycle: **Discover → Identify → Acquire/Test → Deploy → Verify** (5 phases).
- **CVSS Score:** **9.0–10.0 = Critical** (patch in 24–48h); **7.0–8.9 = High** (1–2 weeks); **4.0–6.9 = Medium** (30 days); **0.1–3.9 = Low** (as resources permit).
- **Default rollback window** for both LCUs and Feature Updates is **10 days**, extendable to 60 days via DISM for Feature Updates.
- **MSRC** = Microsoft Security Response Center — official source for CVE disclosures and patch advisories.
- **Phased Ring Deployment:** **Canary → Early Adopter → Business → Mass** = 1–2% → 5–10% → 30–50% → 100%.
- **Component-Based Servicing (CBS)** is the internal Windows store that tracks all updates and enables rollback.
- **Windows Update Agent (WUA)** is the client-side service; **Update Session Orchestrator (USO)** is the newer orchestrator in Windows 10/11.
- **Default client detection frequency** to WSUS = every 17–22 hours (configurable).
- **Sync protocol:** WSUS ↔ Microsoft Update over **HTTPS port 443**.
- **Last true Service Pack for Windows: Windows 7 SP1 (2011)** — no Service Packs exist for Windows 10 or 11.
- **Compliance relevance:** PCI-DSS 6.2, HIPAA §164.308, ISO 27001 A.12.6.1 — all mandate timely patch management.
- **Zero-day patch priority:** Patch immediately on release; do not wait for the monthly cycle.
- **Always test patches in a lab** before mass deployment to avoid enterprise-wide outages.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Windows Safely
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY > - **Module:** Module-3 > - **Topic:** Operating Windows Safely ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Operating Windows Safely (KTU 2024 Definition):** The discipline of configuring, managing, and using the Microsoft Windows operating system in a manner that preserves the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) of the system and its data. It encompasses secure user account management, enforcing the **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)**, applying operating system hardening techniques, managing access control lists (ACLs), and maintaining defense-in-depth at the host level. It is a critical sub-domain of **host security** in the broader Network and System Security syllabus, focusing on the *client-side* and *server-side* hardening of Windows machines against malware, privilege escalation, unauthorized access, and social engineering. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Bank Vault" Analogy for Windows Security:** > Imagine your Windows PC is a **bank vault**: > - The **Administrator account** is the bank manager who holds the master key to every safe deposit box. > - The **Standard User account** is a customer who only has a key to their personal safe deposit box. > - **User Account Control (UAC)** is the security guard at the door who asks, *"Are you really sure you want to open the manager's vault?"* every time a sensitive action is requested. > - **NTFS Permissions** are the individual locks on each safe deposit box — even if a customer walks into the vault room, they can only open their own box. > - **Windows Defender & Firewall** are the vault's alarm system and the security cameras monitoring the perimeter. > > *Operating Windows Safely* means ensuring the keys are unique, the locks are strong, the cameras are on, and the alarm is armed. ### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **PoLP (Principle of Least Privilege)** | Granting only the minimum permissions necessary to perform a task | | **UAC (User Account Control)** | A mandatory access control mechanism introduced in Windows Vista | | **NTFS** | New Technology File System — supports granular per-file/folder permissions | | **ACL (Access Control List)** | The ordered list of ACEs that defines who can access an object and how | | **ACE (Access Control Entry)** | A single rule (User + Permission) inside an ACL | | **LAPS** | Local Administrator Password Solution | | **BitLocker** | Full-volume encryption feature of Windows | ### 1.4 Standard Metrics / Defaults in Windows Security - **Default minimum password length** in Windows: **0 characters** (must be enforced via policy to **≥ 8**) - **Default account lockout threshold**: **0 attempts** (recommended: **5–10 invalid attempts**) - **Default lockout duration**: Not defined (recommended: **15–30 minutes**) - **Standard UAC consent prompt level**: **Level 3 (Notify on third-party app changes only)** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Operating Windows Safely > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pillar 1: Account Hygiene** — Managing *who* can log in. > **Pillar 2: Privilege Management** — Controlling *what* logged-in users can do. > **Pillar 3: Resource Protection** — Locking down *files, folders, and registry*. > **Pillar 4: Threat Defense** — Detecting and *blocking attacks* (Defender, Firewall, updates). --- ### 2.2 Pillar 1: User Account Types Windows segregates accounts to enforce the **Principle of Least Privilege**. **a) Administrator Account** - Full control over the system: install software, modify system files, manage other accounts, change security policies. - Vulnerable target: if compromised, attacker inherits **SYSTEM-level** privileges. - Should be used **only for administrative tasks**, never for daily browsing. **b) Standard User Account** - Can run applications, modify personal files, install approved software. - Cannot modify system directories, install drivers, or change system-wide settings. - **Recommended default** for everyday use. **c) Guest Account** - Highly restricted, disabled by default in modern Windows. - Intended for temporary, unauthenticated access. **d) Service Accounts (SYSTEM, LocalService, NetworkService)** - Used by Windows services and processes; not for human login. ### 2.3 Pillar 2: User Account Control (UAC) UAC is the cornerstone of Windows privilege separation. It works by: 1. **Splitting user tokens**: When an admin logs in, Windows creates *two* access tokens — a *limited* token (used by default for normal actions) and a *full* token (used only when an action is explicitly elevated). 2. **Consent prompts**: When a process requests the full token, UAC displays a dialog (consent prompt for admins, credential prompt for standard users). 3. **Secure desktop isolation**: The prompt is rendered on a *separate desktop* (SecureDesktop) to prevent UI spoofing by malware. **UAC Notification Levels (Group Policy: `ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin`):** | Level | Description | Use Case | |-------|-------------|----------| | 0 | No prompts (Disable UAC) | High-risk, internal servers only | | 1 | Prompt for credentials on secure desktop | Strict server environments | | 2 | Prompt for consent on secure desktop (default for admins) | KTU recommended default | | 3 | Prompt for consent on secure desktop (default for standard) | KTU recommended default | | 4 | Prompt for credentials | Compatibility mode | | 5 | Prompt for consent | Kiosk-style machines | ### 2.4 Password Policy Components (Account Policies) Defined under: `Computer Configuration → Windows Settings → Security Settings → Account Policies` - **Password Policy** (`secpol.msc`): - *Minimum password length* - *Password complexity*: must contain ≥3 of {uppercase, lowercase, digits, symbols} - *Maximum password age*: typically **42 days** - *Minimum password age*: prevents rapid recycling (typically **1 day**) - *Password history*: remembers last **N** passwords (typically **5–24**) - **Account Lockout Policy**: - *Account lockout threshold*: **5–10** failed attempts - *Account lockout duration*: **15–30 minutes** (or until admin reset) - *Reset account lockout counter after*: **15 minutes** ### 2.5 Pillar 3: NTFS Permissions vs. Share Permissions | Feature | NTFS Permissions | Share Permissions | |---------|------------------|-------------------| | Applies to | Local + network access | Network access only | | Granularity | Per-user, per-file/folder | Per-share, per-user/group | | Effective permission logic | **Cumulative** (additive) for Allow, **Restrictive** for Deny | Less granular | | Applies to filesystem | NTFS only | All filesystems (FAT, NTFS) | | Inheritance | Yes (propagates to child objects) | No | > [!NOTE] > **Combined Effective Permission Rule (Network access to NTFS share):** > For any user accessing a resource over the network, the **most restrictive** of (NTFS permission, Share permission) applies. Locally, only NTFS permissions apply. ### 2.6 Standard NTFS Permission Set | Permission | Read | Write | Modify | Full Control | |------------|:----:|:-----:|:------:|:-------------:| | View file/folder | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Create files/folders | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Modify contents | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Delete | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | | Change permissions | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | | Take ownership | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula / Rule Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | |---|---------|----------------| | 1 | Effective NTFS Permission (multi-group) | $E = \bigcup_{i} P_i \;\cap\; \overline{D}$ where $P_i$ is permission from group $i$ and $D$ is any Deny ACE | | 2 | Effective Network Permission | $E_{net} = \min(P_{NTFS}, P_{Share})$ | | 3 | Failed Login Lockout Trigger | Triggers if `failed_attempts ≥ threshold` within `reset_window` | | 4 | Password Reuse Prevention | New password rejected if it matches any of the last `N` stored hashes (N = history) | | 5 | UAC Elevation Trigger | Triggered if process requests privilege not in restricted SID token | | 6 | Mandatory Integrity Model (MIC) levels | Low < Medium < High < System | | 7 | Effective permission over inheritance | $E = \text{Inherited ACEs} \cup \text{Explicit ACEs}$ (Explicit Deny wins) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Deny Wins" Rule:** In Windows ACL evaluation, an explicit **Deny ACE** always overrides any **Allow ACE**, regardless of position. This is a KTU favorite trick question. ### 2.8 Pillar 4: Defense-in-Depth Tools in Windows | Tool | Function | KTU Relevance | |------|----------|---------------| | **Windows Defender Antivirus** | Real-time malware scanning | Module 3 (Malware) | | **Windows Defender Firewall** | Host-based packet filtering | Module 4 (Firewalls) | | **BitLocker** | AES-128/256 full-disk encryption | Confidentiality at rest | | **Windows Update / WSUS** | Patch management | Vulnerability mitigation | | **AppLocker / SRP** | Application whitelisting | Execution control | | **Windows Event Viewer** | Security audit logs (Logon 4624/4625) | Detection & IR | | **Credential Guard** | Virtualization-based credential protection | Pass-the-hash defense | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Effective NTFS Permission Calculation Algorithm The KTU syllabus expects students to compute the *effective* permission when a user belongs to **multiple groups**. The official Microsoft algorithm is reproduced below with KTU-style annotation. **Given:** User *U* is a member of groups $G_1, G_2, \dots, G_n$. Each group has an ACE (Allow or Deny) on resource *R*. **Step 1 — Collect all ACEs applicable to U** $$ \text{ACE}_{\text{total}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \text{ACE}_{G_i} \;\cup\; \text{ACE}_{\text{user } U} $$ **Step 2 — Separate Allow ACEs and Deny ACEs** $$ \text{AllowSet} = \{a \in \text{ACE}_{\text{total}} \mid a.\text{type} = \text{Allow}\} $$ $$ \text{DenySet} = \{d \in \text{ACE}_{\text{total}} \mid d.\text{type} = \text{Deny}\} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Deny-overrides rule** If any Deny ACE exists in `DenySet` for a specific access right, that right is denied — *regardless* of how many Allow ACEs cover it. $$ E_{\text{final}}(R) = \begin{cases} \text{DENY}, & \text{if } \exists\, d \in \text{DenySet} \text{ covering right } R \\ \bigcup_{a \in \text{AllowSet}} a.\text{rights}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step 4 — Combine with Network Share permission (if access is remote)** $$ E_{\text{remote}} = \min\left(E_{\text{final}}(R),\; P_{\text{share}}\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why "min" and not "sum"?** The "most restrictive" principle exists because the network path introduces an *additional* choke point. Even if NTFS says "Full Control", the share-level permission may say "Read". A user attempting remote access cannot exceed the **narrower** of the two corridors. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem (Typical 7-mark KTU Part B sub-question):** > User *Aman* belongs to groups **HR** and **Developers**. On folder `ProjectX`, the ACLs are: > - HR: **Allow** Read, Write > - Developers: **Allow** Modify > - Aman (direct): **Deny** Delete > - The folder is shared as `\\server\ProjectX` with Share permission for Aman = **Read**. > > *Find the effective permission for Aman when accessing `ProjectX` (a) locally, (b) over the network.* ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — List all applicable ACEs for Aman:** | ACE Source | Type | Rights | |------------|------|--------| | Group HR | Allow | Read, Write | | Group Developers | Allow | Modify (= Read, Write, Execute, Delete) | | Direct (Aman) | Deny | Delete | **Step 2 — Local Access Calculation (No Share):** The Allow set grants: **{Read, Write, Modify}** (Developers' Modify already subsumes Read, Write and adds Execute, Delete). The Deny set explicitly denies **Delete**. Applying Deny-overrides: Delete is removed. $$ E_{\text{local}} = \{\text{Read, Write, Execute}\} $$ **Step 3 — Network Access Calculation:** Share permission for Aman = **Read**. $$ E_{\text{remote}} = \min(\{\text{Read, Write, Execute}\},\; \{\text{Read}\}) = \{\text{Read}\} $$ **Step 4 — Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{E_{\text{local}} = \text{Read, Write, Execute} \qquad E_{\text{remote}} = \text{Read only}} $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Account Lockout Policy — Worked Numerical **Given Policy:** - Lockout threshold = **5** failed attempts - Reset counter = **15** minutes - Lockout duration = **30** minutes - Observation window = a user tries to log in at 10:00, 10:02, 10:05, 10:07, 10:09, 10:12, 10:15 | Attempt # | Time | Outcome | |-----------|------|---------| | 1 | 10:00 | Fail → counter = 1 | | 2 | 10:02 | Fail → counter = 2 | | 3 | 10:05 | Fail → counter = 3 | | 4 | 10:07 | Fail → counter = 4 | | 5 | 10:09 | Fail → counter = 5 → **ACCOUNT LOCKED** | | 6 | 10:12 | Rejected (locked) | | 7 | 10:15 | Rejected (locked) — but counter resets at 10:24 (15 min after last fail at 10:09) | **Unlock time** = 10:09 + 30 min = **10:39** (whichever is later, since lockout is set for 30 min and counter is reset earlier). $$ \boxed{T_{\text{unlock}} = T_{\text{lock}} + T_{\text{duration}}} $$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: UAC Token Splitting Decision Tree ``` Process Request → Token required? │ ┌─────────┴─────────┐ Limited Token Full Token (used by default) (elevation) │ │ Action proceeds UAC prompt on with limited SecureDesktop privileges │ │ ┌──────┴──────┐ User clicks User clicks Yes No │ │ Full token Action denied granted (no privilege → process escalation) elevated ``` The decision is governed by the **requested privilege** (e.g., `SeDebugPrivilege`, `SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege`) listed in the process manifest, compared against the user's *filtered* access token. --- ### 3.6 The Mandatory Integrity Control (MIC) Model — Step-by-Step Windows uses an additional *integrity-level* check that runs **after** the DACL check. It prevents low-integrity processes (e.g., a browser tab) from writing to higher-integrity objects. $$ \text{Access Granted} \iff \text{DACL allows} \;\land\; \text{Subject.Integrity} \geq \text{Object.Integrity} $$ **Integrity Levels (from lowest to highest):** - **Untrusted** (0x0000) — Anonymous processes - **Low** (0x1000) — Browser sandbox, Protected Mode - **Medium** (0x2000) — Standard user token - **High** (0x3000) — Administrator token - **System** (0x4000) — Kernel, services **Example:** A Medium-token process (e.g., Word) **cannot** write to a High-integrity object (e.g., `C:\Windows\System32`) even if DACL technically allows it. The MIC check fails. $$ \boxed{\text{Medium (0x2000)} < \text{High (0x3000)} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Write rejected by MIC}} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Windows Security Defense-in-Depth Stack (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TD User["User / Attacker"] --> L1["Layer 1: Physical Security - BIOS/UEFI Password, BitLocker Pre-Boot PIN"] L1 --> L2["Layer 2: Authentication - Local SAM / Active Directory + Smart Card / MFA"] L2 --> L3["Layer 3: Authorization - User Rights Assignment + NTFS DACLs"] L3 --> L4["Layer 4: Privilege Control - UAC + Mandatory Integrity Control"] L4 --> L5["Layer 5: Execution Control - AppLocker, SRP, SmartScreen, Defender AV"] L5 --> L6["Layer 6: Network Defense - Windows Defender Firewall + IPSec"] L6 --> L7["Layer 7: Audit & Response - Event Viewer, Sysmon, WEF forwarding"] L7 --> L8["Layer 8: Patch Management - Windows Update, WSUS, SCCM"] classDef safe fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#155724 classDef monitor fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#004085 class L1,L2,L3,L4,L5,L6,L8 safe class L7 monitor ``` ### 4.2 UAC Process Flow — Elevation Request Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Process launched by user"] --> B{"Is admin token needed?"} B -- "No, restricted token suffices" --> C["Run with limited token
(Medium integrity)"] B -- "Yes, full token required" --> D["Request elevation to AppInfo service"] D --> E{"UAC consent policy level?"} E -- "Level 0/1" --> F["No prompt or Credential prompt"] E -- "Level 2-5" --> G["Display dialog on SecureDesktop"] G --> H{"User clicks Yes?"} H -- "No" --> I["Action denied, error returned"] H -- "Yes" --> J["Create full token with High integrity"] J --> K["Spawn elevated process"] K --> L["Process runs with full privileges"] C --> M["Process runs normally"] classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 classDef success fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745 classDef denial fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 class B,E,H decision class L,M success class I denial ``` ### 4.3 NTFS ACL Evaluation Decision Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["User requests access to object R"] --> Q1{"Object has inherited ACEs?"} Q1 -- "Yes" --> Merge1["Append inherited ACEs to object's DACL"] Q1 -- "No" --> Q2 Merge1 --> Q2{"User is referenced explicitly or via group?"} Q2 -- "Yes" --> Collect["Collect all matching ACEs"] Q2 -- "No" --> DenyAll["Access Denied"] Collect --> Split["Split into AllowSet and DenySet"] Split --> CheckDeny{"Does DenySet contain the requested right?"} CheckDeny -- "Yes" --> DenyFinal["Access DENIED
(Deny wins)"] CheckDeny -- "No" --> CheckAllow{"Does AllowSet contain the requested right?"} CheckAllow -- "Yes" --> MICCheck["Apply MIC integrity check"] CheckAllow -- "No" --> DenyAll MICCheck --> MICPass{"Subject integrity >= Object integrity?"} MICPass -- "Yes" --> Grant["Access GRANTED"] MICPass -- "No" --> MICDeny["Access DENIED (MIC)"] classDef terminalOK fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745 classDef terminalBad fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 class Grant terminalOK class DenyAll,DenyFinal,MICDeny terminalBad class Q1,Q2,CheckDeny,CheckAllow,MICPass decision ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Safe Windows Operation Workflow ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Identity Layer" IAM["User Identity
SAM / AD Account"] end subgraph "Authentication Layer" AUTH["Logon Process
(Winlogon + LSASS)"] end subgraph "Authorization Layer" AUTHZ["LSA Authorization
+ Privilege Check"] end subgraph "Token Layer" TOK["Access Token Creation
(Split Token for Admins)"] end subgraph "Object Access Layer" OBJ["Object Manager
SRM - Security Reference Monitor"] end subgraph "Audit Layer" AUD["Event Log
Security Log 4624/4625/4663"] end IAM --> AUTH --> AUTHZ --> TOK --> OBJ --> AUD AUD -.feedback.-> IAM classDef layer fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#007bff class IAM,AUTH,AUTHZ,TOK,OBJ,AUD layer ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Explain the Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP). How is it enforced in Windows?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)** is a security concept that requires every user, process, or program to operate with **only the minimum set of permissions** essential to perform its intended function, and no more. It limits the damage that can result from accidents, errors, or unauthorized use. > > **Enforcement in Windows:** > 1. **Account separation** — Windows provides *Standard* and *Administrator* accounts; daily work should use Standard. > 2. **UAC (User Account Control)** — Splits admin tokens; elevation prompts force explicit consent before granting full privileges. > 3. **Mandatory Integrity Control (MIC)** — Tags every process and object with integrity levels (Low, Medium, High, System); low-integrity subjects cannot write to higher-integrity objects. > 4. **NTFS DACLs** — Granular per-user/per-group file permissions. > 5. **Just-In-Time Admin (JIT)** via LAPS / third-party PAM tools. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is User Account Control (UAC)? List any two of its UAC consent levels.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **User Account Control (UAC)** is a mandatory access control feature introduced in Windows Vista that prevents unauthorized changes to the operating system by requiring explicit user consent (or admin credentials) when an action requires elevated privileges. > > **Key mechanisms:** token splitting (limited + full token), elevation prompts, secure desktop isolation, and AppInfo service intermediation. > > **Two UAC consent levels (of the standard 6):** > 1. **Level 0 — No prompts** (UAC effectively disabled; not recommended). > 2. **Level 3 — Prompt for consent for admins (default on Windows 7+)** — Admins see a consent dialog, standard users see a credential prompt. > 3. *(Any one of Level 1, 2, 4, 5 with brief description is also acceptable.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Deny-wins confusion:** Many students mistakenly sum permissions across groups. Always *check for an explicit Deny first* — it overrides every Allow. > 2. **Network vs Local:** Students frequently apply only NTFS permissions for remote access and forget the **Share permission's restrictive cap**. The mark for "network access" specifically tests this. > 3. **Token confusion:** When asked about UAC, students mention only "the popup" but forget the **token-splitting architecture**, which carries 3–4 marks by itself. > 4. **MIC omission:** Many answers on Windows access control skip the Mandatory Integrity Check entirely. Examiners often allocate 2 marks specifically for MIC. > 5. **Inheritance:** Marks are lost when students fail to note that **explicit ACEs override inherited ACEs** in conflicts. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Access Control & NTFS Focus** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]** *Consider the NTFS folder `D:\ProjectAlpha` shared over the network as `\\Server\Alpha`. The DACL is as follows:* - *Group `Designers`: Allow — Read, Write* - *Group `Testers`: Allow — Modify* - *User `Riya` (member of both Designers and Testers): Deny — Write* - *Share-level permission for `Riya`: Change (Read + Write)* - *The object has **High integrity level**; Riya's logon token is **Medium integrity**.* *Answer the following:* *(a) [7 marks] Calculate the effective permission for `Riya` when accessing `D:\ProjectAlpha` **locally**. Show every step of the evaluation.* *(b) [7 marks] Now calculate the effective permission for `Riya` when accessing the resource **over the network** as `\\Server\Alpha`. Also explain what role Mandatory Integrity Control (MIC) plays in this scenario and whether it changes the outcome.* --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Local Access — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Identify all applicable ACEs (1 mark):** - Designers (group): Allow {Read, Write} - Testers (group): Allow {Modify} = {Read, Write, Execute, Delete} - Riya (direct): Deny {Write} **Step 2 — Split into AllowSet and DenySet (1 mark):** $$ \text{AllowSet} = \{\{\text{Read, Write}\},\; \{\text{Read, Write, Execute, Delete}\}\} $$ $$ \text{DenySet} = \{\{\text{Write}\}\} $$ **Step 3 — Union the AllowSet (1 mark):** $$ \bigcup \text{AllowSet} = \{\text{Read, Write, Execute, Delete}\} $$ **Step 4 — Apply Deny-overrides rule (2 marks):** The DenySet contains **Write** → Write is removed. $$ E_{\text{local}} = \{\text{Read, Execute, Delete}\} $$ **Step 5 — Final answer (2 marks):** $$ \boxed{E_{\text{local}} = \text{Read, Execute, Delete (no Write, no Full Control)}} $$ --- **(b) Network Access + MIC — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Apply share-level permission (2 marks):** Share permission for Riya = **{Read, Write}**. **Step 2 — Take the more restrictive of NTFS and Share (2 marks):** $$ E_{\text{remote, pre-MIC}} = \min(\{\text{Read, Execute, Delete}\},\; \{\text{Read, Write}\}) = \{\text{Read}\} $$ (Read is the only right common to both sets.) **Step 3 — Apply MIC check (2 marks):** - Riya's token integrity: **Medium** (0x2000) - Object integrity: **High** (0x3000) - $0x2000 < 0x3000$ → write/execute/delete attempts would be blocked, but **Read is allowed** for lower-integrity subjects viewing higher-integrity objects (Read is permitted downward). **Step 4 — Final answer (1 mark):** $$ \boxed{E_{\text{remote}} = \text{Read only (effective read across both NTFS and Share); MIC further reinforces this by blocking any write attempt.}} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — UAC, Policies & Defense-in-Depth Focus** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]** *(a) [7 marks] Explain in detail the architecture of User Account Control (UAC) in Windows. Use a diagram in your explanation. Discuss the role of the **AppInfo service** and the **Secure Desktop**.* *(b) [7 marks] An organization wants to harden its Windows 10 client machines. Recommend a complete **Account Policy** (with specific numeric values for password and lockout policies) and justify each value. Also list **five** essential defense-in-depth tools (other than antivirus) the IT team should deploy.* --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) UAC Architecture — 7 Marks** **1. Goal of UAC (1 mark):** UAC prevents silent privilege escalation. Even when an attacker compromises a *Medium-integrity* process, they cannot gain *High-integrity* execution without an explicit, conscious user click. **2. Logon and Token Splitting (2 marks):** When an administrator logs in: - **Winlogon** contacts **LSASS** for authentication. - After successful authentication, LSASS creates **two** access tokens: - **Filtered token (Medium integrity)** — used for normal application launches (e.g., opening Word, browsing). Most admin SIDs are stripped; only `S-1-16-0` and standard user rights remain. - **Full token (High integrity)** — held in reserve, used *only* when an elevation request is approved. **3. Elevation Request Flow (2 marks):** - A process requests elevation (manifest declares `requestedExecutionLevel = requireAdministrator`). - The **Application Information Service (AppInfo, running as SYSTEM)** receives the request. - AppInfo evaluates the system's UAC policy and presents a prompt. - On user approval, AppInfo spawns the elevated process with the full token. - On rejection, the process is terminated or started with the limited token. **4. Secure Desktop Isolation (1 mark):** - The consent/credential dialog is rendered on a *separate, isolated desktop* (`WinSta0\Default` vs. `WinSta0\SecureDesktop`). - This prevents malicious processes from injecting code into the prompt UI (UI-hijacking attacks like Shatter attacks). - The Secure Desktop is accessible *only* to SYSTEM-level processes. **5. Mandatory Integrity Control integration (1 mark):** - The elevated process receives a **High-integrity token**; the limited process retains Medium. - Even if a Medium-integrity process is compromised, MIC prevents it from writing to High-integrity system objects. *Sketch of UAC elevation:* ``` User process (Medium token) │ requests elevation ▼ AppInfo Service (SYSTEM) │ displays prompt on SecureDesktop ▼ User clicks Yes ▼ Elevated process spawned (High token) ``` --- **(b) Recommended Account Policy & Defense-in-Depth Tools — 7 Marks** **1. Recommended Account Policy (4 marks):** | Policy | Recommended Value | Justification | |--------|-------------------|---------------| | Minimum password length | **12 characters** | NIST SP 800-63B suggests 8+ for human, but 12+ defends against offline brute force on hash dumps | | Password complexity | **Enabled** | Forces mix of cases, digits, symbols → larger keyspace | | Maximum password age | **90 days** (or use passphrase + no expiry per NIST 2024) | Forces periodic rotation for high-value accounts | | Minimum password age | **1 day** | Prevents user from rapidly cycling back to old password | | Password history | **24 passwords** | Prevents reuse of last 24 hashes | | Account lockout threshold | **10 failed attempts** | Tolerates typos, blocks automated brute force | | Account lockout duration | **30 minutes** | Temporary lockout, no admin intervention needed | | Reset lockout counter | **15 minutes** | Sliding window for the threshold | **2. Five Defense-in-Depth Tools (3 marks):** 1. **BitLocker (Full Disk Encryption)** — Protects data at rest if laptop is stolen. 2. **Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security** — Host-based stateful firewall. 3. **AppLocker / Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC)** — Application whitelisting; only signed, approved executables can run. 4. **Credential Guard** — Virtualization-based security (VBS) protects NTLM/Kerberos secrets from pass-the-hash. 5. **Windows Event Forwarding (WEF) + Sysmon** — Centralized security telemetry for threat hunting. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Operating Windows Safely"** - 🔑 **PoLP is the guiding principle** — Standard users for daily work, Admin only when needed. - 🛡️ **UAC architecture has 5 components**: logon, token splitting, AppInfo service, consent prompt, Secure Desktop. - 📊 **UAC has 6 consent levels (0–5)**; default for standard user is 3, for admin is 5 (Windows 10+). - 🗂️ **DACL = Allow ACEs + Deny ACEs**; evaluation order = **Deny wins, then Allow union, then MIC**. - 🌐 **Network access = `min(NTFS, Share)`** — most restrictive wins. - 🏗️ **MIC integrity ladder**: Untrusted < Low < Medium < High < System. Subject must be ≥ object integrity for write. - 🔐 **Password policy minima**: length ≥ 8 (preferably 12+), complexity on, history ≥ 5. - 🚨 **Lockout policy**: threshold ≤ 10, duration 15–30 min, reset window 15 min. - 🧱 **Defense-in-depth stack**: Physical → Authentication → Authorization → UAC → Execution Control → Firewall → Audit → Patches. - 📝 **Security audit IDs to memorize** (KTU loves these): - `4624` — Successful logon - `4625` — Failed logon - `4634/4647` — Logoff - `4663` — Object access attempt - `4688` — Process creation - ❌ **Common mistakes**: forgetting Deny-wins, ignoring Share permission, omitting MIC, confusing UAC with antivirus. - 🧪 **BitLocker ≠ EFS**: BitLocker encrypts the *entire volume*; EFS encrypts *individual files*. - 🏢 **Enterprise-scale**: For multiple machines, use **Group Policy (GPO)**, not local policy; centralize via **Active Directory**. - 🔄 **Always patch**: Windows Update / WSUS / SCCM is non-negotiable; most ransomware exploits unpatched SMB/RDP vulnerabilities. - 👤 **Built-in Administrator should be renamed + disabled** in enterprise environments (reduce attack surface). - 🪟 **LAPS** automates local admin password rotation across a fleet — strongly recommended for KTU short-answer completeness.

topics.title: Linux Security - Attacks in Linux System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-3
> - **Topic:** Linux Security - Attacks in Linux System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Linux System Attacks** refer to any malicious activity, exploitation, or unauthorized intrusion specifically targeted at the **Linux Operating System**, its kernel, daemons, file system, user space utilities, or networked services. They exploit architectural weaknesses such as the Discretionary Access Control (DAC) model, misconfigured **SUID binaries**, vulnerable network daemons (e.g., SSH, Apache, Samba), or kernel-level race conditions.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based framework, attacks in Linux systems are classified along two fundamental axes:
- **Attack Vector**: Where the attack originates (Remote, Local, Physical, Social).
- **Attack Impact**: The resulting CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) violation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Linux security is a foundational "applied security" module. Students are expected to demonstrate the ability to **identify attack surfaces**, **classify attacker types**, **analyze privilege escalation paths**, and **recommend countermeasures** (defense-in-depth). Marks are heavily awarded for flow diagrams and taxonomy tables.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Castle & The Banquet Hall" Model

Imagine a medieval castle (your Linux system) built with multiple concentric walls (security layers):
- **Outer Wall (Firewall/iptables)**: Keeps the public out.
- **Moat & Drawbridge (SSH / Network Services)**: Only authorized knights (users) cross with a password.
- **Great Hall (User Space)**: Knights can dine, but only in their rank-assigned section (file permissions `rwx`).
- **Royal Treasury (root account)**: A single master key, jealously guarded. If a footman tricks a knight into opening a side door (phishing), he sneaks into the treasury.
- **Underground Tunnels (Kernel)**: Hidden passages even the king doesn't know about. Builders (kernel devs) may leave some unsealed — these become **zero-day exploits**.

> A Linux attack is essentially finding one of these forgotten tunnels, bribing a guard, forging a key, or simply kicking down the drawbridge.

### 1.3 The Threat Landscape: Who Attacks Linux?

| Attacker Class | Skill Level | Motivation | Typical Linux Tactic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Script Kiddie** | Low | Curiosity, fame | Runs pre-compiled exploits (e.g., Metasploit modules) |
| **Hacktivist** | Medium | Ideology | Web defacement, DDoS on Apache/Nginx |
| **Organized Crime** | High | Financial | Ransomware (Linux variants like REvil, LockBit) |
| **Nation-State APT** | Expert | Espionage | Zero-day kernel exploits, rootkits |
| **Insider (Malicious Employee)** | Variable | Revenge, profit | Privilege escalation, data exfiltration |
| **Insider (Negligent Employee)** | Low | Carelessness | Misconfigured SUID, exposed `.ssh/` keys |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Misconception:** "Linux is immune to viruses." This is **FALSE**. Linux malware is rampant in IoT botnets (Mirai), cloud workloads, and crypto-mining worms. Open-source simply means the *attack surface is publicly known*, not that it is absent.

### 1.4 Linux Attack Surface: Where Attacks Land

```
[ Linux Attack Surface Map ]
+--------------------------------+
| Hardware (DMA, Thunderbolt)    |  <-- Cold boot, DMA attacks
+--------------------------------+
| Kernel (syscalls, drivers)     |  <-- Privilege escalation, rootkits
+--------------------------------+
| System Libraries (glibc, libc) |  <-- Buffer overflows (Heartbleed)
+--------------------------------+
| Daemons (sshd, httpd, mysqld)  |  <-- Remote exploits
+--------------------------------+
| User Applications (Firefox)    |  <-- Drive-by downloads
+--------------------------------+
| User (human factor)            |  <-- Social engineering
+--------------------------------+
```

**Constants / Standard Metrics Used in Linux Security (for KTU reference):**

- **Password Entropy** $H$ is measured in **bits**, calculated as $H = L \cdot \log_2(R)$, where $L$ is the password length and $R$ is the size of the character pool.
- **Brute-force Time** is expressed in **seconds**, using the formula $T = \dfrac{R^L}{S}$, where $S$ is the hash attempts per second.
- **CVSS Base Score** is a dimensionless value ranging from **0.0 to 10.0** (Common Vulnerability Scoring System).
- **Standard MTU** for Ethernet is **1500 bytes**; oversized packets indicate fragmentation attacks like **Ping of Death** or **Teardrop**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases of a Linux Attack (Attack Lifecycle / "Kill Chain")

A Linux intrusion typically unfolds in five structured phases, mapped to **MITRE ATT&CK** tactics:

1. **Reconnaissance (TA0043)**: The attacker enumerates the target using tools like `nmap`, `whois`, `dnsenum`, and `Shodan`. Goal: identify open ports (22, 80, 443, 3306), OS fingerprint, and running services.
2. **Weaponization (TA0042)**: The attacker pairs an exploit (e.g., EternalBlue, Dirty COW) with a payload (reverse shell, rootkit, ransomware).
3. **Delivery (TA0041)**: The exploit reaches the Linux host via phishing email, malicious `.deb`/`.rpm` package, compromised repository (XZ Utils backdoor, 2024), or drive-by download.
4. **Exploitation & Installation (TA0002 / TA0003)**: The vulnerability is triggered. A reverse shell (`/dev/tcp` bash, `nc -e`, `meterpreter`) is established. A persistence mechanism (cron job, systemd service) is installed.
5. **Actions on Objectives (TA0040)**: Data exfiltration via `scp`, `rsync`, DNS tunneling; lateral movement via SSH keys; ransomware deployment.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Common Linux Attacks

#### A. Password-Based Attacks
- **Brute Force**: Tries every possible character combination. Defeated by `fail2ban`, account lockout, strong key derivation (`bcrypt`, `yescrypt`).
- **Dictionary Attack**: Uses a wordlist (`/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt`). Defeated by policy enforcement of complex passwords via `pam_pwquality`.
- **Offline Hash Crack**: Attacker steals `/etc/shadow` and cracks with `john` or `hashcat` using GPU. Defeated by salting + modern hashing (SHA-512, yescrypt).
- **Credential Stuffing**: Reuses leaked credentials from other breaches.

#### B. Buffer Overflow Attacks
- **Stack Smashing**: Overflows a stack buffer to overwrite the saved return address. Classic example: **Stack Guard bypass via return-to-libc** and **ROP (Return-Oriented Programming)**.
- **Heap Overflow**: Corrupts `malloc()` metadata.
- **Format String Attack**: Exploits `printf(user_input)` to read/write arbitrary memory.
- **Defenses (built into modern Linux)**: **ASLR** (Address Space Layout Randomization), **NX bit** (No-Execute / DEP), **Stack Canaries** (`-fstack-protector`), **PIE** (Position Independent Executables), **RELRO**.

#### C. Privilege Escalation
- **Vertical (User → root)**: Exploits SUID binaries (e.g., `find / -perm -4000`), kernel exploits (Dirty COW, DirtyPipe), misconfigured `sudo` (`sudo -l`), or writable `/etc/passwd`.
- **Horizontal (User A → User B)**: Exploits weak file permissions, world-readable SSH keys, or shared credentials.
- **Kernel Exploits**: A user triggers a vulnerability in a kernel module to execute code as UID 0. Modern mitigation: **Kernel Address Sanitizer (KASLR)**, **Secure Boot**, **LUKS full-disk encryption**.

#### D. Malware Taxonomy in Linux

| Malware Type | Behavior | Classic Linux Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Virus** | Attaches to files; requires user execution | `Bliss` (1997), `Linux.Rex` |
| **Worm** | Self-propagates over network | `Code Red` variants, `Mirai` (IoT) |
| **Trojan Horse** | Disguised as legitimate software | `XTR/Downloader`, fake `.deb` packages |
| **Rootkit** | Hides its presence from `ls`, `ps` | `Reptile`, `Diamorphine`, `Adore-ng` |
| **Ransomware** | Encrypts user data, demands payment | `REvil`, `LockBit`, `Conti` |
| **Cryptominer** | Hijacks CPU/GPU for Monero mining | `XMRig`, `Kinsing` |
| **Bot / Backdoor** | Receives C2 commands | `BillGates`, `Mirai` |

#### E. Denial of Service (DoS / DDoS)
- **SYN Flood**: Exhausts `tcp_max_syn_backlog` with half-open connections.
- **UDP Flood / ICMP Flood**: Bandwidth saturation.
- **Ping of Death**: Sends malformed packets exceeding 65,535 bytes.
- **Slowloris**: Holds HTTP connections open with partial headers, exhausting Apache worker threads.
- **Local DoS**: `fork bomb` `:(){ :|:& };:`, runaway processes filling `/tmp`.

#### F. Network-Based Attacks
- **Sniffing**: Promiscuous mode NICs capture unencrypted traffic.
- **MITM (Man-in-the-Middle)**: ARP spoofing, DNS spoofing, rogue Wi-Fi.
- **Session Hijacking**: Theft of session cookies or SSH session keys.
- **Port Scanning**: `nmap -sS` (SYN), `-sT` (TCP connect), `-sU` (UDP).

#### G. File System & Boot Attacks
- **Bootloader Attack**: Modifying GRUB to bypass authentication (single-user mode without password).
- **Live USB Persistence**: Booting from USB to read/mount unencrypted disks.
- **Tmpfs / World-Writable**: Planting executables in `/tmp`, `/var/tmp`, `/dev/shm`.

### 2.3 Linux Security Mechanisms (The "Defense-in-Depth" Stack)

| Layer | Mechanism | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware** | TPM 2.0, Secure Boot | Validates bootloader chain-of-trust |
| **Kernel** | ASLR, KASLR, seccomp-bpf | Mitigates memory corruption |
| **Mandatory Access Control** | SELinux (NSA), AppArmor (SUSE) | Enforces least-privilege policy |
| **Discretionary ACLs** | POSIX `rwx` (chmod, chown) | Owner-controlled permissions |
| **Capabilities** | `setcap` (e.g., `cap_net_bind_service`) | Splits root power into 40+ discrete units |
| **PAM (Pluggable Auth Modules)** | `/etc/pam.d/` | Modular authentication policy |
| **Firewall** | iptables, nftables, firewalld, ufw | Packet filtering, NAT |
| **IDS/IPS** | Snort, Suricata, OSSEC, Fail2ban | Detects anomalous behavior |
| **File Integrity** | AIDE, Tripwire, Samhain | Detects `/etc/passwd` changes |
| **Logging** | auditd, syslog-ng, journald | Forensic trail |
| **Cryptography** | LUKS, eCryptfs, GPG | Disk and file encryption |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Password Entropy | $H = L \cdot \log_2(R)$ | $L$=length, $R$=charset size | bits | $R=94$ for printable ASCII |
| 2 | Brute-Force Time | $T = \dfrac{R^L}{2 \cdot S}$ | $S$=hashes/sec | seconds | Divide by 2 (avg. half keyspace) |
| 3 | Attack Probability | $P_A = T_v \cdot V \cdot I$ | $T_v$=threat, $V$=vuln, $I$=impact | dimensionless | Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact |
| 4 | CVSS Base Score | $\text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\, 10))$ | — | 0–10 scale | $0$=None, $10$=Critical |
| 5 | Shannon Channel Capacity (theoretical link saturation) | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $B$=bandwidth, SNR | bits/sec | Used in DDoS bandwidth analysis |
| 6 | Hash Strength (entropy vs. work factor) | $W = 2^H$ | $H$=entropy in bits | operations | Work to brute-force |
| 7 | SYN Flood Backlog Required | $R_{\text{needed}} \ge \text{RPS}_{\text{in}} \times T_{\text{timeout}}$ | RPS, timeout | packets | Defensive sizing for `tcp_max_syn_backlog` |
| 8 | Risk Rating | $\text{Risk} = \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact}$ | Likelihood (1–5), Impact (1–5) | 1–25 | KTU examiner's favorite matrix |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical pipe safety:** In all formulas above, conditional set notation uses `\ge` and `\vert` syntax. Never use a literal `|` character inside a markdown table cell to avoid breaking table rendering.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas and classifications are not academic exercises. In **Cloud Security Engineering** (AWS, Azure, GCP), every Linux EC2/VM instance is hardened using this exact taxonomy. In **DevSecOps pipelines**, `trivy`, `lynis`, and `OpenSCAP` automate checks against these baselines (CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs). In **OT/SCADA environments**, Linux controls power plants and water treatment — a successful attack is a **physical safety incident**, not just a data breach. KTU graduates entering the Kerala IT sector (Infosys, TCS, UST, Quest) will be expected to triage CVEs using CVSS and apply the kill-chain model to incident response.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Brute-Force Time Against a Linux `/etc/shadow` Hash

Suppose an attacker has stolen `/etc/shadow` containing a SHA-512-crypt hash (modern default in Ubuntu 22.04, RHEL 9). A high-end GPU rig (e.g., RTX 4090) achieves approximately $S = 50{,}000$ hashes per second for SHA-512-crypt (because of the 5000 rounds of iteration).

**Given:**
- Password policy: minimum 8 characters, must include uppercase, lowercase, digit, and symbol.
- Character set: $R = 26 + 26 + 10 + 32 = 94$ (printable ASCII minus space, conservatively).

**Find:** Average time to crack by brute force.

**Step 1 — Compute the search space (worst case).**
The number of possible passwords is $R^L$ for length $L$.

$$
N_{\text{keyspace}} = 94^8
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the keyspace size.**
We compute $\log_{10}(94^8)$ for intuition.

$$
\log_{10}(94^8) = 8 \cdot \log_{10}(94) = 8 \cdot 1.9731 \approx 15.785
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_{\text{keyspace}} \approx 10^{15.785} \approx 6.1 \times 10^{15}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute average (mean) attempts.**
On average, the attacker tries half the keyspace.

$$
N_{\text{avg}} = \frac{94^8}{2} \approx 3.05 \times 10^{15}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute time at GPU rate.**
With $S = 5 \times 10^4$ hashes/second:

$$
T = \frac{N_{\text{avg}}}{S} = \frac{3.05 \times 10^{15}}{5 \times 10^4}
$$

$$
T = 6.1 \times 10^{10} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to years.**

$$
T_{\text{years}} = \frac{6.1 \times 10^{10}}{60 \cdot 60 \cdot 24 \cdot 365} = \frac{6.1 \times 10^{10}}{3.154 \times 10^7}
$$

$$
T_{\text{years}} \approx 1934 \text{ years}
$$

**Interpretation:** An 8-character complex password, with modern SHA-512-crypt salting and 5000 rounds, requires ~1934 years on a single GPU. This is why **iteration count** is the single most important defense — increasing rounds to 1,000,000 raises $T$ by **200×**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Attacks to Linux Defenses

| Attack Class | Concrete Linux Example | CVE / Tool | Impact (CIA) | Primary Linux Defense | Secondary Defense |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brute Force SSH | `hydra -l root -P rockyou.txt ssh://target` | — | Confidentiality (C) | `fail2ban`, `AllowUsers` directive, key-based auth | Disable root SSH login (`PermitRootLogin no`) |
| Privilege Escalation | Dirty COW | CVE-2016-5195 | I, C, A | Kernel patch (≥ 4.8.3) | `sysctl kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone=0` |
| Buffer Overflow | Stack smash in `sudo` | CVE-2021-3156 (Baron Samedit) | I, C | Update `sudo`; compile with `-fstack-protector-strong` | PIE, ASLR, seccomp |
| Rootkit | `diamorphine` LKM | — | I, A | `rkhunter`, `chkrootkit`, `AIDE` | Secure Boot, kernel module signing |
| Ransomware | `LockBit` Linux variant | — | A, I | Offline backups, immutable storage | SELinux write-restriction policy |
| DoS (SYN) | `hping3 -S --flood` | — | A | `iptables -m limit --limit 25/s` | `tcp_syncookies=1`, increase backlog |
| MITM | `ettercap -T -M arp:remote` | — | C, I | HTTPS/TLS, HSTS, certificate pinning | DNSSEC, ARP monitoring |
| Format String | `printf(argv[1])` in custom C code | — | I, C | Compiler warnings (`-Wformat-security`) | `FORTIFY_SOURCE=2` |
| Web App (SQLi) | WordPress plugin exploit | — | C, I | ModSecurity WAF, input validation | Least-privilege DB user |
| Cold Boot | RAM dump after power-off | — | C | TPM, full-disk LUKS encryption | Disable DMA via IOMMU |
| Supply Chain | Backdoored `xz-utils` liblzma | CVE-2024-3094 | I, C, A | Reproducible builds, package signing (GPG) | Pin specific versions, sandbox builds |

### 3.3 Detailed Step-by-Step: Privilege Escalation via SUID Binary Abuse (GTFOBins Method)

This is a **vertical privilege escalation** technique frequently tested in KTU labs and Capture-The-Flag (CTF) exams.

**Step 1 — Enumerate SUID binaries owned by root.**
The attacker runs:

$$
\text{find } / \text{ -perm -4000 } -type f \; 2>/dev/null
$$

This returns a list of binaries with the SUID bit set, e.g., `/usr/bin/find`, `/usr/bin/vim`, `/usr/bin/python3`.

**Step 2 — Cross-reference with GTFOBins.**
