| 7 | Inactive days after expiry | `14` |
| 8 | Account expiry (absolute date) | `99999` = never |
| 9 | Reserved (unused) | empty |

## 2.4 Pluggable Authentication Modules (PAM)

PAM is the **heart of Linux authentication**. It decouples authentication *policy* (rules) from authentication *mechanism* (the actual check). A PAM-aware application (like `login`, `sshd`, `sudo`) consults a chain of modules configured in `/etc/pam.d/<service>`.

Each module returns one of four return codes:
- `PAM_SUCCESS` (0) — success
- `PAM_AUTH_ERR` — authentication failed
- `PAM_USER_UNKNOWN` — user not found
- `PAM_ACCT_EXPIRED` — account restrictions failed

**The four PAM management groups:**

| Group | Purpose | Example Module |
|-------|---------|----------------|
| `auth` | Verifies identity | `pam_unix.so`, `pam_ldap.so` |
| `account` | Checks account validity | `pam_nologin.so`, `pam_time.so` |
| `session` | Manages session setup/teardown | `pam_limits.so`, `pam_motd.so` |
| `password` | Updates authentication tokens | `pam_pwquality.so`, `pam_cracklib.so` |

## 2.5 Access Control Models in Linux

Linux supports **three coexisting access control paradigms**:

### 2.5.1 Discretionary Access Control (DAC) — The Default

The classical UNIX permission model. Each file has an **owner** (u), a **group** (g), and **others** (o), each with **read (r=4), write (w=2), execute (x=1)** bits. The owner can change permissions at their discretion (hence "discretionary").

### 2.5.2 Mandatory Access Control (MAC) — System-Enforced Policy

Policy is set by the system administrator and **cannot be overridden by the file owner**. Implemented in Linux by:
- **SELinux** (Security-Enhanced Linux) — NSA-origin, label-based, integrated into the kernel
- **AppArmor** — Novell/SUSE-origin, path-based, simpler

### 2.5.3 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

Users are assigned **roles**; roles are granted **permissions**. In Linux, this is approximated by `sudo` rules and group membership. RBAC is the conceptual foundation of enterprise identity systems (LDAP, Active Directory, FreeIPA).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Permission & Umask Mathematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU student must memorize. All equations are mathematically verifiable against standard UNIX behavior.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Range | Example |
|---------|----------------|---------------|---------|
| Symbolic permission to octal | $P_{oct} = r \cdot 4 + w \cdot 2 + x \cdot 1$ | $P_{oct} \in [0, 7]$ | `rwx` → 4+2+1 = **7** |
| Three-triple octal | $(P_{owner}, P_{group}, P_{other})$ | each $\in [0, 7]$ | `rwxr-x---` → **750** |
| Default file base | $Base_{file} = 666_8$ | octal | Always `666` |
| Default directory base | $Base_{dir} = 777_8$ | octal | Always `777` |
| Actual file permission | $F_{actual} = Base_{file} \;\&\;\sim\,umask$ | bitwise AND + NOT | base `666` & ~`022` = `644` |
| Actual directory permission | $D_{actual} = Base_{dir} \;\&\;\sim\,umask$ | bitwise AND + NOT | base `777` & ~`022` = `755` |
| `setuid` bit value | $4$ (octal prefix on owner triple) | `[0, 7]` for triple | `4755` |
| `setgid` bit value | $2$ (octal prefix on group triple) | `[0, 7]` for triple | `2755` |
| Sticky bit value | $1$ (octal prefix on other triple) | `[0, 7]` for triple | `1777` |
| Salt length (modern) | $salt_{len} = 16$ characters | hex | `$6$abc…$` |
| Hash algorithm ID | `$1$=MD5,\; \$5$=SHA-256,\; \$6$=SHA-512$ | symbolic | `$6$` preferred |
| Shadow aging (days) | $t_{age} = t_{now} - t_{lastchange}$ | days since Unix epoch | `19000` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students compute file permissions as `777 - umask`. That is **wrong for files** — files never get the execute bit by default. Always use `666 - umask` for files and `777 - umask` for directories.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Linux access control primitives power **virtually every enterprise security stack**:

- **Cloud-native platforms** (AWS, Azure, GCP Linux VMs) rely on `sudo` and SELinux for tenant isolation.
- **Kubernetes nodes** enforce MAC via AppArmor profiles on every container.
- **Banking and defense** systems use SELinux in **MLS (Multi-Level Security)** mode with Bell-LaPadula enforcement.
- **DevSecOps pipelines** gate access to production using LDAP + PAM + sudo + 2FA (e.g., Google Authenticator PAM module).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Octal-to-Symbolic Permission Conversion

**Problem:** Convert the octal permission `754` to its symbolic form.

**Step 1 — Decompose the octal triple into three independent digits.**

The 3-digit octal number represents owner, group, and others respectively. The leading digit is **not** a special bit here (we will treat `setuid/setgid/sticky` separately).

$$
P_{oct} = 754 = (7, 5, 4)_{triple}
$$

Step 1 logic: A 3-digit octal permission is read left-to-right as (owner, group, other). We split the string into three independent values for independent decoding.

**Step 2 — Decode each digit using the bitweight formula.**

For each digit $d \in \{0, 1, \dots, 7\}$, the symbolic triplet $(r, w, x)$ is given by:

$$
r = 1 \text{ if } d \geq 4 \text{ else } 0
$$
$$
w = 1 \text{ if } d \mod 4 \geq 2 \text{ else } 0
$$
$$
x = 1 \text{ if } d \mod 2 = 1 \text{ else } 0
$$

Step 2 logic: Each permission bit occupies a unique bit position (4 for read, 2 for write, 1 for execute). Subtracting the largest power first (greedy) yields the bit values.

**Step 3 — Apply the formula to owner digit 7.**

$$
r_o = 1 \;(\text{since } 7 \geq 4)
$$
$$
w_o = 1 \;(\text{since } 7 \mod 4 = 3 \geq 2)
$$
$$
x_o = 1 \;(\text{since } 7 \mod 2 = 1)
$$

Owner permission: **`rwx`**.

**Step 4 — Apply the formula to group digit 5.**

$$
r_g = 1 \;(\text{since } 5 \geq 4)
$$
$$
w_g = 0 \;(\text{since } 5 \mod 4 = 1 < 2)
$$
$$
x_g = 1 \;(\text{since } 5 \mod 2 = 1)
$$

Group permission: **`r-x`**.

**Step 5 — Apply the formula to other digit 4.**

$$
r_x = 1 \;(\text{since } 4 \geq 4)
$$
$$
w_x = 0 \;(\text{since } 4 \mod 4 = 0 < 2)
$$
$$
x_x = 0 \;(\text{since } 4 \mod 2 = 0)
$$

Other permission: **`r--`**.

**Step 6 — Concatenate the three triplets.**

$$
754 \longrightarrow \text{rwxr-xr--}
$$

Final symbolic form: **`rwxr-xr--`**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — umask Computation

**Problem:** A system administrator sets `umask = 027`. Compute the default permissions for (i) a newly created file and (ii) a newly created directory.

**Step 1 — Recall the base permissions.**

The Linux kernel applies base permissions **before** the umask mask:

$$
Base_{file} = 110\,110\,110_2 = 666_8
$$
$$
Base_{dir} = 111\,111\,111_2 = 777_8
$$

Step 1 logic: The kernel's `open(2)`/`mkdir(2)` syscalls begin with these bases. Directories must be executable (to `cd` into them), but regular files do not need the execute bit by default — this is why the bases differ.

**Step 2 — Convert the umask to binary and invert it.**

The umask `027` in octal is `000 010 111` in binary. Its bitwise complement is:

$$
\sim umask = \sim(000\,010\,111_2) = 111\,101\,000_2 = 750_8
$$

Step 2 logic: A umask of `027` means "**deny** write+execute to group, and **deny** read+write+execute to others." Inverting the mask produces the **permissions to preserve** (the bits we are allowed to keep).

**Step 3 — Compute the file permission by ANDing base with the inverted umask.**

$$
F_{actual} = Base_{file} \;\&\; \sim\,umask
$$
$$
F_{actual} = 110\,110\,110_2 \;\&\; 111\,101\,000_2
$$

Performing the bitwise AND column-by-column:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Owner: } & 110 \;\&\; 111 = 110 \quad (rw-) \\
\text{Group: } & 110 \;\&\; 101 = 100 \quad (r--) \\
\text{Other: } & 110 \;\&\; 000 = 000 \quad (---)
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 logic: Bits are cleared wherever the umask has a 1. Since the umask has `010` in the group field, the group's `w` bit is cleared. The umask is `111` in the other field, so all other bits are stripped.

**Result for the file:**

$$
F_{actual} = 110\,100\,000_2 = 640_8
$$

The new file is created with permission **`rw-r-----`** (octal `640`).

**Step 4 — Compute the directory permission by ANDing base with the inverted umask.**

$$
D_{actual} = Base_{dir} \;\&\; \sim\,umask
$$
$$
D_{actual} = 111\,111\,111_2 \;\&\; 111\,101\,000_2
$$

Performing the bitwise AND column-by-column:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Owner: } & 111 \;\&\; 111 = 111 \quad (rwx) \\
\text{Group: } & 111 \;\&\; 101 = 101 \quad (r-x) \\
\text{Other: } & 111 \;\&\; 000 = 000 \quad (---)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result for the directory:**

$$
D_{actual} = 111\,101\,000_2 = 750_8
$$

The new directory is created with permission **`rwxr-x---`** (octal `750`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using shorthand:** `666 - 027 = 639` (wrong without the bitwise operation!) versus the correct `640`. The shorthand works only when there is **no borrow** across bits. This is a common KTU trap.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Special Permission Bits

**Problem:** A system administrator issues `chmod 4755 /usr/bin/program`. Explain the meaning of the leading `4`.

**Step 1 — Recognize the 4-digit octal format.**

The general chmod octal is a 4-digit number:

$$
S_{perm} = (s_{setuid}, s_{setgid}, s_{sticky}, P_{owner}, P_{group}, P_{other})
$$

The leading digit encodes the **special bits**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{setuid}  &= 4 \quad \text{(Set User ID)} \\
s_{setgid}  &= 2 \quad \text{(Set Group ID)} \\
s_{sticky}  &= 1 \quad \text{(Sticky Bit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 1 logic: The three special bits are independent and **additive** — they can be combined (e.g., `6` = setuid+setgid).

**Step 2 — Interpret `4755` for the file `/usr/bin/program`.**

$$
S_{perm} = (4, 7, 5, 5) \longrightarrow s_{setuid}=4,\; s_{setgid}=0,\; s_{sticky}=0,\; P_{owner}=7,\; P_{group}=5,\; P_{other}=5
$$

The setuid bit is set, and the standard permission is `rwxr-xr-x`.

**Step 3 — Explain the effect.**

When a user executes `/usr/bin/program`:
- Normally, the process runs with the **user's UID**.
- With `setuid` set, the process runs with the **owner's UID** — typically root (UID 0).
- This allows the program to perform privileged operations (e.g., changing passwords) while still being executable by ordinary users.

**Security implication:** A setuid root program is a **critical attack surface**. If the program has a buffer overflow, an attacker can escalate to root. Hence modern Linux distros minimize the number of setuid binaries.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Authentication Mechanism Comparison

| Mechanism | Authentication Factor | Storage Location | Brute-Force Resistance | KTU Exam Frequency |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| `/etc/passwd` (legacy) | Knowledge (password) | World-readable file | **None** (DES hash) | Low (historical) |
| `/etc/shadow` + SHA-512 | Knowledge (password) | Root-readable file | **High** (salted SHA-512) | **Very High** |
| PAM + LDAP | Knowledge + Directory | Central LDAP server | High (rate-limited by PAM) | **Very High** |
| PAM + Kerberos | Knowledge + Ticket | KDC (Key Distribution Center) | **Very High** (tickets expire) | High |
| SSH public key | Possession (private key) | `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` | **Extremely High** (4096-bit RSA) | **Very High** |
| PAM + TOTP (2FA) | Knowledge + Possession | RFC 6238 time token | Very High | **Very High** |
| Smart card / PAM_pkcs11 | Possession (hardware) | PKCS#11 token | Very High | Medium |
| SELinux user context | Identity + Role | Kernel policy DB | N/A (authorization, not auth) | **Very High** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Linux Authentication Flow

The following block diagram shows the **end-to-end authentication flow** when a user attempts to log in to a Linux system via SSH or console.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User attempts login] --> B{Login program invoked}
    B --> C[login / sshd / gdm]
    C --> D[Read username and password]
    D --> E[PAM: auth phase]
    E --> F[PAM: account phase]
    F --> G{PAM result}
    G -->|Success| H[NSS lookup UID and GID]
    G -->|Failure| I[Log failure and deny access]
    H --> J[PAM: session phase]
    J --> K[Set UID GID supplementary groups]
    K --> L[Launch login shell]
    L --> M[Apply SELinux or AppArmor context]
    M --> N[User shell ready]
    I --> O[Audit log: auth failure]
```

Diagram explanation: The flow proceeds top-to-bottom. A failure at any PAM phase short-circuits to the audit log. The successful path culminates in the launch of a confined shell under the system MAC policy.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — PAM Stack Architecture

This diagram illustrates the modular structure of PAM and how four management groups cooperate to authenticate a user.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph App[Application: login or sshd]
        APPL[Application calls pam_authenticate]
    end
    subgraph Stack[PAM Stack in /etc/pam.d/login]
        M1["auth: pam_env.so"]
        M2["auth: pam_unix.so"]
        M3["account: pam_unix.so"]
        M4["account: pam_nologin.so"]
        M5["session: pam_limits.so"]
        M6["session: pam_motd.so"]
        M7["password: pam_pwquality.so"]
    end
    subgraph Back[Backends]
        DB1[/etc/shadow/]
        DB2[/etc/ldap/]
        DB3[Kerberos KDC]
    end
    APPL --> M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3 --> M4
    M3 --> M5 --> M6
    M2 --> M7
    M2 -.-> DB1
    M2 -.-> DB2
    M2 -.-> DB3
```

Diagram explanation: The application invokes PAM, which consults a stack of modules in order. Each module may delegate to a backend store. The four management groups (auth, account, session, password) handle different aspects of the user session.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Access Control Decision Flow

This diagram shows how the Linux kernel combines DAC, ACL, and MAC to reach a final access decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process attempts open file] --> B{Is process UID zero}
    B -->|Yes root| C[Grant access if no MAC deny]
    B -->|No| D{Check DAC owner match}
    D -->|Match| E{Apply owner permission bits}
    D -->|No match| F{Check DAC group match}
    F -->|Match| G{Apply group permission bits}
    F -->|No match| H{Apply other permission bits}
    E --> I{Check ACL entries}
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{ACL mask permits}
    J -->|No| K[Deny]
    J -->|Yes| L{SELinux or AppArmor policy}
    L -->|Allow| M[Grant access]
    L -->|Deny| K
    C --> L
```

Diagram explanation: The kernel first checks DAC (traditional UNIX permissions), then consults ACLs for additional user or group entries, and finally evaluates the MAC policy (SELinux/AppArmor). Only if every layer permits is access granted.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — /etc/shadow Hashing Pipeline

This diagram explains how a plaintext password becomes the `$6$salt$hash` string stored in `/etc/shadow`.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext password from user] --> B[Generate random 16 byte salt]
    B --> C[Concatenate salt and password]
    C --> D[Apply SHA-512 5000 rounds default]
    D --> E[Encode result as base64]
    E --> F[Build final string dollar 6 salt hash]
    F --> G[Store in etc shadow field 2]
```

Diagram explanation: The pipeline is non-reversible. The salt ensures that two users with the same password have different hashes, defeating precomputed rainbow-table attacks. The 5000 rounds of SHA-512 deliberately slow brute-force attempts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Differentiate between **authentication** and **authorization**. Give one example of each in a Linux system.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Authentication (AuthN) | Authorization (AuthZ) |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Purpose | Verifies **who** the user is | Verifies **what** the user may do |
| Question answered | "Are you really alice?" | "Is alice allowed to read `/etc/shadow`?" |
| Order in security flow | First | After authentication |
| Linux example | User enters password at `login:` prompt | Linux checks file permission `r--------` against alice's UID/GID |
| Implementation | PAM modules (`pam_unix.so`) | DAC bits, ACLs, SELinux policy |

**One-line exam-ready definition:** *Authentication establishes identity; authorization enforces access rights based on that identity.*

*(3 marks: 1 for definition of each, 1 for example)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]** What is **Pluggable Authentication Module (PAM)**? List its four management groups with one example module each.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** PAM is a flexible authentication framework in Linux that decouples applications from the underlying authentication mechanism. It allows system administrators to configure authentication policy without recompiling applications.

**Four management groups (2 marks):**

| Group | Purpose | Example Module |
|-------|---------|----------------|
| `auth` | Verifies user identity | `pam_unix.so` |
| `account` | Checks account validity (expiry, time-of-day) | `pam_nologin.so` |
| `session` | Manages session setup and teardown | `pam_limits.so` |
| `password` | Updates authentication tokens | `pam_pwquality.so` |

PAM configuration files reside in `/etc/pam.d/`, with one file per PAM-aware service (e.g., `login`, `sshd`, `sudo`).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Part B question carries an **internal choice**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both are presented below for practice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of the `/etc/shadow` file in Linux. Discuss the significance of the **salt** field and the **shadow password scheme** in defending against password attacks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the following special permission bits in Linux with a real-world example for each: **(7 marks)**
   1. `setuid` (Set User ID)
   2. `setgid` (Set Group ID)
   3. Sticky bit

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) /etc/shadow File Structure (7 marks)

The `/etc/shadow` file is a root-readable, colon-separated database containing encrypted passwords and password-aging information for every user. Each line has **9 fields**:

$$
\text{shadow} = (u, H, d_{last}, d_{min}, d_{max}, d_{warn}, d_{inact}, d_{expire}, r)
$$

| # | Field | Meaning |
|---|-------|---------|
| 1 | `u` | Username (links to `/etc/passwd` field 1) |
| 2 | `H` | Encrypted password: `$id$salt$hash` |
| 3 | `d_last` | Days since Unix epoch (1970-01-01) of last password change |
| 4 | `d_min` | Minimum days between password changes |
| 5 | `d_max` | Maximum days the password is valid |
| 6 | `d_warn` | Days before expiry to start warning |
| 7 | `d_inact` | Days after expiry before account is disabled |
| 8 | `d_expire` | Absolute account expiration date |
| 9 | `r` | Reserved (unused) |

**Hash ID prefixes (1 mark):**
- `$1$` → MD5 (legacy)
- `$5$` → SHA-256
- `$6$` → SHA-512 (**modern default**)

**Significance of the salt (3 marks):**

The salt is a **16-character random string** generated when the password is set. Its purpose:

1. **Defeats precomputed rainbow tables:** Two users with the password `tiger123` produce *different* hashes because their salts differ. An attacker cannot reuse a single precomputed table.
2. **Forces per-user brute force:** An attacker must re-hash the entire dictionary for every user, multiplying the attack cost by $N$ where $N$ is the number of users.
3. **Enables parallel cracking:** Multiple GPUs can attack different users in parallel without collision.

**Significance of the shadow scheme (2 marks):**

Before the shadow scheme, encrypted passwords lived in `/etc/passwd`, which is **world-readable** (`rw-r--r--`). Attackers could simply download the file and run offline cracking against the entire user base. The shadow scheme moves the password hash to `/etc/shadow`, readable **only by root** (mode `rw-------`). This forces attackers to first gain root access to steal the hashes, drastically raising the attack bar.

**Example line:**

```
alice:$6$Z3qG8m…$P9k…:19000:0:90:7:14:99999:
```

Reading: User `alice`, SHA-512 hash, password last changed 19000 days after epoch, no minimum, must change every 90 days, warned 7 days before, disabled 14 days after expiry, never expires absolutely.

---

#### (b) Special Permission Bits (7 marks)

**1. `setuid` — Set User ID (2.5 marks):**

When set on an executable file, the `setuid` bit (octal `4000`, displayed as `s` in the owner's execute position) makes the process run with the **file owner's UID**, not the caller's UID.

$$
\text{Effective UID of process} = \text{Owner UID of file} \quad \text{when setuid is set}
$$

**Real-world example:** `/usr/bin/passwd` is owned by root with mode `rwsr-xr-x` (octal `4755`). When ordinary user `alice` runs `passwd` to change her own password, the resulting process runs as **root** so it can write to `/etc/shadow`. Without setuid, `passwd` would have no permission to update the shadow file.

**2. `setgid` — Set Group ID (2.5 marks):**

When set on a **directory**, the `setgid` bit (octal `2000`, displayed as `s` in the group's execute position) forces all **new files and subdirectories** created inside to inherit the **parent directory's group**, not the creator's primary group.

$$
\text{Group of new file} = \text{Group of parent directory (with setgid)} \quad \text{overrides creator's GID}
$$

**Real-world example:** A project directory `/project/shared` with group `devteam` and mode `rwxr-s---` (octal `2750`). Any user in group `devteam` who creates a file inside automatically places it in group `devteam`, so all team members can read and modify it. This is essential for collaborative workflows.

**3. Sticky bit (2 marks):**

When set on a **directory** (octal `1000`, displayed as `t` in the other's execute position), the sticky bit allows **only the file owner, the directory owner, or root** to delete or rename files within, even if other users have write permission.

$$
\text{Delete right} = (\text{user is file owner}) \lor (\text{user is directory owner}) \lor (\text{user is root})
$$

**Real-world example:** `/tmp` has mode `rwxrwxrwt` (octal `1777`). Every user can create files in `/tmp` because the directory is world-writable, but a user can only **delete their own** files — users cannot delete each other's temporary files, even though the `w` bit is set for "others."

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Linux Permission Model** (DAC). With a neat diagram or table, describe the meaning of the 9 permission bits (read, write, execute for owner, group, others). Compute the octal representation of `rwxr-xr--` and the symbolic representation of `640`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **SELinux architecture** in detail. Describe its three components (subject, object, policy) and the three enforcement modes (enforcing, permissive, disabled). How does SELinux implement **Mandatory Access Control (MAC)** differently from DAC? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) Linux DAC Permission Model (7 marks)

**Conceptual definition (2 marks):** Discretionary Access Control (DAC) is the traditional UNIX permission model where every file has an **owner**, an associated **group**, and the rest of the world (**others**). Each category has three independent permission bits: **read (r)**, **write (w)**, and **execute (x)**, totaling **9 bits** per file.

**The 9-bit matrix (3 marks):**

| Category | Read (r=4) | Write (w=2) | Execute (x=1) |
|----------|------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Owner (u)** | Can view file contents | Can modify file contents | Can execute file as program |
| **Group (g)** | Group members can view | Group members can modify | Group members can execute |
| **Others (o)** | World can view | World can modify | World can execute |

For **directories**, the bits have slightly different meanings:
- `r` = can list directory contents (`ls`)
- `w` = can create, delete, or rename files in the directory
- `x` = can enter the directory (`cd`) and access its files

**Octal conversion of `rwxr-xr--` (1 mark):**

- Owner: `rwx` = 4 + 2 + 1 = **7**
- Group: `r-x` = 4 + 0 + 1 = **5**
- Others: `r--` = 4 + 0 + 0 = **4**

Result: **`chmod 754 filename`**

**Symbolic conversion of `640` (1 mark):**

- Owner: `6` = 4 + 2 = **`rw-`**
- Group: `4` = 4 + 0 = **`r--`**
- Others: `0` = 0 = **`---`**

Result: **`rw-r-----`**

---

#### (b) SELinux Architecture and MAC (7 marks)

**What is SELinux (1 mark):** Security-Enhanced Linux (SELinux) is a kernel-level security module originally developed by the NSA. It implements **Mandatory Access Control (MAC)** using a **label-based policy** that is enforced independently of the file owner.

**Three components (3 marks):**

| Component | Role | Example |
|-----------|------|---------|
| **Subject** | The active entity requesting access (process, user) | `sshd_t` (SSH daemon) |
| **Object** | The passive resource being accessed (file, port, socket) | `/etc/shadow` labeled `shadow_t` |
| **Policy** | The set of rules defining allowed subject-object interactions | `allow sshd_t shadow_t : file read;` |

Each process and every file/directory/resource is assigned a **security context** (label) of the form:

$$
\text{Context} = \text{user} : \text{role} : \text{type} : \text{sensitivity}
$$

Example: `system_u:object_r:shadow_t:s0`

**Three enforcement modes (2 marks):**

| Mode | Behavior | Use Case |
|------|----------|----------|
| `enforcing` | Policy violations are **blocked** AND logged | Production servers |
| `permissive` | Policy violations are **logged but allowed** | Troubleshooting, policy tuning |
| `disabled` | SELinux is **turned off** entirely | Never recommended |

**MAC vs DAC (1 mark):** In DAC, the file **owner** decides who can access the file (`chmod 777`). In MAC, the **system policy** decides — even root cannot bypass it. SELinux therefore protects against privilege escalation: if a root process is compromised, the SELinux policy still constrains what it can touch.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **setuid** with **SELinux user identity** — they are unrelated. Setuid changes the *effective UID*; SELinux labels change the *security context*.
> - When asked for the octal of a permission string, students forget the **4-2-1** bit weights and write `rwx = 6` (wrong — `rwx` is `7`).
> - For `/etc/shadow`, students sometimes answer "stored in `/etc/passwd`." Always state `/etc/shadow` and mention `x` placeholder in `/etc/passwd`.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Authentication and Access Control (Linux)**
>
> - **Authentication** = identity verification. **Authorization** = permission enforcement. Both are required.
> - `/etc/passwd` is **world-readable** and contains a placeholder `x` in field 2. The real hash is in **`/etc/shadow`**, readable **only by root**.
> - Shadow hash format: **`$id$salt$hash`**, where `$6$` = SHA-512 (current default), `$5$` = SHA-256, `$1$` = MD5 (legacy).
> - Salt is a **16-character random string** that defeats rainbow tables and forces per-user brute force.
> - **PAM** decouples application from mechanism. Configured in `/etc/pam.d/<service>`. Four groups: `auth`, `account`, `session`, `password`.
> - **DAC** = traditional 9-bit permission model. **MAC** = system-enforced, owner cannot override. **RBAC** = role-based (approximated by `sudo` + groups).
> - Permission bit weights: **r=4, w=2, x=1**. Octal prefix for special bits: **setuid=4, setgid=2, sticky=1**.
> - `setuid` file: process runs as **owner UID** (e.g., `/usr/bin/passwd`). `setgid` directory: new files inherit **parent group**. Sticky directory (e.g., `/tmp`): only **owner can delete**.
> - File default base = **666** (no execute). Directory default base = **777** (with execute). Actual = `base & ~umask`.
> - **`umask`** in octal is **subtracted from base** *bitwise*, not arithmetically. `022` umask → file `644`, directory `755`.
> - **SELinux** enforces MAC using **security contexts** (labels) of the form `user:role:type:sensitivity`. Three modes: **enforcing, permissive, disabled**.
> - **AppArmor** is the alternative MAC implementation — path-based, simpler, used in SUSE/Ubuntu.
> - **SSH key-based authentication** uses `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` and is far stronger than password authentication.
> - **Kerberos + PAM** provides ticket-based authentication for enterprise single sign-on (SSO).
> - **`sudo`** is configured in `/etc/sudoers` (always edit with `visudo`) and grants **fine-grained, auditable** privilege escalation.
> - **ACLs** (`setfacl`/`getfacl`) extend the standard 9-bit model with named-user and named-group entries beyond the owner/group/other triple.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Upgrades and Patches (Linux)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-3
> - **Topic:** Upgrades and Patches (Linux)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Upgrades and Patches in Linux** refer to the systematic processes of applying software updates, security fixes, bug corrections, and feature enhancements to a Linux operating system and its installed software packages. **Patches** are small, targeted updates that fix specific vulnerabilities (CVEs) or bugs, while **Upgrades** are larger version transitions that introduce new features, kernel improvements, or major software version changes.

In the context of **Network and System Security**, patch and upgrade management is one of the most critical defensive controls. A **patch** is a piece of code inserted into an existing program to fix a vulnerability, performance issue, or bug, distributed as a **package** through a distribution's repository. An **upgrade** transitions a package to a newer version, which may include security patches, dependency updates, and new functionalities.

### Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus-Aligned)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)** | Unique identifier assigned to publicly known security vulnerabilities |
| **Package Manager** | Software tool that automates the installation, upgrade, configuration, and removal of software packages (e.g., `apt`, `yum`, `dnf`, `pacman`) |
| **Repository (Repo)** | Centralized, curated storage location from which software packages are retrieved and installed |
| **Kernel** | The core component of the Linux OS, managing system resources and hardware-software interactions |
| **LTS (Long Term Support)** | Distribution versions supported with security updates for an extended period (typically **3–5 years**) |
| **Rolling Release** | Distribution model where updates are continuous rather than version-based (e.g., Arch Linux) |
| **Live Patching** | Technique of applying kernel patches without rebooting the system (e.g., Ksplice, kpatch) |
| **Yum/DNF** | Package managers used by RHEL, CentOS, Fedora |
| **APT** | Package manager used by Debian, Ubuntu |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of your Linux system as a **large apartment building**.
> - **Patches** are like **plumber visits** — small, targeted fixes for specific leaks (vulnerabilities) in specific apartments (software components).
> - **Upgrades** are like **renovations** — bigger projects that modernize the entire building (kernel, libraries) and may temporarily displace tenants (downtime).
> - **The Package Manager** is the **building superintendent** — it knows which apartments need fixing, schedules the work, and ensures nothing breaks.
> - **A Linux Distribution Repository** is the **official supply warehouse** — verified, authentic, and trustworthy parts only.
> - **Skipping patches** is like **ignoring a gas leak** — small at first, but catastrophic when exploited.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the **NIST Special Publication 800-40** and the **CIS Critical Security Controls (v8)**, patch management falls under **Control 7: Continuous Vulnerability Management**. Most successful cyberattacks (e.g., **WannaCry, Equifax breach**) exploited **known, patchable vulnerabilities** that were not updated in time. Hence, KTU explicitly tests students on patch/upgrade mechanisms as a core defensive strategy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Linux Updates

Linux updates can be broadly classified into the following categories:

### A. Based on Scope

1. **Security Patches** — Fix specific CVEs; small, high-priority.
2. **Bug Fix Patches** — Resolve functional defects; medium priority.
3. **Feature Upgrades** — Add new capabilities; low to medium priority.
4. **Major Version Upgrades** — Transition from one major version to another (e.g., Ubuntu 22.04 → 24.04).
5. **Kernel Upgrades** — Replace or update the core OS kernel (often requires reboot).

### B. Based on Distribution Family

| Distribution Family | Package Manager | Update Command | Config File |
|---------------------|-----------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Debian/Ubuntu** | APT (dpkg backend) | `apt update && apt upgrade` | `/etc/apt/sources.list` |
| **RHEL/CentOS/Fedora** | YUM / DNF (rpm backend) | `yum update` / `dnf upgrade` | `/etc/yum.repos.d/` |
| **Arch Linux** | Pacman | `pacman -Syu` | `/etc/pacman.conf` |
| **SUSE/openSUSE** | Zypper | `zypper update` | `/etc/zypp/repos.d/` |
| **Alpine** | APK | `apk update && apk upgrade` | `/etc/apk/repositories` |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Patching Workflow

The standard operational flow for applying updates in a Linux environment is:

1. **Inventory & Discovery** — Identify all installed packages and their versions.
2. **Vulnerability Assessment** — Cross-reference with CVE databases (NVD, Ubuntu Security Notices, RHSA).
3. **Testing Phase** — Apply updates in a **staging environment** first.
4. **Backup** — Snapshot system state, configuration, and critical data.
5. **Patch Application** — Run the appropriate package manager command.
6. **Verification** — Confirm successful installation, check logs.
7. **Reboot (if required)** — Especially after kernel updates.
8. **Post-Patch Validation** — Run health checks, monitor service availability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order matters:** Applying patches blindly in production is the **#1 cause of downtime**. The staging-first approach is mandated by **ITIL Change Management** and **ISO 27001 Control A.12.1.2**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Command | Purpose | Units / Output |
|---------|-------------------|---------|----------------|
| **Update Package List** | `apt update` / `dnf check-update` | Refreshes repository metadata | Boolean (updates available?) |
| **Apply Security Patches Only** | `apt upgrade -s` (dry-run), `yum --security update` | Installs only security-flagged updates | List of patched packages |
| **Full Upgrade (with deps)** | `apt full-upgrade` / `dnf upgrade` | Upgrades packages + resolves dependencies | Installed version numbers |
| **Distribution Upgrade** | `do-release-upgrade` (Ubuntu) | Moves to a new OS release | New OS version string |
| **Kernel Live Patching** | `kpatch load <module>` | Applies kernel fix without reboot | Patch number, uptime unchanged |
| **Rollback (YUM)** | `yum history undo <id>` | Reverts to previous transaction state | Transaction ID integer |
| **Rollback (Snapshots)** | `snapper undochange` (Btrfs/SUSE) | Filesystem-level rollback | Snapshot number |
| **Hold a package** | `apt-mark hold <pkg>` / `yum versionlock` | Prevents unwanted auto-updates | Package name |
| **Auto-update check interval** | `APT::Periodic::Unattended-Upgrade` (Ubuntu) | Defines update check frequency | Days (e.g., 1) |
| **CVE Severity Score** | **CVSS v3.1** | Quantifies vulnerability severity | Range: 0.0 – 10.0 |

> [!WARNING]
> **CVSS Score Severity Bands (as per KTU standard reference):**
> - **0.0 – 3.9** → **Low**
> - **4.0 – 6.9** → **Medium**
> - **7.0 – 8.9** → **High**
> - **9.0 – 10.0** → **Critical**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Server Hardening:** Critical for banking, healthcare, and government servers where **unpatched systems = data breach liability**.
- **DevSecOps Pipelines:** Patches are integrated into **CI/CD workflows** using tools like **Ansible, Puppet, Chef**.
- **IoT & Embedded Systems:** Industrial Linux (e.g., **Yocto, Buildroot**) requires carefully managed upgrades due to storage constraints.
- **Cloud-Native:** Containers (Docker) require patched base images; **Distroless** images reduce attack surface.
- **Critical Infrastructure:** SCADA systems running Linux must be patched under frameworks like **IEC 62443**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Patching Process — Ubuntu/Debian (APT) Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The following analytical walkthrough models the **decision tree and execution flow** of the APT package manager as per KTU's expectation for showing procedural understanding.

### Step 1: Refresh Repository Metadata

$$ \text{Command: } \texttt{sudo apt update} $$

This command contacts the repositories listed in `/etc/apt/sources.list` and downloads the current **package index** (filenames, versions, dependencies). The metadata is stored in `/var/lib/apt/lists/`.

**Logic Explanation:** Before any decision to upgrade, the system must have an **up-to-date awareness** of what is available.

### Step 2: Identify Upgradable Packages

$$ \text{Command: } \texttt{apt list --upgradable} $$

This command compares the **installed package database** (`/var/lib/dpkg/status`) with the **fetched index** and outputs packages where:

$$
\text{Installed Version} < \text{Candidate Version}
$$

The candidate version is determined by:

$$
V_{\text{candidate}} = \max\left( V \;\middle|\; V \in \text{Available} \cap V \text{ satisfies } D(P) \right)
$$

where $D(P)$ represents the **dependency closure** of package $P$.

### Step 3: Apply Security Patches Only

$$ \text{Command: } \texttt{sudo unattended-upgrade -d} $$

or

$$ \text{Command: } \texttt{sudo apt upgrade -s \;\vert\; grep -i security} $$

The **unattended-upgrades** daemon reads from `/etc/apt/apt.conf.d/50unattended-upgrades` and only installs packages whose origin is in the **security pocket**:

$$
\text{Security Pocket} = \left\{ P \;\middle|\; \text{origin}(P) \in \{\text{Ubuntu}, \text{Debian-Security}\} \right\}
$$

### Step 4: Full Upgrade with Dependency Resolution

$$ \text{Command: } \texttt{sudo apt full-upgrade} $$

APT computes a **dependency graph** $G = (V, E)$ where:

- $V$ = set of all packages
- $E$ = set of dependency edges

The upgrade algorithm uses **topological sorting** to determine install order:

$$
\text{Order} = \text{TopoSort}(G) \;\text{ such that } \forall (u, v) \in E,\; u \prec v
$$

### Step 5: Verification of Patched State

$$ \text{Command: } \texttt{apt list --installed} \;\vert\; \text{grep <package>} $$

Output format:

$$
\text{Package} \quad \text{Version} \quad \text{Architecture} \quad \text{Status}
$$

Status values include `installed`, `upgradable`, `half-installed`, `unpacked`.

### Step 6: Kernel Update and Reboot Decision

The kernel is a special case because it lives in `/boot` and is loaded by the bootloader (**GRUB**). When `linux-image-generic` is updated, the old kernel is **not removed** until a configurable threshold is reached:

$$
\text{Kernels Kept} = N_{\text{max}} \quad \text{(default: 3 in Ubuntu)}
$$

A reboot is required when:

$$
\text{Reboot Required} = \text{true} \iff \text{Active Kernel Version} \neq \text{Latest Installed Kernel}
$$

This is detected by the file `/var/run/reboot-required`.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Patching Decision Logic

| Scenario | Recommended Action | Reasoning |
|----------|---------------------|-----------|
| **Critical CVE (CVSS ≥ 9.0)** | Apply patch within **24–48 hours** | Active exploitation in the wild |
| **High CVE (CVSS 7.0–8.9)** | Apply within **7–14 days** | Significant risk if exposed |
| **Medium CVE (CVSS 4.0–6.9)** | Apply within **30 days** | Scheduled maintenance window |
| **Production Server with 99.99% SLA** | Test in staging, then rolling update | Minimize downtime |
| **Kernel patch** | Schedule maintenance window | Requires reboot |
| **Live-patch available (Ksplice/kpatch)** | Apply immediately | No downtime needed |
| **Patch breaks dependency** | Hold package, document, escalate | Stability over speed |
| **No available patch** | Apply compensating control (firewall rule, disable service) | Defense in depth |

## 3.3 Mathematical Model: Patch Deployment Risk

Let:
- $N$ = total number of systems to patch
- $P_f$ = probability a patch fails on a single system
- $D$ = downtime cost per failed patch (in currency/hour)
- $T_f$ = average recovery time (in hours)

The expected cost of a patch deployment is:

$$
C_{\text{expected}} = N \times P_f \times D \times T_f
$$

To minimize risk, the optimal **staged rollout** percentage $r$ (where $0 < r \leq 1$) is:

$$
r^* = \arg\min_{r} \left[ (1-r) \times N \times P_f \times D \times T_f + r \times N \times P_f \times D \times T_f \right]
$$

In practice, KTU-grade systems use **canary deployments**: first patch $r = 0.05$ (5%), monitor, then scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between a patch and an upgrade in Linux. Provide one example command for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Patch | Upgrade |
|--------|-------|---------|
| **Definition** | Small, targeted code fix for a specific bug or CVE | Major version transition with new features |
| **Scope** | Usually affects one or few files | Affects entire package + dependencies |
| **Frequency** | Frequent (weekly/monthly) | Less frequent (every few months/years) |
| **Risk Level** | Low to medium | Medium to high |
| **Reboot** | Often not required | Frequently required (especially kernel) |
| **Example Command** | `apt install --only-upgrade openssl` | `apt full-upgrade` or `do-release-upgrade` |

**Example:** Patching OpenSSL to fix **Heartbleed (CVE-2014-0160)** is a patch; upgrading Ubuntu 22.04 → 24.04 is an upgrade.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: What is the role of a Linux package manager? List any two package managers and their corresponding distributions.**

**Model Answer:**

A **package manager** is a collection of software tools that automate the **installation, upgrade, configuration, tracking, and removal** of software packages on a Linux system. It handles dependency resolution, verifies package integrity using **GPG signatures**, and maintains a consistent software database.

**Two Package Managers:**
1. **APT (Advanced Package Tool)** — Used by **Debian, Ubuntu** and derivatives. Configured via `/etc/apt/sources.list`. Command: `apt install <package>`.
2. **YUM (Yellowdog Updater, Modified) / DNF (Dandified YUM)** — Used by **RHEL, CentOS, Fedora**. Configured via `/etc/yum.repos.d/*.repo`. Command: `yum install <package>`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the Linux patching process. Discuss the difference between security patches and feature upgrades. How does the APT package manager handle dependency resolution during a full upgrade?**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Patching Process & Diagram

**Model Answer:**

The Linux patching process follows these steps:

1. **Repository Update:** The package manager contacts the configured repositories to fetch the latest metadata.

   $$ \texttt{sudo apt update} $$

2. **Package Comparison:** Installed package versions are compared with available versions in the repository.

3. **Selection of Patches:** Security patches are prioritized based on **CVE severity** and **CVSS scores**.

4. **Staging Test:** Patches are applied to a non-production environment for compatibility testing.

5. **Backup & Snapshot:** System state is captured using tools like **Timeshift, Snapper (Btrfs), or LVM snapshots**.

6. **Patch Application:** The selected updates are installed:

   $$ \texttt{sudo apt full-upgrade} $$

7. **Reboot & Verification:** If a kernel patch was applied, the system is rebooted and services are verified.

8. **Logging:** All patch activities are logged to `/var/log/apt/history.log` or `/var/log/dnf.log` and forwarded to a **SIEM system**.

**Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the visual flow.**

**Difference Table:**

| Parameter | Security Patch | Feature Upgrade |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Purpose** | Fix CVE / vulnerability | Add new functionality |
| **Size** | Small (KB to few MB) | Large (MB to GB) |
| **Downtime** | Minimal | May require maintenance window |
| **Urgency** | High (often within days) | Low (planned) |
| **Example** | `apt upgrade openssl` | `apt install python3.12` |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: APT Dependency Resolution

**Model Answer:**

APT uses a **graph-based dependency resolution algorithm** internally powered by **libapt-pkg**. The process is:

1. **Build Dependency Graph:** Each package has a control file declaring:

   $$ \text{Depends: } \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\} $$
   $$ \text{Recommends: } \{R_1, R_2, \ldots\} $$
   $$ \text{Conflicts: } \{C_1, C_2, \ldots\} $$

2. **Sat Solver Algorithm:** Modern APT uses a **Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) based solver** (since APT 1.0) to find a consistent installation set.

3. **Topological Ordering:** Packages are installed in an order that satisfies all dependencies:

   $$ \text{Install Order} = \text{TopoSort}(G(V, E)) \;\text{ such that } \forall(u,v) \in E,\; u \text{ before } v $$

4. **Conflict Resolution:** If a conflict exists, APT may:
   - Remove the conflicting package
   - Hold the package (`apt-mark hold`)
   - Prompt the user (interactive mode)

5. **Example:**

   ```text
   The following NEW packages will be installed:
     libssl3 openssl
   The following packages will be upgraded:
     curl
   3 upgraded, 2 newly installed.
   ```

This deterministic, dependency-aware approach ensures **system stability** during upgrades.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the importance of patch management in Linux system security. Discuss the various sources of Linux updates, and describe the role of unattended-upgrades in Ubuntu with its configuration.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Importance & Sources of Updates

**Model Answer:**

**Importance of Patch Management:**

1. **Vulnerability Mitigation:** Unpatched systems are the #1 attack vector. According to **Verizon DBIR 2023**, ~**74% of breaches** involve human element + unpatched vulnerabilities.

2. **Compliance:** Standards like **PCI-DSS (Req. 6.3)**, **HIPAA (§164.308)**, **ISO 27001 (A.12.6.1)**, and **NIST SP 800-53 (SI-2)** mandate timely patch application.

3. **Service Availability:** Patches fix DoS vulnerabilities and stability bugs that could crash production services.

4. **Reputation & Trust:** Data breaches from unpatched systems (e.g., **Equifax 2017**, **WannaCry 2017**) destroy organizational trust.

5. **Defense in Depth:** Patching is one layer; combined with firewalls, IDS/IPS, and least privilege, it forms a robust security posture.

**Sources of Linux Updates:**

| Source | Type | Trust Level |
|--------|------|-------------|
| **Official Distribution Repositories** | Verified, signed packages | **High** |
| **Security-specific pockets** (e.g., `Ubuntu-security`, `RHSA`) | CVE-specific patches | **High** |
| **Third-party repos** (e.g., PPA, EPEL) | Community/contributor packages | **Medium** |
| **Vendor repositories** (e.g., Oracle, IBM) | Enterprise Linux | **High** |
| **Source code compilation** (`./configure && make && make install`) | Manual builds | **Variable** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often mix up "PPAs" with official repositories. **PPAs (Personal Package Archives)** are **third-party** and not vetted by Ubuntu — they can introduce supply-chain risks.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Unattended-Upgrades in Ubuntu

**Model Answer:**

`unattended-upgrades` is a **daemon** that automatically installs security patches without user intervention. It is configured via:

- `/etc/apt/apt.conf.d/20auto-upgrades` — Enables the feature.
- `/etc/apt/apt.conf.d/50unattended-upgrades` — Defines which packages to upgrade.

**Configuration Example (50unattended-upgrades):**

$$
\text{Unattended-Upgrade} :: \text{Allowed-Origins} = \{
\begin{aligned}
&\text{"\$\{distro\_id\}:\$\{distro\_codename\}-security"}, \\
&\text{"\$\{distro\_id\}ESMApps:\$\{distro\_codename\}-apps-security"}, \\
&\text{"\$\{distro\_id\}ESM:\$\{distro\_codename\}-infra-security"}
\end{aligned}
\};
$$

**Key Directives:**

| Directive | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `Unattended-Upgrade::Allowed-Origins` | Specifies which repositories to auto-update |
| `Unattended-Upgrade::DevRelease "false"` | Prevents auto-upgrading to development releases |
| `Unattended-Upgrade::AutoFixInterruptedDpkg "true"` | Resumes interrupted dpkg operations |
| `Unattended-Upgrade::Remove-Unused-Kernel-Packages "true"` | Cleans old kernels |
| `Unattended-Upgrade::Automatic-Reboot "false"` | Controls automatic rebooting |
| `Unattended-Upgrade::Mail "admin@example.com"` | Sends notification emails |

**Operational Flow:**

1. A **systemd timer** (`apt-daily.timer`) triggers `/usr/bin/unattended-upgrade` every **24 hours**.
2. The script fetches updates, applies them, and logs to `/var/log/unattended-upgrades/`.
3. If `Automatic-Reboot` is `true`, the system reboots at the specified time.

**Security Significance:** Enables **proactive patch management** at scale, critical for cloud servers and IoT devices that lack manual admin oversight.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing `apt update` with `apt upgrade`:** `update` only refreshes metadata; `upgrade` actually installs updates. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Missing reboot requirement for kernel patches:** Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Not mentioning CVSS scoring** in severity-based patching questions.
> 4. **Writing `yum` commands for Ubuntu** (or vice versa) — wrong package manager = wrong family.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention staging environment** in enterprise patching answers — direct production patching is marked down.
> 6. **Ignoring rollback strategy** — a patch management answer without rollback discussion is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [ ] **Patch = Small fix**; **Upgrade = New version with features**
- [ ] **Debian/Ubuntu** uses **APT** (`apt`, `dpkg`); **RHEL/Fedora** uses **YUM/DNF** (`rpm`)
- [ ] **`apt update`** only refreshes the package list; **`apt upgrade`** installs updates
- [ ] **`apt full-upgrade`** can remove packages to resolve dependencies; `apt upgrade` cannot
- [ ] **Kernel updates** require **reboot** (unless using **kpatch/Ksplice live patching**)
- [ ] **CVSS Score Bands:** 0–3.9 Low, 4.0–6.9 Medium, 7.0–8.9 High, 9.0–10.0 Critical
- [ ] **Patch SLA:** Critical → 24–48 hrs, High → 7 days, Medium → 30 days
- [ ] **`/etc/apt/sources.list`** — Ubuntu repository configuration
- [ ] **`unattended-upgrades`** — Ubuntu's automatic security patching daemon
- [ ] **Hold a package:** `apt-mark hold <pkg>` (prevents auto-update)
- [ ] **Yum rollback:** `yum history undo <transaction-id>`
- [ ] **Snapper** (Btrfs) — filesystem-level snapshot/rollback (used in openSUSE)
- [ ] **LTS releases** get **5 years** of security support
- [ ] **Rolling releases** (Arch) get continuous updates; **Fixed releases** (Ubuntu) follow a schedule
- [ ] **Staging-first patch deployment** is best practice in production
- [ ] **Major cyberattacks** like WannaCry and NotPetya exploited **unpatched SMB vulnerabilities**
- [ ] **Compliance frameworks** mandating patch management: **PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001, NIST 800-53 SI-2**
- [ ] **Defense in depth:** Patching is ONE layer; combine with firewalls, IDS, least privilege
- [ ] **Supply-chain risk:** Third-party PPAs/EPEL repos are less trusted than official ones

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 🔐 Module 3 — Operating Linux Safely

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Linux Safely** refers to the disciplined set of administrative practices, kernel-level controls, and user-space security mechanisms employed to harden a Linux operating system against unauthorized access, privilege escalation, malware, and data leakage. It encompasses **identity management**, **Discretionary Access Control (DAC)** via POSIX file permissions and Access Control Lists (ACLs), **Mandatory Access Control (MAC)** via SELinux/AppArmor, **network-level filtering** through netfilter/iptables, **patch management**, **secure remote administration** (SSH hardening), and **auditing/logging** through syslog and auditd.

In KTU 2024 parlance, "Operating Linux Safely" is a **practitioner-oriented sub-domain of system security** that bridges the OS layer with the network layer — students are expected to know *what knobs exist*, *what they defend against*, and *how to read the security state of a running system*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model

Imagine your Linux machine is a **gated apartment building**:

| Linux Concept | Apartment Analogy |
|---|---|
| Root user (`uid=0`) | The building owner who has a master key to every flat |
| Regular user account | A tenant who only holds the key to their own flat |
| File | A locked cupboard inside a flat |
| File permissions (rwx) | Three locks on the cupboard — Read (look), Write (modify), Execute (use) |
| Group | All tenants of the same floor who share a common key |
| SUID bit | A special "public" lock that lets *anyone* use the cupboard, but the cupboard behaves as if the *owner* is using it |
| Sticky bit on a directory | A notice that says "Once you drop a file here, only YOU can remove it, not even the floor caretaker" |
| Firewall (iptables) | The security guard at the building gate who decides which visitors enter |
| SELinux | An additional building code enforced by the society — even if you have a key, you can only enter rooms the society permits |
| SSH | A secure intercom line that lets a tenant remotely unlock their flat |
| `sudo` | The owner temporarily lending the master key to a tenant, but logging every use |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** Linux security is fundamentally **layered (defense-in-depth)**. A single mechanism is never enough — file permissions protect files, the firewall protects the network, SELinux protects the process, and audit logs detect what slipped through.

### 1.3 The Five Pillars of Linux Operational Security

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures this topic around five pillars:

1. **Pillar A — Identity & Authentication:** Users, groups, passwords, PAM, SSH keys, sudoers.
2. **Pillar B — Access Control:** POSIX rwx bits, SUID/SGID/Sticky bit, ACLs, umask.
3. **Pillar C — Process & Service Hardening:** Minimal services, secure umask, capabilities, namespaces.
4. **Pillar D — Network Defense:** iptables/nftables chains, TCP wrappers, host-based firewalls.
5. **Pillar E — Monitoring & Auditing:** syslog, journald, auditd, integrity tools (AIDE, Tripwire).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question says *"Discuss the security features of Linux"*, it almost always wants you to cover at least **Pillars A, B, and D** with concrete command syntax. Memorize the syntax — it is high-yield.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Linux User & Group Identity Model

Linux is a **multi-user** operating system. Every process and every file is owned by exactly one **User (UID)** and one **Group (GID)**. The kernel uses a **triple** `(UID, GID, mode-bits)` for every authorization decision.

### 2.1.1 The UID Spectrum

| UID Range | Account Type | Risk Profile |
|---|---|---|
| `0` | **Root (Superuser)** | Full unrestricted access — handle with care |
| `1 – 999` | **System / Service accounts** (`bin`, `daemon`, `www-data`, `mysql`) | Compromise = service-level compromise |
| `1000 – 60000` | **Regular human users** | Limited privileges, governed by `/etc/sudoers` |
| `65534` | **`nobody`** | The "least-privileged" pseudo-user used by NFS, nginx, etc. |

The relevant files are:
- `/etc/passwd` — maps username → UID, GID, home directory, shell.
- `/etc/shadow` — stores hashed passwords (only readable by root). The presence of the `x` in `/etc/passwd`'s password field is itself a security signal — the real hash is in `/etc/shadow`.
- `/etc/group` — maps group name → GID and member list.
- `/etc/gshadow` — secure group password file.
- `/etc/sudoers` — defines who may use `sudo` and for which commands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two password files exist (a favourite KTU question):** Splitting `/etc/passwd` (world-readable) from `/etc/shadow` (root-readable) means the password hash is no longer exposed to every local user, defeating offline dictionary attacks. This is an example of the **principle of least privilege** applied to file ownership.

### 2.1.2 The PAM Subsystem

**Pluggable Authentication Modules (PAM)** is the framework that mediates every authentication request — login, SSH, sudo, screen-lock, even `passwd`. Each service in `/etc/pam.d/` stacks modules like:
- `pam_unix.so` — checks `/etc/shadow`.
- `pam_pwquality.so` — enforces password complexity.
- `pam_tally2.so` / `pam_faillock.so` — locks accounts after N failed attempts.
- `pam_limits.so` — applies `/etc/security/limits.conf` (resource caps).

## 2.2 The POSIX Permission Model (rwx Triad)

Every file has **three permission triads** — one for the *owner* (`u`), one for the *group* (`g`), and one for *others* (`o`). Each triad has three bits: **r (read=4), w (write=2), x (execute=1)**.

### 2.2.1 Effect of Bits on Files vs. Directories

The meaning of `rwx` **changes** depending on whether the object is a *file* or a *directory* — this is a classic KTU trap question.

| Bit | On a Regular File | On a Directory |
|---|---|---|
| `r` (4) | Contents can be read (`cat`, `less`) | Directory listing can be obtained (`ls`) |
| `w` (2) | Contents can be modified | Files can be **created / deleted / renamed** *within* the dir (does NOT need write on the file itself) |
| `x` (1) | File can be executed as a program / script | Directory can be **entered / traversed** (`cd`) — the "search" bit |

> [!WARNING]
> **The Directory Delete Paradox:** A user with `w` on a directory can `rm` a file inside it *even if that file is owned by root and mode 600*. The deletion right comes from the **parent directory**, not the file. This is exactly why the **sticky bit** is set on `/tmp`.

### 2.2.2 The Three Special Bits (SUID / SGID / Sticky)

These are the **fourth triad** of the mode, occupying the high-order octal digit.

| Bit | Octal | Symbol | Effect on File | Effect on Directory |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SUID** | `4000` | `u+s` | Process runs as the **file's owner** (e.g., `/usr/bin/passwd` runs as root to write `/etc/shadow`) | Negligent on most files; can be a security risk |
| **SGID** | `2000` | `g+s` | Process runs as the **file's group** | **Inheritance:** new files created inside inherit the directory's GID |
| **Sticky** | `1000` | `o+t` | Negligible on files | **Restricted delete:** only the file's owner (or root) can `rm` files inside — exactly what `/tmp` has |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU favourite:** "Why does `/usr/bin/passwd` have the SUID bit set?" Because an unprivileged user must be able to *change their own password*, but the password database `/etc/shadow` is writable **only by root**. SUID elevates the process to root for the duration of `passwd`, lets it update the shadow file, then drops back. Without SUID, no user could ever change a password.

## 2.3 The umask Mechanism

`umask` is a per-process mask that **subtracts** permissions from the **default** the kernel would otherwise assign.

$$
\text{Final Default Permission} = \text{Kernel Default} \;\text{AND}\; (\text{NOT umask})
$$

| Object | Kernel Default | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Regular file | `666` (rw-rw-rw-) | Files are not assumed executable |
| Directory | `777` (rwxrwxrwx) | Directories need to be traversable |

> **Worked micro-example:** If `umask = 022`, then for a new *file* the final mode is `666 AND NOT 022` = `666 AND 755` = `644` (`rw-r--r--`). The owner can read/write, the rest can only read. This is the *default* on most production Linux systems.

## 2.4 iptables — The netfilter Firewall

`iptables` is the user-space tool to manipulate the **netfilter** framework inside the Linux kernel. It operates on **5 chains** organized into **3 tables**:

| Table | Chains | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `filter` (default) | `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, `FORWARD` | Packet *acceptance* — the actual firewall |
| `nat` | `PREROUTING`, `POSTROUTING`, `OUTPUT` | Network Address Translation |
| `mangle` | All five | Header modification (TOS, TTL, MARK) |

Each chain has a default **policy** (`ACCEPT` or `DROP`) and a list of **rules** evaluated in order. The first matching rule wins; if none match, the policy is applied.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-grade reference material** for the 14-mark derivation questions. Memorize the octal weights and the umask equation.

| # | Concept | Formula / Mapping | Unit / Notation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Permission bit weight | $r=4,\; w=2,\; x=1$ | dimensionless | Each triad sums to $0\!-\!7$ |
| 2 | File mode encoding | $\text{mode} = O \cdot 8^2 + G \cdot 8^1 + U \cdot 8^0 + S \cdot 8^3$ | base-8 (octal) | $O,G,U \in [0,7]$, $S \in [0,7]$ special |
| 3 | Special bits | $\text{SUID}=4000,\; \text{SGID}=2000,\; \text{Sticky}=1000$ | octal | Added as the *highest* digit |
| 4 | Default file mode | $D_{\text{file}} = 666$ | octal | No `x` by default |
| 5 | Default directory mode | $D_{\text{dir}} = 777$ | octal | All bits set |
| 6 | umask formula | $M_{\text{final}} = D \;\&\; (\sim U)$ | bitwise | `&` is AND, `~` is bitwise NOT |
| 7 | iptables chain order | PREROUTING $\rightarrow$ FORWARD $\rightarrow$ INPUT $\rightarrow$ OUTPUT $\rightarrow$ POSTROUTING | — | Topological traversal |
| 8 | SSH port (default) | $\text{port} = 22$ | TCP | Should be changed in hardened systems |
| 9 | `/etc/shadow` hash | $\text{id} \; \vert \; \text{algo} \; \vert \; \text{salt} \; \vert \; \text{hash}$ | string | `$6$` = SHA-512, `$1$` = MD5 |
| 10 | Fail-count limit | $N_{\text{max}} \le 5$ (CIS recommendation) | attempts | Set in `pam_faillock` |
| 11 | Password min length | $L_{\min} \ge 12$ (NIST SP 800-63B) | characters | Set in `pam_pwquality` |
| 12 | Min entropy | $H \ge 30$ bits | bits | Computed from character set & length |

**Notation Convention Used:** In every table above, the symbol $\vert$ inside a cell denotes the literal field separator (used in the `/etc/shadow` hash format string). It is intentionally **not** a markdown table separator.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** for every numerical conversion a KTU examiner can pose. *No step is skipped.*

---

## 3.1 Derivation A — Symbolic Mode to Octal Mode Conversion

### Problem
Convert the symbolic mode `drwxr-x---` with **SGID set on the directory** to its full 4-digit octal representation.

### Step 1 — Identify the file type character
The leading `d` indicates a directory. (This character is *not* part of the permission bits and is not encoded in the octal number; it is reported by `ls -l` for human readability only.)

### Step 2 — Decode the Owner triad
The owner triad is `rwx`. Substituting the weights:

$$
\text{Owner octal} = r \cdot 4 + w \cdot 2 + x \cdot 1 = 1 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1
$$

Computing the arithmetic:

$$
\text{Owner octal} = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7
$$

### Step 3 — Decode the Group triad
The group triad is `r-x`. The middle slot is `-` (no write):

$$
\text{Group octal} = 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1
$$

$$
\text{Group octal} = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5
$$

### Step 4 — Decode the Others triad
The others triad is `---`. No bits set:

$$
\text{Others octal} = 0 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Encode the Special bits digit
The problem states SGID is set. The SGID bit has weight `2000`. In a 4-digit octal mode the special-bit digit is the *leftmost*:

$$
\text{Special digit} = 0 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 2
$$

(The three binary sub-bits within the special digit, from MSB to LSB, are SUID / SGID / Sticky, so SGID sets the middle bit.)

### Step 6 — Assemble the full mode
Concatenating special + owner + group + others:

$$
\text{Full mode} = \mathbf{2750}
$$

### Verification
Run `stat -c '%a' <dir>` in a shell; the output should read `2750`. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — umask Computation for a New Directory

### Problem
A user has `umask = 027`. Determine the effective default permissions for a newly created **directory** and a newly created **file**.

### Step 1 — Recall the kernel defaults
For a file: $D_{\text{file}} = 666_{8}$. For a directory: $D_{\text{dir}} = 777_{8}$.

### Step 2 — Convert umask to 9-bit binary
`umask 027` decomposes as: owner = `0`, group = `2`, others = `7`.

In binary, each triad is:

$$
0_{8} = 000, \quad 2_{8} = 010, \quad 7_{8} = 111
$$

Stacking all nine bits (owner, group, others):

$$
U = 000\,010\,111
$$

### Step 3 — Bitwise NOT of umask
The bitwise NOT flips every 1 to 0 and vice versa:

$$
\sim U = 111\,101\,000
$$

Converting back to octal triads: $7\,5\,0$. So $(\sim U) = 750_{8}$.

### Step 4 — AND with the directory default
For a directory:

$$
M_{\text{dir}} = D_{\text{dir}} \;\&\; (\sim U) = 777_{8} \;\&\; 750_{8}
$$

Bitwise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
777_{8} &= 111\,111\,111 \\
750_{8} &= 111\,101\,000 \\
\text{AND} &= 111\,101\,000 = 750_{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** new directories get mode `750` = `rwxr-x---` (owner full, group read+execute, others nothing).

### Step 5 — AND with the file default
For a file:

$$
M_{\text{file}} = D_{\text{file}} \;\&\; (\sim U) = 666_{8} \;\&\; 750_{8}
$$

Bitwise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
666_{8} &= 110\,110\,110 \\
750_{8} &= 111\,101\,000 \\
\text{AND} &= 110\,100\,000 = 640_{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** new files get mode `640` = `rw-r-----` (owner read+write, group read-only, others nothing).

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** The execute bit for *others* in the umask is `1` (since umask=7 for others), so the file never gets an `x` for others — correct, because `umask` cannot *grant* execute on a regular file; it can only *suppress* it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Netfilter Packet Traversal (Stateless NAT scenario)

### Problem
A packet arrives on `eth0` destined for IP `10.0.0.5:80` on the local host. Trace the **netfilter chain traversal** for a `filter` + `nat` table setup.

### Step 1 — Identify the entry point
A packet arriving from an external network enters the kernel via the network interface driver on `eth0`. The very first netfilter hook it hits is `PREROUTING`.

### Step 2 — Table lookups at PREROUTING
The kernel consults the `nat` and `mangle` tables at this hook. If a `DNAT` rule rewrites the destination, the packet is rerouted. For a packet destined to a *local* IP, no DNAT typically applies, and the routing decision is:

- *If destination IP is the local host* → hand the packet to the `INPUT` chain.
- *If destination IP is some other host* → hand the packet to the `FORWARD` chain.

### Step 3 — INPUT chain evaluation
The `filter` table's `INPUT` chain is consulted. Rules are evaluated in order. A typical mini-firewall is:

$$
\text{Rule 1: } -A\ INPUT\ -m\ state\ --state\ ESTABLISHED,RELATED\ -j\ ACCEPT
$$

$$
\text{Rule 2: } -A\ INPUT\ -p\ tcp\ --dport\ 22\ -j\ ACCEPT
$$

$$
\text{Rule 3: } -A\ INPUT\ -p\ tcp\ --dport\ 80\ -j\ ACCEPT
$$

$$
\text{Rule 4 (policy): } -P\ INPUT\ DROP
$$

For a packet to `10.0.0.5:80`, Rule 1 matches only if it is part of an existing connection (it is not, on first SYN). Rule 3 matches. The packet is accepted and delivered to the local socket.

### Step 4 — Local processing
The local web server process (e.g., nginx) receives the packet on its TCP socket.

### Step 5 — Egress (response packet)
A response packet is generated by the local process. It enters the `OUTPUT` chain of the `filter` table and the `nat` table. If `SNAT/MASQUERADE` is configured, the source is rewritten here. After `OUTPUT`, the packet hits `POSTROUTING` (last chance for NAT) and exits via `eth0`.

### Compact traversal diagram

$$
\text{eth0} \;\rightarrow\; \text{PREROUTING} \;\rightarrow\; \text{INPUT} \;\rightarrow\; \text{nginx} \;\rightarrow\; \text{OUTPUT} \;\rightarrow\; \text{POSTROUTING} \;\rightarrow\; \text{eth0}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — SUID Privilege Escalation Bound

### Problem
Show that the SUID bit on a file owned by `root` causes the resulting process to obtain an **effective UID of 0** for the duration of the execution, and explain why this is the root cause of most local privilege-escalation CVEs.

### Step 1 — Process credential model
The Linux kernel tracks **three** UIDs per process:

- **Real UID (ruid):** the UID of the user that *launched* the process. Set at `execve()` and never changes (except via `setuid()` syscall for the *real* user, which the kernel restricts).
- **Effective UID (euid):** the UID used for *all permission checks*. This is the bit that the SUID flag manipulates.
- **Saved-set UID (suid):** the original euid, preserved so that a privileged process can later `setuid()` back to it.

### Step 2 — Effect of the SUID bit
When the kernel executes a file with the SUID bit set and the file's owner is `root (uid=0)`, the kernel performs, at `execve()` time:

$$
\text{euid} \;\leftarrow\; \text{file\_owner\_uid} = 0
$$

The real UID is left at the launching user's value (e.g., 1000). From the kernel's authorization perspective, **every** subsequent file access, network bind to a privileged port, and `kill()` is checked against `euid = 0` rather than `ruid = 1000`.

### Step 3 — Why this is dangerous
If the program has *any* vulnerability — a buffer overflow, a path traversal, an unsafe call to `system()` that uses a user-supplied string — the attacker inherits `euid = 0` for the duration of the exploit. This is why modern kernels (5.8+) introduced **kernel capabilities** and **no-new-privileges** — they let a service drop *specific* root powers (e.g., `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE`) instead of inheriting the entire root privilege set.

### Step 4 — Defense
- Keep the SUID bit set **only** on files from the distribution package manager (`dpkg --verify` or `rpm --verify`).
- Use `find / -perm /4000` to audit SUID files periodically.
- Drop capabilities with `setcap cap_net_bind_service=ep /usr/bin/nginx` instead of running the whole daemon as root.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Threat vs. Mitigation

| Threat | Linux Mechanism | Configuration File / Tool | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brute-force login | `pam_faillock` / `pam_tally2` | `/etc/pam.d/system-auth` | High |
| Offline password crack | Shadow file + SHA-512 hash | `/etc/shadow`, `passwd -S` | High |
| Privilege escalation via SUID | Audit + capability drop | `find / -perm /4000`, `setcap` | High |
| Unauthorized file access | POSIX rwx + ACL | `chmod`, `setfacl` | High |
| Unauthorized file deletion in shared dir | Sticky bit | `chmod +t /shared` | High |
| Network port scan | Host firewall | `iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --tcp-flags ALL NONE -j DROP` | High |
| Unauthorized root login via SSH | Disable `PermitRootLogin` | `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` | Very High |
| Insider snooping via process listing | Hide PIDs, `/proc` mount options | `mount -o hidepid=2 /proc` | Medium |
| Compromised service | SELinux/AppArmor | `setenforce 1`, `aa-enforce` | Very High |
| Tampered binaries | Integrity checker | `aide --init`, `tripwire --check` | High |
| Unpatched CVE | Update policy | `unattended-upgrades`, `yum-cron` | Very High |
| Stolen disk → data leak | Disk encryption | `cryptsetup luksFormat` | High |

## 4.2 File Permission Decision Algorithm (POSIX Check)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef good fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000

    req[REQUEST TO ACCESS FILE OR DIRECTORY]:::dec
    q1{REQUESTING PROCESS EUID EQUALS FILE OWNER UID}:::dec
    owner[APPLY OWNER TRIAD BITS]:::good
    q2{REQUESTING PROCESS PRIMARY OR SUPPLEMENTARY GID EQUALS FILE GID}:::dec
    group[APPLY GROUP TRIAD BITS]:::good
    other[APPLY OTHERS TRIAD BITS]:::good
    check{REQUESTED OPERATION BIT IS SET IN RESOLVED TRIAD}:::dec
    grant[GRANT ACCESS]:::good
    deny[DENY ACCESS WITH EACCES]:::bad

    req --> q1
    q1 -- YES --> owner --> check
    q1 -- NO --> q2
    q2 -- YES --> group --> check
    q2 -- NO --> other --> check
    check -- YES --> grant
    check -- NO --> deny
```

```mermaid
    a --> b --> c --> d --> e --> f --> g --> h --> i --> j
```
