# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Two questions below are modeled on the **3-mark definition/concept** pattern seen in the KTU University End-Semester Examination (ESE) Module 3 papers.

---

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**Q: Explain the significance of the SUID and SGID bits in Linux file permissions. Why is the SUID bit set on `/usr/bin/passwd`? Why is leaving SUID on shell scripts considered dangerous?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **SUID (Set User ID)** and **SGID (Set Group ID)** bits are special permission bits in the Unix/Linux file mode that, when set on an executable file, cause the resulting process to run with the **file owner's UID (for SUID)** or **file group's GID (for SGID)** as its effective identity, rather than the launching user's identity.

`/usr/bin/passwd` has the SUID bit set because it must update `/etc/shadow`, a file that is writable **only by root**. An ordinary user, left without this mechanism, would never be able to change their own password. By setting SUID on `passwd`, the kernel temporarily elevates the process to `euid=0` for the brief duration of the password-update syscall, after which it exits.

The SUID bit is **dangerous on shell scripts** because:
- Shell scripts are interpreted; an attacker can manipulate the interpreter's environment (e.g., `PATH`, `IFS`, `BASH_ENV`) to cause the script to execute unintended commands.
- A buffer overflow or injection in the script effectively runs as root.
- Modern distributions therefore refuse to honour SUID on interpreted scripts; the kernel ignores the bit on files whose shebang references an interpreter.

*(Allocate approx. 1 mark each to: definition, passwd example, script risk — examiner's discretion.)*

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**Q: What is the purpose of the `sticky bit` on a directory? Illustrate with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **sticky bit** is a special permission bit (octal `1000`, symbolized `+t`) that, when set on a directory, **restricts file deletion and renaming** within that directory. Even if a user has write permission (`w`) on the directory — and would therefore normally be able to delete any file inside it — the sticky bit ensures that a file can be removed or renamed **only by the file's owner, the directory's owner, or root**.

**Example:** The directory `/tmp` is world-writable (mode `1777`). Without the sticky bit, any user could `rm` any other user's temporary files, breaking isolation between users. With `+t` set, user `alice` can only delete files that she herself created in `/tmp`; she cannot delete `bob`'s files.

> *Examiner's key mark:* the wording "file's owner, not the directory writ-er" is the key insight — partial credit if the student only says "prevents deletion".

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 long-answer questions have **internal choice** — students answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B. Each choice has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Model solutions below are examiner-grade.

---

### Question B.A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024, Module 3]`

#### (a) Discuss the Linux file permission model in detail. With suitable examples, explain the meaning of r, w, x bits on both files and directories. Convert the symbolic mode `drwxr-sr-x` to its octal equivalent. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. The Three Triads (3 marks):** Linux associates three permission triads with every file/directory — one for the **owner (u)**, one for the **group (g)**, and one for **others (o)**. Each triad has three bits:

- `r` (read, weight `4`): On a *file*, allows reading its contents (`cat`, `less`, `grep`). On a *directory*, allows listing entries (`ls`).
- `w` (write, weight `2`): On a *file*, allows modifying its contents. On a *directory*, allows creating, deleting, and renaming entries *inside* it.
- `x` (execute, weight `1`): On a *file*, allows execution as a program. On a *directory*, allows `cd`-ing into it (the "search" bit) and accessing its entries.

**2. Worked Conversion (4 marks):** Convert `drwxr-sr-x`:

- File type: `d` (directory) — not part of the octal value.
- Owner triad `rwx`:
$$
4 + 2 + 1 = 7
$$
- Group triad `r-s` — note the `s` in place of the execute position. This is **SGID** plus an `x`:
$$
\text{Group bits} = r + 0\cdot w + (s \text{ implies } x) = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5
$$
- Others triad `r-x`:
$$
4 + 0 + 1 = 5
$$
- Special-bit digit: SGID is set, so the special digit is `2`.

Concatenating: special + owner + group + others = **`2755`**.

> *Examiner key marks:* 2 marks for triads + meanings, 3 marks for the conversion computation, 1 mark for noting the `s` shorthand combines SGID+execute, 1 mark for the final octal answer.

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of umask in Linux. If a user has `umask = 027`, compute the default permissions for a newly created file and a newly created directory. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (2 marks):** `umask` is a per-process *bit-mask* that the kernel applies to the default permission set when creating new files and directories. It is the *inverse* of the permissions you want to *grant by default*. The formula is:

$$
M_{\text{effective}} = D_{\text{kernel}} \;\text{AND}\; (\text{NOT } U_{\text{umask}})
$$

Kernel defaults: file → `666` (no execute), directory → `777` (all bits set).

**2. Computation for a file (2.5 marks):** With $U = 027_{8} = 000\,010\,111_{2}$:

$$
\sim U = 111\,101\,000_{2} = 750_{8}
$$

$$
M_{\text{file}} = 666_{8} \;\text{AND}\; 750_{8}
$$

Bitwise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
666_{8} &= 110\,110\,110 \\
750_{8} &= 111\,101\,000 \\
\text{AND} &= 110\,100\,000 = 640_{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** new file → `640` = `rw-r-----` (owner R/W, group R, others none).

**3. Computation for a directory (2.5 marks):**

$$
M_{\text{dir}} = 777_{8} \;\text{AND}\; 750_{8}
$$

Bitwise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
777_{8} &= 111\,111\,111 \\
750_{8} &= 111\,101\,000 \\
\text{AND} &= 111\,101\,000 = 750_{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** new directory → `750` = `rwxr-x---` (owner full, group R+X, others none).

> *Examiner key marks:* 2 for formula + defaults, 2.5 for file, 2.5 for directory. Half-mark for the binary expansion step.

---

### Question B.B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 3 Re-validated]`

#### (a) Describe the iptables firewall architecture in Linux. List the built-in chains and explain the traversal path of an incoming packet destined to a local process. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Architecture (3 marks):** `iptables` is the user-space tool to configure the kernel's `netfilter` packet-processing framework. The framework is organized as **three tables** × **five chains**:

- **Tables:** `filter` (default — packet allow/deny), `nat` (source/destination translation), `mangle` (header modification).
- **Chains:** `PREROUTING`, `INPUT`, `FORWARD`, `OUTPUT`, `POSTROUTING`.

Each chain has a default *policy* (`ACCEPT` or `DROP`) and an ordered list of *rules*; the first matching rule's verdict wins.

**2. Traversal for a local-bound packet (4 marks):**

$$
\text{NIC} \;\rightarrow\; \text{PREROUTING} \;\rightarrow\; (\text{routing decision}) \;\rightarrow\; \text{INPUT} \;\rightarrow\; \text{local process}
$$

Detailed steps:

1. Packet enters via the NIC driver; `PREROUTING` hooks the `nat` and `mangle` tables — typical use is DNAT for port-forwarding.
2. The kernel's routing subsystem examines the destination IP.
3. If the destination is a *local* IP, the packet is queued for the `INPUT` chain.
4. The `filter` table's `INPUT` chain is consulted; rules are evaluated in order. If a rule matches, its target (`ACCEPT`/`DROP`/`REJECT`) is applied.
5. If no rule matches, the chain *policy* is applied.
6. A surviving packet is delivered to the local socket via the kernel's transport layer.

> *Examiner key marks:* 2 for table/chain enumeration, 2 for traversal path, 1 each for default-policy and hook concept.

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of Pluggable Authentication Modules (PAM) in Linux. With a sample PAM configuration, illustrate how account lockout is achieved after 5 failed login attempts. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. PAM Concept (3 marks):** **Pluggable Authentication Modules (PAM)** is a library (`libpam`) and configuration framework that decouples *applications* (login, sshd, sudo, passwd, screen-lockers) from *authentication mechanisms* (passwords, biometrics, smart cards, OTP). Each service has a config file in `/etc/pam.d/`, which **stacks** modules in a defined control flow.

A typical rule line has the form:

$$
\texttt{type\ control\ module-path\ module-args}
$$

Where `type` ∈ {`auth`, `account`, `session`, `password`} and `control` ∈ {`required`, `requisite`, `sufficient`, `optional`, or a numeric return-value list like `[success=1 default=bad]`}.

**2. Account Lockout Configuration (4 marks):** To lock an account after 5 failed attempts for 15 minutes, add to `/etc/pam.d/system-auth` and `/etc/pam.d/password-auth`:

```
auth        required      pam_faillock.so preauth deny=5 unlock_time=900
auth        required      pam_faillock.so authfail deny=5 unlock_time=900
account     required      pam_faillock.so per_user
```

Explanation of the lines:
- Line 1 (`preauth`): before password verification, check the failure count — if already at 5, deny.
- Line 2 (`authfail`): on failed authentication, increment the counter.
- Line 3 (`account`): at session-opening time, deny if the counter is at the threshold.
- `deny=5` — allow at most 5 failed attempts.
- `unlock_time=900` — auto-unlock after 900 seconds (15 min).
- `per_user` — track failures **per user**, not globally (so user A's failures don't lock out user B).

The tracking data is stored in `/var/run/faillock/` (or `/var/log/faillock` on older distros). To manually unlock, the root user runs `pam_tally2 --user=alice --reset` (legacy) or `faillock --user alice --reset` (modern).

> *Examiner key marks:* 2 for PAM concept, 1 for module-stack idea, 1 for sample config, 1 for explanation of `deny` and `unlock_time`, 1 for the location of state.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic — KTU examiner observations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the meaning of `w` on a directory vs. a file.** Many students write "`w` on a directory lets you modify the directory's contents" — but the directory has no "contents" in the file sense; the correct phrasing is "create, delete, rename entries *within* it". Lose 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting the special-bit digit in octal conversion.** A mode like `4755` (SUID set) is frequently written as `755`. Examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 3. **Stating "umask sets the default permissions".** Wrong. umask *restricts* the default. Use "applies" or "subtracts via bitwise AND-with-NOT". Examiner may deduct 0.5–1 mark depending on strictness.
> 4. **Listing the iptables chains but not mentioning the tables.** Half the credit is for tables. Examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 5. **Writing `chmod 777 file` as a "security recommendation".** Immediate red flag — full security question goes wrong. Examiner deducts 1 mark *and* may mark the entire answer as lacking security mindset.
> 6. **Confusing `/etc/passwd` and `/etc/shadow`.** The hash lives in **shadow**, not passwd. Lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 10-minute revision punch list — read this aloud before walking into the exam hall:**

- [ ] Linux security is **defense-in-depth**: identity → DAC → MAC → network → audit.
- [ ] Bit weights are **`r=4`, `w=2`, `x=1`**. Memorize them cold.
- [ ] Full octal mode is **4 digits**: special + owner + group + others.
- [ ] Special bits: **SUID=4000, SGID=2000, Sticky=1000**.
- [ ] `w` on a directory lets you **create/delete/rename** entries — not edit the directory itself.
- [ ] `x` on a directory is the **search bit** (lets `cd` traverse).
- [ ] **Sticky bit** on `/tmp` prevents users from deleting each other's files.
- [ ] **SUID** on `/usr/bin/passwd` lets an unprivileged user write to root-owned `/etc/shadow`.
- [ ] `umask` formula: $\;M = D \;\text{AND}\; (\sim U)\;$. Defaults: file=666, dir=777.
- [ ] `/etc/passwd` is world-readable; `/etc/shadow` is **root-readable** — that's why there are two files.
- [ ] PAM lives in `/etc/pam.d/` and stacks modules with control flags `required` / `requisite` / `sufficient` / `optional`.
- [ ] `pam_faillock` enforces account lockout — modern replacement for legacy `pam_tally2`.
- [ ] iptables has **3 tables** (filter, nat, mangle) and **5 chains** (PREROUTING, INPUT, FORWARD, OUTPUT, POSTROUTING).
- [ ] Local-bound packet path: NIC → PREROUTING → routing → INPUT → local process.
- [ ] SSH hardening minimums: **`PermitRootLogin no`**, key-only auth, change port, restrict users via `AllowUsers`.
- [ ] SELinux/AppArmor provide **MAC** on top of DAC — even root can be denied.
- [ ] Principle of least privilege: prefer **capabilities** (`setcap`) over SUID whenever possible.
- [ ] Always apply patches via `unattended-upgrades` (Debian) or `dnf-automatic` (RHEL).
- [ ] Audit using `find / -perm /4000` periodically to catch rogue SUID files.
- [ ] `/tmp` is the canonical case of `1777` — world-writable + sticky bit.
- [ ] Remember the `s` shorthand in `ls -l` output: it means the special bit is set *and* the execute bit is set. A capital `S` means the special bit is set but the execute bit is **not** (a misconfiguration that effectively nullifies the SUID).

> **End of Module 3 — Operating Linux Safely — KTU PCCCT502 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Browser and Client Risk - How a Web Browser Works
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module-4
> - **Topic:** Web Browser and Client Risk - How a Web Browser Works

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Web Browser?

A **Web Browser** is a client-side software application whose primary function is to fetch, interpret, and render resources from the World Wide Web. Formally, a web browser is a *User Agent* (UA) — a software program acting on behalf of a user — that issues **HTTP/HTTPS requests** to web servers, parses the returned markup (HTML, XML), applies presentational styling (CSS), executes embedded scripts (JavaScript), and presents a visual, interactive graphical interface to the end user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> A web browser is a distributed application client that retrieves, processes, and displays web content while simultaneously acting as the **primary attack surface** for client-side cyber threats such as Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF), phishing, and drive-by downloads.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Browser as a "Universal Translator & Receptionist"

Imagine you walk into a foreign embassy (the **Web Server**) where everyone speaks a different language (HTTP, HTML, CSS, JS, JSON, XML). You, the user, only know your native tongue. A **Web Browser** is your personal **interpreter-receptionist** who:

1. **Takes your request** ("I want the KTU results page") typed as a URL.
2. **Calls the embassy** on your behalf (DNS lookup + TCP/TLS handshake).
3. **Sends a formal letter** (HTTP GET/POST request).
4. **Receives a multi-page reply** (HTML + CSS + JS + media).
5. **Translates, organizes, and decorates** the reply into something visually meaningful.
6. **Hands it to you** on a beautifully arranged tray (the rendered web page).

Now, the **danger**: this receptionist also opens every package sent to you (active content, scripts, cookies). If the embassy is malicious, they can slip in poisoned letters, hidden cameras, or forged signatures — these are the **Client-Side Risks**.

## 1.3 What are Client-Side Risks?

**Client-side risks** (or *client risks*) are security vulnerabilities that originate from the **end-user's machine or browser environment**, rather than from the server. The browser, being a trusted execution environment for untrusted code from arbitrary origins, is the **largest exposed attack surface** in modern computing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> - **Server-side risk** → SQL Injection, Server RCE, Privilege Escalation on the host.
> - **Client-side risk** → XSS, CSRF, Malicious Browser Extensions, Phishing, Drive-by Downloads, Cookie Theft, Clickjacking, Man-in-the-Browser (MitB).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & High-Yield Constants

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Standard Value / Note |
|---|---|---|
| **HTTP Port** | Default unencrypted port | **80** |
| **HTTPS Port** | Default TLS-encrypted port | **443** |
| **SOP** | Same-Origin Policy enforcement | Strict by default |
| **CSP** | Content Security Policy header | Optional, defense-in-depth |
| **User-Agent** | Browser identity string | RFC 7231 compliant |
| **DOM** | Document Object Model | In-memory tree structure |
| **Same-Origin** | `scheme://host:port` triplet | All three must match |
| **TLS Version** | Minimum recommended | **TLS 1.2** (preferably 1.3) |
| **Cookie HttpOnly** | JS-inaccessible flag | Mitigates XSS cookie theft |
| **Cookie Secure** | HTTPS-only transmission | Mitigates MITM cookie theft |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCCCT502 Module 4):**
> KTU expects students to master the **end-to-end browser workflow** (URL parsing → DNS → TCP → TLS → HTTP → Rendering) AND the **taxonomy of client-side attacks** with their corresponding browser-level mitigations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Major Components of a Web Browser (Architectural Breakdown)

A modern browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari) is built as a **multi-process, layered architecture** for security and stability.

### 2.1.1 The User Interface (UI) Layer
- The visible chrome: address bar (omnibox), back/forward buttons, bookmarks, tabs, status bar.
- Handles user input and translates it into browser-engine commands.

### 2.1.2 The Browser Engine (Controller)
- Acts as the **mediator** between the UI and the rendering engine.
- Marshals actions: URL parsing, navigation initiation, data marshaling between processes.

### 2.1.3 The Rendering Engine
- The **heart** of the browser. Responsible for displaying requested content.
  - **Examples:** Blink (Chrome/Edge), Gecko (Firefox), WebKit (Safari).
- Performs: HTML parsing → DOM tree construction → CSS parsing → CSSOM → **Render Tree** → Layout (reflow) → Painting.

### 2.1.4 Networking Layer
- Implements the **HTTP/HTTPS protocol stack** over **TCP/IP** (and QUIC for HTTP/3).
- Handles **DNS resolution**, **TCP connection management**, **TLS handshake**, **HTTP request/response**, caching, and cookie management.

### 2.1.5 JavaScript Engine (JS Interpreter)
- Parses, compiles (JIT), and executes JavaScript code.
  - **Examples:** V8 (Chrome), SpiderMonkey (Firefox), JavaScriptCore (Safari).
- **Security note:** Executes in a **sandboxed** environment with limited OS access.

### 2.1.6 UI Backend
- A generic drawing interface used by the rendering engine (Skia, CoreGraphics, Cairo).
- Translates layout into pixel-level operations on the screen.

### 2.1.7 Data Storage / Persistence Layer
- Manages **Cookies** (RFC 6265), **LocalStorage**, **SessionStorage**, **IndexedDB**, **Cache API**, and **Service Workers**.
- This layer is a **prime target** for client-side attacks.

### 2.1.8 Multi-Process Architecture (Chrome's Innovation)
- **Browser process** (controller), **Renderer processes** (sandboxed per tab), **GPU process**, **Network process**, **Plugin process**, **Extension process**.
- Sandbox provides **OS-level isolation** — even if a renderer is compromised, it cannot access the host system.

## 2.2 The Complete Request-Response Lifecycle (Step-by-Step)

When a user enters `https://www.ktu.edu.in/results`:

1. **URL Parsing** → Browser extracts scheme (`https`), host (`www.ktu.edu.in`), path (`/results`), port (default 443).
2. **HSTS Check** → Is this host on the HSTS preload list? If yes, force HTTPS.
3. **Cache Lookup** → Does the browser have a fresh cached copy? (If so, skip network).
4. **DNS Resolution** → Translate `www.ktu.edu.in` to an IP address (e.g., `203.0.113.42`).
5. **TCP Connection** → Open a TCP socket to the resolved IP on port 443.
6. **TLS Handshake** → Establish an encrypted channel (negotiate cipher, exchange certificates, derive session keys).
7. **HTTP Request** → Send `GET /results HTTP/2` with headers (Host, User-Agent, Accept, Cookie).
8. **Server Processing** → Web server processes the request, queries database.
9. **HTTP Response** → Server returns `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` with `Content-Type: text/html`, body, Set-Cookie headers, security headers.
10. **Browser Receives Data** → Bytes streamed; may arrive in multiple chunks.
11. **Parsing Phase** →
    - **HTML Parser** → builds **DOM Tree** (tokenization → tree construction).
    - **CSS Parser** → builds **CSSOM Tree**.
    - Both merge to form the **Render Tree**.
12. **Script Execution** → `<script>` tags encountered; JS engine executes inline/external scripts.
13. **Layout (Reflow)** → Calculate geometry of every node in the render tree.
14. **Painting** → Convert render tree to pixel layers (compositor).
15. **Display** → Frame is rasterized and pushed to the screen.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formal Representation / Rule | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** | `Origin = (scheme, host, port)`; access allowed **iff** `Origin_A == Origin_B` | Prevents cross-origin DOM/cookie theft |
| **URI Scheme Identifier** | `scheme://authority/path?query#fragment` | URL canonicalization |
| **HTTP Status Class** | `1xx` Info, `2xx` Success, `3xx` Redirection, `4xx` Client Error, `5xx` Server Error | Response classification |
| **TLS Cipher Suite** | `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384` | Forward secrecy + AEAD |
| **XSS Sanitization Function** | `Safe(x) = HTMLEncode(x)` where `<` → `<`, `>` → `>`, `"` → `"` | Input/output encoding |
| **CSP Source Whitelist** | `Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self' https://cdn.ktu.edu` | Restricts script sources |
| **Cookie Scope** | `Domain` + `Path` + `Secure` + `HttpOnly` + `SameSite` flags | Reduces CSRF & XSS exposure |
| **Hash-based Integrity** | `SRI = SHA-384(Body) → Base64` | Detects tampered subresources |
| **CORS Preflight** | `OPTIONS` request before non-simple `POST/PUT/DELETE` | Authorizes cross-origin XHR |
| **Public Suffix List (PSL)** | `eTLD+1` defines registrable domain | Cookie scoping, SOP refinement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Every KTU-affiliated engineering project involving a web frontend, IoT dashboard, REST API, or mobile app **must** understand this lifecycle. Penetration testers, DevSecOps engineers, and web developers all rely on these formulas daily.

## 2.4 Taxonomy of Client-Side Risks (KTU-Exam Essential)

| Risk | Attack Vector | Primary Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** | Injecting malicious JS into a trusted page | Input validation, output encoding, CSP |
| **Stored XSS** | Malicious payload persisted in DB | Server-side sanitization, CSP |
| **Reflected XSS** | Payload in URL/POST body reflected in response | Output encoding, X-XSS-Protection (legacy) |
| **DOM-based XSS** | Client-side JS writes unsanitized data to DOM | Safe DOM APIs (`textContent`), Trusted Types |
| **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)** | Forcing victim to submit authenticated request | Anti-CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies |
| **Clickjacking** | Transparent iframe overlays tricking user clicks | `X-Frame-Options: DENY`, CSP `frame-ancestors` |
| **Session Hijacking** | Stealing session cookie via XSS or MITM | HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies |
| **Man-in-the-Browser (MitB)** | Trojan modifies browser traffic mid-flight | Transaction signing, out-of-band verification |
| **Phishing** | Fake UI mimicking legitimate site | Browser phishing blacklists, EV certificates |
| **Drive-by Download** | Exploit kit triggers on malicious page visit | Sandbox, plugin isolation, browser updates |
| **Malicious Browser Extensions** | Rogue extension exfiltrates data | Least-privilege extension APIs, vetting |
| **SSL Stripping** | Downgrading HTTPS to HTTP | HSTS preload list, HSTS headers |
| **Cookie Theft via JS** | `document.cookie` read by injected script | HttpOnly flag |
| **Typosquatting** | `g00gle.com` → fake login | Browser heuristics, safe browsing API |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Same-Origin Policy Decision Function

The **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** is the cornerstone of browser security. We derive the formal decision function used by every modern browser to determine whether a cross-origin interaction should be permitted.

### Step 1: Define the Origin Triplet

Each URL is normalized to extract its canonical origin components.

$$ \text{Origin}(U) = \big( \text{scheme}(U),\ \text{host}(U),\ \text{port}(U) \big) $$

**Row-by-row explanation:**
- `scheme(U)` → the protocol (`http`, `https`, `ftp`, etc.). Default ports are inferred.
- `host}(U)` → the fully qualified domain name, case-insensitive, IDN-decoded.
- `port}(U)` → the explicit or default port number (e.g., `80`, `443`).

### Step 2: Define the Equivalence Operator

Two origins are considered equal only if **all three** components match exactly (case-insensitive for host).

$$ A \equiv B \iff \begin{cases} \text{scheme}(A) = \text{scheme}(B) \\ \text{host}(A) = \text{host}(B) \\ \text{port}(A) = \text{port}(B) \end{cases} $$

**Row-by-row explanation:**
- First line → Protocols must be identical (`https` cannot access `http` resources).
- Second line → DNS-based comparison, case-insensitive.
- Third line → Ports must match, including the implicit defaults.

### Step 3: The SOP Decision Function

The browser invokes this function before allowing any DOM, cookie, fetch, or storage access.

$$ \text{SOP\_Allow}(A, B) = \begin{cases} \text{True}  & \text{if } A \equiv B \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

**Row-by-row explanation:**
- If origins are identical → access is granted.
- If any component differs → access is **denied by default**.

### Step 4: Worked Example Matrix

| Source Origin (A) | Target Origin (B) | Scheme Match | Host Match | Port Match | SOP Verdict |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `https://ktu.edu.in:443` | `https://ktu.edu.in/results` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | **Allow** |
| `https://ktu.edu.in` | `http://ktu.edu.in` | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | **Deny** |
| `https://ktu.edu.in` | `https://exam.ktu.edu.in` | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | **Deny** |
| `https://ktu.edu.in:443` | `https://ktu.edu.in:8080` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | **Deny** |
| `https://ktu.edu.in` | `https://ktu.edu.in:443` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | **Allow** |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: XSS Payload Transformation Pipeline

To understand why XSS works, we derive the transformation a malicious string undergoes as it travels from attacker → server → browser → DOM.

### Step 1: Attacker Constructs Payload

The attacker supplies a raw malicious string $P$ to a vulnerable input field.

$$ P = \texttt{<script>alert('XSS')</script>} $$

### Step 2: Server Persists or Reflects Without Encoding

If the application fails to apply an output-encoding function $E(\cdot)$, the string is stored verbatim in the database or echoed in the response body.

$$ \text{ResponseBody} = E(P) = P \quad (\text{when } E \text{ is missing}) $$

### Step 3: Browser Tokenizes the Response

The HTML parser encounters the string and treats `<script>` as a legitimate script-opening tag, not as text. The tokenization function is:

$$ \text{Tokens}(B) = \big\{ t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n \big\} \quad \text{where } t_i = \text{ParseToken}(B[i]) $$

### Step 4: Script Execution Invoked

The JavaScript engine receives the script token and executes it in the **origin context of the page** — inheriting all its cookies, privileges, and DOM access.

$$ \text{JSEngine.Execute}(P) = \text{alert('XSS')} \rightarrow \text{Side Effect (Data Exfiltration)} $$

### Step 5: Mitigation — Apply Output Encoding

The correct pipeline inserts an encoding function $E(\cdot)$ that maps dangerous characters to their HTML entity equivalents.

$$ E(x) = \begin{cases} \text{<} & \text{if } x = \text{<} \\ \text{>} & \text{if } x = \text{>} \\ \text{"} & \text{if } x = \text{"} \\ \text{'} & \text{if } x = \text{'} \\ \text{&} & \text{if } x = \text{&} \\ x & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

After encoding, the parser no longer treats the string as markup:

$$ E(P) = \texttt{<script>alert('XSS')</script>} \rightarrow \text{Displayed as text, NOT executed} $$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Browser Security Headers & Their Effects

| Header | Sample Value | Threat Mitigated | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Content-Security-Policy` | `default-src 'self'` | XSS, data injection | "Defense-in-depth gold standard" |
| `X-Frame-Options` | `DENY` | Clickjacking | Older, replaced by CSP |
| `Strict-Transport-Security` | `max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains` | SSL stripping | Use preload list for max effect |
| `X-Content-Type-Options` | `nosniff` | MIME sniffing attacks | One-liner, easy marks |
| `Referrer-Policy` | `no-referrer` | Information leakage | Protects privacy |
| `Permissions-Policy` | `geolocation=(), camera=()` | Feature abuse | Modern replacement for Feature-Policy |
| `Set-Cookie` flags | `HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict` | XSS cookie theft, CSRF | Triple-flag is the KTU-favoured answer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Deployment:** Google, KTU's own e-governance portal, and banking apps deploy **all** of the above headers. Missing even one is a finding in any penetration test report.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Browser Multi-Layered Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph uiLayer["USER INTERFACE LAYER"]
        addressBar["Address Bar / Omnibox"]
        tabMgr["Tab Manager"]
        bookmarks["Bookmarks Bar"]
    end

    subgraph ctrlLayer["BROWSER ENGINE / CONTROLLER"]
        navCtrl["Navigation Controller"]
        ipcBus["IPC Bus"]
    end

    subgraph renderLayer["RENDERING ENGINE - BLINK or GECKO"]

        cssParser["CSS Parser"]
        domTree["DOM Tree Builder"]
        cssomTree["CSSOM Tree Builder"]
        renderTree["Render Tree"]
        layoutEng["Layout Engine - Reflow"]
        paintEng["Paint Engine"]
    end

    subgraph jsLayer["JAVASCRIPT ENGINE - V8 or SPIDERMONKEY"]
        parser["JS Parser"]
        jit["JIT Compiler"]
        gc["Garbage Collector"]
        sandbox["JS Sandbox"]
    end

    subgraph netLayer["NETWORKING LAYER"]
        dnsClient["DNS Resolver"]
        httpStack["HTTP and HTTPS Stack"]
        tlsMod["TLS Module"]
        cacheMgr["Cache Manager"]
        cookieJar["Cookie Store"]
    end

    subgraph dataLayer["DATA STORAGE LAYER"]
        localStore["LocalStorage"]
        sessionStore["SessionStorage"]
        idb["IndexedDB"]
        sw["Service Workers"]
    end

    subgraph secLayer["SECURITY SUBSYSTEM"]
        sop["Same-Origin Policy Enforcer"]
        cspMod["CSP Validator"]
        phishingDB["Phishing Blacklist DB"]
        hstsList["HSTS Preload List"]
    end

    addressBar --> navCtrl
    navCtrl --> dnsClient
    dnsClient --> tlsMod
    tlsMod --> httpStack
    httpStack --> cacheMgr
    cacheMgr --> htmlParser
    htmlParser --> domTree
    cssParser --> cssomTree
    domTree --> renderTree
    cssomTree --> renderTree
    renderTree --> layoutEng
    layoutEng --> paintEng
    paintEng --> tabMgr

    htmlParser --> parser
    parser --> jit
    jit --> sandbox
    sandbox --> domTree

    httpStack --> cookieJar
    cookieJar --> sop
    cacheMgr --> cspMod
    navCtrl --> hstsList
    navCtrl --> phishingDB
```

## 4.2 End-to-End Web Request-Response Flow (Client Risk View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User
    participant B as Browser
    participant DNS as DNS Resolver
    participant T as TCP and TLS Layer
    participant S as Web Server
    participant DB as Backend Database
    participant ATK as Attacker

    U->>B: Type URL and press Enter
    B->>B: Parse URL and check HSTS list
    B->>DNS: Resolve hostname to IP
    DNS-->>B: Return IP address
    B->>T: Open TCP connection on port 443
    T->>S: Initiate TLS handshake
    S-->>T: Send X.509 certificate
    T-->>B: Establish secure session
    B->>S: HTTP GET request with headers and cookies
    S->>DB: Query backend data
    DB-->>S: Return records
    S-->>B: HTTP 200 OK with HTML, CSS, JS body
    B->>B: Parse HTML, build DOM
    B->>B: Parse CSS, build CSSOM
    B->>B: Execute JavaScript in sandbox

    Note over ATK,B: RISK INJECTION POINT
    ATK-->>B: Inject malicious script via XSS or phishing
    B->>B: Sandbox executes untrusted JS
    B-->>ATK: Stolen cookies or session token exfiltrated
    ATK-->>S: Replay stolen session token

    B-->>U: Render final page
```

    session --> fix["Session Fixation"]

    malw --> ext["Malicious Browser Extensions"]
