> These state mechanisms are the **direct attack surface** for session hijacking, XSS, and CSRF — all of which are examined in the next topics of this KTU module.

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

HTTP powers **every** modern web system: REST APIs used by mobile apps, microservices communicating internally (service mesh), IoT telemetry, single-page applications (SPAs), GraphQL endpoints, and CDN edge nodes. A KTU graduate who understands HTTP request/response anatomy is equipped to debug with `curl`, Burp Suite, Wireshark, and Chrome DevTools — skills directly relevant to penetration testing, DevSecOps, and secure software development roles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Breakdown of a URL

The **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)** is the address an HTTP client sends. Every URL decomposes into a fixed structural skeleton:

$$\text{URL} = \underbrace{\text{scheme}}_{\text{protocol}} \mathbin{:}\!/ \!\!/ \underbrace{\text{authority}}_{\text{user:pass@host:port}} \underbrace{\text{path}}_{\text{/segment/segment}} \underbrace{\text{query}}_{\text{?key=value}} \underbrace{\text{fragment}}_{\text{\#anchor}}$$

**Worked Example:** Decompose `https://admin:p%40ss@shop.ktu.edu.in:8443/cart/add?id=42&qty=3#billing`

| Component | Extracted Value |
|---|---|
| Scheme | `https` |
| Userinfo | `admin:p%40ss` (URL-encoded `@`) |
| Host | `shop.ktu.edu.in` |
| Port | `8443` (non-default HTTPS port) |
| Path | `/cart/add` |
| Query | `?id=42&qty=3` |
| Fragment | `#billing` (client-side only — **never sent to server**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fragment (`#...`)** is **never transmitted to the server** by the browser. It exists purely for client-side navigation. This is a classic KTU conceptual trap.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Anatomy of an HTTP Transaction

Let us trace the precise sequence of a single HTTP/1.1 GET request to `http://example.com/page.html`.

**Step 1 — Browser parses the URL.**

The browser extracts:
- Scheme → `http`
- Host → `example.com`
- Path → `/page.html`
- Port → default `80`

**Step 2 — DNS resolution.**

The browser queries the OS resolver → ISP recursive resolver → authoritative nameserver → returns an A or AAAA record (e.g., `93.184.216.34`).

**Step 3 — TCP three-way handshake.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Client} &\xrightarrow{\text{SYN, seq}=x} \text{Server} \\
\text{Server} &\xrightarrow{\text{SYN-ACK, seq}=y, ack}=x+1} \text{Client} \\
\text{Client} &\xrightarrow{\text{ACK, seq}=x+1, ack}=y+1} \text{Server}
\end{aligned}
$$

Connection is now established on **port 80**.

**Step 4 — HTTP request is serialized and sent.**

The browser writes the following bytes onto the socket:

```
GET /page.html HTTP/1.1\r\n
Host: example.com\r\n
User-Agent: Chrome/120.0\r\n
Accept: text/html\r\n
Connection: keep-alive\r\n
\r\n
```

**Step 5 — Server processes and responds.**

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n
Content-Type: text/html\r\n
Content-Length: 1024\r\n
Date: Mon, 15 Sep 2025 12:00:00 GMT\r\n
\r\n
<!DOCTYPE html>...
```

**Step 6 — Connection lifecycle decision.**

Because `Connection: keep-alive` is set, the TCP connection **remains open** for additional requests (HTTP/1.1 default behavior), avoiding repeated TCP handshakes.

### 3.3 Comparative Matrix — Persistent vs Non-Persistent Connections

| Property | Non-Persistent (HTTP/1.0 default) | Persistent (HTTP/1.1 default) |
|---|---|---|
| TCP connections per object | New connection per object | One connection reused |
| Setup overhead | High (3-way handshake per object) | Amortized over N requests |
| Throughput for a page with 10 objects | 10 handshakes | 1 handshake |
| RTT cost | $2n$ RTT per object | $2$ RTT for the page |
| Header overhead | Repeated | Amortized (but still text-heavy) |
| Pipelining | Not supported | Optional (rarely used due to head-of-line blocking) |

### 3.4 Status Code Decision Logic (Analytical Flow)

When answering "what status code should server X return?", follow this decision tree:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Request received?} &\Rightarrow \text{No} \rightarrow \textbf{4xx Client Error} \\
\text{Request understood?} &\Rightarrow \text{No} \rightarrow \textbf{4xx Client Error} \\
\text{Auth required?} &\Rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \textbf{401 / 403} \\
\text{Resource exists?} &\Rightarrow \text{No} \rightarrow \textbf{404 Not Found} \\
\text{Server can fulfill?} &\Rightarrow \text{No} \rightarrow \textbf{5xx Server Error} \\
\text{New resource created?} &\Rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \textbf{201 Created} \\
\text{Action required?} &\Rightarrow \text{Redirect} \rightarrow \textbf{3xx Redirection} \\
\text{Default success?} &\Rightarrow \textbf{200 OK}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Header Security Mapping (Analytical Matrix)

| Security Risk | Mitigating HTTP Header | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|
| Protocol downgrade | `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| XSS injection | `Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Clickjacking | `X-Frame-Options: DENY` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| MIME sniffing | `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Referer leakage | `Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin` | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Cookie theft via XSS | `Set-Cookie: HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Excessive caching of sensitive data | `Cache-Control: no-store` | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> A student who memorizes this matrix can answer 70% of KTU's "explain web security headers" Part B sub-questions without needing the textbook.

### 3.6 Sample Calculation — Performance Trade-off

**Question:** A webpage contains **1 base HTML file** and **8 embedded image objects**. Each TCP handshake costs **1 RTT**. Estimate the RTT savings of HTTP/1.1 persistent connections over HTTP/1.0 non-persistent connections for fetching this page.

**Solution (step-by-step):**

Let $R$ denote one round-trip time.

**HTTP/1.0 (Non-Persistent):**

$$\text{TCP Handshakes} = 9 \text{ objects} \times 1 \text{ RTT per object} = 9R$$

Plus, for each object, **one additional RTT** to transmit the request and receive the response start (HTTP-overhead RTT):

$$\text{HTTP RTTs} = 9R \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Total} = 9R + 9R = 18R$$

**HTTP/1.1 (Persistent):**

$$\text{TCP Handshake} = 1R \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{HTTP RTTs} = 9R \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Total} = 1R + 9R = 10R$$

**Savings:**

$$\Delta RTT = 18R - 10R = 8R \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Savings} = \frac{8R}{18R} \times 100\% \approx 44.4\%$$

**Interpretation:** Persistent connections eliminate **8 redundant TCP handshakes**, reducing total latency by roughly **44%** for this 9-object page. This is precisely why HTTP/1.1 made persistent connections the default.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End HTTP Request-Response Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Browser URL Bar: User Types Address] --> B[Step 1: Parse URL]
    B --> C[Step 2: DNS Lookup]
    C --> D[Step 3: TCP Three-Way Handshake Port 80 or 443]
    D --> E[Step 4: Send HTTP Request Message]
    E --> F[Step 5: Web Server Receives and Routes Request]
    F --> G[Step 6: Application Logic Executes]
    G --> H[Step 7: Generate HTTP Response Message]
    H --> I[Step 8: Send Response to Client]
    I --> J[Step 9: Browser Renders Response Body]
    J --> K{Connection Keep-Alive?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Reuse TCP Connection for Next Request]
    K -- No --> M[Step 10: TCP FIN Teardown]
    L --> E
    M --> N[End of Transaction]
```

### 4.2 HTTP Request vs Response Message Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REQ[HTTP REQUEST MESSAGE]
        R1[Request Line: METHOD URI HTTP/Version]
        R2[Header Block: Host, User-Agent, Accept, Cookie, Authorization]
        R3[CRLF Blank Line Separator]
        R4[Optional Message Body: Form Data, JSON Payload]
    end
    subgraph RES[HTTP RESPONSE MESSAGE]
        S1[Status Line: HTTP/Version Code Phrase]
        S2[Header Block: Server, Content-Type, Set-Cookie, Date]
        S3[CRLF Blank Line Separator]
        S4[Message Body: HTML, JSON, Image, Binary Stream]
    end
    REQ -->|Sent Over TCP Socket| ServerNode[Web Server]
    ServerNode -->|Processed and Responded| RES
```

### 4.3 HTTP vs HTTPS — Layered Security Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HTTPLAYERS[HTTP Stack Insecure]
        H1[Application Layer: HTTP Semantics]
        H2[Transport Layer: TCP]
        H3[Network Layer: IP]
        H4[Eavesdropper Can Read All Bytes in Cleartext]
    end
    subgraph HTTPSLAYERS[HTTPS Stack Secure]
        S1[Application Layer: HTTP Semantics Unchanged]
        S2[Security Layer: TLS 1.3 Handshake and Record Protocol]
        S3[Transport Layer: TCP or QUIC]
        S4[Network Layer: IP]
        S5[Eavesdropper Sees Only Encrypted Ciphertext]
    end
    HTTPLAYERS -.->|Replace With| HTTPSLAYERS
```

### 4.4 HTTP Methods Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Client Wants to Perform Action on Resource] --> Q1{Action Type?}
    Q1 -- Retrieve Data --> M1[GET Method Safe and Idempotent]
    Q1 -- Create New Resource --> M2[POST Method Non-Idempotent]
    Q1 -- Replace Resource Fully --> M3[PUT Method Idempotent]
    Q1 -- Modify Resource Partially --> M4[PATCH Method Non-Idempotent]
    Q1 -- Remove Resource --> M5[DELETE Method Idempotent]
    Q1 -- Probe Metadata Only --> M6[HEAD Method Safe]
    Q1 -- Check Allowed Methods --> M7[OPTIONS Method Used in CORS Preflight]
```

### 4.5 Status Code Class Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Server Decides on Status Code] --> Y{Request Successfully Processed?}
    Y -- In Progress --> C1[1xx Informational 100 Continue]
    Y -- Yes Resource Returned --> C2[2xx Success 200 OK 201 Created 204 No Content]
    Y -- Redirect Needed --> C3[3xx Redirection 301 Moved 302 Found 304 Not Modified]
    Y -- Client Error --> C4{What Kind of Client Error?}
    C4 -- Bad Syntax --> C4a[400 Bad Request]
    C4 -- Missing Auth --> C4b[401 Unauthorized]
    C4 -- Forbidden --> C4c[403 Forbidden]
    C4 -- Not Found --> C4d[404 Not Found]
    Y -- Server Error --> C5{What Kind of Server Error?}
    C5 -- Generic Crash --> C5a[500 Internal Server Error]
    C5 -- Bad Upstream --> C5b[502 Bad Gateway]
    C5 -- Overloaded --> C5c[503 Service Unavailable]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* **Explain the structure of an HTTP request message with a suitable example. List any four commonly used HTTP request methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An HTTP request message consists of three mandatory sections and one optional section:

1. **Request Line** — Contains the method, request URI, and HTTP version. Example: `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1`.
2. **Header Block** — Zero or more `Header-Name: Header-Value` lines terminated by CRLF. Common headers include `Host`, `User-Agent`, `Accept`, `Cookie`.
3. **Empty Line (CRLF)** — A single blank line (CRLF) that separates headers from the body.
4. **Message Body** — Optional payload (used with POST, PUT, PATCH). For GET and DELETE, the body is typically empty.

**Four common HTTP methods:**

- **GET** — Retrieves a resource; safe and idempotent.
- **POST** — Submits data to be processed; not idempotent; creates resources.
- **PUT** — Replaces a resource entirely; idempotent.
- **DELETE** — Removes a resource; idempotent.

*(Full marks awarded for clear structure identification + 4 methods with one-line semantics each.)*

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]* **Differentiate between HTTP and HTTPS. Why is HTTPS considered essential for modern web applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | HTTP | HTTPS |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | HyperText Transfer Protocol | HTTP over TLS/SSL |
| Port | **80** | **443** |
| Encryption | None — plaintext | TLS 1.2/1.3 — encrypted |
| Certificate | Not required | X.509 server certificate |
| URL Scheme | `http://` | `https://` |
| Browser Indication | "Not Secure" warning | Padlock icon |

**Why HTTPS is essential:**

1. **Confidentiality** — Prevents eavesdropping of credentials, session tokens, and personal data on open networks.
2. **Integrity** — TLS MACs (or AEAD ciphers) detect any in-flight tampering by MITM attackers.
3. **Authentication** — Server certificates bind a domain name to a verified public key, mitigating impersonation.
4. **SEO & Compliance** — Google ranks HTTPS sites higher; regulations like PCI-DSS and GDPR mandate it for personal data.

*(Allocate ~1.5 marks for the comparison table and ~1.5 marks for the "why" justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Attempt Either A or B)*

*[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]*

**Q.A (a) [7 marks]** Describe the complete HTTP request-response transaction lifecycle from URL entry to page rendering. Include the role of DNS, TCP, and message structure in your answer.

**Q.A (b) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of HTTP statelessness. Discuss how cookies and sessions maintain state, and identify the security risks associated with each mechanism.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HTTP Request-Response Lifecycle [7 marks]:**

The HTTP transaction is a coordinated multi-stage process:

**Stage 1 — URL Parsing [1 mark]:**
The browser decomposes the entered URL into scheme, host, port, path, query, and fragment. The fragment is stripped before transmission.

**Stage 2 — DNS Resolution [1 mark]:**
The browser queries the system resolver → recursive resolver → authoritative nameserver to translate the hostname into an IPv4 (A record) or IPv6 (AAAA record) address. For `https://shop.ktu.edu.in`, the resolver returns the server's IP.

**Stage 3 — TCP Connection [1 mark]:**
A TCP three-way handshake (`SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`) establishes a reliable byte stream. For HTTP, the destination port is **80**; for HTTPS, it is **443**.

**Stage 4 — HTTP Request Transmission [1.5 marks]:**
The client serializes the request message:
```
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: shop.ktu.edu.in
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: text/html
Connection: keep-alive
```
The empty CRLF terminates the header section.

**Stage 5 — Server Processing & Response [1.5 marks]:**
The web server (Apache, Nginx, IIS) receives the request, routes it to the appropriate handler (e.g., `/cart/add` invokes a PHP/Python/Node controller), executes business logic, queries databases if needed, and constructs the response:
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8
Content-Length: 2048
```
followed by the HTML body.

**Stage 6 — Rendering & Connection Reuse [1 mark]:**
The browser parses the HTML, fires additional sub-requests for CSS/JS/images (reusing the persistent TCP connection thanks to HTTP/1.1's `keep-alive`), executes scripts, and paints the page.

---

**(b) HTTP Statelessness and State Management [7 marks]:**

**The Statelessness Problem [1.5 marks]:**
HTTP is inherently **stateless** — every request is independent. The server treats each request as a fresh interaction with no memory of prior requests from the same client. This is efficient for scalability but inadequate for applications requiring user identity, shopping carts, or login sessions.

**Cookies — Client-Side State [2 marks]:**
A **cookie** is a small key-value pair that the server instructs the client to store via the `Set-Cookie` response header. On subsequent requests, the browser automatically reattaches the cookie using the `Cookie` request header. Cookies typically hold a **session ID** — an opaque reference to server-side state. Secure flag attributes include `HttpOnly` (blocks JS access), `Secure` (HTTPS-only transmission), and `SameSite` (CSRF mitigation).

**Sessions — Server-Side State [1.5 marks]:**
A **session** is a server-maintained data structure (often in memory or Redis) that stores user-specific data keyed by a session ID. The session ID travels in the cookie. This decouples sensitive data from the client and allows server-side revocation.

**Security Risks [2 marks]:**
- **Session Hijacking** — Attacker steals the session ID via XSS, network sniffing (if HTTP not HTTPS), or physical access, then impersonates the victim.
- **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** — Injected JavaScript reads `document.cookie` and exfiltrates the session ID to the attacker.
- **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)** — Attacker tricks the victim's browser into sending an authenticated request to a target site using the victim's session cookie.
- **Session Fixation** — Attacker plants a known session ID into the victim's browser, then logs in using that ID.

**Mitigations:** Use `HttpOnly` + `Secure` + `SameSite=Strict` cookies, enforce HTTPS, implement CSRF tokens, and rotate session IDs after authentication.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

*[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]*

**Q.B (a) [7 marks]** Explain HTTP status codes in detail. Categorize them into 1xx, 2xx, 3xx, 4xx, and 5xx classes with at least two examples per class.

**Q.B (b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the layered architecture of HTTPS. Describe how TLS transforms HTTP into a secure protocol and list three critical security headers used in HTTPS deployments.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HTTP Status Codes [7 marks]:**

HTTP status codes are three-digit integers emitted by the server in the status line of every response. They communicate the outcome of the request to the client.

**1xx Informational [1 mark]:**
- **100 Continue** — Server acknowledges receipt of request headers; client should send the body.
- **101 Switching Protocols** — Server agrees to upgrade the connection (e.g., to WebSocket).

**2xx Success [1.5 marks]:**
- **200 OK** — Standard success response; the requested resource is in the body.
- **201 Created** — A new resource has been successfully created (typically after POST).
- **204 No Content** — Success but no body to return (common after DELETE).

**3xx Redirection [1.5 marks]:**
- **301 Moved Permanently** — Resource has permanently relocated; the new URL is in `Location` header. Browsers cache this.
- **302 Found** — Temporary redirect; the client should follow the new URL for this request only.
- **304 Not Modified** — Client may use its cached copy; no body is sent.

**4xx Client Error [1.5 marks]:**
- **400 Bad Request** — Malformed syntax; server cannot parse.
- **401 Unauthorized** — Authentication required or failed.
- **403 Forbidden** — Server understood but refuses to authorize.
- **404 Not Found** — Resource does not exist at the given URI.

**5xx Server Error [1.5 marks]:**
- **500 Internal Server Error** — Generic server-side failure (unhandled exception).
- **502 Bad Gateway** — Upstream server returned an invalid response.
- **503 Service Unavailable** — Server is overloaded or under maintenance; `Retry-After` header may indicate downtime.

---

**(b) HTTPS Layered Architecture & Security Headers [7 marks]:**

**HTTPS = HTTP + TLS.** The application semantics of HTTP are preserved; TLS operates as a sub-layer between HTTP and TCP, transforming the cleartext byte stream into an authenticated, encrypted channel.

**Layered Architecture [3 marks]:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Layer 7 (Application)} &\rightarrow \text{HTTP Semantics (Methods, Headers, Bodies)} \\
\text{Layer 6 (Presentation)} &\rightarrow \text{TLS 1.3 Record Protocol (Encryption, MAC, AEAD)} \\
\text{Layer 5/4 (Transport)} &\rightarrow \text{TCP (Reliability) — or QUIC for HTTP/3} \\
\text{Layer 3 (Network)} &\rightarrow \text{IP (Routing)}
\end{aligned}$$

**TLS Transformation Steps [2 marks]:**
1. **Handshake** — Client and server negotiate cipher suite, exchange certificates, and establish shared session keys (ECDHE for forward secrecy).
2. **Record Protocol** — Every HTTP byte is fragmented, authenticated (HMAC or AEAD tag), and encrypted (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) before transmission.
3. **Result** — Wire traffic is now opaque ciphertext to any eavesdropper, and any in-flight tampering invalidates the AEAD tag and causes connection termination.

**Three Critical Security Headers [2 marks]:**
- **Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS)** — `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload` — Forces browsers to use HTTPS only, preventing protocol downgrade attacks.
- **Content-Security-Policy (CSP)** — `Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://cdn.ktu.edu.in` — Restricts sources of executable scripts, mitigating XSS.
- **Set-Cookie Hardening** — `Set-Cookie: sessionid=abc; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict` — Prevents client-side script access, mandates HTTPS, and blocks cross-site cookie transmission.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Module 4:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HTTP with HTTPS** — Writing "HTTPS uses a different protocol than HTTP." It does not. HTTPS *is* HTTP — it just rides over TLS. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the Fragment Rule** — Claiming the URL fragment (`#anchor`) is sent to the server. It is **never sent** — it is purely client-side.
> 3. **Mixing Idempotency and Safety** — Saying "GET is safe but not idempotent." GET is **both** safe *and* idempotent. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Omitting TCP and DNS** — When asked about the HTTP transaction, students often jump straight to "the browser sends a request." Examiners expect DNS resolution + TCP handshake **before** the HTTP request is mentioned. Missing these costs ~2 marks.
> 5. **Treating Status Codes as Text** — Students write "the server returns OK" instead of `200 OK`. Always quote the numeric code with its phrase.
> 6. **Skipping the Empty Line in Message Structure** — The CRLF separator between headers and body is **mandatory**. Diagrams that omit it are marked down.
> 7. **Forgetting Security Headers** — A Part B question on HTTP message structure that does *not* mention `Strict-Transport-Security`, `Content-Security-Policy`, or cookie security flags will be flagged as incomplete by KTU examiners expecting Module 4 security integration.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **HTTP** is an **application-layer, stateless, request-response** protocol (RFC 9110), default port **80**, cleartext.
- **HTTPS** = HTTP + **TLS**, default port **443**, encrypted with AEAD ciphers.
- **HTTP Message = Status/Request Line + Headers + CRLF + (Optional) Body.**
- **Methods**: `GET` (safe + idempotent), `POST` (non-idempotent), `PUT` (idempotent), `DELETE` (idempotent), `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`, `PATCH`.
- **Status Codes**: 1xx informational → 2xx success → 3xx redirect → 4xx client error → 5xx server error.
- **Statelessness** is patched via **cookies + sessions + tokens**, but each introduces attack surfaces (hijacking, XSS, CSRF, fixation).
- **HTTP/1.0** = non-persistent (1 connection per object); **HTTP/1.1** = persistent (default keep-alive); **HTTP/2** = binary multiplexing; **HTTP/3** = QUIC over UDP.
- **Security Headers to memorize**: `Strict-Transport-Security`, `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Referrer-Policy`, plus cookie flags `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite`.
- **URL Fragment (`#...`) is NEVER sent to the server** — purely client-side.
- **TCP handshake (3 packets) precedes HTTP** — students must mention DNS + TCP in any transaction-level question.
- **HTTPS does not change HTTP semantics** — only the transport security layer changes.
- **KTU High-Yield Triad** for Module 4: HTTP architecture → TLS upgrade → Security headers + cookie hardening.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Server and Client Contents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY > - **Module:** Module Module-4 > - **Topic:** Server and Client Contents # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Server-Side Content — Formal Academic Definition **Server Content** refers to all assets, code, data, and services that reside, execute, and are stored on the **web/application server** (and connected back-end infrastructure such as databases, file systems, and mail servers). The client (browser) requests these resources, and the server processes, generates, and transmits the response. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, server content typically includes: - **Static Web Files:** HTML pages, CSS files, images, downloadable documents. - **Server-Side Scripts:** PHP, Python (Django/Flask), Java (JSP/Servlets), ASP.NET, Node.js, Ruby on Rails. - **Back-End Databases:** MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, MongoDB, Redis. - **Server Configuration Data:** `.htaccess` files, web server configuration (Apache, Nginx, IIS), environment variables. - **Web Services & APIs:** REST endpoints, SOAP services, GraphQL resolvers. - **Logs and Session Stores:** Server access logs, authentication tokens, session files. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The server is the **trust anchor** of the web application. Every vulnerability that exposes server content (source code, credentials, configuration, or database tables) leads to **total compromise** of confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the **CIA triad**). ## 1.2 Client-Side Content — Formal Academic Definition **Client Content** refers to all assets, code, and data that are **downloaded to and executed within the user's browser** (or mobile application). The client renders this content, executes scripts, stores local data, and sends requests back to the server. Client content includes: - **HTML, CSS, and DOM:** The rendered Document Object Model. - **Client-Side Scripts:** JavaScript (ES6+), TypeScript, WebAssembly (Wasm), VBScript (legacy). - **Browser Storage:** Cookies, `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, IndexedDB, Web SQL. - **Embedded Objects:** Java applets, Flash (deprecated), ActiveX, PDF readers, Silverlight. - **Browser Metadata:** User-Agent string, referrer, IP address, installed plugins, screen resolution, geolocation. - **Third-Party Libraries:** jQuery, React, Angular, Vue.js loaded from CDNs. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Model Imagine a **restaurant** (the web application): - **Server Content = The Kitchen and Pantry** 🍳 The kitchen has recipes (source code), the pantry has ingredients (database), and the head chef (server process) prepares dishes. Customers **never enter the kitchen**. If a customer sneaks in and steals the secret recipe book, the entire restaurant is compromised. This is why we lock the kitchen door (firewall, authentication) and keep recipes in a safe (encryption at rest). - **Client Content = The Dining Table and Menu** 🍽️ The menu (HTML) is handed to the customer, who reads it, makes selections, and sends the order back (form submission, AJAX). A malicious customer could tamper with the menu (XSS), forge a fake order (CSRF), or peek at what the next table ordered (session hijacking via cookies). The restaurant must therefore sanitize the menu before printing it (input validation, output encoding). > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** The server is **trusted** but the client is **hostile**. This fundamental asymmetry is the root cause of nearly every web application vulnerability. ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations - **Module 4 Weightage:** Web security constitutes a high-weightage module in KTU's Network and System Security course. Server/Client content differentiation is the **foundation** upon which all subsequent attacks (SQLi, XSS, CSRF) are built. - **Industry Relevance:** According to **OWASP Top 10 (2021)**, **Injection** (A03) and **Cross-Site Scripting** (A03) — both server and client content attacks — remain top critical risks. - **Exam Pattern:** Direct 3-mark questions ask for definitions; 14-mark questions require comparative analysis of server vs. client vulnerabilities with attack–defense mapping. - **Bloom's Taxonomy Level:** This topic tests **Remember (L1)**, **Understand (L2)**, and **Apply (L3)** cognitive levels as per KTU 2024 OBE norms. ## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in This Topic (Highlighted for Exams) | Metric | Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **HTTP Port (Plaintext)** | **80** | Default unencrypted web traffic | | **HTTPS Port (Encrypted)** | **443** | TLS/SSL secured web traffic | | **Cookie Size Limit** | **4096 bytes** | Maximum per cookie in browsers | | **URL Maximum Length** | **~2048 characters** | Practical browser limit | | **Same-Origin Policy** | **Scheme + Host + Port** | Triplet that defines origin | | **HTTP Methods** | **GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, OPTIONS, HEAD** | RESTful verbs | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Request–Response Cycle The interaction between server and client content follows a strict **stateless protocol** (HTTP) layered over a stateful transport (TCP). Let us break this down step-by-step: ### Step 1: User Initiates Request The user types a URL or clicks a link. The browser resolves the domain (via **DNS**), opens a **TCP connection** on port 80/443, and sends an **HTTP request** containing: - **Request Line:** `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1` - **Headers:** `Host`, `User-Agent`, `Cookie`, `Accept`, `Referer` - **Body (optional):** Form data, JSON payload, file upload ### Step 2: Server Processes Request The web server (Apache/Nginx/IIS) receives the request, routes it to the appropriate handler (PHP engine, Java servlet, Python WSGI app), and the server-side script: 1. Reads input parameters from the request 2. Queries the database if needed 3. Applies business logic 4. Generates a response (usually HTML + JSON) ### Step 3: Response Transmission The server returns an **HTTP response**: - **Status Line:** `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` - **Headers:** `Content-Type`, `Set-Cookie`, `Cache-Control`, `Content-Security-Policy` - **Body:** HTML, CSS, JavaScript, JSON, XML, binary file ### Step 4: Client Renders and Executes The browser parses HTML, builds the **DOM tree**, fetches referenced resources (CSS, images, scripts), and executes JavaScript. Client-side scripts may: - Modify the DOM dynamically - Make asynchronous calls (AJAX, Fetch API, WebSockets) - Store data in browser storage - Send data back to the server ## 2.2 Why Server and Client Contents Behave Differently | Aspect | Server Content | Client Content | |---|---|---| | **Execution Environment** | Controlled, trusted, hardened OS | Untrusted, user-controlled browser | | **Visibility to Attacker** | Hidden (black box) | Fully visible (white box) | | **Mutability** | Immutable (read-only to client) | Mutable (DOM can be modified) | | **Data Sensitivity** | High (credentials, keys, DB) | Low–Medium (tokens, UI state) | | **Attack Surface** | Input validation, code execution, DB queries | Script injection, DOM manipulation, storage theft | | **Defense Posture** | Authentication, authorization, WAF | Output encoding, CSP, Same-Origin Policy | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates all critical concepts, mathematical relationships, and boundary conditions for this topic. **Memorize this table for KTU exams.** | Concept | Formula / Definition | Boundary / Limit | Unit / Context | |---|---|---|---| | **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** | $O = (S, H, P)$ where $S$=scheme, $H$=host, $P$=port | All three must match | Web security boundary | | **CORS Preflight Check** | $\text{Allowed} \iff S_{\text{req}} \subseteq S_{\text{allow}}$ | Whitelist of origins | Cross-origin requests | | **Base64 Encoding Size** | $L_{\text{encoded}} = \lceil \frac{4 \cdot L_{\text{input}}}{3} \rceil$ | $L_{\text{encoded}} \geq L_{\text{input}}$ | Bytes | | **Cookie Size** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert C_i \vert \leq 4096$ | 4096 bytes per cookie | Bytes | | **URL Encoding** | $\text{reserved} \mapsto \%\text{HH}$ | ASCII printable only | URL syntax | | **Hash Function Security** | $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ | $n \geq 256$ (SHA-256) | Bits | | **HMAC Verification** | $\text{Valid} \iff \text{HMAC}(K, M) = T_{\text{received}}$ | Constant-time compare | Authentication | | **CSP Directive** | $\text{Policy} = \sum_{i} d_i$ | Whitelist source lists | Header value | | **SQL Injection Risk** | $\text{Risk} = \frac{\vert \text{Unsanitized Inputs} \vert}{\vert \text{Total Inputs} \vert}$ | $0 \leq \text{Risk} \leq 1$ | Probability | | **XSS Payload Execution** | $P_{\text{XSS}} = 1 - P_{\text{sanitization}}$ | $0 \leq P \leq 1$ | Probability | | **CSRF Token Entropy** | $E = \log_2(N)$ | $E \geq 128$ bits | Bits | | **HTTP Request Size** | $S_{\text{req}} = L_{\text{headers}} + L_{\text{body}}$ | Typically $S_{\text{req}} \leq 8 \text{ KB}$ (default) | Kilobytes | | **SSL/TLS Handshake RTT** | $T_{\text{total}} = 2 \times RTT + T_{\text{crypto}}$ | TLS 1.3: 1-RTT, TLS 1.2: 2-RTT | Milliseconds | | **Password Hash Strength** | $W = L \times \log_2(C)$ | $L$=length, $C$=charset size | Bits | | **Session Timeout** | $T_{\text{expire}} = T_{\text{last\_access}} + \Delta t$ | $\Delta t \leq 30$ min (typical) | Minutes | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Understanding server and client content separation is **not merely academic** — it drives real engineering decisions: 1. **DevSecOps Pipelines:** Modern CI/CD tools (Jenkins, GitHub Actions) scan server-side code with **SAST** (Static Application Security Testing) and client-side code with **DAST** (Dynamic Application Security Testing) separately. 2. **Content Delivery Networks (CDNs):** Services like Cloudflare and Akamai cache **client-facing static content** at edge servers globally, while server-side dynamic content remains at the origin — a direct exploitation of the server/client distinction. 3. **Single Page Applications (SPAs):** React, Angular, and Vue.js blur the line by moving logic to the client. Security teams must re-evaluate threat models because **business logic now runs in the hostile browser environment**. 4. **Mobile and IoT:** Native mobile apps act as "thick clients" with their own storage, certificate pinning, and local databases. The same server/client content principles apply, but with platform-specific attack vectors. 5. **Microservices Architecture:** Each microservice has its own server-side content, and inter-service communication (gRPC, REST) introduces new client-side trust boundaries that must be secured with **mutual TLS** and **JWT validation**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Same-Origin Policy Decision Algorithm The Same-Origin Policy (SOP) is the **cornerstone of browser security**. Let us formally derive the decision rule. ### Given: Two URLs: $U_1$ with components $(S_1, H_1, P_1)$ and $U_2$ with components $(S_2, H_2, P_2)$. Where: - $S$ = Scheme (http, https, ftp, file) - $H$ = Host (domain name or IP) - $P$ = Port number ### To Find: Whether a script from $U_1$ can access resources from $U_2$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: $$ \text{Step 1: Define the origin function} $$ $$ O(U) = (S(U), H(U), P(U)) $$ This function extracts the origin triplet from any given URL. $$ \text{Step 2: Define the equality relation} $$ $$ O(U_1) = O(U_2) \iff (S_1 = S_2) \land (H_1 = H_2) \land (P_1 = P_2) $$ All three components must be identical. The logical AND ($\land$) operator enforces this strict matching. $$ \text{Step 3: Define the access control decision} $$ $$ \text{Access}(U_1 \rightarrow U_2) = \begin{cases} \text{ALLOW} & \text{if } O(U_1) = O(U_2) \\ \text{DENY} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ $$ \text{Step 4: Example — Permitted Access} $$ Given $U_1 = \text{https://ktu.ac.in:443/page1.html}$ and $U_2 = \text{https://ktu.ac.in:443/page2.html}$: $$ O(U_1) = (\text{https},\ \text{ktu.ac.in},\ 443) $$ $$ O(U_2) = (\text{https},\ \text{ktu.ac.in},\ 443) $$ $$ O(U_1) = O(U_2) \implies \text{Access} = \text{ALLOW} $$ $$ \text{Step 5: Example — Denied Access (Different Port)} $$ Given $U_1 = \text{https://ktu.ac.in:443/admin}$ and $U_2 = \text{https://ktu.ac.in:8080/api}$: $$ O(U_1) = (\text{https},\ \text{k}\text{tu.ac.in},\ 443) $$ $$ O(U_2) = (\text{https},\ \text{ktu.ac.in},\ 8080) $$ $$ 443 \neq 8080 \implies O(U_1) \neq O(U_2) \implies \text{Access} = \text{DENY} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Cookie Security — The `Secure` and `HttpOnly` Flag Enforcement ### Given: A cookie $C$ with name, value, and attributes. The browser stores $C$ and decides whether to send it. ### To Find: The transmission rule for cookie $C$ given security flags. ### Derivation: $$ \text{Step 1: Define cookie transmission conditions} $$ A cookie $C$ with the `Secure` flag is transmitted only over **HTTPS**: $$ T_{\text{secure}}(C) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } \text{protocol} = \text{HTTPS} \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ $$ \text{Step 2: Define HttpOnly flag effect} $$ A cookie $C$ with the `HttpOnly` flag is **inaccessible to JavaScript**: $$ J_{\text{access}}(C) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } \text{HttpOnly} = \text{false} \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{if } \text{HttpOnly} = \text{true} \end{cases} $$ $$ \text{Step 3: Combined transmission decision} $$ $$ \text{Send}(C, \text{request}) = T_{\text{secure}}(C) \land J_{\text{allowed}}(C, \text{request}) $$ Where $J_{\text{allowed}}$ checks if the JavaScript access path is permitted. $$ \text{Step 4: Example — Secure session cookie} $$ Given a session cookie: `Set-Cookie: SESSIONID=abc123; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Strict` The browser enforces: 1. `Secure`: Only sent over HTTPS (port 443) 2. `HttpOnly`: Not accessible via `document.cookie` 3. `SameSite=Strict`: Not sent in cross-site requests $$ \text{Send}(C, \text{cross-site request}) = \text{TRUE} \land \text{TRUE} \land \text{FALSE} = \text{FALSE} $$ The cookie is **not sent** in cross-site requests, preventing CSRF attacks. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Attack Vector Mapping The following matrix maps each **attack type** to the **content layer it targets**, the **vulnerability exploited**, and the **defense mechanism**. | Attack | Target Layer | Content Exploited | Vulnerability | Defense Mechanism | |---|---|---|---|---| | **SQL Injection** | Server | Database queries, server scripts | Unsanitized input concatenation | Parameterized queries, stored procedures | | **Remote Code Execution (RCE)** | Server | Server-side scripts, OS commands | Unsafe eval, command injection | Input validation, sandboxing, least privilege | | **Path Traversal** | Server | File system | `../` sequences in file paths | Canonicalization, chroot jails | | **XXE (XML External Entity)** | Server | XML parsers | External entity references | Disable DTD processing, use JSON | | **SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery)** | Server | HTTP client libraries | Unvalidated URL fetching | Allowlist domains, disable redirects | | **Stored XSS** | Client (persistent) | Database → HTML page | Unsanitized output | Output encoding, CSP | | **Reflected XSS** | Client (transient) | URL parameters, error pages | Input reflected without encoding | Input validation, output encoding | | **DOM-Based XSS** | Client | JavaScript DOM manipulation | Unsafe innerHTML, eval() | Trusted Types, DOM sanitization | | **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)** | Client → Server | Cookies, session tokens | No anti-CSRF token | SameSite cookies, CSRF tokens, Origin header check | | **Clickjacking** | Client | iframe, UI redress | Missing X-Frame-Options | `X-Frame-Options: DENY`, CSP `frame-ancestors` | | **Session Hijacking** | Client ↔ Network | Cookies, session IDs | No Secure flag, no HTTPS | HTTPS, Secure/HttpOnly flags, session regeneration | | **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** | Network | HTTP traffic | Unencrypted communication | TLS 1.3, HSTS, certificate pinning | | **CORS Misconfiguration** | Client | Browser CORS policy | Wildcard `*` with credentials | Strict origin allowlist | | **Open Redirect** | Client | URL parameters | Unvalidated redirect targets | Allowlist redirect URIs | ## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Identifying Content Layer for an XSS Attack ### Problem: A user reports that a social media site displays their profile name as `` after they set their bio to that value. Identify the **content layer** affected and explain the **attack chain**. ### Solution: **Step 1: Identify the attack type** The malicious script is **stored** in the database and **reflected** to all visitors — this is **Stored XSS** (also called Persistent XSS). **Step 2: Trace the content flow** $$ \text{Attacker Input} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP POST}} \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SQL INSERT}} \text{Database} $$ $$ \text{Visitor Request} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP GET}} \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SQL SELECT}} \text{Database} \xrightarrow{\text{Server Script}} \text{HTML Response} \xrightarrow{\text{Browser}} \text{DOM} $$ **Step 3: Identify the vulnerable layer** The vulnerability exists at the **server-side output encoding** stage. The server retrieves the stored value from the database and **inserts it directly into HTML** without encoding: $$ \text{Vulnerable Code (PHP):} $$ ```php echo "
" . $user_bio . "
"; ``` If `$user_bio` contains ``, the browser parses and executes the script. **Step 4: Apply the fix** $$ \text{Secure Code (PHP):} $$ ```php echo "
" . htmlspecialchars($user_bio, ENT_QUOTES, 'UTF-8') . "
"; ``` The `htmlspecialchars()` function converts: - `<` → `<` - `>` → `>` - `"` → `"` - `'` → `'` The browser now renders the script as **literal text** rather than executable code. **Step 5: Defense in depth** Beyond output encoding, add multiple layers: 1. **Content Security Policy (CSP):** `Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'` 2. **HttpOnly cookies:** Prevent session theft via XSS 3. **Input validation:** Reject inputs containing ``. - **Mitigation:** Content Security Policy, Subresource Integrity (SRI), output encoding. **Type 3: Cookies** Small text files stored by the browser to maintain state. - **Security Risk:** Cookie theft via XSS, session hijacking, CSRF exploitation. - **Example Attack:** `document.cookie` accessed by injected script and exfiltrated. - **Mitigation:** Set `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, `SameSite=Strict` flags. Use JWT in `Authorization` header instead. **Type 4: Browser Storage (localStorage / sessionStorage / IndexedDB)** Web Storage APIs for persistent client-side data. - **Security Risk:** Sensitive data stored unencrypted and accessible to any JavaScript on the same origin. - **Example Attack:** XSS payload reads `localStorage.getItem('authToken')`. - **Mitigation:** Never store sensitive data in client storage. Encrypt before storage. Use sessionStorage for short-lived data. **Type 5: Browser Metadata** Information about the user's browser, OS, and environment. - **Security Risk:** Fingerprinting for tracking and targeted attacks. - **Example Attack:** Combining User-Agent, screen resolution, installed plugins to uniquely identify users. - **Mitigation:** Use Tor Browser or Brave for privacy. Server-side rate limiting based on behavior. **Type 6: Third-Party Libraries** External JavaScript libraries loaded from CDNs. - **Security Risk:** Supply chain attacks (e.g., event-stream incident in 2018). - **Example Attack:** Compromised npm package injects cryptocurrency mining code. - **Mitigation:** Subresource Integrity (SRI) hashes. Dependency scanning. Self-host critical libraries. **Type 7: Embedded Objects** PDFs, videos, iframes, and other media embedded in web pages. - **Security Risk:** Malicious PDFs exploiting viewer vulnerabilities. Clickjacking via hidden iframes. - **Mitigation:** Sandbox iframes with `sandbox` attribute. Set `X-Frame-Options: DENY`. Use Google's PDF viewer. --- **Part (b) — XSS and CSRF Attack Analysis:** **XSS Attack Diagram (Stored XSS):** ``` Attacker Server Victim's Browser | | | |--POST bio= | | |---Store in DB | | | | | |<---GET profile page-----| | | | | |---HTML with `) - Restricts script sources to same origin + whitelisted CDN - Prevents `eval()` and `new Function()` - Enforces Trusted Types for DOM manipulation **Defense in Depth Summary Table:** | Attack | SOP | CSP | SameSite Cookies | CSRF Token | Output Encoding | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Stored XSS** | Partial | ✓✓✓ | — | — | ✓✓✓ | | **Reflected XSS** | Partial | ✓✓✓ | — | — | ✓✓✓ | | **DOM XSS** | Partial | ✓✓ | — | — | ✓✓ | | **CSRF** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✗ | | **Cookie Theft** | Partial | ✓ | ✓✓ (HttpOnly) | — | — | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write "SOP prevents CSRF." This is **WRONG**. SOP prevents reading the response, but CSRF exploits the fact that cookies are **automatically sent** with cross-site requests. SOP is **insufficient** against CSRF. > > 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **output encoding** with **input validation**. Input validation rejects bad data; output encoding neutralizes bad data before rendering. Both are needed, but they serve different purposes. > > 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to mention **HTTPS/TLS** as the foundation of all web security. Without encryption, all other defenses are moot. > > 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Writing only the attack description without the **defense mechanism**. KTU awards 50% marks for attacks and 50% for defenses. > > 5. **Mark Loss Zone:** Questions on "server vs. client content" frequently lose marks because students do not provide **examples** for each type. Always give at least two concrete examples per category. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this before every exam:** ### Server-Side Content - ✅ **Definition:** Content that resides, executes, and is stored on the **web/application server**. - ✅ **Examples:** PHP scripts, Java servlets, Python WSGI apps, MySQL databases, `.htaccess` configs, server logs, REST API endpoints. - ✅ **Key Attacks:** SQL Injection, RCE, Path Traversal, XXE, SSRF, File Upload, Privilege Escalation. - ✅ **Key Defenses:** Parameterized queries, input validation, least privilege, WAF, code review, dependency scanning. - ✅ **Trust Level:** **HIGH trust** — but vulnerable to application-layer attacks. ### Client-Side Content - ✅ **Definition:** Content that is **downloaded to and executed within the user's browser**. - ✅ **Examples:** HTML DOM, JavaScript, CSS, cookies, `localStorage`, browser metadata, third-party libraries, embedded objects. - ✅ **Key Attacks:** XSS (Stored, Reflected, DOM-based), CSRF, Clickjacking, Cookie Theft, Open Redirect, DOM Manipulation. - ✅ **Key Defenses:** Output encoding, Content Security Policy (CSP), Same-Origin Policy, SameSite cookies, HttpOnly flag, Subresource Integrity (SRI). - ✅ **Trust Level:** **ZERO trust** — the browser environment is considered hostile. ### Critical Security Mechanisms (Must Memorize) - ✅ **Same-Origin Policy (SOP):** Defined by `(Scheme, Host, Port)` triplet. Enforced by **browser**. - ✅ **CORS:** Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — server-set headers to **relax** SOP for legitimate APIs. - ✅ **CSP:** Content Security Policy — restricts which resources the browser can load/execute. - ✅ **Cookie Flags:** `Secure` (HTTPS only), `HttpOnly` (no JS access), `SameSite=Strict/Lax` (cross-site control). - ✅ **TLS 1.3:** Mandatory for all web traffic. Provides encryption, authentication, and integrity. - ✅ **HSTS:** HTTP Strict Transport Security — forces HTTPS even if user types `http://`. - ✅ **Subresource Integrity (SRI):** Hash verification for third-party scripts loaded from CDNs. ### Key Differentiators (Exam Favorite) | Parameter | Server-Side | Client-Side | |---|---|---| | Execution Location | Web server | User browser | | Visibility | Hidden (black box) | Visible (white box) | | Mutability | Read-only to client | Fully modifiable | | Trust Level | Trusted | Untrusted | | Primary Language | PHP, Java, Python, C# | JavaScript, HTML, CSS | | Storage | Database, file system | Cookies, localStorage | | Attack Type | Injection, RCE | XSS, CSRF | | Defense | Input validation, WAF | Output encoding, CSP | ### Formulas to Remember - ✅ **SOP Match:** $O_1 = O_2 \iff S_1 = S_2 \land H_1 = H_2 \land P_1 = P_2$ - ✅ **Password Entropy:** $W = L \times \log_2(C)$ - ✅ **HMAC Verification:** $\text{Valid} \iff \text{HMAC}(K, M) = T_{\text{received}}$ (constant-time compare) - ✅ **CSRF Token Entropy:** $E = \log_2(N) \geq 128$ bits ### Final Exam Wisdom - 🎯 Always answer in **structured format** with headings, sub-points, and diagrams. - 🎯 For 14-mark questions, use the **"Attack → Mechanism → Impact → Defense"** framework. - 🎯 For comparison questions, always use a **markdown table**. - 🎯 Mention **OWASP Top 10** classification wherever relevant — examiners love this. - 🎯 Use **real-world examples** (e.g., 2017 Equifax breach due to Apache Struts vulnerability) to demonstrate depth of understanding. - 🎯 Always conclude with **defense in depth** — show that you understand security is layered, not a single solution.

topics.title: Attacking Web Servers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-4
> - **Topic:** Attacking Web Servers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Web Server Attacks** are a specialized class of cyberattacks that target the software, hardware, or infrastructure components of web servers (e.g., Apache, Nginx, IIS) and the web applications hosted upon them. These attacks aim to compromise the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) of the server, exfiltrate sensitive data, deface websites, or pivot deeper into the internal network. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme NEP-aligned framework, web server attacks are categorized under **application-layer threats (Layer 7 of the OSI Model)** and form a critical component of the **ethical hacking and penetration testing lifecycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module-4):** Attacking Web Servers is grouped under "Web Application Security" alongside SQL Injection and XSS. Students must understand both **server-side vulnerabilities** (covered here) and **client-side attacks**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **shopping mall** (the web server) with a **front door** (HTTP/HTTPS port 80/443), **security guards** (firewall/WAF), and **shopkeepers** (web applications). An attacker attacking the web server is like a thief who:
- Tries to **slam the door open** repeatedly (DoS/DDoS)
- **Forges a fake ID** to enter (Authentication Bypass)
- **Tricks the shopkeeper** into revealing the cash register PIN (Injection Attacks)
- **Plants a hidden camera** in the dressing room to spy on customers (XSS)
- **Breaks the front glass window** of the mall to cause panic (Buffer Overflow)

The web server is the central nervous system of any online service. If it falls, the entire business reputation, customer trust, and data integrity collapse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Clarification (Board Exam Focus):**
> - **Web Server** = Software (Apache, Nginx) + Hardware infrastructure
> - **Web Application** = The actual code/scripts (PHP, Python, Java) running ON the server
> - Attacks can target either, but "Attacking Web Servers" in KTU Module-4 focuses on **server-level** exploits.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Severity Scoring

| Metric | Standard Value | Description |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| **CVSS Score Range** | **0.0 – 10.0** | Common Vulnerability Scoring System severity |
| **Critical Threshold** | **9.0 – 10.0** | Immediate patching required |
| **OWASP Top 10** | Released 2021 | Industry-standard web vulnerability ranking |
| **HTTP Default Port** | **Port 80 (HTTP)** and **Port 443 (HTTPS)** | Standard communication channels |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Web Server Attacks (KTU Board Classification)

Web server attacks are classified by the **attack vector** and the **target layer**:

#### **A. Network-Level Attacks on Web Servers**
1. **Denial of Service (DoS) Attack**
2. **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) Attack**
3. **SYN Flood Attack**
4. **Smurf Attack (ICMP Echo Reply Flood)**
5. **DNS Amplification Attack**

#### **B. Server-Software-Level Attacks**
1. **Directory Traversal Attack** (also called Path Traversal / Dot-Dot-Slash)
2. **Buffer Overflow Attack**
3. **HTTP Response Splitting**
4. **Web Server Misconfiguration Exploitation**
5. **Remote Code Execution (RCE)**

#### **C. Application-Level / File-Level Attacks**
1. **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** *(covered in adjacent topic but referenced)*
2. **SQL Injection (SQLi)** *(covered in adjacent topic but referenced)*
3. **Command Injection / OS Command Injection**
4. **Remote File Inclusion (RFI) / Local File Inclusion (LFI)**

#### **D. Authentication & Session Attacks**
1. **Brute Force / Dictionary Attack** on admin panels
2. **Session Hijacking** (stealing cookies via XSS)
3. **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)** on HTTP (non-TLS)

### 2.2 Detailed Mechanism: The "Why and How" of Each Major Attack

#### **2.2.1 DoS vs. DDoS Attack**

**Why it works:** A web server has finite resources (CPU, RAM, bandwidth, socket pool). The attacker's goal is to exhaust these resources so legitimate users cannot establish connections.

**How it happens:**
- The attacker floods the server with a massive volume of HTTP requests
- The server allocates TCP sockets, memory buffers, and CPU cycles to each request
- When the limit is exceeded, the server either **crashes, hangs, or refuses new connections**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition - Direct Recall:** A **DoS attack** originates from a **single source**, while a **DDoS attack** originates from **multiple distributed sources** (botnets) making it extremely difficult to block by IP filtering alone.

#### **2.2.2 Directory Traversal Attack**

**Why it works:** Web servers often use user-supplied input to construct file paths. Poor input validation allows attackers to escape the intended directory.

**How it happens:**
- A legitimate URL: `http://victim.com/photos/sunset.jpg`
- The server translates this to: `/var/www/html/photos/sunset.jpg`
- The attacker injects: `http://victim.com/photos/../../../etc/passwd`
- The server resolves to: `/etc/passwd` (Linux password file)
- Result: Sensitive system files are exposed

The special sequence `../` is called **"dot-dot-slash"** and is the cornerstone of this attack.

#### **2.2.3 Buffer Overflow Attack**

**Why it works:** Programs allocate fixed-size memory buffers. If input exceeds the buffer size without bounds checking, it overwrites adjacent memory.

**How it happens:**
- A web form expects a username of max 20 characters
- The attacker sends a payload of 2000 characters
- The excess data overwrites the **return address** in the stack
- The processor jumps execution to attacker-supplied code (called **shellcode**)
- Result: **Remote Code Execution (RCE)** with server privileges

#### **2.2.4 DNS Amplification Attack (Reflection-Based DDoS)**

**Why it works:** The DNS protocol is connectionless (uses UDP), and DNS responses can be up to **~50x larger** than the query. Attackers spoof the victim's IP as the source and ask many open DNS servers to "amplify" the response toward the victim.

**How it happens:**
- Attacker sends **small DNS query** (60 bytes) to thousands of open resolvers
- Source IP is **spoofed** to be the victim's IP
- Each resolver sends a **large DNS response** to the victim
- The victim's bandwidth is saturated by traffic they never requested

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **For Module-4 (Attacking Web Servers), most questions are qualitative/analytical. Numerical formulas appear in DDoS sizing, amplification factor, and severity scoring.**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amplification Factor (AF)** | $AF = \dfrac{\text{Response Size (bytes)}}{\text{Request Size (bytes)}}$ | Measures DDoS amplification power | Dimensionless ratio |
| **DDoS Bandwidth Equation** | $B_{total} = N_{bots} \times B_{per\_bot}$ | Total attack bandwidth = bots × per-bot bandwidth | Gbps / Mbps |
| **Botnet Size Estimate** | $N_{bots} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{B_{per\_bot}}$ | Reverse calculation | Number of nodes |
| **CVSS Base Score (simplified)** | $Score = f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A)$ | Function of 8 base metrics (Kaspersky 2023 ref.) | 0.0 – 10.0 |
| **Buffer Overflow Offset** | $Offset = \text{Buffer\_Size} - \text{Valid\_Input\_Length}$ | Bytes needed to reach return address | Bytes |
| **TCP Three-Way Handshake** | $SYN \rightarrow SYN-ACK \rightarrow ACK$ | Connection establishment protocol | Phases |
| **SYN Flood Resource Exhaustion** | $M_{sockets} = T \times R$ | Half-open connections over time | Count |

> **Note on Constants used in Industry:**
> - **DNS Amplification Factor (typical):** 28x to 54x
> - **NTP Amplification Factor:** Up to **556x** (largest known)
> - **memcached Amplification Factor:** Up to **51,000x** (record-breaking, Cloudflare 2018)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding web server attacks is foundational for:
- **Security Engineers** designing hardened server configurations
- **DevSecOps Teams** integrating WAFs (Web Application Firewalls) like ModSecurity, Cloudflare
- **Penetration Testers** conducting authorized red-team engagements
- **Incident Response Teams** performing forensic analysis post-breach
- **Network Architects** deploying rate-limiting, load balancers, and Anycast networks

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivative 1: Calculating DDoS Attack Bandwidth (Step-by-Step)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A botnet consists of 5,000 compromised IoT devices. Each device generates 2 Mbps of attack traffic. Calculate the total DDoS bandwidth targeting a web server.

**Step 1: Identify Given Parameters**

$$
N_{bots} = 5000
$$

$$
B_{per\_bot} = 2 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2: State the Governing Equation**

$$
B_{total} = N_{bots} \times B_{per\_bot}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the Values**

$$
B_{total} = 5000 \times 2 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 4: Perform the Multiplication**

$$
B_{total} = 10000 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to Standard Networking Units (Gbps)**

$$
1 \text{ Gbps} = 1000 \text{ Mbps}
$$

$$
B_{total} = \dfrac{10000}{1000} \text{ Gbps} = 10 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Final Answer:** $B_{total} = \mathbf{10 \text{ Gbps}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always state units explicitly. "Mbps" vs "MBps" vs "Gbps" is a common differentiator. **1 Byte = 8 bits**, so MBps (megabytes/sec) is 8× larger than Mbps (megabits/sec).

---

### 3.2 Derivative 2: DNS Amplification Factor Calculation

**Problem:** A DNS query sent to an open resolver is **45 bytes** in size. The corresponding DNS response is **1350 bytes**. Calculate the amplification factor.

**Step 1: Identify the Given Data**

$$
\text{Request Size} = 45 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Response Size} = 1350 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Amplification Factor Formula**

$$
AF = \dfrac{\text{Response Size}}{\text{Request Size}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$
AF = \dfrac{1350}{45}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Division**

$$
AF = 30
$$

**Step 5: Interpret the Result**

The attacker sends 1 GB of malicious DNS queries, but the victim receives 30 GB of unsolicited DNS response traffic.

$$
\text{Effective Attack Volume} = 1 \text{ GB} \times 30 = 30 \text{ GB}
$$

**Final Answer:** $AF = \mathbf{30\times}$ amplification

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Demonstrates understanding of *reflection* and *amplification* as the two distinct pillars of modern DDoS attacks. Reflection = using legitimate servers as unwitting accomplices. Amplification = exploiting protocol size asymmetry.

---

### 3.3 Derivative 3: SYN Flood Resource Exhaustion Analysis

**Problem:** A web server has a default backlog queue of 50 half-open connections. An attacker initiates 100 SYN packets per second. After how many seconds will the backlog queue be completely full, preventing new legitimate connections?

**Step 1: Identify the Variables**

$$
M_{sockets} = 50 \text{ (max backlog)}
$$

$$
R = 100 \text{ SYN/sec (attack rate)}
$$

**Step 2: Define the Time to Saturation**

$$
T_{sat} = \dfrac{M_{sockets}}{R}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$
T_{sat} = \dfrac{50}{100}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Result**

$$
T_{sat} = 0.5 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

In just half a second, the server's connection queue is exhausted. Any new legitimate user attempting to connect will time out, effectively achieving the DoS objective.

**Final Answer:** $T_{sat} = \mathbf{0.5 \text{ seconds}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing *half-open* (SYN received, ACK not yet received) with *fully-established* connections. In a SYN flood, the attacker **never sends the final ACK**, leaving the slot locked.

---

### 3.4 Derivative 4: Botnet Size Estimation (Reverse Calculation)

**Problem:** A web server has a maximum ingress bandwidth capacity of 1 Gbps (1000 Mbps). Each IoT bot can generate 250 Kbps. How many bots are needed to saturate the server?

**Step 1: Standardize Units**

$$
B_{total} = 1000 \text{ Mbps} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ Kbps}
$$

$$
B_{per\_bot} = 250 \text{ Kbps}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Inverse Botnet Equation**

$$
N_{bots} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{B_{per\_bot}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$
N_{bots} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{250}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Result**

$$
N_{bots} = 4000
$$

**Step 5: Real-World Interpretation**

A 4,000-device botnet is realistic. The **Mirai botnet (2016)** used approximately **145,000 IoT devices** to launch a 1.1 Tbps attack against Dyn DNS provider, disrupting Twitter, Netflix, and Reddit.

**Final Answer:** $N_{bots} = \mathbf{4000 \text{ devices}}$

---

### 3.5 Derivative 5: Path Traversal Depth Calculation

**Problem:** A web application is hosted in the directory `/var/www/html/app/`. The attacker wants to reach the system file `/etc/shadow`. Calculate how many `../` sequences are required.

**Step 1: Identify Current and Target Paths**

$$
\text{Current} = \text{/var/www/html/app/}
$$

$$
\text{Target} = \text{/etc/shadow}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Directory Levels to Climb Out**

The current directory has 4 levels: `var → www → html → app`

$$
\text{Levels} = 4
$$

**Step 3: Construct the Payload**

The attacker needs 4 `../` sequences to escape to the filesystem root, then descends to `/etc/shadow`:

$$
\texttt{../../../../etc/shadow}
$$

**Step 4: URL-Encoded Form (for actual HTTP transmission)**

Spaces and slashes in traversal payloads are usually URL-encoded for evasion:

$$
\texttt{..\%2F..\%2F..\%2F..\%2Fetc\%2Fshadow}
$$

**Final Answer:** $4 \times$ `../` sequences required: `../../../../etc/shadow`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Point:** Modern servers apply **path canonicalization** (resolving `..` before file access). KTU examiners often ask: *"Why does `../` fail on hardened servers?"* — Answer: because of canonicalization + chroot jails + WAF rules.

---

### 3.6 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix (Attack-Defense Mapping)

| Attack Type | OSI Layer | Server Resource Targeted | Primary Defense | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DoS / DDoS** | Layer 3-4-7 | Bandwidth, Sockets, CPU | Rate limiting, Anycast, CDN | **High** |
| **SYN Flood** | Layer 4 (TCP) | Half-open socket backlog | SYN cookies, Firewall | **High** |
| **Buffer Overflow** | Layer 7 (App) | Memory, Stack | ASLR, DEP, Stack canaries | **Medium** |
| **Directory Traversal** | Layer 7 (App) | File system | Path canonicalization, WAF | **High** |
| **RFI / LFI** | Layer 7 (App) | File system, RCE | Disable allow_url_include | **Medium** |
| **HTTP Response Splitting** | Layer 7 (App) | HTTP headers, Cache | Header sanitization | **Low** |
| **DNS Amplification** | Layer 7 (UDP/DNS) | Victim's bandwidth | BCP38, Rate limiting | **Medium** |
| **Server Misconfig** | Layer 7 (App) | Admin panels, Default creds | Hardening (CIS Benchmarks) | **High** |
| **Slowloris (Slow HTTP)** | Layer 7 (App) | Apache thread pool | ModSecurity, mod_reqtimeout | **Medium** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Anatomy of a DDoS Attack (Reflection + Amplification)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Attacker Master] -->|Spoofed IP Header with Victim IP| B[Open DNS Resolver 1]
    A -->|Spoofed IP Header with Victim IP| C[Open DNS Resolver 2]
    A -->|Spoofed IP Header with Victim IP| D[Open DNS Resolver 3]
    A -->|Spoofed IP Header with Victim IP| E[Open DNS Resolver N]
    B -->|Large DNS Response to Victim| F[Target Web Server]
    C -->|Large DNS Response to Victim| F
    D -->|Large DNS Response to Victim| F
    E -->|Large DNS Response to Victim| F
    F -->|Server Overwhelmed| G[Legitimate Users Denied Service]
    
    style A fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#ffa500,color:#000000
    style G fill:#555555,color:#ffffff
```

**Figure 4.1:** The attacker exploits open DNS resolvers as unwitting "reflectors," each amplifying a small query into a large response directed at the victim. The result is bandwidth exhaustion and service unavailability.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Directory Traversal Attack Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Browser] -->|HTTP GET Request| B[Web Server]
    B -->|Validates Input| C{Input Sanitization Check}
    C -->|Contains ../ Pattern| D[Block Request - WAF Triggered]
    C -->|Clean Input| E[Serve Requested File]
    F[Attacker] -->|Crafted URL with ../| B
    F -->|URL: ../../../../etc/passwd| B
    G[Hardened Server] -->|Path Canonicalization| H[Resolves to /var/www/html/app/../../../../etc/passwd]
    H -->|Normalized to /etc/passwd| I[Access Control Check]
    I -->|Insufficient ACL| J[File Disclosed to Attacker]
    I -->|Proper ACL| K[Access Denied - 403 Forbidden]
    
    style F fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#00aa00,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#00aa00,color:#ffffff
```

**Figure 4.2:** Path traversal flow showing how an attacker attempts to escape the web root using `../` sequences, and how proper input validation and canonicalization block the attack.

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Buffer Overflow Memory Layout & Stack Smashing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Stack Memory Layout - Before Overflow] --> B[High Address]
    B --> C[Function Arguments]
    C --> D[Return Address - 4 bytes]
    D --> E[Saved Frame Pointer - 4 bytes]
    E --> F[Local Buffer - 20 bytes]
    F --> G[Low Address - Stack Pointer]
    
    H[Stack Memory Layout - After Overflow] --> I[High Address]
    I --> J[Function Arguments]
    J --> K[Overwritten Return Address - Points to Shellcode]
    K --> L[Corrupted Frame Pointer]
    L --> M[Buffer - Filled with 2000 bytes]
    M --> N[Shellcode Injected - 20 bytes]
    N --> O[Low Address - Stack Pointer]
    
    style A fill:#aaddff,color:#000000
    style H fill:#ffaaaa,color:#000000
    style K fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
```

**Figure 4.3:** Comparison of stack memory before and after a buffer overflow, illustrating how the attacker overwrites the return address to redirect execution flow to injected shellcode.

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Defense Architecture for Web Server

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Internet Traffic] --> B[Cloud-Based DDoS Scrubbing Center]
    B -->|Malicious Traffic Dropped| C[Dropped]
    B -->|Legitimate Traffic Passes| D[CDN Edge Node]
    D --> E[Web Application Firewall - WAF]
    E -->|Inspects HTTP Headers and Body| F{Threat Detected}
    F -->|Yes - XSS, SQLi, Traversal| G[Block - Log Incident]
    F -->|No - Clean Request| H[Load Balancer]
    H --> I[Web Server 1 - Apache or Nginx]
    H --> J[Web Server 2 - Apache or Nginx]
    H --> K[Web Server N - Auto-scaling]
    I --> L[Backend Database]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    
    style C fill:#555555,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0099ff,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#ff9900,color:#000000
    style L fill:#00cc66,color:#ffffff
```

**Figure 4.4:** A multi-layered defense-in-depth architecture. Cloud scrubbing handles volumetric DDoS, WAF blocks application-layer attacks, and load balancing distributes legitimate traffic across multiple servers.

---

### 4.5 Diagram 5: SYN Flood Attack Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker
    participant Server
    participant LegitUser
    
    Note over Server: Backlog Queue Capacity: 50 slots
    
    loop Flood Phase
        Attacker->>Server: SYN (Seq=x)
        Server->>Attacker: SYN-ACK (Seq=y, Ack=x+1)
        Note over Attacker: Attacker NEVER sends ACK
        Server->>Server: Half-open slot locked
    end
    
    Attacker->>Server: 50th SYN - Queue FULL
    
    LegitUser->>Server: SYN
    Server--xLegitUser: Connection Dropped or Timeout
    Note over Server,LegitUser: DoS achieved - legitimate user cannot connect
    
    style Attacker fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style LegitUser fill:#00aa00,color:#ffffff
    style Server fill:#ffa500,color:#000000
```

**Figure 4.5:** Sequence diagram of a SYN flood. The attacker initiates thousands of SYN packets but never completes the handshake, leaving the server's backlog queue full of half-open connections that block legitimate users.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define a Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack. How does it differ from a traditional DoS attack? Mention one real-world example.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack** is a malicious attempt to disrupt the normal traffic of a targeted server, service, or network by overwhelming the target or its surrounding infrastructure with a flood of Internet traffic generated from **multiple distributed sources** (a botnet).

**Difference from DoS:**

| Parameter | DoS | DDoS |
|-----------|-----|------|
| Source Count | Single source | Multiple distributed sources |
| Mitigation Difficulty | Easy - block one IP | Hard - many IPs to block |
| Bandwidth Volume | Low to moderate | Extremely high (Tbps scale) |
| Detection | Simple | Complex (looks like normal spikes) |

**Real-World Example:** The **Mirai botnet attack (October 2016)** infected approximately 145,000 IoT devices (cameras, DVRs, routers) and launched a **1.1 Tbps** DDoS attack against Dyn DNS, causing major outages for Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and Airbnb.

---

#### **Question 2: What is a Directory Traversal Attack? Explain with an example how an attacker can use `../` to access sensitive files.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Directory Traversal Attack** (also called Path Traversal or Dot-Dot-Slash attack) is a web application vulnerability that allows an attacker to read arbitrary files on the server that are normally inaccessible, by manipulating file path references using the `../` (parent directory) sequence.

**Example:**
- **Legitimate URL:** `http://bank.com/accounts/balance.pdf`
  - Server resolves to: `/var/www/html/accounts/balance.pdf`
- **Malicious URL:** `http://bank.com/accounts/../../../../etc/passwd`
  - Server resolves to: `/etc/passwd` (Linux user account database)

By chaining `../` sequences, the attacker escapes the web root directory and accesses sensitive operating system files such as `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, configuration files, or even application source code.

**Defense:** Modern servers apply **path canonicalization** (resolving `..` before filesystem access) and deploy **Web Application Firewalls (WAF)** to detect traversal patterns.

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4 Choice]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the different types of web server attacks. Discuss the **DoS attack**, **DDoS attack**, and **DNS amplification attack** with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Buffer Overflow Attack** on a web server. How does the attacker exploit the stack memory to achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE)? Discuss at least three defense mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Q. A (a):**

Web server attacks are a broad category of cyberattacks that exploit vulnerabilities in the web server software, the operating system, or the network infrastructure supporting it. The most critical categories include:

**1. Denial of Service (DoS) Attack:**

A DoS attack aims to make a web server unavailable to its legitimate users by overwhelming it with a flood of requests or by exploiting a vulnerability that causes the server to crash.

**How it works:**
- The attacker identifies a single source IP and a vulnerability
- The attacker floods the target with HTTP requests, malformed packets, or high-volume traffic
- The server exhausts its resources (CPU, RAM, network sockets)
- Legitimate users experience slow responses or complete service unavailability

**2. Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) Attack:**

A DDoS attack is a coordinated attack using multiple compromised machines (called a **botnet**) distributed across the internet.

**How it works:**
- Attacker compromises thousands of machines (bots/zombies) using malware
- Attacker sends a single command to all bots via Command & Control (C2) server
- All bots simultaneously flood the victim with requests
- Mitigation is difficult because the traffic comes from legitimate-looking distributed sources

**3. DNS Amplification Attack:**

A reflection-based DDoS attack exploiting the connectionless UDP protocol of DNS and the size asymmetry between DNS queries and responses.

**How it works:**
- Attacker spoofs the victim's IP address as the source
- Attacker sends small DNS queries (~60 bytes) to open DNS resolvers
- Each resolver replies with a large DNS response (~1350 bytes) to the victim
- Amplification factor: **up to 54x** for DNS, **up to 51,000x** for memcached
- Victim's bandwidth is saturated with unsolicited responses

**Diagram Reference:** See Figure 4.1 in SECTION 4 for visual representation.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q. A (b):**

**Buffer Overflow Attack - Detailed Explanation:**

A buffer overflow occurs when a program writes more data into a fixed-size memory buffer than it can hold, causing the excess data to overwrite adjacent memory locations.

**Stack Memory Layout (refer to Figure 4.3):**

The stack grows from high memory addresses to low memory addresses. A typical function's stack frame contains:
- **Local variables and buffers** (low address)
- **Saved frame pointer** (EBP/RBP)
- **Return address** (EIP/RIP)
- **Function arguments** (high address)

**Attack Steps:**

1. **Reconnaissance:** Attacker identifies a vulnerable function (e.g., `strcpy()`, `gets()`) that does not check input length
2. **Payload Crafting:** Attacker creates a malicious input longer than the buffer size
3. **Memory Overwrite:** Excess bytes overwrite the saved frame pointer and **return address**
4. **Shellcode Injection:** The payload includes malicious machine code (shellcode) typically placed in the buffer itself
5. **Control Flow Hijack:** When the function returns, the CPU jumps to the attacker's shellcode address
6. **Remote Code Execution:** The shellcode executes with the web server's privileges (often SYSTEM/root)

**Three Defense Mechanisms:**

1. **Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR):** Randomizes the memory addresses of the stack, heap, and libraries, making it difficult for the attacker to predict where to jump.

2. **Data Execution Prevention (DEP) / No-Execute (NX) Bit:** Marks the stack and heap as non-executable. Even if the attacker injects shellcode, the CPU refuses to execute it.

3. **Stack Canaries (Stack Cookies):** A random value is placed between the buffer and the return address. Before the function returns, the program verifies the canary. If corrupted, the program aborts.

**Additional Defenses:** Modern compilers like GCC include `-fstack-protector`, and languages like Java, Python, and C# are inherently immune due to bounds checking.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4 Choice]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Directory Traversal Attack** in detail. Discuss how the attacker uses `../` sequences to escape the web root directory. Provide example payloads and discuss four mitigation strategies. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Describe the **SYN Flood Attack** with a neat sequence diagram. Explain how it exhausts the server's connection backlog. What is a **SYN Cookie** and how does it mitigate this attack? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Q. B (a):**

**Directory Traversal Attack - Comprehensive Explanation:**

A directory traversal (or path traversal) vulnerability allows an attacker to read arbitrary files on the web server, including application code, configuration files, and sensitive system data, by manipulating path input.

**Mechanism:**

Web applications often construct file paths using user input. For example, a "view document" feature:
- Input: `report.pdf`
- Server code: `fopen("/var/www/html/docs/" + user_input, "r")`
- Result: `/var/www/html/docs/report.pdf` ✓

**Attack:**

The attacker replaces `report.pdf` with `../../../../etc/passwd`:
- Final path: `/var/www/html/docs/../../../../etc/passwd`
- After canonicalization: `/etc/passwd`
- Server reads the system password file ❌

**Example Payloads:**

| Payload | Target File |
|---|---|
| `../../../etc/passwd` | Linux user accounts |
| `..\\..\\..\\windows\\win.ini` | Windows initialization file |
| `....//....//....//etc/passwd` | Bypass simple filter (double dot) |
| `%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fetc%2fpasswd` | URL-encoded bypass |
| `..%252f..%252f..%252fetc/passwd` | Double URL-encoded bypass |

**Four Mitigation Strategies:**

1. **Path Canonicalization:** Resolve the full path before file access and verify it begins with the expected base directory.
2. **Input Whitelisting:** Allow only alphanumeric characters and specific allowed filenames; reject all path components.
3. **Web Application Firewall (WAF):** Deploy ModSecurity or commercial WAFs with traversal signatures.
4. **Run Web Server in a Chroot Jail:** Restrict the web server process to access only a specific subtree of the filesystem.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q. B (b):**

**SYN Flood Attack - Detailed Explanation:**

The SYN flood is a Layer 4 Denial of Service attack that exploits the **TCP Three-Way Handshake** mechanism.

**Normal TCP Handshake:**
1. Client → Server: **SYN** (synchronize)
2. Server → Client: **SYN-ACK** (synchronize-acknowledge)
3. Client → Server: **ACK** (acknowledge)
4. Connection is established ✓

**Attack Mechanism:**

1. Attacker sends a flood of **SYN** packets to the target server
2. The server allocates a slot in its **backlog queue** and replies with **SYN-ACK**
3. The attacker **never sends the final ACK**
4. The half-open connection remains in the queue waiting for the ACK
