- **DNSSEC:** Cryptographically signs responses — forgery is computationally infeasible.
- **Source Port Randomization:** Increases entropy beyond 16 bits to 32+ bits.
- **0x20 Encoding (Case Randomization):** Mixes upper/lower case in the query name, adding bits of entropy.
- **Transaction ID Randomization:** Use of strong PRNGs (no sequential IDs).
- **BCP 38 / uRPF Filtering:** ISP-level filtering to prevent IP spoofing at the network edge.

---

**B. DNS Amplification Attack (Detailed):**

**Mechanism:**
This is a **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS)** attack that abuses **open recursive resolvers** and the fact that a small DNS query (e.g., 60 bytes asking for `ANY isc.org`) can elicit a **very large response** (often 4000+ bytes) — an **amplification factor of ~50–70x**.

- The attacker forges the **source IP** of the query to be the **victim's IP**.
- Sends the query to thousands of open DNS resolvers worldwide.
- Each resolver sends its large response to the **victim**, not the attacker.
- The victim's bandwidth is saturated by the combined flood of responses.

**Amplification Factor:**

$$
A_f = \frac{\text{Response Size (bytes)}}{\text{Query Size (bytes)}}
$$

For a typical `ANY` query, $A_f \approx 50$ to $70$. Combined with the multiplier of ~10,000 open resolvers, the effective amplification can reach **500,000x or more**.

**Famous Example:** The **2013 Spamhaus DDoS attack** peaked at **300 Gbps** using DNS amplification, briefly considered the largest DDoS in history.

**Countermeasures:**
- **Response Rate Limiting (RRL):** Configures resolvers to throttle identical responses to a single source.
- **Closing Open Resolvers:** Restrict recursion to authorized clients only.
- **BCP 38 / uRPF:** Network ingress filtering to block source-IP spoofing.
- **Anycast DNS Infrastructure:** Distributes attack load across many locations.
- **DNS Cookies (RFC 7873):** Adds an authentication token to prevent spoofed-source amplification.

---

**Summary of the Security Mindset:**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Describing DNS as "just a phonebook" and *forgetting* the security dimension. Module 4 of PCCCT502 is **Network and System Security** — any answer on DNS that does *not* mention cache poisoning, amplification, or DNSSEC will be **marked down by 2–3 marks** automatically.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **recursive** and **iterative** queries in resolution diagrams. Remember: **client → resolver = recursive**; **resolver → authoritative = iterative**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Stating that DNSSEC provides **confidentiality** (encryption). It does **NOT** — it only provides **authenticity and integrity** of responses. For confidentiality, use **DoH (DNS over HTTPS, RFC 8484)** or **DoT (DNS over TLS, RFC 7858)**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting the **default port numbers** — UDP/53 for queries, TCP/53 for zone transfers, TCP/853 for DoT, UDP/853 for DoT, TCP/443 for DoH. Examiners love to test these.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Writing "DNS is centralized" — this is a **fatal conceptual error** that costs 1–2 marks. DNS is **distributed and hierarchical**.

*(7 marks: 2 for listing threats + 3 for cache poisoning detail + 2 for amplification detail)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 / DNS Purpose**

- [ ] **DNS Definition:** Hierarchical, distributed, decentralized naming system; RFC 1034/1035; created by Paul Mockapetris in 1983.
- [ ] **Three Core Purposes:** Name-to-Address Resolution, Reverse Resolution (PTR), Service Discovery & Policy Publication.
- [ ] **Port Numbers:** UDP/53 (queries), TCP/53 (zone transfers, large responses), TCP/853 (DoT), TCP/443 (DoH).
- [ ] **13 Root Servers:** Letters A through M; operated by 12 organizations; anycast-replicated globally.
- [ ] **Message Size:** Legacy 512 bytes; EDNS0 raises it to 4096 bytes.
- [ ] **Max Lengths:** FQDN = 253 characters; Label = 63 characters; Labels per FQDN = 127.
- [ ] **Recursive Query:** Client → Resolver; promises to do all work.
- [ ] **Iterative Query:** Resolver → Authoritative; returns best available answer or referral.
- [ ] **Header:** 12 bytes fixed; contains ID (16-bit), flags, and 4 count fields.
- [ ] **Major Record Types:** A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, PTR, TXT, SOA, SRV, DNSKEY, RRSIG, DS.
- [ ] **SOA Fields:** Serial, Refresh, Retry, Expire, Minimum TTL.
- [ ] **DNSSEC:** Provides **authenticity and integrity** (NOT confidentiality). Uses RRSIG, DNSKEY, DS, NSEC/NSEC3. Chain of Trust starts at Root KSK trust anchor.
- [ ] **Cache Poisoning:** Race condition attack on Transaction ID + source port; mitigated by DNSSEC and entropy enhancements.
- [ ] **Kaminsky Attack (2008):** Uses random subdomains to bypass cache hits; one of the most important DNS vulnerabilities.
- [ ] **DNS Amplification:** Reflection DDoS using open resolvers; amplification factor 50–70x; mitigation via RRL, BCP 38, closed resolvers.
- [ ] **DNS Tunneling:** Data exfiltration via encoded subdomain labels; used by malware C2 channels.
- [ ] **DoH (RFC 8484):** DNS over HTTPS — port 443 — encrypts DNS queries.
- [ ] **DoT (RFC 7858):** DNS over TLS — port 853 — encrypts DNS queries.
- [ ] **Real-World Impact:** DNS is foundational to **email (MX), web (A/AAAA), CDN routing, anti-spam (SPF/DKIM/DMARC), and zero-trust security architectures.**
- [ ] **Exam Tip:** Always answer DNS questions in *both* the **functional** and **security** perspectives for full KTU marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Issues with DNS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-4
> - **Topic:** Security Issues with DNS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DNS (Domain Name System) Security Issues** refer to the set of vulnerabilities, attack vectors, and architectural weaknesses inherent in the original DNS protocol (RFC 1035) that allow malicious actors to manipulate, redirect, hijack, or disrupt the name-resolution process. Since traditional DNS lacks authentication and integrity verification, it is inherently vulnerable to spoofing, poisoning, and tunneling attacks.

The Domain Name System was originally designed in 1983 under the assumption of a **trusted, cooperative internet**. As a result, it operates predominantly over **UDP port 53** using **plaintext, unauthenticated queries and responses**, making it susceptible to a wide spectrum of cyber-attacks targeting confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Unsigned Postal System"**

Imagine the world's postal system where every letter is delivered in a **transparent glass envelope** with **no signature, no seal, and no return address verification**. Anyone — a thief, a prankster, or a spy — can:
1. Read the letter mid-transit (sniffing),
2. Change the address inside the envelope (spoofing),
3. Inject fake letters claiming to be from your bank (cache poisoning),
4. Or flood the post office with millions of letters to shut it down (DDoS).

The DNS protocol is exactly this **"unsigned postal system"**. Your computer asks, "Where is `google.com`?" and trusts whatever answer comes back first — without verifying whether the reply genuinely came from Google's authoritative server or from an impostor sitting on the wire.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **UDP Port 53** — Default port for DNS queries (stateless, connectionless).
- **TTL (Time-To-Live)** — Lifetime in seconds of a cached DNS record (typically **300 to 86400** seconds).
- **Resolver Cache** — Local temporary storage of recently resolved domain-to-IP mappings.
- **Authoritative Server** — The final, trusted source-of-truth server for a domain.
- **Recursive Resolver** — An intermediary server that performs the full lookup chain on behalf of a client.
- **DNSSEC** — **D**omain **N**ame **S**ystem **SEC**urity Extensions (RFC 4033).
- **Amplification Factor** — Ratio of response bytes to query bytes, commonly **28× to 54×** in DNS amplification DDoS.

### 1.4 Classification of DNS Security Threats

DNS threats can be categorized into three CIA-triad violation classes:

| CIA Triad Property Violated | Attack Type |
|----------------------------|-------------|
| **Integrity / Authentication** | Cache Poisoning, DNS Spoofing, Pharming, DNS Rebinding |
| **Confidentiality** | DNS Sniffing, DNS Tunneling (data exfiltration) |
| **Availability** | DNS Flood DDoS, NXDOMAIN Attack, Amplification Attack |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major DNS Security Attack Vectors

#### A. DNS Cache Poisoning (Most Critical KTU Topic)

Cache poisoning is the **injection of a fraudulent DNS record into a resolver's cache**, causing all subsequent users of that resolver to be redirected to a malicious IP.

**Operational Mechanism (Why & How):**

1. **Why it works:** Original DNS uses **16-bit Transaction IDs (TXID)** and predictable source ports. With ~65,536 possible TXIDs, brute-forcing is feasible within the narrow response window.
2. **How it proceeds:**
   - Attacker monitors the network for an outgoing DNS query (e.g., for `bank.com`).
   - Before the legitimate reply arrives, the attacker **races** the resolver by flooding fake UDP responses with guessed TXIDs.
   - If a forged packet arrives first with a matching TXID, the resolver **caches the malicious A-record**.
   - All users querying that resolver are now redirected to the attacker's server for the duration of the TTL.

#### B. DNS Spoofing (Kaminsky Attack Variant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Kaminsky Attack (2008)** elevated cache poisoning by exploiting the **bailiwick checking gap**. Instead of poisoning a single `A` record, the attacker poisons the **NS (Name Server) records** for an entire zone, gaining persistent control over all subdomains.

#### C. DNS Hijacking

- **Registrar-level hijack:** Attacker transfers domain ownership via social engineering at the registrar.
- **Router-level hijack:** Malware modifies home router DNS settings to point to rogue resolvers.
- **Local file hijack:** Malware edits the local `hosts` file to map trusted domains to malicious IPs.

#### D. DNS Tunneling

Attackers encapsulate **non-DNS data** (HTTP, SSH, command-and-control payloads) inside DNS query/response packets to bypass firewalls. Since most networks allow port 53 traffic, this serves as a covert channel.

#### E. DNS Amplification Attack (DDoS)

A reflection-based volumetric DDoS where the attacker sends **small queries (~60 bytes)** to open DNS resolvers with the **victim's IP spoofed as the source**. The resolvers reply to the victim with **large responses (~3000+ bytes)**, achieving a massive bandwidth multiplier.

#### F. DNS Rebinding Attack

The attacker controls a malicious DNS server that **alternates its responses** between the attacker's real IP and **127.0.0.1** (loopback), bypassing the **Same-Origin Policy** of browsers to access internal network devices.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Description | Typical Value |
|---------|-----------------|-------------|---------------|
| **DNS Amplification Factor** | $\displaystyle AF = \frac{S_{response}}{S_{query}}$ | Ratio of response payload size to query size | $28 \times$ to $54 \times$ |
| **DNS Transaction ID Space** | $\vert TXID \vert = 2^{16}$ | Total possible TXIDs for brute-force guessing | $65{,}536$ |
| **Birthday Attack Probability** | $\displaystyle P \approx 1 - e^{-\frac{n^2}{2N}}$ | Probability of TXID collision after $n$ attempts from $N$ space | — |
| **Successful Poisoning Attempts (Kaminsky)** | $\displaystyle n \approx \sqrt{2N \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-P}\right)}$ | Number of guesses required for success probability $P$ | $\approx 700$ for $P = 99\%$ |
| **Cache Pollution Window** | $\displaystyle T_{window} = \frac{TTL_{record}}{2}$ | Vulnerability window before cache expiry | Variable |
| **DNS Packet Size (UDP)** | $\vert P_{max} \vert \le 512$ bytes | Legacy maximum; extended via EDNS to **4096 bytes** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Table Formatting:** In all KTU board answers, write amplification factor as $AF = S_{response} / S_{query}$ using the `\vert` (or `\mid`) LaTeX delimiter to avoid markdown table parsing conflicts.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DNSSEC Implementation:** Internet registrars (Verisign, Cloudflare, Google) deploy DNSSEC to cryptographically sign zones.
- **Enterprise Defense:** Companies use **Response Rate Limiting (RRL)** to throttle DNS amplification abuse.
- **Threat Intelligence:** SIEM systems parse DNS logs for tunneling indicators (high-entropy subdomains, TXT record spikes).
- **Cybersecurity Frameworks:** DNS security is a core pillar of **NIST SP 800-81** and **MITRE ATT&CK TA0007** (Discovery tactic).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DNS Amplification Factor Calculation

Let us derive the amplification factor for a classical `ANY` query amplification attack against an open recursive resolver.

**Given:**
- Attacker query packet size (with IP spoofing headers): $S_q = 64$ bytes.
- Responder's `ANY` query response size (full zone transfer-style answer): $S_r = 3456$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Define the amplification factor equation.**

$$
AF = \frac{S_r}{S_q}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known byte values.**

$$
AF = \frac{3456 \text{ bytes}}{64 \text{ bytes}}
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the numerical evaluation.**

$$
AF = 54.0
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the effective attacker bandwidth multiplier.**

If the attacker has an uplink of $B_{attacker} = 1$ Gbps, the induced traffic toward the victim becomes:

$$
B_{victim} = B_{attacker} \times AF = 1 \text{ Gbps} \times 54.0 = 54 \text{ Gbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is precisely why the **2016 Dyn DNS attack** (Mirai botnet) reached peak volumes exceeding **1.2 Tbps** — by exploiting tens of millions of misconfigured open resolvers.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Attack-by-Attack Countermeasure Mapping

| Attack Vector | Root Cause | Countermeasure | Effectiveness |
|---------------|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| **Cache Poisoning** | Predictable TXID, unauthenticated UDP | **DNSSEC + Source Port Randomization (RFC 5452)** | High |
| **Kaminsky Attack** | Bailiwick gap in BIND versions < 9.5 | **0x20-bit encoding + DNSSEC** | Very High |
| **DNS Hijacking** | Weak registrar authentication | **Registrar locks (ClientTransferProhibited)** | High |
| **DNS Tunneling** | Permissive outbound port 53 | **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI), block TXT queries** | Medium |
| **Amplification DDoS** | Open resolvers, IP spoofing | **RRL + BCP 38 (Ingress Filtering)** | High |
| **DNS Rebinding** | Weak DNS TTLs, browser SOP gaps | **DNS pinning, firewall segmentation** | Medium |
| **Pharming** | Hosts file tampering, router compromise | **DNSSEC + endpoint EDR** | High |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: DNSSEC Chain of Trust Verification

The DNSSEC validation process establishes cryptographic trust from the **root zone** down to the queried domain.

**Step 1 — Trust Anchor Initialization.**

The resolver is configured with the **Root Zone KSK (Key Signing Key)** as the trust anchor.

$$
KSK_{root} = \text{public key } P_{root}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify the Root Zone's DS Record.**

The root zone signs the **DS (Delegation Signer)** record of the TLD (e.g., `.com`). The resolver verifies:

$$
\text{Verify}(DS_{com}, KSK_{root}) \rightarrow \text{Valid?}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the TLD's DNSKEY.**

The TLD's `DNSKEY` record must be signed by its `KSK`, producing a `RRSIG` (Resource Record Signature):

$$
\text{Verify}(RRSIG_{com}, KSK_{com}) \rightarrow \text{Valid?}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Authoritative Zone's Records.**

The authoritative server for `bank.com` returns its `A` record with a corresponding `RRSIG`, signed by its `ZSK (Zone Signing Key)`:

$$
\text{Verify}(RRSIG_{A}, ZSK_{bank.com}) \rightarrow \text{Valid?}
$$

**Step 5 — Build the Chain.**

A successful verification chain mathematically looks like:

$$
KSK_{root} \xrightarrow{\text{signs}} DS_{com} \xrightarrow{\text{signs}} DNSKEY_{com} \xrightarrow{\text{signs}} RRSIG_{bank.com}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **End-to-end verification establishes authenticity and integrity** for every record in the chain. Note that DNSSEC does **NOT** provide **confidentiality** — DNS queries remain visible in plaintext.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Birthday Attack on DNS TXID

Cache poisoning is essentially a **birthday problem**. The probability of collision after $n$ guesses in a space of $N = 2^{16}$ TXIDs is:

$$
P(n) \approx 1 - e^{-\frac{n^2}{2N}}
$$

**Step 1 — For $P = 0.5$ (50% success), solve for $n$:**

$$
0.5 = 1 - e^{-\frac{n^2}{2 \times 65536}}
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the exponential term:**

$$
e^{-\frac{n^2}{131072}} = 0.5
$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm:**

$$
-\frac{n^2}{131072} = \ln(0.5) = -0.6931
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$:**

$$
n^2 = 131072 \times 0.6931 = 90{,}862.2
$$

$$
n \approx 301.4
$$

**Conclusion:** Only **~301 random TXID guesses** are needed to have a 50% chance of poisoning a DNS resolver with a 16-bit TXID space — a trivially feasible attack.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DNS Cache Poisoning Attack Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Victim Client] -->|"Q: bank.com A?"| B[Recursive Resolver]
    B -->|"Forward Query"| C[Authoritative NS Bank.com]
    R[Attacker Sniffer] -.->|"Monitor UDP 53 traffic"| B
    R ==>|"FORGED packet: guessed TXID, fake IP 6.6.6.6"| B
    C -->|"LEGITIMATE response TXID=48291 IP=1.2.3.4"| B
    B -->|"First valid TXID wins!"| D{Resolver Race Condition}
    D -->|"Attacker wins race"| E["Cache: bank.com -> 6.6.6.6 (POISONED)"]
    D -->|"Legitimate wins"| F["Cache: bank.com -> 1.2.3.4 (SAFE)"]
    E --> G[All subsequent users redirected to attacker]
    F --> H[Normal browsing continues]
```

### 4.2 DNSSEC Chain of Trust Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root["Root Zone KSK Trust Anchor"] -->|"Signs via DS"| TLD["COM TLD KSK"]
    TLD -->|"Signs via DS"| Bank["bank.com Zone KSK"]
    Bank -->|"Signs via ZSK"| ARec["A Record of bank.com"]
    ARec -->|"Attached"| RRSIG["RRSIG Digital Signature"]
    RRSIG -->|"Validated against"| PubKey["Public Key from DNSKEY"]
    PubKey -->|"Matches parent DS?"| Trust["Chain of Trust VERIFIED"]
```

### 4.3 DNS Amplification DDoS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Attacker["Attacker Botnet"] -->|"Spoofed Source = Victim IP"| Open1[Open Resolver A]
    Attacker -->|"Spoofed Source = Victim IP"| Open2[Open Resolver B]
    Attacker -->|"Spoofed Source = Victim IP"| Open3[Open Resolver C]
    Open1 -->|"Large Response 3000+ bytes"| Victim
    Open2 -->|"Large Response 3000+ bytes"| Victim
    Open3 -->|"Large Response 3000+ bytes"| Victim
    Victim["Victim Server / Target"] -->|"Service Down"| Outage[Outage State]
```

### 4.4 DNS Tunneling Exfiltration Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Mal["Malware on Internal Host"] -->|"Encoded data in subdomain: aGVsbG8.badsite.com"| DNS1[Internal Recursive Resolver]
    DNS1 -->|"Query forwarded"| DNS2[Authoritative for badsite.com]
    DNS2 -->|"Attacker-controlled NS"| AttackerNS[Attacker Nameserver]
    AttackerNS -->|"Decodes TXT response"| C2[Command and Control Server]
    C2 -.->|"New commands in TXT record"| DNS2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024]**
>
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **DNS Cache Poisoning** and **DNS Hijacking**. Which attack persists longer and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DNS Cache Poisoning | DNS Hijacking |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Attack Surface** | Recursive resolver's cache | Registrar account / router DNS settings |
| **Persistence** | Lasts only until TTL expiry | Permanent until manually reverted |
| **Scope** | Affects all users of the poisoned resolver | Affects specific victim(s) |
| **Mechanism** | Forged UDP response injection | Social engineering or malware |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Harder (within network traffic) | Easier (visible in DNS config) |

**DNS Hijacking persists longer** because once the attacker's nameservers are configured at the registrar or router level, the malicious redirection continues **until the user manually intervenes**, whereas cache poisoning expires automatically with the TTL (typically 300–86400 seconds).

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
>
> **Q2.** What is the **DNS amplification factor**, and what is its significance in volumetric DDoS attacks?

**Model Answer:**

The **DNS amplification factor** is defined as the ratio of the response packet size to the request packet size:

$$
AF = \frac{S_{response}}{S_{query}}
$$

Its significance in DDoS is that a small query (≈64 bytes) can generate a massive response (≈3000 bytes), achieving an amplification factor of **28× to 54×**. When multiplied across millions of open resolvers, a modest attacker botnet of 1 Gbps can induce **tens of Gbps** of traffic against a victim, easily saturating links and causing service outages. This is why DNS amplification remains a top-tier DDoS vector despite being decades old.

---

### Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of the DNS protocol** and identify **three fundamental design weaknesses** that make it vulnerable to attacks. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **step-by-step execution of a Kaminsky DNS Cache Poisoning attack**. How does DNSSEC mitigate this attack? (7 marks)

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is **DNS Tunneling**? Explain its working mechanism, the data encoding process, and list four detection indicators used by enterprise SOCs. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the **DNS amplification factor** for a scenario where the attacker sends 1500 spoofed `ANY` queries of 60 bytes each to an open resolver that returns 3300-byte responses. If the attacker has 500 Mbps uplink capacity, what is the peak DDoS bandwidth hitting the victim? Suggest two countermeasures. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solutions

#### Question A — Solution

**(a) DNS Architecture & Design Weaknesses (7 marks)**

The DNS hierarchy consists of:

1. **Root Servers** (13 logical, ~1500+ anycast instances).
2. **TLD Servers** (`.com`, `.org`, country-code TLDs).
3. **Authoritative Name Servers** (final source of truth for a zone).
4. **Recursive Resolvers** (intermediaries that perform lookups on behalf of clients).

**Three fundamental design weaknesses:**

1. **Lack of Authentication:** DNS responses are not cryptographically signed. Any host can claim to be the authoritative server.
2. **Predictable Transaction IDs:** Original 16-bit TXIDs are too small (65,536 possibilities) and not sufficiently random, enabling birthday attacks.
3. **UDP-Based Connectionless Transport:** UDP provides no handshake, making source-IP spoofing trivial — the foundation of amplification DDoS.

**(b) Kaminsky Attack & DNSSEC Mitigation (7 marks)**

```
Step 1: Attacker queries resolver for a NON-EXISTENT random
        subdomain like "xyz123.bank.com".
Step 2: Resolver forwards query to authoritative server.
Step 3: Attacker FLOODS resolver with forged responses that
        include an AUTHORITATIVE NS record pointing to
        attacker's malicious server (e.g., ns1.evil.com).
Step 4: If a forged response's TXID matches the resolver's
        pending query, the NS record is cached.
Step 5: Now EVERY future lookup for *.bank.com is sent to
        attacker's server — giving FULL control of the zone.
```

**Why it is devastating:** Unlike traditional cache poisoning, the attacker doesn't wait for a specific record to expire; they can keep poisoning rapidly until success.

**DNSSEC Mitigation:**

DNSSEC cryptographically signs every record using public-key cryptography. Each zone's `DNSKEY` is authenticated by its parent's `DS` record, forming an unbroken **chain of trust** from the root KSK. Since forged responses lack a valid `RRSIG`, the resolver rejects them outright. This eliminates the Kaminsky attack vector because **authentication replaces guesswork**.

---

#### Question B — Solution

**(a) DNS Tunneling (7 marks)**

DNS tunneling encapsulates non-DNS protocols (HTTP, SSH, TCP) within DNS query/response packets to bypass network firewalls and exfiltrate data covertly.

**Working Mechanism:**
1. The attacker registers a domain (e.g., `evilcorp.com`) and configures its authoritative NS to point to a malicious server.
2. Malware on the victim's host encodes data (e.g., `aGVsbG8=` for "hello") into the **subdomain label** of a DNS query: `aGVsbG8.evilcorp.com`.
3. The internal recursive resolver, unaware of malicious intent, forwards the query to `evilcorp.com`'s NS.
4. The attacker's server decodes the subdomain, executes commands, and returns the result inside a **TXT record**.
5. This bidirectional channel persists even across strict corporate firewalls because **port 53 is almost always open**.

**Four SOC Detection Indicators:**

1. **High-entropy subdomain labels** (e.g., `a8f3e9c2b1.evilcorp.com`).
2. **Unusual TXT record query volume** from a single host.
3. **Long DNS queries** exceeding typical hostname lengths (>50 characters).
4. **Anomalous outbound traffic to non-corporate DNS servers** (e.g., direct queries to 8.8.8.8 from internal hosts).

**(b) Amplification Factor Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Query size: $S_q = 60$ bytes
- Response size: $S_r = 3300$ bytes
- Number of queries: $n = 1500$
- Attacker bandwidth: $B_{att} = 500$ Mbps

**Step 1 — Compute Amplification Factor:**

$$
AF = \frac{S_r}{S_q} = \frac{3300}{60} = 55
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Effective Bandwidth at the Responder:**

$$
B_{resp} = n \times S_r \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 1500 \times 3300 \times 8 = 39{,}600{,}000 \text{ bits/query burst}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Sustained DDoS Bandwidth at the Victim:**

Since each attacker's query produces a 55× larger response:

$$
B_{victim} = B_{attacker} \times AF = 500 \text{ Mbps} \times 55 = 27{,}500 \text{ Mbps} \approx 27.5 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Two Countermeasures:**

1. **Source IP Verification (BCP 38 / RFC 2827):** ISPs should perform ingress filtering to drop packets with spoofed source addresses at the network edge.
2. **Response Rate Limiting (RRL):** Configure authoritative resolvers to send identical responses to a single source at a throttled rate, capping amplification potential.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing "DNS Spoofing" with "DNS Cache Poisoning":** These are often used interchangeably in textbooks, but technically spoofing refers to forging a single response, while poisoning refers to the *act of caching* that spoof. Use precise terminology in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Forgetting that DNSSEC does NOT provide confidentiality:** Many students incorrectly claim DNSSEC encrypts DNS traffic. It only authenticates. Confidentiality is provided by **DoH (DNS over HTTPS)** or **DoT (DNS over TLS)**.
> 3. **Skipping the chain-of-trust explanation:** When asked about DNSSEC, you **must** draw the `KSK → DS → ZSK → RRSIG` chain to earn full marks. A bare paragraph without the hierarchy loses 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Amplification formula units:** Always show both numerator and denominator units (bytes/bytes = dimensionless) to demonstrate conceptual clarity.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- DNS originally designed in **1983 (RFC 1035)** with **no security layer**.
- DNS operates on **UDP port 53**, enabling spoofing and amplification.
- **Cache Poisoning** exploits the **16-bit TXID** space (~65,536 possibilities).
- **Kaminsky Attack (2008)** poisons the **NS records** of an entire zone.
- **Amplification Factor** $AF = S_r / S_q$, typically **28× – 54×**.
- Birthday attack probability: $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2/(2N)}$ → only **~301 guesses** needed for 50% success.
- **DNSSEC** adds **authentication + integrity** (NOT confidentiality) via **RRSIG, DNSKEY, DS, NSEC/NSEC3**.
- Chain of trust: **Root KSK → TLD DS → Zone DNSKEY → RRSIG**.
- **DNS Tunneling** uses subdomain encoding (e.g., Base32/Base64) to bypass firewalls via port 53.
- **DNS Rebinding** alternates DNS responses to bypass browser Same-Origin Policy.
- **Countermeasures:** Source port randomization, DNSSEC, RRL, BCP 38 ingress filtering, DPI on port 53.
- **DoH / DoT** (DNS over HTTPS/TLS) provide confidentiality, complementing DNSSEC's authentication.
- The **2016 Dyn Attack** peaked at **1.2 Tbps** via DNS amplification using the Mirai botnet.
- Remember to use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX for absolute values inside KTU answer scripts to avoid formatting errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DNS Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module-4
> - **Topic:** DNS Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DNS Attacks** are a class of cyberattacks that exploit vulnerabilities in the **Domain Name System (DNS)** protocol — the hierarchical, distributed naming system defined in **RFC 1034** and **RFC 1035** — to redirect users to malicious destinations, exfiltrate data, disrupt service availability, or gain unauthorized access to network resources.

The **Domain Name System** functions as the "phonebook of the Internet," translating human-readable domain names (e.g., `www.ktu.ac.in`) into machine-readable **IP addresses** (e.g., `103.241.136.10`). Because virtually every Internet transaction begins with a DNS lookup, compromising this system provides attackers with a powerful, stealthy pivot point for large-scale intrusion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Phonebook Poisoner" Analogy**
> Imagine you ask a librarian (the DNS resolver) for the address of a bank. Normally, the librarian checks the official registry and gives you the correct address. Now imagine a **fraudster (attacker)** sneaks in during lunch break and **writes a fake address in the registry (cache poisoning)**. When you arrive, you walk straight into the **fraudster's identical-looking office (phishing site)**, and hand over your money willingly. The bank's security guards never even know you were misled. That is precisely how a **DNS Spoofing/Cache Poisoning attack** works at Internet scale.

### 1.3 Why DNS is a High-Value Target

| Property | Attacker Advantage |
|----------|-------------------|
| **Trust Relationship** | DNS responses are inherently trusted by the OS and applications |
| **Ubiquity** | Every web, email, and API call triggers a DNS lookup |
| **Stateless Protocol** | Original UDP-based DNS uses **port 53** with no built-in authentication |
| **Recursive Caching** | A single poisoned record can affect millions of users (TTL-based persistence) |
| **Transparency** | Users have zero visibility into DNS lookups happening in the background |

### 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Sub-Topics Mandated by KTU 2024 (Module-4):**
> 1. DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning
> 2. DNS Hijacking (Registrar & Hosts-file based)
> 3. DNS Tunneling (Data Exfiltration & C2)
> 4. DNS Amplification & Reflection DDoS
> 5. NXDOMAIN Attack & Phantom Domain Attack
> 6. DNS Rebinding Attack
> 7. Subdomain Takeover
> 8. Defense Mechanisms (DNSSEC, DoH, DoT)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Normal DNS Query (Pre-Attack Baseline)

Before analyzing attacks, the *legitimate* transaction must be understood:

1. **User** types `www.example.com` in the browser.
2. **Stub Resolver** (OS-level) sends a UDP query to the **Recursive Resolver** (ISP/Google `8.8.8.8` / Cloudflare `1.1.1.1`).
3. Recursive Resolver queries **Root Server** → **TLD Server** (`.com`) → **Authoritative Nameserver** for `example.com`.
4. The **A/AAAA record** is returned and **cached** locally for the duration of the **TTL (Time-To-Live)** field (typically **300s to 86400s**).
5. Browser opens TCP connection to the resolved IP.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of DNS Attacks (KTU Board-Favorite Classification)

#### **A. DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning (Kaminsky Attack variant)**
The attacker injects a forged DNS response into the resolver's cache **before** the legitimate response arrives, causing future users to be redirected to a malicious IP.

#### **B. DNS Hijacking**
Modifies DNS configuration at the **client side** (malware altering `hosts` file) or **registrar side** (attacker transfers domain ownership).

#### **C. DNS Tunneling**
Encodes non-DNS data (HTTP, SSH, command payloads) inside DNS query/response packets to bypass firewalls — used for **Command-and-Control (C2)** and **data exfiltration**.

#### **D. DNS Amplification Attack (DRDoS)**
A **Distributed Reflection Denial-of-Service** attack where small queries (60 bytes) are forged with the victim's IP as the source, eliciting massive responses (>4000 bytes) from open resolvers — achieving an **amplification factor of 50–80x**.

#### **E. NXDOMAIN Attack**
Floods the resolver with queries for non-existent domains, exhausting cache memory and CPU.

#### **F. Phantom Domain Attack**
Configures an authoritative server to send **slow or no responses**, keeping resolver threads stuck in `wait` state, exhausting the connection pool.

#### **G. DNS Rebinding Attack**
A malicious webpage tricks the browser into bypassing the **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** by rapidly rebinding the domain name to an internal IP (e.g., `192.168.0.1` router).

### 2.3 The Kaminsky DNS Cache Poisoning — Root Cause Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Birthday Paradox Vulnerability (RFC 5452 Mandate)**
> The attack exploits the fact that DNS transaction IDs are only **16 bits (65,536 possible values)**. By the **Birthday Paradox**, with only **≈ 309 spoofed responses**, the attacker has a **~50% probability** of guessing the correct transaction ID. Modern spoofing uses **source port randomization** (raising entropy to ~32 bits = **4.29 billion combinations**) to mitigate this, but the underlying protocol weakness persists.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize This Table for KTU Board Exams**

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Units / Notes |
|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| DNS Transaction ID entropy | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | bits (legacy) |
| DNS + Port entropy (DNSSEC-era) | $2^{32} \approx 4.29 \times 10^{9}$ | bits |
| Birthday attack threshold | $N_{attempts} \approx 1.177 \sqrt{2^{n}}$ | For 50% success |
| Amplification Factor | $AF = \frac{\text{Response Size (bytes)}}{\text{Query Size (bytes)}}$ | Unitless ratio |
| DNS Amplification typical AF | $AF \approx 50$ to $80$ | ANY query attack |
| Kaminsky success probability | $P \approx 1 - e^{-k^2 / 2N}$ | $k$ = guesses, $N$ = ID space |
| Cache TTL persistence | $T_{persist} = TTL_{value}$ | seconds |
| Tunneling data rate (theoretical) | $R_{tunnel} = \frac{P_{payload} \times 8}{T_{query}}$ | bits/second |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SOC Analysts** use DNS log analysis (Passive DNS, **DNSDB**, **RiskIQ**) to detect DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm) callbacks from malware.
- **CDN providers** (Akamai, Cloudflare) deploy **Anycast** to absorb DNS amplification DDoS at scale.
- **National CERTs** (CERT-In, India) issue advisories on DNS hijacking campaigns like the **Sea Turtle** and **DNSpionage** operations.
- **Enterprise firewalls** integrate **DNS Sinkholing** to block known-malicious domains and C2 channels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DNS Amplification Bandwidth Math

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU-typical 14-mark derivation):** An attacker uses **10,000 open DNS resolvers** to launch an amplification attack. Each query is **60 bytes**; each response is **4,000 bytes** (ANY query for `ripe.net`). The attacker has a **1 Gbps** uplink. Calculate: (a) Amplification Factor, (b) Total attack bandwidth directed at the victim.

**Step 1 — Calculate the Amplification Factor (AF):**

$$AF = \frac{\text{Response Size}}{\text{Query Size}}$$

$$AF = \frac{4000 \text{ bytes}}{60 \text{bytes}}$$

$$AF \approx 66.67$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the per-resolver response bandwidth:**

Each resolver receives the 60-byte query and emits a 4000-byte response.

$$B_{per\_resolver} = \frac{S_{response} \times 8}{T_{RTT}}$$

Assuming the attacker sustains a 1 Gbps stream distributed across 10,000 resolvers:

$$B_{attack} = B_{attacker} \times AF$$

$$B_{attack} = 1 \text{ Gbps} \times 66.67$$

$$B_{attack} \approx 66.67 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 3 — Practical interpretation:**

> A **1 Gbps** uplink (achievable with a modest business broadband line) can be weaponized into a **66.67 Gbps** volumetric DDoS — sufficient to saturate a mid-sized enterprise's entire ISP uplink. This is precisely why the **2016 Dyn DNS attack** (Mirai botnet) reached **1.2 Tbps** and disrupted Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and GitHub across the US East Coast.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Attack-vs-Defense Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Favorite Comparison Table — Memorize for KTU 14-Mark Questions**

| Attack Vector | Protocol Layer | Primary Impact | Best-Known Defense | KTU Frequency |
|---------------|----------------|----------------|--------------------|---------------|
| **Cache Poisoning** | Application (L7) | Redirection / Phishing | **DNSSEC** (RFC 4033) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **DNS Hijacking** | Registrar / OS | Persistence / MITM | Registry Lock + **2FA** | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **DNS Tunneling** | Application (L7) | C2 / Exfiltration | **IDS/IPS** with DPI (e.g., Zeek) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Amplification DDoS** | Network (L3/L4) | Bandwidth Saturation | **Rate Limiting + Anycast + BCP38** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **NXDOMAIN Flood** | Application (L7) | Cache Exhaustion | Response Rate Limiting (RRL) | ⭐⭐ |
| **Phantom Domain** | Application (L7) | Thread Starvation | Aggressive timeout + worker pool tuning | ⭐⭐ |
| **DNS Rebinding** | Application (L7) | Internal Network Pivot | **DNS Pinning** + Same-Origin enforcement | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Subdomain Takeover** | Application (L7) | Brand Abuse / Cookie Theft | **Dangling DNS record** audits (AWS S3) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Kaminsky Attack Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7-Stage Kaminsky Exploit (CVE-2008-1447)**

1. **Attacker** sends a query for `random123.kevinsbank.com` to the target resolver. (No cache hit.)
2. Resolver forwards query upstream to authoritative server.
3. **Attacker floods** the resolver with **thousands of forged responses** for `random123.kevinsbank.com`, each carrying:
   - A **guessed 16-bit TXID**
   - An **AUTHORITY section** injecting `NS` record → `kevinsbank.com = ns1.evil.com` (attacker's server)
   - An **ADDITIONAL section** injecting `A` record → `ns1.evil.com = 6.6.6.6`
4. If the guessed TXID matches **before** the real response arrives, the resolver accepts the forgery.
5. The poisoned cache now resolves **all** subdomains of `kevinsbank.com` to the attacker's IP.
6. Legitimate users entering `login.kevinsbank.com` are silently redirected to **6.6.6.6** (phishing site).
7. Persistence lasts for the **TTL of the NS record** (often 86400 seconds = **24 hours**).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DNS Resolution & Attack Injection Points

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **legitimate DNS resolution chain** and the **4 critical injection points** where attackers can hijack the flow. Memorize this for the 14-mark "Explain DNS attack surface" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Browser] -->|1. Query www.bank.com| B[Stub Resolver OS]
    B -->|2. UDP/53 Query| C[Recursive Resolver 8.8.8.8]
    C -->|3. Query| D[Root Server]
    D -->|4. Referral to .com| C
    C -->|5. Query .com TLD| E[TLD Server .com]
    E -->|6. Referral to Authoritative| C
    C -->|7. Query| F[Authoritative NS Bank]
    F -->|8. A record response 1.2.3.4| C
    C -->|9. Cached reply| A

    A1[Attacker 1: HOSTS file injection] -.->|alters step 1| B
    A2[Attacker 2: Cache Poisoning] -.->|forges step 8| C
    A3[Attacker 3: Registrar Hijack] -.->|alters step 7| F
    A4[Attacker 4: TLD Compromise] -.->|alters step 5| E

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style B fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style D fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style E fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style F fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,color:#000
    style A1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style A4 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 DNS Amplification DDoS — Attack Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram illustrates the **DRDoS amplification topology**. The attacker is a low-bandwidth orchestrator; the open resolvers become unwitting reflectors; the victim absorbs the multiplied bandwidth.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AT[Attacker 1 Gbps uplink] -->|1. Forged UDP packets\nSource IP = Victim\nQuery ANY ripe.net| RES1[Open Resolver A]
    AT -->|Forged packet| RES2[Open Resolver B]
    AT -->|Forged packet| RES3[Open Resolver C]
    AT -->|Forged packet| RESN[Open Resolver N x 10000]

    RES1 -->|2. 4000-byte response\nto Victim IP| VIC[Victim Server]
    RES2 -->|Response| VIC
    RES3 -->|Response| VIC
    RESN -->|Response| VIC

    VIC -->|3. Bandwidth saturation\nService down| DOWN[Denial of Service]

    style AT fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style RES1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style RES2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style RES3 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style RESN fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style VIC fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style DOWN fill:#cc0000,stroke:#000000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 DNS Tunneling — C2 / Exfiltration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MAL[Malware on Victim Host] -->|1. Encode data\nin subdomain of evil.com| NS1[Local DNS Resolver]
    NS1 -->|2. Query TXT record| NS2[Attacker NS evil.com]
    NS2 -->|3. Decode subdomain\nretrieve exfiltrated data| C2[Attacker C2 Server]
    C2 -->|4. TXT response with\nnext-stage payload| NS1
    NS1 -->|5. Response| MAL

    style MAL fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style NS1 fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style NS2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#cc0000,stroke:#000000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **DNS Spoofing** and **DNS Hijacking**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | DNS Spoofing | DNS Hijacking |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Attack Point** | Resolver cache (in-transit) | Registrar / Client OS hosts file |
| **Method** | Forged DNS response with guessed TXID | Unauthorized change of NS records at registrar or local host file modification |
| **Persistence** | Limited to TTL of poisoned record | Persistent until reverted |
| **Scale** | Affects all users of the resolver | Affects specific client or whole domain |
| **Mitigation** | DNSSEC + source port randomization | Registry Lock + 2FA + endpoint hardening |

> ⚠️ Award full 3 marks only if the student explicitly mentions **TXID forgery** for Spoofing and **NS record modification** for Hijacking.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is a **DNS Amplification Attack**? Mention its amplification factor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A DNS Amplification Attack is a **Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)** technique in which the attacker sends small DNS queries (≈ 60 bytes) with the **victim's IP address spoofed** as the source, to multiple open DNS resolvers. The resolvers, in turn, send large responses (4000+ bytes) to the victim, overwhelming its bandwidth. The **amplification factor** typically ranges from **50× to 80×**, meaning a 1 Gbps attack stream can generate a **50–80 Gbps** volumetric flood directed at the victim.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Question A (14 Marks)]**

**Q A (a)** Explain **DNS Cache Poisoning** with a neat diagram. Describe the **Kaminsky Attack** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** DNS Cache Poisoning is an attack in which forged DNS information is introduced into a resolver's cache, causing it to return incorrect IP addresses to subsequent users until the cache entry expires (TTL-based persistence).

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
1. Attacker identifies target resolver (e.g., `8.8.8.8`).
2. Forces the resolver to issue a lookup for a *random* domain under the target zone (e.g., `xyz123.targetbank.com`).
3. Floods the resolver with **thousands of forged DNS responses** in rapid succession, each containing a **guessed 16-bit Transaction ID (TXID)** and a **forged AUTHORITY section** that delegates the entire `targetbank.com` zone to the attacker's nameserver.
4. If a forged response arrives *before* the legitimate one and the TXID guess is correct, the resolver caches the malicious NS record.
5. From this moment, **every** user of that resolver querying `login.targetbank.com` is redirected to the attacker's server.

**Kaminsky Enhancement (2 marks):** Dan Kaminsky (2008) discovered that instead of poisoning a *single* hostname, an attacker can poison the **NS record for the entire domain** by querying *non-existent* random subdomains. This makes the attack succeed in **seconds** rather than waiting for TTL expiry, and works even on patched servers.

**Mitigation (1 mark):** **DNSSEC** (RFC 4033) cryptographically signs DNS records, making forgery computationally infeasible. Additionally, **source port randomization** raises entropy to 32 bits.

**Diagram Reference:** Use Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

**Q A (b)** With a neat diagram, explain **DNS Amplification Attack**. An attacker has a 500 Mbps link and 8,000 open resolvers. Each query is 80 bytes; each response is 3,200 bytes. Calculate the **amplification factor** and the **total attack bandwidth**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (2 marks):** Use a flowchart identical to Section 4.2 (Attacker → Open Resolvers → Victim).

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- Attacker spoofs victim's source IP and sends small DNS queries (ANY type) to open resolvers worldwide.
- Each resolver unwittingly replies to the victim with a large DNS response.
- The bandwidth multiplier enables low-resource attackers to take down high-capacity targets.

**Calculation (3 marks):**

$$AF = \frac{S_{response}}{S_{query}} = \frac{3200}{80} = 40$$

$$B_{attack} = B_{attacker} \times AF = 500 \text{ Mbps} \times 40 = 20{,}000 \text{ Mbps}$$

$$B_{attack} = 20 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Conclusion:** A modest 500 Mbps link can weaponize into a **20 Gbps** volumetric flood — easily sufficient to overwhelm a small enterprise's perimeter.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)]**

**Q B (a)** Explain **DNS Tunneling** with a diagram. How is it used by attackers for **Command-and-Control (C2)** and **data exfiltration**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** DNS Tunneling encapsulates non-DNS protocols (HTTP, SSH, TCP) inside DNS query/response packets, abusing the DNS protocol to bypass firewalls and proxy filters that typically allow UDP/53 traffic.

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
1. Attacker registers a domain (e.g., `evil.com`) and configures its authoritative NS to point to a malicious server running tunneling software (e.g., **dnscat2**, **iodine**).
2. Malware on the victim host encodes exfiltrated data (e.g., `AABBCC123`) as a **subdomain label** of `evil.com` → `AABBCC123.evil.com`.
3. The query reaches the attacker's NS via the standard recursive resolution chain.
4. The attacker's tunnel server decodes the subdomain, extracts the data, and replies with a TXT record containing the next-stage C2 command.
5. The entire exchange appears as ordinary DNS traffic to the corporate firewall.

**Use Cases (2 marks):**
- **C2:** Beaconing, payload download, lateral movement commands.
- **Exfiltration:** Stealthy transfer of credentials, documents, and PII.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Use Section 4.3 of these notes.

**Mitigations (1 mark):** Deploy **deep packet inspection** (e.g., Zeek, Suricata) at the egress, monitor **entropy** of subdomain labels, and block direct outbound UDP/53 from user subnets.

---

**Q B (b)** Explain **DNS Rebinding Attack** and **Subdomain Takeover**. Mention two countermeasures for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**DNS Rebinding (3.5 marks):**
- Attacker hosts a malicious webpage on `evil.com` (IP: `6.6.6.6`).
- The DNS server is configured with a **very low TTL (e.g., 1 second)**.
- The victim's browser loads the page, then performs a **JavaScript-driven re-fetch**.
- Within the TTL window, the authoritative server **rebinds** `evil.com` to an **internal IP** (e.g., `192.168.1.1` — the home router).
- The Same-Origin Policy is **bypassed** because the origin is still `evil.com`, but the destination is now the private network.

**Countermeasures:** (i) DNS Pinning (browsers cache the first IP for the session); (ii) `Host:` header validation on internal devices; (iii) Browser-side private IP filtering (Chrome's `Secure DNS` and `Private Network Access`).

**Subdomain Takeover (3.5 marks):**
- A company creates a CNAME pointing `blog.company.com` to a third-party service (e.g., an **AWS S3 bucket**, **Heroku app**, or **Azure Cloud App**).
- The service is decommissioned, but the **DNS CNAME is never removed** (a *dangling DNS record*).
- An attacker registers the same service (creates a new S3 bucket with the original name).
- The subdomain now points to attacker-controlled content.
- Used for **cookie theft, phishing, brand impersonation, and session hijacking**.

**Countermeasures:** (i) Continuous **DNS audit** with tools like `subjack` or `can-i-take-over-xyz`; (ii) Immediate DNS record revocation upon service deprovisioning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention TXID and source port** in Cache Poisoning → loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing DNS Spoofing with DNS Hijacking** → these are *not* synonyms; spoofing is cache-based, hijacking is registrar/host-based.
> 3. **Omitting the amplification factor formula** in DRDoS derivations → full 3 marks deducted.
> 4. **Missing the Kaminsky 2008 reference** → examiners explicitly test this.
> 5. **Ignoring DNSSEC** in any DNS attack answer → mandatory mention for 1 mark.
> 6. **In Tunneling answers**, students often forget to mention **TXT record abuse** for C2 reply — important.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam (Module-4, DNS Attacks)**

- **DNS = UDP/53, hierarchical, distributed, trust-based** — inherently vulnerable.
- **Cache Poisoning (Kaminsky 2008)** → forges TXID (16 bits) to inject NS record; mitigated by **DNSSEC + port randomization (32-bit entropy)**.
- **DNS Hijacking** → modifies NS at registrar or `hosts` file; mitigated by **Registry Lock + 2FA**.
- **DNS Tunneling** → encodes data in subdomain labels (C2/exfil); tools: **dnscat2, iodine**; detected via **subdomain entropy analysis**.
- **DNS Amplification DDoS** → small query, large response; **AF = Response/Query ≈ 50–80×**; mitigation: **Rate Limiting + Anycast + BCP38 ingress filtering**.
- **NXDOMAIN Attack** → floods non-existent queries; mitigation: **Response Rate Limiting (RRL)**.
- **Phantom Domain** → slow authoritative server hangs resolver threads; mitigation: **aggressive timeout + worker pool sizing**.
- **DNS Rebinding** → low-TTL rebinding to bypass SOP into private network; mitigation: **DNS Pinning + Private Network Access (Chrome)**.
- **Subdomain Takeover** → dangling CNAMEs; tools: **subjack, can-i-take-over-xyz**; mitigation: **continuous DNS hygiene audits**.
- **Real-world Incidents:** **Dyn DNS attack (Oct 2016, Mirai, 1.2 Tbps)**, **Sea Turtle (2019)**, **DNSpionage (2018–19)**.
- **Defense Stack:** **DNSSEC (RFC 4033) + DoH (RFC 8484) + DoT (RFC 7858) + Response Rate Limiting + Anycast + IDS/IPS with DPI**.
- **Key RFCs to cite:** **RFC 1034/1035** (DNS base), **RFC 4033** (DNSSEC), **RFC 5452** (DNS forgery resistance), **RFC 7873** (RRL), **RFC 8484** (DoH).
- **Mnemonic:** **"S-H-T-A-N-R-P-S"** = **S**poofing, **H**ijacking, **T**unneling, **A**mplification, **N**XDOMAIN, **R**ebinding, **P**hantom, **S**ubdomain-Takeover.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WAP Gateway
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT502 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module-4
> - **Topic:** WAP Gateway

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **WAP Gateway** (Wireless Application Protocol Gateway) is an intermediary application-layer server that acts as a **protocol translator and proxy** between the **wireless domain** (mobile handsets using the optimized WAP protocol suite over bearer networks like GSM/GPRS/CDMA) and the **Internet/Web domain** (origin servers speaking standard HTTP/TCP/IP). It is the keystone element of the WAP 1.x architecture and a residual, evolved function in WAP 2.0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCCCT502 / M4):** The WAP Gateway is studied under security because it represents a classic **trusted-third-party trust-translation problem**, popularly referred to as the *"WAP Gap"* — a frequent KTU essay/fundamental question.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Customs & Translator at the Seaport"

Imagine a small fishing village (the *mobile phone*) whose inhabitants speak only a tribal dialect (the *WAP stack — WDP/WTP/WSP/WTLS/WML*). The villagers want to buy goods from a giant overseas warehouse (the *Internet web server*) where everyone speaks standard English (the *TCP/IP + HTTP/SSL stack*). They cannot talk directly because:

1. The village uses small canoes on a narrow river (narrowband GSM/GPRS bearers).
2. The warehouse uses massive cargo ships on the open sea (broadband Internet).

A **Customs Officer + Translator** sits at the river mouth. He:
- Unloads the canoe (receives WAP-stack request from mobile).
- **Translates** the tribal dialect into English (decodes WML, terminates WTLS, converts WSP/WTP/WDP into HTTP/TCP).
- **Repackages** the goods onto a cargo ship (forwards the request as standard HTTP/TCP/IP).
- Receives the cargo ship back, re-translates, and reloads the canoe.

That official is the **WAP Gateway**. He is essential — but because he *understands both languages*, the villagers' secrets (credit card numbers, PINs) are momentarily **fully visible in plaintext on his desk**. This is the entire WAP security controversy.

### 1.3 Core Components Surrounding the WAP Gateway

| Component | Domain | Stack | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mobile Client (WAP Device)** | Wireless | WAP Stack + WML/WMLScript | Issues WAP requests, renders WML decks |
| **WAP Gateway** | Both | WAP Stack ↔ HTTP/TCP/IP | Translates, caches, optionally transcodes content |
| **Origin Server (Web Server)** | Web | HTTP/TCP/IP (+ optional TLS) | Hosts the actual content/service |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard reference RFC for the WAP architecture: **WAP Forum / Open Mobile Alliance (OMA) architecture specification**. In KTU 2024 answers, always cite the *WAP Forum* as the standards body that defined the gateway.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The WAP Protocol Stack (The "Why" of the Gateway)

Mobile networks in the late 1990s had **3 fundamental constraints** that HTTP/TCP/IP could not handle efficiently:
- **Narrow bandwidth** (9.6 kbps GSM CSD, early GPRS).
- **High latency** (often 500–1000 ms RTT over GSM).
- **Small, low-power screens** and **limited RAM/CPU** in handsets.

The WAP Forum therefore designed an optimized, layered stack that mirrors the OSI model but with stripped-down, binary-encoded protocols. The gateway exists to terminate this stack and re-speak HTTP.

**Layered WAP 1.x Stack (top to bottom):**

1. **WAE — Wireless Application Environment** (application): WML, WMLScript, WTA, content formats.
2. **WSP — Wireless Session Protocol** (session): HTTP-like request/response, connection-oriented and connectionless services.
3. **WTP — Wireless Transaction Protocol** (transaction): reliable request/response, no concept of large byte streams.
4. **WTLS — Wireless Transport Layer Security** (security): optional encryption + authentication, optimized for low bandwidth.
5. **WDP — Wireless Datagram Protocol** (transport): bearer-independent, maps onto UDP-like services.
6. **Bearers** (physical): GSM CSD, GPRS, CDMA, SMS, USSD, etc.

### 2.2 Core Functions of the WAP Gateway (The "How")

The gateway performs **seven canonical operations**, each of which is a frequent KTU short-note target:

1. **Protocol Translation:** WSP/WTP/WDP ↔ HTTP/TCP/IP.
2. **Content Encoding/Transcoding:** Converts HTML to WML (or vice versa in WAP 2.0 push); may also re-encode images (e.g., WBMP) and compress text to fit small screens.
3. **Connection Adaptation:** Maintains persistent TCP connections to the web (HTTP keep-alive) while serving many bursty WAP clients over slow bearers — improving perceived latency.
4. **Caching/Proxying:** Caches WML decks and WMLScript to reduce bearer usage.
5. **Security Translation:** Terminates **WTLS** from the mobile and (optionally) initiates **TLS/HTTPS** to the origin server. **This is the security hot-spot.**
6. **Push Service:** Implements **WAP Push (PAP — Push Access Protocol)** to deliver content initiated by the server to the handset (e.g., MMS notifications, stock alerts, SLAs).
7. **Billing / Usage Logging:** Generates CDRs (Call Detail Records) for the carrier — the gateway is the natural metering point.

### 2.3 WAP Push vs WAP Pull

- **WAP Pull:** Mobile-initiated. The user types a URL → handset sends WSP GET → gateway proxies to origin → gateway returns the deck. This is the dominant model.
- **WAP Push (PAP — Push Access Protocol):** Server-initiated. The Push Initiator (PI) sends an XML document (PAP) over HTTP/S to the **Push Proxy Gateway (PPG)**, which then delivers a WAP push (via SMS bearer or IP) to the handset, triggering an SMS-Centric Service Indication (SI) or Service Loading (SL).

### 2.4 WAP Security Subsystem

| Security Element | KTU Role | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **WTLS** | Mandatory counterpart of TLS in WAP stack | Supports AES, 3DES, RC5, SHA-1, MD5; uses **WTLSCertificate** (X.509 variant) |
| **WIM — Wireless Identity Module** | Smart-card-like tamper-resistant chip on the SIM/USIM for **private-key storage** and WTLS handshake acceleration | Critical for mobile banking; analogous to PC's smart card |
| **WMLScript SignText** | Application-layer digital signature function using WIM-stored keys | Used for non-repudiation in m-commerce |
| **WPKI — Wireless PKI** | PKI adapted for short cert chains and compressed encodings | Optimised for low bandwidth |
| **WAP Gap** | The vulnerability created at the gateway between WTLS and TLS termination | Discussed in §3.3 |

### 2.5 WAP 1.x vs WAP 2.0 (KTU Comparison Table)

| Feature | WAP 1.x | WAP 2.0 (2002) |
|---|---|---|
| Mobile → Gateway | WAP stack (WSP/WTP/WDP) | HTTP/TCP/IP directly (WAP stack optional) |
| Gateway role | Heavy translator | Optional proxy / cache / Push proxy |
| Mobile → Server security | WTLS (terminated at gateway) | End-to-end **TLS** possible (eliminates WAP gap if no proxy) |
| Content format | WML, WMLScript | XHTML Mobile Profile + WML backwards compat |
| Bearer | CSD, GPRS, SMS, USSD | GPRS, EDGE, UMTS, Wi-Fi |
| Transport | WDP over UDP-like | TCP/IP directly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Most KTU questions still test **WAP 1.x** because that is *where the gateway security problem exists*. If a question says "WAP Gateway" without a version, assume 1.x.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Sheet

| # | Parameter / Constant | KTU-Standard Value / Mapping | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | WAP default gateway port (WAP 1.x push) | **2948 (WAP-PUSH)**, **9200 (WAP connectionless)**, **9201 (WAP secure connectionless)** | TCP/UDP port |
| 2 | WAP-Push (PAP) default port | **2949** | TCP |
| 3 | WSP connectionless default port | **9200** | UDP |
| 4 | WTLS connectionless default port | **9202** (secure) / **9203** | UDP |
| 5 | Bearer minimum bandwidth assumption | **≈ 9.6 kbps** (GSM CSD baseline) | kbps |
| 6 | WTLS cipher suites | AES, 3DES, RC5-128, IDEA, RC5-64, DES-56 | bits |
| 7 | WTLS MAC | SHA-1, MD5, SHA-256 (in WAP 2.0) | bits |
| 8 | WML deck size recommendation | **≤ 1.4 KB** per deck (for fast rendering on 1999-era handsets) | KB |
| 9 | WMLScript "SignText" hash output | Truncated to fit SMS-payload constraints | bits |
| 10 | WAP Push SI (Service Indication) max size | **≤ 140 bytes** (one SMS PDU) | bytes |
| 11 | WAP 2.0 mobile profile | XHTML Mobile Profile 1.0 / 1.1 / 1.2 | standard |
| 12 | Wireless PKI cert. encoding | WTLS-style compressed X.509, target **< 1000 bytes** | bytes |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-throughs

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walk-through — A WAP 1.x Pull Transaction (Mobile → Origin Server)

Below is the **complete request/response lifecycle** through the gateway. KTU often asks students to "trace the path of a WAP request"; this is the canonical answer.

**Assumed scenario:** A user on a GSM/GPRS Nokia handset requests `https://bank.com/balance.wml`.

**Step 1 — DNS / Bearer Setup (outside gateway)**
The handset resolves `bank.com` via the carrier's WAP APN. The bearer (GPRS PDP context) is set up. The handset opens a **WSP session** over WTP/WDP to the gateway's well-known port.

**Step 2 — WSP Session Establishment**
The handset sends a `S-Connect` (WSP) PDU → WTP wraps it in a `Invoke` (with `TCless=false`) → WDP encapsulates into UDP datagram → transmitted to gateway port **9200** (WSP connectionless) or over a confirmed WTP transaction (port **9201**).

**Step 3 — WTLS Handshake (if user enabled security)**
Before WSP sends the HTTP-equivalent `GET`, the gateway and handset perform a **WTLS handshake**:
1. ClientHello (supported cipher suites, compression methods, client_random).
2. ServerHello + ServerKeyExchange (using **WIM-stored certificate**).
3. ClientKeyExchange (premaster secret, optionally encrypted to WIM).
4. ChangeCipherSpec, Finished.
At this point, all subsequent WSP data is **encrypted with WTLS** between handset and gateway.

**Step 4 — WSP `GET` Translation**
Handset sends `WSP GET /balance.wml HTTP/1.1\nHost: bank.com\n` over the secure WTP transaction. The gateway receives it.

**Step 5 — Gateway Decryption and Re-encapsulation**
The gateway **terminates WTLS** (decrypts the WSP payload), then builds a **standard HTTP/1.1 GET** request to `bank.com:443` over **TCP**.

**Step 6 — Optional TLS to Origin Server**
The gateway opens a **new TLS session** to `bank.com`, performs TLS handshake using its own IP/identity, and forwards the GET.

**Step 7 — Origin Server Response**
The bank server returns an HTTPS response (HTML/XML, but typically WML). The gateway **terminates TLS**, optionally **transcodes** the WML (or HTML→WML), and **encrypts with WTLS** for the handset.

**Step 8 — WTP/WSP Delivery to Handset**
Gateway sends the encrypted WSP `Reply` PDU back over WTP. The handset decrypts with WTLS using its session keys and renders the WML deck.

**Mathematical summary of the path (security boundaries):**

$$
\text{Handset} \;\xrightarrow[\text{WTLS}]{\text{secure}} \;\text{WAP Gateway} \;\xrightarrow[\text{TLS (optional)}]{\text{re-encrypted}} \;\text{Origin Server}
$$

The gateway sits in the middle and is therefore the **trust pivot**.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Walk-through — A WAP Push (PAP) Transaction

**Step 1 — Content Originator** (e.g., stock-alert service) builds a PAP XML push message:
```xml
<pap>
  <push-message push-id="p123" deliver-before-timestamp="2024-12-31T12:00:00Z">
    <address address-value="WAPPUSH=+919999999999/TYPE=PLMN@ppg.carrier.com"/>
    <quality-of-service priority="high" deliver-after-timestamp="..."/>
    <push-content type="application/vnd.wap.si" href="https://bank.com/offer.wml">...</push-content>
  </push-message>
</pap>
```

**Step 2 — Push Initiator (PI) → PPG.** PI sends the XML over **HTTP POST** to the **Push Proxy Gateway (PPG)** at the carrier, typically on **port 2949** (PAP).

**Step 3 — PPG Authentication & Authorization.** PPG authenticates PI (using PI-ID and shared secret / TLS client cert) and checks the user's push subscription profile.

**Step 4 — PPG → Mobile.** The PPG converts the push into a **WAP Push SI (Service Indication)** encoded as a binary **WBXML** payload, then delivers it to the handset over the SMS bearer (SMS-DELIVER PDU pointing to a WAP Push port) — this is how a user sees the pop-up alert.

**Step 5 — User Action.** The user accepts the push → handset performs a **WAP Pull** (§3.1) to fetch `offer.wml` from `bank.com`.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Security Analysis — The "WAP Gap" Problem

The most important security analysis in this module. Below is the **complete deconstruction** a KTU 14-mark answer should contain.

**Premise.** In WAP 1.x, end-to-end security between the handset and the origin server is **impossible** if the gateway is in-line, because the WAP protocol stack on the handset cannot directly speak HTTP/TCP/IP. Therefore:

$$
\exists \; x \in \text{request payload} \;\text{(e.g., credit-card number)} \quad \text{such that} \quad x \;\text{is in plaintext in the gateway RAM/disk at some instant}.
$$

**Step-by-step attack surface mapping:**

1. **At time $t_0$:** payload is in the handset's RAM — protected only by WTLS session keys.
2. **At time $t_1$:** payload is in the bearer network (encrypted by WTLS).
3. **At time $t_2$ (the critical instant):** payload arrives at the gateway. The gateway's WTLS stack calls `SSL_read()` → plaintext is in the gateway's user-space buffer. **Here the gateway process — and any privileged user on the gateway host — can read the data.**
4. **At time $t_3$:** gateway opens a TLS session to origin server, calls `SSL_write()`. The data is now encrypted again.
5. **At time $t_4$:** on the wired network (encrypted by TLS).
6. **At time $t_5$:** at the origin server (decrypted by TLS termination).

**Concrete threat vectors** (each = 1 KTU mark):

- **Malicious operator at the carrier:** can dump RAM or sniff plaintext at $t_2$.
- **Compromised gateway software:** a backdoored WAP proxy can log credit cards.
- **Legal interception / law enforcement:** with a single warrant to the carrier (gateway operator), all m-commerce transactions are visible — far easier than breaking TLS to the bank.
- **Logging / CDRs:** many gateways log payloads for debugging — credit-card numbers get written to disk in plaintext.
- **WAP Push spoofing:** an attacker who knows the PPG endpoint can forge SI/SL pushes to deliver phishing links.

**Mitigations (each = 1 KTU mark):**

| # | Mitigation | Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **End-to-end TLS** in WAP 2.0 (mobile speaks HTTPS directly) | Eliminates the gap if no proxy is used |
| 2 | **WAP Gateway as a hardened, FIPS 140-2 device** with HSM | Reduces but does not eliminate risk |
| 3 | **Disable payload logging** in gateway | Procedural |
| 4 | **Use WIM for key storage** so the gateway never sees long-term keys | Limits scope |
| 5 | **Tunnelling** (IPsec from gateway to origin) | Trust shifted to gateway |
| 6 | **Use application-level crypto** (e.g., SET, 3-D Secure) on top of WAP | Defence in depth |
| 7 | **WAP 2.0 Push Proxy Gateway with mutual TLS** | Secures PI↔PPG leg |

### 3.4 Comparison — WTLS vs TLS (KTU favourite 7-mark sub-question)

| Attribute | WTLS | TLS 1.0 (1999) |
|---|---|---|
| Layer in stack | WAP transport | TCP |
| Data unit | Datagram (supports datagram bearer, e.g., UDP, SMS) | Byte stream over TCP |
| Connection | Connectionless and connection-oriented | Connection-oriented only |
| Compression | Built-in (because of low bandwidth) | Not built-in (later added in TLS 1.3 record) |
| Certificate | Compressed X.509 (WTLSCert) | Standard X.509 |
| Key material | WIM (smart card) optional | Client cert / smart card optional |
| Re-handshake cost | Expensive → optimised cached sessions | Expensive → session resumption |
| Cipher suites | Smaller set, optimised for low power | Larger set |

**Equivalence mapping:**

$$
\text{WTLS} \;\approx\; \text{TLS} - \text{TCP-stream assumptions} + \text{datagram mode} + \text{mandatory compression} + \text{WIM binding}
$$

```mermaid
    G -->|HTTP TCP IP over Internet| O[Origin Web Server Bank Portal]
    O -->|HTTP response| G
    G -->|WSP reply encrypted with WTLS| H

    subgraph security_layer["Security Perimeter"]
        direction TB
        S1[WTLS Tunnel Handset to Gateway]
        S2[Plaintext in Gateway RAM The WAP Gap]
        S3[Optional TLS Gateway to Origin]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3
    end

    classDef secure fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef plain fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    class S1 secure
    class S2 plain
    class S3 secure
```

```mermaid
        WTLS1 --> WDP1[WDP Wireless Datagram]
        WDP1 --> B1[Bearer GSM GPRS SMS]
    end
```

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram

    participant PPG as Push Proxy Gateway Carrier

    PPG->>SMSC: SMS DELIVER with WBXML SI payload

    H->>H: User accepts push
```
