## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define WAP Gateway. List any two of its core functions.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
A WAP Gateway is an intermediary server that translates between the WAP protocol stack on mobile handsets (WSP/WTP/WDP/WTLS) and the standard HTTP/TCP/IP stack on the Internet, enabling mobile users to access web content.
**Core functions (any two):**
1. Protocol translation between WAP stack and HTTP/TCP/IP.
2. Content transcoding between HTML and WML, including image format conversion.
3. Caching of WML decks to reduce bearer bandwidth consumption.
4. Termination of WTLS and optional re-encryption with TLS to the origin server (security translation).
5. WAP Push delivery via PAP for server-initiated content.
6. CDR generation for carrier billing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is the WAP Gap? Why is it a security concern?*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
The WAP Gap refers to the brief instant inside a WAP Gateway where, after the gateway terminates the WTLS session from the handset and before it opens a new TLS session to the origin server, application data (e.g., credit card numbers, PINs, OTPs) resides **in plaintext in the gateway's memory**. It is a security concern because:
1. A malicious or compromised gateway operator can read sensitive data.
2. Debug/logging functions may persist this plaintext to disk.
3. Lawful-interception becomes trivial (one warrant to the carrier).
4. It violates the *end-to-end security* principle of TLS.
The WAP Gap is the principal motivation for the move to **WAP 2.0**, which supports end-to-end TLS between handset and origin server.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse** *WAP Gateway* with a *GGSN* (Gateway GPRS Support Node). The GGSN is a Layer-3 packet gateway; the WAP Gateway is an application-layer protocol translator.
> - **Do NOT claim** that WAP 1.x provides *end-to-end* security — that is the single biggest marking deduction in any 14-mark WAP answer.
> - **Always draw** the layered stack (handset side vs server side) and **label the WAP Gap** explicitly.
> - **Cite the WAP Forum / OMA** as the standards body; do not invent ISO numbers.
> - **Distinguish** WAP Push (PAP, server-initiated) from WAP Pull (WSP GET, client-initiated).

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *WAP Architecture & Gateway Functions*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **WAP 1.x architecture** and explain the **role of the WAP Gateway** in bridging the wireless and Internet domains. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **WAP protocol stack** used in the handset. Compare each WAP layer with its closest Internet-protocol counterpart. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) WAP 1.x Architecture (7 marks):**
The WAP 1.x architecture has **three primary elements**: the WAP mobile client (handset), the WAP Gateway, and the Origin Server (web/application server). The handset runs a micro-browser and speaks the WAP stack; it cannot natively run HTTP over TCP/IP because of bearer and device constraints. The WAP Gateway sits at the carrier and performs **seven canonical functions**:
1. *Protocol translation* (WSP/WTP/WDP ↔ HTTP/TCP/IP).
2. *Content adaptation* (HTML → WML, image transcoding).
3. *Caching* of WML decks to minimise bearer traffic.
4. *Connection optimisation* (persistent HTTP to origin, multiplexed WSP bearers).
5. *Security translation* (WTLS ↔ TLS).
6. *Push delivery* (PAP / WAP Push SI / SL).
7. *Billing and CDR generation*.

The gateway is therefore an **application-layer proxy** that adapts the slow wireless world to the fast wired world.

**(b) WAP Protocol Stack and Comparison (7 marks):**

| WAP Layer | Function | Closest Internet Counterpart | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| WAE (Wireless Application Environment) | Defines WML, WMLScript, WTA | HTTP + HTML + JavaScript | WML is deck/card based; cards ≤ 1.4 KB |
| WSP (Wireless Session Protocol) | HTTP-like request/response | HTTP/1.x | Binary-encoded headers, confirmed + unconfirmed services |
| WTP (Wireless Transaction Protocol) | Reliable transactions, ack/nack | TCP (subset) | No byte streams, no fragmentation guarantees; lighter |
| WTLS (Wireless Transport Layer Security) | Encryption + integrity + auth | TLS/SSL | Datagram mode, built-in compression, WIM binding |
| WDP (Wireless Datagram Protocol) | Bearer-independent transport | UDP | Maps onto many bearers (GSM, GPRS, SMS, CDMA, USSD) |
| Bearer | Physical/Link layer | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, LTE PHY | GSM CSD ≈ 9.6 kbps, GPRS ≈ 56–114 kbps |

A clean comparison emphasizes that **every WAP layer is a *lightweight, binary, connection-tolerant analogue* of an Internet layer**, deliberately engineered for the low-bandwidth, high-latency, low-power mobile environment.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *WAP Security & WAP Gap*

**(a)** Explain the **WAP security architecture** covering WTLS, WIM, and WMLScript SignText. How does WTLS differ from TLS? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **WAP Gap**? Discuss the security implications and list at least **four mitigation strategies**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) WAP Security Architecture (7 marks):**
1. **WTLS (Wireless Transport Layer Security):** Provides confidentiality, integrity, and authentication between the handset and the WAP Gateway. It supports both connection-oriented and connectionless services, multiple cipher suites (AES, 3DES, RC5), and built-in compression to conserve bandwidth. It is analogous to TLS but optimised for low-power, narrow-bandwidth environments.
2. **WIM (Wireless Identity Module):** A tamper-resistant hardware element — typically embedded in the SIM/USIM or on a separate smart card — that stores the user's private key and performs computationally expensive public-key operations (e.g., RSA private-key decryption) off the handset's main CPU. It is the mobile equivalent of a PC's smart card reader and is mandatory for high-value transactions such as m-banking.
3. **WMLScript SignText:** An application-layer function that produces a digital signature over user-entered data (e.g., the amount in a money transfer) using a WIM-resident private key. The signature, along with the certificate, is sent to the server for non-repudiation.
4. **WPKI (Wireless PKI):** Adapts standard X.509 PKI for low bandwidth by using **compressed certificates** and **short certificate chains**.
5. **WAP-2.0 / TLS:** WAP 2.0 enables the handset to speak HTTP/TLS directly, providing true end-to-end security.

**WTLS vs TLS (differences):** WTLS supports datagram-mode operation (TLS is byte-stream only over TCP); WTLS has built-in compression (TLS 1.3 added it later); WTLS uses compressed X.509 certificates; WTLS binds keys to the WIM hardware element; WTLS is optimised for connectionless WDP transport.

**(b) The WAP Gap (7 marks):**
The *WAP Gap* is the security vulnerability created inside the WAP Gateway when it terminates the WTLS session from the handset and before it establishes a new TLS session to the origin server. During this interval, all application data — including credit card numbers, OTPs, PINs, and authentication tokens — exists **in plaintext** within the gateway's process memory, and may be:
- Read by a malicious or compromised gateway operator,
- Captured by debug logging to disk,
- Subpoenaed by law enforcement with a single warrant to the carrier,
- Exposed to insider attacks (privileged users on the gateway host).

The WAP Gap therefore **violates the end-to-end security principle** of the OSI/TLS model.

**Mitigations (any four):**
1. **Adopt WAP 2.0 with end-to-end TLS** between the handset and the origin server, bypassing the WAP gateway for the security leg.
2. **Harden the WAP gateway** using FIPS 140-2 certified hardware, HSMs for key storage, and strict access controls.
3. **Disable payload-level logging** in the gateway and enforce strict data-retention policies.
4. **Use WIM-based client authentication** so long-term keys never enter the gateway in plaintext.
5. **Tunnel** origin-bound traffic over IPsec between the gateway and the origin data centre.
6. **Layer application-level cryptography** (e.g., SET, 3-D Secure) on top of WAP, providing defence-in-depth even if the WAP Gap is exploited.
7. **Mutually authenticate the PPG** with Push Initiators using TLS client certificates for WAP Push delivery.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **WAP Gateway = application-layer proxy** that translates between the WAP stack and HTTP/TCP/IP.
- It performs **seven core functions**: protocol translation, content adaptation, caching, connection optimisation, security translation, push delivery, and CDR generation.
- **WAP 1.x stack (top → bottom):** WAE → WSP → WTP → WTLS → WDP → Bearer.
- **WAP 2.0 stack:** Mobile speaks HTTP/TCP/IP directly; gateway is optional, push-only, or proxy-only.
- **WAP Pull** = user-initiated WSP GET through the gateway.
- **WAP Push** = server-initiated; PI → PPG (PAP over **port 2949**) → handset via SMS bearer with WBXML SI/SL payload.
- **Default WAP ports:** WSP connectionless **9200** UDP; WSP confirmed **9201**; WTLS **9202/9203**; WAP-Push **2948**; PAP **2949**.
- **WTLS** is a *datagram-aware, compression-enabled, WIM-bound* counterpart of TLS.
- **WIM** stores private keys in tamper-resistant hardware on the SIM/USIM; mandatory for non-repudiation.
- **WMLScript SignText** = mobile equivalent of a digital signature over user input.
- **WPKI** uses compressed X.509 certificates and short chains to save bandwidth.
- **WAP Gap** = the plaintext window inside the gateway between WTLS termination and TLS initiation — the most important security concept in this module.
- **WAP Gap is the reason** for the move to WAP 2.0 and end-to-end TLS.
- **Mitigations** for the WAP Gap: WAP 2.0 + end-to-end TLS, hardened gateway with HSM, no payload logging, WIM-based authentication, IPsec tunneling, application-level crypto (SET, 3-D Secure), and mutual TLS for the PPG.
- **Do not** confuse the WAP Gateway (application layer) with the GGSN (packet layer) or the SMS-C (telecom layer).
- **Standards body:** WAP Forum (now OMA — Open Mobile Alliance). Do not invent ISO numbers.
- **Common KTU pitfall:** Writing that "WTLS provides end-to-end security between handset and server" — **deduction guaranteed**.
